<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipbuilding Quality Steels (also called Marine Structural Steels or Hull Structural Steels)** are rolled carbon-manganese and micro-alloyed steel plates, sections, and bars that have been manufactured under the strict surveillance of a marine classification society (e.g., **ABS, LR, DNV, BV, GL, KR, NK, IRS**) and are certified to possess a guaranteed combination of **strength, toughness, weldability, and formability** suitable for fabricating the hull, superstructure, and primary load-bearing members of sea-going ships and offshore units.

These steels are designated by **alphanumeric grade codes** (e.g., Grade A, AH36, EH40, FH32) where:

- **The leading letter(s)** indicate the minimum Charpy V-notch impact test temperature.
- **The numerals (32, 36, 40)** indicate the minimum specified tensile strength in **kgf/mm² (≈ ksi)** or, in modern usage, the nominal yield-strength class in **N/mm²**.
- **The letters H, I, F, etc.** designate the strength/toughness category.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024, Module 1):** Students must be able to identify the four major class-society designations (**A/B/D/E** for normal-strength, and **AH/DH/EH/FH** with 32, 36, 40 suffixes for higher-strength), and recall the mechanical-property thresholds (yield strength, tensile strength, elongation, and impact energy) for each.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **car that must drive across a frozen lake in winter**. You would not use ordinary mild-steel body panels (which become brittle below 0 °C) or high-carbon tool steel (which is strong but cracks when welded). You would select a **purpose-built alloy** that is simultaneously:

- Strong enough to bear the weight of passengers (high yield strength).
- Tough enough to absorb the impact of hitting an ice ridge at 4 °C (Charpy impact energy).
- Soft enough to be bent and welded into a chassis without cracking (good formability & weldability).

**Shipbuilding quality steels are the maritime equivalent of that "perfectly balanced alloy"** — they are engineered to remain ductile and tough at temperatures as low as **−40 °C** (for FH grades) while still offering the strength-to-weight advantage a modern vessel demands. The classification society acts as a **third-party "quality inspector"** that stamps each plate, guaranteeing the shipowner and the insurer that every tonne of steel meets the published property envelope.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Mandate)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum Yield Strength | σ_y | 235 – 390 | N/mm² |
| Minimum Tensile Strength | σ_u | 400 – 510 | N/mm² |
| Minimum Elongation | %EL | 18 – 22 | % |
| Charpy V-Notch Impact Energy | KV | 27 – 46 | J |
| Test Temperature for Impact | T | 0, −20, −40, −60 | °C |
| Carbon Equivalent | CE (IIW) | ≤ 0.45 | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These six metrics are the **backbone of all hull-steel specifications** prescribed by **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** — the global body that harmonizes the rules of all major ship-registers. A student must be able to recall these values during KTU ESE valuation.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Philosophy of Shipbuilding Steels

Marine classification societies grade hull steel on **two independent axes**:

1. **Strength axis** → *Normal Strength (NS)* or *Higher Strength (HS)*.
2. **Toughness axis** → *Sub-zero impact grade* (A/B/D/E/F or AH/DH/EH/FH).

### 2.1.1 Normal-Strength Hull Steels (NS)

| Grade | Min σ_y (N/mm²) | Min σ_u (N/mm²) | Min %EL | Impact Temp (°C) | Min KV (J) | Heat Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Grade A | **235** | **400 – 520** | **22** | 20 | — | AR / N |
| Grade B | **235** | **400 – 520** | **22** | 0 | 27 | N |
| Grade D | **235** | **400 – 520** | **22** | −20 | 27 | N |
| Grade E | **235** | **400 – 520** | **22** | −40 | 27 | TMCP / QT |

> A, B, D, and E are rolled-carbon-manganese steels. The only difference between them is the **Charpy impact test temperature** and the **delivery condition** (As-Rolled, Normalized, or Thermo-Mechanically Controlled Processed).

### 2.1.2 Higher-Strength Hull Steels (HS)

The **32, 36, 40** suffix is the *nominal tensile-strength class* in **kgf/mm²**:

| Grade Family | Min σ_y (N/mm²) | Min σ_u (N/mm²) | Min %EL (L = 200 mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| AH32 / DH32 / EH32 / FH32 | **315** | **440 – 590** | 19 / 18 / 17 / 16 |
| AH36 / DH36 / EH36 / FH36 | **355** | **490 – 620** | 19 / 18 / 17 / 16 |
| AH40 / DH40 / EH40 / FH40 | **390** | **510 – 660** | 19 / 18 / 17 / 16 |

> The **second letter (H)** in the grade code indicates "Higher-strength hull steel." The **first letter (A, D, E, F)** indicates the **impact-test temperature** (0 °C, −20 °C, −40 °C, −60 °C respectively) — *A* is *room temperature*, *F* is *arctic*.

## 2.2 Delivery / Heat-Treatment Conditions

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the student's ability to **link delivery condition ↔ grain size ↔ toughness**. This is a high-yield module-1 topic.

| Condition | Symbol | Process | Resulting Microstructure | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| As-Rolled | AR | Hot rolling only | Banded ferrite + pearlite | Grade A (warm-water vessels) |
| Normalized | N | Reheated to A_c3 + air cooled | Fine, equiaxed ferrite + pearlite | Grades B, D |
| Thermo-Mechanically Controlled Processed | TMCP | Controlled rolling + accelerated cooling | Ultra-fine grain ferrite | Grades E, AH/DH/EH/FH |
| Quenched & Tempered | QT | Reheated, water-quenched, tempered | Tempered martensite/bainite | EH36, FH36, FH40 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fine grain size = high yield strength + high low-temperature toughness (Hall-Petch relationship).** This is *why* TMCP and QT grades can be simultaneously strong AND cryogenically tough — the same principle that makes the material suitable for LPG tankers operating at −162 °C (but those use 9 % Ni steel, a different topic).

## 2.3 The Carbon-Equivalent (CE) — Weldability Master Parameter

Weldability is **not measured directly**; it is predicted from chemical composition via the **Carbon-Equivalent (CE)** index. Two formulas are in use:

### 2.3.1 IIW (International Institute of Welding) Formula

$$CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

### 2.3.2 Pcm (Ito-Bessyo) Formula — Used for TMCP / HS Steels

$$P_{cm} = C + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Mn}{20} + \dfrac{Cu}{20} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{Cr}{20} + \dfrac{Mo}{15} + \dfrac{V}{10} + 5B$$

> **Why two formulas?** The IIW formula assumes **as-rolled / normalized** C-Mn steels (CE ≤ 0.45 is the safe-weldable limit). The Pcm formula is **calibrated for low-carbon micro-alloyed TMCP steels** (Pcm ≤ 0.22 is the modern target for EH/FH grades without preheat).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below verbatim — these are the *only* formulas the KTU board examiner expects in Module 1 numericals.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Limit for Hull Steel |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Carbon-equivalent (weldability index) | ≤ **0.45 %** for AR/N; ≤ **0.40 %** for HS |
| 2 | $P_{cm} = C + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Mn}{20} + \dfrac{Cu}{20} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{Cr}{20} + \dfrac{Mo}{15} + \dfrac{V}{10} + 5B$ | Low-C micro-alloyed CE | ≤ **0.22 %** for TMCP/EH/FH |
| 3 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Hall-Petch (grain-size strengthening) | Lower d ⇒ higher σ_y |
| 4 | $KV_{T} = KV_{0} \cdot e^{k(T - T_0)}$ | Empirical impact-energy vs. temperature (Arrhenius-type) | Used to qualify heat-treatment |
| 5 | $UTS = \sigma_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ | Engineering ultimate tensile strength | Direct from tensile test |

> **Note on LaTeX:** All vertical bars / division strokes inside the table above are written using `\dfrac{...}{...}` so the markdown table pipe-syntax is not broken.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Hull design (LR / ABS rules):** The grade directly fixes the **allowable bending stress** in the longitudinal strength calculation. Using AH36 instead of Grade A allows a **~ 35 % reduction in plate thickness**, which directly **decreases the lightweight displacement** and increases cargo deadweight.
2. **Welding procedure qualification:** CE dictates whether **preheating** (100–200 °C) is mandatory before arc strikes.
3. **Arctic / Polar-class operation:** Ice-class vessels require **E/F-grade** steels to survive impact at **−40 °C to −60 °C**.
4. **LNG / LPG carriers (cryogenic):** Use 9 % Ni steel, austenitic stainless, or invar liners — *outside* Module-1 scope but useful context.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Carbon-Equivalent Calculation (IIW)

**Problem:** A Grade D hull steel plate has the following ladle chemical analysis (in weight %):

| C | Mn | Si | S | P | Cr | Ni | Mo | Cu | V |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.16 | 1.10 | 0.30 | 0.025 | 0.020 | 0.04 | 0.03 | 0.01 | 0.02 | 0.005 |

**Task:** Compute the IIW carbon equivalent and decide whether the steel is weldable **without preheat** in the as-rolled condition (rule-of-thumb safe limit CE ≤ 0.40 % for plates ≤ 25 mm).

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.** Every alloying element contributes to CE through its individual weighting factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 0.16 \\
Mn &= 1.10 \\
Cr &= 0.04 \\
Mo &= 0.01 \\
V  &= 0.005 \\
Ni &= 0.03 \\
Cu &= 0.02
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the IIW formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CE_{IIW} &= C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15} \\
         &= 0.16 + \dfrac{1.10}{6} + \dfrac{(0.04 + 0.01 + 0.005)}{5} + \dfrac{(0.03 + 0.02)}{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term numerically.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{Mn}{6}        &= \dfrac{1.10}{6}     &= 0.1833 \\
\dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5}   &= \dfrac{0.055}{5}    &= 0.0110 \\
\dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}    &= \dfrac{0.05}{15}    &= 0.0033
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the components.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CE_{IIW} &= 0.16 + 0.1833 + 0.0110 + 0.0033 \\
         &= 0.3576 \; \% \;\;\approx\; 0.36 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the limit and conclude.**

$$
CE_{IIW} = 0.36 \;\% \;<\; 0.40 \;\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Weldable without preheat (plate thickness } \le 25 \text{ mm)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The steel is **weldable in the as-rolled condition** for plates up to 25 mm thick, without the need for preheating. (A small margin remains for the 25–40 mm range.)

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pcm Calculation for a TMCP EH36 Plate

**Problem:** A TMCP EH36 plate has chemistry (wt %):

| C | Mn | Si | Cr | Ni | Mo | Cu | V | B |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.09 | 1.45 | 0.25 | 0.05 | 0.20 | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.003 | 0.0008 |

**Task:** Compute the **Pcm** and check if it is ≤ 0.22 % (the IACS target for HS-TMCP plates).

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Substitute into the Pcm formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cm} &= C + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Mn}{20} + \dfrac{Cu}{20} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{Cr}{20} + \dfrac{Mo}{15} + \dfrac{V}{10} + 5B \\
       &= 0.09 + \dfrac{0.25}{30} + \dfrac{1.45}{20} + \dfrac{0.10}{20} + \dfrac{0.20}{60} + \dfrac{0.05}{20} + \dfrac{0.05}{15} + \dfrac{0.003}{10} + 5(0.0008)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{Si}{30}  &= 0.0083 \\
\dfrac{Mn}{20}  &= 0.0725 \\
\dfrac{Cu}{20}  &= 0.0050 \\
\dfrac{Ni}{60}  &= 0.0033 \\
\dfrac{Cr}{20}  &= 0.0025 \\
\dfrac{Mo}{15}  &= 0.0033 \\
\dfrac{V}{10}   &= 0.0003 \\
5B              &= 0.0040
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the terms.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cm} &= 0.0900 + 0.0083 + 0.0725 + 0.0050 + 0.0033 + 0.0025 + 0.0033 + 0.0003 + 0.0040 \\
       &= 0.1892 \;\% \;\;\approx\; 0.19 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the IACS target.**

$$
P_{cm} = 0.19 \;\% \;<\; 0.22 \;\% \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{TMCP EH36 — fully weldable without preheat, no HAZ cracking risk}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Low Pcm reflects a **low-carbon, Nb-V-Ti micro-alloyed** design — the steel gains its strength from **grain refinement (Hall-Petch)** and **precipitation strengthening**, *not* from carbon. This is the modern industrial approach.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. Process Linkage

| Property Tested | Test Standard | Acceptance Criterion (EH36) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength | ASTM A370 / ISO 6892-1 | ≥ 355 N/mm² | Plate's load-bearing capacity |
| Tensile Strength | ASTM A370 | 490 – 620 N/mm² | Resistance to plastic collapse |
| Elongation | ASTM A370 | ≥ 17 % (L = 200 mm) | Formability & ductility |
| Charpy V-Notch @ −40 °C | ASTM A370 / ISO 148-1 | ≥ 34 J (average of 3) | Brittle-fracture resistance |
| Bend Test (180°) | ASTM A370 | No cracks | Weldability + formability |
| Chemical Analysis | ASTM A751 | Per IACS UR W11 | Weldability / CE control |

---

## 3.4 Strength-Strengthening Mechanism Triangle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern TMCP EH/FH grades derive their properties from **three concurrent mechanisms**, all operating simultaneously:

1. **Grain refinement (Hall-Petch):**

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

where $d$ = average grain diameter. Smaller $d$ ⇒ larger $\sigma_y$ and **simultaneously** higher low-T toughness (the *only* strengthening mechanism that improves both).

2. **Solid-solution strengthening:** Substitutional Mn, Ni, Cu atoms distort the ferrite lattice, raising σ_y.
3. **Precipitation strengthening:** Nano-scale NbC, TiC, VN particles pin dislocations and grain boundaries.

> **The trick of TMCP:** Deliberately suppress (3) for the *welding* pass — dissolve the carbides during the weld thermal cycle — and rely on (1) for toughness. This is why **TMCP + low Pcm** is the *only* route to combine 390 N/mm² yield with −60 °C impact toughness.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Grade A, Grade B, Grade D, and Grade E hull structural steels used in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Grade A | Grade B | Grade D | Grade E |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| σ_y (min) | 235 N/mm² | 235 N/mm² | 235 N/mm² | 235 N/mm² |
| Charpy test T | +20 °C | 0 °C | −20 °C | −40 °C |
| Min KV | Not required | 27 J | 27 J | 27 J |
| Delivery | AR | N | N | TMCP / QT |
| Application | Inland / warm water | Coastal | Ocean-going | Polar / Arctic |

> All four belong to the **Normal-Strength (NS) family** with identical strength levels — only the **toughness** and **delivery condition** differ.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the IIW Carbon-Equivalent (CE) formula in evaluating the weldability of shipbuilding steels?**

**Model Answer (3 key points):**

1. The IIW formula $CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ **converts the combined alloy content into an equivalent carbon percentage**.
2. It predicts the **hardness and martensite-formation tendency** in the **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** of a weld.
3. **CE ≤ 0.40 %** ⇒ weldable without preheat for plates up to 25 mm; **CE > 0.45 %** ⇒ preheat mandatory; **CE > 0.60 %** ⇒ post-weld heat-treatment (PWHT) required.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part-B question is split into (a) and (b), 7 marks each. *Internal choice* means the examiner offers either **Or-A** or **Or-B** — the student picks one.

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model 2024] — (14 Marks)

**(a)** *With neat sketches/tables, classify the various grades of shipbuilding quality steels and explain how the IACS grade designation (A, B, D, E, AH, DH, EH, FH) reflects strength and toughness.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The IACS grade designation encodes two independent parameters:

**Step 1 — Strength Class.** The numeral (32, 36, 40) or the absence of a numeral determines the **tensile/yield strength** of the plate.

- *No numeral* → Normal Strength (σ_y ≥ 235 N/mm², σ_u 400–520 N/mm²)
- *32* → σ_y ≥ 315, σ_u 440–590 N/mm²
- *36* → σ_y ≥ 355, σ_u 490–620 N/mm²
- *40* → σ_y ≥ 390, σ_u 510–660 N/mm²

**Step 2 — Toughness Letter.** The first letter dictates the **Charpy V-notch test temperature**:

- **A** = +20 °C (room temperature)
- **B** = 0 °C
- **D** = −20 °C
- **E** = −40 °C
- **F** = −60 °C (Arctic / Polar-class)

**Step 3 — Combined Reading.** A plate stamped **"EH36"** therefore means: *Higher-strength hull steel* (H), tested at *−40 °C* (E), with a *36 kgf/mm²* tensile class. See the master classification tree in Section 4.1.

**(b)** *A 20-mm thick grade D plate has the chemical composition (wt %): C = 0.18, Mn = 1.20, Si = 0.30, Cr = 0.05, Ni = 0.04, Mo = 0.02, Cu = 0.03, V = 0.005. Calculate the IIW carbon equivalent and comment on its weldability.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

$$
CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CE_{IIW} &= 0.18 + \dfrac{1.20}{6} + \dfrac{(0.05 + 0.02 + 0.005)}{5} + \dfrac{(0.04 + 0.03)}{15} \\
         &= 0.18 + 0.2000 + \dfrac{0.075}{5} + \dfrac{0.07}{15} \\
         &= 0.18 + 0.2000 + 0.0150 + 0.0047 \\
         &= 0.3997 \;\% \;\;\approx\; 0.40 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Weldability comment.** CE = 0.40 % is exactly at the **safe-weldable limit** for plates up to 25 mm. The plate can be welded **without preheat**, provided the heat-input is controlled and the welding procedure is qualified to **IACS UR W11**. Above 25 mm thickness, **a preheat of 75–100 °C** is recommended as a precautionary measure.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model 2024] — (14 Marks) **[Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** *Explain the role of TMCP (Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing) and Quench-Tempering (QT) in achieving high yield-strength together with excellent low-temperature impact toughness in modern shipbuilding steels.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — TMCP.** TMCP combines **controlled rolling** (in the non-recrystallization region of austenite) with **accelerated cooling** directly off the rolling mill. Two outcomes:
- Austenite grains are **pancake-flattened**, restricting the recrystallized ferrite grain size to ≤ 5 µm.
- Nano-scale **NbC/TiC precipitates** pin the austenite boundaries during rolling.

The result: σ_y rises by 100–150 N/mm² *without* raising carbon, and the **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** drops by 30–50 °C — exactly what is needed for FH grades (tested at −60 °C).

**Step 2 — QT.** Quench-Tempering reheats the rolled plate into the austenite region, water-quenches to form **martensite/bainite**, and tempers at 600–650 °C to restore toughness. Used for the **EH40, FH40** and the *very thick* plates (≥ 50 mm) where TMCP cooling rate cannot penetrate the plate centre.

**Step 3 — Why both matter.** Both routes exploit the **Hall-Petch relation** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — *the only strengthening mechanism that improves strength and toughness simultaneously*. This is the central engineering rationale for E/F-class hull steels.

**(b)** *An EH36 TMCP plate has the following chemistry (wt %): C = 0.08, Si = 0.22, Mn = 1.40, Cu = 0.10, Ni = 0.18, Cr = 0.04, Mo = 0.04, V = 0.003, B = 0.0008. Compute the Pcm and verify whether it qualifies as low-Pcm TMCP steel.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Apply the Pcm formula.**

$$
P_{cm} = C + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Mn}{20} + \dfrac{Cu}{20} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{Cr}{20} + \dfrac{Mo}{15} + \dfrac{V}{10} + 5B
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cm} &= 0.08 + \dfrac{0.22}{30} + \dfrac{1.40}{20} + \dfrac{0.10}{20} + \dfrac{0.18}{60} + \dfrac{0.04}{20} + \dfrac{0.04}{15} + \dfrac{0.003}{10} + 5(0.0008) \\
       &= 0.0800 + 0.0073 + 0.0700 + 0.0050 + 0.0030 + 0.0020 + 0.0027 + 0.0003 + 0.0040 \\
       &= 0.1743 \;\% \;\;\approx\; 0.17 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification.**

$$
P_{cm} = 0.17 \;\% \;<\; 0.22 \;\% \;\;\checkmark
$$

The plate **qualifies as low-Pcm TMCP steel** and may be welded **without preheat** up to about 50 mm thickness, provided the welding procedure is qualified to **IACS UR W11** and the heat-input is kept below 50 kJ/cm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing IIW-CE with Pcm.** IIW is for AR/N steels (limit 0.40–0.45 %); Pcm is for TMCP/QT low-C steels (limit 0.22 %). Writing one for the other costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units** of yield/tensile strength (N/mm², *not* MPa, *not* kgf/mm²). Examiner deducts **0.5 mark** per omission.
> 3. **Skipping the "conclusion" sentence** in CE problems. The numerical answer alone is worth only ~ 4 of 7 marks; the welding-implication statement earns the balance.
> 4. **Mixing up "A" with "AH"** in grade tables. "A" is normal-strength; "AH" is higher-strength. The H makes a 100 N/mm² difference.
> 5. **Not mentioning the delivery condition** when listing grades. Examiner explicitly tests AR / N / TMCP / QT understanding.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Shipbuilding Quality Steels**

- [ ] **IACS** = International Association of Classification Societies; harmonizes all class rules (ABS, LR, DNV, BV, GL, KR, NK, IRS).
- [ ] **Two strength families:** Normal Strength (Grade A/B/D/E, σ_y = 235 N/mm²) and Higher Strength (AH/DH/EH/FH 32/36/40, σ_y = 315/355/390 N/mm²).
- [ ] **Letter code (A/B/D/E/F):** A = +20 °C, B = 0 °C, D = −20 °C, E = −40 °C, F = −60 °C (Charpy test temperature).
- [ ] **Numeral code (32/36/40):** nominal UTS in kgf/mm² → 440/490/510 N/mm² class.
- [ ] **Delivery conditions:** AR (Grade A), N (B, D), TMCP (E, AH, DH, EH), QT (FH, EH40/FH40).
- [ ] **IIW CE formula** (AR/N steels): limit ≤ 0.40 % for no-preheat welding up to 25 mm.
- [ ] **Pcm formula** (TMCP/QT steels): limit ≤ 0.22 % for no-preheat welding up to ~ 50 mm.
- [ ] **Hall-Petch** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$: the *only* mechanism that raises both strength AND toughness.
- [ ] **Fine grain = both strong and cryogenically tough**; this is *why* TMCP dominates modern hull steel.
- [ ] **CE > 0.45 %** ⇒ mandatory preheat; **CE > 0.60 %** ⇒ mandatory PWHT.
- [ ] **EH36** = Higher-strength, tested at −40 °C, 36 kgf/mm² class — the *workhorse* of modern container & bulker hulls.
- [ ] **FH-grade** steel = Polar Code / Ice-class vessel qualification (–60 °C impact).
- [ ] **Mechanical test schedule per plate:** Tensile (σ_y, σ_u, %EL), Charpy (3 specimens, average ≥ 27–46 J), Bend (180°), Chemical (C, Mn, S, P + micro-alloys).
- [ ] **ASTM A370 / ISO 6892-1** = tensile test standards; **ISO 148-1 / ASTM A370** = Charpy test standards; **IACS UR W11** = welding-procedure qualification.
- [ ] **Arctic / LNG / LPG** service needs *cryogenic* materials (9 % Ni, austenitic SS, Invar) — *outside Module 1*, do not confuse with hull-steel grades.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mild Steels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mild Steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mild Steel (MS)**, also designated as **Low Carbon Steel** or **Plain Carbon Steel**, is a ferrous alloy of iron and carbon in which the carbon content is restricted within the range of **0.05% to 0.25%** by weight, with no deliberately added alloying elements beyond the standard limits for manganese ($\le 1.0\%$), silicon ($\le 0.4\%$), sulphur ($\le 0.05\%$) and phosphorus ($\le 0.05\%$).

According to the **IS 2062:2011** (Bureau of Indian Standards) classification, mild steel is the most widely used structural grade in marine and offshore applications, commonly designated by grades such as **E250 (Fe 410)** and **E300 (Fe 440)**. The carbon content is the single most dominant variable that dictates strength, ductility, weldability, and machinability.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> Think of mild steel as the **"all-purpose flour"** of the metal world. Just as plain flour is used in almost every kitchen recipe—from bread to cakes to pancakes—mild steel is the default starting material for nearly every metalworking application, from ship hulls and bridges to nuts and bolts. It is not the strongest, nor the most corrosion-resistant, nor the prettiest, but it is **cheap, predictable, weldable, ductile, and available everywhere**—which makes it the workhorse of marine engineering.

If pure iron is like soft butter (too soft to hold shape under load) and high-carbon steel is like glass (strong but brittle), then mild steel is like **hard cheese**—firm enough to support structural loads, yet soft enough to bend, roll, forge, and weld without cracking.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Property | Symbol/Unit | Typical Value for Mild Steel |
|----------|-------------|------------------------------|
| Density | $\rho$ | **7850 kg/m³** |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **200 GPa** |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | **0.30** |
| Melting Point | $T_m$ | **1480 °C – 1520 °C** |
| Thermal Conductivity | $k$ | **45 W/m·K** |
| Electrical Resistivity | $\rho_e$ | **1.5 × 10⁻⁷ Ω·m** |
| Specific Heat Capacity | $c_p$ | **490 J/kg·K** |
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion | $\alpha$ | **11.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** |

### 1.4 Classification Based on Carbon Content (KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mild steel occupies the **lowest carbon band** of the plain carbon steel family, making it the most ductile and weldable grade.

| Designation | Carbon Content (% C) | KTU Exam Tag |
|-------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Dead Mild Steel / Extra Mild | 0.05 – 0.15 | Lowest strength, highest ductility |
| Mild Steel (Standard) | 0.15 – 0.25 | Most common marine structural grade |
| Medium Carbon Steel | 0.25 – 0.50 | Higher strength, lower weldability |
| High Carbon Steel | 0.50 – 1.00 | Springs, cutting tools |
| Ultra-High Carbon Steel | 1.00 – 2.00 | Knives, chisels |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Microstructural Theory

> [!NOTE]
> At room temperature, mild steel in its **hot-rolled, as-supplied (normalized)** condition exists predominantly as two phases:
> 1. **Ferrite ($\alpha$-Fe)** — A Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) phase, soft and ductile, occupying roughly **80–90%** of the microstructure.
> 2. **Pearlite** — A lamellar eutectoid mixture of alternating ferrite and cementite (Fe₃C) plates, occupying roughly **10–20%**.

The **ferrite-to-pearlite ratio** is determined by the carbon content using the **Lever Rule** applied to the iron–iron carbide (Fe–Fe₃C) phase diagram at the eutectoid temperature of **727 °C**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the phases present.**
For a hypoeutectoid steel (C < 0.77%), the microstructure consists of proeutectoid ferrite + pearlite.

**Step 2 — Apply the Lever Rule.**
The mass fraction of each phase is given by the tie-line at 727 °C.

**Step 3 — Compute pearlite fraction using carbon mass balance.**

$$\% \text{Pearlite} = \frac{C_0 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Compute proeutectoid ferrite fraction.**

$$\% \text{Proeutectoid Ferrite} = \frac{0.77 - C_0}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Correlate microstructure to mechanical properties.**
- Higher ferrite → higher ductility, lower strength
- Higher pearlite → higher strength, lower ductility, better machinability

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CHEAT SHEET — All formulas required for Mild Steel problems in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\%P = \dfrac{C_0 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100$ | Pearlite fraction (Lever Rule) | % |
| 2 | $\%\alpha = \dfrac{0.77 - C_0}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100$ | Proeutectoid ferrite fraction | % |
| 3 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Hall–Petch strengthening relation | MPa |
| 4 | $\sigma_{uts} \approx 400 + 1000 \cdot \%C$ | Approximate UTS vs. carbon (for MS) | MPa |
| 5 | $\%EL = 50 - 100 \cdot \%C$ | Approximate % elongation at fracture | % |
| 6 | $HB \approx 80 + 250 \cdot \%C$ | Approximate Brinell hardness | HB |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal strain | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal stress (constrained) | MPa |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{uts}}$ | Strain-hardening ratio (formability index) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $W = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \varepsilon$ | Strain energy per unit volume | MJ/m³ |

> **Notation Reminder:** $C_0$ = actual carbon content (wt.%), $d$ = average grain diameter (mm), $\sigma_0$ = friction stress (~70 MPa for ferrite), $k_y$ = Hall–Petch coefficient (~0.74 MPa·m¹ᐟ²), $E$ = Young's modulus (200 GPa), $\alpha$ = CTE (11.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Marine and Offshore Fields

| Application Sector | Specific Use of Mild Steel |
|--------------------|------------------------------|
| Ship hull plating | IS 2062 E250 grade for plate panels |
| Marine structural beams | Rolled sections (I, H, T, angle, channel) |
| Piping systems | Low-pressure seawater/freshwater lines |
| Anchor chains and shackles | Forged MS components |
| Welding electrodes | E6013, E7018 filler rods are MS-based |
| Ship-repair and fabrication | Easily cut, rolled, and welded in dry dock |

> **Real-World Trade-off:** Mild steel is selected in marine service because of its **superior weldability** and **formability**, even though its corrosion rate in seawater (≈ **0.1–0.5 mm/year**) is significantly higher than that of stainless steel or cupro-nickel alloys. This is why mild steel is always protected by coatings, cathodic protection, or both.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Problem 1: Pearlite Fraction by Lever Rule (14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem:** A mild steel used in a ship's hull contains **0.20% C**. Determine the percentages of (a) proeutectoid ferrite, (b) pearlite, (c) eutectoid ferrite within pearlite, and (d) total ferrite in the microstructure at room temperature.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant phase-diagram tie-line.**

At the eutectoid temperature $T = 727\,°C$, the relevant compositions on the Fe–Fe₃C diagram are:

- $\alpha$-ferrite composition: $C_\alpha = 0.022\%$
- Eutectoid composition: $C_E = 0.77\%$
- Alloy composition: $C_0 = 0.20\%$

**Step 2 — Verify the alloy is hypoeutectoid.**

$$C_0 = 0.20\% < 0.77\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Alloy is hypoeutectoid (mild steel)}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate pearlite fraction using the Lever Rule.**

$$\% \text{Pearlite} = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_E - C_\alpha} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Pearlite} = \frac{0.20 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Pearlite} = \frac{0.178}{0.748} \times 100 = 23.80\%$$

**Step 4 — Calculate proeutectoid ferrite fraction.**

$$\% \text{Proeutectoid Ferrite} = \frac{C_E - C_0}{C_E - C_\alpha} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Proeutectoid Ferrite} = \frac{0.77 - 0.20}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100 = \frac{0.57}{0.748} \times 100 = 76.20\%$$

**Step 5 — Calculate eutectoid ferrite contained within the pearlite.**

The pearlite itself is composed of 88% ferrite and 12% cementite (by mass, calculated from the eutectoid lever rule). Therefore:

$$\% \text{Eutectoid Ferrite} = \%P \times 0.88 = 23.80 \times 0.88 = 20.94\%$$

**Step 6 — Calculate total ferrite (proeutectoid + eutectoid).**

$$\% \alpha_{\text{total}} = 76.20 + 20.94 = 97.14\%$$

**Step 7 — Cross-check the mass balance.**

$$\% \alpha_{\text{total}} + \%\text{Fe}_3\text{C} = 97.14 + 2.86 = 100\% \quad \checkmark$$

where $\%\text{Fe}_3\text{C} = 23.80 \times 0.12 = 2.86\%$.

**Step 8 — Final Result Table.**

| Phase | Mass % |
|-------|--------|
| Proeutectoid Ferrite | 76.20 |
| Eutectoid Ferrite (in Pearlite) | 20.94 |
| Total Ferrite | 97.14 |
| Cementite (in Pearlite) | 2.86 |
| **Total** | **100.00** |

### 3.2 Derivative Problem 2: Hall–Petch Strengthening in Mild Steel

**Step 1 — State the Hall–Petch relation.**

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

**Step 2 — Insert the standard constants for low-carbon steel.**

$$\sigma_0 = 70\,\text{MPa}, \quad k_y = 0.74\,\text{MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}, \quad d = 25\,\mu\text{m} = 25 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $d^{-1/2}$.**

$$d^{-1/2} = (25 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{25 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200\,\text{m}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the grain-boundary strengthening contribution.**

$$k_y \cdot d^{-1/2} = 0.74 \times 200 = 148\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the total yield strength.**

$$\sigma_y = 70 + 148 = 218\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with experimental data.**

The experimentally reported yield strength of normalized 0.20% C mild steel is **220–250 MPa**, which agrees well with the calculated value, confirming the validity of the Hall–Petch model for mild steel.

### 3.3 Derivative Problem 3: Thermal Stress During Welding of MS Plate

> **Problem:** Two 0.20% C mild steel plates of length $L = 1.0$ m are butt-welded. During welding, a local temperature of $\Delta T = 600\,°C$ is reached. If the joint is rigidly constrained, calculate the thermal stress developed.

**Step 1 — Free thermal strain.**

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 11.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 600 = 6.96 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Constrained thermal stress (Hooke's Law).**

$$\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = 200 \times 10^{9} \times 6.96 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 1.392 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa} = 1392\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Critical observation.**

This calculated stress (1392 MPa) far exceeds the **yield strength (≈ 250 MPa)** of mild steel. Therefore, the steel will **yield locally, deform plastically, and relieve the stress** during cooling. The residual stress that remains is therefore approximately equal to the yield strength:

$$\sigma_{\text{residual}} \approx \sigma_y = 250\,\text{MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This residual welding stress is a major reason why marine welded structures suffer from **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** and **corrosion fatigue** in service. Post-Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT) is mandatory for critical marine pressure-vessel applications to relieve this stress.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define mild steel and specify its typical carbon content range. Why is it the most commonly used steel for marine structural applications?**

**Model Answer:**
Mild steel is a low-carbon plain carbon steel containing **0.05% to 0.25% carbon** by weight, with no significant alloying additions. The balance is iron plus small amounts of manganese, silicon, sulphur, and phosphorus as residuals.

It is preferred for marine structural applications because of its:
- Excellent **weldability** (low C-equivalent, minimal hard HAZ)
- High **ductility and formability** for rolling and bending of plate
- **Low cost** and **wide availability** in standard structural sections
- Adequate **strength** (250–410 MPa UTS) for hull and deck applications
- **Ease of cutting, drilling, and machining** during ship construction

(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + range, 2 marks for marine advantages.)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the typical microstructure, density, and Young's modulus of mild steel.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Microstructure:** Proeutectoid ferrite (~80–95%) + Pearlite (~5–20%), both visible under optical microscopy after nital etching.
- **Density:** $\rho = 7850\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- **Young's Modulus:** $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$

(3 marks — 1 mark each.)

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (ESE Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Module Question Pattern:** Each module yields one 14-mark question with **internal choice**. Below are two complete, independent alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — 0.20% C Mild Steel for Ship Hull Plate**

**(a)** A marine grade mild steel used for a ship's hull contains 0.20% C. Calculate the mass fractions of:
1. Pearlite
2. Proeutectoid ferrite
3. Total ferrite
4. Cementite (Fe₃C) in the microstructure at room temperature.

**(b)** Explain the effect of carbon content on the mechanical properties of mild steel. How does the welding behaviour of mild steel differ from that of medium carbon steel?

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

Given: $C_0 = 0.20\%$, $C_\alpha = 0.022\%$, $C_E = 0.77\%$, cementite composition = 6.67% C.

**1. Pearlite Fraction**

$$\%P = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_E - C_\alpha} \times 100 = \frac{0.20 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100 = 23.80\%$$

**2. Proeutectoid Ferrite Fraction**

$$\%\alpha_{\text{pro}} = \frac{C_E - C_0}{C_E - C_\alpha} \times 100 = \frac{0.77 - 0.20}{0.748} \times 100 = 76.20\%$$

**3. Total Ferrite**

$$\%\alpha_{\text{total}} = \%\alpha_{\text{pro}} + (\%P \times 0.88) = 76.20 + (23.80 \times 0.88) = 76.20 + 20.94 = 97.14\%$$

**4. Cementite**

$$\%\text{Fe}_3\text{C} = \%P \times 0.12 = 23.80 \times 0.12 = 2.86\%$$

**Verification:** $97.14 + 2.86 = 100.00\%$ ✓

| Phase | Mass % |
|-------|--------|
| Total Ferrite | 97.14 |
| Cementite | 2.86 |

(7 marks — 2 marks for pearlite, 2 marks for ferrite, 1.5 marks for cementite, 1.5 marks for mass-balance verification.)

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Effect of Carbon Content on Mechanical Properties (Table):**

| Property | Trend with Increasing C (within 0.05–0.25%) |
|----------|-------------------------------------------|
| Tensile Strength | Increases (≈ +100 MPa per 0.1% C) |
| Yield Strength | Increases |
| Hardness (HB) | Increases |
| % Elongation | Decreases |
| Impact Toughness | Decreases |
| Weldability | Decreases sharply |

**Welding Behaviour — Mild Steel vs. Medium Carbon Steel:**

| Aspect | Mild Steel (0.05–0.25% C) | Medium Carbon Steel (0.25–0.50% C) |
|--------|----------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Preheating | Not required | Mandatory (150–300 °C) |
| Hard HAZ | Absent | Martensite formation risk |
| Cracking Susceptibility | Low | High (cold cracking) |
| Filler Rods | E6013, E7018 standard | Low-hydrogen rods only |
| Post-Weld Heat Treatment | Optional | Usually required |
| Ease of Repair Welding | Easy | Difficult and risky |

> Mild steel's low carbon equivalent (CE < 0.35) ensures that the heat-affected zone (HAZ) cools to a soft ferrite-plus-pearlite structure without forming brittle martensite, allowing welding without preheating in most marine workshop conditions.

(7 marks — 3 marks for property table, 4 marks for welding comparison with reasoning.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Heat Treatment & Marine Service**

**(a)** Discuss the various heat treatment processes applicable to mild steel. Explain how normalizing differs from annealing for a 0.20% C steel plate intended for ship hull construction.

**(b)** A 1.5 m long MS plate of grade E250 is rigidly clamped between two immovable supports. If the temperature rises by 500 °C during a fire exposure, calculate:
1. The free thermal strain
2. The thermal stress if unyielded
3. The actual residual stress after local yielding (assume $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $E = 200$ GPa)

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Heat Treatment Processes for Mild Steel:**

1. **Normalizing** — Heat to 880–920 °C (above $A_{c3}$), hold for ~1 hour per 25 mm thickness, air cool. Refines grain, homogenizes structure, improves toughness and machinability.
2. **Annealing (Full)** — Heat to 850–900 °C, hold, furnace cool slowly. Softens the steel, relieves internal stress, maximizes ductility.
3. **Stress Relieving** — Heat to 600–650 °C (below $A_{c1}$), hold, slow cool. Specifically targets residual welding/machining stresses without changing microstructure significantly.
4. **Spheroidizing** — Prolonged heating just below $A_{c1}$ (~700 °C). Not common for MS but occasionally used.
5. **Hardening / Quenching** — Not applicable to MS (cannot form significant martensite due to low C).
6. **Tempering** — Not applicable independently for MS (no quench-hardening step).

**Normalizing vs. Annealing for 0.20% C MS Plate:**

| Parameter | Normalizing | Full Annealing |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| Austenitizing Temp. | 880–920 °C | 850–900 °C |
| Cooling Medium | Still air (faster) | Furnace (very slow) |
| Cooling Rate | Moderate (~30 °C/min) | Very slow (~3 °C/min) |
| Resulting Grain Size | Fine | Coarse |
| Hardness | Slightly higher (~120 HB) | Lower (~105 HB) |
| Toughness | High (fine grain) | Moderate |
| Strength | Higher (Hall–Petch) | Lower |
| Production Time | Shorter | Longer |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Preferred For Marine Plate | **Yes — standard shipbuilding practice** | Only for re-softening prior to cold forming |

> For ship hull plates, **normalizing** is the industry-preferred delivery condition (per IS 2062) because it provides a fine-grained, tough, and dimensionally stable plate that resists brittle fracture in cold-sea service.

(7 marks — 3 marks for listing heat treatments, 4 marks for normalizing vs. annealing comparison.)

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

Given: $L = 1.5$ m, $\Delta T = 500\,°C$, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}/°C$, $E = 200$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa.

**1. Free Thermal Strain**

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 500 = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{th} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

(2 marks)

**2. Theoretical Thermal Stress if Fully Elastic**

$$\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = 200 \times 10^{9} \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 1.2 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa} = 1200\,\text{MPa}$$

(2 marks)

**3. Actual Residual Stress after Local Yielding**

Since $\sigma_{th} = 1200$ MPa $\gg \sigma_y = 250$ MPa, the steel yields locally. Plastic deformation relieves most of the stress, and the residual stress equals the yield strength:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{residual}} = \sigma_y = 250\,\text{MPa}}$$

(2 marks)

> **Engineering Insight:** This residual compressive (or tensile, depending on boundary conditions) stress of ~250 MPa is responsible for **warpage, distortion, and stress-corrosion cracking** observed in fire-exposed ship decks. It also explains why fire-fighting water-spray cooling is preferred over sudden quenching—rapid cooling would superimpose a second thermal-stress cycle on the residual stress.

(1 mark for insight.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Mild Steel Problems (KTU 2024 Valuation Key Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Wrong tie-line endpoints:** Students often use 0.008% C (solubility of C in ferrite at room temperature) instead of **0.022% C** (solubility at 727 °C). The Lever Rule MUST be applied at the **eutectoid temperature**, not at room temperature. Marks lost: up to 4.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the proeutectoid phase:** For hypoeutectoid steels, you MUST calculate proeutectoid ferrite separately from eutectoid ferrite. Reporting only "ferrite = 97%" without specifying both will cost 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Unit confusion in Hall–Petch:** $d$ must be in **metres** when $k_y$ is expressed in MPa·m¹ᐟ². Using $d$ in mm gives answers off by a factor of ~31.6.
>
> 4. **Thermal stress without yielding check:** Students often write $\sigma_{th} = 1392$ MPa and stop, without noting that the material yields. The examiner expects a clear statement: *"This exceeds the yield strength; the material yields and the residual stress equals σ_y."* Missing this costs 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Wrong IS grade recall:** Always use **IS 2062 E250** or **E300** for marine MS, not "A36" (which is ASTM, not KTU-expected). Although A36 is functionally similar, KTU papers expect IS nomenclature.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Mild Steels" — KTU ESE Module 1**

- **Definition:** Low-carbon steel with **0.05–0.25% C**, no alloying additions.
- **Standard Grades (Indian):** **IS 2062 E250, E300, E350, E410, E450** (yield strength in MPa).
- **Microstructure:** **Ferrite (~97%) + Pearlite (~3%)** for 0.20% C MS.
- **Eutectoid reference temperature:** **727 °C**; eutectoid composition: **0.77% C**.
- **Lever Rule endpoints at 727 °C:** Ferrite = 0.022% C; Cementite = 6.67% C.
- **Density:** **7850 kg/m³**; **Young's Modulus:** **200 GPa**; **Poisson's Ratio:** 0.30.
- **Hall–Petch constants for ferrite:** $\sigma_0 \approx 70$ MPa, $k_y \approx 0.74$ MPa·m¹ᐟ².
- **Weldability:** Excellent (no preheat needed for CE < 0.35).
- **Corrosion rate in seawater:** **0.1–0.5 mm/year** (always requires protection).
- **Preferred delivery condition for marine plate:** **Normalized (IS 2062 N)**.
- **Carbon effect on properties:** ↑C ⇒ ↑strength, ↑hardness, ↓ductility, ↓weldability.
- **Heat treatments applicable:** Normalizing, Annealing, Stress Relieving (NOT hardening).
- **Thermal stress formula:** $\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ (clamped condition).
- **Residual stress limit:** $\sigma_{\text{residual}} = \sigma_y$ (after local yielding).
- **Marine applications:** Hull plating, superstructure, decks, bulkheads, pipes, brackets.
- **Protection systems used with MS:** Epoxy coatings + Cathodic Protection (sacrificial Zn/Al anodes).
- **Key codes to remember:** **IS 2062:2011** (MS), **IS 3039** (rolled sections), **ASTM A36** (US equivalent).

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Topic: Mild Steels**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, OENST612 — Marine Materials, Corrosion Prevention and Protection.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal Strength Steels (A & E Classes)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Normal Strength Steels (A & E Classes) # Module 1 – Normal Strength Steels (A & E Classes) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Normal Strength Steels (NSS)** are carbon-manganese structural steels used in shipbuilding and offshore fabrication whose specified minimum yield strength does **not exceed 235 MPa (34 ksi)** for thicknesses up to 100 mm. They are designated by **letter grades (A, B, D, E)** under classification society rules (IACS Unified Requirements UR W1, UR W2, ASTM A131, ABS Rules, DNV GL, Lloyd's Register). Within this family, **Class A** and **Class E** represent the two ends of the toughness spectrum — A being the baseline "general-purpose" grade tested only at ambient temperature, and E being the premium notch-tough grade tested down to **–40 °C**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight – IACS Unified Requirement UR W1:** All IACS member societies (ABS, BV, DNV, LR, KR, CCS, ClassNK, RINA, IRS, PRS, CRS, KRS) classify ship-plate steels using the same letter code. The letter **does NOT indicate chemistry** — it strictly indicates the **Charpy V-notch impact test temperature and minimum absorbed energy**. | Designation | Grade Type | Min. Yield (MPa) | Impact Test Temp. | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Grade A** | Normal Strength | 235 | 0 °C (Class A: 20 °C / 0 °C per society) | Hull bottom, side shell — non-critical regions | | **Grade B** | Normal Strength | 235 | 0 °C | Deck plates, bulkheads | | **Grade D** | Normal Strength | 235 | –20 °C | Side shell in cold zones | | **Grade E** | Normal Strength | 235 | –40 °C | Bow, stern, ice-strengthened regions, polar service | ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are choosing a **water bottle for a trek in Kerala**: - A **plain plastic bottle (Class A)** is cheap, light, and works fine if the temperature stays around 25–30 °C. You do not test it for extreme cold. - A **double-walled stainless-steel thermos (Class E)** is built with the same liquid capacity and weight class, but it has been **drop-tested, frozen-tested, and impact-tested** so that even if you are trekking in Munnar at –2 °C or carrying it on a rattling jeep, the bottle will not crack. In ships, the "bottle" is a steel plate. The "trekking temperature" is the **sea temperature the ship sails through** — tropical (0 °C test is enough for the A grade) vs. polar/ice-stern regions (–40 °C test needed for the E grade). The same 235 MPa yield strength is kept; only the **Charpy toughness** changes. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Takeaway:** "Normal Strength" refers to yield strength ≤ 235 MPa, **not** the strength level of the steel's chemistry. The terminology is historical and refers to pre-IHCS (Improved Higher-tensile Category Steel) classification where 235 MPa was the standard. ### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **Yield strength, σ_y = 235 MPa** (for all A, B, D, E grades up to 100 mm thickness) - **Tensile strength, σ_u = 400 – 520 MPa** - **Elongation, ε ≥ 22 %** (on 200 mm gauge) - **Charpy V-notch energy, KV ≥ 27 J** (longitudinal specimen — the standard IACS value for Grade A at 0 °C; Grade E requires the same 27 J at –40 °C) - **Density, ρ = 7,850 kg/m³** - **Young's modulus, E = 210 GPa** - **Poisson's ratio, ν = 0.30** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The IACS Grade-Letter System — Decoded The classification societies (ABS, DNV, Lloyd's, etc.) classify ship-plate steel using a **two-character code** (e.g., "A", "EH36", "DH40"). The logic is: **Position 1 — Letter (A, B, D, E):** - Specifies the **Charpy V-notch (CVN) impact test temperature**. - Same letter ⇒ same toughness requirement, regardless of which society issues the certificate. - A = mildest toughness; E = toughest. **Position 2 — Optional "H" and number (e.g., A36, EH36):** - "H" stands for **Higher Strength**. - Number 32, 36, 40 corresponds to yield strength in ksi × 10 (i.e., 32 = 32,000 psi ≈ 315 MPa, 36 = 36,000 psi ≈ 355 MPa, 40 = 40,000 psi ≈ 390 MPa). - *This topic is purely Normal Strength*, so this "H" designator does **not** apply to Grade A or E (both stay at 235 MPa). **Position 3 — Optional "TMCP" or "CR" suffix:** - Indicates **Thermo-Mechanical Control Process** (TMCP) or **Control Rolled (CR)** delivery condition. - TMCP is a modern alternative to normalizing for achieving fine grain. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Why "A" vs "E" The decision between Class A and Class E is **not** based on load, but on **service temperature + dynamic loading risk**. | Step | Decision Factor | Class A Acceptable? | Class E Required? | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Plate thickness ≤ 100 mm | ✔ | ✔ | | 2 | Service temperature ≥ 0 °C | ✔ | ✔ | | 3 | Service temperature 0 °C to –20 °C | ✖ (need D) | ✔ | | 4 | Service temperature –20 °C to –40 °C | ✖ | ✔ | | 5 | Ice-strengthened bow/stern | ✖ | ✔ (per IACS UR I1) | | 6 | Polar Class PC1–PC7 vessels | ✖ | ✔ (with F-grade Arctic steel) | | 7 | Highly stressed hatch corners, sheer strakes | Optional | Strongly recommended | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Board Pattern:** When the question asks "Why is Grade E used at the bow of a vessel sailing in Arctic waters?" — your model answer must mention **DBTT (Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature)** and the **Charpy 27 J at –40 °C** requirement. Mark allocation is 2/3 marks for the impact-test reasoning, 1 mark for the DBTT explanation. ## 2.3 Metallurgical Background — Why Does E Have Better Toughness Than A? Both A and E are essentially **carbon-manganese (C-Mn) steels** with similar yield strength (235 MPa). The differences lie in: 1. **Chemical Composition Tightening:** - **Class A:** C ≤ 0.21 %, Mn = 0.60 – 1.60 % (loose control) - **Class E:** C ≤ 0.18 %, Mn = 0.70 – 1.60 %, with **mandatory micro-alloying** (Nb, V, Ti, or Al — at least one grain-refining element) - Class E also has lower **Sulfur (S ≤ 0.035 %)** and **Phosphorus (P ≤ 0.035 %)** to reduce inclusions. 2. **Grain Refinement:** - Class A is usually **hot-rolled** (as-rolled) — coarse austenite grains, larger ferrite grains. - Class E is **Normalized (N)** or **TMCP (Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing)** — fine, equiaxed ferrite + pearlite grains. - Grain size effect on toughness follows the **Hall-Petch relationship** (extended to cleavage strength): $$\sigma_{cleavage} = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$ where $d$ is the average ferrite grain diameter. Smaller $d$ ⇒ higher cleavage stress ⇒ tougher steel. 3. **Inclusion Shape Control:** - Class E is often produced with **Ca-treatment** or low-S, low-O practice to make MnS inclusions globular rather than elongated. Elongated MnS is a crack-initiator at low temperature. 4. **Deoxidation Practice:** - Class A is typically **semi-killed** (acceptable for some societies) or fully killed. - Class E is **fully killed with Al** (≥ 0.020 % acid-soluble Al) — required to suppress the porosity that would otherwise become a crack initiation site at low temperature. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **single most-tested formula set** for this topic in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the symbols, units, and limits. | # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Legend | Units | Used For | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield stress, $F$ = tensile load, $A_0$ = original area | MPa (N/mm²) | Tensile test interpretation | | 2 | $\sigma_u = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | $\sigma_u$ = ultimate tensile strength | MPa | UTS determination | | 3 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$ | $\varepsilon$ = % elongation | % | Ductility check | | 4 | $KV_{long} \geq 27 \text{ J}$ at test temp | Charpy V-notch, longitudinal | Joule | Grade A/B/C/D/E toughness | | 5 | $KV_{trans} \geq 20 \text{ J}$ at test temp | Transverse (through-thickness) | Joule | For thicknesses $\geq$ 25 mm | | 6 | $\sigma_{cleavage} = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | Hall–Petch cleavage | MPa | Explaining grain refinement | | 7 | $T_{DBTT} \propto \ln(\dot{\varepsilon})$ | Strain-rate sensitivity of transition | K (or °C) | Why E is needed at high strain-rate zones | | 8 | $R_{p0.2}$ = 0.2 % proof stress (offshore) | For thin plates where yield is gradual | MPa | Substitute for $\sigma_y$ | | 9 | $\sigma_{allowable} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{FS}$, $FS = 1.67$ for NSS | Factor of safety per IACS | MPa | Scantling calculation | | 10 | $E = 210 \text{ GPa},\ \rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Stiffness and density | GPa, kg/m³ | Buckling and weight calcs | > **Note on absolute values:** The notation for "magnitude of stress" should be written as $\vert \sigma \vert$ in running text. In the table above, no $\vert$ symbols appear, so no rendering conflict. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering 1. **Hull scantling (UR S21, S22):** Plate thickness $t = f(\sigma_y, L, B, T, C_B)$ — using 235 MPa as the base yield for all A–E classes simplifies global hull-girder strength calculations. 2. **Ice-class vessels (IACS UR I1, Polar Code):** Class E is the entry requirement for IA, IB, IC ice classes; higher Polar Classes (PC1–PC7) demand F-class steel (E with extra toughness). 3. **FPSO topsides:** Even when not exposed to ice, E-grade is used in **splash zone** and **hatch coaming** regions for fatigue resistance. 4. **Welding compatibility:** Both A and E are readily weldable by SMAW, GMAW, FCAW with standard E7018 / E7028 electrodes. E-grade needs slightly more attention to **interpass temperature** (≤ 230 °C) and **heat input** (≤ 50 kJ/cm) to preserve the as-rolled grain refinement in the HAZ. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charpy Energy ↔ DBTT Relationship (Knee-of-Curve Method) The Charpy V-notch impact test gives a curve of absorbed energy $KV$ vs. test temperature $T$. For ship-plate steels, the "knee" defines the **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)**. KTU board questions frequently ask you to derive the mean energy criterion: ### Step 1 — Define the energy axis Let the Charpy curve be expressed empirically as: $$KV(T) = KV_{max} - (KV_{max} - KV_{min}) \cdot \left[ 1 + \tanh\!\left(\dfrac{T - T_{0}}{C}\right) \right] / 2$$ where: - $KV_{max}$ = upper shelf energy (typically 200–300 J for E-grade) - $KV_{min}$ = lower shelf energy (typically 5–15 J for E-grade) - $T_0$ = inflection point (≈ DBTT for ½(KV_max + KV_min)) - $C$ = width parameter (controls the slope of transition) ### Step 2 — Set the criterion KTU/IACS criterion: $KV \geq 27$ J. Substituting: $$27 = KV_{max} - (KV_{max} - KV_{min}) \cdot \dfrac{1 + \tanh[(T_{27} - T_0)/C]}{2}$$ Rearranging: $$\dfrac{1 + \tanh[(T_{27} - T_0)/C]}{2} = \dfrac{KV_{max} - 27}{KV_{max} - KV_{min}}$$ ### Step 3 — Solve for $T_{27}$ Take the inverse hyperbolic tangent on both sides: $$\tanh\!\left(\dfrac{T_{27} - T_0}{C}\right) = \dfrac{2(KV_{max} - 27)}{KV_{max} - KV_{min}} - 1 = \dfrac{KV_{max} - 2 \cdot 27 - KV_{min}}{KV_{max} - KV_{min}}$$ $$\dfrac{T_{27} - T_0}{C} = \tanh^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{KV_{max} - KV_{min} - 54}{KV_{max} - KV_{min}}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Final form $$\boxed{\,T_{27} = T_0 + C \cdot \tanh^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{54}{KV_{max} - KV_{min}}\right)\,}$$ ### Step 5 — Numerical example For Grade E: $KV_{max} = 240$ J, $KV_{min} = 10$ J, $C = 25$ °C, $T_0 = -30$ °C. Compute the inner fraction: - $KV_{max} - KV_{min} = 230$ J - $54 / 230 = 0.2348$ - $1 - 0.2348 = 0.7652$ - $\tanh^{-1}(0.7652) = 1.001$ rad Therefore: $$T_{27} = -30 + 25 \times 1.001 = -30 + 25.03 = -4.97\ ^\circ\text{C}$$ **Interpretation:** Grade E reaches the 27 J criterion at about **–5 °C**, well above the –40 °C test requirement — confirming that Grade E has a large safety margin in ice-class service. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Allowable Stress for NSS Plate in Ship Hull IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR) define allowable stress for normal strength steel as: ### Step 1 — Yield-based allowable $$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{FS}, \quad FS = 1.67$$ $$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{235}{1.67} = 140.72\ \text{MPa} \approx 141\ \text{MPa}$$ ### Step 2 — Tensile-based allowable $$\sigma_{allow,u} = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{FS_u}, \quad FS_u = 2.00,\ \sigma_u = 410\ \text{MPa (mean)}$$ $$\sigma_{allow,u} = \dfrac{410}{2.00} = 205\ \text{MPa}$$ ### Step 3 — Governing allowable The smaller of the two governs: $$\sigma_{allow,governing} = \min(141,\ 205) = 141\ \text{MPa}$$ ### Step 4 — Plate thickness for a given hull-girder bending stress For a simply-supported hull girder section under sagging moment $M_s$: $$t_{bottom} = \dfrac{M_s}{\sigma_{allow,governing} \cdot Z_{net} / b}$$ Substituting $\sigma_{allow} = 141$ MPa for a representative $M_s = 1.5 \times 10^9$ N·mm and section modulus $Z/b = 1.2 \times 10^4$ mm²/m: $$t_{bottom} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^9}{141 \times 1.2 \times 10^4} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^9}{1.692 \times 10^6} = 886.5\ \text{mm}$$ This would obviously be subdivided into multiple plates — the derivation shows that using **NSS E-grade** (same $\sigma_y$) gives the same thickness as Grade A in pure strength calculation; the choice of E is dictated by **toughness**, not strength. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — A vs E vs Higher Strength | Property | Grade A | Grade E | AH36 (for reference) | |---|---|---|---| | Yield, $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | 235 | 235 | 355 | | Tensile, $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | 400 – 520 | 400 – 520 | 490 – 620 | | Elongation (%) | ≥ 22 | ≥ 22 | ≥ 21 | | CVN test temp (°C) | 0 | –40 | 0 | | Min. CVN energy (J) | 27 | 27 | 34 | | Typical delivery | As-rolled (AR) | Normalized (N) or TMCP | TMCP / QT | | Deoxidation | Semi-killed / Killed | Fully killed with Al | Fully killed | | Carbon (% max) | 0.21 | 0.18 | 0.18 | | Mn (%) | 0.60 – 1.60 | 0.70 – 1.60 (often ≥ 1.20) | 0.90 – 1.60 | | Nb/V/Ti micro-alloy | Optional | Mandatory (≥ 1) | Mandatory | | Cost (relative) | 1.0× | 1.15 – 1.25× | 1.5 – 1.7× | | Weldability (CE) | CE ≤ 0.40 | CE ≤ 0.40 | CE ≤ 0.45 | | Application | Inner-bottom, side shell (warm zone) | Bow, stern, ice belt, hatch coaming | Hatch covers, sheer strakes, sheer strakes of large ULCCs | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** When asked to "compare Grade A and Grade E," present the comparison as a **table** in the exam — this saves time and earns full method marks. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Equivalence between ksi and MPa for IACS Numbers Many classification rules still publish strengths in **ksi** alongside MPa. The conversion is: $$1\ \text{ksi} = 6.8948\ \text{MPa}$$ For Grade A/E: $$\sigma_y = 34\ \text{ksi} = 34 \times 6.8948 = 234.42\ \text{MPa} \approx 235\ \text{MPa}$$ For AH36: $$\sigma_y = 36\ \text{ksi} = 36 \times 6.8948 = 248.21\ \text{ksi-MPa}$$ Wait — IACS rounds and specifies 355 MPa for "36 ksi-grade" because the historical "36" referred to **tensile class not yield**. Modern rules re-specify: $$\sigma_y = 355\ \text{MPa},\quad \sigma_u = 490 - 620\ \text{MPa}$$ This mismatch is the most common source of confusion in KTU exam answers. The "36" is the tensile-strength class index, **not** a yield-strength index. **State this explicitly in your answer to gain the 1 mark that examiners allocate for unit awareness.** # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — IACS Grade Classification Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Shipbuilding Steel Plate] --> nss{Normal Strength
sigma_y le 235 MPa} start --> hss[Higher Strength
sigma_y 315 to 390 MPa] nss --> A[Grade A
Test at 0 deg C
KV ge 27 J] nss --> B[Grade B
Test at 0 deg C
KV ge 27 J
Fully killed] nss --> D[Grade D
Test at minus 20 deg C
KV ge 27 J] nss --> E[Grade E
Test at minus 40 deg C
KV ge 27 J
Normalized or TMCP] hss --> AH[AH32 AH36 AH40] hss --> DH[DH32 DH36 DH40] hss --> EH[EH32 EH36 EH40] A --> useA[Inner bottom
Side shell warm zones
Bulkheads] B --> useB[Deck plates
Superstructure] D --> useD[Side shell mid-zones
Sea chest areas] E --> useE[Bow region
Stern region
Ice belt
Polar service] style start fill:#FFD580,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style nss fill:#FFB347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style hss fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style A fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000 style B fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000 style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style E fill:#7CFC00,stroke:#000 style useA fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000 style useB fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000 style useD fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000 style useE fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#000 style AH fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style DH fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style EH fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Microstructural Difference Between A and E ```mermaid flowchart LR chemA[Chemistry Grade A

Coarse ferrite plus pearlite
Elongated MnS] procE --> grainE[Grain size 5 to 10 micrometer
Fine equiaxed ferrite
Globular inclusions] grainA --> mechA[DBTT around 0 deg C
Suitable for tropical waters] grainE --> mechE[DBTT below minus 40 deg C
Suitable for ice and polar] mechA --> appA[Use warm zones] mechE --> appE[Use cold zones and ice belt] style chemA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style chemE fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000 style procA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style procE fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000 style grainA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style grainE fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000 style mechA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style mechE fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000 style appA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style appE fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Charpy Curve and Grade Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD temp[Charpy Test Temperature Axis] -->|minus 40 deg C| critE[Grade E Test Point
Must exceed 27 J] temp -->|minus 20 deg C| critD[Grade D Test Point
Must exceed 27 J] temp -->|0 deg C| critA[Grade A Test Point
Must exceed 27 J] upper[Upper Shelf 200 to 300 J
Ductile fracture] transition[Transition Region
