at temperatures where
Grade A becomes brittle] style temp fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000 style critE fill:#7CFC00,stroke:#000 style critD fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style critA fill:#FFB347,stroke:#000 style upper fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000 style transition fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style lower fill:#F08080,stroke:#000 style shiftE fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style verdict fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each Mermaid chart is intentionally drawn as a **block-level architecture** rather than a photorealistic micrograph. This complies with the KTU 2024 syllabus requirement for **schematic process flow understanding** and ensures the diagram is renderable in any markdown viewer (GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Set B)] > **"Define Normal Strength Steel as per IACS classification. What is the significance of the letter 'E' in Grade E steel?"** (3 marks) **Model Answer:** Normal Strength Steels (NSS) are carbon-manganese structural steels with a **specified minimum yield strength of 235 MPa** used in ship hull construction (1 mark). They are designated by letters **A, B, D, and E** based on the **Charpy V-notch impact test temperature** as per IACS Unified Requirement UR W1 (1 mark). The letter **'E'** signifies the **highest notch-toughness** grade in the NSS family, requiring a minimum impact energy of **27 J at –40 °C**. This makes Grade E suitable for **bow, stern, ice-strengthened, and polar service regions** where the plate must remain ductile at very low temperatures (1 mark). --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper Set A)] > **"Why is Grade A steel not permitted for the bow plating of an ice-class vessel?"** (3 marks) **Model Answer:** 1. Grade A is tested for Charpy impact only at **0 °C**, where it must absorb ≥ 27 J. In ice-class service, the bow plating can experience temperatures as low as **–30 °C to –40 °C** due to sub-zero sea water and ambient air (1 mark). 2. At such low temperatures, the **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** of Grade A steel is exceeded, leading to **cleavage fracture** under impact from ice (1 mark). 3. **Grade E** steel, with a 27 J Charpy energy at –40 °C, is mandatory for ice-strengthened bow plating per **IACS UR I1** (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] > **(a)** Explain the **IACS classification of ship-plate steels**. Discuss the significance of the letter designation (A, B, D, E) and how it relates to mechanical properties, with particular focus on **Grade A** and **Grade E**. (7 marks) > > **(b)** A ship hull plate of **Grade E** steel is to be used in the bow region. The Charpy test on a sample gives: $KV_{max} = 240$ J, $KV_{min} = 10$ J, inflection temperature $T_0 = -30$ °C, width parameter $C = 25$ °C. **Calculate the temperature at which the plate just satisfies the 27 J criterion** and comment on its suitability for ice-class service. (7 marks) #### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** The International Association of Classification Societies (IACS) provides a **unified grading system** for ship-building steels via Unified Requirements UR W1 (steels), UR W2 (higher-strength), and UR S21/S22 (CSR). All NSS are assigned a letter A, B, D, or E. **Significance of the letter (2 marks):** The letter designates the **Charpy V-notch impact test temperature** and **minimum energy**: - **A**: tested at 0 °C, 27 J - **B**: tested at 0 °C, 27 J (fully killed) - **D**: tested at –20 °C, 27 J - **E**: tested at –40 °C, 27 J (normalized or TMCP) All four grades share the **same yield strength (235 MPa)**, the same UTS range (400–520 MPa), and the same minimum elongation (22 %). **Grade A focus (2 marks):** Grade A is the **baseline** normal strength steel. It is typically **as-rolled (AR)**, semi-killed or fully killed, with a permissible C up to 0.21 % and Mn in the 0.60 – 1.60 % range. Micro-alloying is optional. Coarse ferrite-pearlite structure gives acceptable toughness at ambient temperature, making it suitable for **inner-bottom, side-shell (warm latitudes), and bulkheads** in non-critical regions. **Grade E focus (2 marks):** Grade E is the **premium** normal strength steel. It is **fully killed with Al** (≥ 0.020 % acid-soluble Al), with tighter chemistry (C ≤ 0.18 %), **mandatory micro-alloying (Nb, V, Ti or Al alone)**, and lower S/P. It is delivered in **normalized (N)** or **TMCP** condition, yielding a **fine-grained ferrite-pearlite structure** that shifts the DBTT well below –40 °C. Used for **bow, stern, ice belt, hatch coaming, polar service**, and other cold-zone / high-strain-rate applications. --- #### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Recall the derived formula (1 mark):** $$T_{27} = T_0 + C \cdot \tanh^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{54}{KV_{max} - KV_{min}}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the given values (1 mark):** - $KV_{max} - KV_{min} = 240 - 10 = 230$ J - $54 / 230 = 0.2348$ - $1 - 0.2348 = 0.7652$ **Step 3 — Compute the inverse hyperbolic tangent (2 marks):** $$\tanh^{-1}(0.7652) = \dfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + 0.7652}{1 - 0.7652}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1.7652}{0.2348}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\ln(7.518) = \dfrac{1}{2}(2.017) = 1.008$$ **Step 4 — Compute $T_{27}$ (1 mark):** $$T_{27} = -30 + 25 \times 1.008 = -30 + 25.20 = -4.80\ ^\circ\text{C}$$ **Step 5 — Comment on suitability (2 marks):** The plate reaches the 27 J criterion at approximately **–4.8 °C**, which is **far above** the –40 °C test requirement mandated for Grade E. This provides a large **safety margin of about 35 °C** for ice-class service. Hence, this plate is **highly suitable** for bow plating in ice-class vessels operating in sub-zero waters, with significant reserve toughness against accidental overcooling or impact overloads. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "T_27 = –4.8 °C, so the steel will fail in Arctic ice." This is the **opposite** of the correct interpretation! The –40 °C is the **test temperature** at which the steel is **required to pass** the 27 J criterion. The fact that the steel passes at much warmer temperatures means it is **safe** at –40 °C. Lose 2 marks if you mis-state this. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] > **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Charpy V-notch impact test** and the **DBTT (Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature)** concept. How does this concept justify the use of **Grade E** steel for Arctic service instead of Grade A? (7 marks) > > **(b)** A Grade A plate of thickness **30 mm** is used as a hatch coaming on a vessel. **Evaluate whether the plate should be upgraded to Grade E** given that the service environment is the North Sea (winter SST ≈ 4 °C, occasional polar lows to –10 °C). Justify your answer with reference to IACS UR W1 and the relevant Charpy test data. (7 marks) #### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks) **Charpy V-notch test (3 marks):** The Charpy V-notch (CVN) test is a **standardized high-strain-rate impact test** (ASTM E23 / ISO 148-1). A 55 × 10 × 10 mm specimen with a 2 mm deep V-notch (45° included angle, 0.25 mm root radius) is struck by a pendulum hammer of known initial energy (usually 300 J). The **absorbed energy KV (Joules)** is measured as the difference between initial and final pendulum height. Three specimens are usually tested per condition; the **average KV** is reported. **DBTT concept (2 marks):** As test temperature decreases, the failure mode transitions from **ductile (microvoid coalescence, high KV)** to **brittle cleavage (low KV)**. The transition occurs over a range of temperature, and the **Ductile-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)** is conventionally defined as the temperature at which KV equals a specific criterion (e.g., 27 J for IACS, 20 J for API 5L). Fine-grained steel has a **lower DBTT** and a **higher upper-shelf energy** than coarse-grained steel. **Justification for Grade E in Arctic (2 marks):** - Arctic sea temperatures reach **–30 °C to –40 °C**, well below the DBTT of Grade A. - Grade A, tested only at 0 °C, would undergo brittle cleavage under ice impact. - Grade E, with **mandatory fine-grained normalized/TMCP microstructure** and **mandatory 27 J at –40 °C**, has its DBTT well below the Arctic service temperature, ensuring **ductile energy absorption** during ice impact. --- #### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Identify the service temperature extremes (1 mark):** - Normal North Sea SST: +4 °C to +12 °C - Polar-low event: down to **–10 °C** - Plate thickness: 30 mm (within NSS range) **Step 2 — Compare against IACS UR W1 (2 marks):** - Grade A requires 27 J at 0 °C. - Grade D requires 27 J at –20 °C. - Grade E requires 27 J at –40 °C. - For service down to –10 °C, **Grade D** is the *minimum* acceptable by strict rule, but Grade E offers more margin. **Step 3 — Risk assessment for hatch coaming (2 marks):** Hatch coamings are **high-stress concentration zones** (welded corners, cyclic loading from cargo, slamming during pitching). Even at –10 °C, a single polar low combined with a slamming load can cause **strain rates in excess of 10 s⁻¹**, shifting the DBTT upward by 15 – 20 °C. Effective service condition approaches **–25 to –30 °C**. **Step 4 — Recommendation (2 marks):** The plate **should be upgraded to Grade E** because: 1. The cost increment is only **15 – 25 %** versus the safety benefit. 2. The 35 °C safety margin in DBTT accommodates both **polar-low temperature excursions** and **high strain-rate loading** at the hatch coaming. 3. Failure of a hatch coaming at sea is a **catastrophic risk** (cargo loss, water ingress, total loss of vessel). 4. Most classification societies will accept Grade E in place of Grade A without design change (same σ_y). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often give a single-word answer "Yes" or "No" without quantifying the **strain-rate correction to DBTT**. The examiner allocates **1 mark for the strain-rate argument** specifically. If you only mention temperature without strain rate, you lose that mark. Include the phrase "**dynamic loading raises the apparent DBTT by 15–20 °C**" in your answer. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **NSS = σ_y ≤ 235 MPa**; letter codes A, B, D, E describe **impact toughness only**, not strength. - [ ] **A, B** → 0 °C; **D** → –20 °C; **E** → –40 °C. - [ ] Charpy criterion: **KV ≥ 27 J** (longitudinal), **KV ≥ 20 J** (transverse for thick plates). - [ ] **Same yield, different toughness** — A and E are interchangeable for pure strength design. - [ ] Grade E requires **normalizing or TMCP**, **Al-killed**, **mandatory microalloy (Nb/V/Ti)**, and tighter **C ≤ 0.18 %**, **S/P ≤ 0.035 %**. - [ ] **DBTT** is lowered by grain refinement (Hall–Petch: $\sigma_{cleavage} \propto d^{-1/2}$). - [ ] Use Grade E for **bow, stern, ice-belt, hatch coaming, polar service, splash zone**. - [ ] IACS rule: **UR W1 (steels), UR I1 (ice strengthening), UR S21/S22 (CSR hull girder)**. - [ ] Allowable stress for NSS: **σ_allow = σ_y / 1.67 ≈ 141 MPa** (governs over tensile-based allowable). - [ ] Weld E-grade with **heat input ≤ 50 kJ/cm** and **interpass ≤ 230 °C** to preserve HAZ toughness. - [ ] The "ksi" labels (32, 36, 40) refer to the **tensile-strength class index**, NOT the yield in ksi — clarify in every answer to earn the unit-awareness mark. - [ ] Polar-class vessels need **E-grade as a minimum**; PC1–PC7 (Polar Code) require **F-grade** (E + extra notch toughness at –60 °C). - [ ] Always state **strain-rate correction to DBTT** when justifying an upgrade from A → D → E in exam answers.

topics.title: High Tensile Steels (HTS) Grades
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** High Tensile Steels (HTS) Grades

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**High Tensile Steels (HTS)**, also known as **High-Strength Low-Alloy (HSLA) steels** in the marine classification context, are a category of carbon–manganese and micro-alloyed structural steels possessing a **minimum yield strength exceeding 355 MPa** (typically ranging from **355 MPa to 690 MPa**) achieved through controlled rolling, normalizing, quenching-tempering, or **Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing (TMCP)**.

As per **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** unified requirements and **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1** syllabus, HTS for marine applications are graded primarily under:

- **IACS UR S6 / W2** — Hull structural steels
- **ASTM A36 / A572 / A633** — Structural grades
- **API 2H / 2W / 2Y** — Offshore platform steels
- **EN 10025** grades like **S355, S420, S460, S690**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 1, HTS is contextualized as the **primary load-bearing material** for ship hulls, offshore jackets, and marine pressure vessels. Its selection directly determines the **lightweight efficiency**, **corrosion allowance margin**, and **fatigue life** of marine structures.

The defining metallurgical identity of HTS is the combination of:

$$\text{Low Carbon Content (C} \leq 0.20\%\text{)} \;+\; \text{Micro-alloying (Nb, V, Ti, B)} \;+\; \text{Refined Grain Structure (ASTM grain size} \geq 8\text{)}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reinforced Concrete of the Sea"

Imagine building a long suspension bridge across a wide river. You could use **ordinary clay bricks**, but you'd need a wall 2 meters thick to carry the load. Alternatively, you could use **engineered reinforced concrete** — thinner, lighter, but equally strong.

**High Tensile Steel is the "engineered reinforced concrete" of marine engineering.** Just like reinforced concrete uses steel rebars (re-inforcement) embedded in cement to dramatically boost tensile strength, HTS uses:

- **Micro-alloying elements (Nb, V, Ti)** acting like "internal rebars" at the atomic lattice level
- **Controlled rolling/cooling** acting like "vibration during curing" that compacts the grain structure

The result? A steel plate that is **30–40% stronger** than mild steel of the **same thickness**, or equivalently, a plate that can be **20–25% thinner** while carrying the **same load** — saving **tonnes of steel per ship**, which directly translates to **fuel savings, higher payload, and better stability**.

## 1.3 Classification of Marine HTS — The Nomenclature Code

Marine HTS grades follow a **standardized alphanumeric code** defined by IACS and classification societies:

$$\underbrace{\text{X}}_{\text{Quality}} \; \underbrace{\text{Y}}_{\text{Yield}} \; \underbrace{\text{Z}}_{\text{Impact Temp}}$$

Where:

- **X (Quality Letter):** A (general), D (higher), E (extra), F (special/low-temp)
- **Y (Yield Number):** 32 → **315 MPa**, 36 → **355 MPa**, 40 → **390 MPa**
- **Z (Suffix):** Indicates impact testing temperature

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Interpretation:** A plate designated **"EH36"** means: Extra-quality hull steel with a **minimum yield strength of 355 MPa**, tested at **-40°C** for Charpy V-notch impact.

### 1.4 Common Marine HTS Grades — Quick Reference Table

| Grade Family | Yield Strength (MPa) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Impact Test Temp (°C) | Typical Application |
|--------------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **AH32** | 315 | 440–590 | 0 | Hull bottom plating |
| **AH36** | 355 | 490–620 | 0 | Side shell, strength deck |
| **DH36** | 355 | 490–620 | -20 | Ice-strengthened hulls |
| **EH36** | 355 | 490–620 | -40 | Polar vessels, LNG carriers |
| **FH36** | 355 | 490–620 | -60 | Arctic-class ships |
| **AH40** | 390 | 510–660 | 0 | Heavy-loaded bulk carriers |
| **DH40** | 390 | 510–660 | -20 | Naval hulls |
| **EH40** | 390 | 510–660 | -40 | Ice-breakers |
| **FH40** | 390 | 510–660 | -60 | Specialized arctic ops |

---

## 1.5 Distinction Between HTS and Mild Steel

> [!NOTE]
> **Mild Steel vs HTS — The Core Difference**
>
> - **Mild Steel (MS)**: Yield strength ≈ **235 MPa**, used for non-critical panels, secondary structures.
> - **High Tensile Steel (HTS)**: Yield strength ≥ **355 MPa**, used for primary hull girder, sheer strake, and strength deck.
>
> Class societies mandate HTS in regions of **high stress concentration** to reduce plate thickness and weight without compromising structural integrity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Metallurgical Basis of High Strength in HTS

The superior mechanical properties of HTS arise from **four synergistic strengthening mechanisms**:

### 2.1.1 Grain Refinement Strengthening (Hall-Petch Mechanism)

Finer grains impede dislocation motion across grain boundaries. The contribution is governed by the **Hall-Petch equation**:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

Where:

- $\sigma_y$ = yield strength (MPa)
- $\sigma_0$ = lattice friction stress (≈ **50 MPa** for ferrite)
- $k_y$ = Hall-Petch coefficient (≈ **17 MPa·mm$^{1/2}$** for low-carbon steel)
- $d$ = average grain diameter (mm)

> **Engineering Insight:** Reducing grain size from **ASTM 6 (≈ 45 µm)** to **ASTM 10 (≈ 11 µm)** can increase yield strength by approximately **80–100 MPa** — without adding alloying cost. This is why **TMCP (Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing)** is the dominant modern production route.

### 2.1.2 Solid Solution Strengthening

Alloying elements like **Mn, Si, Ni** dissolve in the ferrite lattice and create lattice strain, impeding dislocation motion. The strengthening contribution per wt% is given empirically as:

$$\Delta\sigma_{ss} = \sum_i K_i \cdot X_i$$

Where $K_i$ is the strengthening coefficient and $X_i$ is the wt% of solute $i$.

| Element | $K_i$ (MPa per wt%) | Marine HTS Typical % |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Mn | 37 | 0.6–1.6 |
| Si | 83 | 0.1–0.5 |
| Ni | 33 | 0.0–0.4 |
| Cu | 38 | 0.0–0.35 |

### 2.1.3 Precipitation Strengthening (Micro-alloying)

Niobium (Nb), Vanadium (V), and Titanium (Ti) form **carbides and nitrides** (e.g., NbC, VC, TiN) that precipitate at grain boundaries and within grains, blocking dislocation movement:

$$\Delta\sigma_p = \frac{0.84 \cdot G \cdot b}{L_p}$$

Where:
- $G$ = shear modulus (**≈ 80 GPa** for ferrite)
- $b$ = Burgers vector (**≈ 0.248 nm**)
- $L_p$ = mean inter-particle spacing (m)

> **Real-world note:** **Niobium (Nb)** is the most potent precipitation strengthener, typically added at **0.015–0.05%** in HTS. It also suppresses austenite grain growth during rolling.

### 2.1.4 Transformation Strengthening

Rapid cooling (water quenching) transforms austenite into **acicular ferrite, bainite, or martensite** — microstructures significantly harder than equiaxed ferrite-pearlite of mild steel.

### 2.2 Total Strength Model

The yield strength of HTS can be approximated as the **linear superposition** of all contributions:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \sigma_{ss} + \sigma_p + \sigma_{grain} + \sigma_{transformation}$$

This decomposition (the **"strengthening ledger"**) is a critical KTU high-yield concept — examiners often ask students to identify **which mechanism dominates** for a given grade.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formula table is exam-critical. Memorize the equations, units, and physical interpretation.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Engineering stress at yield (F = load, A₀ = original area) | MPa |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain (elongation) | dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Hall-Petch grain refinement | MPa |
| 4 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_{mild} + \Delta\sigma_{ss} + \Delta\sigma_p + \Delta\sigma_{grain}$ | Total strengthening model | MPa |
| 5 | $K_{IC} = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture toughness (Y = geometry factor, a = crack length) | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ |
| 6 | $CVN_{req} = 27 \; \text{J (typical Class rule)}$ | Charpy V-notch minimum for hull plate | Joules |
| 7 | $\sigma_{fatigue} = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b$ | Basquin's fatigue law | MPa |
| 8 | $CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Carbon Equivalent (IIW formula) | wt% |
| 9 | $P_{cm} = C + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Mn+Cu}{20} + \dfrac{Cr}{20} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{V}{10} + 5B$ | Critical Metal Parameter (Pcm) for weldability | wt% |
| 10 | $t_{req} = \dfrac{F \cdot SF}{\sigma_y \cdot k}$ | Required plate thickness for a given load | mm |
| 11 | $\sigma_{von} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 - \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress | MPa |
| 12 | $W_{saved} = (\rho \cdot A \cdot L) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{t_{HTS}}{t_{MS}}\right)$ | Weight saved by using HTS over MS | tonnes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Carbon Equivalent (CE):** For marine HTS weldability, the **IIW formula (Eq. 8)** is universally applied. A CE ≤ **0.40%** is mandatory for **unrestricted welding without preheating**. Most modern HTS grades achieve **CE ≤ 0.38%** through lean chemistry + micro-alloying.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Marine Service

In a **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** or **ULCV (Ultra Large Container Vessel)**, the **hull girder** is subjected to enormous **still-water bending moments** and **wave-induced hogging/sagging stresses**.

By replacing **AH36** with **EH40** in critical regions:
- Plate thickness reduces by ~**15–20%**
- Hull weight reduces by **~2,000–4,000 tonnes**
- Deadweight capacity (cargo) **increases proportionally**
- Fuel consumption per nautical mile **decreases by 3–6%**

This is why the **classification society rules** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV, Class NK, IRS) prescribe **escalating yield strength requirements** for larger vessels, deeper drafts, and more severe service conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivations and Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1.1 Hall-Petch Derivation — Grain Refinement Strength

**Problem:** A marine HTS plate has ferrite grains of average diameter **d₁ = 20 µm**. After TMCP processing, the grain size reduces to **d₂ = 8 µm**. Calculate the increase in yield strength due to grain refinement.

**Given:**
- $\sigma_0 = 50 \; \text{MPa}$
- $k_y = 17 \; \text{MPa} \cdot \text{mm}^{1/2}$
- $d_1 = 20 \; \mu\text{m} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mm}$
- $d_2 = 8 \; \mu\text{m} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mm}$

**Step 1 — Initial yield strength (σ_y1):**

$$\sigma_{y1} = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d_1^{-1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y1} = 50 + 17 \cdot (20 \times 10^{-3})^{-1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y1} = 50 + 17 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{0.020}\right)^{1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y1} = 50 + 17 \cdot (50)^{1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y1} = 50 + 17 \cdot 7.0711$$

$$\sigma_{y1} = 50 + 120.21 = 170.21 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Final yield strength (σ_y2) after TMCP:**

$$\sigma_{y2} = 50 + 17 \cdot (8 \times 10^{-3})^{-1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y2} = 50 + 17 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{0.008}\right)^{1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y2} = 50 + 17 \cdot (125)^{1/2}$$

$$\sigma_{y2} = 50 + 17 \cdot 11.1803$$

$$\sigma_{y2} = 50 + 190.07 = 240.07 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Strength increment:**

$$\Delta\sigma_y = \sigma_{y2} - \sigma_{y1} = 240.07 - 170.21$$

$$\boxed{\Delta\sigma_y \approx 69.86 \; \text{MPa}}$$

**Interpretation:** A grain refinement from **20 µm to 8 µm** (a reduction of 60% in diameter) yields approximately **70 MPa increase** in yield strength — purely through microstructural control, with **no additional alloying cost**. This is the **economic and engineering genius** of TMCP.

---

### 3.1.2 Carbon Equivalent (CE) Calculation — Weldability Assessment

**Problem:** A marine **EH36** steel plate has the following ladle analysis (wt%):

$$\text{C} = 0.16, \; \text{Mn} = 1.45, \; \text{Si} = 0.35, \; \text{Cr} = 0.08, \; \text{Mo} = 0.02, \; \text{V} = 0.005, \; \text{Ni} = 0.15, \; \text{Cu} = 0.12$$

Determine the **IIW Carbon Equivalent** and assess if preheating is required.

**Step 1 — Substitute into IIW formula:**

$$CE_{IIW} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each term:**

$$C = 0.16 \; \text{wt\%}$$

$$\dfrac{Mn}{6} = \dfrac{1.45}{6} = 0.2417 \; \text{wt\%}$$

$$\dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} = \dfrac{0.08 + 0.02 + 0.005}{5} = \dfrac{0.105}{5} = 0.021 \; \text{wt\%}$$

$$\dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15} = \dfrac{0.15 + 0.12}{15} = \dfrac{0.27}{15} = 0.018 \; \text{wt\%}$$

**Step 3 — Sum all contributions:**

$$CE_{IIW} = 0.16 + 0.2417 + 0.021 + 0.018$$

$$\boxed{CE_{IIW} = 0.4407 \; \text{wt\%} \approx 0.44\%}$$

**Step 4 — Weldability Assessment:**

Since $CE_{IIW} = 0.44\% > 0.40\%$, the plate **falls in the precautionary zone**:
- **Mandatory preheating** to **100–150°C** before welding
- **Control of heat input** to **15–35 kJ/cm**
- **Post-weld hydrogen control** essential

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The acceptance limit for unrestricted preheat-free welding varies by society. **ABS** allows CE ≤ 0.40%, while **DNV** mandates **Pcm ≤ 0.20%** for **E-grade** plates in cold service. Always specify which standard applies.

---

### 3.1.3 Plate Thickness Reduction — Quantitative Weight Saving

**Problem:** A bulk carrier deck plate is currently specified as **mild steel (MS) of yield strength 235 MPa**, with a required thickness of **t_MS = 28 mm**. If the plate is replaced with **AH36 (σ_y = 355 MPa)** while maintaining the same load-bearing capacity, compute:
1. New plate thickness
2. Weight saved per square meter
3. Total weight saved for a deck area of **A = 12,000 m²** (assume steel density ρ = 7850 kg/m³)

**Step 1 — New thickness using inverse proportionality:**

$$\dfrac{t_{HTS}}{t_{MS}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}}$$

$$t_{HTS} = t_{MS} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}} = 28 \cdot \dfrac{235}{355}$$

$$t_{HTS} = 28 \cdot 0.6620 = 18.54 \; \text{mm}$$

Rounded up to standard plate thickness: **t_HTS = 19 mm** (or 20 mm for engineering safety margin).

**Step 2 — Weight per m² saved:**

$$\Delta W_{per \, m^2} = \rho \cdot (t_{MS} - t_{HTS}) \cdot 1 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$\Delta W_{per \, m^2} = 7850 \cdot (0.028 - 0.01854)$$

$$\Delta W_{per \, m^2} = 7850 \cdot 0.00946 = 74.26 \; \text{kg/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total weight saved:**

$$\Delta W_{total} = 74.26 \times 12{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\Delta W_{total} \approx 891{,}120 \; \text{kg} \approx 891 \; \text{tonnes}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This **891-tonne reduction** translates to **891 tonnes of additional cargo capacity** — which over the 25-year operational life of a bulk carrier can generate **multi-million dollar revenue gains** for the shipowner.

---

### 3.1.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of HTS Grades

| Parameter | Mild Steel (Grade A) | AH36 | DH36 | EH36 | FH36 | EH40 |
|-----------|----------------------|------|------|------|------|------|
| **Yield Strength (MPa)** | 235 | 355 | 355 | 355 | 355 | 390 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 400–520 | 490–620 | 490–620 | 490–620 | 490–620 | 510–660 |
| **Elongation (min %)** | 22 | 21 | 21 | 21 | 21 | 20 |
| **Charpy Test Temp (°C)** | +20 | 0 | -20 | -40 | -60 | -40 |
| **Charpy Energy (min J)** | 27 | 34 | 34 | 34 | 34 | 39 |
| **Max C (wt%)** | 0.21 | 0.18 | 0.18 | 0.18 | 0.16 | 0.16 |
| **CE$_{IIW}$ (max)** | 0.40 | 0.38 | 0.38 | 0.38 | 0.38 | 0.40 |
| **Typical Delivery Condition** | HR | TMCP/N | TMCP/N | TMCP/Q&T | Q&T | Q&T/TMCP |
| **Service Environment** | Temperate hull | General hull | Cold seas | Polar | Arctic | Ice-strengthened |
| **Relative Cost Index** | 1.0× | 1.15× | 1.20× | 1.30× | 1.45× | 1.40× |

> **Legend:** HR = Hot Rolled, TMCP = Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing, N = Normalized, Q&T = Quenched & Tempered.

---

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — HTS Grade Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine Structure Requirement] --> B{Service Temperature?}
    B -->|T >= 0 °C| C{Load Intensity?}
    B -->|0 > T >= -20 °C| D{Load Intensity?}
    B -->|-20 > T >= -40 °C| E{Load Intensity?}
    B -->|T < -40 °C| F{Load Intensity?}

    C -->|Moderate| G[Grade A Mild Steel]
    C -->|High| H[AH32 / AH36]

    D -->|Moderate| I[DH32 / DH36]
    D -->|High| J[AH36 TMCP]

    E -->|Moderate| K[EH32 / EH36]
    E -->|High| L[EH36 Q&T / EH40]

    F -->|Moderate| M[FH32 / FH36]
    F -->|High| N[FH36 / FH40 Q&T]

    H --> O{CE Check}
    J --> O
    L --> O
    N --> O

    O -->|CE <= 0.38| P[Direct Welding]
    O -->|CE > 0.38| Q[Preheat Required 100-150 °C]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Manufacturing & Microstructure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Materials: Fe + C + Mn + Microalloy Nb V Ti] --> B[Steelmaking: BOF or EAF]
    B --> C[Continuous Casting: Slabs]
    C --> D{Reheating Furnace 1200 °C}
    D --> E[Controlled Rolling: Roughing + Finishing]
    E --> F[Accelerated Cooling 5-15 °C/sec]
    F --> G{Microstructure}

    G -->|Normalized| H[Equiaxed Ferrite + Pearlite]
    G -->|TMCP| I[Fine Ferrite + Bainite]
    G -->|Quenched Tempered| J[Tempered Martensite Bainite]

    H --> K[Yield ~235-315 MPa]
    I --> L[Yield ~355-420 MPa]
    J --> M[Yield ~390-690 MPa]

    K --> N[Plate: Grade A or B]
    L --> O[Plate: AH36 DH36 EH36]
    M --> P[Plate: EH40 FH40 or High Strength 690]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Strengthening Mechanism Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Total Yield Strength sigma_y] --> B[sigma_0: Lattice Friction 50 MPa]
    A --> C[sigma_ss: Solid Solution 30-80 MPa]
    A --> D[sigma_ppt: Precipitation 50-150 MPa]
    A --> E[sigma_gr: Grain Refinement 50-120 MPa]
    A --> F[sigma_tr: Transformation 100-300 MPa]

    C --> C1[Mn Si Ni Cu in ferrite]
    D --> D1[NbC VC TiN precipitates]
    E --> E1[Hall-Petch d to power of -1/2]
    F --> F1[Acicular Ferrite Martensite Bainite]
```

## 4.4 Hull Plate Location Map — Where HTS is Used

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ShipHull[Marine Vessel Cross Section]
        A[Sheer Strake AH36 EH36] --> B[Strength Deck EH36 EH40]
        B --> C[Side Shell DH36 EH36]
        C --> D[Bottom Shell AH36 DH36]
        D --> E[Bilge Strake EH36 Q and T]
        E --> F[Keel Plate EH36 FH36]
    end

    G[Stem EH40 Arctic] --> A
    H[Stern Frame AH36] --> C
    I[Bulkhead DH36] --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The hull regions highlighted with **EH36 / EH40 / FH36** correspond to areas of **maximum combined stress** (bending + shear + local loads). Class society rules mandate **higher strength + better toughness** in these zones to resist **brittle fracture** — especially at **low service temperatures**.

---
