<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Alloy Designation** is the standardized, alphanumeric coding system used by international engineering bodies (AISI/SAE, UNS, ISO, ASTM) to uniquely identify the chemical composition, mechanical characteristics, and metallurgical family of a metallic alloy. It is the universal "engineering language" that allows a metallurgist in Kochi to specify the exact same material as an engineer in Hamburg without ambiguity.

For marine engineering applications, alloy designation is the **first line of defense** against material failure because the code directly reveals whether the alloy is suitable for chloride-laden seawater service, sour (H₂S) environments, or cryogenic LNG cargo systems.

## 1.2 Why Standardization? — The Real-World Analogy

Imagine you walk into a pharmacy in a foreign country and ask for "fever medicine." The pharmacist looks confused — do you want Paracetamol, Ibuprofen, or Aspirin? Standardized naming eliminates this ambiguity.

In the same way, before designation systems existed (pre-1920s), a steel mill in Sheffield would call their 0.4% C, 1% Cr steel **"Imperial Grade B"**, while a mill in Pittsburgh called the same composition **"Diamond Special"**. This caused catastrophic failures, including several ship sinkings during WWI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Note (KTU Hot Topic):** The Titanic's brittleness and rivet failures (1912) were partly due to the absence of a universal alloy designation system. Post-Titanic, AISI formalized the 4-digit steel system in 1929.

## 1.3 The Five Major Designation Bodies Relevant to Marine Engineering

| Body | Scope | Typical Use in Ships |
|---|---|---|
| **AISI / SAE** | Carbon, Alloy, Stainless Steels | Hull plate, shaft, propeller |
| **UNS (Unified Numbering System)** | All metals | Cross-referencing globally |
| **AA (Aluminum Association)** | Wrought & Cast Al alloys | Superstructures, LNG tanks |
| **CDA (Copper Development Assoc.)** | Cu, Brass, Bronze | Sea-water piping, condensers |
| **ISO / EN** | European harmonized | Class society approvals |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 AISI/SAE Carbon & Alloy Steel Designation (4-Digit System)

This is the **most frequently examined** designation scheme for marine steels.

### Decoding Logic
$$\underbrace{X}_{\text{Family}}\,\underbrace{X}_{\text{Modifier}}\,\underbrace{XX}_{\text{Carbon Content}}$$

- **First digit (or two digits):** Identifies the alloy family / principal alloying element.
- **Last two digits:** Nominal carbon content in **hundredths of a percent (0.01 %)**.
- **Middle digit(s):** Minor variations or modifier.

### The "Family Decoder" Table

| Series | Family | Marine Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **10xx** | Plain Carbon (Mn ≤ 1.0 %) | A36 hull plate (structural) |
| **11xx** | Plain Carbon (resulfurized) | Free-cutting bolts |
| **13xx** | Mn (~1.75 %) | Higher-strength shafts |
| **23xx** | Ni (3.5 %) | Cryogenic LNG tanks |
| **25xx** | Ni (5.0 %) | Toughness-critical parts |
| **31xx** | Ni (1.25 %) + Cr (0.65 %) | Gears, pinions |
| **40xx** | Mo (0.20–0.30 %) | High-temp boiler tubes |
| **41xx** | Cr (0.5–1.0 %) + Mo (0.2 %) | Marine crankshafts |
| **43xx** | Ni-Cr-Mo | Heavy-duty forgings |
| **46xx** | Ni (1.8 %) + Mo (0.25 %) | Large marine shafts |
| **51xx** | Cr (0.8–1.0 %) | Bearings (52100) |
| **52xx** | Cr (1.45 %) | High-hardness wear parts |
| **61xx** | Cr-V (spring steel) | Valve springs |
| **86xx** | Ni-Cr-Mo | Armor, high-stress bolts |
| **92xx** | Si-Mn (spring) | Marine springs |

## 2.2 Stainless Steel Designation — The "Marine Trifecta"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For ocean-going vessels, only **austenitic** and **duplex** stainless steels are typically allowed by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) for seawater service.

### Austenitic (300 Series) — Cr-Ni Non-Magnetic

| Designation | Composition | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|
| **302** | 18 % Cr, 8 % Ni | General hardware |
| **304** | 18 Cr / 8 Ni (18/8 SS) | Interior fittings, food grade |
| **304L** | Low C (≤0.03 %) | Welded tanks — avoids sensitization |
| **316** | 16 Cr / 10 Ni / 2 Mo | **Seawater pumps, propellers** |
| **316L** | Low C version of 316 | **Welded marine structures** |
| **321** | Ti-stabilized | Exhaust manifolds (high T) |
| **347** | Nb-stabilized | Chemical tanker piping |

### Ferritic / Martensitic (400 Series) — Cr only, Magnetic

- **430:** Ferritic, decorative; **NOT** for seawater.
- **410:** Martensitic, turbine blades, cutlery.
- **17-4 PH:** Precipitation-hardened, propeller shafts (UNS S17400).

### Duplex (UNS S3xxxx) — High Strength + Cl⁻ Resistance
- **2205 (S32205):** 22 % Cr, 5 % Ni, 3 % Mo — standard marine duplex.
- **2507 (S32750):** Super-duplex for hot brine.

## 2.3 Aluminum Alloy Designation (AA System)

$$\underbrace{X}_{\text{Major Alloy}}\,\underbrace{X}_{\text{Variant}}\,\underbrace{XX}_{\text{Alloy/Impurity ID}}$$

| Series | Major Alloying Element | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|
| **1xxx** | ≥ 99 % pure Al | Chemical tank linings |
| **2xxx** | Cu | Aircraft (NOT seawater) |
| **3xxx** | Mn | Hull plates (5083 is 5xxx, not 3xxx) |
| **5xxx** | Mg | **Marine hulls, decks** (5083, 5086, 5454) |
| **6xxx** | Mg + Si | Superstructures (6061, 6082) |
| **7xxx** | Zn | High-strength military boats |

### Temper Designations (Mandatory Suffix)

| Code | Meaning | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|
| **O** | Annealed (softest) | Forming |
| **H1x** | Strain-hardened | Sheet panels |
| **H2x** | Strain-hardened + partially annealed | Hull plates |
| **H3x** | Strain-hardened + stabilized | High-T service |
| **T4** | Solution heat-treated + naturally aged | — |
| **T6** | Solution + artificially aged | **6061-T6** (most common structural Al) |
| **T7** | Solution + over-aged | 7075-T7 |

## 2.4 Copper & Copper Alloy Designation (UNS C-System)

This is the **most heavily tested** area for marine engineers because copper alloys dominate **seawater piping, heat exchangers, and condensers**.

| UNS Series | Family | Marine Application |
|---|---|---|
| **C101–C159** | Pure Coppers (≥ 99 %) | Electrical busbars, cathodes |
| **C210–C280** | Brasses (Cu-Zn) — Red/Yellow | Plumbing |
| **C260** | Cartridge Brass (70/30) | Decorative, ammunition |
| **C360** | Free-Machining Brass | Bolts, fittings |
| **C443–C445** | Admiralty Brass | Heat exchangers |
| **C464–C467** | Naval Brass (Cu-Zn-Sn) | Marine hardware |
| **C510–C524** | Phosphor Bronze (Cu-Sn-P) | Bearings, gears |
| **C608–C613** | Aluminum Bronze | **Pump impellers, propellers** |
| **C687** | Aluminum Brass | **Condenser tubes** |
| **C706** | Cu-Ni 90/10 | **Seawater piping, hull cladding** |
| **C715** | Cu-Ni 70/30 | **High-velocity seawater, fire mains** |
| **C722** | Cu-Ni with Cr | Specialized naval |

## 2.5 Cast Iron Designation (ASTM)

| Class | Tensile Strength (ksi) | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|
| **20** | 20 ksi min | Drainage, low-stress |
| **30** | 30 ksi min | Pump bodies |
| **40** | 40 ksi min | Engine blocks |
| **60** | 60 ksi min | High-stress gear housings |

> Special cast irons:
> - **Ni-Resist (ASTM A436):** Ni-alloyed austenitic CI — pump casings for seawater.
> - **High-Si Iron (Durichlor):** 14.5 % Si — sulfuric acid service.

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Decoder Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 14-mark numerical problems, you will be asked to **decode** an alloy. Memorize these three formulas:

$$
\text{Carbon Content (wt \%)} = \frac{\text{Last 2 digits of AISI code}}{100}
$$

$$
\text{Carbon Range (e.g., 1040)} = 0.37\% \text{ to } 0.44\%
$$

$$
\text{PREN (Pitting Resistance Eq. Number)} = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N
$$

**Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner's Favourite):**

- **PREN ≥ 40:** Marine-grade stainless (e.g., 2507).
- **PREN 25–40:** Standard seawater service (e.g., 316).
- **PREN < 25:** NOT for seawater (e.g., 304 in stagnant brine).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Decoding Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Decoding AISI 4340 Steel

> **Problem:** Identify the family, principal alloying elements, and carbon content of **AISI 4340**. State its typical marine application.

### Step 1 — Isolate the digits

$$
\text{Code} = \underbrace{4}_{\text{D1}}\,\underbrace{3}_{\text{D2}}\,\underbrace{4}_{\text{D3}}\,\underbrace{0}_{\text{D4}}
$$

### Step 2 — Interpret the first two digits (Family)

From the **Family Decoder Table** (Section 2.1):

$$
43\text{xx} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Ni-Cr-Mo alloy steel family}
$$

- Nominal Ni = 1.8 %
- Nominal Cr = 0.8 %
- Nominal Mo = 0.25 %

### Step 3 — Compute carbon content

$$
\%C = \frac{40}{100} = 0.40\,\%\,\text{(nominal)}
$$

Actual specification range per AISI: **0.38 % C to 0.43 % C**.

### Step 4 — Cross-reference mechanical properties

AISI 4340 after Q&T (Quench & Temper) at 200 °C:

$$
\text{Ultimate Tensile Strength} \approx 1250\,\text{MPa}
$$

$$
\text{Yield Strength} \approx 1100\,\text{MPa}
$$

$$
\text{Charpy Impact at } {-40\,°C} \approx 35\,\text{J}
$$

### Step 5 — Marine application

> **Used for:** Heavy-duty marine crankshafts, propeller shaft couplings, helicopter-deck landing gears, submarine pressure hull fittings.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Decoding AA 5083-H116

> **Problem:** A marine architect specifies **5083-H116** aluminum for a patrol-boat hull. Decode the alloy and temper.

### Step 1 — Split the designation

$$
\underbrace{5}_{\text{Series}}\,\underbrace{0}_{\text{Variant}}\,\underbrace{83}_{\text{ID}} - \underbrace{H1}_{\text{Strain Hardened}}\,\underbrace{16}_{\text{Specific Temper}}
$$

### Step 2 — Series identification

$$
5\text{xxx} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Al-Mg alloy (Mg is principal alloying element)}
$$

5083 nominal composition:

$$
\%Mg = 4.0\text{–}4.9 \qquad \%Mn = 0.4\text{–}1.0 \qquad \%Cr = 0.05\text{–}0.25
$$

### Step 3 — Temper decoding

- **H1** = Strain-hardened only (no thermal treatment).
- **H16** = 3/4 hard temper (UTS ≈ 305 MPa).

> **H116** specifically denotes strain-hardened with **intergranular corrosion resistance guaranteed** — this is the marine-grade temper requirement per ASTM B928.

### Step 4 — Why 5083-H116 over 6061-T6 for hulls?

$$
\text{PREN}_{\text{5083}} \approx 0.5\%Mg + 0.25\%Cr \approx \text{Suitable for marine splash zone}
$$

5083 has superior **seawater corrosion resistance** and is **readily weldable**, whereas 6061-T6 loses strength in the HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: PREN Calculation for Marine Stainless Selection

> **Problem:** Compare 304, 316, and 2205 stainless steels for a seawater pump impeller. Use PREN.

Given nominal compositions:

| Alloy | %Cr | %Mo | %N |
|---|---|---|---|
| 304 | 18.0 | 0.0 | 0.05 |
| 316 | 16.0 | 2.0 | 0.05 |
| 2205 | 22.0 | 3.0 | 0.14 |

### Step 1 — Compute PREN for 304

$$
PREN_{304} = 18 + (3.3 \times 0) + (16 \times 0.05) = 18 + 0 + 0.8 = 18.8
$$

### Step 2 — Compute PREN for 316

$$
PREN_{316} = 16 + (3.3 \times 2) + (16 \times 0.05) = 16 + 6.6 + 0.8 = 23.4
$$

### Step 3 — Compute PREN for 2205

$$
PREN_{2205} = 22 + (3.3 \times 3) + (16 \times 0.14) = 22 + 9.9 + 2.24 = 34.14
$$

### Step 4 — Decision matrix

$$
PREN_{2205} \approx 34 \;>\; PREN_{316} \approx 23 \;>\; PREN_{304} \approx 19
$$

For a high-speed seawater pump impeller (> 8 m/s flow), **2205 duplex** is mandatory because:

- pitting resistance is highest,
- chloride-induced SCC (Stress Corrosion Cracking) is suppressed (duplex structure has no continuous austenite path).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Decoding UNS C70600 (90/10 Cupronickel)

> **Problem:** Specify the chemistry and a marine use case for **UNS C70600**.

### Step 1 — Read the family code

$$
\text{UNS C}70600 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Copper Alloy family, series 7}
$$

### Step 2 — Series identification

$$
\text{C7xxxx} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Cu-Ni alloys (Cupronickels)}
$$

### Step 3 — Decode "06"

For Cu-Ni alloys, the last two digits represent **nominal nickel percentage × 100**:

$$
\%Ni_{\text{nominal}} = \frac{06}{100} = 0.06 \;\text{?}
$$

Wait — correct interpretation: for the C700 series, the middle digits **06, 07, 10, 30** denote standard alloy variants:

- C70600 = **90 % Cu / 10 % Ni + 1.5 % Fe + 1 % Mn**.
- C71500 = **70 % Cu / 30 % Ni**.

### Step 4 — Marine use

- Hull seawater piping on naval frigates.
- Fire-fighting seawater mains (Class society preferred alloy).
- Tube sheets of heat exchangers, desalination evaporators.

> **Iron addition (1–2 %)** is critical — it improves impingement resistance against high-velocity seawater flow at the inlet ends.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Hierarchical Alloy Designation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Alloy Designation Master] --> B1[AISI / SAE 4-Digit System]
    A0 --> B2[UNS Unified System]
    A0 --> B3[AA Aluminum System]
    A0 --> B4[CDA Copper System]

    B1 --> C1A[Plain Carbon 10xx]
    B1 --> C1B[Alloy Steels 13xx to 92xx]
    B1 --> C1C[Stainless 2xx 3xx 4xx]

    C1C --> D1A[Austenitic 300 Series]
    C1C --> D1B[Ferritic 400 Series]
    C1C --> D1C[Martensitic 400 Series]
    C1C --> D1D[Duplex UNS S32 S33]

    B2 --> C2A[S Series Steels]
    B2 --> C2B[C Series Coppers]
    B2 --> C2C[N Series Nickel Alloys]
    B2 --> C2D[A Series Aluminum]
    B2 --> C2E[T Series Titanium]

    B3 --> C3A[1xxx Pure Al]
    B3 --> C3B[2xxx Al Cu]
    B3 --> C3C[3xxx Al Mn]
    B3 --> C3D[5xxx Al Mg Marine]
    B3 --> C3E[6xxx Al Mg Si Structural]
    B3 --> C3F[7xxx Al Zn Aerospace]

    B4 --> C4A[C1xx Pure Copper]
    B4 --> C4B[C2xx C3xx Brasses]
    B4 --> C4C[C4xx Tin Bronzes]
    B4 --> C4D[C6xx Aluminum Bronze]
    B4 --> C4E[C7xx Cupronickel Marine]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Decision Tree: Selecting a Marine Alloy for Seawater Service

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Service Environment} --> Q2[Seawater Immersion]
    Q1 --> Q3[Atmospheric Marine]
    Q1 --> Q4[Sour H2S]

    Q2 --> R1{Flow Velocity m per s}
    R1 --> S1[Less than 3]
    R1 --> S2[3 to 8]
    R1 --> S3[More than 8]

    S1 --> T1[Cu Ni 90 10 C706]
    S1 --> T2[Bronze C464]

    S2 --> T3[Cu Ni 70 30 C715]
    S2 --> T4[Duplex 2205]

    S3 --> T5[Super Duplex 2507]
    S3 --> T6[Titanium Grade 2]

    Q3 --> U1[Al 5083 H116]
    Q3 --> U2[Al Bronze C614]

    Q4 --> V1[AISI 410 SS]
    Q4 --> V2[Monel 400 N04400]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Block: AISI Steel Code Decoder Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Input 4 Digit Code] --> P1[Extract Digits]
    P1 --> P2[Map First 2 Digits to Family]
    P1 --> P3[Divide Last 2 Digits by 100]
    P2 --> P4[Determine Alloying Elements]
    P3 --> P5[Compute Carbon Pct]
    P4 --> P6[Cross Ref Mechanical Properties]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> Out[Final Material Report]

    Out --> Out1[Family Name]
    Out --> Out2[Chemistry]
    Out --> Out3[UTS Yield Elongation]
    Out --> Out4[Marine Application]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**What is meant by the designation AISI 1020? List its typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**
AISI 1020 denotes a plain-carbon steel from the **10xx series** (no significant alloying additions beyond Mn ≤ 1.0 %). The last two digits **20** indicate a nominal carbon content of:

$$
\%C = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20\,\% \quad (\text{actual range } 0.18 \text{ to } 0.23\,\%)
$$

It is a low-carbon, weldable, cold-formable "mild steel." **Marine applications:** structural angles, low-pressure piping, shipyard consumable stock, and non-critical hull brackets.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between AISI 304 and AISI 316 stainless steels from the perspective of marine corrosion.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | AISI 304 | AISI 316 |
|---|---|---|
| % Cr | 18 | 16 |
| % Ni | 8 | 10 |
| % Mo | **0** | **2** |
| PREN | ≈ 19 | ≈ 23 |
| Cl⁻ SCC resistance | Poor in stagnant brine | Good |
| Seawater suitability | Splash-zone only | Continuous immersion allowed |
| Cost | Lower | ~25 % higher |

The presence of **2 % Mo** in 316 forms a stable molybdate passive film that resists pitting by chloride ions — the primary corrosion mechanism in marine environments.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern:** Answer **one** of the two alternatives. Each contains sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — AISI Steel Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With neat classification, explain the AISI/SAE 4-digit designation system for plain-carbon and alloy steels. Decode the alloy **AISI 4140**, listing its family, chemistry, and a marine application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine propeller-shaft coupling is to be manufactured. Compare AISI 1040, 4140, and 4340 steels in terms of carbon content, hardenability, and suitability. Justify your selection. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A

**(a) AISI 4-Digit Decoder:**
- **Family code 41:** Cr-Mo alloy steel (≈ 1 % Cr, 0.2 % Mo).
- **Carbon code 40:** %C = 0.40 % (range 0.38–0.43 %).
- **Marine use:** High-strength marine couplings, drill collars (offshore), and connecting rods.
- Composition: C 0.40 %, Cr 1.0 %, Mo 0.2 %, Mn 0.8 %.
- After Q&T at 400 °C, UTS ≈ 1100 MPa, Yield ≈ 950 MPa.

**(b) Comparative Selection:**

| Property | 1040 | 4140 | 4340 |
|---|---|---|---|
| % C | 0.40 | 0.40 | 0.40 |
| Alloying | None | 1 Cr, 0.2 Mo | 1.8 Ni, 0.8 Cr, 0.25 Mo |
| Hardenability | Low (water quench only) | Medium | High (oil quench, thick sections) |
| Toughness | Moderate | Good | **Excellent** |
| Cost | ₹ | ₹₹ | ₹₹₹ |

For a **large-diameter marine propeller-shaft coupling** (> 200 mm cross-section):
- 1040 cannot be through-hardened → rejected.
- 4140 may have a soft core.
- **4340 is selected** because the Ni addition ensures through-thickness hardness and impact toughness even at –20 °C Arctic service.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Marine Non-Ferrous Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the UNS designation system for copper alloys used in marine engineering. With proper chemistry, discuss the suitability of **Cu-Ni 90/10 (C70600)** and **Cu-Ni 70/30 (C71500)** for seawater piping and condensers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patrol boat hull is to be fabricated from an aluminum alloy. Justify the selection of **AA 5083-H116** over **AA 6061-T6** and **AA 7075-T6**, with reference to designation decoding, weldability, and corrosion resistance. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution B

**(a) UNS C-System for Marine Copper Alloys:**

The UNS C-series runs from **C10000 (pure copper) to C80000 (special alloys)**. For seawater service:

- **C70600 — 90/10 Cupronickel:**
  - Nominal: 88.7 % Cu, 10 % Ni, 1.3 % Fe, 0.9 % Mn.
  - PREN ≈ 24 (due to Ni + Fe synergy).
  - **Use:** Hull seawater piping, fire mains, heat-exchanger tubes up to 3 m/s flow.

- **C71500 — 70/30 Cupronickel:**
  - Nominal: 69.5 % Cu, 30 % Ni, 0.5 % Fe, 0.5 % Mn.
  - PREN ≈ 30.
  - **Use:** High-velocity (up to 8 m/s) condenser tubes, desalination brine heaters, naval combat-system coolers.

The **Fe addition (1–2 %)** is the metallurgical secret: iron stabilizes the protective **Cu₂O passive film** against erosion-corrosion at high flow rates.

**(b) Aluminum Alloy Selection for Hull:**

| Property | 5083-H116 | 6061-T6 | 7075-T6 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Series | 5xxx (Al-Mg) | 6xxx (Al-Mg-Si) | 7xxx (Al-Zn) |
| UTS (MPa) | 305 | 310 | 572 |
| Weldability | **Excellent** | Good (HAZ softens) | Poor |
| Seawater corrosion | **Excellent** | Good | **Poor (SCC risk)** |
| H116 temper | Intergranular corrosion tested | N/A | N/A |
| Cost | ₹₹ | ₹ | ₹₹₹ |

**Selection:** **5083-H116** wins on all three counts for a welded patrol-boat hull:
1. **Weldability:** Mg alloys do not form low-melting eutectics; HAZ retains strength.
2. **Corrosion:** No Cu or Zn to drive galvanic / SCC attack.
3. **Temper:** H116 specifically certifies resistance to **exfoliation and intergranular corrosion** — a marine class requirement.

7075-T6 is rejected because Zn + tensile stress + seawater = catastrophic SCC. 6061-T6 is rejected because welding drops the T6 temper (over-aged in HAZ).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Wrong decimal place in carbon content** — writing "4 %" instead of "0.40 %" for AISI 1040. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing UNS S-series with AISI** — e.g., writing "2205 SS" is acceptable but "S32205" must be UNS.
> 3. **Forgetting the temper suffix** — specifying "6061" instead of "6061-T6" loses 1 mark in long answers.
> 4. **No application linkage** — describing only chemistry without a marine use case deducts 30–40 % marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the "L" suffix** — 316 vs 316L is a welded vs non-welded distinction; examiners love this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ AISI system uses **4 digits**; last two = **carbon × 100**.
- ✅ **Stainless 300 series = austenitic** (non-magnetic, seawater-OK only if Mo added).
- ✅ **316 = 18/8 + 2 Mo** — the **marine workhorse**.
- ✅ **Duplex 2205** has higher strength and SCC resistance than 316.
- ✅ **AA 5xxx (Al-Mg)** = marine hull alloy; **5083-H116** is the gold standard.
- ✅ **6061-T6** is structural Al but **welds poorly**.
- ✅ **7075-T6** = high strength but **forbidden in seawater** (SCC).
- ✅ **UNS C706 = 90/10 Cu-Ni**; **C715 = 70/30 Cu-Ni**.
- ✅ **Iron (1–2 %)** in Cu-Ni is critical for **impingement resistance**.
- ✅ **PREN ≥ 40** = super-duplex / super-austenitic = hot brine service.
- ✅ Always pair your designation with a **temper** (T6, H116, O, etc.) where applicable.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark answers: **chemistry + mechanical property + marine application** = full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding Requirements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Welding Requirements – Marine Engineering Context

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Welding Requirements

### Formal Academic Definition

In the marine and offshore domain, **Welding Requirements** refer to the codified set of metallurgical, mechanical, geometric, procedural, and qualification specifications that govern the joining of metallic components used in ship hulls, offshore platforms, submarine pipelines, pressure vessels, and propulsion systems. These requirements ensure that welded joints possess adequate **strength, toughness, fatigue resistance, and corrosion resistance** under the aggressive marine service environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Welding requirements in marine engineering constitute the prescribed rules for base-metal selection, filler-metal compatibility, joint preparation, preheat/interpass temperature control, post-weld heat treatment (PWHT), weld procedure qualification, and non-destructive testing (NDT) — all governed by classification societies such as **ABS, DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, and IRS**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are stitching two large pieces of thick, heavy leather (the steel plates of a ship's hull) using a hot needle (the welding arc) and a special thread (the filler wire). If you just push the hot needle without preparing the edges, or use the wrong thread, the seam will rip when the ship sails through stormy seas. **Welding requirements are essentially the "tailor's rule book"** that tells the welder:
- How to cut the leather edges (joint preparation)
- What thread to use (filler metal selection)
- How hot the needle should be (welding parameters)
- How to test the seam afterwards (NDT)
- What certification the tailor must hold (welder qualification)

Without this rule book, a ship would be unsafe — just like a poorly stitched leather bag would fall apart.

### Key Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Minimum yield strength of marine mild steel (Grade A):** **235 MPa**
- **Standard preheat temperature for low-alloy marine steels:** **75 °C – 150 °C**
- **Maximum allowable hardness in HAZ (per DNV):** **350 HV10**
- **Charpy V-notch impact test temperature for marine structures:** **0 °C (tropical) to –40 °C (Arctic)**
- **Standard interpass temperature limit:** **≤ 250 °C** for C-Mn steels

> [!NOTE]
> Welding requirements are NOT just about joining metal — they are an **integrated quality assurance system** that combines material science, mechanical engineering, and metallurgical process control to guarantee structural integrity throughout the vessel's service life (typically **25–30 years**).

---

## Why Welding is Critical in Marine Engineering

Marine structures operate under extreme conditions:
1. **Dynamic wave loading** → cyclic fatigue
2. **Saline seawater corrosion** → requires corrosion-resistant weld metal
3. **Low service temperatures** (especially in Arctic/Polar shipping) → demands impact toughness
4. **High hydrostatic pressure** (submarines, deep-sea pipelines) → requires defect-free welds
5. **Fire resistance** (engine rooms, oil tankers) → requires high-temperature performance

Therefore, **every weld must be engineered, not just executed**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Welding Requirements

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups welding requirements into **five interdependent pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Base Metal Requirements
- **Material grade specification** (e.g., ASTM A36, A131 Grade A–EH, API 5L X65)
- **Chemical composition limits** (C, Mn, S, P, Si)
- **Mechanical property minimums** (yield strength, tensile strength, elongation)
- **Through-thickness ductility** (Z-direction, to avoid lamellar tearing)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** governs weldability. Higher CE → greater risk of hydrogen cracking.

### Pillar 2: Filler Metal Requirements
- **Compatibility with base metal** (e.g., E7018 for C-Mn steel, E309L for SS-to-CS dissimilar joints)
- **Mechanical property matching** (over-matching for strength-critical joints)
- **Low-hydrogen classification** (e.g., H4, H8, H16 — H₂ content in ml/100g)
- **AWS / ISO classification compliance**

### Pillar 3: Joint Design & Preparation Requirements
- **Weld joint type**: butt, fillet, T-joint, lap, corner
- **Groove angle**: typically **60° ± 5°** for V-groove
- **Root gap**: **2–4 mm** for SMAW/GTAW
- **Root face (land)**: **1–2 mm**
- **Backing/root pass** requirements for full penetration

### Pillar 4: Process & Procedural Requirements
- **Welding process selection**: SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, FCAW, SAW, PAW
- **Preheat temperature (Tp)**: based on CE and thickness
- **Interpass temperature (Ti)**: maximum allowable
- **Heat input (HI)**: controlled to avoid coarse grain HAZ
- **Post-Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT)**: stress relief at **595–705 °C**

### Pillar 5: Qualification & Testing Requirements
- **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** — written procedure
- **Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)** — tested procedure
- **Welder Qualification Test (WPQ)** — individual welder performance
- **NDT methods**: VT, RT, UT, MT, PT
- **Mechanical testing**: tensile, bend, Charpy V-notch, hardness

## 2.2 Carbon Equivalent Formulas — KTU High-Yield

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Carbon Equivalent is the single most important number for assessing weldability in marine steels.**

### IIW Formula (International Institute of Welding)

$$CE_{IIW} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

### Pcm Formula (Ito-Bessyo) — for low-carbon modern steels

$$P_{cm} = C + \frac{Si}{30} + \frac{Mn + Cu}{20} + \frac{Cr}{20} + \frac{Ni}{60} + \frac{Mo}{15} + \frac{V}{10} + 5B$$

### Heat Input Formula

$$HI = \frac{k \times V \times I}{v}$$

Where:
- $V$ = arc voltage (Volts)
- $I$ = welding current (Amperes)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/min)
- $k$ = thermal efficiency factor (0.6 for SMAW, 0.8 for GMAW/SAW)
- $HI$ is in **kJ/mm**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Condition | Units | Application |
|-----------|---------------------|-------|-------------|
| Carbon Equivalent (IIW) | $CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | % | Weldability assessment |
| Pcm (low-C steels) | $P_{cm} = C + \frac{Si}{30} + \frac{Mn+Cu}{20} + \frac{Cr}{20} + \frac{Ni}{60} + \frac{Mo}{15} + \frac{V}{10} + 5B$ | % | Modern steel HAZ cracking |
| Heat Input | $HI = \frac{k \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | kJ/mm | Controls HAZ grain size |
| Preheat (CE-based) | $T_p = 750 \cdot CE - 150$ (approx.) | °C | Avoids HAZ hydrogen cracking |
| Cooling Time t₈/₅ | Empirical (Rykalin 2D/3D) | s | Determines HAZ microstructure |
| Weld Metal Tensile | $\sigma_{u,w} \geq \sigma_{u,base}$ (or over-match) | MPa | Strength requirement |
| HAZ Hardness Limit | $HV_{10} \leq 350$ | HV10 | Per DNV / ABS rules |
| Charpy Energy | $KV \geq 27\,J$ (typical) at service T | J | Toughness requirement |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use the vertical pipe `|` inside any markdown table cell.** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` when expressing absolute values or such delimiters in LaTeX inside tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- The **IIW CE** is used on the shop floor by welding engineers to decide **whether preheating is mandatory** before striking an arc on a marine plate.
- **Heat input control** directly determines whether the HAZ becomes coarse-grained and brittle, or fine-grained and tough — a life-or-death matter in **Arctic tanker operations**.
- **Pcm** is the modern industry-preferred formula for **high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steels** used in **submarine pressure hulls**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input Expression

**Starting Point:** Welding heat input is defined as the **energy supplied per unit length of weld**.

### Step 1: Define Instantaneous Power Delivered to the Weld Pool

The electrical power delivered by the arc is:

$$P_{arc} = V \times I$$

Where $V$ is in volts and $I$ is in amperes, giving power in **watts (W)**.

### Step 2: Apply Thermal Efficiency Factor

Not all arc energy is transferred to the workpiece. A fraction $k$ (process-dependent) actually heats the joint:

$$P_{effective} = k \times V \times I$$

Typical values:
- SMAW (Stick): $k = 0.6$
- GMAW (MIG/MAG): $k = 0.8$
- GTAW (TIG): $k = 0.6$
- SAW (Submerged Arc): $k = 1.0$
- PAW (Plasma): $k = 0.6$

### Step 3: Convert to Energy Per Unit Length

If the arc travels a distance $L$ in time $t$, the travel speed is:

$$v = \frac{L}{t}$$

Energy per unit length:

$$HI = \frac{P_{effective}}{v} = \frac{k \times V \times I}{v}$$

### Step 4: Unit Conversion

If $V$ is in V, $I$ in A, and $v$ in mm/min, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \frac{k \times V \times I}{v} \quad \left[\frac{\text{V} \cdot \text{A}}{\text{mm/min}}\right] \\
&= \frac{k \times V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000} \quad \left[\frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}\right] \\
&= \frac{k \times V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right] \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final form used in marine classification rules:**

$$HI = \frac{60 \times k \times V \times I}{1000 \times v} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A marine plate is welded using SAW. Arc voltage = 32 V, current = 600 A, travel speed = 400 mm/min. Calculate the heat input.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= 1.0 \quad (\text{SAW}) \\
V &= 32\,\text{V} \\
I &= 600\,\text{A} \\
v &= 400\,\text{mm/min} \\
\\
HI &= \frac{60 \times 1.0 \times 32 \times 600}{1000 \times 400} \\
&= \frac{1\,152\,000}{400\,000} \\
HI &= 2.88\,\text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A heat input of **2.88 kJ/mm** is within the typical acceptable range of **1.5–3.5 kJ/mm** for C-Mn marine steels per DNV rules.

## 3.3 Derivation: Preheat Temperature from Carbon Equivalent

### Empirical Relation (DNV / AWS D1.1 adapted)

For C-Mn and low-alloy marine steels, preheat temperature is empirically tied to CE:

$$T_p = 750 \times CE - 150 \quad [°C]$$

with a minimum of **75 °C** when CE ≥ 0.40.

### Worked Example

Given base metal composition: C = 0.18%, Mn = 1.20%, Cr = 0.10%, Mo = 0.05%, V = 0.02%, Ni = 0.10%, Cu = 0.15%.

Step 1: Calculate IIW Carbon Equivalent.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CE &= C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15} \\
&= 0.18 + \frac{1.20}{6} + \frac{0.10+0.05+0.02}{5} + \frac{0.10+0.15}{15} \\
&= 0.18 + 0.200 + 0.034 + 0.0167 \\
CE &= 0.4307\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Apply preheat formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_p &= 750 \times 0.4307 - 150 \\
&= 323.0 - 150 \\
T_p &= 173\,°C
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3: Round to nearest standard value.

$$T_p \approx 175\,°C$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since CE > 0.40, mandatory preheating is required. The 175 °C preheat prevents **hydrogen-induced cold cracking** in the HAZ.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When Is Preheat Required?

| Plate Thickness (mm) | CE (%) | Preheat Required? | Minimum Tp (°C) |
|----------------------|--------|-------------------|-----------------|
| < 20 | < 0.40 | No | 20 (ambient) |
| 20–40 | < 0.40 | Optional | 75 |
| Any | 0.40–0.60 | **Yes** | 100–175 |
| Any | > 0.60 | **Yes** | 175–250 |
| Quenched & Tempered | Any | **Yes** | 150–200 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Marine Welding Requirement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine Welding Requirement System] --> B[Base Metal Specification]
    A --> C[Filler Metal Selection]
    A --> D[Joint Design Prep]
    A --> E[Welding Process Control]
    A --> F[Qualification and Testing]

    B --> B1[Chemical Composition]
    B --> B2[Mechanical Properties]
    B --> B3[Carbon Equivalent CE]

    C --> C1[AWS ISO Classification]
    C --> C2[Hydrogen Control H4 H8 H16]
    C --> C3[Strength Match]

    D --> D1[Groove Type V U J]
    D --> D2[Groove Angle 60 deg]
    D --> D3[Root Gap 2 to 4 mm]
    D --> D4[Root Face 1 to 2 mm]

    E --> E1[Preheat Tp]
    E --> E2[Interpass Ti 250 C max]
    E --> E3[Heat Input HI kJ per mm]
    E --> E4[PWHT 595 to 705 C]

    F --> F1[WPS Document]
    F --> F2[PQR Test Record]
    F --> F3[Welder WPQ]
    F --> F4[NDT VT RT UT MT PT]
    F --> F5[Mechanical Test Tensile Bend Charpy]

    B3 --> E1
    E1 --> E4
    E2 --> E4
    E3 --> E4
    F1 --> F4
    F1 --> F5
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: From Plate to Qualified Weld

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Marine Steel Plate Arrival] --> P2[Material Certificate Verification]
    P2 --> P3{CE Calculation}
    P3 -->|CE less than 0.40| P4[No Preheat]
    P3 -->|CE 0.40 to 0.60| P5[Preheat 100 to 175 C]
    P3 -->|CE greater than 0.60| P6[Preheat 175 to 250 C]
    P4 --> P7[Joint Preparation Groove Cut Bevel]
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Welding Per Approved WPS]
    P8 --> P9[Visual Examination VT]
    P9 --> P10[NDT RT or UT or MT]
    P10 --> P11{Meets Acceptance}
    P11 -->|Yes| P12[Release to Service]
    P11 -->|No| P13[Repair or Reject]
    P13 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Weld Joint Geometry (V-Groove Butt Joint)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Plate A] --> G2[Groove Angle 60 deg]
    G2 --> G3[Root Face 1.5 mm]
    G3 --> G4[Root Gap 2 to 3 mm]
    G4 --> G5[Root Pass 1]
    G5 --> G6[Fill Passes 2 to N]
    G6 --> G7[Cap Pass Final]
    G7 --> G8[Plate B]
    G8 --> H1[HAZ Heat Affected Zone]
    H1 --> H2[Parent Metal Unaffected]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the above schematic, the **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** is the metallurgically modified region adjacent to the weld where the parent metal does not melt but experiences a thermal cycle. The HAZ is often the **weakest region** in a marine weld and requires stringent toughness control.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Carbon Equivalent (CE) and state its significance in marine welding.**

**Model Answer:**
Carbon Equivalent is a numerical value obtained by converting the alloying content of a steel into an equivalent percentage of carbon, used to assess **weldability**. The IIW formula is:

$$CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$$

**Significance:**
- CE ≤ 0.40 → good weldability, no preheat usually required.
- CE 0.40–0.60 → moderate weldability, preheat mandatory.
- CE > 0.60 → poor weldability, strict preheat + low-hydrogen electrodes essential.

In marine welding, CE governs **susceptibility to hydrogen-induced cracking** in the HAZ — a critical concern for ship hulls and offshore structures. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods used for marine welds and state what each detects.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Visual Testing (VT)** – Detects surface defects such as undercut, cracks, porosity, and bead irregularities. **(1 mark)**
2. **Radiographic Testing (RT)** – Uses X-rays or gamma rays to detect internal defects such as porosity, slag inclusions, and lack of fusion. **(1 mark)**
3. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** – Uses high-frequency sound waves to detect internal defects, including planar defects like cracks and lack of fusion; preferred for thick marine plates. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **five pillars of welding requirements** as applied to marine construction, with reference to classification society rules (DNV / ABS / Lloyd's).

**(b) [7 marks]** A marine structural joint is welded using GMAW process with the following parameters: V = 28 V, I = 250 A, travel speed = 300 mm/min, thermal efficiency k = 0.8. Calculate the heat input and comment on its suitability for a 25 mm thick ASTM A131 Grade A plate.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

The five pillars governing marine welding requirements are:

1. **Base Metal Requirements:** Marine plates must conform to ASTM A131 (Grades A–EH) or equivalent DNV/ABS grades. Key checks include chemical composition (C ≤ 0.21% for Grade A), tensile strength (400–520 MPa), yield strength (≥ 235 MPa), and through-thickness ductility (Z-direction reduction of area ≥ 25%) to prevent lamellar tearing in T-joints. **(2 marks)**

2. **Filler Metal Requirements:** Filler metals must be selected per AWS A5.1/A5.18 classifications. For C-Mn marine steels, low-hydrogen electrodes (e.g., E7018, H4 grade) are mandated. The weld metal must have tensile strength **equal to or higher than** the base metal, and Charpy impact energy ≥ 27 J at service temperature. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Joint Design & Preparation:** Standard V-groove with 60° ± 5° included angle, root face 1–2 mm, root gap 2–4 mm. Backing strips or ceramic backing may be used for single-sided welds. Surface must be clean, dry, and free from mill scale, oil, or rust within 20 mm of the groove. **(1.5 marks)**

4. **Process & Procedural Control:** WPS must specify preheat temperature, interpass temperature (max 250 °C), heat input limits, and PWHT (if required, 595–705 °C for 1 hour/inch). For thick sections, the t₈/₅ cooling time must be controlled. **(1 mark)**

5. **Qualification & Testing:** WPS must be qualified by a PQR test. Welder must hold valid WPQ certificate. Production welds undergo 100% VT and a defined percentage of NDT (typically 10–20% RT or UT for critical joints). Mechanical tests on procedure qualification coupons include tensile, bend, Charpy, and hardness. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 1: Identify given values.**
- V = 28 V, I = 250 A, v = 300 mm/min, k = 0.8

**Step 2: Apply heat input formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \frac{60 \times k \times V \times I}{1000 \times v} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 0.8 \times 28 \times 250}{1000 \times 300} \\
&= \frac{336\,000}{300\,000} \\
HI &= 1.12\,\text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Comment on suitability.**
For a 25 mm thick ASTM A131 Grade A plate, the typical acceptable heat input range is **1.5–3.5 kJ/mm** per DNV rules. A calculated HI of **1.12 kJ/mm is below the recommended minimum**, which may result in:
- Excessively rapid cooling
- Risk of hydrogen cracking
- Insufficient fusion

**Recommendation:** Increase voltage, current, or reduce travel speed to bring HI into the **1.5–2.0 kJ/mm** range, or use a different process. **(2 marks for commentary)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches, describe the **typical V-groove butt joint preparation** for marine steel plates. List the standard dimensional tolerances per AWS D1.1 / DNV.

**(b) [7 marks]** A steel with composition C = 0.16%, Mn = 1.35%, Cr = 0.25%, Mo = 0.10%, V = 0.03%, Ni = 0.20%, Cu = 0.20% is to be welded for a marine deck plate. Calculate the IIW Carbon Equivalent and the recommended preheat temperature. Justify whether PWHT is required.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Standard V-Groove Butt Joint for Marine Plates (per AWS D1.1 / DNV):**

```
         Plate A         Plate B
            \  60 deg  /
             \        /
              \      /
        _______\____/_______
        1-2 mm   2-4 mm
        (root    (root
         face)    gap)
              \  |  /
               \ | /
                \|/
         ====== weld ======
```

**Standard Dimensional Tolerances (for thickness 10–50 mm):** **(7 marks distributed as below)**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Tolerance |
|-----------|--------|---------------|-----------|
| Groove angle | θ | 60° | ± 5° |
| Root face | f | 1.5 mm | +1.0, –0.0 |
| Root gap | g | 3 mm | +1.0, –0.0 |
| Plate alignment | — | Flush | ≤ 1 mm offset |
| Land/Bevel finish | — | Smooth | Ra ≤ 12.5 μm |

**Description (4 marks):**
- The joint edges are bevelled to form a 60° included angle.
- A small root face (land) of 1–2 mm is left to prevent burn-through.
- A root gap of 2–4 mm is maintained for electrode/arc access.
- The root pass is deposited first (often GTAW for quality), followed by fill and cap passes.
- For thicker plates (> 25 mm), a double-V or U-groove may be used to reduce weld volume.
- Cleaning: The groove and 20 mm adjacent surface must be ground/cleaned to bright metal. **(3 marks for process and preparation steps)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Step 1: List chemical composition.**
C = 0.16%, Mn = 1.35%, Cr = 0.25%, Mo = 0.10%, V = 0.03%, Ni = 0.20%, Cu = 0.20%

**Step 2: Apply IIW Carbon Equivalent formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CE &= C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15} \\
&= 0.16 + \frac{1.35}{6} + \frac{0.25+0.10+0.03}{5} + \frac{0.20+0.20}{15} \\
&= 0.16 + 0.225 + 0.076 + 0.0267 \\
CE &= 0.4877\% \approx 0.49\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate preheat temperature.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_p &= 750 \times CE - 150 \\
&= 750 \times 0.4877 - 150 \\
&= 365.8 - 150 \\
T_p &= 215.8\,°C
\end{aligned}
$$

Recommended preheat: **~215 °C** (round to 200 °C in practice). **(3 marks)**

**Step 4: PWHT justification.**

Since CE > 0.40 and the plate is used in a marine environment subject to cyclic loading:
- **PWHT is recommended** at **595–625 °C** for 1 hour per inch of thickness to:
  - Relieve residual welding stresses
  - Reduce HAZ hardness
  - Improve fatigue life under wave-loading cycles

For a deck plate, where dynamic stresses are significant, **stress-relief PWHT is mandatory per DNV**. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor "60"** in heat input unit conversion — a common 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Mixing up IIW and Pcm formulas** — only one is asked; do not write both unless specifically requested.
> 3. **Skipping the unit "kJ/mm"** in the final answer — always state units.
> 4. **Not commenting on suitability** in numerical problems — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for the engineering interpretation, not just the calculation.
> 5. **Confusing "interpass" with "preheat"** — preheat is applied before striking the arc; interpass is maintained between successive passes.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** is the master index for weldability — IIW for general steels, Pcm for modern low-C steels.
- ✅ **Heat Input (HI)** must be controlled within classification society limits (typically **1.5–3.5 kJ/mm** for C-Mn marine steels).
- ✅ **Preheat is mandatory** when CE > 0.40 or thickness > 20 mm for many grades.
- ✅ **Interpass temperature** must not exceed **250 °C** for C-Mn marine steels.
- ✅ **Five Pillars:** Base metal, Filler metal, Joint design, Process control, Qualification & Testing.
- ✅ **NDT is not optional** — 100% VT is mandatory; 10–20% RT/UT for critical marine joints.
- ✅ **Welder qualification (WPQ)** and **procedure qualification (PQR/WPS)** are legally required under IACS rules.
- ✅ **PWHT** at **595–705 °C** relieves residual stress and is required for thick marine sections.
- ✅ **HAZ hardness limit:** **350 HV10 max** per DNV / ABS.
- ✅ **Joint tolerances** (V-groove): 60° ± 5° angle, 1–2 mm root face, 2–4 mm root gap.
- ✅ **Low-hydrogen electrodes** (E7018, H4 grade) are the marine industry default.
- ✅ **Through-thickness Z-direction properties** matter for T-joints to prevent lamellar tearing.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory aid for KTU exams:**
> *"Welding requirements = **C**arbon equivalent + **H**eat input + **P**reheat + **Q**ualification (WPS/PQR/WPQ) + **T**esting (NDT + Mechanical)."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strength of Aluminium Compared to Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strength of Aluminium Compared to Steel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In marine and offshore structural engineering, the **strength of a material** is characterized primarily by its **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)**, **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{uts}$)**, and **Specific Strength** (also called *strength-to-weight ratio*, $\sigma/\rho$). When comparing aluminium and steel, a simple absolute strength comparison is *misleading* because the two metals have vastly different **densities ($\rho$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Strength of aluminium compared to steel"* refers to the comparative evaluation of mechanical strength properties (yield, tensile, fatigue) and the derived **specific strength (strength-to-weight ratio)** between ferrous (steel) and non-ferrous (aluminium) alloys used in ship hulls, superstructures, deckhouses, and offshore platforms.

### 1.2 Standard Reference Values (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Property | Structural Steel (ASTM A36) | Aluminium Alloy 6061-T6 | Aluminium Alloy 7075-T6 |
|----------|----------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Density ($\rho$)** | **7850 kg/m³** | **2700 kg/m³** | **2810 kg/m³** |
| **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)** | **250 MPa** | **276 MPa** | **503 MPa** |
| **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{uts}$)** | **400–500 MPa** | **310 MPa** | **572 MPa** |
| **Elastic Modulus ($E$)** | **200 GPa** | **69 GPa** | **72 GPa** |
| **Elongation at Failure** | **23 %** | **12–17 %** | **6–11 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Although the *absolute* tensile strength of structural steel is higher than that of pure aluminium, the **specific strength** of aluminium alloys (especially the 7000-series) is **2 to 4 times** that of mild steel, making aluminium the preferred lightweight structural material in marine applications.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Backpacker's Choice:**
> Imagine two hikers carrying poles. Hiker A uses a *steel pole* (very strong, but heavy — 7.85 kg per metre). Hiker B uses an *aluminium alloy pole* (lighter — 2.7 kg per metre, slightly less strong, but still strong enough). When you ask *"Which pole is stronger?"* the steel pole wins. But when you ask *"Which pole gives the most strength per kilogram carried on a long trek?"*, the aluminium pole wins hands down.
>
> A **ship** is exactly this backpacker. It must float (low weight matters!) and still withstand wave loads. So marine architects ask: **"How much strength do I get for every kilogram of hull I build?"** — and aluminium frequently wins that contest.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Marine Engineering

- **Lightweight hulls** → higher payload, lower fuel consumption, higher speed.
- **Corrosion resistance** in seawater (with proper alloying) → lower maintenance.
- **Non-magnetic** → suitable for naval mine countermeasure vessels and research ships.
- **Recyclability** → aligns with IMO green shipping regulations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Engineering Metrics of "Strength"

When KTU examiners ask *"Compare the strength of aluminium and steel"*, they expect you to address **three distinct metrics**, not just one:

1. **Absolute Strength** — yield / tensile strength in MPa.
2. **Specific Strength** — strength divided by density.
3. **Stiffness-to-Weight Ratio** — elastic modulus divided by density.

A material can be **strong** (high $\sigma_{uts}$) without being **structurally efficient** (high $\sigma/\rho$).

### 2.2 Why Aluminium Beats Steel on a Weight Basis (The "Why")

- **Atomic Structure Argument:** Aluminium has an FCC (Face-Centred Cubic) crystal structure, which provides more slip systems than the BCC structure of ferrite in mild steel. This allows aluminium to be **work-hardened and precipitation-hardened** to impressive strength levels (e.g., 7075-T6 reaches 572 MPa).
- **Density Argument:** Aluminium's atomic mass (27 g/mol) is far lower than iron's (56 g/mol), and its FCC packing, combined with a larger atomic radius, gives it roughly **one-third the density of steel**.
- **Alloying Argument:** Zinc, magnesium, copper, and silicon additions create **precipitation-hardenable** aluminium alloys (2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx series) whose strength rivals structural steel.

### 2.3 Where Steel Still Wins (The "How" — Limitations of Aluminium)

- **Stiffness:** Aluminium's elastic modulus (~70 GPa) is only **one-third** that of steel (~200 GPa). For the same load, an aluminium beam deflects **three times more** than a steel beam of identical dimensions. This is why aluminium ship hulls often need **larger cross-sections** or **closely spaced stiffeners**.
- **Fatigue endurance under cyclic wave loading:** Aluminium alloys generally have lower fatigue limits (~40–50% of $\sigma_{uts}$) than steels (~50–60%).
- **Fire resistance:** Aluminium loses strength rapidly above **~200 °C**, whereas steel retains useful strength up to **~550 °C**.
- **Galvanic corrosion:** When aluminium is coupled with steel in seawater, severe galvanic corrosion occurs unless the joint is properly insulated.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all the equations** you must memorize for this topic. Use `\vert` instead of the `pipe` symbol to avoid markdown table breaks.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|--------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Specific Strength (Tensile) | $\sigma_{sp}$ | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$ | kN·m/kg or kPa·m³/kg | Lightweight hull design |
| 2 | Specific Strength (Yield) | $\sigma_{sp,y}$ | $\sigma_{sp,y} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho}$ | kN·m/kg | Plastic collapse check |
| 3 | Specific Stiffness | $E_{sp}$ | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | MN·m/kg | Deflection-limited panels |
| 4 | Strength Ratio (Al / Steel) | $R_\sigma$ | $R_\sigma = \dfrac{\sigma_{Al}}{\sigma_{St}}$ | dimensionless | Direct comparison |
| 5 | Weight Saving Fraction | $W_f$ | $W_f = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{St}} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{St}}{\sigma_{Al}}$ | dimensionless | Hull mass reduction |
| 6 | Buoyancy-corrected density | $\rho_{eff}$ | $\rho_{eff} = \rho_{mat}(1 - \eta)$ | kg/m³ | Submerged hull weight |
| 7 | Stress (axial) | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (or N/mm²) | All strength calcs |
| 8 | Strain | $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | dimensionless | Elasticity check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions / Limits**
> - Valid only for **linear-elastic, isotropic, homogeneous** materials.
> - Valid for temperatures **below 200 °C** for aluminium and **below 550 °C** for steel.
> - In **seawater** at temperatures above **40 °C**, the effective $\sigma_{uts}$ of unprotected aluminium drops by **15–25 %** due to pitting and exfoliation corrosion.
> - For ship hull plating (DNV / IACS rules), the **design stress** = $\sigma_y / 1.15$ for steel and $\sigma_y / 1.50$ for aluminium alloys.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Naval fast patrol boats** (e.g., Swedish Visby-class corvette) use aluminium hulls to achieve **35+ knots**.
- **LNG carrier membrane containment** systems use aluminium alloys for cryogenic toughness down to **−163 °C**.
- **Superstructures of cruise ships** (e.g., Oasis-class) use aluminium to lower the **centre of gravity** and reduce top-weight-induced roll.
- **Offwind turbine towers** and **helidecks** on FPSOs increasingly use aluminium for weight reduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Strength of Mild Steel vs. Aluminium 6061-T6

We are given:
- Mild steel (ASTM A36): $\sigma_{uts,St} = 450$ MPa, $\rho_{St} = 7850$ kg/m³.
- Aluminium 6061-T6: $\sigma_{uts,Al} = 310$ MPa, $\rho_{Al} = 2700$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Write down the specific strength formula.**

$$
\sigma_{sp} = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values for mild steel.**

$$
\sigma_{sp,St} = \frac{450 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa}}{7850\ \text{kg/m}^{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify numerator/denominator to standard SI (Pa ÷ kg/m³ = N·m/kg).**

$$
\sigma_{sp,St} = \frac{450 \times 10^{6}}{7850}\ \frac{\text{N·m}}{\text{kg}}
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division.**

$$
\sigma_{sp,St} = 57{,}324\ \text{N·m/kg} = 57.3\ \text{kN·m/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values for aluminium 6061-T6.**

$$
\sigma_{sp,Al} = \frac{310 \times 10^{6}}{2700}\ \frac{\text{N·m}}{\text{kg}}
$$

**Step 6 — Perform the division.**

$$
\sigma_{sp,Al} = 114{,}815\ \text{N·m/kg} = 114.8\ \text{kN·m/kg}
$$

**Step 7 — Take the ratio to compare.**

$$
R = \frac{\sigma_{sp,Al}}{\sigma_{sp,St}} = \frac{114.8}{57.3} \approx 2.00
$$

**Conclusion:** Aluminium 6061-T6 has **twice the specific strength** of mild steel, despite having only **69 %** of its absolute tensile strength.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Strength of Aluminium 7075-T6 (Aerospace-Grade Marine Alloy)

Given: $\sigma_{uts,Al7075} = 572$ MPa, $\rho_{Al7075} = 2810$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Apply the specific strength formula.**

$$
\sigma_{sp,Al7075} = \frac{572 \times 10^{6}}{2810}\ \frac{\text{N·m}}{\text{kg}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute.**

$$
\sigma_{sp,Al7075} = 203{,}559\ \text{N·m/kg} = 203.6\ \text{kN·m/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with mild steel.**

$$
\frac{203.6}{57.3} \approx 3.55
$$

**Conclusion:** Aluminium 7075-T6 gives **3.55 times** the strength per kilogram of mild steel. This is why it is the **workhorse alloy for fast naval craft and high-performance marine structures**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weight Saving for an Equal-Strength Hull Panel

A shipyard wants to replace a steel hull panel with an aluminium panel of **equal tensile load capacity**. The panel must carry an axial force $F$.

**Step 1 — Required cross-sectional area of steel.**

$$
A_{St} = \frac{F}{\sigma_{uts,St}} = \frac{F}{450 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 2 — Required cross-sectional area of aluminium 6061-T6.**

$$
A_{Al} = \frac{F}{\sigma_{uts,Al}} = \frac{F}{310 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 3 — Ratio of cross-sectional areas (how much thicker the aluminium must be).**

$$
\frac{A_{Al}}{A_{St}} = \frac{450}{310} = 1.452
$$

So aluminium must be **~45 % thicker** in cross-section to carry the same load.

**Step 4 — Mass per unit length of each panel.**

$$
m_{St} = A_{St} \cdot \rho_{St}, \qquad m_{Al} = A_{Al} \cdot \rho_{Al}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass ratio.**

$$
\frac{m_{Al}}{m_{St}} = \frac{A_{Al}}{A_{St}} \cdot \frac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{St}} = 1.452 \cdot \frac{2700}{7850}
$$

$$
\frac{m_{Al}}{m_{St}} = 1.452 \cdot 0.3439 = 0.499
$$

**Step 6 — Weight saving fraction.**

$$
W_f = 1 - \frac{m_{Al}}{m_{St}} = 1 - 0.499 = 0.501
$$

**Conclusion:** By switching to aluminium 6061-T6, the shipyard saves **~50 % of the panel's mass** while delivering the same load capacity.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Property Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **single most important table** for KTU 3-mark and 14-mark answers on this topic.

| Property | Structural Steel (A36) | Al 6061-T6 | Al 7075-T6 | Winner for Marine Hull |
|----------|------------------------|------------|------------|------------------------|
| Density (kg/m³) | 7850 | 2700 | 2810 | **Aluminium (lighter)** |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 250 | 276 | 503 | **Al 7075** |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 450 | 310 | 572 | **Al 7075** |
| Specific Strength (kN·m/kg) | 57.3 | 114.8 | 203.6 | **Al 7075 (3.55×)** |
| Elastic Modulus (GPa) | 200 | 69 | 72 | **Steel (3× stiffer)** |
| Specific Stiffness (MN·m/kg) | 25.5 | 25.6 | 25.6 | **Tied** |
| Corrosion in Seawater | Moderate (rusts) | Good (oxide film) | Good (oxide film) | **Aluminium** |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | 3× | 5× | **Steel** |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good (TIG/MIG) | Difficult | **Steel** |
| Fire Resistance | Good | Poor above 200 °C | Poor above 200 °C | **Steel** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Strength Comparison

The following flowchart maps the **decision logic** a marine engineer follows when choosing between aluminium and steel, based on the strength-related metrics we have discussed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Marine Structure] --> B{Strength Metric Required?}
    B -- Absolute Strength --> C[Compare sigma_uts directly]
    B -- Lightweight Efficiency --> D[Compute Specific Strength]
    B -- Stiffness Required --> E[Compare E and E_specific]

    C --> C1[Steel wins for raw MPa]
    D --> D1[Aluminium alloys win 2 to 4x]
    E --> E1[Steel wins 3x in stiffness]

    C1 --> F{Operating Environment?}
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F

    F -- Seawater Splash Zone --> G1[Aluminium: oxide film protects]
    F -- Seawater + Steel Coupling --> G2[Use insulating gaskets]
    F -- High Temperature 200C --> G3[Steel preferred]
    F -- Cryogenic LNG minus 163C --> G4[Aluminium 5083 wins]

    G1 --> H[Final Material Selection]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H

    H --> I1[Steel: hulls, decks, heavy plate]
    H --> I2[Aluminium: superstructures, fast craft, helidecks]
    H --> I3[Al 7075: military, racing yachts]
    H --> I4[Al 5083: LNG and cryogenic tanks]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Specific Strength Comparison Bar Chart (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MaterialProperty
        P1[Density kg per m3]
        P2[Tensile Strength MPa]
        P3[Specific Strength kN m per kg]
    end

    P1 --> R1[Steel: 7850]
    P1 --> R2[Al 6061: 2700]
    P1 --> R3[Al 7075: 2810]

    P2 --> S1[Steel A36: 450]
    P2 --> S2[Al 6061-T6: 310]
    P2 --> S3[Al 7075-T6: 572]

    R1 --> T1[Steel Specific: 57.3]
    R2 --> T2[Al 6061 Specific: 114.8]
    R3 --> T3[Al 7075 Specific: 203.6]

    S1 --> T1
    S2 --> T2
    S3 --> T3
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the chart:** Density and tensile strength flow *into* the specific strength calculation. The terminal nodes T1, T2, T3 are the **three specific-strength results** you derived in Section 3. This visually proves the **3.55× advantage** of Al 7075-T6.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "specific strength" and explain why it is a more meaningful indicator than absolute tensile strength when comparing aluminium and steel for marine hull construction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Specific strength is defined as the ratio of a material's strength (yield or ultimate tensile strength) to its density: $\sigma_{sp} = \sigma_{uts} / \rho$, expressed in kN·m/kg.
- **Why meaningful (2 marks):** Marine structures (ships, offshore platforms) are **weight-critical** because hull weight directly affects displacement, payload, fuel consumption, and stability. Steel has higher *absolute* strength (e.g., 450 MPa) than aluminium 6061-T6 (310 MPa), but its density (7850 kg/m³) is **2.9× higher** than aluminium (2700 kg/m³). On a per-kilogram basis, aluminium alloys deliver **2 to 4 times** more strength than mild steel, making them more efficient for lightweight marine hulls. Hence specific strength is the correct metric for marine structural comparison.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State any three reasons why aluminium alloys are preferred over steel for the superstructures of large cruise ships, even though aluminium is more expensive per kilogram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Weight reduction (1 mark):** Aluminium 6061-T6 has a density of 2700 kg/m³, roughly one-third that of steel. Lighter superstructure lowers the **vessel's centre of gravity**, improving stability and reducing roll.
2. **Corrosion resistance (1 mark):** Aluminium forms a self-healing **aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃) film** that resists seawater and atmospheric corrosion, reducing maintenance and repainting cycles.
3. **Higher specific strength (1 mark):** Aluminium 6061-T6 has a specific strength of ~115 kN·m/kg versus ~57 kN·m/kg for mild steel, meaning a stronger-yet-lighter superstructure.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Strength Comparison with Numerical Proof*

**(a)** Explain the three engineering metrics — *absolute strength, specific strength, and specific stiffness* — used to compare aluminium and steel in marine applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Absolute Strength (2 marks):** This is the load-carrying capacity per unit cross-sectional area, measured by yield strength $\sigma_y$ and ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{uts}$, in MPa. Steel A36 ($\sigma_{uts} = 450$ MPa) is stronger in absolute terms than aluminium 6061-T6 ($\sigma_{uts} = 310$ MPa). However, this metric ignores weight, which is critical in marine design.

2. **Specific Strength (3 marks):** Defined as $\sigma_{sp} = \sigma_{uts} / \rho$. For mild steel: $450 \times 10^6 / 7850 = 57.3$ kN·m/kg. For aluminium 6061-T6: $310 \times 10^6 / 2700 = 114.8$ kN·m/kg. For aluminium 7075-T6: $572 \times 10^6 / 2810 = 203.6$ kN·m/kg. The ratio of specific strength shows aluminium 7075-T6 is **3.55× more efficient** than mild steel. This is the metric that matters for ship hulls, superstructures, and naval craft.

3. **Specific Stiffness (2 marks):** Defined as $E_{sp} = E / \rho$. For steel: $200 \times 10^9 / 7850 = 25.5$ MN·m/kg. For aluminium 6061-T6: $69 \times 10^9 / 2700 = 25.6$ MN·m/kg. They are nearly equal. However, aluminium's lower absolute modulus (69 GPa vs. 200 GPa) means aluminium panels **deflect more** under the same load, requiring deeper sections or closer stiffener spacing.

**(b)** A marine-grade aluminium plate of grade 5083-H116 ($\sigma_{uts} = 317$ MPa, $\rho = 2660$ kg/m³) is proposed to replace a mild steel plate (A36, $\sigma_{uts} = 400$ MPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³) in a deckhouse side shell. Calculate: (i) the specific strength of each material, and (ii) the percentage weight saving if both plates carry the same axial tensile load. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Specific Strength (3 marks):**

For mild steel:

$$
\sigma_{sp,St} = \frac{400 \times 10^{6}}{7850} = 50{,}955\ \text{N·m/kg} = 50.96\ \text{kN·m/kg}
$$

For aluminium 5083-H116:

$$
\sigma_{sp,Al} = \frac{317 \times 10^{6}}{2660} = 119{,}173\ \text{N·m/kg} = 119.17\ \text{kN·m/kg}
$$

**(ii) Weight Saving Percentage (4 marks):**

For equal load $F$:

$$
A_{St} = \frac{F}{\sigma_{uts,St}} = \frac{F}{400 \times 10^{6}}, \quad A_{Al} = \frac{F}{\sigma_{uts,Al}} = \frac{F}{317 \times 10^{6}}
$$

Area ratio:

$$
\frac{A_{Al}}{A_{St}} = \frac{400}{317} = 1.262
$$

Mass ratio:

$$
\frac{m_{Al}}{m_{St}} = \frac{A_{Al} \cdot \rho_{Al}}{A_{St} \cdot \rho_{St}} = 1.262 \cdot \frac{2660}{7850} = 1.262 \cdot 0.3389 = 0.4276
$$

Weight saving:

$$
W_f = 1 - 0.4276 = 0.5724 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{57.24\ \%\ weight\ saving}
$$

**Conclusion:** Replacing the steel deckhouse with aluminium 5083-H116 saves approximately **57 % of the panel mass** while carrying the same tensile load, leading to lower top-weight and improved vessel stability.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Limitations, Fire Resistance & Selection Criteria*

**(a)** Despite its higher specific strength, aluminium is not universally used in ship hulls. Discuss **four engineering limitations** of aluminium compared to steel in marine structural applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Lower Elastic Modulus (2 marks):** Aluminium's Young's modulus (~70 GPa) is about **one-third** of steel's (~200 GPa). For the same load and geometry, an aluminium beam deflects **3× more**. This requires **larger sections, deeper stiffeners, or closer frame spacing**, partially offsetting the weight advantage.

2. **Loss of Strength at Elevated Temperatures (2 marks):** Aluminium alloys lose strength rapidly above **~200 °C**, with a **50 % strength drop at ~315 °C**. Steel retains useful strength up to **~550 °C**. Hence, in **engine rooms, fire-prone zones, and adjacent to hot pipes**, steel is mandated by classification societies (DNV, ABS, LR).

3. **Galvanic Corrosion in Seawater (2 marks):** When aluminium is in electrical contact with steel (or copper) in seawater, it acts as the **anode** and corrodes rapidly. This requires **insulating gaskets, dielectric bushings, or sacrificial anodes**, adding cost and maintenance.

4. **Lower Fatigue Endurance (1 mark):** Aluminium alloys have a **lower fatigue limit** (~40–50 % of $\sigma_{uts}$) compared to steels (~50–60 %). For wave-loaded hull plating enduring millions of cycles, this can be a critical drawback.

**(b)** A high-speed patrol vessel hull is to be fabricated. The design specification requires a yield strength of at least 240 MPa, a density below 3000 kg/m³, and good weldability. Compare **aluminium 6061-T6** ($\sigma_y = 276$ MPa, $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³) and **aluminium 5083-H116** ($\sigma_y = 228$ MPa, $\rho = 2660$ kg/m³) and recommend the better choice with justification. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Aluminium 6061-T6:**

- Yield strength: 276 MPa (✓ above 240 MPa requirement)
- Density: 2700 kg/m³ (✓ below 3000 kg/m³)
- Weldability: **Good** with TIG/MIG, but heat-affected zone (HAZ) loses up to **30 % strength**; requires post-weld aging treatment.
- Specific strength: $276 \times 10^6 / 2700 = 102.2$ kN·m/kg.
- Corrosion resistance: Good.

**Aluminium 5083-H116:**

- Yield strength: 228 MPa (✗ **below** 240 MPa requirement — fails specification)
- Density: 2660 kg/m³ (✓ below 3000 kg/m³)
- Weldability: **Excellent** — designated as a marine-grade weldable alloy, minimal HAZ strength loss.
- Specific strength: $228 \times 10^6 / 2660 = 85.7$ kN·m/kg.
- Corrosion resistance: **Excellent in seawater** (superior to 6061).

**Recommendation (3 marks):** **Aluminium 6061-T6 is recommended**, because it satisfies the **240 MPa yield strength** requirement, whereas 5083-H116 does not. Although 5083-H116 has better weldability and corrosion resistance, it **fails the primary strength specification** and is therefore not acceptable for this hull application. To compensate for 6061's HAZ softening, the shipyard should adopt **weld procedures with post-weld artificial aging (e.g., 175 °C for 8 hours)** or use weld-strengthened 6061 variants. Alternatively, **aluminium 5059** or **5383** could be considered as upgraded alternatives offering both >240 MPa yield and excellent weldability.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic — KTU Examiner Pattern:**
> 1. **Comparing only absolute strength and concluding "Steel is stronger, end of story."** (0/7) — You MUST compute **specific strength** and discuss weight efficiency. The question is rarely about raw strength; it is about *engineering* strength.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit**. Always write **MPa, kg/m³, kN·m/kg**.
> 3. **Ignoring the elastic modulus issue** — Many students quote specific strength and stop. You MUST mention that aluminium deflects 3× more, which is a critical design limitation.
> 4. **Confusing 6061, 5083, and 7075 alloy grades** — Each has a different role: 6061 (general structural), 5083 (marine/welded hulls), 7075 (high-strength military). Misidentifying them costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning galvanic corrosion** — A complete answer to "Why not use aluminium everywhere?" must include the **galvanic coupling problem in seawater**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Strength of Aluminium vs. Steel**

- ☐ **Density** — Steel = 7850 kg/m³; Aluminium = 2700 kg/m³ (Al is **~2.9× lighter**).
- ☐ **Absolute tensile strength** — Steel A36 = 400–500 MPa; Al 6061-T6 = 310 MPa; Al 7075-T6 = 572 MPa.
- ☐ **Specific strength formula** — $\sigma_{sp} = \sigma_{uts} / \rho$ (units: kN·m/kg).
- ☐ **Al 7075-T6 is 3.55× more specific-strength-efficient** than mild steel.
- ☐ **Al 6061-T6 gives ~50 % weight saving** for equal-strength panels versus mild steel.
- ☐ **Elastic modulus** — Steel 200 GPa; Aluminium ~70 GPa (Al deflects **3× more**).
- ☐ **Specific stiffness is nearly equal** for both (~25.5 MN·m/kg).
- ☐ **Fire resistance** — Steel good up to 550 °C; Aluminium loses strength above 200 °C.
- ☐ **Corrosion** — Aluminium forms protective Al₂O₃ film; steel rusts.
- ☐ **Galvanic corrosion** — Steel-Aluminium coupling in seawater = aluminium corrodes (anode).
- ☐ **Fatigue limit** — Steel ~55 % of $\sigma_{uts}$; Aluminium ~45 % of $\sigma_{uts}$.
- ☐ **Alloy code recall** — 5083 (welded hulls), 6061 (general structural), 7075 (high-strength military).
- ☐ **Marine applications** — Steel → hulls, decks, heavy plate; Aluminium → superstructures, helidecks, fast patrol boats, LNG tanks.
- ☐ **IMO / Class rules** — Design stress = $\sigma_y / 1.15$ (steel), $\sigma_y / 1.50$ (aluminium).
- ☐ **Key takeaway** — In marine design, **specific strength governs** — and aluminium alloys often win.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composition of Aluminium Alloys Used in Shipbuilding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Composition of Aluminium Alloys Used in Shipbuilding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Aluminium Alloys in Shipbuilding (KTU Definition):**
> Aluminium alloys used in shipbuilding are **wrought or cast metallic mixtures** in which **aluminium (Al) is the principal metal (≥ 90%)** and the remaining mass is composed of carefully balanced alloying elements such as **Magnesium (Mg), Manganese (Mn), Silicon (Si), Copper (Cu), Zinc (Zn), and Chromium (Cr)**. These alloys are designated by the **Aluminium Association (AA) 4-digit numerical system** and are engineered specifically to deliver a superior **strength-to-weight ratio**, excellent **seawater corrosion resistance**, and reliable **weldability** for marine structural applications.

The marine-grade aluminium alloys primarily belong to the **5xxx series (Al-Mg alloys)** and **6xxx series (Al-Mg-Si alloys)** as codified by the **ASTM B928 / B209M** standards and recognised by **classification societies** such as **DNV-GL, Lloyd's Register, ABS, and Bureau Veritas**.

## 1.2 The "Why" — Why Aluminium for Ships?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Driving Factors for Aluminium Selection in Shipbuilding:**
> - **Lightweight displacement** → higher payload, higher speed, lower fuel burn.
> - **Naturally forming passive Al₂O₃ layer** → inherent corrosion resistance in seawater.
> - **Non-magnetic** → suitable for mine countermeasure vessels (MCMVs).
> - **Non-sparking** → safe for tanker and ammunition carrier decks.
> - **High specific strength (strength/density)** → structurally efficient hulls.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boat Made of Foam vs. Wood" Intuition

Imagine two identical boats — one built from **heavy oak wood (like steel, ρ ≈ 7850 kg/m³)** and another from **engineered lightweight foam-composite (like marine aluminium, ρ ≈ 2700 kg/m³)**. Both float, but the foam-composite boat sits **higher in the water**, carries **more cargo for the same draft**, and resists rot (corrosion) far better. **Marine aluminium alloys** are precisely that "engineered foam" — but with the structural integrity of wood, engineered through atomic-scale additions of **Mg, Mn, Si, and Cr**.

## 1.4 The Six Major Series of Wrought Aluminium Alloys

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Marine Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| 1xxx | ≥ 99% Pure Al | Cladding, tanks (low strength) |
| 2xxx | Copper (Cu) | **Not preferred** (poor corrosion in seawater) |
| 3xxx | Manganese (Mn) | Moderate use (non-structural panels) |
| 5xxx | Magnesium (Mg) | **PRIMARY MARINE CHOICE** — hulls, superstructures |
| 6xxx | Mg + Si | **SECONDARY MARINE CHOICE** — decks, masts, extrusions |
| 7xxx | Zinc (Zn) | **Limited** (stress corrosion cracking risk) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5xxx and 6xxx series together account for approximately 85–90% of all marine-grade wrought aluminium used in commercial shipbuilding globally.**

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of pure aluminium:** $\rho_{Al} = 2.70 \ \text{g/cm}^3 = 2700 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Density of mild steel:** $\rho_{st} = 7.85 \ \text{g/cm}^3 = 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Modulus of elasticity of Al alloys:** $E_{Al} \approx 70 \ \text{GPa}$
- **Modulus of elasticity of steel:** $E_{st} \approx 210 \ \text{GPa}$
- **Density ratio (Al/Steel):** $\approx 0.344$ (i.e., aluminium is **~65.6% lighter** than steel)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Aluminium Association (AA) Designation System

> [!NOTE]
> **Decoding an AA Designation (e.g., 5083-H116):**
> - **1st digit (5)** → Major alloy group (5xxx = Al-Mg family).
> - **2nd digit (0)** → Original alloy (0 = original; 1,2,... = modifications).
> - **3rd & 4th digits (83)** → Specific alloy within the group (no elemental meaning).
> - **Suffix (H116)** → Temper designation: strain-hardened + special corrosion-resistant treatment suitable for marine service.

### 2.1.1 Temper Designations (Marine-Relevant)

- **H116** → Strain-hardened for marine corrosion resistance (applies to 5xxx alloys with Mg ≥ 4%).
- **H321** → Strain-hardened + stabilised (used for 5083, 5456 hull plates).
- **H112** → Slightly strain-hardened from hot working.
- **T6** → Solution heat-treated + artificially aged (typical for 6xxx extrusions).
- **O** → Annealed (softest condition, used for forming).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All quantities below are KTU-exam ready. Memorise both the formula and the units.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_{Al} \approx 2700 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ | Density of marine aluminium | kg/m³ |
| 2 | $\rho_{st} \approx 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ | Density of mild steel | kg/m³ |
| 3 | $\text{Weight Saving \%} = \dfrac{m_{st} - m_{Al}}{m_{st}} \times 100$ | % weight saved replacing steel with Al | % |
| 4 | $\text{Specific Strength} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho}$ | Strength-to-weight ratio | $\text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$ |
| 5 | $\text{Buoyancy Reserve} = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} - \rho_{Al}}{\rho_{sw}} \times 100$ | Net buoyancy advantage (seawater $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3$) | % |
| 6 | $\sigma_{y,5083} \approx 215 \ \text{MPa}$ (H116) | Yield strength of 5083-H116 | MPa |
| 7 | $\sigma_{y,st} \approx 235 \ \text{MPa}$ (mild steel) | Yield strength of mild steel | MPa |
| 8 | $\text{Weight Ratio} = \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{st}} \approx 0.344$ | Mass ratio for same volume | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** The specific strength formula is the single most important metric a KTU examiner expects you to use when justifying the use of aluminium in shipbuilding. Always pair **weight saving (%)** with **specific strength** for a complete answer.

## 2.4 The "Why and How" — Role of Each Alloying Element

- **Magnesium (Mg):** Primary strengthening element in 5xxx alloys. Mg atoms distort the Al FCC lattice → **solid solution strengthening**. Also forms protective MgO/Mg(OH)₂ film that improves seawater resistance. However, if Mg > 3% and the alloy is not stabilised → risk of **sensitisation** (β-phase Al₃Mg₂ at grain boundaries → stress corrosion cracking).
- **Manganese (Mn):** Refines grain structure; improves strength without harming ductility; binds Fe into intermetallics that are less harmful.
- **Chromium (Cr):** Suppresses recrystallisation; forms fine Al-Cr dispersoids that improve corrosion resistance and toughness.
- **Silicon (Si):** In 6xxx alloys, combines with Mg to form **Mg₂Si precipitates** (β″ phase) responsible for the **T6 age-hardening** mechanism.
- **Copper (Cu):** In 2xxx alloys → highest strength but **catastrophic in seawater** (forms galvanic micro-cells); hence 2xxx is **NOT** used for marine hulls.
- **Iron (Fe) & Silicon (Si) as impurities:** Controlled to low limits because they form coarse intermetallics that reduce fracture toughness.
