# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Percentage Weight Saving (Aluminium vs Steel for Equal Volume)

> **Problem (KTU style):** A ship superstructure panel has a volume of $V = 0.50 \ \text{m}^3$. Compare the mass if manufactured from (a) mild steel, and (b) aluminium alloy 5083. Calculate the percentage weight saving.

### Step 1 — Recall densities

We are given:

$$\rho_{st} = 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^3 \quad \text{(mild steel, standard reference)}$$

$$\rho_{Al} = 2700 \ \text{kg/m}^3 \quad \text{(representative marine aluminium, 5xxx series)}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate mass of steel panel

The fundamental relation is:

$$m = \rho \times V$$

Substituting for steel:

$$m_{st} = \rho_{st} \times V = 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 0.50 \ \text{m}^3$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$m_{st} = 3925 \ \text{kg}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate mass of aluminium panel

Substituting for aluminium 5083:

$$m_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \times V = 2700 \ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 0.50 \ \text{m}^3$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$m_{Al} = 1350 \ \text{kg}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the absolute mass difference

$$\Delta m = m_{st} - m_{Al} = 3925 - 1350 = 2575 \ \text{kg}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate percentage weight saving

The standard percentage weight-saving formula is:

$$\text{Weight Saving \%} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{m_{st}} \times 100$$

Substituting:

$$\text{Weight Saving \%} = \dfrac{2575}{3925} \times 100$$

Performing the division:

$$\text{Weight Saving \%} = 0.6561 \times 100$$

Final answer:

$$\boxed{\text{Weight Saving} \approx 65.6\%}$$

### Step 6 — Cross-check using the density ratio

Using the direct ratio method:

$$\dfrac{m_{Al}}{m_{st}} = \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{st}} = \dfrac{2700}{7850} \approx 0.344$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Weight Saving \%} = (1 - 0.344) \times 100 = 65.6\% \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The two methods agree.** This cross-verification technique is highly valued by KTU examiners and earns easy method marks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Strength Comparison

> **Problem (KTU style):** Aluminium alloy 5083-H116 has a yield strength of $\sigma_y = 215 \ \text{MPa}$ and density $\rho_{Al} = 2700 \ \text{kg/m}^3$. Mild steel has $\sigma_y = 235 \ \text{MPa}$ and $\rho_{st} = 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^3$. Calculate and compare the specific strength of both materials.

### Step 1 — Recall the specific strength formula

The definition of specific strength is:

$$\text{Specific Strength} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$$

### Step 2 — Convert units for consistency

We convert yield strength to consistent units ($\text{N/m}^2$ already, but expressed per kg for ratio clarity):

$$\sigma_{y,5083} = 215 \ \text{MPa} = 215 \times 10^6 \ \text{N/m}^2 = 215 \times 10^3 \ \text{kN/m}^2$$

$$\sigma_{y,st} = 235 \ \text{MPa} = 235 \times 10^6 \ \text{N/m}^2 = 235 \times 10^3 \ \text{kN/m}^2$$

### Step 3 — Calculate specific strength of 5083-H116 aluminium

$$\text{SS}_{Al} = \dfrac{215 \times 10^6 \ \text{N/m}^2}{2700 \ \text{kg/m}^3}$$

Performing the division:

$$\text{SS}_{Al} = 79{,}629.6 \ \text{N}\cdot\text{m/kg} \approx 79.6 \ \text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate specific strength of mild steel

$$\text{SS}_{st} = \dfrac{235 \times 10^6 \ \text{N/m}^2}{7850 \ \text{kg/m}^3}$$

Performing the division:

$$\text{SS}_{st} = 29{,}936.3 \ \text{N}\cdot\text{m/kg} \approx 29.9 \ \text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$$

### Step 5 — Compare the two

$$\dfrac{\text{SS}_{Al}}{\text{SS}_{st}} = \dfrac{79.6}{29.9} \approx 2.66$$

Final result:

$$\boxed{\text{5083-H116 aluminium has } \approx 2.66 \text{ times the specific strength of mild steel}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** Even though mild steel has a higher absolute yield strength (235 > 215 MPa), aluminium 5083-H116 is **far more efficient on a per-kg basis**. This is the core engineering reason naval architects prefer aluminium for **fast vessels, superstructures, and topside modules**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Buoyancy Advantage Calculation

> **Problem (KTU style):** A marine aluminium plate of volume $V$ displaces seawater of density $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3$. Show that aluminium components provide an effective net upward buoyancy compared to steel.

### Step 1 — Net buoyancy force formula

The net buoyancy (uplift) force per unit volume is:

$$F_{net} = (\rho_{sw} - \rho_{material}) \times V \times g$$

### Step 2 — For aluminium

$$F_{net,Al} = (1025 - 2700) \times V \times g = -1675 \ V \ g$$

(Negative sign indicates it still sinks, but with reduced effective weight.)

### Step 3 — For steel

$$F_{net,st} = (1025 - 7850) \times V \times g = -6825 \ V \ g$$

### Step 4 — Effective weight (in seawater) comparison

$$W_{eff,Al} = |F_{net,Al}| = 1675 \ V \ g \ \text{(per unit volume)}$$

$$W_{eff,st} = |F_{net,st}| = 6825 \ V \ g \ \text{(per unit volume)}$$

### Step 5 — Ratio

$$\dfrac{W_{eff,Al}}{W_{eff,st}} = \dfrac{1675}{6825} \approx 0.2454$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{Aluminium structures weigh only } \approx 24.5\% \text{ of their steel equivalent in seawater}}$$

This dramatic reduction translates directly into **increased payload, reduced draft, and higher transit speeds**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Marine Alloys vs Steel

| Property | AA 5083-H116 | AA 6061-T6 | Mild Steel (Grade A) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | **2700** | **2700** | 7850 |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 215 | 240 | 235 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 305 | 310 | 410 |
| Specific Strength (kN·m/kg) | **79.6** | **88.9** | 29.9 |
| Seawater Corrosion | Excellent | Good | Poor (needs coating) |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Modulus of Elasticity (GPa) | 70 | 70 | 210 |
| Marine Application | Hulls, superstructure | Extrusions, fittings | Hulls (with coating) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: List the principal alloying elements in AA 5083 and explain why this alloy is the most widely used marine aluminium alloy.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Principal alloying element:** Magnesium (4.0–4.9%) with Mn (0.40–1.0%) and Cr (0.05–0.25%) as secondary additions. (1 mark)
- **Strengthening mechanism:** Solid solution strengthening by Mg; strain hardening via H116/H321 temper. (1 mark)
- **Marine suitability:** Excellent seawater corrosion resistance, superior weldability without loss of strength in the heat-affected zone, and highest strength among non-heat-treatable aluminium alloys. (1 mark)

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]

**Q: Why are 2xxx series (Al-Cu) alloys generally avoided in shipbuilding hull construction?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- 2xxx alloys contain 3.5–4.5% Cu, which forms **CuAl₂ (θ-phase)** precipitates. (1 mark)
- Cu creates **galvanic micro-cells** with the aluminium matrix, leading to **severe pitting and exfoliation corrosion** in seawater. (1 mark)
- Additionally, 2xxx alloys are **heat-treated (T3/T4)** and lose strength in the weld heat-affected zone, making them unsuitable for **welded marine hulls**. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: (a)** With the help of the Aluminium Association (AA) designation system, explain the composition, properties, and shipbuilding applications of the following marine aluminium alloys:
- (i) AA 5083
- (ii) AA 5454
- (iii) AA 6061-T6

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

**(i) AA 5083 — Marine "King" Alloy (3 marks sub-allocation)**

- **Composition:** Al-balance; Mg 4.0–4.9%; Mn 0.40–1.0%; Cr 0.05–0.25%; Fe ≤ 0.40%; Si ≤ 0.40%; Cu ≤ 0.10%; Zn ≤ 0.25%.
- **Properties:** Yield strength 215 MPa (H116); density 2700 kg/m³; excellent weldability; excellent seawater corrosion resistance; non-heat-treatable (strengthened by solid solution + strain hardening).
- **Applications:** Hull plates of fast ferries, naval patrol vessels, LNG carrier spherical tank supports, superstructures of cruise ships.

**(ii) AA 5454 — Moderate Strength Alloy (2 marks sub-allocation)**

- **Composition:** Al-balance; Mg 2.4–3.0%; Mn 0.50–1.0%; Cr 0.05–0.20%; Si ≤ 0.25%.
- **Properties:** Lower Mg content → lower strength (yield ~200 MPa) but better formability and ductility; good weldability.
- **Applications:** Marine piping, pressure vessels, deck plating where complex forming is required.

**(iii) AA 6061-T6 — Heat-Treatable Extrusion Alloy (2 marks sub-allocation)**

- **Composition:** Al-balance; Mg 0.8–1.2%; Si 0.40–0.80%; Cu 0.15–0.40%; Cr 0.04–0.35%.
- **Properties:** Heat-treatable (T6 → solution treated + artificially aged); yield strength ~240 MPa; excellent extrudability; good corrosion resistance (lower than 5083 but acceptable for non-submerged parts).
- **Applications:** Masts, helidecks, gangways, deck fittings, mast platforms.

---

**(b)** A ship superstructure panel of volume 0.75 m³ is to be fabricated. Calculate the weight saving (in kg and %) if the panel is made of AA 5083-H116 (ρ = 2700 kg/m³) instead of mild steel (ρ = 7850 kg/m³). Comment on the impact on the vessel's centre of gravity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Step 1 — Mass of steel panel**

$$m_{st} = \rho_{st} \times V = 7850 \times 0.75 = 5887.5 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of aluminium panel**

$$m_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \times V = 2700 \times 0.75 = 2025 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Absolute mass saving**

$$\Delta m = 5887.5 - 2025 = 3862.5 \ \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage weight saving**

$$\text{Weight Saving \%} = \dfrac{3862.5}{5887.5} \times 100 = 65.6\%$$

**Step 5 — Impact on centre of gravity (CG)**

- The superstructure is located **above the waterline** (high vertical position).
- A reduction of 3862.5 kg at this elevated position significantly **lowers the vertical centre of gravity (KG)** of the vessel.
- A lower KG → **greater initial transverse stability (GM)**, improved metacentric height, and **reduced roll amplitude** — all critical for passenger comfort and safety.
- Additional benefits: reduced hull girder bending moment, lower fuel consumption, and increased payload capacity.

**Final boxed answer:**

$$\boxed{\Delta m = 3862.5 \ \text{kg}, \quad \text{Weight Saving} = 65.6\%}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Internal Choice]

**Q: (a)** Compare the composition, mechanical properties, corrosion behaviour, and typical shipboard applications of **AA 5xxx series alloys (5083, 5086, 5456)** with **AA 6xxx series alloys (6061, 6082)**. Use a comparative table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Comparative Table (5 marks sub-allocation):**

| Parameter | AA 5xxx (5083/5086/5456) | AA 6xxx (6061/6082) |
|---|---|---|
| **Major alloying element** | Mg (2.4–5.5%) | Mg + Si (forms Mg₂Si) |
| **Strengthening mechanism** | Solid solution + strain hardening (H-tempers) | Precipitation hardening (T6 temper) |
| **Heat-treatable** | No (non-heat-treatable) | Yes (heat-treatable) |
| **Weldability** | Excellent (no HAZ strength loss) | Good (some strength loss in HAZ) |
| **Seawater corrosion resistance** | Excellent (sensitisation risk if Mg > 3% and not stabilised) | Good (inferior to 5xxx in submerged service) |
| **Formability** | Good | Moderate (better in T4 / O temper) |
| **Typical marine use** | Hull plates, superstructures, patrol boats | Masts, extrusions, gangways, helidecks |
| **Specific alloys in shipbuilding** | 5083, 5086, 5454, 5456 | 6061-T6, 6082-T6 |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Slightly higher (heat treatment adds cost) |

**Conclusion (2 marks sub-allocation):** 5xxx series alloys dominate **welded hull applications** because they do not lose strength in the weld heat-affected zone. 6xxx series alloys are preferred for **extruded structural sections** (masts, helidecks, gangways) where complex profiles and high strength are needed.

---

**(b)** A marine aluminium alloy 5083-H116 component has a yield strength of 215 MPa. A mild steel component of the same function has a yield strength of 235 MPa. Compare their **specific strengths** and discuss which is more suitable for a **fast patrol boat hull**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Step 1 — Specific strength of 5083-H116**

$$\text{SS}_{Al} = \dfrac{215 \times 10^6 \ \text{N/m}^2}{2700 \ \text{kg/m}^3} = 79{,}629.6 \ \text{N}\cdot\text{m/kg} \approx 79.6 \ \text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Specific strength of mild steel**

$$\text{SS}_{st} = \dfrac{235 \times 10^6 \ \text{N/m}^2}{7850 \ \text{kg/m}^3} = 29{,}936.3 \ \text{N}\cdot\text{m/kg} \approx 29.9 \ \text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison ratio**

$$\dfrac{\text{SS}_{Al}}{\text{SS}_{st}} = \dfrac{79.6}{29.9} \approx 2.66$$

**Step 4 — Suitability for fast patrol boat hull**

Although the absolute yield strength of steel (235 MPa) is greater than that of 5083 (215 MPa), the **specific strength** of 5083 is **~2.66× higher**. For a fast patrol boat hull, the priority is to:
- **Maximise speed and acceleration** → requires low mass.
- **Minimise displacement** → allows higher payload and shallower draft.
- **Reduce inertia** → improves manoeuvrability and reduces fuel burn.

Therefore, **AA 5083-H116 aluminium is the preferred material** for fast patrol boat hulls despite its lower absolute yield strength.

**Final boxed answer:**

$$\boxed{\text{SS}_{Al} \approx 79.6 \ \text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg} \ \gg \ \text{SS}_{st} \approx 29.9 \ \text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg}; \ \text{AA 5083 is preferred for fast patrol boat hulls.}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing temper vs alloy designation** — Do NOT write "5083-T6". 5083 is non-heat-treatable; the correct temper is H116, H321, or H112. 6061 is heat-treatable and uses T6.
> 2. **Forgetting the Cu limit in 5083** — Always state "Cu ≤ 0.10%" because it controls galvanic corrosion.
> 3. **Writing "aluminium doesn't corrode"** — A common WRONG statement. Aluminium forms a protective Al₂O₃ passive layer, but it still corrodes in specific conditions (galvanic, crevice, pitting in chloride media).
> 4. **Ignoring units in specific strength** — KTU examiners specifically look for the unit `kN·m/kg` (or `N·m/kg`). Marks are deducted if units are missing.
> 5. **Mixing 2xxx with 5xxx/6xxx** — 2xxx (Al-Cu) is NEVER used for marine hulls. Memorise this distinction.
> 6. **Forgetting H116 / H321 temper significance** — These are not random codes; they denote "strain-hardened + marine corrosion-resistant temper" — explicitly state this in answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Marine aluminium = primarily 5xxx (Al-Mg) and 6xxx (Al-Mg-Si) series.**
- [x] **AA 5083-H116** is the **most widely used** marine aluminium alloy (hulls, superstructures).
- [x] **AA 5454** — moderate Mg, used for piping and pressure vessels.
- [x] **AA 5456** — highest Mg, used for high-speed naval hulls.
- [x] **AA 6061-T6** and **AA 6082-T6** — heat-treatable extrusions (masts, helidecks).
- [x] **AA 5086** — weldability specialist for small craft.
- [x] **2xxx series (Al-Cu) is FORBIDDEN** for marine hulls (galvanic corrosion).
- [x] **7xxx series (Al-Zn)** has limited marine use due to **stress corrosion cracking**.
- [x] **Density of aluminium = 2700 kg/m³; steel = 7850 kg/m³; ratio ≈ 0.344.**
- [x] **Weight saving when replacing steel with Al ≈ 65.6%** (for equal volume).
- [x] **Specific strength of 5083-H116 ≈ 79.6 kN·m/kg** vs steel ≈ 29.9 kN·m/kg (≈ 2.66× higher).
- [x] **Mg provides solid solution strengthening** in 5xxx alloys.
- [x] **Si + Mg forms Mg₂Si precipitates** responsible for T6 strengthening in 6xxx alloys.
- [x] **Cr (0.05–0.25%)** inhibits recrystallisation and improves toughness.
- [x] **Mn (0.40–1.0%)** refines grain structure and binds harmful Fe.
- [x] **H116 / H321 tempers** are mandatory marine-service tempers for 5xxx alloys.
- [x] **Aluminium is non-magnetic** → ideal for MCMVs (mine countermeasure vessels).
- [x] **Aluminium is non-sparking** → safe for tanker and ammunition decks.
- [x] **Al₂O₃ passive film** provides inherent corrosion resistance but is vulnerable to galvanic coupling with copper-based alloys.
- [x] **Eff weight in seawater for Al = 24.5% of steel** → major buoyancy advantage.
- [x] **Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV)** approve specific alloys for marine structural use.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages of Using Aluminium Over Steel in Shipbuilding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages of Using Aluminium Over Steel in Shipbuilding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)**
> **Aluminium (Al)** is a lightweight, non-ferrous, ductile structural metal belonging to Group **13 (IIIA)** of the periodic table (atomic number **13**, atomic mass **26.98 g/mol**, density **2.70 g/cm³**). In marine engineering, *aluminium alloys* (not pure aluminium) — particularly the **5xxx (Al-Mg)**, **6xxx (Al-Mg-Si)**, and **7xxx (Al-Zn)** series — are preferred for shipbuilding because of their superior **strength-to-weight ratio**, inherent **corrosion resistance** in seawater, and excellent **formability** for hull plates and superstructures.

**Steel**, by contrast, is an **iron-carbon alloy** (with typical marine-grade variants such as **AH36, DH36, EH36** per IACS standards) possessing a density of **7.85 g/cm³** and a yield strength between **235–355 MPa** for mild grades, but requiring **active corrosion protection** (painting, cathodic protection) in marine service.

The *advantage* of aluminium is therefore not absolute superiority but a **trade-off optimisation** — sacrificing a small degree of ultimate tensile strength in exchange for **mass reduction, fuel economy, payload increase, and intrinsic corrosion resistance**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Think of it like this…)**
> Imagine you have to build a backpack-carrying boat. You have two materials to choose from:
> - **Steel** = a *heavy-duty iron rod* that is very strong but makes the boat heavy, drinks more fuel, and rusts if you don't paint it every season.
> - **Aluminium** = a *lightweight titanium-frame hiking pole* — almost as strong, **one-third the weight**, and naturally forms a thin invisible oxide skin that protects it from rust, *almost like a built-in raincoat*.
>
> For a **warship, ferry, or high-speed patrol boat** where *every kilogram matters* (speed, payload, fuel, range), aluminium is like swapping a brick for a feather that still holds the load. That is the essence of the aluminium-over-steel advantage.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical & Engineering Constants (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Material Constants to Memorise for KTU 2024 ESE**
> - Density of Steel (mild): **ρ_steel = 7850 kg/m³**
> - Density of Aluminium (5083-H116 marine alloy): **ρ_Al ≈ 2700 kg/m³**
> - Density ratio: **ρ_Al / ρ_steel ≈ 0.344** (i.e., aluminium is ~34.4% the weight of steel for equal volume)
> - Specific gravity of aluminium: **2.70–2.81** (depending on alloy)
> - Young's Modulus of Steel: **E_steel ≈ 200–210 GPa**
> - Young's Modulus of Aluminium: **E_Al ≈ 70 GPa**
> - Modulus ratio: **E_Al / E_steel ≈ 0.333**
> - Melting point of pure aluminium: **660.3 °C**
> - Melting point of mild steel: **≈ 1500 °C**
> - Pitting Resistance Equivalence Number (PREN) of marine Al-5083: **≈ 30–35 (as-welded)**
> - Pitting potential of pure Al in seawater: **≈ −0.75 V (SCE)** (highly active, but protective Al₂O₃ film forms instantly)

---

## 1.4 Module 1 Context (Syllabus Placement)

According to the KTU 2024 OENST612 syllabus, this topic falls under **Module 1: Introduction to Marine Materials and Material Properties**, where students must compare the mechanical, physical, and chemical behaviour of structural metals used in ship construction. The advantages of aluminium over steel form a **frequently tested 14-mark Part B question** because it integrates *mechanics, corrosion science, and naval architecture* into a single comparative analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Aluminium's Advantage Over Steel

The advantages of aluminium over steel in shipbuilding are **not arbitrary marketing claims** but are derived from quantifiable engineering properties. These can be organised under seven fundamental pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Weight Reduction (Density Advantage)
For an **equal volume** of material, aluminium weighs only **~34%** of steel. For an **equal stiffness** (EI) requirement, a thicker aluminium plate can still be **~50% lighter** than the equivalent steel plate because the increased section modulus partially offsets the lower modulus of elasticity.

### Pillar 2 — High Specific Strength (Strength-to-Weight Ratio)
Specific strength is **strength divided by density**. Even though aluminium's yield strength (~215–345 MPa for 5xxx/6xxx series) is *lower than* high-tensile steel (up to 690 MPa), its **low density** makes the specific strength *comparable or superior* in many grades.

### Pillar 3 — Corrosion Resistance (Intrinsic Passivation)
Aluminium spontaneously forms a thin, adherent, self-healing **aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃) film** of thickness **2–10 nm** in air, and a hydrated **Al₂O₃·H₂O / Al(OH)₃** layer in seawater. This film:
- Is **electrically insulating**,
- Re-forms within **milliseconds** if scratched,
- Acts as a **barrier to oxygen and chloride diffusion**.

Steel, conversely, forms a porous, non-protective **iron oxide (rust, Fe₂O₃·nH₂O)** that spalls and accelerates corrosion.

### Pillar 4 — Fuel Economy & Range
A 1% reduction in hull weight translates (in naval architecture approximation) to **0.5–1.0% increase in fuel efficiency** for a given speed. Aluminium superstructures on naval vessels reduce top-weight → improve **stability (metacentric height, GM)**.

### Pillar 5 — Ease of Fabrication & Formability
Aluminium has:
- Elongation at break: **12–25%** (vs. ~21% for mild steel),
- Hot/cold workability,
- **Lower rolling/forging temperatures** (~450 °C),
- Excellent **weldability** using MIG/TIG processes (5xxx and 6xxx series).

### Pillar 6 — Non-Magnetic & Non-Sparking
Aluminium is **paramagnetic** (relative permeability ≈ 1.000022) — making it the preferred hull material for **mine countermeasures vessels (MCMV)** and **degaussing-friendly warships** where magnetic signatures must be minimised.

### Pillar 7 — Recyclability & Sustainability
- Recycling aluminium consumes only **~5% of the energy** required for primary production.
- Recycled aluminium retains **identical mechanical properties**.
- Steel recycling energy is ~25–35% of primary production.

---

## 2.2 Limitations (Honest Trade-offs — Expected in KTU Answers)

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitations to Acknowledge (Will fetch full marks if mentioned)**
> 1. **Lower Young's Modulus** → More deflection under load → requires thicker sections.
> 2. **Lower fatigue strength** at high stress cycles compared to steel.
> 3. **Galvanic corrosion risk** when coupled with copper, brass, or steel without isolation.
> 4. **Higher initial material cost** (aluminium ~3–4× cost of mild steel per kg, but lifecycle cost can be lower).
> 5. **Lower fire resistance** — loses strength above **~200 °C** vs. steel's **~550 °C**.
> 6. **Sensitivity to chloride pitting** if the protective film breaks down at temperatures > 60–80 °C.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Formulas — At Least 2 Will Appear in Every 14-Mark Question**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes / Typical Value (Marine) |
|---|----------|---------|------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | Specific Strength | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ (or kN·m/kg) | Al-5083: ~80 kN·m/kg; Mild Steel: ~30 kN·m/kg |
| 2 | Specific Stiffness | $E/\rho$ | $\text{GPa}/(\text{g/cm}^3)$ | Al-5083 ≈ 25.9; Mild Steel ≈ 26.1 (nearly equal!) |
| 3 | Weight Ratio (equal vol.) | $W_{Al}/W_{st} = \rho_{Al}/\rho_{st}$ | dimensionless | ≈ 0.344 |
| 4 | Bending Stress (Euler-Bernoulli beam) | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | MPa | Used to compute section modulus |
| 5 | Deflection (Cantilever, UDL) | $\delta = \dfrac{w L^4}{8 E I}$ | mm | Aluminium: ~3× deflection of steel at same geometry |
| 6 | Corrosion Rate (mass loss) | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ | mm/yr (mpy) | ASTM G1 standard; $K = 8.76 \times 10^4$ (mm units) |
| 7 | Pitting Index (for Al alloys) | $PREN_{Al} = \%Mg + 0.5 \times \%Mn$ | dimensionless | Higher = more pitting resistant |
| 8 | Galvanic Series Position (in seawater) | Al: −0.75 to −0.95 V (SCE) | Volt (SCE) | More anodic than steel (steel ≈ −0.60 V) |
| 9 | Fuel Saving (approx.) | $\Delta Fuel \% \approx 0.5 \times \Delta W_{\text{hull}} \%$ | % | Naval architecture thumb rule |
| 10 | Hull-Girder Bending Stiffness | $EI = E \cdot \dfrac{b \cdot t^3}{12}$ | N·m² | $b$=breadth, $t$=plate thickness |

> **Note (per system protocol):** Vertical bars in the formulas above are written as `\vert` or `\mid` only where unavoidable. All absolute-value or "given" separators in the table use plain parentheses or written words to keep the markdown parser stable.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Aluminium is Chosen |
|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Naval Patrol Boats** (e.g., Indian Navy's *T-80 Mod* design lineage) | Light weight → high speed, lower top-weight |
| **Fast Ferries & Hydrofoils** (Boeing 929, INCAT) | Weight reduction → higher payload + speed |
| **Mine Counter Measure Vessels (MCMV)** | Non-magnetic → low magnetic signature |
| **Superstructures of Warships** | Lower top-weight → improved stability, reduced radar cross-section |
| **LNG Carrier Secondary Barriers** | Cryogenic compatibility (Al retains toughness at −162 °C) |
| **Yacht & Small Craft Hulls** | Corrosion resistance, ease of fabrication |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Derivation Set — KTU 2024 ESE Style**
> The following derivations are the **most frequently tested numerical/logical chains** on this topic. Each step is written out exhaustively. **Do not skip any line** when reproducing in the exam.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Weight Reduction for Equal Stiffness (Bending of a Plate)

### Problem Setup
A ship hull plate is to be redesigned from **mild steel** to **aluminium alloy 5083-H116**, while maintaining the same **bending stiffness** $EI$ (since hull-girder stiffness governs hull deflection). Determine the weight saving percentage.

### Given
- Mild steel: $E_s = 210\ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_s = 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Aluminium 5083: $E_a = 70\ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_a = 2700\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Original steel plate dimensions: width $b$, thickness $t_s$
- Aluminium plate: same width $b$, thickness $t_a$ (to be found)

### Step 1 — Write Stiffness Equality
For a rectangular plate (unit-width strip), the second moment of area is

$$
I_s = \dfrac{b \cdot t_s^{\,3}}{12}
$$

$$
I_a = \dfrac{b \cdot t_a^{\,3}}{12}
$$

Equating $E_s I_s = E_a I_a$:

$$
E_s \cdot \dfrac{b \cdot t_s^{\,3}}{12} = E_a \cdot \dfrac{b \cdot t_a^{\,3}}{12}
$$

### Step 2 — Cancel Common Factors
Width $b$ and the divisor $12$ cancel on both sides, leaving:

$$
E_s \cdot t_s^{\,3} = E_a \cdot t_a^{\,3}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $t_a$ in Terms of $t_s$

$$
t_a^{\,3} = \dfrac{E_s}{E_a} \cdot t_s^{\,3}
$$

$$
t_a = t_s \cdot \left(\dfrac{E_s}{E_a}\right)^{1/3}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\dfrac{E_s}{E_a} = \dfrac{210}{70} = 3.0
$$

$$
t_a = t_s \cdot (3.0)^{1/3} = t_s \cdot 1.4422
$$

### Step 5 — Compare Masses (per unit area)
Mass per unit area: $m = \rho \cdot t$

For steel: $m_s = \rho_s \cdot t_s = 7850 \cdot t_s$

For aluminium: $m_a = \rho_a \cdot t_a = 2700 \cdot (1.4422 \, t_s) = 3893.94 \, t_s$

### Step 6 — Calculate Weight Ratio

$$
\dfrac{m_a}{m_s} = \dfrac{3893.94}{7850} = 0.4960
$$

### Step 7 — Convert to Percentage Weight Saving

$$
\%\ \text{Weight Saving} = (1 - 0.4960) \times 100\% = 50.4\ \%
$$

> **Conclusion:** Replacing mild steel with **Al-5083** while maintaining **equal bending stiffness** saves approximately **50% of the hull plate weight**. This is a *practical* number used in real ship-design optimization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Strength Comparison

### Step 1 — Define Specific Strength

$$
\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}
$$

### Step 2 — Insert Values

For **Mild Steel** ($\sigma_{y,s} = 235\ \text{MPa}$, $\rho_s = 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$):

$$
\sigma_{sp,\,s} = \dfrac{235 \times 10^6}{7850} = 29{,}936\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

For **Al-5083-O** ($\sigma_{y,a} = 145\ \text{MPa}$, $\rho_a = 2700\ \text{kg/m}^3$):

$$
\sigma_{sp,\,a} = \dfrac{145 \times 10^6}{2700} = 53{,}704\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Ratio

$$
\dfrac{\sigma_{sp,\,a}}{\sigma_{sp,\,s}} = \dfrac{53{,}704}{29{,}936} = 1.79
$$

> **Conclusion:** Al-5083's specific strength is **~1.79× that of mild steel**, meaning **1 kg of Al-5083 carries nearly 1.79× the load of 1 kg of mild steel**. (For high-tensile steel AH36 at 355 MPa, ratio is ~1.27.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Galvanic Corrosion Current (Wagner-Traud Mixed-Potential Sketch)

When aluminium is coupled to steel in seawater without insulation, a **galvanic cell** forms.

### Step 1 — Electrochemical Potentials (SCE, seawater)
- Steel: $E_{st} \approx -0.60\ \text{V}$
- Aluminium: $E_{Al} \approx -0.78\ \text{V}$

Since $E_{Al} < E_{st}$, **aluminium becomes the anode** and corrodes preferentially.

### Step 2 — Corrosion Current Density (Wagner-Traud)
For a coupled pair, the mixed potential $E_{mix}$ satisfies:

$$
i_a(E_{mix}) = -i_c(E_{mix})
$$

The galvanic current density is approximately (using linear Tafel approximation):

$$
i_{galv} = \dfrac{E_{c} - E_{a}}{\dfrac{1}{k_a} + \dfrac{1}{k_c}}
$$

where $k_a$, $k_c$ are the anodic/cathodic Tafel slopes converted to conductance.

### Step 3 — Mass Loss Rate (Faraday's Law)

$$
\dot{m}_{Al} = \dfrac{M_{Al} \cdot i_{galv} \cdot A_{Al}}{n \cdot F}
$$

where:
- $M_{Al} = 26.98\ \text{g/mol}$
- $n = 3$ (Al³⁺)
- $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$
- $A_{Al}$ = exposed area of Al (m²)

### Step 4 — Numerical Illustration
If $i_{galv} = 5\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{A/m}^2$, and area $A_{Al} = 1\ \text{m}^2$:

$$
\dot{m}_{Al} = \dfrac{26.98 \times 5\times 10^{-2} \times 1}{3 \times 96{,}485}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{Al} = 4.66 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/s} = 15.4\ \text{g/hr}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Without isolation, **aluminium will dissolve to protect steel** at a rate of ~15 g/hr per m². This is why KTU/IMO rules mandate **insulating gaskets and zinc/aluminium anodes** at every Al-steel joint.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Print-and-Learn)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print This Table — Appears in 90% of 14-Mark Answers as the Backbone**

| Property | Mild Steel (AH36) | Aluminium 5083-H116 | Al Advantage? |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Density (kg/m³) | 7850 | 2700 | ✅ Yes (66% lighter) |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 210 | 70 | ❌ No (3× lower) |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 355 | 215 | ❌ Lower |
| Specific Strength (kN·m/kg) | 45.2 | 79.6 | ✅ ~1.76× better |
| Specific Stiffness (GPa·cm³/g) | 26.7 | 25.9 | ≈ Parity |
| Corrosion Rate in seawater | 0.1–0.5 mm/yr (unprotected) | 0.01–0.05 mm/yr | ✅ ~10× better |
| Melting Point (°C) | ~1500 | 575–640 | ❌ Lower (fire risk) |
| Magnetic Signature | Ferromagnetic | Paramagnetic | ✅ MCMV use |
| Recyclability Energy | 25–35% of primary | 5% of primary | ✅ Yes |
| Cost (USD/kg, 2024) | ~0.8–1.0 | ~2.5–3.5 | ❌ Higher upfront |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Visual Aid Standard:** Every answer carrying more than 7 marks **must include at least one schematic**. The diagrams below follow the Mermaid-safe compilation protocol (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, no markdown inside double-quoted labels).

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Material Selection Decision Flow for Marine Structural Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Structural Requirement: Hull Plate] --> B{Need Lightweight?}
    B -- YES --> C{Need High Speed?}
    C -- YES --> D[Aluminium Alloy - 5xxx or 6xxx Series]
    C -- NO --> E{High Magnetic Signature Risk?}
    E -- YES --> F[Aluminium - Non-Magnetic]
    E -- NO --> G[FRP Composite or Hybrid]
    B -- NO --> H{Cost Sensitive?}
    H -- YES --> I[Conventional Mild Steel - AH36]
    H -- NO --> J{High-Tensile Steel - DH36 or EH36]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style D fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e8b57
    style F fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e8b57
    style I fill:#fff4d4,stroke:#cc9900
    style J fill:#fff4d4,stroke:#cc9900
    style G fill:#f4d4e8,stroke:#993399
```

**Interpretation:** The flowchart shows that **aluminium is selected when weight OR magnetic signature is critical** — exactly the cases of fast patrol boats, MCMVs, and naval superstructures. Steel dominates where cost and structural thickness are the priority.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Architecture of Aluminium Ship Hull (Functional Modules)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MODULE_A[RAW MATERIAL]
        A1[Bauxite Ore]
        A2[Bayer Process]
        A3[Smelting - Hall Heroult]
        A4[Primary Aluminium Ingot]
    end

    subgraph MODULE_B[ALLOYING]
        B1[Alloy 5083: Mg 4 to 5 percent, Mn 0.7 percent]
        B2[Alloy 6082: Mg 1.0 percent, Si 1.0 percent]
        B3[Alloy 5456: Mg 5.0 to 5.5 percent]
    end

    subgraph MODULE_C[FABRICATION]
        C1[Rolling to Plate]
        C2[Cold Forming]
        C3[MIG or TIG Welding]
        C4[Machining and Drilling]
    end

    subgraph MODULE_D[CORROSION PROTECTION]
        D1[Anodizing AA25 or AA20]
        D2[Marine Epoxy Primer]
        D3[Anti-Fouling Top Coat]
        D4[Sacrificial Al-Zn-In Anode]
    end

    subgraph MODULE_E[IN-SERVICE MONITORING]
        E1[Linear Polarisation Resistance]
        E2[Ultrasonic Thickness Gauging]
        E3[Visual Hull Inspection - Class Survey]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    A4 --> B1
    A4 --> B2
    A4 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3
    C4 --> D4
    D3 --> E1
    D3 --> E2
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> E3
    E2 --> E3

    style MODULE_A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style MODULE_B fill:#fff4d4,stroke:#cc9900
    style MODULE_C fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e8b57
    style MODULE_D fill:#f4d4e8,stroke:#993399
    style MODULE_E fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc3333
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram tracks the full **materials-engineering value chain** of an aluminium ship hull — from bauxite to in-service corrosion monitoring. The five functional blocks correspond directly to the syllabus learning outcomes of OENST612.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Corrosion Mechanism Comparison (Al vs. Steel in Seawater)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Aluminium Surface in Seawater] --> B1[O2 plus H2O Attack]
    B1 --> C1[Formation of Al2O3 plus H2O Film]
    C1 --> D1{Is Film Intact?}
    D1 -- YES --> E1[Corrosion HALTED: Protected]
    D1 -- NO Cl or Cu ions --> F1[Pitting at Micro-defects]
    F1 --> G1[Self-healing within Milliseconds]

    H1[Steel Surface in Seawater] --> I1[O2 plus H2O plus Cl Attack]
    I1 --> J1[Formation of Fe2O3 plus nH2O - Rust]
    J1 --> K1{Is Rust Protective?}
    K1 -- NO --> L1[Porous Rust - Spalls Off]
    L1 --> M1[Accelerated Layer-by-Layer Corrosion]
    M1 --> N1[Active Painting or Cathodic Protection REQUIRED]

    style E1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e8b57
    style G1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e8b57
    style N1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc3333
    style M1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc3333
```

**Interpretation:** This flowchart crystallises the **single most important corrosion-based reason** aluminium is favoured over steel: **passivation**. Aluminium's oxide is *protective and self-healing*; steel's oxide is *porous and non-protective*. This alone saves shipyards millions in coating maintenance over a 25-year service life.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

### Question A.1 — 3 Marks
**Q: Define "specific strength" and explain why aluminium alloys are preferred over mild steel for ship superstructures on the basis of this property.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Specific strength is defined as the ratio of a material's yield (or ultimate tensile) strength to its density:
>
> $$\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$$
>
> It is measured in units of kN·m/kg and is the key metric when **mass is a design constraint**.

> **Application to shipbuilding (2 marks):** Although the yield strength of Al-5083 (~215 MPa) is lower than that of mild steel (235–355 MPa), its density (2700 kg/m³) is **~34%** of steel's (7850 kg/m³). This makes the specific strength of Al-5083 **~1.8× that of mild steel**. For a ship superstructure, **lower top-weight improves metacentric height (GM), reduces draft, increases payload, and saves fuel**, making aluminium the preferred material in high-speed patrol boats, naval frigates, and luxury yachts.

---

### Question A.2 — 3 Marks
**Q: List any three advantages of aluminium over steel in shipbuilding. Justify each in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Light weight (1 mark):** Aluminium has a density of **2700 kg/m³**, about one-third that of steel (**7850 kg/m³**), which reduces hull weight and increases fuel efficiency.
2. **Corrosion resistance (1 mark):** Aluminium naturally forms a thin, adherent, self-healing **Al₂O₃ passive film** in seawater, eliminating the need for continuous repainting required by steel hulls.
3. **Non-magnetic nature (1 mark):** Aluminium is **paramagnetic** with relative permeability ~1, making it the preferred hull material for **mine countermeasure vessels (MCMV)** where low magnetic signature is critical for survivability.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Dec 2023 Pattern — Internal Choice Mandatory]**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**

> **Q: With the help of a comparative property table, discuss the advantages and limitations of using aluminium alloys over mild steel in shipbuilding. Illustrate your answer with at least one design calculation showing weight savings for equal bending stiffness. (14 marks)**

#### (a) Comparative Property Table — Advantages & Limitations (7 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Students often lose 2–3 marks by omitting the limitations. KTU examiners specifically look for a balanced answer — mention at least 3 disadvantages.**

| S.No. | Property / Aspect | Mild Steel (AH36) | Aluminium Alloy (5083) | Verdict |
|-------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------------|---------|
| 1 | Density (kg/m³) | 7850 | 2700 | Al advantage: **~66% lighter** |
| 2 | Young's Modulus (GPa) | 210 | 70 | Steel advantage: **3× stiffer** |
| 3 | Yield Strength (MPa) | 355 | 215 | Steel advantage |
| 4 | Specific Strength (kN·m/kg) | 45.2 | 79.6 | **Al advantage: ~1.76×** |
| 5 | Specific Stiffness | 26.7 | 25.9 | Roughly equal |
| 6 | Corrosion in seawater (mm/yr) | 0.1–0.5 (unprotected) | 0.01–0.05 | **Al advantage: ~10× better** |
| 7 | Passivation Film | Porous, non-protective rust | Al₂O₃ self-healing | **Al advantage** |
| 8 | Melting Point (°C) | ~1500 | 575–640 | Steel advantage (fire safety) |
| 9 | Magnetic Signature | Ferromagnetic | Paramagnetic | **Al advantage for MCMV** |
| 10 | Cost (USD/kg) | ~0.9 | ~2.8 | Steel advantage (upfront) |
| 11 | Recyclability | 25–35% of primary energy | 5% of primary energy | **Al advantage** |
| 12 | Galvanic Corrosion | Acts as cathode in couples | Acts as anode in couples | **Steel advantage** |

**Advantages in summary (3 marks):**
- Lightweight (1 mark)
- High specific strength (1 mark)
- Intrinsic corrosion resistance via Al₂O₃ film (1 mark)
- Non-magnetic → MCMV use *(bonus point)*

**Limitations in summary (2 marks):**
- Lower Young's modulus → more deflection (1 mark)
- Higher fire risk (low melting point) (1 mark)
- Galvanic corrosion risk with steel, copper, brass (carry-forward)

#### (b) Design Calculation — Weight Saving at Equal Bending Stiffness (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Mild steel plate: $E_s = 210\ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_s = 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$, thickness $t_s = 10\ \text{mm}$
- Al-5083 plate: $E_a = 70\ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_a = 2700\ \text{kg/m}^3$, thickness $t_a = ?$

**Stiffness equality (Bending):**

$$
E_s \cdot t_s^{\,3} = E_a \cdot t_a^{\,3}
$$

$$
t_a = t_s \cdot \left(\dfrac{E_s}{E_a}\right)^{1/3} = 10 \cdot (3.0)^{1/3} = 14.42\ \text{mm}
$$

**Mass per m² (1 m² area):**

$$
m_s = \rho_s \cdot t_s = 7850 \times 0.010 = 78.5\ \text{kg/m}^2
$$

$$
m_a = \rho_a \cdot t_a = 2700 \times 0.01442 = 38.93\ \text{kg/m}^2
$$

**Weight Saving:**

$$
\%\ \text{Saving} = \left(1 - \dfrac{38.93}{78.5}\right) \times 100\% = 50.4\ \%
$$

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** The aluminium plate must be **44% thicker** but still ends up **~50% lighter** at equal bending stiffness, justifying its use in ship hulls and superstructures.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

> **Q: Explain the corrosion behaviour of aluminium alloys in seawater. Why is aluminium preferred over steel for mine countermeasure vessels (MCMV)? Discuss at least four advantages of aluminium over steel in marine applications, with relevant material property values. (14 marks)**

#### (a) Corrosion Behaviour of Aluminium Alloys in Seawater (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Passivation (2 marks):**
Aluminium spontaneously forms a thin, adherent, electrically insulating **Al₂O₃** film in air (thickness 2–10 nm). In seawater, this film hydrates to form **Al₂O₃·H₂O / Al(OH)₃** (Bayerite/Gibbsite), thickness ~10–100 nm. This film is **self-healing** — if mechanically damaged, it reforms within milliseconds in oxygenated water.

**Step 2 — Anodic Reaction (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Al} \longrightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3 e^{-}
$$

**Step 3 — Cathodic Reaction (1 mark):**

$$
\text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} + 4 e^{-} \longrightarrow 4 \text{OH}^{-}
$$

**Step 4 — Film Stability and Breakdown (2 marks):**
- Pitting occurs at Cl⁻ concentrations > ~1000 ppm (typical seawater is ~19000 ppm), at temperatures > 50 °C, or in acidic regions (crevice corrosion).
- Pitting potential: $E_{pit} \approx -0.75\ \text{V (SCE)}$.
- **5xxx series alloys** (Al-Mg) are most resistant because Mg stabilises the Al₂O₃ film.

**Step 5 — Galvanic Compatibility (1 mark):**
In an Al-steel couple, $E_{Al} \approx -0.78\ \text{V}$ vs. $E_{st} \approx -0.60\ \text{V}$. Aluminium acts as the **anode** and corrodes preferentially. Mitigation: **insulating gaskets, zinc/aluminium sacrificial anodes, careful material pairing**.

#### (b) Aluminium Preference for MCMV & Four Advantages (7 marks)

**Why MCMV?** (2 marks)
- MCMVs must have **low magnetic, acoustic, and pressure signatures** to avoid triggering naval mines.
- Aluminium is **non-magnetic** (paramagnetic, relative permeability ≈ 1.000022) and **non-sparking** → ideal hull material.
- Real example: Indian Navy's *Mahe-class MCMV* and several NATO MCMVs use aluminium or glass-reinforced plastic hulls.

**Four Key Advantages (1.25 marks each):**

1. **Lightweight — Density 2700 kg/m³** (vs. 7850 for steel). Specific strength of Al-5083 = 79.6 kN·m/kg vs. 45.2 for steel.
2. **Corrosion Resistance** — Self-healing Al₂O₃ film → ~10× lower corrosion rate in seawater (0.01–0.05 mm/yr vs. 0.1–0.5 mm/yr).
3. **Non-magnetic** — Paramagnetic, ideal for degaussing and MCMV service.
4. **High Recyclability** — Recycling uses only **5% of primary energy**, supporting sustainable ship-design policies of IMO (MARPOL Annex VI).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the formula sheet** (e.g., writing specific strength in MPa without dividing by density) — *loses 1 mark per such instance*.
> 2. **Mentioning only advantages, not limitations** — KTU 2024 explicitly tests the **trade-off understanding**. A balanced answer carries +2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing "aluminium alloy 5083" with "pure aluminium"** — Pure Al has $\sigma_y \approx 30\ \text{MPa}$ (useless for hulls); always cite the **alloy grade** (5xxx/6xxx/7xxx) and its **H-temper** (H116, H321, T6).
> 4. **Writing the formula for corrosion rate without specifying ASTM G1 standard** — Examiners expect the **standard reference** for full credit.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in a 14-mark answer** — A simple flowchart or comparison table is worth **at least 2 marks** in the marking scheme.
> 6. **Writing "aluminium doesn't corrode"** — Wrong. Aluminium *does* corrode, but the **corrosion product (Al₂O₃) is protective**, unlike rust on steel. The distinction is worth 1 mark.
> 7. **Ignoring galvanic corrosion when Al is connected to steel** — This is a **classic 2-mark follow-up question** in KTU exams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 60-Second Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- ✅ Aluminium density = **2700 kg/m³**; steel density = **7850 kg/m³** → weight ratio **0.344**.
- ✅ Al-5083 marine alloy is the most used for ship hulls (5xxx series, Al-Mg).
- ✅ Specific strength of Al-5083 ≈ **79.6 kN·m/kg** (1.76× mild steel).
- ✅ Young's modulus of Al = **70 GPa** (one-third of steel's 210 GPa) → design with **thicker sections**.
- ✅ Equal-stiffness redesign yields **~50% weight saving** (derivation in §3.1).
- ✅ Aluminium forms **self-healing Al₂O₃ passive film** in seawater (2–10 nm in air, hydrates in water).
- ✅ Al is **anodic to steel** in seawater → galvanic corrosion risk; mitigate with **insulation + sacrificial anodes**.
- ✅ Al is **paramagnetic** → MCMV / minesweeper hulls.
- ✅ Pitting potential of Al in seawater ≈ **−0.75 V (SCE)**; Cl⁻ and high T accelerate pitting.
- ✅ Al's **melting point (575–640 °C)** is low → **fire protection** is a mandatory design consideration (insulation, water mist systems).
- ✅ Recycling of Al needs only **5% of primary energy**; steel needs **25–35%**.
- ✅ IMO & Classification Societies (IACS, IRS, DNV, Lloyd's) accept Al-5083, 5086, 5454, 5456, 6061, 6082 for marine structural use.
- ✅ For MCMV → Al hull; for cost-sensitive cargo → mild steel; for ice-strengthened hulls → high-tensile steel (EH36).
- ✅ For LNG / cryogenic service → Al retains toughness at −162 °C (advantage).
- ✅ Numerical ability required: **mass ratio, specific strength, weight-saving % from equal-stiffness derivation, galvanic-current mass loss (Faraday's Law).**
- ✅ Always include a **comparison table + one diagram** in a 14-mark answer.

---

topics.title: Types of Corrosion in Marine Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Corrosion in Marine Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Marine Corrosion?

**Marine corrosion** is defined as the irreversible deterioration and degradation of metallic materials (and certain non-metallic composites) caused by electrochemical, chemical, or biological interactions with the surrounding seawater, marine atmosphere, splash zone, tidal zone, or seabed sediments. It is fundamentally an **electrochemical process** that involves the simultaneous occurrence of an **anodic oxidation reaction** (metal dissolution) and a **cathodic reduction reaction** (oxygen reduction, hydrogen evolution, or water reduction) coupled through an ionic electrolyte (seawater with conductivity ≈ **4 S/m** to 5.5 S/m, average salinity **35 g/kg or 35 PSU**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Marine corrosion is the destructive attack of a metal by chemical or electrochemical reaction with its marine environment, manifesting in distinct morphological forms, each governed by unique metallurgical, environmental, and electrochemical driving forces.*

---

## 1.2 The Seawater Electrolyte — Why Is It So Aggressive?

Seawater is a complex, near-neutral electrolyte (pH ≈ 8.0 – 8.3) with the following key properties that make it uniquely aggressive compared to freshwater or atmospheric exposure:

| Parameter | Typical Value | Corrosion Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Salinity | **35 PSU** | High ionic conductivity → low electrolyte resistance |
| pH | **8.0 – 8.3** | Neutral, favors oxygen reduction kinetics |
| Dissolved O₂ | **5 – 8 mL/L** | Sustains cathodic reaction |
| Electrical Conductivity | **≈ 4.5 S/m** | Promotes galvanic coupling over long distances |
| Temperature | **–2 °C to +35 °C** | Influences biofilm and reaction kinetics |
| Chloride concentration | **≈ 19,000 ppm** | Destroys passive films (pitting trigger) |
| Biological content | Variable | Microbially Influenced Corrosion (MIC) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Analogy — "The Seawater Battery":**
> Imagine two dissimilar metal plates (say, aluminum and copper) accidentally connected and dropped into a bucket of seawater. Almost instantly, the bucket becomes a working battery. The aluminum starts dissolving (it loses mass, develops pits), and hydrogen bubbles start popping on the copper. This is marine corrosion in its purest form. Seawater is not just "salty water" — it is a **highly conductive electrolyte** that closes the electrical circuit of nature, allowing electrons stripped from one metal surface to flow through seawater ions to be consumed on another surface. Every type of marine corrosion we will study is essentially a variation of this simple, but devastating, electrochemical theme.

---

## 1.3 KTU Classification of Marine Corrosion Types

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies corrosion in the marine environment broadly into **two domains**, each containing multiple mechanisms:

### **A. Electrochemical / Wet Corrosion (8 Forms)**
1. Uniform (General) Corrosion
2. Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion
3. Pitting Corrosion
4. Crevice Corrosion
5. Intergranular Corrosion
6. Dealloying (Selective Leaching)
7. Erosion-Corrosion
8. Cavitation Corrosion

### **B. Environmentally-Assisted Cracking (EAC) and Bio-Related (6 Forms)**
9. Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
10. Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)
11. Corrosion Fatigue (CF)
12. Fretting Corrosion
13. Exfoliation Corrosion
14. Microbially Influenced Corrosion (MIC)

Each form exhibits a **distinct morphological fingerprint** (visual appearance, depth profile, location preference) that an experienced KTU examiner or marine surveyor uses as a diagnostic tool.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Classical Forms of Marine Corrosion — Engineering Analysis

### **2.1.1 Uniform (General) Corrosion**

**Definition:** Uniform corrosion is the most common and predictable form, in which the entire exposed metal surface loses material at approximately the same rate, producing a relatively even thickness reduction.

**Marine Context:** The most frequently observed attack on unprotected carbon steel hulls, ballast tank internals, and structural members continuously immersed in seawater. It is **cathodically controlled by oxygen diffusion** through the hydrodynamic boundary layer.

**Mechanism:**

$$
\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \quad (\text{anode})
$$

$$
\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-} \quad (\text{cathode})
$$

The overall rate is limited by the diffusion of dissolved O₂ to the metal surface, described by Fick's first law. In aerated seawater, typical corrosion rates for mild steel are **0.1 – 0.5 mm/year**.

---

### **2.1.2 Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion**

**Definition:** Galvanic corrosion occurs when two metals with different electrochemical potentials are electrically coupled in a common electrolyte, causing preferential accelerated dissolution of the more **active (anodic)** metal.

**Marine Context:** Critical concern in ships where bronze propellers are coupled to steel shafts, stainless steel pumps mate with cast iron housings, and copper-based heat exchangers join to titanium piping.

**The Galvanic Series in Seawater (Memory Aid for KTU Exams):**

> **Most Active (Anode, Corrodes) →**
> Magnesium → Zinc → Aluminum alloys → Mild Steel → Cast Iron → Stainless Steel (active) → Copper → Brass → Bronze → Monel → Stainless Steel (passive) → Titanium → Graphite → Platinum
> **← Most Noble (Cathode, Protected)**

**Key Driver:** The potential difference **ΔE = E_cathode − E_anode**, and the corrosion current **I** is determined by:

$$
I = \frac{\Delta E}{R_a + R_c + R_e + R_m}
$$

Where **R_a, R_c, R_e, R_m** are the anodic, cathodic, electrolyte, and metallic path resistances respectively. In highly conductive seawater, **R_e is small**, so galvanic corrosion propagates over **large distances (up to metres)**, unlike in freshwater.

---

### **2.1.3 Pitting Corrosion**

**Definition:** Pitting corrosion is a highly **localized** form that produces small cavities or "pits" on an otherwise passive surface. It is the **most insidious and dangerous** form because it causes **massive cross-sectional loss with minimal mass change**.

**Marine Context:** Stainless steel propellers, superduplex piping in desalination plants, and aluminium alloy superstructures exposed to chloride-rich marine atmosphere.

**Mechanism — The Autocatalytic Pit:**

A pit nucleates at a passive film defect (e.g., MnS inclusion). Within the pit:
- Metal dissolution produces M^(n+) cations.
- Hydrolysis of M^(n+) generates H⁺ (lowering pH to 1 – 3).
- Cl⁻ ions migrate into the pit to maintain charge neutrality.
- The pit becomes a **self-sustaining occluded cell** with the surrounding passive surface acting as the cathode.

$$
\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2 + 2\text{H}^{+}
$$

The aggressive, acidified, chloride-rich environment inside the pit accelerates attack, often growing at **mm/year vertical** rates with negligible lateral spread.

---

### **2.1.4 Crevice Corrosion**

**Definition:** A localized form occurring in narrow gaps or shielded areas where the electrolyte is stagnant, leading to differential aeration and aggressive chemistry.

**Marine Context:** Bolted joints, gaskets, threaded fasteners, riveted lap joints, and O-ring seal grooves in seawater pumps.

**Mechanism — Differential Aeration Cell:**
- **Outside the crevice:** O₂-rich → Cathode.
- **Inside the crevice:** O₂-depleted → Anode (corrodes).
- Chloride enrichment, acidification, and cation hydrolysis inside the crevice create a **critical crevice solution** analogous to a pit.

The critical geometry is defined by a crevice gap **g < 0.1 mm** (too narrow for bulk diffusion).

---

### **2.1.5 Intergranular Corrosion (IGC)**

**Definition:** Selective attack along the **grain boundaries** of a metal, leaving behind intact but weakened grains. Common in improperly heat-treated austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 304, 316) and aluminium marine alloys (5xxx, 6xxx series).

**Marine Context:** Propeller shafts, seawater pipework, desalination evaporator tubes.

**Mechanism — Sensitization:**
In austenitic stainless steels, exposure to **450 °C – 850 °C** causes chromium to combine with carbon at grain boundaries, precipitating **Cr₂₃C₆**. This depletes the adjacent zone of Cr below the **12% threshold** required for passivation, making it anodic.

> **Stabilization Methods (KTU favorites):**
> - Use **low-carbon grades** (304L, 316L with C ≤ 0.03%).
> - Add **stabilizing elements** like Ti (321) or Nb (347) that preferentially form carbides, preserving Cr in solution.
> - Apply **solution annealing** at 1050 °C followed by rapid quenching.

---

### **2.1.6 Dealloying (Selective Leaching)**

**Definition:** The selective electrochemical removal of the more active alloying element, leaving behind a porous, mechanically weak skeleton of the noble element.

**Marine Context:**
- **Dezincification** of brass (Cu-Zn) — removal of Zn, leaving porous Cu sponge. Very common in **naval brass condenser tubes** and **through-hull fittings**.
- **Denickelification** of Cu-Ni alloys.
- **Graphitic corrosion** of cast iron — Fe matrix dissolves, graphite skeleton remains.

**KTU Classification of Dezincification:**
- **Plug-type dezincification** — local, deep penetration.
- **Layer-type dezincification** — broad, uniform surface attack.

The mechanism is governed by **selective dissolution of Zn** (E° = –0.76 V) from brass, which leaves behind a porous Cu network that retains shape but loses mechanical integrity.

---

### **2.1.7 Erosion-Corrosion**

**Definition:** Accelerated material loss arising from the combined action of electrochemical corrosion and mechanical surface removal by a high-velocity, particle-laden fluid.

**Marine Context:** Fire-fighting system piping, sand-laden seawater intakes, condenser tube inlets where sand/shell debris impacts walls, and bilge piping during pump operation.

**Mechanism:** The hydrodynamic shear of turbulent flow **mechanically strips the protective film** (corrosion product, biofilm, passive oxide) faster than it can reform, exposing fresh metal to fresh electrolyte. Flow velocities above a **critical value (≈ 1 – 4 m/s depending on alloy)** trigger this attack.

Key variables:
- Velocity
- Particle impingement angle (≈ 30°–60° gives deepest attack)
- Turbulence intensity
- Alloy hardness
- Solution chemistry

> **Distinguishing Feature:** Erosion-corrosion produces **directional, scalloped, or horse-shoe-shaped** grooves, with shiny metal surfaces free of corrosion product — quite unlike the bulky deposits of uniform corrosion.

---

### **2.1.8 Cavitation Corrosion**

**Definition:** A special form of erosion-corrosion caused by the formation and violent collapse of **vapor bubbles** in a high-velocity liquid at regions of low local pressure (Bernoulli effect).

**Marine Context:** Propeller blades (especially near tips), diesel engine cylinder liners, pump impellers, and high-speed ship sonar domes.

**Mechanism:** When local pressure drops below the **vapor pressure of water (≈ 2.3 kPa at 20 °C)**, vapor bubbles nucleate. When these bubbles collapse in a higher-pressure region, they generate **shock pressures of up to 100 MPa**, sufficient to mechanically deform and rip out metal grains, which are then chemically attacked by the surrounding seawater.

The collapse pressure estimate is given by:

$$
P_{\text{collapse}} = P_0 \cdot \left( \frac{P_0}{P_v} \right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}
$$

Where **P_0** is the ambient pressure, **P_v** is the vapor pressure, and **γ = 1.4** is the isentropic exponent for water vapor.

---

## 2.2 The Six EAC and Bio-Related Forms — Engineering Analysis

### **2.2.1 Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)**

**Definition:** Brittle fracture of a susceptible alloy under the **simultaneous action** of a tensile stress and a specific corrosive environment. Neither the stress alone nor the environment alone causes failure — both are required.

**Marine Context:**
- **Chloride SCC of austenitic SS** (e.g., 304 in warm seawater > 50 °C) — transgranular.
- **Caustic SCC** of carbon steel in boiler tubes (alkaline environment).
- **Sulfide SCC** of high-strength marine bolts.

**KTU-required Models:**
- **Slip-dissolution model (Ford-Andresen):** Repeated film rupture at slip steps exposes fresh metal that dissolves faster than it can re-passivate.
- **Film-induced cleavage model:** Hydrogen from the cathodic reaction enters the metal and cleaves along {100} planes.
- **Adsorption-induced localized slip model:** Surface adsorption lowers cohesion.

The threshold stress is denoted **K_ISCC** (stress intensity for SCC) and is a critical design parameter.

---

### **2.2.2 Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)**

**Definition:** Loss of ductility and fracture resistance of a metal caused by the absorption of **atomic hydrogen (H)** into its lattice, often produced by cathodic polarization (e.g., during cathodic protection or acid pickling).

**Marine Context:** High-strength steel chains, mooring lines, subsea bolts, welded marine structures. Severity increases with **tensile strength > 1000 MPa**.

**Mechanism:** Atomic H diffuses into the lattice, concentrates at **traps** (dislocations, voids, MnS inclusions, prior austenite grain boundaries), reduces cohesive energy of iron, and leads to brittle **H-induced cracking (HIC)** or **delayed fracture** after the load is held for hours to days.

> **KTU Pitfall:** Always distinguish **HE (internal H effect)** from **HIC (H-Induced Cracking from bulk sulfide inclusions in sour service)**. Examiners love to test this.

---

### **2.2.3 Corrosion Fatigue (CF)**

**Definition:** The synergistic degradation of a metal subjected to **cyclic loading** in a corrosive environment, leading to crack initiation at corrosion pits and accelerated crack growth.

**Marine Context:** Ship propeller shafts (rotating bending), offshore platform risers in waves, drill-string in offshore drilling.

**Mechanism:** Corrosion pits act as **stress concentrators**, reducing the effective fatigue threshold. The fatigue limit that exists in air (e.g., S-N knee at 10⁶–10⁷ cycles for steel) **disappears** in seawater — corrosion is unbounded.

The empirical Paris law for crack growth rate in corrosive environments:

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C \left( \Delta K \right)^m
$$

Where **a** is crack length, **N** is cycle count, **ΔK** is the stress intensity range, and **C, m** are environmentally and metallurgically dependent.

---

### **2.2.4 Fretting Corrosion**

**Definition:** Surface damage occurring at the **contact area of two materials undergoing small-amplitude relative motion** (typically < 100 μm) under load, with simultaneous chemical attack.

**Marine Context:** Bearing seats, flanged connections of seawater piping under vibration, bolted propeller hubs, ball-and-socket joints of offshore platforms.

**Mechanism:** Microscopic slip disrupts the protective oxide film, exposing fresh metal that oxidizes rapidly. The trapped oxide debris acts as an **abrasive** that accelerates wear. The simultaneous chemical and mechanical action creates pits surrounded by coloured oxide debris (red-brown for iron).

---

### **2.2.5 Exfoliation Corrosion**

**Definition:** A form of subsurface attack that proceeds **along grain boundaries parallel to the rolled/extruded surface**, causing layers of metal to peel off like pages of a book.

**Marine Context:** Rolled aluminium-magnesium plates (5083, 5086) used in patrol boat hulls, marine extrusions.

**Mechanism:** In wrought aluminium alloys, elongated grain boundaries in the rolling direction become preferential anodic paths. Corrosion products (Al(OH)₃) have a **larger molar volume** than the parent metal, generating **wedging stress** that mechanically lifts surface layers.

---

### **2.2.6 Microbially Influenced Corrosion (MIC)**

**Definition:** Corrosion accelerated or directly caused by the metabolic activity of microorganisms (bacteria, archaea, fungi, algae) on metal surfaces.

**Marine Context:** Ballast tanks, bilges, fire-main piping with stagnant water, heat exchangers, offshore subsea structures, jetty piles.

**Key Organisms — The KTU "Big Four":**

| Microbe | Type | Corrosion Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **SRB** (Sulfate-Reducing Bacteria — *Desulfovibrio*) | Anaerobic | Reduces SO₄²⁻ → S²⁻ → FeS, severe pitting, **10× rate increase** |
| **APB** (Acid-Producing Bacteria) | Aerobic/Anaerobic | Generates organic acids → pH < 2 → uniform attack |
| **IOB** (Iron-Oxidizing Bacteria) | Aerobic | Oxidizes Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺, forms tubercles, O₂ differentials |
| **SOB** (Sulfur-Oxidizing Bacteria) | Aerobic | Oxidizes S²⁻ → H₂SO₄ (concentrated, pH ~ 0.5) |

> **Diagnostic Clue for KTU:** MIC produces **clusters of shallow, smooth-edged pits** often under hard, dark-coloured tubercles (FeS mounds) — in contrast to the sharp, jagged crystalline pits of chloride pitting.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The following equations are the absolute minimum required for problem-solving in Module 2. Memorize the units, sign conventions, and applicability limits.

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Standard Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta E = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Driving EMF of galvanic corrosion | V (volts) | Galvanic corrosion — sign of potential difference |
| 2 | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_a + R_c + R_e + R_m}$ | Mixed potential corrosion current | A (amperes) | Galvanic corrosion rate |
| 3 | $\eta = a + b \log \vert i \vert$ | Tafel equation (Butler-Volmer) | V, A/m² | Polarization behaviour |
| 4 | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln a_{M^{n+}}$ | Nernst equation for equilibrium potential | V | Pourbaix diagram construction |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{M \cdot i}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Corrosion rate from corrosion current density | mm/year | Converting i_corr to mm/yr |
| 6 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law of mass loss | kg | Mass loss from corrosion current |
| 7 | $P_{\text{collapse}} = P_0 \left( \dfrac{P_0}{P_v} \right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | Vapor bubble collapse pressure | Pa | Cavitation corrosion intensity |
| 8 | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$ | Paris law for fatigue crack growth | m/cycle | Corrosion fatigue life |
| 9 | $i_L = \dfrac{n F D C}{\delta}$ | Limiting current density (oxygen reduction) | A/m² | Cathodic control of uniform corrosion |
| 10 | $K_{\text{ISCC}} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress intensity for SCC | MPa√m | Critical crack length for SCC |

Where the symbols in the above are:

- **R** = 8.314 J/(mol·K) (universal gas constant)
- **F** = 96,485 C/mol (Faraday's constant)
- **n** = charge number (electrons transferred)
- **M** = molar mass of metal
- **ρ** = density of metal
- **D** = diffusion coefficient
- **δ** = diffusion boundary layer thickness
- **Y** = geometric factor (≈ 1.0 for surface crack, ≈ 1.12 for through-thickness)
- **a, b** = Tafel constants (empirical; a ≈ 0.05–0.15 V; b ≈ 0.04–0.12 V/decade)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Corrosion Type Identification

| Marine Application | Critical Corrosion Type | Design Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Ship hull (mild steel) | Uniform + galvanic + pitting | Cathodic protection + coating |
| Stainless propeller | SCC + pitting + cavitation | Use duplex SS, limit Cl⁻ + T, smooth finish |
| Aluminium superstructure | Pitting + exfoliation | Use 5xxx H116/H321 tempers, anodizing |
| Bronze through-hull | Dezincification | Use naval brass with **Sn + As** inhibitors |
| Ballast tanks | Uniform + MIC + pitting | Hard coatings, biocide dosing, ICCP |
| Seawater piping | Erosion-corrosion + pitting | Limit velocity, use Cu-Ni 90/10 or 70/30 |
| Mooring chains | HE + corrosion fatigue | Use R4-grade quenched & tempered steel, NDT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** Identifying the corrosion type is **80% of the solution**. Once identified, standard mitigation strategies are textbook — examiners award full marks only if the diagnosis is correct.
