# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Corrosion Rate from Corrosion Current Density (Faraday's Law)

This is the **most-frequently-asked KTU 2024 numerical problem**. Let us derive it step-by-step.

### **Setup**
The corrosion current density **i_corr** (A/m²) is obtained from a Tafel extrapolation of a polarization curve. We want to convert this to an engineering-friendly corrosion rate **CR** in mm/year (or mpy — mils per year).

### **Step 1: Write the Fundamental Faraday Equation**

The mass of metal dissolved per unit area per unit time is given by Faraday's law of electrolysis:

$$
m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

For current density (per unit area), divide both sides by area **A** and time **t**:

$$
\frac{m}{A \cdot t} = \frac{i_{\text{corr}} \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

The left-hand side is the mass-loss rate per unit area, call it **ṁ** (kg/(m²·s)).

### **Step 2: Convert Mass Loss to Thickness Loss (Penetration Depth)**

Penetration depth is mass-loss rate divided by density **ρ** of the metal:

$$
CR_{\text{linear}} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{i_{\text{corr}} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}
$$

This gives units of m/s, which is inconvenient for engineering.

### **Step 3: Convert m/s to mm/year**

There are **1000 mm in 1 m** and **31,536,000 s** in 1 year (365.25 × 24 × 3600). So:

$$
1 \text{ m/s} = 1 \times 10^3 \text{ mm/m} \times 31.536 \times 10^6 \text{ s/year} = 3.1536 \times 10^{10} \text{ mm/year}
$$

Therefore, the final conversion factor from **A/m²** to **mm/year** is:

$$
CR \text{ [mm/year]} = \frac{i_{\text{corr}} \cdot M \cdot 3.1536 \times 10^{10}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot 10^6}
$$

After simplification with **F = 96,485 C/mol**:

$$
CR \text{ [mm/year]} = \frac{i_{\text{corr}} \cdot M \cdot K}{n \cdot \rho}
$$

Where the **conversion constant K = 3.27 × 10⁻³ mm·g/(µA·cm²·year)** when i_corr is in **µA/cm²** and CR is in **mm/year**.

### **Step 4: Final Convenient Form**

$$
\boxed{CR \text{ [mm/year]} = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{i_{\text{corr}} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}}
$$

> **Note:** For the alternative unit **mpy** (mils per year, 1 mil = 0.001 inch = 0.0254 mm), the constant becomes **K = 0.129** when i_corr is in **µA/cm²** and **M/(n·ρ)** is in **cm³/(eq·mol)**. The relationship is **1 mm/yr ≈ 39.4 mpy**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Tafel Slope from Butler-Volmer Equation

The Tafel equation is essential for interpreting polarization curves and Evans diagrams. Let us derive it from electrochemical kinetics.

### **Step 1: Start with the Butler-Volmer Equation**

The net current density across an electrode-electrolyte interface is the sum of anodic (oxidation) and cathodic (reduction) contributions:

$$
i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right) - \exp\left( \frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right) \right]
$$

Where:
- **i_0** = exchange current density
- **α_a, α_c** = charge transfer coefficients (typically 0.5 each)
- **η** = overpotential = E − E_eq
- **R, T, F** = gas constant, absolute temperature, Faraday's constant

### **Step 2: Apply the Large Overpotential Limit (Tafel Approximation)**

For **η ≫ RT/F ≈ 25.7 mV at 25 °C**, the cathodic exponential becomes negligible, leaving:

$$
i \approx i_0 \exp\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right)
$$

### **Step 3: Take the Natural Logarithm of Both Sides**

$$
\ln i = \ln i_0 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}
$$

### **Step 4: Convert to Base-10 Logarithm (Standard Engineering Form)**

Using **ln x = 2.303 log x**:

$$
\log i = \log i_0 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{2.303 \, RT}
$$

### **Step 5: Solve for Overpotential (Tafel Form)**

Rearranging:

$$
\eta = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log i - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log i_0
$$

In the canonical Tafel form:

$$
\boxed{\eta = a + b \log \vert i \vert}
$$

Where the Tafel slope is:

$$
b_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F}
$$

At **25 °C (T = 298 K)** with **α_a = 0.5**:

$$
b_a = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{0.5 \times 96485} \approx 0.118 \text{ V/decade}
$$

This is the classic anodic Tafel slope for iron in aerated neutral solutions — a value **directly cited in KTU problems**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Tafel Extrapolation Method for i_corr

This derivation explains **how i_corr is read from a polarization curve** — a standard 7-mark KTU problem.

### **Step 1: Write the Anodic and Cathodic Tafel Equations**

Anodic branch:

$$
\eta_a = b_a \log \left( \frac{i}{i_0} \right)
$$

Cathodic branch:

$$
-\eta_c = b_c \log \left( \frac{i}{i_0} \right)
$$

### **Step 2: Express Total Cell Polarization**

For a corroding system with no external current, the total polarization **E_corr − E_0** is zero for the mixed electrode, but the local anodic and cathodic currents both equal **i_corr** in magnitude.

### **Step 3: Wagner-Traud Mixed-Potential Formulation**

The corrosion current density **i_corr** is found at the intersection of the anodic and cathodic Tafel lines on a **log i vs E** (Evans diagram). At this intersection:

$$
E_{\text{corr}} = b_a \log i_{\text{corr}} - b_a \log i_{0,a} = b_c \log i_{0,c} - b_c \log i_{\text{corr}}
$$

### **Step 4: Solve for i_corr**

Equating the two expressions for **E_corr** and rearranging:

$$
(b_a + b_c) \log i_{\text{corr}} = b_a \log i_{0,a} + b_c \log i_{0,c}
$$

$$
\boxed{i_{\text{corr}} = i_{0,a}^{b_a/(b_a + b_c)} \cdot i_{0,c}^{b_c/(b_a + b_c)}}
$$

> **KTU Tip:** When **b_a = b_c = b** (symmetric), this simplifies to **i_corr = √(i_{0,a} · i_{0,c})**, the **geometric mean** of the two exchange current densities.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Limiting Current Density for Oxygen Reduction in Stagnant Seawater

This controls uniform corrosion rate in the cathodic region.

### **Step 1: Fick's First Law for Steady-State Diffusion**

$$
J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}
$$

Where **J** is the molar flux (mol/(m²·s)), **D** is the diffusion coefficient of O₂ in seawater (≈ 2 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s at 25 °C), and **dC/dx** is the concentration gradient.

### **Step 2: Apply Linear Gradient Approximation (Nernst Diffusion Layer)**

The concentration drops linearly from bulk value **C_b** to zero at the metal surface over a diffusion boundary layer of thickness **δ** (≈ 0.1 – 0.5 mm in quiescent seawater):

$$
J = D \frac{C_b}{\delta}
$$

### **Step 3: Convert to Current Density Using Faraday's Law**

The cathodic reaction is **O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻**, so **n = 4** electrons per O₂ molecule:

$$
i_L = n F J = \frac{4 F D C_b}{\delta}
$$

### **Step 4: Insert Typical Marine Values**

For **C_b = 0.25 mol/m³** (= 8 mg/L ÷ 32 g/mol), **D = 2 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s**, **δ = 0.3 mm = 3 × 10⁻⁴ m**, **F = 96485 C/mol**:

$$
i_L = \frac{4 \times 96485 \times 2 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.25}{3 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
i_L \approx 6.4 \times 10^{-1} \text{ A/m}^2 \approx 64 \text{ µA/cm}^2
$$

This corresponds to a uniform corrosion rate of:

$$
CR = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{64 \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87} \approx 0.74 \text{ mm/year}
$$

— well within the typical **0.1 – 1.0 mm/year** range reported for mild steel in quiescent seawater.

---

## 3.5 Derivation V: Galvanic Corrosion Current from Ohm's Law on Mixed-Metal Coupling

### **Step 1: Couple the Two Polarization Equations**

When metal **A** (anode) is coupled to metal **B** (cathode), the operating point is found where the anodic current of A equals the cathodic current of B:

$$
i_{\text{anodic},A} = i_{\text{cathodic},B} = I_{\text{galvanic}}
$$

### **Step 2: Write Tafel Expressions for Both**

$$
i_{0,A} \cdot 10^{(\eta - E_A^0)/b_{a,A}} = i_{0,B} \cdot 10^{(E_B^0 - \eta)/b_{c,B}}
$$

### **Step 3: Solve for the Coupled Potential η_coupled**

After logarithmic manipulation (setting **η = E_coupled**):

$$
E_{\text{coupled}} = \frac{b_{a,A} \cdot b_{c,B}}{b_{a,A} + b_{c,B}} \cdot \left[ \frac{E_B^0 - E_A^0}{b_{a,A} \cdot b_{c,B}} + \log \left( \frac{i_{0,A}}{i_{0,B}} \right) \right] + E_A^0
$$

### **Step 4: Compute Galvanic Current Density**

The galvanic current is then:

$$
\boxed{I_{\text{galvanic}} = i_{0,A} \cdot 10^{(E_{\text{coupled}} - E_A^0)/b_{a,A}}}
$$

> **KTU Pearl:** This shows that increasing **b_c,B** (cathode Tafel slope) **decreases** the galvanic current. In practice, the stainless steel cathode has a high Tafel slope, so its O₂ reduction is sluggish, and the resulting galvanic attack is less catastrophic than a simple ΔE calculation would suggest. This is a frequent **conceptual KTU 14-mark question**.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — KTU Pattern Numerical

**Problem:** A seawater ballast tank uses a coupled pair of mild steel (anode, E_A⁰ = –0.61 V) and 70/30 Cu-Ni alloy (cathode, E_B⁰ = –0.25 V vs SCE). The area ratio is **A_A:A_B = 10:1**, exchange current densities are **i₀,A = 10⁻⁶ A/m²**, **i₀,B = 10⁻⁴ A/m²**, and the Tafel slopes are **b_a,A = 0.06 V/dec**, **b_c,B = 0.12 V/dec**. Seawater resistance is negligible.

**Find:** (a) The coupled corrosion current density, (b) The corrosion rate of the steel anode in mm/year, (c) Predict which zone (anode or cathode) will dominate tank failure.

### **Solution:**

**Step (a) — Coupled Potential:**

$$
E_{\text{coupled}} = \frac{(0.06)(0.12)}{0.18} \left[ \frac{-0.25 - (-0.61)}{0.06 \times 0.12} + \log \left( \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-4}} \right) \right] + (-0.61)
$$

$$
E_{\text{coupled}} = 0.04 \left[ \frac{0.36}{0.0072} + (-2) \right] - 0.61
$$

$$
E_{\text{coupled}} = 0.04 \times (50 - 2) - 0.61 = 0.04 \times 48 - 0.61 = 1.92 - 0.61 = 1.31 \text{ V}
$$

> *Note: 1.31 V is non-physical; this indicates the Tafel form is misused for a 0.36 V cell. The proper Tafel form is consistent only when overpotentials are small relative to the Tafel region. For KTU, examiners expect the use of the simpler ΔE-driven Ohm's law model when resistances are negligible:*

**Correct KTU Approach (Ohm's Law with negligible R_e):**

$$
I_{\text{galvanic}} = \frac{E_B^0 - E_A^0}{R_a + R_c + R_m}
$$

With **E_B⁰ − E_A⁰ = 0.36 V** and assuming total internal resistance from the cell to be **R_total = 0.5 Ω** (typical for area 1 m² at 10:1 ratio), then:

$$
I_{\text{galvanic}} = \frac{0.36}{0.5} = 0.72 \text{ A}
$$

The anodic current density on the steel:

$$
i_{\text{anode}} = \frac{0.72}{10 \times 1} = 0.072 \text{ A/m}^2 = 7.2 \, \mu\text{A/cm}^2
$$

**Step (b) — Corrosion Rate in mm/year:**

$$
CR = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{7.2 \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87}
$$

$$
CR = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{402.12}{15.74} = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 25.55 \approx 0.0836 \text{ mm/year}
$$

**Step (c) — Dominant Failure Zone:** The mild steel anode at 0.0836 mm/year is within design tolerance (< 0.1 mm/year) for a properly maintained ballast tank, but the Cu-Ni cathode is essentially protected and will not corrode. **The anode (mild steel) is the failure-zone**, accelerated ~50% over uncoupled immersion rate of 0.05 mm/year.

---

## 3.7 KTU Analytical Matrix — Corrosion Type Identification Flow

The following matrix is the KTU-recommended decision tree for identification in viva or descriptive answers:

| Observed Feature | Most Likely Type | Confirmatory Test |
|---|---|---|
| Even thickness loss | Uniform | Weight loss coupon + visual |
| Pits on passive surface, Cl⁻ present | Pitting | Optical/SEM profilometry |
| Pits at gaskets/bolts, orange deposits | Crevice | Disassemble and inspect |
| Cracking transgranular + Cl⁻ + warm | SCC (Cl⁻) | Slow strain rate test (SSRT) |
| Cracking intergranular | IGC | ASTM A262 Practice A–E |
| Brassy color, porous copper remaining | Dezincification | Optical microscopy |
| Grooves in flow direction | Erosion-corrosion | Velocity + profilometry |
| Holes/cavities on propeller tips | Cavitation | Visual + Reynolds calc |
| Brown oxide debris, vibration source | Fretting | Microscopy + vibration log |
| Layered peeling on Al plate | Exfoliation | Metallographic cross-section |
| Black tubercles, pH 1–3 under them | MIC | Microbiological culture |
| Cracks under cyclic load in seawater | Corrosion fatigue | ΔK-da/dN curve |
| Sudden brittle failure of high-strength steel | HE | Hydrogen analysis + fracture surface |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between uniform corrosion and pitting corrosion in the marine environment. Provide two diagnostic features of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Uniform Corrosion | Pitting Corrosion |
|---|---|---|
| **Distribution** | Even attack across entire exposed surface | Localized to discrete cavities |
| **Predictability** | Highly predictable by weight-loss coupon | Unpredictable; pits may penetrate deeply with negligible mass loss |
| **Diagnostic Markers** | Generalized thinning; uniform rust/oxide layer | Small surface opening leading to deep cavity; often covered by corrosion product cap |
| **Mechanism** | Cathodically controlled by O₂ diffusion | Autocatalytic; Cl⁻-enriched, low-pH pit interior |
| **Marine Example** | Mild-steel hull in quiescent immersion | Stainless steel propeller blade in Cl⁻-rich water |
| **Rate Metric** | 0.1 – 0.5 mm/year (mild steel) | Penetration rate up to several mm/year; pitting factor (depth / average) > 5 |

**Mark Distribution (KTU Style):**
- 1 mark — clear definition and contrast.
- 1 mark — two diagnostic features.
- 1 mark — marine example for each.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**What is dezincification? Why is it specifically a concern for naval brass condenser tubes in seawater service?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Dezincification** is a form of dealloying in which the zinc component of a Cu-Zn brass alloy (typically 60/40 or 70/30) is selectively leached out by the corrosive action of seawater, leaving behind a porous, mechanically weak, reddish sponge of copper that retains the original shape but loses all structural integrity.

**Why it concerns naval brass tubes:**
1. **Cooling service in seawater:** Naval brass (Cu 60%, Zn 39%, Sn 1%) was historically the workhorse material for marine condenser and heat-exchanger tubes due to good thermal conductivity. The combined effect of hot seawater + galvanic coupling to tube-sheets (often Muntz metal) creates ideal dezincification conditions.
2. **Internal flow + stagnation:** The combination of low velocity, biofilm, and differential aeration initiates selective Zn dissolution.
3. **Mechanical failure mode:** Once dezincified, the tube wall is mechanically weak. Differential thermal expansion or water-hammer shock can cause sudden tube rupture, leading to **seawater ingress into the boiler/condensate system**, a critical safety and operational hazard.
4. **Modern mitigation:** Use of **inhibited brass** with **0.02 – 0.06% arsenic (As)** or **antimony (Sb)** addition, or migration to **Cu-Ni 90/10 or 70/30** alloys that do not suffer from dealloying.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark — Definition with chemistry.
- 1 mark — Marine operational hazard explained.
- 1 mark — Mitigation strategy cited.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, typically split as **7 + 7** (or sometimes 8 + 6). Internal choice is mandatory. Below are **two fully solved parallel questions** representing the choice.

---

### **Question B — Choice (A) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, modified 2024 scheme]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain, with a neat sketch and a worked numerical example, how the Tafel extrapolation method is used to determine the **corrosion current density (i_corr)** and the **corrosion rate** of a marine structural steel in seawater from a polarization curve. State the necessary assumptions and limitations of the method.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the mechanism of **chloride-induced stress corrosion cracking (Cl-SCC)** in austenitic stainless steels used for marine pump shafts. Discuss the role of (i) temperature, (ii) chloride concentration, (iii) applied stress, and (iv) material microstructure in governing susceptibility.

---

#### **Model Solution to B(A)(a):**

**Tafel Extrapolation Method — Step-by-Step Derivation and Procedure**

**Step 1 — Experimental Setup:**
A potentiostat applies a controlled potential to a working electrode (the marine steel coupon immersed in seawater at controlled temperature, typically 25 °C) versus a reference electrode (SCE or Ag/AgCl), and the resulting current is measured via a counter electrode (Pt mesh). The applied potential is swept slowly (≈ 0.1 – 1 mV/s) in both anodic and cathodic directions from the **open-circuit potential (OCP or E_corr)**.

**Step 2 — Plotting the Polarization Curve:**
The data is plotted as **E (V)** on the y-axis versus **log |i| (A/cm²)** on the x-axis. The curve has three regions:
- Cathodic Tafel region (η < –120 mV)
- Mixed-control region (–120 mV < η < +120 mV)
- Anodic Tafel region (η > +120 mV)

**Step 3 — Tafel Slopes from Linear Extrapolation:**
The linear segments of the anodic and cathodic branches are extrapolated back to the corrosion potential **E_corr**. Their intercepts with **E_corr** give the corrosion current density **i_corr** on the log scale.

**Step 4 — Mathematical Relationship:**
The anodic and cathodic Tafel equations at the corrosion potential:

$$
E_{\text{corr}} - E_A^0 = b_a \log \left( \frac{i_{\text{corr}}}{i_{0,a}} \right)
$$

$$
E_B^0 - E_{\text{corr}} = b_c \log \left( \frac{i_{\text{corr}}}{i_{0,c}} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:**

For a typical structural marine steel in aerated seawater at 25 °C, the Tafel extrapolation gives:
- E_corr = –0.62 V (vs SCE)
- Anodic Tafel slope b_a = 0.060 V/decade
- Cathodic Tafel slope b_c = 0.120 V/decade
- Intersection log(i_corr) = –4.0 → **i_corr = 10⁻⁴ A/cm² = 100 µA/cm²**

**Step 6 — Conversion to Corrosion Rate:**

$$
CR = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{i_{\text{corr}} \times M}{n \times \rho}
$$

With **i_corr = 100 µA/cm²**, **M_Fe = 55.85 g/mol**, **n = 2**, **ρ_Fe = 7.87 g/cm³**:

$$
CR = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{100 \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87} = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 354.8 = 1.16 \text{ mm/year}
$$

**Assumptions and Limitations (KTU-required, 2 marks):**
1. The Tafel regions are linear — requires overpotential > 50 mV.
2. Activation polarization is the only rate-controlling step — ignores concentration polarization at high currents.
3. No change in corrosion mechanism during the scan (e.g., passive film breakdown).
4. Single anodic and single cathodic reaction — true only for simple systems.
5. Steady-state conditions — sweep rate must be slow enough.

---

#### **Model Solution to B(A)(b):**

**Chloride-Induced Stress Corrosion Cracking in Austenitic Stainless Steel (Marine Pump Shaft)**

**Mechanism (3 marks):**

Chloride-induced SCC in austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 304, 316) follows a **transgranular** path with **branching cracks** that originate at the surface. The mechanism is best described by the **slip-dissolution model (Ford-Andresen)**:

1. The passive Cr₂O₃ film on the steel surface is locally ruptured by mechanical slip-step emergence under tensile stress.
2. The exposed bare metal dissolves rapidly in the Cl⁻-rich aerated electrolyte, generating a sharp notch.
3. The notch tip re-passivates, the process repeats, and a crack advances incrementally at a rate typically 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴ mm/s.
4. Hydrogen generated by the cathodic reaction (especially at low pH in the crack) diffuses into the metal, embrittling the crack tip and accelerating propagation via the **film-induced cleavage mechanism**.

**Governing Factors (4 marks, 1 each):**

**(i) Temperature:**
Critical. Cl-SCC rarely occurs below **50 °C** in neutral seawater. Above 60 °C, the failure probability increases dramatically. Marine pumps handling warm seawater (engine-room cooling pumps, desalination brine pumps) are particularly vulnerable. **Mitigation: keep T < 50 °C** or upgrade to **duplex (2205) or super-duplex (2507)** stainless steels.

**(ii) Chloride Concentration:**
Threshold for SCC initiation is typically **Cl⁻ > 50 – 100 ppm** in aerated, warm water. Seawater at 19,000 ppm Cl⁻ is well above this, making it a guaranteed SCC environment. The role of Cl⁻ is to depassivate the crack tip and prevent re-passivation.

**(iii) Applied Stress:**
The stress must be **tensile** (residual or service-applied) and must exceed a threshold stress intensity **K_ISCC**, typically **15 – 30 MPa√m** for 304 SS in Cl⁻. Stress relief, proper welding procedures, and shot peening to introduce compressive surface stresses are key mitigations.

**(iv) Material Microstructure:**
- **Austenite stability:** 304 is highly susceptible due to metastable austenite that can transform to brittle martensite at crack tips. **316 (with Mo)** is slightly better due to improved pitting resistance (PREN > 25).
- **Grain size:** Coarse grains increase SCC susceptibility.
- **Cold work:** Introduces residual tensile stress and dislocation density that promotes slip-step formation.
- **Duplex SS:** Two-phase ferrite-austenite microstructure offers **far superior SCC resistance** — preferred marine pump shaft material.

**Conclusion (best practice for KTU):** For marine pump shafts, replace 304/316 with **super-duplex 2507 (PREN > 42)** if T > 50 °C and Cl⁻ is high, or use **Ni-Cr-Mo alloys (Alloy 625, C-276)** for extreme service.

---

### **Question B — Choice (B) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, modified 2024 scheme]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Classify the **eight forms of electrochemical marine corrosion** with one diagnostic feature and one marine engineering example for each. Prepare the classification as a **comparison table**.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **autocatalytic mechanism of pitting corrosion** in stainless steel exposed to seawater. How does the **pitting resistance equivalent number (PREN)** of an alloy govern its susceptibility? Calculate the PREN of (i) SS 304, (ii) SS 316, (iii) Duplex SS 2205, given:

> **PREN = %Cr + 3.3 × %Mo + 16 × %N**

---

#### **Model Solution to B(B)(a):**

**Classification of Electrochemical Marine Corrosion (8 Forms) — 7-Mark Table**

| # | Type | Diagnostic Feature | Marine Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Uniform (General)** | Even thickness reduction across entire surface | Unprotected mild-steel hull plate, ballast tank internals |
| 2 | **Galvanic (Bimetallic)** | Severe localized attack on the active metal near the couple junction | Bronze propeller on steel shaft, SS pump in cast-iron body |
| 3 | **Pitting** | Small surface opening leading to deep, narrow cavity; autocatalytic | SS 304 seawater pipe elbow, SS propeller blade |
| 4 | **Crevice** | Attack confined to narrow gaps, outside is cathodic and clean | Bolted lap joints, gasketed flanges, threaded fasteners |
| 5 | **Intergranular (IGC)** | Cracking along grain boundaries; visible after etching | Sensitized 304 SS pipe weld HAZ, Al-Mg alloy plate |
| 6 | **Dealloying (Dezincification)** | Original shape retained, but porous and weak; colour change to red | Naval brass condenser tubes, Muntz metal pipe fittings |
| 7 | **Erosion-Corrosion** | Directional grooves, scallops, or horseshoe patterns; shiny metal | Sand-laden seawater inlet pipe, fire-main tee junction |
| 8 | **Cavitation** | Honeycomb or sponge-like cavities on high-RPM surfaces | Propeller tips (esp. supercavitating), pump impellers |

**Note for marking:**
- 5 marks distributed across the table (½ mark per type for accurate feature, ¼ mark for example).
- 1 mark for completeness of coverage and neatness.
- 1 mark for any one additional correct insight (e.g., distinguishing passivating vs. non-passivating alloys).

---

#### **Model Solution to B(B)(b):**

**Autocatalytic Mechanism of Pitting Corrosion (3 marks):**

A pit initiates at a **passive-film defect** (e.g., MnS inclusion, scratch, weld undercut) on the SS surface exposed to aerated Cl⁻-rich seawater. The sequence of events:

1. **Pit nucleation:** Cl⁻ ions adsorb on the passive Cr₂O₃ film at the defect site and locally dissolve the chromium oxide, exposing bare Fe-Cr-Ni metal.

2. **Anodic dissolution inside the pit:** The bare metal oxidizes:
   $$\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$
   $$\text{Cr} \rightarrow \text{Cr}^{3+} + 3e^{-}$$

3. **Hydrolysis-driven acidification:** Metal cations hydrolyze, releasing H⁺:
   $$\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2 + 2\text{H}^{+}$$
   $$\text{Cr}^{3+} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cr(OH)}_3 + 3\text{H}^{+}$$
   The pH inside the pit drops to **1 – 3**.

4. **Chloride migration:** To maintain electroneutrality, Cl⁻ ions from bulk seawater migrate into the pit, forming concentrated FeCl₂, CrCl₃ solutions (Cl⁻ > 5 M).

5. **Cathodic reaction outside:** On the surrounding passive surface, oxygen reduction proceeds:
   $$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$$

6. **Autocatalytic acceleration:** The local acidification and Cl⁻ enrichment prevent re-passivation, sustaining rapid metal dissolution. The pit grows **vertically into the metal**, often at **mm/year rates**, while the surface opening remains small (a "subsurface" pit).

**Role of PREN — Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (2 marks):**

The **PREN** quantifies an alloy's resistance to pitting based on its key alloying chemistry. Higher PREN → higher pitting resistance.

**Given:** **PREN = %Cr + 3.3 × %Mo + 16 × %N**

**Composition Table:**

| Alloy | %Cr | %Mo | %N | PREN Calculation | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SS 304** | 18.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 18.0 + 0 + 0 | **18.0** |
| **SS 316** | 17.0 | 2.5 | 0.0 | 17.0 + 3.3 × 2.5 + 0 | **25.25** |
| **Duplex SS 2205** | 22.0 | 3.0 | 0.15 | 22.0 + 9.9 + 2.4 | **34.3** |

**Critical PREN Thresholds in Seawater Service:**
- PREN < 25: **High pitting risk** (e.g., 304).
- PREN 25 – 32: **Moderate pitting risk** (e.g., 316, 904L).
- PREN > 32: **Low pitting risk in marine ambient** (e.g., 2205 duplex, 2507 super-duplex).
- PREN > 40: **Required for hot, stagnant, or deep-well seawater service** (e.g., 254 SMO, AL-6XN, Alloy 625).

**Conclusion:** For seawater service at moderate temperature, the KTU-recommended minimum is **PREN ≥ 32** (duplex 2205). Below this value, pitting is statistically likely within 5 – 10 years of service.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks (Module 2, Marine Corrosion Types):**
>
> 1. **Confusing pitting and crevice corrosion:** Both produce localized cavities, but **pitting** initiates on **open surfaces** at film defects, while **crevice** is **confined to shielded gaps** (g < 0.1 mm). Examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** for mixing these.
>
> 2. **Using "stainless steel" without specifying grade:** When asked about corrosion susceptibility, never say "stainless steel corrodes"; specify the **grade (304, 316, 2205, 2507)** and its **PREN**. Generic answers lose 1 – 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Mixing up stress corrosion cracking (SCC) and hydrogen embrittlement (HE):** SCC requires a **specific environment + tensile stress**; HE requires **hydrogen ingress + high strength**. A 7-mark answer that conflates the two will lose 2 – 3 marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the cathodic reaction in corrosion mechanism answers:** Anode reaction alone is **not** corrosion. Always write both half-cell reactions and the electron-flow path.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the role of biofilm in marine corrosion:** Seawater has biological activity. A complete KTU answer should mention that the **first 24 hours** of marine immersion forms a **conditioning film → biofilm → calcareous deposit** sequence that fundamentally alters the corrosion kinetics. Omitting this loses a 1-mark "depth" credit.
>
> 6. **Using "corrosion rate" without units:** Always specify **mm/year** or **mpy** and state the measurement basis (weight loss, Tafel, linear polarization resistance).
>
> 7. **Not stating assumptions in Tafel/derivation problems:** Tafel extrapolation assumes **activation-controlled, single-step, no concentration polarization**. A full-marks answer must list these.
>
> 8. **Galvanic series ≠ EMF series:** The galvanic series is **measured in actual seawater** and reflects practical behaviour (e.g., passive SS is noble, active SS is active). The EMF series is **thermodynamic**, in standard conditions, and **does not predict** marine galvanic behaviour accurately. Examiners will deduct marks for confusing them.
>
> 9. **Forgetting that 2205 duplex is magnetic, 316 is not:** When asked about alloy selection for marine service, mention magnetic-permeability consequences (e.g., degaussing, naval mine signature).
>
> 10. **Not including a sketch in 14-mark answers:** A 14-mark question is expected to have a **labelled diagram** worth **1 – 2 marks**. Skipping the sketch costs a guaranteed 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — Rapid Revision Checklist (Types of Marine Corrosion):**
>
> - **Master the 14 corrosion types** (8 electrochemical + 6 EAC/bio) with **one diagnostic feature** for each.
> - **Memorize the marine galvanic series** in order: Mg → Zn → Al → Steel → Cast iron → SS (active) → Cu → Brass → Bronze → Monel → SS (passive) → Ti → Graphite → Pt.
> - **Always pair anode + cathode reactions** when writing corrosion mechanism answers.
> - **PREN = %Cr + 3.3%Mo + 16%N** is the key pitting metric. PREN ≥ 32 for duplex SS, ≥ 40 for hot/stagnant seawater.
> - **Seawater properties** to memorize: pH ≈ 8.2, Cl⁻ ≈ 19,000 ppm, O₂ ≈ 5–8 mL/L, salinity = 35 PSU, conductivity ≈ 4.5 S/m.
> - **Cl⁻ threshold for SCC of 304 SS:** T > 50 °C and Cl⁻ > 50 ppm. Below these, SCC is rare.
> - **Splash zone has the highest corrosion rate (2 – 5 mm/year)** in marine atmosphere exposure — alternate wet/dry + O₂ access + no permanent calcareous deposit.
> - **Ballast tank top-head space is the worst** corrosion zone in tankers — gas-phase O₂ + condensate + MIC + no protective coating.
> - **SRB is the #1 marine MIC organism** — produces H₂S and FeS, gives 10× acceleration over abiotic corrosion.
> - **Dealloying rule of thumb:** the more active element (lower E⁰) is selectively removed (Zn from brass, Al from Cu-Al, Ni from Cu-Ni).
> - **Cavitation forms at local P < P_vapor**, collapses with shock pressure up to 100 MPa — direct propeller tip damage.
> - **Galvanic corrosion in seawater propagates over metres** due to high conductivity; in freshwater only centimetres.
> - **Faraday's law constant: 3.27 × 10⁻³ mm·g/(µA·cm²·year)** for i_corr in µA/cm² → CR in mm/year.
> - **Tafel slope at 25 °C, α = 0.5: b ≈ 0.118 V/decade** — memorize this.
> - **Stress corrosion cracking** in austenitic SS requires **3 conditions simultaneously**: susceptible alloy, specific environment, tensile stress.
> - **Hydrogen embrittlement** severity ∝ tensile strength. Mitigation: bake-out at 200 – 300 °C for high-strength steels after acid pickling.
> - **Erosion-corrosion is a function of velocity** — Cu-Ni 90/10 tolerates up to ~3 m/s; Cu-Ni 70/30 up to ~4 m/s; mild steel only ~1 m/s.
> - **Always draw a labelled sketch** for any descriptive 14-mark answer.
> - **Cite the marine-specific example** for every corrosion type — examiners reward real-world linkage.
> - **For corrosion rate problems, include: value, units, basis, and an engineering comment** (e.g., "0.5 mm/year — within design life, but recommend ICCP retrofit for compliance").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrosion Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Corrosion Identification** is the systematic process of detecting, recognizing, classifying, and quantifying the various forms and mechanisms of degradation occurring in metallic (and certain non-metallic) materials exposed to corrosive environments. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for marine engineering, it encompasses the application of **visual inspection**, **non-destructive testing (NDT)**, **electrochemical techniques**, and **microstructural analysis** to diagnose the type, extent, rate, and root cause of corrosion in marine structures, hulls, propellers, pipelines, heat exchangers, and offshore installations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Scope):**
> Corrosion identification deals with the recognition of the *form* (morphology), *mechanism* (electrochemistry), and *cause* (environmental/material variables) of corrosion. It is the *first step* in any corrosion control strategy—because you cannot protect what you have not correctly diagnosed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters in Marine Engineering:**
> The marine environment (3.5% NaCl seawater, high humidity, biofouling, varying oxygen levels, galvanic coupling with dissimilar metals) produces the **most aggressive natural corrosion conditions on Earth**. Proper identification is the gateway to selecting the correct *prevention method* (coatings, cathodic protection, alloy selection, inhibitors).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Corrosion Detective" Analogy

Imagine you are a **forensic detective** arriving at a "crime scene" on a ship's hull. You see rust streaks, pits, cracks, and discoloration. Just as a detective would collect fingerprints, examine bullet trajectories, and run DNA analysis, a **corrosion engineer** uses a toolkit of inspection and analytical methods to identify *who* (which corrosion mechanism), *how* (which pathway), and *how fast* (kinetics) the damage occurred.

### The Three Pillars of Corrosion Identification

| Pillar | What It Answers | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Morphological Identification** | *What does it look like?* | Photographing the crime scene |
| **Electrochemical Identification** | *How fast is the attack progressing?* | Measuring the heartbeat of the victim |
| **Root-Cause Identification** | *Why did it happen?* | Interviewing witnesses, reviewing CCTV |

---

## 1.3 The Eight Recognized Forms of Marine Corrosion

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to master the **eight classical forms of corrosion** that can be identified in marine service. Mastery begins with their **morphological signature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 8 Forms of Corrosion (NACE/ISO Standard Classification):**
> 1. **Uniform (General) Corrosion** — even material loss across the entire exposed surface
> 2. **Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion** — preferential attack on the *anode* of two electrically connected dissimilar metals
> 3. **Pitting Corrosion** — localized, narrow-depth cavities with the rest of the surface largely unaffected
> 4. **Crevice Corrosion** — intense localized attack within confined spaces (gaskets, lap joints, bolt threads)
> 5. **Intergranular Corrosion (IGC)** — preferential attack along grain boundaries (e.g., sensitized stainless steel)
> 6. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — crack propagation under the combined action of tensile stress + corrosive medium
> 7. **Erosion–Corrosion** — accelerated attack due to relative motion between fluid and surface
> 8. **Dealloying (Selective Leaching)** — preferential removal of one element from an alloy (e.g., dezincification of brass)

> [!WARNING]
> **A ninth form — *Microbial-Induced Corrosion (MIC)* — is increasingly tested in KTU 2024 papers** because of its relevance to ballast tanks, seawater piping, and subsea structures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Framework of Corrosion Identification

A rigorous KTU answer should follow the **3-Stage Identification Logic**:

1. **Stage I — Macroscopic (Visual) Screening**
   - Examine surface morphology with the naked eye, magnifying glass, or low-power stereo microscope.
   - Record: color of corrosion product, distribution pattern, depth of attack, location relative to geometry (joints, edges, welds).

2. **Stage II — Instrumental Confirmation**
   - Apply NDT (ultrasonic, radiographic, eddy current) to detect subsurface damage and quantify wall thickness loss.
   - Apply electrochemical techniques (LPR, Tafel, EIS) to measure the *instantaneous* corrosion rate.

3. **Stage III — Microstructural and Chemical Analysis**
   - Cross-sectional metallography to reveal grain-boundary attack, pit profiles, and crack morphology.
   - SEM/EDS to identify corrosion products and deposit compositions.
   - XRD for crystalline phase identification of rust layers.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Methods of Corrosion Identification

### A. Visual Inspection (The Primary Method)

Visual inspection is the **starting point** in *every* corrosion survey. It requires no equipment, costs almost nothing, and correctly identifies **~70%** of common forms in well-lit conditions.

- **Tools:** Flashlight, magnifying glass (10×), borescope, recording camera, pit-depth gauge.
- **Checklist Items:**
  - Discoloration and staining
  - Pits (number, size, shape — round, narrow, subsurface)
  - Tubercles (in marine environments)
  - Wall thickness loss (using UT gauge)
  - Crack patterns (branching = SCC; straight = fatigue)
  - Product color (reddish-brown = Fe₂O₃; black = Fe₃O₄; green = Cu corrosion)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip — A common 3-mark question:** *"List the limitations of visual inspection."*
> **Answer:** Cannot detect sub-surface corrosion; subjective (depends on inspector); cannot quantify rate; limited to accessible surfaces; cannot identify microbiological attack reliably.

### B. Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Methods

| NDT Method | Working Principle | What It Identifies | Marine Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | High-frequency sound waves reflect off defects and back-wall | Wall-thickness loss, laminations, pits, cracks | Hull plates, piping, pressure vessels |
| **Radiographic Testing (RT)** | X-rays or gamma rays penetrate material; defects absorb differentially | Volumetric defects, cracks, porosity in welds | Welded joints, cast propellers |
| **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** | Induced eddy currents are distorted by defects/changes in conductivity | Surface and near-surface cracks, pitting, coating thickness | Heat-exchanger tubes, condenser tubes |
| **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** | Discontinuities distort magnetic flux; iron particles cluster at defect | Surface and sub-surface cracks in ferromagnetic alloys | Welds, castings, forgings |
| **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT/PT)** | Liquid penetrant seeps into surface cracks; developer reveals them | Surface-breaking cracks and porosity (any material) | Non-magnetic stainless steel components |
| **Acoustic Emission (AE)** | Detects transient elastic waves released by active crack growth | Real-time crack growth, active SCC, leaking pipes | Pressure vessels, storage tanks |

### C. Electrochemical Identification Techniques

These methods **directly probe the corrosion current** flowing at the metal–electrolyte interface and are the *gold standard* for measuring instantaneous corrosion rate.

#### 2.2.1 Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR)

A small perturbation (typically ±10 mV) is applied around the corrosion potential, and the resulting current is measured. The **polarization resistance** $R_p$ is obtained from the slope.

#### 2.2.2 Tafel Extrapolation

A larger polarization (±250 mV) is applied on both sides of $E_{corr}$, producing the characteristic Tafel plot. The cathodic and anodic Tafel slopes $\beta_c$ and $\beta_a$ are determined, and the corrosion current $I_{corr}$ is read at the intersection point.

#### 2.2.3 Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)

A small-amplitude AC signal (1–10 mV) is applied over a wide frequency range (10⁵ Hz to 10⁻³ Hz), and the impedance response is recorded. The data is fitted to an **equivalent circuit** to extract $R_p$, $C_{dl}$, and solution resistance $R_s$.

#### 2.2.4 Electrochemical Noise (ECN)

Measures the natural, spontaneous fluctuations in potential and current between two identical electrodes. It can detect **localized corrosion** (pitting, SCC) without disturbing the system.

### D. Coupon and Probe Methods

- **Coupon Testing:** Pre-weighed metal strips are exposed in the system for 30–90 days, retrieved, cleaned, and re-weighed. The mass loss gives the *time-averaged* corrosion rate.
- **Electrical Resistance (ER) Probes:** A corroding element's resistance increases as its cross-section decreases. Provides online, real-time trend data.
- **Hydrogen Probes:** Measure the hydrogen evolved during corrosion (especially useful in sour H₂S environments).

### E. Microstructural / Surface Analysis

- **Optical Microscopy:** Cross-sectioning reveals pit geometry, grain-boundary attack, and crack paths.
- **SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy):** High-magnification imaging of fracture surfaces (cleavage facets = brittle/HCC; dimples = ductile).
- **EDS (Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy):** Identifies elemental composition of corrosion products and deposits.
- **XRD (X-Ray Diffraction):** Identifies crystalline corrosion products (e.g., goethite α-FeOOH, lepidocrocite γ-FeOOH, akaganeite β-FeOOH — all common in marine rust).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-2 Master Formula Table** — All of these have been tested in past papers.

| # | Formula | Description / Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's Law: mass of metal dissolved | grams |
| 2 | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}$ | Corrosion rate from mass loss | mm/yr or mpy |
| 3 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Galvanic cell EMF (driving force) | Volts |
| 4 | $\eta = \beta \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Butler–Volmer / Tafel equation | Volts, A/cm² |
| 5 | $R_p = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}\bigg\vert_{\Delta E \to 0}$ | Polarization resistance (slope at $E_{corr}$) | Ω·cm² |
| 6 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303\,(\beta_a + \beta_c) R_p}$ | Stern–Geary equation | A/cm² |
| 7 | $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern–Geary constant (typically 13–52 mV) | Volts |
| 8 | $CR_{mm/yr} = 0.00327 \cdot \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate from current density | mm/yr |
| 9 | $CR_{mpy} = 0.129 \cdot \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate in mils per year | mpy |
| 10 | $EW = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | Equivalent weight of metal | g/eq |
| 11 | $Z = Z_{Re} - j Z_{Im}$ | Complex impedance in EIS | Ω |
| 12 | $C_{dl} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Double-layer capacitance | Farads |
| 13 | $\sigma = \dfrac{FD\,(C_{bulk} - C_{surface})}{\delta}$ | Diffusion-limited corrosion (Fick's 1st Law) | varies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variable Definitions (K = constant 87.6 for mm/yr, 534 for mpy, EW = equivalent weight, ρ = density in g/cm³, M = molar mass, n = electrons transferred, F = 96485 C/mol).**

| **Hull plating** (submerged zone) | Visual + UT thickness gauging + LPR probe |
| **Ballast tanks** | Visual + coupon + ER probe + microbiological culturing |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday's Law and the Corrosion Rate Equation

The cornerstone equation linking *electrochemistry* to *engineering corrosion rate* is derived below. This is the **most-asked derivation in KTU ESE papers** for this module.

### Starting Point: Charge Transferred by Corrosion Current

The total electric charge $Q$ (in Coulombs) that flows through the corroding metal during a time $t$ is given by Ohm's-law-like reasoning:

$$
Q = I \cdot t
$$

where:
- $I$ = corrosion current (Amperes)
- $t$ = exposure time (seconds)

### Step 2 — Moles of Electrons Involved

By definition of the Faraday constant $F$ (which is the charge carried by **one mole of electrons**), the number of moles of electrons is:

$$
n_{e^-} = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F}
$$

### Step 3 — Moles of Metal Dissolved

If the anodic reaction transfers $n$ electrons per atom dissolved (e.g., $n = 2$ for $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$), then by stoichiometry:

$$
n_{metal} = \dfrac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
$$

### Step 4 — Mass of Metal Lost

Multiplying by the molar mass $M$ (g/mol) gives the mass dissolved:

$$
W = n_{metal} \cdot M = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

This is **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**, the foundation of all quantitative corrosion work.

### Step 5 — Expressing Corrosion Rate in mm/yr

To convert to a *practical* engineering unit (mm/yr), we divide mass loss by the *exposed area* $A$ and *time* $t$, and then multiply by a unit-conversion constant $K$ that includes the metal's density $\rho$:

$$
CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}
$$

Substituting the expression for $W$:

$$
CR = \dfrac{K \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{A \cdot t \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}
$$

Note that $t$ cancels (this is why the formula is time-independent for a steady current), giving:

$$
CR = \dfrac{K \cdot I \cdot M}{A \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho}
$$

For $CR$ in **mm/yr**, $I$ in **Amperes**, $A$ in **cm²**, and using the **definition of equivalent weight** $EW = M/n$:

$$
CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot I \cdot EW}{A \cdot \rho}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical constant 0.00327** comes from: $\dfrac{K_{mm/yr}}{F} = \dfrac{87.6}{96485} \approx 9.08 \times 10^{-4}$. Multiplying by 3.6 (the seconds-per-year conversion folded in) gives **0.00327**. Students should **memorize both constants** — 0.00327 (mm/yr) and 0.129 (mpy).

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A carbon-steel pipe section (density $\rho = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $EW = 27.92$ g/eq) of exposed area $80 \text{ cm}^2$ is exposed to seawater. The measured corrosion current is $I = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$ A. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/yr** and **mpy**.

**Solution:**

$$
CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot I \cdot EW}{A \cdot \rho}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot (4.0 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 27.92}{80 \cdot 7.85}
$$

Step-by-step:

- Numerator: $0.00327 \cdot 4.0 \times 10^{-4} = 1.308 \times 10^{-6}$
- Numerator × EW: $1.308 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 27.92 = 3.652 \times 10^{-5}$
- Denominator: $80 \cdot 7.85 = 628$
- $CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{3.652 \times 10^{-5}}{628} = 5.81 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mm/yr}$

> [!TIP]
> This extremely low value indicates that the system is *passive* (e.g., a stainless steel in service). If the metal were carbon steel in seawater, expect $CR$ in the range **0.1 – 0.5 mm/yr**.

In **mpy** (mils per year):

$$
CR_{mpy} = 0.129 \cdot CR_{mm/yr} \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{EW} \cdot 1000
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A common KTU mistake:** Forgetting to convert the current from total amps to *current density* $i = I/A$ before using the Stern–Geary equation. The equation $i_{corr} = B/R_p$ uses **current density** (A/cm²), not raw current.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stern–Geary Equation from Butler–Volmer Kinetics

### The Butler–Volmer Equation

For an electrode far from equilibrium, the net current density is the difference between anodic and cathodic partial current densities:

$$
i = i_a - i_c = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(\dfrac{-\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]
$$

where $\eta = E - E_{corr}$ is the **overpotential**, $\alpha_a$ and $\alpha_c$ are transfer coefficients, and $i_0$ is the exchange current density.

### Step 2 — Tafel Approximation at Large $\vert \eta \vert$

When $\vert \eta \vert \gg RT/F$ (typically $> 50$ mV), one exponential dominates. For anodic polarization:

$$
i_a \approx i_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right)
$$

Taking logarithm:

$$
\eta = -\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_a F} \log i_0 + \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_a F} \log i_a
$$

Defining the **anodic Tafel slope** $\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_a F}$:

$$
\eta = \beta_a \log\left(\dfrac{i_a}{i_0}\right)
$$

Similarly, for cathodic:

$$
\eta = -\beta_c \log\left(\dfrac{i_c}{i_0}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Linearization at Small $\eta$ (LPR)

For small $\eta$ (within $\pm 10$ mV of $E_{corr}$), expand the exponentials to first order:

$$
i \approx i_0 \left[ 1 + \dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} - 1 + \dfrac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right] = i_0 \cdot \dfrac{(\alpha_a + \alpha_c) F \eta}{RT}
$$

At the corrosion potential, $i_a = i_c = i_{corr}$ and $i_0$ is replaced by $i_{corr}$:

$$
i = \dfrac{i_{corr}(\alpha_a + \alpha_c) F \eta}{RT}
$$

Therefore, the **polarization resistance** is:

$$
R_p = \dfrac{\eta}{i} = \dfrac{RT}{i_{corr} F (\alpha_a + \alpha_c)}
$$

Using the definitions of Tafel slopes $\beta_a = RT/(\alpha_a F)$ and $\beta_c = RT/(\alpha_c F)$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{R_p} = \dfrac{i_{corr}}{\beta_a} + \dfrac{i_{corr}}{\beta_c} = i_{corr}\left(\dfrac{1}{\beta_a} + \dfrac{1}{\beta_c}\right)
$$

Solving for $i_{corr}$:

$$
i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_p}
$$

Defining the Stern–Geary constant:

$$
B = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}
$$

We arrive at the **canonical Stern–Geary Equation**:

$$
i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU loves this equation:** It is the *bridge* between a simple electrical measurement ($R_p$) and the *corrosion current* (and thus the corrosion rate). A student who can derive and apply this scores the full 14 marks on the corresponding Part-B question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pit Stability Product (Pitting Identification Criterion)

A useful criterion for identifying *stable* pits is the **pit stability product** $i \cdot x$, where $i$ is the current density inside the pit and $x$ is the pit depth. This was developed by Galvele (1967).

$$
i \cdot x \geq i \cdot x_{crit}
$$

For stainless steels in chloride media, the critical value is approximately:

$$
(i \cdot x)_{crit} \approx 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $i \cdot x > 10^{-6}$ A/cm → **stable pit** (grows indefinitely).
- If $i \cdot x < 10^{-6}$ A/cm → **metastable pit** (repassivates).

> [!NOTE]
> This criterion is how engineers *identify whether observed pits are active threats* or simply re-passivated surface defects — a frequent 7-mark KTU sub-question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identifying Corrosion Form by Visual Signature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Master Identification Matrix** — Memorize for the 14-mark "describe" question.

| Observation / Feature | Likely Corrosion Form | Confirmatory Test |
|---|---|---|
| Even, widespread thinning with reddish-brown rust | Uniform corrosion | Weight-loss coupon |
| Pits near a Cu-Ni / bronze fitting; brass turns spongy, red | Dezincification (dealloying) | SEM + EDS for Cu/Zn ratio |
| Deep, narrow, dark pits; rest of surface clean | Pitting corrosion | Potentiodynamic polarization (breakdown $E$) |
| Heavy attack under gaskets, bolt heads, lap joints | Crevice corrosion | Cross-section microscopy, O₂ profile |
| Cracks branching like a tree, on stainless steel in chloride | Stress Corrosion Cracking | Slow-strain-rate test, DPT, MT |
| Shiny groove downstream of an elbow or pump | Erosion–corrosion | Visual + flow-pattern analysis |
| Crater-like pits with directional elongation | Cavitation corrosion | Visual + flow velocity profile |
| Surface staining near welded area of SS304 in service | Intergranular corrosion | ASTM A262 Practice E (Strauss test) |
| Discrete patches of rust in ballast tank, musty smell | MIC | Culturing, SEM observation of tubercles |
| Preferential attack on one side of a weld | Weldment corrosion / galvanic | Visual + hardness profile |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem — Galvanic Identification (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Two metals are coupled in seawater: **Aluminium** ($E_{Al/Al^{3+}} = -1.66$ V) and **Copper** ($E_{Cu/Cu^{2+}} = +0.34$ V). Calculate the driving EMF and identify the anode.

**Solution:**

The more negative metal is the anode and corrodes preferentially. The cell EMF:

$$
E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}
$$

$$
E_{cell} = E_{Cu} - E_{Al} = (+0.34) - (-1.66) = 2.00 \text{ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical interpretation:** This is why *direct contact between aluminium hulls and copper-based propellers/bronzes* is forbidden in shipbuilding without an insulating gasket. The 2.0 V driving force causes **catastrophic galvanic corrosion** of the aluminium within weeks.

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Corrosion Identification Decision Tree

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete decision process a corrosion engineer follows when first encountering an unknown damage pattern on a marine structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Observed Damage on Marine Component] --> stageA{Stage 1 Visual Inspection}
    stageA --> stageA1[Photograph and Document Morphology]
    stageA1 --> stageA2{Attack Pattern?}
    stageA2 -->|Even thinning across surface| pathA1[Uniform Corrosion]
    stageA2 -->|Localized pits| pathA2[Pitting Corrosion]
    stageA2 -->|Cracks branching| pathA3[SCC]
    stageA2 -->|Attack at joints gaskets| pathA4[Crevice Corrosion]
    stageA2 -->|One metal in a couple is spongy| pathA5[Galvanic or Dealloying]
    stageA2 -->|Grooves in flow direction| pathA6[Erosion Corrosion]
    stageA2 -->|Pits near welds in SS| pathA7[Intergranular]
    pathA1 --> stageB[Stage 2 NDT Confirmation]
    pathA2 --> stageB
    pathA3 --> stageB
    pathA4 --> stageB
    pathA5 --> stageB
    pathA6 --> stageB
    pathA7 --> stageB
    stageB --> stageB1[UT for Wall Thickness]
    stageB --> stageB2[Eddy Current for Surface Defects]
    stageB --> stageB3[DPT or MT for Cracks]
    stageB --> stageB4[Radiography for Internal Voids]
    stageB1 --> stageC[Stage 3 Electrochemical Tests]
    stageB2 --> stageC
    stageB3 --> stageC
    stageB4 --> stageC
    stageC --> stageC1[LPR Measurement for icorr]
    stageC --> stageC2[Tafel Plot for Tafel Slopes]
    stageC --> stageC3[EIS for Film Resistance]
    stageC --> stageC4[ECN for Localized Activity]
    stageC1 --> stageD[Stage 4 Microstructural Analysis]
    stageC2 --> stageD
    stageC3 --> stageD
    stageC4 --> stageD
    stageD --> stageD1[Cross Section Metallography]
    stageD --> stageD2[SEM and EDS]
    stageD --> stageD3[XRD of Corrosion Product]
    stageD1 --> finalA[Confirmed Corrosion Form and Rate]
    stageD2 --> finalA
    stageD3 --> finalA
    finalA --> finalB[Recommend Prevention Method]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The engineer starts at the top, follows the branch matching the visual signature, and progressively confirms the diagnosis with increasingly sophisticated (and expensive) methods. In KTU viva, the examiner expects you to *explain why each stage is necessary*.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an LPR Corrosion Rate Probe

The Linear Polarization Resistance probe is the most common *online* corrosion-rate sensor used on ships and offshore platforms. The following block diagram shows its internal architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    envBlock[Marine Electrolyte Seawater] --> interfaceA[Metal Working Electrode WE]
    interfaceA --> resistorA[Reference Half Cell RE]
    interfaceA --> resistorB[Counter Electrode CE]
    resistorA --> ampBlock[High Impedance Differential Amplifier]
    resistorB --> ampBlock
    ampBlock --> signalCond[Signal Conditioning and Filter]
    signalCond --> adcBlock[Analog to Digital Converter]
    adcBlock --> microcontroller[Mcu Processor with Polarization Algorithm]
    microcontroller --> displayUnit[Display or DCS Output]
    microcontroller --> memoryLog[Data Logger]
    microcontroller --> alarmUnit[Alarm Threshold Comparator]
    alarmUnit --> alarmLED[High Corrosion Rate Alert]
    microcontroller --> commPort[4 to 20 mA or HART Output]
    commPort --> externalDCS[Ship Management System]
```
