# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer Type)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define corrosion identification. List any four methods used for identifying corrosion in marine structures.**

**Model Answer:**

Corrosion identification is the systematic process of detecting, classifying, and quantifying the form, mechanism, and extent of corrosion damage in materials, using visual, instrumental, electrochemical, and microstructural techniques.

**Four identification methods:**
1. **Visual inspection** — examining surface morphology, rust color, pitting pattern.
2. **Ultrasonic thickness gauging** — measuring remaining wall thickness of pipes and hull plates.
3. **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR)** — measuring instantaneous corrosion current density.
4. **Metallographic examination** — cross-sectioning to reveal grain-boundary attack, pit profiles, and crack paths.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the Stern–Geary constant B in corrosion rate measurement? Mention its typical range.**

**Model Answer:**

The Stern–Geary constant $B$ relates the polarization resistance $R_p$ to the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ through the equation:

$$
i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}
$$

where $B$ incorporates the anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes:

$$
B = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}
$$

**Significance:**
- Allows conversion of the easily measurable $R_p$ into the *practical* corrosion rate (mm/yr or mpy).
- Eliminates the need to perform a full Tafel extrapolation for routine monitoring.

**Typical range:** $B$ is generally between **13 mV** (for passive systems) and **52 mV** (for active corroding systems), with a commonly assumed value of **26 mV** for carbon steel in aerated water.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **morphological identification features** of the following corrosion forms commonly observed in marine service:
**(i) Pitting corrosion** (4 marks)
**(ii) Crevice corrosion** (3 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR) technique** for identifying and measuring corrosion rate. Derive the Stern–Geary equation relating $i_{corr}$ to $R_p$. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a)(i) Pitting Corrosion — Identification Features (4 marks):**

Pitting corrosion is one of the most *insidious* forms of attack because the bulk of the surface appears untouched while deep, narrow cavities propagate.

**Visual identification features:**
1. **Discrete cavities** of varying diameter, often with their mouths *narrower* than their internal depth (subsurface growth).
2. **Pits may be covered** by a perforated scale or corrosion product cap (tubercles), giving a misleading impression of shallow attack.
3. **Pit interiors** are often polished or bright, while the surrounding surface is dull and stained.
4. **Pit bottoms** may show crystallographic facets (in stainless steel) or stepped terraces (in carbon steel).
5. **Distribution:** Random on horizontal surfaces, but may be gravity-oriented on vertical surfaces (pits grow downward).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Identification test:** Potentiodynamic polarization in chloride solution reveals a sharp current rise at the **pitting potential** $E_p$. If the protection potential $E_{prot}$ is above $E_{corr}$, the alloy is immune; otherwise, pitting is thermodynamically possible.

**Sketch description:** A horizontal pipe wall with several circular pits — some open, some with rust caps; cross-section showing narrow mouth and wide interior.

---

**(a)(ii) Crevice Corrosion — Identification Features (3 marks):**

1. **Location:** Always confined to shielded regions — gaskets, lap joints, bolt threads, under deposits, inside heat-exchanger tube-to-tube-sheet joints.
2. **Geometry dependency:** Crevice gap typically **< 1 mm** (wider gaps do not become oxygen-depleted).
3. **Visual signature:** Pits or shallow attack *only within the crevice*; the open surface is often unaffected.
4. **Mechanism signature:** Differential aeration cell — oxygen-rich bulk solution supports cathodic reaction; oxygen-starved crevice becomes anodic.
5. **Marine indicator:** Heavy rust staining bleeding from joints, sometimes combined with microbiological tubercles (MIC).

**Sketch description:** A bolted plate lap joint with heavy attack inside the joint gap and clean external surfaces.

---

**(b) LPR Technique and Stern–Geary Derivation (7 marks):**

**Principle:** When a small DC polarization (typically $\pm 10$ mV) is applied to a corroding electrode around its free corrosion potential $E_{corr}$, the resulting current $i$ is linearly proportional to the applied overpotential $\eta$ in this narrow region.

**Apparatus:** Three-electrode cell — **Working Electrode (WE)** = the test metal, **Reference Electrode (RE)** = saturated calomel or Ag/AgCl, **Counter Electrode (CE)** = platinum or graphite. A **potentiostat** controls the potential and measures the current.

**Procedure:**
1. Immerse the three electrodes in the test electrolyte (e.g., seawater).
2. Monitor $E_{corr}$ for 30 min to allow stabilization.
3. Apply a small linear potential sweep from $E_{corr} - 10$ mV to $E_{corr} + 10$ mV at a slow scan rate (e.g., 0.1 mV/s).
4. Plot $\eta$ vs. $i$; the slope near the origin is the **polarization resistance** $R_p$.

**Derivation of Stern–Geary equation:** (Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for full working.)

Starting from the Butler–Volmer equation, applying the small-$\eta$ linearization, and substituting Tafel slope definitions:

$$
i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p} \quad \text{where} \quad B = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}
$$

**Corrosion rate calculation:**

$$
CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}
$$

**Advantages of LPR:**
- **Fast** (one polarization sweep in 5–10 minutes).
- **Non-destructive** — the same sample can be re-measured.
- **Quantitative** — gives instantaneous $i_{corr}$ in A/cm².
- Suitable for **online field monitoring** in ballast tanks, pipelines, and process vessels.

**Limitations:**
- Linear approximation valid only within $\pm 10$ mV.
- $B$ is not precisely known a priori and is often assumed (introduces 30–50% uncertainty).
- Cannot distinguish between uniform and localized corrosion (gives an "average" rate).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Describe in detail the **galvanic series** and how it is used to identify galvanic corrosion risk in marine structural design. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Coupon Testing Method** and the **Electrical Resistance (ER) Probe Method** for corrosion identification. Compare their advantages and limitations. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) The Galvanic Series and Galvanic Corrosion Identification (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The galvanic series is an *empirical* ranking of metals and alloys based on their **measured corrosion potentials in a specific environment** (typically flowing seawater at 25 °C). It differs from the *theoretical* EMF series in that it reflects the *real* behavior of commercial alloys (including their passive films), not just thermodynamic half-cell potentials.

**Portion of the Galvanic Series in Seawater (anodic end → cathodic end):**
- **Most Active (Anodic, corrodes):** Magnesium → Zinc → Aluminium alloys → Mild Steel → Cast Iron → Stainless Steel (active) → Lead → Tin → Brasses → Copper → Bronze → Monel → Austenitic SS (passive) → Titanium → **Most Noble (Cathodic, protected):** Graphite, Platinum

**Use in Design and Identification:**
1. **Predicting galvanic couples:** Any two metals in electrical contact and immersed in the same electrolyte will form a galvanic cell. The one higher in the series (more active) is the **anode** and corrodes.
2. **Quantifying risk:** The voltage difference between the two metals gives a rough idea of the **driving force**; however, the *current* flowing depends on the polarization characteristics of both metals, not just the EMF.
3. **Area effect:** A small anode coupled to a large cathode is the **worst-case** situation (current density on the anode is enormous). Example: a small brass valve in a steel pipe (large cathode, small anode) causes rapid pitting of the steel at the joint.
4. **Marine design rule:** Avoid coupling metals separated by more than ~0.15 V in the galvanic series, or use **insulating gaskets** to break the electrical path, and apply **cathodic protection** to the more active member.

**Identification in service:** A corroded region located *exactly* between two dissimilar metals, with the *more-active* metal preferentially attacked, is a strong indicator of galvanic corrosion. The corroded surface often shows a *rust deposit* from the iron and a *greenish-blue* stain from copper dissolution.

---

**(b) Coupon Testing and ER Probe Methods (7 marks):**

**Coupon Testing Method:**

A **coupon** is a small, pre-weighed, geometrically defined strip of the *same material* as the structure being studied. It is mounted on an insulated holder and exposed in the actual service environment for a predetermined period (typically 30, 60, or 90 days).

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh the coupon accurately to $\pm 0.1$ mg.
2. Measure dimensions to compute the exposed surface area.
3. Mount in the system at a representative location.
4. After retrieval, chemically clean (per ASTM G1) to remove corrosion products *without* attacking the base metal.
5. Re-weigh and calculate mass loss.

**Corrosion rate calculation:**

$$
CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}
$$

where $W$ = mass loss (mg), $A$ = area (cm²), $t$ = exposure time (hr), $\rho$ = density (g/cm³).

**Advantages:**
- Direct, unambiguous, requires no electronics.
- Provides *time-averaged* corrosion rate.
- Multiple coupons at different locations reveal spatial variability.

**Limitations:**
- **Time-consuming** — needs weeks to months.
- **Not real-time** — corrosion event between retrievals is missed.
- Coupon holder itself may disturb local flow and chemistry.
- Cleaning procedure introduces operator-dependent error.

**Electrical Resistance (ER) Probe Method:**

The ER probe contains a *thin element* of the same alloy exposed to the same electrolyte. As the element corrodes, its **cross-sectional area decreases**, and its **electrical resistance increases** according to:

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho_{el} \cdot L}{A_{cs}}
$$

where $\rho_{el}$ = electrical resistivity, $L$ = length, $A_{cs}$ = cross-sectional area. The instrument measures the resistance continuously, converts it to "metal loss," and displays it as a corrosion rate.

**Advantages:**
- **Real-time, online** measurement.
- Suitable for **process control** and alarm generation.
- Works in **non-conductive** environments (unlike LPR).
- Long-term, continuous data without operator intervention.

**Limitations:**
- Measures only the *probe element*, not the actual structure (assume identical conditions).
- Resolution is limited by element thickness and temperature compensation.
- Cannot distinguish uniform from localized attack.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Coupon | ER Probe |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement type | Discrete, time-averaged | Continuous, real-time |
| Equipment cost | Very low | Moderate–high |
| Operator skill | Moderate | Low (automated) |
| Detection of localized attack | Possible (by visual) | Poor |
| Output | Final mass loss | Continuous trend |
| Best use | Surveys, baseline studies | Online process control |

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Corrosion Identification Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up series.** Students often confuse the *Galvanic Series* (empirical, in seawater) with the *EMF Series* (theoretical, standard conditions). The galvanic series is what *real engineers* use; the EMF series is *only* for textbook thermodynamic calculations.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Faraday's law.** The constant 0.00327 is for **mm/yr** with current in **amperes** and area in **cm²**. A student using 0.129 (mpy constant) with current in mA/cm² will be off by a factor of 1000.
> 3. **Confusing $R_p$ (polarization resistance) with $R_s$ (solution resistance) and $R_{ct}$ (charge-transfer resistance).** In EIS Nyquist plots, these are different features at different frequency ranges.
> 4. **Not stating the *assumed* Stern–Geary constant.** Examiners deduct marks if you write $i_{corr} = B/R_p$ without specifying the value of $B$ used.
> 5. **Neglecting the *area effect* in galvanic corrosion.** Stating the EMF is not enough — always discuss the cathode-to-anode area ratio and its impact on corrosion current density.
> 6. **Describing an identification method without mentioning its *limitations*.** KTU 2024 marks specifically allocate ~2 marks for limitations; skipping them costs you those marks.
> 7. **Using Python or any programming code in the answer.** This is a *non-CS* engineering paper; code snippets will earn zero credit and may lead to negative marking.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second KTU Revision Bullet-List:**
> - **Corrosion identification** = detect + classify + quantify + diagnose root cause.
> - **Eight forms of corrosion** to identify: Uniform, Galvanic, Pitting, Crevice, IGC, SCC, Erosion, Dealloying (plus MIC as a bonus).
> - **Visual inspection** is always the *first* step; it identifies ~70% of cases.
> - **NDT methods:** UT (thickness), RT (voids), ET (surface), MT (ferromagnetic cracks), DPT (any material surface cracks), AE (active damage).
> - **Electrochemical methods:** LPR (rate), Tafel (mechanism), EIS (film), ECN (localized).
> - **Faraday's Law:** $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$.
> - **Corrosion rate from mass loss:** $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}$.
> - **Stern–Geary equation:** $i_{corr} = B / R_p$, with $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \in [13, 52]$ mV.
> - **Practical constants:** 0.00327 (mm/yr·cm²·g/A·eq), 0.129 (mpy·cm²·g/A·eq).
> - **Galvanic series** in seawater is used in *design*; **EMF series** is used in *theory*.
> - **Area effect** in galvanic corrosion: small anode + large cathode = catastrophic.
> - **Pit stability product** $i \cdot x \ge 10^{-6}$ A/cm is the criterion for *stable* pitting.
> - **Three-stage identification logic:** Visual → NDT + Electrochemistry → Microstructural/SEM.
> - **Coupon** = time-averaged, slow, accurate, but not real-time.
> - **ER probe** = real-time, continuous, but measures a *sacrificial element*, not the actual structure.
> - **LPR probe** = gold standard for *online* rate monitoring on ships and offshore platforms.
> - **A 2.0 V cell** exists between aluminium and copper in seawater — never couple them directly on a ship.
> - Always conclude a 14-mark answer with **prevention recommendations** — KTU rewards the "diagnosis → cure" flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Corrosion: Types of Steel and Tendency of Corrosion in Various Types of Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Corrosion: Types of Steel and Tendency of Corrosion in Various Types of Steel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU OENST612 (Marine Materials, Corrosion Prevention and Protection)**, the *tendency of corrosion in various types of steel* is defined as the inherent thermodynamic and kinetic susceptibility of a ferrous alloy to undergo electrochemical oxidation when exposed to a marine environment, governed by its **chemical composition**, **microstructure**, **metallurgical phase distribution**, and **position in the galvanic series**.

The corrosion tendency of any steel is quantitatively expressed through:

- **Electrode Potential (E°)** — measured in **Volts (V)** versus the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE).
- **Corrosion Current Density (i_corr)** — measured in **A/m²** or **μA/cm²**.
- **Corrosion Rate (CR)** — measured in **mm/year (mmpy)** or **mils per year (mpy)**.

The governing thermodynamic relationship is the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{Oxidised Species}]}{[\text{Reduced Species}]}\right) \quad \text{at } 25^{\circ}\text{C}$$

where **n** is the number of electrons transferred per ion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2:**
> The official Module 2 syllabus mandates the study of "Types of Steel" (carbon steel, alloy steel, stainless steel, tool steel, HSLA steel, weathering steel, and cast iron) and explicitly links the *metallurgical classification* of each steel to its *electrochemical corrosion behaviour* in marine service conditions. The phrase *"tendency of corrosion"* is a direct KTU Board keyword and must be answered with reference to the **Galvanic Series**, **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)**, and the **Passivation Mechanism (Pourbaix Diagram for Fe–H₂O system)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Seating Arrangement in a Hall"

Imagine a large wedding hall where guests (metal atoms in the steel) are seated according to their *social rank* (electrochemical nobility):

- The **most reactive** guests are seated closest to the **entrance door** (the anodic end) — they will always be picked first to leave (corrode first).
- The **most noble** guests sit on the **stage/dais** (the cathodic end) — they never leave unless forced.
- **Alloying elements** (Cr, Ni, Mo, Cu) act like *special invitations* that promote a guest to a more prestigious seat, *moving them up the nobility ranking*.
- **Carbon atoms** act like *uninvited gatecrashers* who distort the seating and create **micro-galvanic cells** between ferrite (α-Fe) and cementite (Fe₃C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:**
> Steel is *not* a single material — it is a *family* of iron-carbon alloys with widely varying corrosion behaviour. The **carbon content**, **alloying additions**, and **heat treatment** dictate where the steel "sits" in this electrochemical hall, and therefore *how fast* it will rust in seawater.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Electrode Potential | E° | **V (SHE)** | Thermodynamic nobility |
| Corrosion Current Density | i_corr | **μA/cm²** | Kinetic rate indicator |
| Corrosion Rate | CR | **mmpy** | Engineering service life |
| Pilling–Bedworth Ratio | PBR | **dimensionless** | Oxide film protectiveness |
| Passivity Range | ΔE_p | **mV** | Width of passive window |
| Pitting Resistance Equiv. | PREN | **dimensionless** | Resistance to pitting |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Steels Used in Marine Service

Marine engineering employs a wide spectrum of steels. Their corrosion behaviour is dictated primarily by the **Fe–C–X ternary/quaternary system** (X = Cr, Ni, Mo, Cu, Mn, Si, etc.).

### 2.1.1 Carbon Steels (Plain Carbon Steels)
- **Composition:** Fe + C (0.02%–2.0%) + residual Mn, Si, S, P.
- **Sub-classes:**
  - **Low Carbon (Mild) Steel:** C < 0.30% — most common marine structural steel.
  - **Medium Carbon Steel:** 0.30%–0.60% — used for shafts, couplings.
  - **High Carbon Steel:** 0.60%–1.0% — springs, cutting tools.
- **Microstructure:** Ferrite (α-Fe, BCC) + Pearlite (alternating α-Fe + Fe₃C lamellae).
- **Corrosion Tendency:** **VERY HIGH** in seawater. No passivation. Forms porous, non-protective rust (Fe₂O₃·H₂O / FeO(OH)).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Point:** Carbon steel is **NOT** corrosion-resistant by composition. Its only corrosion defence is **coating** (paint, cathodic protection) or **section thickness allowance** (corrosion allowance of 1–3 mm on hull plates).

### 2.1.2 Low-Alloy / High-Strength Low-Alloy (HSLA) Steels
- **Composition:** C < 0.20% + small additions of Mn (up to 1.5%), Cr, Ni, Mo, Cu (each 0.2%–1.0%), plus microalloying (Nb, V, Ti).
- **Corrosion Tendency:** **MODERATE** — about 2–4× better than mild steel in marine atmosphere, but not in immersed zones.
- **Mechanism:** Cu and P promote formation of a *denser, adherent rust layer* that reduces oxygen diffusion to the substrate.

### 2.1.3 Weathering Steels (e.g., COR-TEN®)
- **Composition:** Fe + C (low) + **Cu (0.25–0.55%)**, **Cr (0.5–1.5%)**, **P (0.07–0.15%)**, **Ni (≤0.65%)**.
- **Corrosion Tendency:** **LOW in atmospheric exposure** but **HIGH in immersed/splash zones**.
- **Mechanism:** Forms a *stable, tightly adherent, dark brown patina* of Cu/Cr-enriched amorphous oxy-hydroxide that acts as a self-healing barrier.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trap:** Weathering steel is **NOT** suitable for permanently immersed marine service (e.g., submerged hulls, offshore legs). It is specified only for **splash zone** and **atmospheric zone** marine structures.

### 2.1.4 Stainless Steels
Stainless steels are the **Cr-bearing alloys (Cr ≥ 10.5%)** that derive corrosion resistance from a passive Cr₂O₃ film.

| Sub-class | Microstructure | Typical Grade | Marine Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Austenitic** | FCC (γ-Fe) | SS 304, SS 316, SS 316L | Piping, valves, propeller shafts |
| **Ferritic** | BCC (α-Fe) | SS 430, SS 409 | Heat exchanger tubes (low Cl⁻) |
| **Martensitic** | BCT (α'-Fe) | SS 410, SS 420 | Pump shafts, cutlery |
| **Duplex** | α + γ (50:50) | SAF 2205, SAF 2507 | Seawater piping, offshore umbilicals |
| **Super-austenitic** | FCC + 6% Mo | SS 254 SMO, AL-6XN | High-Cl⁻ service, desalination |
| **Precipitation-Hardening (PH)** | Martensitic + intermetallics | 17-4 PH | Marine fasteners, propeller hubs |

### 2.1.5 Cast Iron
- **Composition:** Fe + C (2–4%) + Si (1–3%).
- **Forms:** Grey, White, Ductile (Nodular), Malleable.
- **Corrosion Tendency:** **MODERATE** in seawater, but suffers from **graphitic corrosion** — the iron matrix corrodes, leaving a soft, porous graphite network (a phenomenon called *graphitisation*).
- **Marine Use:** Historically for sea-water piping (now largely replaced by GRP/cupronickel/SS).

## 2.2 The Six Key Factors That Govern the Tendency of Corrosion in Steel

### Factor 1 — Carbon Content and Microstructural Heterogeneity
- Higher C → more **Fe₃C (cementite) cementite network** → more **micro-galvanic cells** between α-Fe (anode) and Fe₃C (cathode).
- This causes **graphitic corrosion** and **pitting at pearlite colonies**.

### Factor 2 — Alloying Elements (Cr, Ni, Mo, Cu, Si, Al)
- **Cr (≥10.5%):** Forms passive **Cr₂O₃** film — the *single most important alloying element* for corrosion resistance.
- **Ni:** Stabilises austenite; improves resistance to **SCC in caustic media**.
- **Mo (2–6%):** Dramatically improves **pitting and crevice corrosion resistance** in chloride media. Forms MoO₃ / MoCl₃ barrier at pit bottoms.
- **Cu:** Promotes dense rust patina in weathering steels.
- **Si, Al:** Improve **high-temperature oxidation** resistance (form SiO₂ / Al₂O₃ scales).

### Factor 3 — Heat Treatment & Microstructure
- **Annealed (ferrite + pearlite)** → heterogeneous → micro-galvanic corrosion.
- **Quenched & Tempered (tempered martensite)** → homogeneous → uniform corrosion.
- **Solution-annealed (austenitic SS)** → fully austenitic → best general corrosion resistance.
- **Sensitised (austenitic SS heated 450–850°C)** → **Cr-depleted zones** at grain boundaries → **intergranular corrosion (IGC)**.

### Factor 4 — Environmental Variables
- **pH:** Below pH 4 → active corrosion of Fe; above pH 9 → tendency to passivate (in aerated conditions).
- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** Higher DO → higher corrosion rate (cathodic reaction = O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻).
- **Chloride (Cl⁻) concentration:** Aggressively breaks passive film on SS → **pitting**.
- **Temperature:** Increases rate via Arrhenius kinetics; but also lowers O₂ solubility.
- **Velocity/Flow:** High velocity → **erosion–corrosion**, **cavitation damage**.

### Factor 5 — Stress State
- **Tensile stress** + **corrosive environment** → **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)**.
- Susceptible alloys:
  - Austenitic SS in **Cl⁻** media (transgranular SCC).
  - Mild steel in **hot caustic / hot nitrate / carbonate–bicarbonate** (caustic embrittlement).

### Factor 6 — Position in the Galvanic Series (Most Critical for Marine!)
The marine galvanic series ranks alloys in **flowing seawater** (ASTM G82). Relative nobility:

```
NOBLE (Cathode, Protected)  ←───────────────────  ACTIVE (Anode, Corrodes)
   Pt, Au
   Graphite
   Ti
   Super-austenitic SS (6% Mo)
   Duplex SS 2507
   Austenitic SS 316 (passive)
   Austenitic SS 304 (passive)
   Ferritic SS 430
   Ni–Al Bronze
   90/10 Cu–Ni
   Admiralty Brass
   Aluminium Bronze
   Cast Iron
   Carbon Steel (mild steel)
   HSLA Steel
   Aluminium alloys
   Zinc
   Magnesium                    ← MOST ACTIVE (sacrificial anode)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Hook:**
> *"Explain with sketches the tendency of corrosion in various types of steel in a marine environment."*
> A complete answer MUST contain:
> 1. Classification of steels.
> 2. The galvanic position of each.
> 3. The passivation mechanism for SS.
> 4. At least one marine case study (e.g., SS 304 vs SS 316 in ballast tanks).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Faraday's Law of Corrosion | $CR = \frac{K \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | **mmpy** | Converts lab i_corr to engineering corrosion rate |
| 2 | Pilling–Bedworth Ratio | $PBR = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$ | dimensionless | Predicts oxide protectiveness; ideal 1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2 |
| 3 | PREN (Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number) | $PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$ | dimensionless | Grades SS; PREN > 32 required for warm seawater |
| 4 | Nernst Equation (25 °C) | $E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0592}{n}\log\frac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ | V (SHE) | Predicts equilibrium potential |
| 5 | Tafel Slope Relation | $\eta = \beta \log \frac{i}{i_{0}}$ | V | Determines i_corr from polarisation curves |
| 6 | Mixed-Potential (Evans Diagram) | $E_{a=c} \rightarrow i_{corr}$ | μA/cm² | Predicts galvanic current between two metals |
| 7 | Pourbaix Boundary (Fe) | $E = -0.440 - 0.0591 \text{pH}$ | V | Defines Fe/Fe²⁺ active-passive boundary |
| 8 | Arrhenius Corrosion Rate | $CR = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | mmpy | Temperature dependence of corrosion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Numerical Constants Used in the Above Formulas:**
> - **K** (Faraday constant for rate conversion) = **3.27 × 10⁻³ mm·g/(μA·cm·yr)**.
> - **EW** (Equivalent Weight of Fe) = **27.92 g/eq**.
> - **ρ_Fe** = **7.87 g/cm³**.
> - **F** (Faraday) = **96,485 C/eq**.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Marine Engineering

- **Hull Plate Selection:** Cost-driven mild steel requires **cathodic protection + coating**; HSLA allows **thinner plates** at equivalent strength.
- **Propeller Material:** Manganese–nickel–aluminium bronze (MAB) selected over stainless to avoid SCC in salt water.
- **Seawater Cooling Pipes:** Cu–Ni 90/10 or 70/30 used to resist **impingement attack**.
- **Offshore Platform Jackets:** Splash-zone sections may use **weathering steel** + protective coating; submerged zone uses **mild steel + cathodic protection (Al or Zn anodes)**.
- **Desalination Plants:** Super-austenitic or duplex SS (PREN > 40) mandatory due to high Cl⁻ and warm brine temperatures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday's Law of Corrosion (Engineering Form)

The fundamental Faraday relationship for mass loss is:

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where **m** is the mass of metal dissolved (g), **I** is the anodic current (A), **t** is the time (s), **M** is the molar mass (g/mol), **n** is the valency of the dissolving ion, and **F = 96,485 C/eq** is the Faraday constant.

### Step-by-Step Conversion to Corrosion Rate (CR) in mmpy

**Step 1:** Start with the mass loss equation (above).

**Step 2:** Express current density **i** (A/cm²) and time **t** (s):

$$I = i \cdot A$$

where **A** is the exposed surface area in cm². Substituting:

$$m = \frac{i \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3:** Convert mass loss to volume loss using the metal density **ρ** (g/cm³):

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{i \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 4:** Convert volume to a *linear* thickness loss **Δh** (cm) by dividing by area:

$$\Delta h = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 5:** Convert seconds to years (1 yr = 3.1536 × 10⁷ s) and cm to mm (× 10 mm/cm):

$$CR = \Delta h \cdot \frac{3.1536 \times 10^{7} \text{ s}}{\text{yr}} \cdot \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{\text{cm}}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting the Faraday constant F = 96,485 C/eq and grouping the numerical prefactors, the **practical KTU engineering form** is obtained:

$$\boxed{CR = \frac{K \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}}$$

where:
- **K = 3.27 × 10⁻³ mm·g/(μA·cm·yr)**
- **EW = M / n** (equivalent weight, g/eq)
- **i_corr** is corrosion current density in **μA/cm²**
- **ρ** is metal density in **g/cm³**
- **CR** is the corrosion rate in **mmpy** (millimetres per year)

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A marine structural steel plate exhibits a corrosion current density **i_corr = 10 μA/cm²** in seawater. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mmpy**.

**Given:**
- i_corr = 10 μA/cm²
- For Fe in aerated seawater, the anodic reaction is Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻, so n = 2.
- M_Fe = 55.85 g/mol
- ρ_Fe = 7.87 g/cm³

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute equivalent weight:

$$EW = \frac{M}{n} = \frac{55.85}{2} = 27.925 \text{ g/eq}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the engineering form:

$$CR = \frac{(3.27 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (10) \cdot (27.925)}{7.87}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator terms:

$$(3.27 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (10) \cdot (27.925) = 0.9131$$

**Step 4:** Divide by density:

$$CR = \frac{0.9131}{7.87} = 0.1160 \text{ mmpy}$$

**Step 5:** Engineering interpretation:

A corrosion rate of **~0.12 mmpy** indicates *mild uniform corrosion* — typical of carbon steel in quiescent, aerated seawater. To extend a 20-year hull life, an additional **corrosion allowance of ~2.3 mm** must be specified.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) for Iron

The PBR governs whether the oxide layer is **protective** (1 < PBR < 2), **cracking** (PBR < 1), or **spalling** (PBR > 2). For iron (Fe → Fe₂O₃ / Fe₃O₄):

**Step 1:** Identify oxide and metal parameters. For hematite **Fe₂O₃** on Fe:
- M_oxide = 159.69 g/mol
- M_metal = 55.85 g/mol
- n (number of metal atoms in one mole of oxide) = 2
- ρ_oxide ≈ 5.25 g/cm³
- ρ_metal ≈ 7.87 g/cm³

**Step 2:** Apply the PBR formula:

$$PBR = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values:

$$PBR = \frac{159.69 \cdot 7.87}{2 \cdot 55.85 \cdot 5.25}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator:

$$159.69 \times 7.87 = 1256.76$$

**Step 5:** Compute denominator:

$$2 \times 55.85 \times 5.25 = 586.43$$

**Step 6:** Final ratio:

$$PBR_{Fe} = \frac{1256.76}{586.43} = 2.14$$

### Interpretation
PBR > 2 → the oxide **exfoliates (spalls)** under growth stresses. This is precisely why **carbon steel rusts progressively** — the Fe₂O₃·H₂O (hydrated rust) layer keeps flaking off, exposing fresh metal. Stainless steel overcomes this because its **Cr₂O₃ passive film has PBR ≈ 1.7** (ideal protective behaviour).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PREN Comparison of Common Marine Stainless Steels

The Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number is:

$$PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$$

| Steel | %Cr | %Mo | %N | PREN | Seawater Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SS 304 | 18.0 | 0.0 | 0.05 | 18.8 | **NOT** suitable for Cl⁻ service |
| SS 316 | 16.5 | 2.0 | 0.05 | 23.2 | Limited — pitting in warm seawater |
| SS 316L | 17.0 | 2.5 | 0.05 | 25.3 | Marginal for warm seawater |
| Duplex 2205 | 22.0 | 3.0 | 0.15 | **32.3** | **Good** for ambient seawater |
| Super-duplex 2507 | 25.0 | 4.0 | 0.30 | **43.0** | **Excellent** — offshore umbilicals |
| Super-austenitic 254 SMO | 20.0 | 6.0 | 0.20 | **43.0** | **Excellent** — desalination |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule-of-Thumb:**
> - **PREN < 25** → not for marine immersion.
> - **25 ≤ PREN < 32** → limited marine service, must avoid stagnant Cl⁻.
> - **PREN ≥ 32** → recommended for warm / flowing seawater.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Corrosion Behaviour of Steels in Marine Zones

| Steel Type | Atmospheric | Splash Zone | Tidal Zone | Immersed (Submerged) | Mud Zone |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Carbon Steel | Low | **HIGH** | **HIGHEST** | Moderate | Variable |
| HSLA Steel | Low–Moderate | High | High | Moderate | Variable |
| Weathering Steel | **Lowest** | **Low (after patination)** | High | **NOT recommended** | High |
| Austenitic SS 304 | Very Low | Moderate (pitting) | **Pitting** | **NOT recommended** | Severe pitting |
| Austenitic SS 316 | Very Low | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Pitting risk |
| Duplex SS 2205 | Negligible | Very Low | Very Low | **Low** | Low |
| Cast Iron (Grey) | Low | Moderate | High (graphitic) | High (graphitic) | Severe graphitisation |
| Cu–Ni 90/10 | Negligible | Very Low | Very Low | **Very Low** | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *tidal and splash zones* are the **most aggressive** corrosion zones in marine service due to **alternate wet–dry cycling** (oxygen replenishment), **biological fouling**, and **mechanical impact of waves**. This is why **corrosion protection design** always concentrates thickness allowance, coating, and cathodic protection in this band.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Classification of Steels for Marine Service

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STEELS FOR MARINE SERVICE] --> B[Carbon Steels]
    A --> C[Alloy Steels]
    A --> D[Stainless Steels]
    A --> E[Cast Irons]

    B --> B1[Low Carbon less than 0.30 percent - Mild Steel]
    B --> B2[Medium Carbon 0.30 to 0.60 percent]
    B --> B3[High Carbon 0.60 to 1.0 percent]

    C --> C1[HSLA High Strength Low Alloy]
    C --> C2[Weathering Steels Cu Cr P]
    C --> C3[Ni Cr Mo Alloy Steels]

    D --> D1[Austenitic SS 304 316 316L]
    D --> D2[Ferritic SS 430 409]
    D --> D3[Martensitic SS 410 420]
    D --> D4[Duplex SS 2205 2507]
    D --> D5[Super Austenitic SS 254 SMO]
    D --> D6[Precipitation Hardening 17-4 PH]

    E --> E1[Grey Cast Iron]
    E --> E2[White Cast Iron]
    E --> E3[Ductile Nodular Cast Iron]
    E --> E4[Malleable Cast Iron]

    D1 --> R1[Passive Cr2O3 film - Good Cl pitting risk]
    D4 --> R2[Duplex structure - High PREN - Excellent in seawater]
    C2 --> R3[Patina - Good atmospheric only]
    B1 --> R4[Active - Requires coating and CP]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Six Factors Governing the Tendency of Corrosion in Steel

```mermaid
graph LR
    F[TENDENCY OF CORROSION IN STEEL] --> P1[Factor 1 Chemical Composition]
    F --> P2[Factor 2 Microstructure]
    F --> P3[Factor 3 Heat Treatment]
    F --> P4[Factor 4 Marine Environment]
    F --> P5[Factor 5 Applied Stress]
    F --> P6[Factor 6 Galvanic Coupling]

    P1 --> S1[Carbon content increases micro galvanic cells]
    P1 --> S2[Cr greater than 10.5 percent enables passivation]
    P1 --> S3[Mo greater than 2 percent boosts pitting resistance]
    P1 --> S4[Cu promotes dense rust patina]

    P2 --> M1[Ferrite plus Pearlite - heterogeneous - micro galvanic]
    P2 --> M2[Austenite FCC - homogeneous - passive]
    M1 --> M3[Accelerated localised corrosion]
    M2 --> M4[Uniform corrosion or stable passivity]

    P3 --> H1[Annealing - ferrite plus pearlite]
    P3 --> H2[Quenching plus Tempering - tempered martensite]
    P3 --> H3[Sensitisation 450 to 850 C - Cr depletion - IGC]
    H3 --> H4[Intergranular corrosion in austenitic SS]

    P4 --> E1[pH - acidic accelerates active corrosion]
    P4 --> E2[Chloride ions - pitting and crevice attack]
    P4 --> E3[Dissolved oxygen - cathodic depolariser]
    P4 --> E4[Flow velocity - erosion corrosion]
    P4 --> E5[Temperature - Arrhenius kinetics]

    P5 --> T1[Tensile stress plus Cl - SCC of austenitic SS]
    P5 --> T2[Hot caustic - caustic embrittlement of mild steel]
    P5 --> T3[Cyclic stress plus seawater - corrosion fatigue]

    P6 --> G1[Galvanic Series Position]
    P6 --> G2[Area ratio anode to cathode effect]
    P6 --> G3[Dissimilar metal coupling in marine pipelines]
    G1 --> G2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Pourbaix Diagram of Fe-H2O System (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    PB[POURBAIX DIAGRAM Fe H2O - 25 C] --> Z1[Region 1 - LOW pH HIGH E - Fe2 plus stable - ACTIVE CORROSION]
    PB --> Z2[Region 2 - MODERATE pH HIGH E - Fe2O3 stable - PASSIVE]
    PB --> Z3[Region 3 - HIGH pH HIGH E - FeO4 2- stable - TRANSPASSIVE]
    PB --> Z4[Region 4 - LOW pH LOW E - Fe metal stable - IMMUNE]
    PB --> Z5[Region 5 - MODERATE pH LOW E - Fe3O4 stable - MILD PROTECTION]
    PB --> Z6[Region 6 - HIGH pH - HFeO2 minus stable - ACTIVE again]

    Z2 --> APP[STAINLESS STEEL OPERATES HERE WITH Cr - Cr2O3 passive film stable from pH 4 to pH 10]
    Z1 --> MAR[CARBON STEEL IN MARINE pH 8 - OXYGENATED ZONE CORRODES ACTIVELY TO Fe2 plus]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Marine Corrosion Zones on a Steel Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST[STEEL MARINE STRUCTURE VERTICAL] --> AT[ZONE 1 ATMOSPHERIC - ABOVE SPLASH - DRY]
    ST --> SP[ZONE 2 SPLASH - WAVES IMPACT - WET DRY CYCLES - MOST AGGRESSIVE]
    ST --> TD[ZONE 3 TIDAL - ALTERNATE IMMERSION AND EMERSION - HIGH]
    ST --> IM[ZONE 4 IMMERSED - CONTINUOUS SUBMERGENCE - CATHODIC PROTECTION ACTIVE]
    ST --> MD[ZONE 5 MUD - BELOW SEABED - ANAEROBIC - SRB ATTACK POSSIBLE]

    AT --> C1[Carbon steel OK with coating]
    SP --> C2[HSLA plus cathodic protection plus coating]
    TD --> C3[Duplex SS or weathering steel plus CP]
    IM --> C4[Carbon steel plus Al or Zn sacrificial anodes]
    MD --> C5[Carbon steel plus CP plus biocides for SRB]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exam:**
> Mermaid diagrams render natively in GitHub, GitLab, and most modern Markdown editors. For the KTU answer sheet, students must **redraw** the key logic in **hand-sketched block diagrams** — these are mandatory for full marks in a 14-mark question.
