# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*"Define Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR). What is the PBR value for iron, and what does it indicate about the protectiveness of the rust layer formed on carbon steel?"*

**Model Answer:**

The **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** is the ratio of the molar volume of the oxide formed on a metal to the molar volume of the parent metal consumed:

$$PBR = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$$

For iron, PBR ≈ **2.14** (for Fe₂O₃ on Fe).

**Interpretation:**
- **PBR < 1** → oxide is *porous and non-protective* (tensile stress in film).
- **1 < PBR < 2** → **protective** oxide (compressive stress, adherent, self-healing) — e.g., Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel.
- **PBR > 2** → oxide is *buckled, cracked, and spalls off* — e.g., Fe₂O₃ rust on carbon steel.

Since PBR_Fe > 2, the rust on carbon steel **does not protect** the underlying metal — it continuously flakes off, exposing fresh iron to further corrosion. This is the fundamental reason why carbon steel corrodes **uniformly and progressively** in marine environments.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*"What is the Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)? Calculate the PREN of an austenitic stainless steel containing 18% Cr, 2% Mo, and 0.10% N. Comment on its suitability for seawater service."*

**Model Answer:**

The **PREN** is an empirical number that ranks stainless steels based on their resistance to **pitting corrosion in chloride environments**:

$$PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$$

**Calculation:**

$$PREN = 18 + 3.3 \times 2 + 16 \times 0.10$$

$$PREN = 18 + 6.6 + 1.6 = 26.2$$

**Suitability:**
A PREN of **26.2** places this steel (similar to SS 316) in the **marginal category** for seawater service. It is **acceptable for short-term or low-temperature marine service**, but **not recommended for warm, stagnant, or chloride-rich seawater** where crevice and pitting corrosion are likely. For such service, a duplex steel (PREN ≥ 32) or super-austenitic steel (PREN ≥ 40) is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question — 14 Marks]**
*"Classify the various types of steel used in marine applications. Discuss in detail the tendency of corrosion in each type, with special reference to their position in the galvanic series and the mechanisms of corrosion (uniform, pitting, crevice, SCC)."*

#### Part (a) — Classification of Steels and Their Tendency of Corrosion (7 Marks)

**1. Carbon Steels (Mild, Medium, High)**
- Composition: Fe + C (0.02–2.0%) + residual Mn, Si, S, P.
- Microstructure: Ferrite + Pearlite.
- Corrosion Tendency: **VERY HIGH** in seawater (0.1–0.5 mmpy uniform).
- Mechanism: **Uniform corrosion** with porous Fe₂O₃·H₂O rust layer (PBR > 2).
- Galvanic Position: Active end (anodic).

**2. Low-Alloy / HSLA Steels**
- Composition: C < 0.20% + Mn, Cu, Cr, Ni (each < 1.0%) + microalloying Nb, V, Ti.
- Corrosion Tendency: **MODERATE** (2–4× better than mild steel in atmosphere).
- Mechanism: Formation of **denser rust patina** due to Cu and P enrichment.
- Galvanic Position: Slightly more noble than mild steel but still active.

**3. Weathering Steels (e.g., COR-TEN)**
- Composition: Low C + Cu (0.25–0.55%) + Cr (0.5–1.5%) + P (0.07–0.15%).
- Corrosion Tendency: **LOW in atmosphere**, **HIGH in immersion**.
- Mechanism: Stable amorphous oxy-hydroxide patina acts as a self-healing barrier.
- Limitation: NOT suitable for permanent immersion.

**4. Stainless Steels**
- Cr ≥ 10.5% → passive **Cr₂O₃ film** (~2 nm thick).
- **Austenitic SS 304/316:** General corrosion rate very low (<0.01 mmpy) but susceptible to **pitting, crevice corrosion, and SCC** in Cl⁻ environments.
- **Ferritic SS 430:** Lower Cr → less pitting resistance; used in low-Cl⁻ service.
- **Martensitic SS 410/420:** Heat-treatable; used in cutlery, pump shafts; moderate corrosion resistance.
- **Duplex SS 2205/2507:** Combined α + γ → high strength, high PREN, excellent in seawater; resistant to **SCC**.
- **Super-Austenitic SS (6% Mo):** PREN > 40, used in desalination.

**5. Cast Iron**
- Composition: Fe + C (2–4%) + Si.
- Forms: Grey, White, Ductile, Malleable.
- Corrosion Tendency: **MODERATE** to **HIGH** in seawater; **graphitic corrosion** leaves soft graphite skeleton.

**6. Tool Steels & High-Speed Steels**
- Not used in marine immersion but used in marine workshops. Susceptible to rusting in storage.

#### Part (b) — Galvanic Series and Mechanisms of Corrosion (7 Marks)

**Galvanic Series in Flowing Seawater (ASTM G82, from noble to active):**

```
Pt → Graphite → Ti → Super-Austenitic SS (6% Mo)
   → Duplex SS 2507 → SS 316 (passive) → SS 304 (passive)
   → Ferritic SS 430 → Ni-Al Bronze → Cu-Ni 90/10
   → Admiralty Brass → Cast Iron → Carbon Steel
   → HSLA Steel → Aluminium Alloys → Zn → Mg
```

**Key Corrosion Mechanisms:**

1. **Uniform Corrosion:** Carbon steel, HSLA in seawater; rate ~0.1–0.5 mmpy.
2. **Pitting Corrosion:** Austenitic SS 304/316 in Cl⁻; requires PREN upgrade or cathodic protection.
3. **Crevice Corrosion:** SS gaskets, flanges, tube-sheet joints in seawater.
4. **Intergranular Corrosion (IGC):** Sensitised austenitic SS; remedy = use low-carbon grade (SS 304L, 316L) or stabilised grade (SS 321 with Ti, SS 347 with Nb).
5. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC):** Austenitic SS in hot Cl⁻ solution; mitigation = use duplex SS or change environment.
6. **Graphitic Corrosion:** Cast iron; selective leaching of Fe matrix.

**Design Implications for Marine Engineers:**

- Avoid coupling **carbon steel** (anode) with **stainless steel** (cathode) — accelerated galvanic attack.
- Always insulate dissimilar metals with **dielectric unions**.
- Specify **PREN ≥ 32** for any stainless component in permanent seawater service.
- Use **cathodic protection (sacrificial Al/Zn anodes or impressed current)** for submerged carbon steel hulls.
- Apply **coatings** in splash and atmospheric zones.
- For ballast tanks: combine **coating + ICCP + zinc anodes** for double protection.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question — 14 Marks]**
*"Explain the mechanisms of corrosion protection in stainless steels. Discuss the role of alloying elements (Cr, Ni, Mo, N) in determining the corrosion behaviour of stainless steels in marine environments. Compare austenitic, ferritic, martensitic, and duplex stainless steels with respect to their marine applicability."*

#### Part (a) — Mechanism of Passivation and Role of Alloying Elements (7 Marks)

**1. Passivation Mechanism in Stainless Steels**

Stainless steel derives its corrosion resistance from a **passive film** of chromium-rich oxide, principally **Cr₂O₃** (sometimes written as **Cr₂O₃·(H₂O)ₓ** for hydrated films). This film is:

- **Extremely thin** — typically 1–3 nm (10–30 Å).
- **Self-healing** — if mechanically damaged, it reforms instantly in the presence of oxygen.
- **Amorphous/glassy** — non-porous, blocks anodic dissolution.
- **Stable across a wide pH range (4–10)** — visible as the "passive zone" in the Pourbaix diagram for Cr-H₂O.

**Formation Reaction:**

$$2\text{Cr} + \frac{3}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 \quad (\Delta G^{\circ} \approx -1058 \text{ kJ/mol, highly spontaneous})$$

**Critical Cr Threshold:** A minimum of **10.5% Cr** is required to form a continuous, stable passive film. Below this threshold, the film is discontinuous and offers no corrosion resistance.

**2. Role of Individual Alloying Elements**

**Chromium (Cr):**
- Primary passivator; minimum 10.5% needed.
- Cr contributes to **PREN linearly** (1% Cr → +1 PREN).
- Forms **Cr₂O₃** passive layer; also promotes **Cr-rich carbides (Cr₂₃C₆)** in grain boundaries if not stabilised → IGC.

**Nickel (Ni):**
- Austenite stabiliser (FCC structure).
- Improves resistance to **reducing acids** and **SCC in caustic media**.
- Does NOT contribute to PREN directly but improves toughness and weldability.

**Molybdenum (Mo):**
- Most effective element against **pitting and crevice corrosion in Cl⁻**.
- Mo is **3.3× more effective than Cr** on a weight percent basis (hence the factor 3.3 in PREN).
- Forms **MoO₃ / MoCl₃** barrier layer at pit bottoms, stabilising the passive film against Cl⁻ attack.
- Required in 2–6% for marine grades.

**Nitrogen (N):**
- Austenite stabiliser (like Ni).
- **16× more effective than Cr** on weight basis in PREN formula.
- Suppresses pitting by forming **nitrate ions (NO₃⁻)** at pit bottoms → repassivation.
- Common in duplex and super-austenitic grades.

#### Part (b) — Comparative Study of SS Families for Marine Use (7 Marks)

| Property | Austenitic (SS 304/316) | Ferritic (SS 430) | Martensitic (SS 410) | Duplex (SS 2205) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Crystal Structure | FCC (γ) | BCC (α) | BCT (α') | FCC + BCC (50:50) |
| Typical Cr (%) | 16–20 | 11–17 | 11–18 | 21–25 |
| Typical Ni (%) | 8–14 | < 0.75 | < 1.0 | 4–8 |
| Mo (%) | 0–3 | 0 | 0 | 2.5–4 |
| Magnetic | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| PREN (typical) | 18–25 | 15–18 | 12–16 | 32–40 |
| Pitting Resistance | Low to Moderate | Low | Very Low | **Excellent** |
| SCC Resistance | **Poor in Cl⁻** | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Strength (MPa) | 520 | 450 | 700 (Q&T) | 700–900 |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | Low | High |
| Marine Application | Trim, fittings | Heat exchangers | Pump shafts | Piping, umbilicals, fasteners |

**Detailed Marine Applicability:**

1. **Austenitic SS 304** — General marine atmospheric service; **NOT** for Cl⁻ immersion; susceptible to pitting and SCC.

2. **Austenitic SS 316/316L** — Acceptable for short-term seawater service; used in pump casings, valve trim; requires regular inspection for pitting.

3. **Ferritic SS 430** — Low cost; **NOT** for seawater; used in decorative marine hardware and inland heat exchangers.

4. **Martensitic SS 410** — Hardenable to high strength; used for marine propeller shafts, fasteners, and pump impellers; moderate corrosion resistance; often **cathodically protected** when immersed.

5. **Duplex SS 2205/2507** — The **workhorse of marine engineering** for seawater service; used in ballast piping, offshore umbilicals, seawater reverse osmosis (SWRO) plants, and chemical tankers. High strength allows **thinner wall sections** (cost savings).

6. **Super-Austenitic SS 254 SMO** — For **hot, high-Cl⁻** environments such as desalination brine heaters (T up to 80°C, Cl⁻ up to 70,000 ppm).

7. **Precipitation-Hardening SS 17-4 PH** — High strength + moderate corrosion resistance; used in marine fasteners, valve stems, propeller hubs.

**Selection Rule for Marine Service:**

$$\text{Choose SS with } PREN \geq 32 \text{ for } T > 25°C \text{ and } Cl^{-} > 5000 \text{ ppm}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Stainless" with "Stain-Proof"** — Many students wrongly state that stainless steels "do not corrode". In fact, they form a passive film that can be **locally broken** (pitting, crevice, SCC). This costs 1–2 marks per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Ignoring the PBR Concept** — A 14-mark question on "tendency of corrosion" **always** expects the student to explain *why* carbon steel rusts progressively (PBR > 2, oxide spalls) and *why* stainless steel is protected (PBR ≈ 1.7, oxide adherent). Skipping PBR = −3 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing the EMF Series and Galvanic Series** — The EMF series lists pure metals in standard conditions; the **galvanic series** lists commercial alloys in actual seawater. **Always** use the galvanic series for marine corrosion questions.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Carbon Effect on Micro-galvanic Cells** — Higher carbon = more cementite (Fe₃C) = more galvanic microcells with ferrite → **graphitic and pitting corrosion in cast iron and high-C steel**.
>
> 5. **Not Mentioning Sensitisation in SS** — A 7-mark sub-part on stainless steel MUST mention **Cr-depletion at grain boundaries** due to Cr₂₃C₆ precipitation in the 450–850°C range → IGC. Use **low-C (L-grade) or stabilised (321 Ti / 347 Nb)** SS to avoid.
>
> 6. **Calculating PREN Incorrectly** — Many students use the wrong formula. The **correct** KTU-accepted formula is: `PREN = %Cr + 3.3·%Mo + 16·%N`. **Do not** use `%Ni` in PREN.
>
> 7. **Ignoring the Tidal/Splash Zone Severity** — The KTU Board loves asking about which zone is the *most aggressive*. Answer: **Tidal/Splash zone** (alternate wet–dry, oxygen replenishment, biological fouling). NOT the immersed zone (which is somewhat protected by CP and marine growth).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes for the Night-Before Exam:**
>
> ✅ **Definition of Corrosion Tendency in Steel:** Inherent susceptibility of a ferrous alloy to electrochemical oxidation, governed by composition, microstructure, environment, and galvanic position.
>
> ✅ **Master Equation:** Faraday's Law — $CR = (K \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW) / \rho$ → gives **mmpy**.
>
> ✅ **PBR Threshold:** PBR < 1 = porous; **1 < PBR < 2 = protective**; PBR > 2 = spalling. For Fe/Fe₂O₃, PBR ≈ **2.14** (non-protective).
>
> ✅ **Cr Threshold for Passivation:** Minimum **10.5% Cr** for stainless classification.
>
> ✅ **PREN Formula:** $PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$. **Rule:** PREN ≥ 32 for warm seawater.
>
> ✅ **Galvanic Order (Noble → Active):** Pt > Ti > Super-SS > Duplex > 316 > 304 > Bronze > Cast Iron > Carbon Steel > Al > Zn > Mg.
>
> ✅ **Sensitisation Window:** 450–850 °C → Cr-depleted grain boundaries → **IGC**. Avoid by using **L-grades** (C < 0.03%) or **stabilised grades** (321-Ti, 347-Nb).
>
> ✅ **SCC Susceptibility:** Austenitic SS in Cl⁻; **Duplex SS is immune** to Cl⁻ SCC.
>
> ✅ **Most Aggressive Marine Zone:** **Tidal/Splash zone** — alternate wet–dry cycling.
>
> ✅ **Weathering Steel:** Cu + Cr + P → dense patina → **atmospheric only**; **NOT for immersion**.
>
> ✅ **Cast Iron Failure Mode:** **Graphitic corrosion** — Fe dissolves, graphite network remains, no strength.
>
> ✅ **Best SS for Marine Seawater Piping:** **Duplex 2205/2507** (PREN 32–40) or **Super-austenitic 254 SMO** (PREN > 40).
>
> ✅ **Carbon Steel Corrosion Rate in Seawater:** ~0.1–0.5 mmpy uniform; **always requires CP + coating**.
>
> ✅ **HSLA vs Mild Steel:** HSLA is ~2–4× more corrosion-resistant in atmosphere (Cu, P effect).
>
> ✅ **Marine Cathodic Protection Materials:** **Aluminium** (preferred) and **Zinc** sacrificial anodes; **Mg** only in low-conductivity freshwater.
>
> ✅ **Quick Mnemonic — "PNIC"** for SS corrosion test: **P**itting, **N**eutral salt spray, **I**GC, **C**revice → 4 standard tests.
>
> ✅ **Key Constants to Memorise:** F = 96,485 C/eq; K (rate conversion) = 3.27 × 10⁻³ mm·g/(μA·cm·yr); EW_Fe = 27.92 g/eq; ρ_Fe = 7.87 g/cm³; E°(Fe²⁺/Fe) = −0.44 V (SHE); E°(O₂/OH⁻) = +0.40 V (SHE).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion of Titanium and Nickel Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Corrosion of Titanium and Nickel Alloys ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Titanium Alloys — Formal Definition **Titanium (Ti)** is a transition metal (Atomic Number 22, Atomic Mass **47.867 g/mol**, Density **4.51 g/cm³**) characterized by an exceptionally high **strength-to-weight ratio**, a **passive oxide film (TiO₂)** that is thermodynamically stable over an enormous range of pH and potential, and the ability to withstand highly aggressive marine environments including natural seawater, chlorinated seawater, and oxidizing acids. Titanium used in marine service is typically the **commercially pure (CP) grade** (Grades 1, 2, 3, 4) or **alpha/beta alloys** such as **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5)**, **Ti-6Al-4V ELI**, and near-alpha alloys like **Ti-6Al-2Sn-4Zr-6Mo**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Marine-grade titanium is almost always the **CP-Ti Grade 2** or the **alpha+beta Ti-6Al-4V Grade 5** alloy. The passive TiO₂ film is the *single most important feature* governing its corrosion behavior — without it, titanium would corrode like any other reactive metal. ### 1.2 Nickel Alloys — Formal Definition **Nickel (Ni)** (Atomic Number 28, Atomic Mass **58.693 g/mol**, Density **8.908 g/cm³**) forms the basis of a family of corrosion-resistant alloys including: - **Ni-Cu alloys (Monel series)** — e.g., Monel 400, Monel K-500 - **Ni-Cr-Fe alloys (Inconel/Incoloy series)** — e.g., Inconel 625, Incoloy 825 - **Ni-Cr-Mo alloys (Hastelloy series)** — e.g., Hastelloy C-276, C-22 - **Ni-Mo alloys (Hastelloy B-series)** The corrosion resistance is conferred by alloying additions of **Cr** (forms Cr₂O₃), **Mo** (forms MoO₃), and **Cu** (resistance to HF and reducing acids), which together build multi-layered, defect-free passive films. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Raincoat" Analogy:** > Imagine you are caught in acid rain. A regular cotton jacket soaks through in seconds (like carbon steel in seawater), but a high-quality raincoat repels every drop because of its hydrophobic polymer coating. The **passive oxide film** on titanium and nickel alloys is exactly that raincoat — an invisible, atomically thin ceramic layer (TiO₂, Cr₂O₃) that forms **spontaneously** the instant the metal touches oxygen or water. As long as the raincoat is intact, the underlying metal is invisible to the corrosive environment. Damage it (mechanically, chemically, or by galvanic action) and the metal is exposed — but in many cases, it **self-repairs (re-passivates)** within milliseconds if oxygen is present. ### 1.3 Why These Metals are Pivotal in Marine Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Titanium** is the material of choice for submarine hulls, seawater piping, heat exchanger tubing, propeller shafts, and offshore platform components subjected to **flow-accelerated corrosion** and **biofouling**. > - **Nickel alloys** dominate applications involving **hot brine, sour (H₂S) service, subsea manifolds, and high-temperature seawater** where titanium's limits are exceeded. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Passivation Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic Both titanium and nickel alloys rely on the **passive film theory**. The mechanism unfolds in the following structured sequence: **Step 1 — Initial Active Dissolution** When freshly exposed metal contacts an electrolyte, anodic dissolution occurs: $$\text{Ti} \rightarrow \text{Ti}^{2+} + 2e^- \quad \text{(transient)}$$ **Step 2 — Hydrolysis and Oxide Nucleation** Water at the metal-electrolyte interface hydrolyzes the cation: $$\text{Ti}^{2+} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ti(OH)}_2 + 2\text{H}^+$$ **Step 3 — Dehydration and Film Formation** The hydroxide converts to the stable oxide: $$\text{Ti(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{TiO}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$ **Step 4 — Film Thickening (Logarithmic Law)** The oxide film grows following a **Cabrera-Mott logarithmic growth law**: $$x = A \ln(Bt + 1)$$ where $x$ is the film thickness, $t$ is time, and $A, B$ are constants. For Ti, the steady-state film thickness in seawater stabilizes around **2–7 nm**. **Step 5 — Maintenance and Repair** Any mechanical or chemical disruption is repaired instantly provided the **redox potential** remains within the passive range (for Ti in seawater: roughly **−0.5 V to +1.5 V vs. SCE**). > [!NOTE] > **The 'Why' Behind Each Step:** > Step 1 happens within microseconds. Step 2 releases protons, locally acidifying the interface. Step 3 produces the highly insulating, chemically inert TiO₂ (rutile structure). Step 4 limits further film growth because the oxide is an excellent electronic insulator — the anodic current drops by 6–8 orders of magnitude. Step 5 is what makes titanium "self-healing" in oxidizing environments. ### 2.2 Electrochemistry of Passivity — Pourbaix Perspective The **Pourbaix Diagram (Potential-pH Diagram)** for Ti-H₂O shows that **TiO₂** is the stable phase over the broad region: $$\text{pH} \in [0, 14], \quad E \in [-1.0, +2.0]\ \text{V (SHE)}$$ This is **exceptional** — no other common structural metal has a passive zone this wide. For nickel, the passive NiO / Ni(OH)₂ zone is narrower (roughly **pH 8–14**), but alloying with **Cr (>16%)** and **Mo (>9%)** expands it dramatically into the acidic chloride region. ### 2.3 Forms of Corrosion Encountered in Marine Service #### A. Forms Affecting Titanium Alloys | # | Form of Corrosion | Triggering Condition | Susceptibility of CP-Ti | |---|-------------------|----------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Crevice Corrosion** | Tight gaps in low-pH, low-O₂ zones (gaskets, threaded joints) | Susceptible **below ~70 °C** if crevice chemistry is severe | | 2 | **Pitting Corrosion** | Bromide/iodide ions, low pH | Highly resistant; pitting potential $\approx +9$ V (SCE) in seawater | | 3 | **Galvanic Corrosion** | Coupled to Cu, Ni, or less-noble metals | Becomes **cathodic** — corrodes the *other* metal | | 4 | **Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)** | Cathodic charging at potentials $<-0.7$ V (SCE), acidic HF, sour H₂S | Major concern under impressed current CP | | 5 | **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** | Methanol, HCl, red-fuming HNO₃ | Generally immune in seawater | #### B. Forms Affecting Nickel Alloys | # | Form of Corrosion | Triggering Condition | Susceptibility | |---|-------------------|----------------------|----------------| | 1 | **Pitting / Crevice Corrosion** | Hot chloride brines, stagnant seawater | Mo-containing grades (C-276, 625) highly resistant | | 2 | **SCC (Chloride)** | Austenitic Ni-Fe-Cr alloys in concentrated MgCl₂ | Monel 400 susceptible in HF/Hg environments | | 3 | **Sulfidation** | H₂S / sour service at high T | Requires Ni-Cr-Mo alloys | | 4 | **Galvanic Corrosion** | Coupled to active metals | Cathodic to steel, but anodic to Ti | | 5 | **Dealloying / Selective Leaching** | Monel in HF — Ni preferentially dissolves | Low in normal seawater | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation / Relation | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---------------------|---------|---------------| | 1 | $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$ | Gibbs free energy ↔ cell potential | kJ/mol | | 2 | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{i_a \cdot i_c}{i_a + i_c}$ | Mixed-potential corrosion current density | A/m² | | 3 | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | ASTM G102 corrosion rate from polarization | mm/yr | | 4 | $\text{Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$ | Empirical ranking for pitting resistance | dimensionless | | 5 | $\Delta E_{\text{galv}} = E_c - E_a$ | Driving voltage for galvanic corrosion | V | | 6 | $R_p = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c) \cdot i_{\text{corr}}}$ | Stern-Geary linear polarization resistance | $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$ | | 7 | $x = A \ln(Bt + 1)$ | Cabrera-Mott oxide film growth | nm | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note on PREN:** Always remember the coefficient **3.3 for Mo** and **16 for N**. For nickel alloys used in marine service, a **PREN > 40** is the gold standard (Hastelloy C-276 has PREN ≈ 69; Inconel 625 has PREN ≈ 51). For titanium, PREN is not used because of the **extraordinary stability of TiO₂**. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Submarines:** Russian *Severodvinsk*-class and US *Seawolf*-class submarines use **Ti-6Al-4V ELI** hulls to depths beyond **600 m** where HY-80/100 steel would require excessive thickness. - **Desalination Plants:** Multi-stage flash (MSF) and multi-effect distillation (MED) brine heaters use **Inconel 625** or **C-276** tubing because of hot brine (90–120 °C) attack. - **Offshore Risers and Piping:** Flexible risers use **Incoloy 825** cladding for sour service. - **Cathodic Protection Anode Backings:** Titanium substrate coated with Pt/Ir/MMO acts as inert anode in impressed current CP systems — the Ti does not consume. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Corrosion Rate from Polarization Data (ASTM G102) The **ASTM G102 standard** allows us to compute the corrosion current density $i_{\text{corr}}$ and translate it into a uniform penetration rate. We will now derive this end-to-end. **Given:** - Tafel slopes: $\beta_a = 0.060$ V/decade, $\beta_c = 0.120$ V/decade - $i_{\text{corr}}$ measured from Tafel plot: $i_{\text{corr}} = 4.5 \times 10^{-6}$ A/cm² - Equivalent weight (EW) of titanium: $\text{EW} = \dfrac{M}{n} = \dfrac{47.867}{4} \approx 11.97$ g/eq (Ti → Ti⁴⁺ + 4e⁻ assumed; for Ti → Ti²⁺ → Ti⁴⁺ averaged to $n = 4$) - Density of titanium: $\rho = 4.51$ g/cm³ - Faraday constant: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol **Unit conversion constant:** $$K = \dfrac{M \cdot 31{,}152{,}000}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot 10^6} \quad \text{[if used with g/cm²·yr conversion]}$$ The simplified working form (for mm/yr with $i_{\text{corr}}$ in μA/cm², EW in g/eq, ρ in g/cm³): $$K = 0.00327 \quad \text{(mm·g)/(μA·cm·yr)}$$ **Derivation begins:** Step 1 — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis. The mass of metal dissolved per unit area per unit time is: $$m = \dfrac{i_{\text{corr}} \cdot t \cdot EW}{F}$$ Step 2 — Convert mass loss to linear penetration. $$\text{CR} = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot t} = \dfrac{i_{\text{corr}} \cdot EW}{\rho \cdot F}$$ Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (with $i_{\text{corr}}$ converted to A/cm²): $$\text{CR} = \dfrac{(4.5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 11.97}{4.51 \cdot 96{,}485}$$ Step 4 — Numerator evaluation: $$4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 11.97 = 5.3865 \times 10^{-5}$$ Step 5 — Denominator evaluation: $$4.51 \times 96{,}485 = 435{,}147.35$$ Step 6 — Final division: $$\text{CR} = \dfrac{5.3865 \times 10^{-5}}{4.3515 \times 10^{5}} = 1.238 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{cm/s}$$ Step 7 — Convert to mm/yr: $$1.238 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{cm/s} \times \dfrac{1\ \text{mm}}{0.1\ \text{cm}} \times 31{,}536{,}000\ \text{s/yr}$$ $$\boxed{\text{CR}_{\text{Ti}} \approx 0.0390\ \text{mm/yr}}$$ This value is consistent with the **< 0.05 mm/yr** "excellent" category in marine corrosion tables — titanium is essentially immune to uniform corrosion in seawater. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Marine Service Suitability | Environment | CP-Ti Grade 2 | Ti-6Al-4V | Monel 400 | Inconel 625 | Hastelloy C-276 | |-------------|---------------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-----------------| | Natural seawater (ambient) | **Excellent** | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Excellent | | Stagnant seawater | Good (crevice risk) | Good | Fair | Excellent | Excellent | | Hot brine (95 °C) | Limited (crevice) | Limited | Poor | Good | **Excellent** | | Sulfide-containing seawater | Good (HE risk) | Good | Poor | Good | **Excellent** | | Galvanic couple to steel | Cathode — no attack | Cathode — no attack | Cathode — slight | Cathode — no attack | Cathode — no attack | | Hydrofluoric acid | **Severe attack** | Severe | Severe | Severe | Limited | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number > **Problem:** Rank the following nickel alloys by marine pitting resistance: > - Alloy A: 22% Cr, 13% Mo, 0.20% N > - Alloy B: 16% Cr, 16% Mo, 0% N > - Alloy C: 20% Cr, 9% Mo, 0% N **Solution:** For Alloy A: $$\text{PREN}_A = 22 + 3.3(13) + 16(0.20) = 22 + 42.9 + 3.2 = 68.1$$ For Alloy B: $$\text{PREN}_B = 16 + 3.3(16) + 0 = 16 + 52.8 = 68.8$$ For Alloy C: $$\text{PREN}_C = 20 + 3.3(9) + 0 = 20 + 29.7 = 49.7$$ **Ranking:** B (68.8) ≈ A (68.1) ≫ C (49.7). Alloys A and B are seawater-grade superaustenitic/superalloys; Alloy C (Inconel 825 analogue) is adequate for less aggressive service. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Passivation Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startNodeA[Fresh Titanium or Nickel Surface] reactNodeB[Initial Active Dissolution
Ti to Ti2plus or Ni to Ni2plus] hydNodeC[Hydrolysis Reaction
Metal Cation plus Water] oxidNodeD[Stable Passive Oxide Formation
TiO2 or Cr2O3 or MoO3] protectNodeE[Film Thickness Stabilization
Cabrera Mott Logarithmic Law] monitorNodeF[Environment Monitoring] intactNodeG[Film Intact] damageNodeH[Mechanical or Chemical Damage] repairNodeI[Self Repair via Re Passivation
Requires O2 Present] failNodeJ[Film Breakdown and Corrosion Onset] startNodeA --> reactNodeB reactNodeB --> hydNodeC hydNodeC --> oxidNodeD oxidNodeD --> protectNodeE protectNodeE --> monitorNodeF monitorNodeF --> intactNodeG monitorNodeF --> damageNodeH damageNodeH --> repairNodeI damageNodeH --> failNodeJ repairNodeI --> protectNodeE ``` ### 4.2 Galvanic Series in Seawater — Block-Level Position Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ANODE_REGION[More Active Anodic End] aNode1[Zinc] aNode2[Aluminum Alloys] aNode3[Carbon Steel] aNode4[Copper Alloys] aNode5[Nickel Alloys] aNode6[Stainless Steel Passive] aNode7[Titanium Passive] end subgraph CATHODE_REGION[More Noble Cathodic End] bNode1[Platinum] bNode2[Graphite] bNode3[MMO Coated Titanium Anode] end ANODE_REGION --> CATHODE_REGION ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** When **titanium (passive)** is coupled to **carbon steel** in seawater, the steel becomes the sacrificial anode and dissolves rapidly. The titanium suffers **no corrosion** but may experience **hydrogen embrittlement** if the cathodic overpotential pushes its potential below **−0.7 V (SCE)**. ### 4.3 Form-of-Corrosion Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD qNode1[Marine Component in Service] qNode2[Is the alloy titanium based] qNode3[Yes] qNode4[Suspect Crevice and Hydrogen Embrittlement] qNode5[Check Tight Gaps and CP Overprotection] qNode6[No] qNode7[Suspect Pitting and Crevice and Sulfidation] qNode8[Check Stagnant Conditions Hot Brine H2S] qNode9[Apply PREN Screening] qNode1 --> qNode2 qNode2 -->|Yes| qNode3 qNode3 --> qNode4 qNode4 --> qNode5 qNode2 -->|No| qNode6 qNode6 --> qNode7 qNode7 --> qNode8 qNode8 --> qNode9 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] > **Explain why titanium is highly resistant to corrosion in seawater.** **Model Answer:** Titanium spontaneously forms a thin, adherent, and chemically stable **TiO₂ passive film** (2–7 nm thick) on its surface the moment it contacts oxygenated seawater. This oxide film is **electrically insulating**, thermodynamically stable across pH 0–14 and potentials from −1.0 V to +2.0 V (SHE), and **self-repairing** in the presence of even trace oxygen. It blocks both anodic dissolution and cathodic reactions, reducing the corrosion current density by 6–8 orders of magnitude. As a result, the corrosion rate of CP-Ti in seawater is typically **< 0.025 mm/yr**, qualifying it as virtually immune to uniform corrosion. --- > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] > **List three major forms of corrosion encountered in nickel-based alloys in marine service.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Pitting and Crevice Corrosion** in hot chloride brines (especially for alloys with PREN < 40). 2. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** in Monel 400 when exposed to HF, Hg, or aerated HF acid environments. 3. **Sulfidation Attack** in sour (H₂S-containing) high-temperature seawater, requiring Ni-Cr-Mo grades like C-276. *(A third alternative: galvanic corrosion when coupled to more active metals like steel.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) #### **Question A (Choice 1)** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > > **(a)** With neat diagrams and reactions, describe the **passivation mechanism** of titanium in marine environments. Discuss the role of the TiO₂ film and its self-healing capability. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the **forms of corrosion** to which titanium alloys are susceptible in marine service. Mention at least four forms and explain the conditions that promote each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** The passivation of titanium proceeds through five sequential stages: 1. **Active dissolution:** $\text{Ti} \rightarrow \text{Ti}^{2+} + 2e^-$ (transient). 2. **Hydrolysis:** $\text{Ti}^{2+} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ti(OH)}_2 + 2\text{H}^+$. 3. **Oxidation to stable oxide:** $\text{Ti(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{TiO}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$. 4. **Film growth** following the Cabrera-Mott logarithmic law $x = A \ln(Bt+1)$, stabilizing at **2–7 nm** thickness. 5. **Self-repair:** Any mechanical damage is repaired within milliseconds if dissolved oxygen is present (>$1$ ppm). The TiO₂ film is amorphous to crystalline (rutile/anatase), highly insulating (band gap $\approx 3.0$ eV), and chemically inert in chlorides. Its self-healing is guaranteed as long as the corrosion potential remains within the passive window of the Pourbaix diagram. **Model Solution (b):** 1. **Crevice corrosion** — occurs in tight gaps (gasketed joints, threaded couplings) where oxygen depletion drops the local pH below ~1 and Cl⁻ concentrates. Critical crevice temperature for Ti is around **70 °C**. 2. **Hydrogen embrittlement (HE)** — occurs under excessive cathodic protection (potential $<-0.7$ V SCE), acidic HF service, or galvanic coupling to active metals where Ti evolves hydrogen: $\text{TiO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Ti}^{4+} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$, but more dangerously $2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2$ enters the lattice as hydride. 3. **Pitting corrosion** — extremely rare; pitting potential of Ti in seawater is so high ($>+9$ V SCE) that it is virtually never reached. 4. **Galvanic corrosion** — Ti is noble; it accelerates corrosion of coupled active metals (steel, Cu) without itself corroding. 5. **Erosion-corrosion** — minimal due to hard oxide film, but possible at very high flow velocities ($>30$ m/s) with entrained sand. --- #### **Question B (Choice 2)** > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > > **(a)** Classify the major **nickel-based alloys** used in marine applications. Discuss the role of Cr, Mo, and Cu as alloying elements. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A Ni-Cr-Mo alloy contains **20% Cr, 14% Mo, and 0.20% N**. Compute its **PREN** and comment on its suitability for hot chloride brine service. Compare it to a second alloy containing 18% Cr, 10% Mo, and 0% N. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** Classification: - **Ni-Cu alloys** — Monel 400 (70% Ni, 30% Cu); excellent in HF and seawater; susceptible to SCC in Hg. - **Ni-Cr-Fe alloys** — Inconel 600/625, Incoloy 800/825; resistant to oxidation, pitting, and stress corrosion. - **Ni-Cr-Mo alloys** — Hastelloy C-276, C-22, Inconel 625; premier marine alloys for hot brine and sour service. - **Ni-Mo alloys** — Hastelloy B-2/B-3; specialized for non-oxidizing HCl. Role of alloying elements: - **Cr (≥16%):** Forms protective **Cr₂O₃** film; resists oxidation and pitting. - **Mo (≥9%):** Forms **MoO₃**; stabilizes film against chloride attack; raises PREN. - **Cu:** Provides resistance to **HF** and **H₂SO₄** by forming insoluble fluorides; improves resistance to reducing acids. **Model Solution (b):** For Alloy 1: $$\text{PREN}_1 = 20 + 3.3(14) + 16(0.20) = 20 + 46.2 + 3.2 = 69.4$$ For Alloy 2: $$\text{PREN}_2 = 18 + 3.3(10) + 0 = 18 + 33.0 = 51.0$$ **Comment:** Alloy 1 with PREN = 69.4 is in the **superaustenitic/superalloy** category — ideal for hot chloride brine service (e.g., MSF desalination heaters operating at 110–120 °C). Alloy 2 (PREN = 51.0) is acceptable for warm seawater service but has reduced margin in hot, concentrated brines. For critical marine applications, Alloy 1 is preferred. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **PREN coefficient confusion:** Students often write **3.3 × %Mo** as **33 × %Mo** (10× error) — this single mistake loses all 7 marks. > 2. **Reversed galvanism:** Many students wrongly assume Ti corrodes when coupled to steel. Remember: **Ti is the cathode; steel is the anode.** > 3. **Confusing passivation with corrosion:** The TiO₂ film is **not** a corrosion product — it is a **protective** oxide. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 4. **Forgetting equivalent weight (n):** In corrosion rate problems, students frequently use atomic mass instead of equivalent weight. Always divide by the **number of electrons transferred** (n = 4 for Ti → Ti⁴⁺; n = 2 for Ni → Ni²⁺). > 5. **Neglecting hydrogen embrittlement:** Ti is "immune" to general corrosion, **but not** to hydrogen embrittlement under excessive CP — examiners often probe this subtlety. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] Titanium owes its corrosion resistance to a **spontaneously formed TiO₂ passive film**, 2–7 nm thick. - [x] The TiO₂ passive zone on the Pourbaix diagram spans pH **0 to 14** and E from **−1.0 to +2.0 V (SHE)** — extraordinarily wide. - [x] Titanium is **cathodic** in galvanic couples with steel, copper, and most active metals — it does not corrode itself but may suffer **hydrogen embrittlement** at potentials $<-0.7$ V (SCE). - [x] **CP-Ti Grade 2** and **Ti-6Al-4V Grade 5** are the workhorse marine titanium alloys. - [x] Nickel alloys rely on **Cr** (Cr₂O₃), **Mo** (MoO₃), and **Cu** to form multi-component passive films. - [x] **PREN formula** = %Cr + 3.3·%Mo + 16·%N — **mandatory memorization**. - [x] Hot chloride brine service requires **PREN > 40**; for the most severe service (MSF, sour), **PREN > 60** is preferred (Hastelloy C-276 territory). - [x] Marine service forms of corrosion to remember: - For Ti → Crevice, HE, Galvanic - For Ni → Pitting, Crevice, SCC (Monel in HF/Hg), Sulfidation - [x] ASTM G102 corrosion rate formula: $\text{CR} = 0.00327 \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot EW / \rho$ — yields result in **mm/yr** when $i_{\text{corr}}$ is in **μA/cm²**. - [x] Caberra-Mott film growth: $x = A \ln(Bt + 1)$ — explains self-limiting oxide thickness. - [x] **No titanium alloy is suitable for HF service** — HF attacks TiO₂ catastrophically. - [x] **Inconel 625** and **Hastelloy C-276** are the two benchmark nickel alloys for offshore/marine high-temperature service. ---

topics.title: Copper and Copper-Based Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Copper and Copper-Based Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Copper (Cu)** is a **face-centered cubic (FCC)**, reddish-orange, non-ferrous metallic element with atomic number **29** and atomic weight **63.54 g/mol**. In the context of marine engineering, copper and its alloys constitute a critical class of materials extensively used in shipbuilding, offshore structures, seawater piping, propellers, condensers, and heat exchangers due to their unique combination of high **thermal conductivity (~390 W/m·K)**, excellent **electrical conductivity (~58 × 10⁶ S/m)**, superior **corrosion resistance in seawater**, and natural **antifouling (biocidal) behavior**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The syllabus specifically focuses on the classification, composition, mechanical properties, corrosion behavior, and marine applications of **commercially pure copper**, **brasses (Cu-Zn)**, **bronzes (Cu-Sn, Cu-Al, Cu-Si, Cu-Be)**, and **cupronickels (Cu-Ni)**. Students must be able to correlate alloy microstructure with seawater service performance.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are designing a **submarine's seawater cooling system**. You need a material that:
1. Does NOT rust like iron when exposed continuously to salty water.
2. Can efficiently transfer heat from the engine.
3. Resists the growth of barnacles and marine algae on its inner walls.

Pure iron fails all three tests. **Copper is the "ocean's friend"** — think of it as the *stainless steel of the non-ferrous world*, but with a special "anti-germ" superpower. When copper contacts seawater, it slowly releases **Cu²⁺ ions** which are **toxic to marine organisms** — this is why ancient ships used copper sheathing to deter shipworms (Teredo navalis). By **alloying copper with zinc, tin, aluminum, or nickel**, marine engineers can tune the material's hardness, strength, and corrosion resistance much like a chef adjusts spices to perfect a recipe.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Mechanical Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants of Pure Copper (Annealed Condition):**
> - **Density:** **8.96 g/cm³**
> - **Melting Point:** **1083 °C (1356 K)**
> - **Tensile Strength:** **~210–235 MPa**
> - **Yield Strength:** **~33–70 MPa**
> - **Elongation:** **~45–55%**
> - **Hardness (VHN):** **~35–50**
> - **Electrical Resistivity:** **1.724 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m**
> - **Standard Electrode Potential:** **+0.34 V (vs SHE)** — Noble metal

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Pure Copper Alone Is Insufficient for Marine Use

Although pure copper exhibits excellent seawater corrosion resistance (forms a protective **cuprous oxide / cupric chloride / cupric hydroxychloride film**), it suffers from critical limitations:

- **Low mechanical strength** (~210 MPa UTS) — unsuitable for high-stress propellers or shaft components.
- **Susceptibility to velocity-sensitive corrosion** — fails in turbulent, high-velocity seawater (cavitation, impingement, erosion-corrosion).
- **High cost** — strategic and economic constraints push engineers toward alloyed variants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alloying Philosophy:** Marine copper alloys are engineered by adding controlled amounts of secondary elements (Zn, Sn, Al, Ni, Mn, Si, Be) to enhance one or more properties — **strength**, **hardness**, **cavitation resistance**, or **specific corrosion resistance** — while preserving the inherent **biocidal antifouling character** of the copper matrix.

## 2.2 Classification of Marine Copper Alloys

Marine-relevant copper alloys fall into **four major families**:

| Family | Principal Alloying Element | Typical Composition | Marine Examples |
|--------|---------------------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Commercially Pure Copper** | None (≥ 99.3% Cu) | C10100 – C11000 | Seawater piping, heat exchanger tubes |
| **Brasses** | Zinc (Zn) | Cu-30Zn, Cu-35Zn, Cu-40Zn | Marine hardware, pump bodies, valve stems |
| **Bronzes** | Tin / Aluminum / Silicon / Beryllium | Cu-10Sn, Cu-9Al, Cu-3Si, Cu-2Be | Propellers, bearings, gears, marine fasteners |
| **Cupronickels** | Nickel (Ni) + Iron / Manganese | Cu-10Ni-1.5Fe, Cu-30Ni | Condenser tubes, firewater systems, hull sheathing |

## 2.3 Brass Family — Cu-Zn Alloys

### 2.3.1 Phase Constitution
- **α-Brass** (Zn < 35%): Single-phase FCC solid solution. **Ductile, cold-workable**, used for deep drawing and tubes.
- **α+β' Brass** (35% < Zn < 45%): Two-phase, β' is **brittle ordered BCC** (CsCl-type). **Hot-workable, less ductile**.
- **γ-Brass** (Zn > 45%): Industrial use is limited.

### 2.3.2 Dezincification — The Marine Nemesis

> [!WARNING]
> **Dezincification** is a **selective leaching corrosion** phenomenon where zinc preferentially dissolves from brass in seawater, leaving behind a porous, mechanically weak, copper-rich sponge. This is **the single most important failure mode** of marine brasses.

**Mechanism (Step-by-Step):**
1. Both Cu and Zn atoms oxidize: $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$ and $\text{Cu} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^-$
2. Cu²⁺ ions re-deposit as metallic copper on the surface (because E° of Cu²⁺/Cu is more positive).
3. The net effect: zinc is selectively removed; structure becomes porous and loses strength.

**Mitigation Strategies:**
- **Inhibited Admiralty Brass (Cu-30Zn-1Sn-0.04As)** — addition of **arsenic (0.02–0.06%)** suppresses dezincification.
- **Use of cupronickels** instead of brass for critical seawater service.

### 2.3.3 Common Marine Brasses
- **Cartridge Brass (Cu-30Zn):** 70% Cu, 30% Zn — α-phase, used for marine hardware.
- **Muntz Metal (Cu-40Zn):** 60% Cu, 40% Zn — α+β', used for marine bolts and sheathing.
- **Admiralty Brass (Cu-29Zn-1Sn-0.04As):** Heat exchanger tubes.
- **Naval Brass (Cu-39Zn-0.75Sn):** Marine hardware requiring higher strength.

## 2.4 Bronze Family — Cu-Sn, Cu-Al, Cu-Si, Cu-Be Alloys

### 2.4.1 Tin Bronzes (Cu-Sn)
- **Composition:** Typically 5–12% Sn, balance Cu.
- **Microstructure:** Single-phase α solid solution (due to large atomic size mismatch, tin dissolves extensively in copper).
- **Marine Use:** Bearings, bushings, worm gears, propellers in low-speed vessels.
- **Property:** Excellent **fatigue resistance** and **load-bearing capacity**.
- **Problem:** Susceptible to **"tin sweat"** — tin-rich liquid exudation during slow solidification in thick sections.

### 2.4.2 Aluminum Bronzes (Cu-Al)
- **Composition:** 8–12% Al, with optional Fe, Ni, Mn additions.
- **Microstructure:**
  - < 9% Al → single-phase α (FCC).
  - 9–12% Al → two-phase α + β (martensitic, hardenable).
- **Marine Use:** **Propellers**, pump impellers, valve bodies, ship fittings.
- **Key Advantages:** Superior **cavitation erosion resistance**, excellent strength (~600–800 MPa UTS after heat treatment), good corrosion resistance.

### 2.4.3 Silicon Bronzes (Cu-Si)
- Composition: 3% Si, often with 1% Mn.
- Marine use: Marine fasteners, hardware, architectural marine elements.
- Property: Good strength + excellent weldability + corrosion resistance.

### 2.4.4 Beryllium Bronzes (Cu-Be)
- Composition: 0.5–2% Be, with 0.2–0.5% Co or Ni.
- Marine use: **Non-sparking tools**, marine instrument springs, undersea cable connectors.
- Property: Extraordinary strength (up to **1400 MPa UTS** after age hardening) + non-magnetic.

## 2.5 Cupronickel Family — Cu-Ni Alloys

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cupronickels are the GOLD STANDARD for marine heat exchangers and seawater piping.** They combine the corrosion resistance of copper with the strength and erosion resistance imparted by nickel.

- **90-10 Cupronickel:** 90% Cu, 10% Ni, + 1.5% Fe + 0.5% Mn — most common in marine condensers.
- **70-30 Cupronickel:** 70% Cu, 30% Ni, + 0.5% Fe + 0.5% Mn — used in higher-velocity, more aggressive service.

**Role of Iron (1–2%):**
- Iron precipitates as fine, dispersed **Fe-rich intermetallics** that drastically improve **resistance to erosion-corrosion** in high-velocity seawater (up to **4.5 m/s** for 90-10, **6.0 m/s** for 70-30).
- The iron also refines the grain structure and stabilizes the protective oxide film.

**Role of Manganese (0.5–1%):**
- Acts as a **deoxidizer** during casting and improves **sulfur tolerance** (MnS formation prevents hot shortness).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---------|---------------------|-------|-------|
| **Electrical Conductivity (%IACS)** | $\% \text{IACS} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{sample}}}{\sigma_{\text{std Cu}}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | IACS = International Annealed Copper Standard; $\sigma_{\text{std Cu}} = 5.8 \times 10^7 \,\text{S/m}$ |
| **Corrosion Rate (Mass Loss Method)** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | mm/year | W = mass loss (mg), D = density (g/cm³), A = area (cm²), T = time (h) |
| **Corrosion Rate (Faraday's Law)** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | grams | I = current (A), t = time (s), M = molar mass, n = valence, F = 96500 C/mol |
| **Mean Stress (Cold Work)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | MPa | Cold working increases dislocation density |
| **Recrystallization Temperature** | $T_{\text{rex}} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$ | K | For pure Cu: $T_m = 1356$ K → $T_{\text{rex}} \approx 542$ K (269 °C) |
| **Work Hardening (Hollomon Eq.)** | $\sigma = K \cdot \varepsilon^{n}$ | MPa | K = strength coefficient, n = strain hardening exponent (Cu ≈ 0.30–0.35) |
| **Thermal Conductivity Estimate (Rule of Mixture)** | $k_{\text{alloy}} = \sum x_i k_i$ | W/m·K | $x_i$ = volume/mass fraction, $k_i$ = pure element conductivity |
| **Erosion-Corrosion Allowable Velocity (90-10 Cu-Ni)** | $V_{\max} \approx 4.5$ | m/s | Above this, erosion-corrosion risk increases sharply |
| **Standard Potential (Cu/Cu²⁺)** | $E^\circ = +0.34$ | V (SHE) | More noble than Fe (−0.44 V) and Zn (−0.76 V) |
| **Galvanic Series Order (Seawater)** | $\text{Mg} < \text{Zn} < \text{Al} < \text{Steel} < \text{Brass} < \text{Cu-Ni} < \text{Cu} < \text{Ni} < \text{Ti} < \text{Pt}$ | — | Cu-Ni is more noble than brass in seawater |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in KTU-endorsed marine design codes (e.g., **DNV-RP-B101**, **ASTM B111**, **B122**) for selecting copper alloy tubes in condensers, predicting service life, and designing cathodic protection schemes.

# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Corrosion Rate from Mass Loss (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem (Sample):** A 90-10 cupronickel condenser tube of surface area **150 cm²** and density **8.94 g/cm³** loses **245 mg** after **720 hours** of seawater exposure. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/year** and **mpy** (mils per year).

**Given:**
- $W = 245$ mg
- $D = 8.94$ g/cm³
- $A = 150$ cm²
- $T = 720$ h

**Step 1 — Apply the standard corrosion rate formula.**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times 245}{8.94 \times 150 \times 720}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$
87.6 \times 245 = 21462
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$
8.94 \times 150 = 1341; \quad 1341 \times 720 = 965520
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{21462}{965520} = 0.02223 \,\text{mm/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mpy (1 mm/year = 39.37 mpy).**

$$
\text{CR} = 0.02223 \times 39.37 = 0.875 \,\text{mpy}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is an **excellent** corrosion rate — consistent with the 0.02–0.05 mm/year range expected for well-protected cupronickel in clean seawater.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electrical Conductivity (%IACS) of an Alloyed Copper

**Problem (Sample):** A **Cartridge Brass (Cu-30Zn)** is reported to have electrical conductivity of **28% IACS**. Given the standard conductivity of pure annealed copper is **5.8 × 10⁷ S/m**, find the actual conductivity of the brass in **S/m** and **% IACS** if a tin addition of 2% reduces the conductivity by a further 22% relative.

**Step 1 — Convert %IACS to absolute conductivity for the brass baseline.**

$$
\sigma_{\text{brass}} = 0.28 \times 5.8 \times 10^7 = 1.624 \times 10^7 \,\text{S/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 22% reduction due to tin addition (final = 78% of brass).**

$$
\sigma_{\text{final}} = 0.78 \times 1.624 \times 10^7 = 1.2667 \times 10^7 \,\text{S/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Express as %IACS.**

$$
\% \text{IACS} = \frac{1.2667 \times 10^7}{5.8 \times 10^7} \times 100 = 21.84\%
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This drastic conductivity loss illustrates the **trade-off in marine alloy design** — gaining strength or corrosion resistance comes at the cost of electrical performance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recrystallization Temperature for Pure Copper

**Step 1 — Convert melting point to Kelvin.**

$$
T_m = 1083 + 273 = 1356 \,\text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the empirical rule $T_{\text{rex}} \approx 0.4 \, T_m$.**

$$
T_{\text{rex}} = 0.4 \times 1356 = 542.4 \,\text{K}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert back to Celsius.**

$$
T_{\text{rex}} = 542.4 - 273 = 269.4 \,^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Engineering Significance:** Above ~270 °C, cold-worked copper (e.g., a drawn seawater pipe) begins to soften via recrystallization — critical when designing for **fire-rated marine systems** or **hot-side service** near engine exhausts.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cold Work Percentage and Yield Strength Increase

**Problem (Sample):** A 90-10 Cu-Ni plate is cold-rolled from an initial thickness of **20 mm** to **14 mm**. Determine the **% cold work** and the approximate increase in yield strength if the original annealed yield strength is **150 MPa** and the strain-hardening exponent $n = 0.30$.

**Step 1 — % Cold Work formula.**

$$
\% \text{CW} = \frac{t_0 - t_f}{t_0} \times 100 = \frac{20 - 14}{20} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

**Step 2 — True strain from cold work.**

$$
\varepsilon_T = \ln\!\left(\frac{t_0}{t_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{20}{14}\right) = \ln(1.4286) = 0.3567
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hollomon's equation to estimate flow stress.**

$$
\sigma = K \cdot \varepsilon_T^{n}
$$

For Cu-Ni, typical $K \approx 600$ MPa. Therefore:

$$
\sigma = 600 \times (0.3567)^{0.30}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute exponent.**

$$
(0.3567)^{0.30} = e^{0.30 \times \ln(0.3567)} = e^{0.30 \times (-1.0312)} = e^{-0.3094} = 0.7339
$$

**Step 5 — Final flow stress.**

$$
\sigma = 600 \times 0.7339 = 440.3 \,\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Yield strength gain.**

$$
\Delta \sigma_y = 440.3 - 150 \approx 290 \,\text{MPa (almost doubled)}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Cold rolling 90-10 Cu-Ni by just 30% can **nearly triple the yield strength**, a technique exploited in producing **high-strength condenser tube sheets**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Galvanic Corrosion Current Between Cu-Ni and Steel

**Problem (Sample):** A 90-10 Cu-Ni tube is inadvertently coupled to a **mild steel** flange in seawater. The exposed area of steel is **10× larger** than the Cu-Ni. Given:
- $E_{\text{Cu-Ni}} = -0.25$ V (SCE)
- $E_{\text{steel}} = -0.65$ V (SCE)
- Total cell resistance $R = 0.5 \,\Omega$

Compute the galvanic corrosion current.

**Step 1 — Driving voltage of the galvanic cell.**

$$
\Delta E = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}} = (-0.25) - (-0.65) = 0.40 \,\text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's law to the corrosion circuit.**

$$
I = \frac{\Delta E}{R} = \frac{0.40}{0.5} = 0.80 \,\text{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Current density on the small Cu-Ni cathode (area factor).**

If $A_{\text{steel}} = 10 \, A_{\text{Cu-Ni}}$, total area is concentrated cathodically on the small Cu-Ni:

$$
I_{\text{per Cu-Ni}} \approx \frac{I \times A_{\text{steel}}}{A_{\text{steel}} + A_{\text{Cu-Ni}}} \approx 0.727 \,\text{A}
$$

**Step 4 — Faraday's law — mass loss per day on steel anode.**

$$
m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{\text{Fe}}}{n \cdot F} = \frac{0.80 \times 86400 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96500}
$$

$$
m = \frac{3860544}{193000} = 20.0 \,\text{g/day of iron}
$$

**Engineering Warning:** This illustrates why **insulating flanges (dielectric unions)** and **zinc / aluminum sacrificial anodes** are mandatory between Cu-Ni and steel in marine piping.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Marine Alloy Selection

| Required Service | Recommended Alloy | Why |
|------------------|------------------|-----|
| Seawater condenser tubes (moderate velocity) | 90-10 Cu-Ni (with Fe, Mn) | Erosion-corrosion resistance + antifouling |
| Aggressive high-velocity cooling | 70-30 Cu-Ni | Higher Ni → superior film stability |
| Marine propellers | Ni-Al Bronze (Cu-9Al-5Ni-5Fe) | Cavitation resistance + strength |
| Pump impellers | Mn-Al Bronze | Toughness + corrosion resistance |
| Bearings & bushings | Tin Bronze (Cu-10Sn) | Fatigue + load capacity |
| Non-sparking marine tools | Cu-Be (Cu-1.9Be-0.25Co) | Age-hardenable to 1400 MPa |
| Marine fasteners, low cost | Naval Brass (Cu-39Zn-0.75Sn) | Moderate strength + dezincification resistance |
| Decorative / architectural marine | Silicon Bronze | Good weldability + aesthetics |
| Seawater piping (non-condenser) | Cu-DHP (C12200) | Clean, dezincification-free, weldable |
| Heat-exchanger tube sheets | Aluminum Brass (Cu-22Zn-2Al-0.04As) | Inhibited, good for cooler service |
