# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**"Differentiate between brasses and bronzes. Why are brasses susceptible to dezincification in seawater service?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Brasses | Bronzes |
|----------|---------|---------|
| Principal alloying element | Zinc (Zn) | Tin (Sn) / Al / Si / Be |
| Color | Yellow-gold | Reddish-brown to gold |
| Marine cost | Lower | Higher |
| Corrosion mode | Dezincification risk | Generally more resistant |

**Dezincification Susceptibility (1.5 marks):** Brasses contain zinc which has a **more negative standard electrode potential (−0.76 V)** than copper (+0.34 V). In chloride-rich seawater, zinc atoms preferentially dissolve as Zn²⁺ ions. The remaining copper ions re-deposit as a **porous, mechanically weak metallic copper sponge**, destroying mechanical integrity. This is mitigated by adding **0.02–0.06% arsenic** to produce "inhibited admiralty brass."

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**"Explain the role of iron and manganese additions in 90-10 cupronickel for marine condenser tube applications."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Iron (1.0–1.8%):** Precipitates as fine, uniformly dispersed **Fe-rich intermetallic particles** that act as barriers against erosion-corrosion in high-velocity seawater. It also **refines the grain structure** and stabilizes the protective **Cu₂O film**. (1.5 marks)
2. **Manganese (0.5–1.0%):** Acts as a **deoxidizer** during casting, removing dissolved oxygen and sulfur to prevent porosity and hot shortness. It also improves **weldability and formability** of the final tube. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**(a) Classify the various copper-based alloys used in marine engineering. Discuss the composition, microstructure, properties, and specific applications of (i) aluminum bronzes and (ii) 90-10 cupronickel. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the mechanism of dezincification in marine brasses. Discuss the metallurgical and design strategies adopted to prevent dezincification failure in seawater systems. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Classification of Marine Copper Alloys (3 marks):**

Marine copper alloys are classified into **four major families** based on the principal alloying element:
1. **Commercially Pure Copper** (≥ 99.3% Cu) — e.g., C10100, C11000, C12200.
2. **Brasses** — Cu-Zn system; sub-divided into α (Zn<35%) and α+β (35–45% Zn).
3. **Bronzes** — originally Cu-Sn, now includes Cu-Al, Cu-Si, Cu-Be.
4. **Cupronickels** — Cu-Ni-Fe-Mn system (most common: 90-10 and 70-30).

**(i) Aluminum Brones (Cu-Al System) — 2 marks:**

- **Composition:** 8–12% Al, with Fe (up to 5%), Ni (up to 5%), Mn (up to 1.5%) as secondary additions. The most important marine variant is **Nickel-Aluminum Bronze (Cu-9Al-5Ni-5Fe)**.
- **Microstructure:** Below 9% Al → single-phase **α (FCC) solid solution**. Between 9–12% Al → two-phase **α + β (martensitic) structure**, which is hardenable by quenching and tempering.
- **Properties:** Excellent cavitation-erosion resistance; high tensile strength (up to 800 MPa after heat treatment); superior corrosion resistance in seawater; good castability.
- **Applications:** **Marine propellers** (largest application), pump impellers, valve bodies, ship fittings, gears.

**(ii) 90-10 Cupronickel — 2 marks:**

- **Composition:** 88.5–90% Cu, 9–11% Ni, 1.0–1.8% Fe, 0.5–1.0% Mn.
- **Microstructure:** Single-phase **α solid solution** of Cu-Ni with dispersed Fe-rich intermetallic particles.
- **Properties:** Outstanding resistance to **biofouling** (releases Cu²⁺ ions), excellent resistance to **erosion-corrosion** up to 4.5 m/s, good thermal conductivity for heat transfer, weldable.
- **Applications:** **Condenser and heat-exchanger tubes**, seawater piping, firewater systems, marine cladding.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Mechanism of Dezincification (4 marks):**

Dezincification is a **selective leaching corrosion** phenomenon. It occurs in two possible mechanisms:

**Mechanism A — Selective Dissolution + Re-plating (most accepted):**
1. Both copper and zinc atoms in the brass undergo anodic oxidation: $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$ and $\text{Cu} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^-$
2. Because Cu²⁺ has a more positive standard potential (+0.34 V) compared to Zn²⁺ (−0.76 V), the copper ions re-deposit as **metallic copper** on the brass surface.
3. The net result: zinc is selectively removed; the residual structure is a **porous, mechanically weak, copper-rich sponge** with drastically reduced strength.

**Mechanism B — Simultaneous Dissolution with Re-deposition:** Similar to A but emphasizes that Cu²⁺ re-deposits preferentially in the porous zinc-depleted zone.

**Visual Effect:** The surface turns **red (pure Cu color)** instead of yellow, and the material becomes brittle and spongy.

**Conditions Favoring Dezincification:** Stagnant or slow-moving seawater, high chloride content, low oxygen, temperatures around 60–80 °C, and Zn content > 15%.

**Prevention Strategies (3 marks):**

| Strategy | Mechanism | Example |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **Arsenic inhibition (0.02–0.06%)** | As forms a protective film that blocks selective Zn dissolution | Admiralty Brass, Aluminum Brass |
| **Lower Zn content** | Reduces driving force for Zn leaching | 70-30 brass (α-phase) |
| **Cupronickel substitution** | Single-phase, no Zn to leach | Replace brass tube with 90-10 Cu-Ni |
| **Cathodic protection** | Sacrificial Zn or Al anodes suppress anodic reaction | Marine pipelines |
| **Dielectric insulation** | Electrical isolation from dissimilar metals | Use of non-conductive gaskets |
| **Velocity control** | Avoid stagnation (0.6–1.8 m/s ideal) | Pipe design codes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) writing only the **two mechanisms** without naming the **specific alloy inhibitor** (arsenic), (2) confusing dezincification with **dealloying** of brasses in general, and (3) failing to mention the **color change (yellow → red)** as a diagnostic indicator. Always include: *Why*, *How*, and *How to prevent*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam Style]**

**(a) Discuss the mechanical and physical properties of pure copper. Explain why pure copper is not used in heavily loaded marine structural applications, and describe how alloying modifies its properties. (7 marks)**

**(b) With a neat sketch, describe the microstructure and heat treatment of beryllium copper (Cu-1.9Be-0.25Co). Justify its use in non-sparking marine tools. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Mechanical & Physical Properties of Pure Copper (Annealed) — 3 marks:**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Tensile strength | 210–235 MPa |
| Yield strength | 33–70 MPa |
| Elongation | 45–55% |
| Hardness (VHN) | 35–50 |
| Density | 8.96 g/cm³ |
| Melting point | 1083 °C |
| Thermal conductivity | 390 W/m·K |
| Electrical conductivity | 58 × 10⁶ S/m (100% IACS) |
| Crystal structure | FCC |
| Corrosion potential (seawater) | −0.20 V (SCE) |

**Limitations of Pure Copper in Marine Structural Use (2 marks):**
1. **Low strength** (~210 MPa UTS) — inadequate for propellers, gears, bearings, shafting.
2. **High ductility** — creeps under sustained load.
3. **Susceptible to velocity-sensitive corrosion** — fails in turbulent seawater flow.
4. **Soft and wears rapidly** — poor tribological performance.
5. **Expensive and strategic** — economic incentive to use alloyed variants.

**Alloying Strategy (2 marks):**
- **Zinc addition (brasses):** Solid solution strengthening + better cold workability.
- **Tin addition (tin bronzes):** Improves strength and bearing capacity.
- **Aluminum addition (Al-bronzes):** Heat-treatable martensitic β-phase → strength up to 800 MPa.
- **Nickel addition (cupronickels):** Improves erosion-corrosion resistance and stabilizes the protective film.
- **Beryllium addition (Cu-Be):** Age-hardening → strength up to 1400 MPa.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Composition & Microstructure of Cu-Be (2.5 marks):**

- **Composition:** 1.9% Be, 0.25% Co, balance Cu.
- **As-solution-treated state:** Above ~790 °C, beryllium fully dissolves → single-phase **α (FCC) supersaturated solid solution**. Rapid quenching ("solution treatment") retains this metastable state — soft and ductile (~200 HV, ~450 MPa UTS).
- **Aged state:** Re-heating to 300–350 °C causes **Guinier-Preston (GP) zone formation** followed by precipitation of **γ' (CuBe) coherent precipitates** → extreme age hardening. Final strength: **~1200–1400 MPa UTS**, hardness ~400 HV.

**Heat Treatment Stages (2.5 marks):**

| Stage | Temperature | Result |
|-------|-------------|--------|
| 1. Solution treatment | 780–800 °C, 1 hr | Homogenized α phase |
| 2. Quench | Water | Retain supersaturated α |
| 3. Cold working (optional) | Room temp | Dislocation network aids precipitation |
| 4. Age hardening | 300–350 °C, 2–3 hr | γ' precipitates → peak hardness |

**Justification for Non-Sparking Marine Tools (2 marks):**
- The Cu-Be alloy is **non-ferromagnetic** and has a **high ignition temperature** for impact sparks.
- It produces **no incendiary sparks** when struck against rocks, steel, or rust.
- Critical for **oil tankers, FPSO vessels, LNG carriers, and fuel bunkering stations** where any spark can ignite hydrocarbon vapors.
- Also exhibits **high strength and fatigue resistance**, ensuring tool durability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist (For KTU ESE Day-Before Revision):**

- ✅ **Pure copper** = FCC, 1083 °C MP, σᵤ ≈ 210 MPa — good conductivity, low strength.
- ✅ **Brasses (Cu-Zn):** α-phase (Zn<35%) = ductile; α+β (Zn 35–45%) = hot workable; risk = **dezincification**.
- ✅ **Inhibited Admiralty Brass = Cu-30Zn-1Sn-0.04As** — As prevents dezincification.
- ✅ **Tin Bronzes (Cu-10Sn):** Bearings, gears, fatigue-resistant.
- ✅ **Aluminum Bronzes (Cu-9Al-5Ni-5Fe):** **Propellers, pump impellers** — best cavitation resistance.
- ✅ **Beryllium Bronze (Cu-1.9Be):** Non-sparking tools, age-hardenable to **1400 MPa UTS**.
- ✅ **90-10 Cu-Ni:** Condenser tubes, with 1.5% Fe + 0.5% Mn — Fe resists erosion-corrosion.
- ✅ **70-30 Cu-Ni:** More aggressive service, higher velocity (up to 6 m/s).
- ✅ **Dezincification** is selective Zn leaching; prevent with As, low Zn, or switch to cupronickel.
- ✅ **Cu-Ni and steel are galvanically incompatible** — use dielectric unions or sacrificial anodes.
- ✅ **Cu²⁺ ions are toxic to marine organisms** → natural antifouling effect.
- ✅ **E°(Cu²⁺/Cu) = +0.34 V (SHE)** — copper is cathodic to steel, zinc, aluminum in seawater.
- ✅ **Recrystallization temperature of Cu ≈ 0.4 Tₘ = 270 °C** — annealing design limit.
- ✅ **Cold work formula:** $\%CW = \dfrac{t_0 - t_f}{t_0} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Corrosion rate formula:** $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87.6 W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 frequently asked topics:** Comparison of brasses vs bronzes, dezincification mechanism + prevention, role of Fe/Mn in cupronickels, propellers = Al-bronze, condenser tubes = 90-10 Cu-Ni.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always present your answer using **classification tables, microstructure-property relationships, and a concluding "applications" statement**. This structure directly aligns with KTU's outcome-based evaluation (CO₂: *Identify the properties of materials relevant to marine applications*) and is favored by board examiners for full-mark awards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zinc
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Zinc

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Zinc

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Zinc (Zn)** is a bluish-white, lustrous, diamagnetic metal with atomic number **30** and an atomic mass of **65.38 g/mol**. It belongs to Group **12 (IIB)** of the periodic table. In marine corrosion engineering, zinc is one of the most strategically important non-ferrous metals due to its position high in the galvanic series, making it an effective **sacrificial anode** for cathodic protection (CP) of steel structures in seawater.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Zinc is studied under Module 2 as a primary sacrificial anode material, alongside its derivatives like zinc coatings (galvanizing), zinc-rich primers, and zinc alloy anodes (Zn-Al, Zn-In).

### Key Physical & Mechanical Constants

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Atomic Number | **30** |
| Atomic Mass | **65.38 g/mol** |
| Density | **7.14 g/cm³** |
| Melting Point | **419.5 °C** |
| Boiling Point | **907 °C** |
| Crystal Structure | **HCP (Hexagonal Close-Packed)** |
| Standard Electrode Potential (Zn²⁺/Zn) | **−0.763 V vs SHE** |
| Practical Seawater Potential | **−1.10 V vs Ag/AgCl** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Electrode Potential** of zinc is **−0.763 V** versus the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE). This strongly negative value is the *fundamental reason* zinc corrodes preferentially to steel in galvanic couples.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking in the rain carrying a paper bag. If you put a **small piece of zinc** in the bag along with an **iron nail**, and the bag gets wet, the zinc will corrode (turn white with zinc oxide/hydroxide) while the **iron nail remains shiny**. The zinc has literally *sacrificed* itself to protect the iron — acting like a **bodyguard** that takes all the punches so the VIP (steel) stays unharmed.

This is exactly the principle of **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)** used in:
- Ship hulls
- Offshore platforms
- Submarine pipelines
- Port steel piles

## 1.3 Why Zinc is Special in Marine Environments

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three engineering virtues make zinc irreplaceable in marine corrosion control:
> 1. **Electrochemically Active:** More negative potential than steel → preferential dissolution.
> 2. **Stable in Seawater:** Forms adherent, partially protective corrosion products (ZnO, Zn(OH)₂, Zn-hydroxychloride).
> 3. **Predictable Performance:** Nearly constant operating potential in seawater, simplifying CP system design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrochemical Theory of Zinc as a Sacrificial Anode

### 2.1.1 Galvanic Couple Formation

When zinc is electrically connected to steel and immersed in an electrolyte (seawater), a **galvanic cell** is formed:

- **Anode (Zinc):** Undergoes oxidation
$$\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

- **Cathode (Steel):** Dissolved oxygen is reduced (in aerated seawater)
$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$$

- **Net Reaction:**
$$2\text{Zn} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{Zn(OH)}_2$$

### 2.1.2 Driving Force Calculation

The **driving voltage (ΔE)** of the zinc-steel galvanic couple:

$$\Delta E = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$$

$$\Delta E = E_{\text{Fe}} - E_{\text{Zn}} = (-0.61\,\text{V}) - (-1.10\,\text{V}) = +0.49\,\text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This positive **0.49 V** driving voltage ensures spontaneous current flow from zinc to steel, providing effective cathodic protection.

### 2.1.3 Anode Capacity & Efficiency

The **theoretical electrochemical capacity** of zinc (mass of metal consumed per unit charge):

$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{\text{Atomic Mass}}{n \cdot F} = \frac{65.38}{2 \times 96485} = 0.339\,\text{g/(A·h)}$$

The **practical anode capacity** of zinc in seawater is approximately **780 A·h/kg** (compared to theoretical 820 A·h/kg), giving a **utilization efficiency** of about **90–95%**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Value | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Anode Reaction | $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$ | — | Sacrificial dissolution |
| 2 | Cathode Reaction (seawater) | $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$ | — | Oxygen reduction |
| 3 | Theoretical Capacity | $C_{th} = \dfrac{M}{nF}$ | g/(A·h) | Anode design |
| 4 | Practical Capacity (Zn) | $C_p \approx 780$ | A·h/kg | Field design |
| 5 | Anode Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{C_p}{C_{th}} \times 100$ | % | Performance metric |
| 6 | Anode Mass Required | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C_p \cdot \eta}$ | kg | Design calculation |
| 7 | Anode Life | $L = \dfrac{m \cdot C_p}{I}$ | years | Service interval |
| 8 | Current Output | $I = \dfrac{(E_c - E_a) - IR}{R}$ | A | Ohm's law for CP |
| 9 | Zn²⁺ Solubility Product | $K_{sp}(\text{Zn(OH)}_2) \approx 3 \times 10^{-17}$ | — | Passivation check |
| 10 | Seawater Resistivity | $\rho \approx 20$–$30$ | Ω·cm | Electrolyte property |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always use **Ag/AgCl** (in seawater) or **Cu/CuSO₄** (in soil) as the **reference electrode** when quoting protection potentials. Never mix reference electrodes in calculations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Ship Hull Protection:** Zinc bracelet anodes welded to hull plates.
2. **Offshore Wind Foundations:** Sled-mounted zinc anodes for monopiles.
3. **Pipeline Landfalls:** Zinc ribbon anodes in transition zones.
4. **Ballast Tanks:** Zinc anodes in segregated ballast compartments.
5. **Galvanized Steel Rebar:** Zinc-coated rebar for marine concrete structures.

### 2.4 Failure Mechanism: Passivation Problem

> [!WARNING]
> In **high-resistivity electrolytes** (fresh water, saline mud), zinc may develop a **tenacious ZnO/Zn(OH)₂ film** that passivates the surface and **drastically reduces current output**. This is why **pure zinc** is unsuitable for fresh-water CP — **Mg or Mg-alloy anodes** are preferred instead.

To overcome passivation in warm seawater, **zinc alloy anodes** are used:
- **Zn–Al** (with Al for grain refinement)
- **Zn–Al–Cd** (historically, now restricted due to Cd toxicity)
- **Zn–Al–In** (modern, environmentally compliant)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Electrochemical Capacity of Zinc

### Step 1 — State Faraday's Law of Electrolysis

According to **Faraday's First Law**, the mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed:

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass consumed (grams)
- $Q$ = total electric charge (Coulombs)
- $M$ = molar mass (g/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per atom
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**

### Step 2 — Identify Zinc-Specific Parameters

For zinc dissolution:
- Molar mass, $M = 65.38$ g/mol
- Valence change, $n = 2$ (Zn → Zn²⁺)
- Faraday constant, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

### Step 3 — Express Capacity Per Unit Charge

The theoretical capacity is the mass consumed per ampere-hour:

$$C_{th} = \frac{m}{Q} = \frac{M}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{th} &= \frac{65.38\,\text{g/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485\,\text{C/mol}} \\
&= \frac{65.38}{192{,}970}\,\text{g/C} \\
&= 3.389 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{g/C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to A·h/kg (Engineering Unit)

Since $1\,\text{A·h} = 3600\,\text{C}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{th} &= 3.389 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{g/C} \times 3600\,\text{C/A·h} \\
&= 1.220\,\text{g/A·h} \\
&= 1220\,\text{g/(A·h·kg)}\,\text{of metal}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this is per **gram**, so per **kg**:

$$C_{th} = 1.220 \times 1000 = 1220\,\text{g/(A·h·kg)} = 1.220\,\text{kg/(A·h·kg)}$$

Reformatted:

$$C_{th} = 820\,\text{A·h/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $\frac{1000}{1.220} = 819.7 \approx 820$ A·h/kg ✓

### Step 6 — Compute Utilization Efficiency

The **practical capacity** of zinc in seawater ≈ **780 A·h/kg**.

$$
\eta = \frac{C_p}{C_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{780}{820} \times 100 = 95.1\%
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Anode Mass Calculation

**Problem:** A marine pipeline requires a current of **$I = 5$ A** continuously for **$T = 5$ years**. Using zinc anodes with practical capacity **$C_p = 780$ A·h/kg** and efficiency **$\eta = 0.95$**, calculate the **anode mass required**.

### Step 1 — Calculate Total Charge

Total operating time in hours:

$$t = 5 \times 365 \times 24 = 43{,}800\,\text{hours}$$

Total charge delivered:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= I \times t \\
&= 5\,\text{A} \times 43{,}800\,\text{h} \\
&= 219{,}000\,\text{A·h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Anode Mass Equation

$$m = \frac{Q_{total}}{C_p \cdot \eta}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{219{,}000}{780 \times 0.95} \\
&= \frac{219{,}000}{741} \\
&= 295.5\,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** A minimum of **295.5 kg** of zinc anode mass is required. In practice, a **design safety factor of 1.1–1.3** is added, yielding ≈ **325–385 kg** of installed anodes.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Zinc vs. Other Sacrificial Anodes

| Parameter | Zinc | Magnesium | Aluminum |
|-----------|------|-----------|----------|
| Standard Potential (V vs SHE) | −1.10 (seawater) | −1.55 | −1.05 (seawater) |
| Theoretical Capacity (A·h/kg) | 820 | 2200 | 2980 |
| Practical Capacity (A·h/kg) | 780 | 1100–1230 | 1500–2700 |
| Density (g/cm³) | 7.14 | 1.74 | 2.70 |
| Seawater Suitability | **Excellent** | Poor (passivates) | Excellent |
| Soil Suitability | Moderate | **Excellent** | Moderate |
| Cost (relative) | Medium | High | Low |
| Environmental Concern | Low | High (corrosion rate) | Low |
| Open-Circuit Voltage (Ag/AgCl) | −1.10 V | −1.55 V | −1.05 V |
| Typical Applications | Marine hulls, pipelines | Underground tanks | Offshore structures |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) — Block Functional Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the electrochemical pathway, electron flow, and ionic transport in a zinc-based SACP system protecting a marine steel structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Electrolyte["SEAWATER ELECTROLYTE (NaCl solution)"]
        direction TB
        NaCl["Na+ and Cl- ions conduct current"]
        O2Red["Dissolved O2 diffuses to steel surface"]
    end

    subgraph AnodeAssembly["ZINC SACRIFICIAL ANODE"]
        direction TB
        ZnMetal["Zn metal block / bracelet"]
        ZnOx["Oxidation: Zn to Zn2+ + 2e-"]
        ZnProduct["Corrosion product: ZnO / Zn hydroxide"]
        ZnMetal --> ZnOx
        ZnOx --> ZnProduct
    end

    subgraph SteelStructure["PROTECTED STEEL STRUCTURE (Cathode)"]
        direction TB
        SteelSurf["Steel surface receives electrons"]
        ORR["O2 + 2H2O + 4e- to 4OH-"]
        ProtectiveFilm["Formation of protective OH- film"]
        SteelSurf --> ORR
        ORR --> ProtectiveFilm
    end

    subgraph Wiring["ELECTRICAL CONNECTION"]
        direction TB
        Wire["Insulated copper cable / direct weld"]
    end

    ZnOx -- "Electrons flow through wire" --> Wiring
    Wiring -- "Electrons delivered to steel" --> SteelSurf
    ZnOx -- "Zn2+ enters electrolyte" --> NaCl
    O2Red -- "O2 reaches cathode" --> ORR
```

## 4.2 Galvanic Series Position of Zinc — Block Architecture

This diagram shows zinc's strategic placement relative to common marine metals in the **galvanic series in flowing seawater**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ActiveEnd["MOST ACTIVE (ANODIC / CORRODED END)"]
        Mg["Magnesium -1.55 V"]
        Zn["ZINC -1.10 V (Sacrificial Anode Choice)"]
        Al["Aluminum alloys -0.95 to -1.05 V"]
    end

    subgraph Middle["REFERENCE / NOBLE"]
        Cd["Cadmium -0.82 V"]
        Fe["Mild Steel -0.61 V"]
        Pb["Lead -0.55 V"]
        Cu["Copper -0.35 V"]
    end

    subgraph NobleEnd["MOST NOBLE (CATHODIC / PROTECTED END)"]
        Ni["Nickel -0.25 V"]
        Ag["Silver 0.00 V"]
        Au["Gold +0.15 V"]
        Pt["Platinum +0.25 V"]
    end

    Mg --> Zn
    Zn --> Al
    Al --> Cd
    Cd --> Fe
    Fe --> Pb
    Pb --> Cu
    Cu --> Ni
    Ni --> Ag
    Ag --> Au
    Au --> Pt
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule:** In a galvanic couple, the metal **higher up** (more active) corrodes and **protects** the metal **lower down** (more noble). Zinc, being above steel, is the classic sacrificial partner.

## 4.3 Life-Cycle Flow of a Zinc Anode — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start["Manufacturing Zn Alloy Casting"] --> QC["Composition Check: Al 0.1-0.5%, Fe less than 0.005%"]
    QC --> Install["Bolted / Welded to Structure"]
    Install --> Active["Active Dissolution Phase (Uniform Corrosion)"]
    Active --> Monitor["Periodic Potential Survey"]
    Monitor --> Decision{"Potential still protective?"}
    Decision -- Yes --> Monitor
    Decision -- No --> Replace["Replacement Scheduled"]
    Replace --> Recycle["Spent Anode Sent for Zinc Recycling"]
    Recycle --> Start
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Quality Parameter:** **Iron impurity in zinc anodes must be ≤ 50 ppm**. Excess iron causes hard spots, uneven dissolution, and passivation in service.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Why is zinc used as a sacrificial anode for protecting steel in seawater?**

**Model Answer:**

Zinc is used as a sacrificial anode because of its **more negative electrochemical potential** (−0.763 V vs SHE; −1.10 V vs Ag/AgCl in seawater) compared to steel (−0.61 V vs SHE). When electrically connected to steel and immersed in seawater:

1. Zinc preferentially corrodes (oxidizes): Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻
2. Electrons flow from zinc to steel, polarizing steel cathodically.
3. The dissolved O₂ reduction reaction is shifted to the steel surface, preventing iron dissolution.
4. The steel surface is maintained at a protective potential (typically −0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl).

Additional advantages: (i) high practical capacity (≈ 780 A·h/kg), (ii) stable operating potential, (iii) uniform corrosion products that do not insulate the surface, and (iv) non-toxic corrosion products.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: List any three limitations of pure zinc as a sacrificial anode.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Passivation in high-resistivity electrolytes:** In fresh water or resistive mud, zinc forms a tenacious ZnO/Zn(OH)₂ film that reduces current output drastically.
2. **Iron impurity sensitivity:** Even trace iron (Fe > 50 ppm) causes localized pitting and uneven dissolution, reducing anode life.
3. **Limited driving voltage:** With only ≈ 0.49 V driving potential vs steel, zinc is unsuitable for coatings or high-resistivity soils where a higher driving voltage is needed (Mg alloys preferred).
4. **Cadmium in legacy alloys:** Traditional Zn–Al–Cd anodes are environmentally restricted due to Cd toxicity, requiring modern Zn–Al–In replacements.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**Q: (a)** Explain with neat sketches the principle and working of sacrificial anode cathodic protection (SACP) using zinc anodes for a marine pipeline. Discuss the criteria for material selection of zinc anode. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A marine subsea pipeline requires a continuous cathodic protection current of **8 A** for a design life of **10 years**. Calculate the total mass of zinc anodes required. Take practical capacity = 780 A·h/kg and utilization factor = 0.90. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Principle, Working & Material Selection (7 Marks)

**Principle:** Sacrificial anode cathodic protection is an electrochemical method in which a more active metal (zinc) is electrically connected to the structure to be protected (steel pipeline). Due to the difference in electrochemical potential, the zinc preferentially corrodes (acts as anode), while the steel is polarized cathodically and remains protected.

**Working Mechanism:**

The two simultaneous electrochemical reactions are:

- At **zinc anode (oxidation):**
$$\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

- At **steel cathode (reduction of dissolved O₂):**
$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$$

The electrons released by zinc travel through the metallic path (welded/bolted connection) to the steel, suppressing the anodic dissolution of iron:

$$\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \quad \text{(suppressed)}$$

The ionic current in the seawater completes the circuit — Zn²⁺ ions migrate into the bulk electrolyte while Cl⁻ ions migrate toward the zinc surface.

**Installation Schematic (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram):**
- Bracelet-type zinc anodes are mounted directly on the pipeline at calculated intervals.
- Alternative: Sled-mounted zinc anodes placed alongside the pipeline at specified spacings.

**Material Selection Criteria for Zinc Anode:**

| Criterion | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| Electrochemical potential | More negative than −1.05 V vs Ag/AgCl |
| Practical capacity | ≥ 780 A·h/kg |
| Anode efficiency | ≥ 90% |
| Iron content | ≤ 0.005% (50 ppm) |
| Copper content | ≤ 0.005% |
| Cadmium (legacy alloys) | 0.05–0.10% |
| Aluminum (in Zn-Al alloys) | 0.1–0.5% |
| Mechanical strength | Adequate for handling and installation |
| Corrosion product | Adherent, non-insulating |
| Cost & availability | Economically viable |

---

#### Part (b) — Anode Mass Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Current demand, $I = 8$ A
- Design life, $T = 10$ years
- Practical capacity, $C_p = 780$ A·h/kg
- Utilization factor, $u = 0.90$

**Step 1: Total operating hours**

$$t = 10 \times 365 \times 24 = 87{,}600\,\text{hours}$$

**Step 2: Total charge required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= I \times t \\
&= 8 \times 87{,}600 \\
&= 700{,}800\,\text{A·h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Theoretical anode mass (without losses)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{ideal} &= \frac{Q}{C_p} \\
&= \frac{700{,}800}{780} \\
&= 898.5\,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply utilization factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{design} &= \frac{m_{ideal}}{u} \\
&= \frac{898.5}{0.90} \\
&= 998.3\,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The total mass of zinc anodes required = **998.3 kg ≈ 1000 kg**. With a typical design safety factor of 1.1, an additional **10%** (≈ 100 kg) is recommended, making the **installed mass ≈ 1100 kg** distributed in anode bracelets or sleds along the pipeline route.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**Q: (a)** Discuss the role of zinc in marine corrosion protection. Compare zinc, magnesium, and aluminum sacrificial anodes with respect to potential, capacity, and applications. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A ship hull of surface area **2500 m²** requires cathodic protection. Design current density for bare steel in seawater = **150 mA/m²**. Calculate the total current demand and the **number of zinc bracelet anodes** required, given each anode delivers **2.5 A** with an operating life of **4 years**. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Role of Zinc & Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)

**Role of Zinc in Marine Corrosion Protection:**

Zinc is the **workhorse sacrificial anode** of the marine industry. Its applications include:

1. **Cathodic Protection of Ship Hulls:** Bracelet anodes bolted around the hull, rudder, and propeller shaft.
2. **Offshore Platform Protection:** Anodes mounted on jacket legs and risers.
3. **Subsea Pipelines:** Sled-mounted anodes spaced at design intervals.
4. **Harbor Steel Piles:** Submerged pile protection in jetty construction.
5. **Galvanizing:** Hot-dip zinc coating on steel plates and rebars.
6. **Zinc-Rich Paints:** Primer coatings for above-waterline marine structures.

**Comparative Analysis Table:**

| Property | Zinc | Magnesium | Aluminum |
|----------|------|-----------|----------|
| Open-circuit potential vs Ag/AgCl (V) | −1.10 | −1.55 | −1.05 |
| Theoretical capacity (A·h/kg) | 820 | 2200 | 2980 |
| Practical capacity (A·h/kg) | 780 | 1100–1230 | 1500–2700 |
| Density (g/cm³) | 7.14 | 1.74 | 2.70 |
| Best medium | **Seawater** | Soil & freshwater | Seawater |
| Driving voltage vs steel | ≈ 0.49 V | ≈ 0.94 V | ≈ 0.44 V |
| Cost | Moderate | High | Low |
| Passivation tendency | Moderate (in low-conductivity water) | Low | Low |
| Typical applications | Ship hulls, offshore structures, pipelines | Underground tanks, well casings, soil | Offshore wind, large hulls |

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Logic:** Zinc is preferred in **seawater** because its open-circuit potential is well-matched and corrosion products are non-insulating. Mg is chosen for **low-resistivity soils/freshwater**. Al is increasingly used for **large offshore structures** due to high capacity and low mass.

---

#### Part (b) — Hull Current Demand & Anode Count Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Hull surface area, $A = 2500\,\text{m}^2$
- Design current density, $i = 150\,\text{mA/m}^2 = 0.150\,\text{A/m}^2$
- Each anode capacity (current output), $I_a = 2.5\,\text{A}$
- Operating life requirement, $T = 4$ years

**Step 1: Total current demand**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= A \times i \\
&= 2500 \times 0.150 \\
&= 375\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Number of anodes required (electrical basis)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{I_{total}}{I_a} \\
&= \frac{375}{2.5} \\
&= 150\,\text{anodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Verify anode life matches design life**

Total charge per anode:

$$Q_{anode} = 2.5 \times 4 \times 8760 = 87{,}600\,\text{A·h}$$

Mass of each anode (using $C_p = 780$ A·h/kg, $\eta = 0.90$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{anode} &= \frac{87{,}600}{780 \times 0.90} \\
&= \frac{87{,}600}{702} \\
&= 124.8\,\text{kg per anode}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total installed anode mass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{total} &= 150 \times 124.8 \\
&= 18{,}720\,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The ship hull requires **150 zinc bracelet anodes**, each weighing approximately **125 kg**, with a total installed zinc mass of **18,720 kg (≈ 18.7 tonnes)**. In practice, anodes are distributed evenly around the hull circumference — typically forward, midship, and aft — with higher density near the bow (turbulent water → higher current demand).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Mixing mA/m² with A/m² is the #1 mark-loss reason. Always convert: 150 mA/m² = **0.150 A/m²**.
> 2. **Forgetting utilization factor:** If $u$ is given, you **must** divide by it. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong reference electrode:** Always state "vs Ag/AgCl" for seawater and "vs Cu/CuSO₄" for soil. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous references.
> 4. **Hours vs Years confusion:** Multiply years by **8760** (not 365 or 24) to get total hours.
> 5. **Ignoring design safety factor:** KTU answers mentioning safety factor (typically 1.1–1.3) score higher marks as it reflects industrial practice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Zinc atomic data:** $Z = 30$, $M = 65.38$ g/mol, density = **7.14 g/cm³**, HCP structure.
- **Standard potential:** $E^\circ_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.763\,\text{V vs SHE}$.
- **Practical seawater potential:** $E_{\text{Zn}} = -1.10\,\text{V vs Ag/AgCl}$.
- **Driving voltage vs steel in seawater:** ≈ **0.49 V**.
- **Theoretical anode capacity:** **820 A·h/kg** (Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻).
- **Practical capacity (seawater):** ≈ **780 A·h/kg**.
- **Anode efficiency:** ≈ **90–95%**.
- **Key half-reactions to memorize:**
  * Anode: $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$
  * Cathode: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$
- **Critical impurity limits:** Fe ≤ 50 ppm, Cu ≤ 50 ppm.
- **Modern alloys:** Zn–Al, Zn–Al–In (Cd-free).
- **Design formula trio:**
  * $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{C_p \cdot \eta}$ — Anode mass
  * $L = \dfrac{m \cdot C_p}{I}$ — Anode life
  * $I = A \cdot i$ — Total current demand
- **Best medium for zinc anodes:** **Flowing seawater** (resistivity 20–30 Ω·cm).
- **Avoid:** Pure zinc in high-resistivity freshwater/soil → use Mg anodes instead.
- **Cathodic protection criterion for steel:** −0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl (seawater).
- **Galvanizing vs. SACP:** Galvanizing is a *barrier + sacrificial* coating; SACP uses a *remote sacrificial anode* — both rely on zinc's negative potential.
- **Environmental advantage:** Zn²⁺ is an essential micronutrient; Zn corrosion products are **non-toxic** to marine ecology.
- **Industry standards:** Refer to **NACE SP0176** (SACP of offshore structures) and **ISO 13174** for CP of marine pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aluminium and Its Alloys: Corrosion on Propeller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aluminium and Its Alloys: Corrosion on Propeller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aluminium – The Marine Lightweight Metal

**Aluminium (Al)** is a silvery-white, soft, low-density, ductile metallic element with atomic number **13** and a face-centred cubic (FCC) crystal structure. Its density is **2.71 g/cm³** (approximately one-third that of steel, **7.85 g/cm³**), with a melting point of **660 °C** and a standard electrode potential of **−1.66 V vs SHE**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Aluminium is the most abundant metal in the Earth's crust (~8%). In marine applications, pure aluminium is rarely used; instead, **aluminium alloys** (combinations of Al with Cu, Mg, Si, Mn, Zn) are preferred for their superior mechanical strength while retaining corrosion resistance.

## 1.2 Aluminium Alloys – The Tailored Strength

Aluminium alloys are designated by a **four-digit numerical system** (AA / Aluminium Association system), where the first digit identifies the principal alloying element:

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Marine Relevance |
|--------|---------------------------|------------------|
| 1xxx | Pure Aluminium (≥ 99 %) | Excellent corrosion resistance, low strength |
| 2xxx | Copper (Al-Cu) | High strength, **poor** seawater resistance |
| 3xxx | Manganese (Al-Mn) | Moderate strength, good formability |
| 4xxx | Silicon (Al-Si) | Welding wire, cladding |
| 5xxx | Magnesium (Al-Mg) | **Excellent seawater resistance** |
| 6xxx | Magnesium + Silicon (Al-Mg-Si) | Good corrosion resistance, structural |
| 7xxx | Zinc (Al-Zn-Mg-Cu) | Highest strength, susceptible to SCC |
| 8xxx | Other elements (Li, Sn, etc.) | Specialty alloys |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine workhorses:** Alloys **5083, 5086, 5454, 5456** (5xxx series) are the most widely used in ship hulls, superstructures, and aluminium-bronze-replacement propeller applications due to their exceptional resistance to chloride-containing seawater.

## 1.3 Aluminium Propellers – The Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a high-speed racing boat skimming across the ocean surface. The **propeller** spins furiously underwater, acting like a giant fan blade. Traditional ship propellers are made of **Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (NAB)** because bronze is heavy but tough. Now consider an **aluminium propeller** — it is roughly **half the weight** of an equivalent bronze propeller.

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of an aluminium propeller as a *race-car wheel* — feather-light and fast, but more sensitive to *rough road conditions* (cavitation, chloride attack, galvanic coupling). Bronze, in contrast, is the *heavy-duty truck tyre* — slower but more forgiving. Aluminium saves weight (better acceleration, higher RPM, fuel economy) but demands extra care to survive the *aggressive marine environment*.

The propeller is the **most corrosion-prone component** on a marine vessel because it simultaneously experiences:
- **Mechanical stresses** (centrifugal, hydrodynamic)
- **Electrochemical attack** (galvanic, pitting)
- **Cavitation damage** (collapsing vapour bubbles)
- **Biofouling** (marine growth)
- **Erosion-corrosion** (suspended particles + flow)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Aluminium Suffers Corrosion in Seawater

Aluminium spontaneously forms a thin, adherent, protective **aluminium oxide layer (Al₂O₃)** of thickness 2–10 nm on exposure to air or water:

$$4\text{Al} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$$

This passive film gives aluminium its *apparent* corrosion resistance. However, in **chloride-rich** seawater, chloride ions (Cl⁻) penetrate and locally break down the film, initiating **pitting corrosion**.

## 2.2 Corrosion Mechanisms Specifically Attacking Aluminium Propellers

### 2.2.1 Pitting Corrosion
- **Cause:** Local breakdown of the Al₂O₃ passive film by Cl⁻ ions.
- **Appearance:** Small, deep, dark pits, often hidden under corrosion product (white Al(OH)₃).
- **Propagation:** Inside the pit: $\text{Al} \rightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^-$; cathodic reaction outside: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$.
- **Inside pit:** $\text{Al}^{3+} + 3\text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{AlCl}_3$ (acidic, autocatalytic).

### 2.2.2 Galvanic Corrosion
- **Cause:** Coupling of aluminium with dissimilar metals (steel shaft, bronze hub, monel bolts).
- **Driving Force:** Potential difference in the **Galvanic Series in Seawater**.
- **Aluminium position:** Most anodic (active) → it **sacrifices itself**.

> [!WARNING]
> Aluminium is the **most anodic metal commonly used in marine engineering**. When coupled to even slightly nobler metals like stainless steel or bronze, severe galvanic attack on aluminium occurs at the joint.

### 2.2.3 Cavitation Corrosion (Most Critical for Propellers)
- **Mechanism:** Rapid formation and collapse of vapour bubbles in low-pressure regions of the rotating blade.
- **Bubble collapse** generates **shock pressures** up to **400 MPa**, physically eroding the protective oxide film.
- **Result:** Synergistic *mechanical + chemical* damage. Bare metal exposed beneath the eroded oxide → rapid electrochemical attack.

### 2.2.4 Crevice Corrosion
- **Location:** Under propeller hub, behind keyways, beneath marine growth, inside bolt threads.
- **Mechanism:** Oxygen-depleted crevice → differential aeration cell → metal dissolution inside the crevice.

### 2.2.5 Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
- **Susceptible Alloys:** 2xxx (Al-Cu) and 7xxx (Al-Zn) series.
- **Mechanism:** Tensile stress + chloride environment → intergranular cracking → catastrophic blade failure.

### 2.2.6 Erosion-Corrosion
- High-velocity water flow + suspended sand/silt particles → accelerated metal loss on leading edges of blades.

### 2.2.7 Intergranular Corrosion
- Occurs in improperly heat-treated 2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx alloys where precipitates form at grain boundaries, creating galvanic micro-cells.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---------------------|-----------|---------------|
| Density of Al | $\rho = 2.71$ | g/cm³ |
| Density of Bronze (NAB) | $\rho_{NAB} \approx 7.6$ | g/cm³ |
| Weight saving | $\Delta W = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{NAB}}\right) \times 100 \approx 64\%$ | Percentage |
| Faraday's Law of Corrosion | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | g (mass lost) |
| Corrosion Rate (mm/yr) | $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ | mm/year |
| Corrosion Current Density | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | A/cm² or mA/cm² |
| Pitting Potential (Al in 3 % NaCl) | $E_{pit} \approx -0.75$ to $-0.85$ | V vs SCE |
| Galvanic Corrosion Current | $I_g = \dfrac{E_c - E_a}{R_a + R_c}$ | Ampere |
| Nernst Equation (Half-cell) | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | V at 25 °C |
| Cavitation Pressure (collapse) | $P_c \approx P_v \left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_v}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | Pa |
| Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN) | $PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants used:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (Faraday), $M_{Al} = 26.98$ g/mol, $n = 3$ (valence of Al), $K = 87.6$ (conversion factor for mm/yr).

## 2.4 Real-World Significance

Aluminium propellers are favoured on **fast patrol boats, pleasure craft, hydrofoils, and surface-effect ships (SES)** where weight reduction directly translates to **speed, fuel economy, and payload capacity**. However, every kilogram of weight saved on the propeller demands **engineering compensation** through:
- Sacrificial **zinc or aluminium anodes** on the shaft
- **Anti-fouling + anti-cavitation coatings**
- **Cathodic protection (Impressed Current or sacrificial anode)**
- Selection of **5xxx series alloys** with ≥ 3 % Mg (best seawater grades)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Corrosion Rate of Aluminium in Seawater Using Faraday's Law

**Given Data (typical exam problem):**
- Aluminium propeller, area $A = 0.5$ m²
- Exposure time $T = 1$ year
- Density of Al: $D = 2.71$ g/cm³
- Measured mass loss: $W = 240$ g
- Atomic mass of Al: $M = 26.98$ g/mol
- Valence: $n = 3$
- Faraday constant: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

**Find:** Corrosion rate in **mm/year**.

**Solution Steps:**

**Step 1 — Apply the standard corrosion-rate formula (ASTM G102).**

$$CR \left(\dfrac{\text{mm}}{\text{yr}}\right) = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$$

where $K = 87.6 \times 10^4$ when units are in cm·g·hr system, simplifying to **87.6** when $W$ is in g, $A$ in cm², $T$ in hours, $D$ in g/cm³.

**Step 2 — Convert units.** Area: $A = 0.5 \text{ m}^2 = 5000 \text{ cm}^2$. Time: $T = 1 \text{ yr} = 8760 \text{ hr}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times 240}{5000 \times 8760 \times 2.71}$$

$$CR = \dfrac{21{,}024}{1.1870 \times 10^8}$$

$$CR = 1.771 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mm/yr.**

$$CR = 1.771 \times 10^{-4} \times 8760$$

$$\boxed{CR \approx 1.55 \text{ mm/year}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.** This is a **moderately high corrosion rate** (acceptable limit for marine aluminium is generally < 0.5 mm/yr). The propeller would suffer **excessive pitting** without protection.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Galvanic Corrosion Between Aluminium Propeller and Steel Shaft

**Given:** Aluminium propeller (area $A_a = 0.6$ m²) coupled to a steel shaft (area $A_c = 0.05$ m²).

**Galvanic Series Potentials (in flowing seawater):**
- Aluminium alloy 5083: $E_a = -0.78$ V vs SCE
- Carbon steel shaft: $E_c = -0.61$ V vs SCE

**Cathodic/Anodic polarisation resistances:** $R_a = 0.4$ Ω, $R_c = 1.2$ Ω.

**Step 1 — Compute the driving EMF.**

$$\Delta E = E_c - E_a = -0.61 - (-0.78) = 0.17 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute galvanic current using Ohm's law analogue.**

$$I_g = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_a + R_c} = \dfrac{0.17}{0.4 + 1.2} = \dfrac{0.17}{1.6}$$

$$\boxed{I_g = 0.1063 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute galvanic current density on aluminium (the anodic area).**

$$i_{corr} = \dfrac{I_g}{A_a} = \dfrac{0.1063}{0.6 \times 10^4} = 1.77 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Convert to corrosion rate via Faraday's Law.**

$$CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M \cdot 87.6}{n \cdot F \cdot D}$$

$$CR = \dfrac{1.77 \times 10^{-5} \times 26.98 \times 87.6}{3 \times 96{,}485 \times 2.71}$$

$$CR = \dfrac{0.04183}{7.844 \times 10^5}$$

$$\boxed{CR \approx 5.33 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mm/yr} = 0.053 \text{ mm/yr}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering conclusion.** Although the rate seems modest, **all corrosion occurs at the propeller–shaft interface**, leading to localised pitting and loss of fit. A **sacrificial zinc anode** (Zn potential $E = -1.10$ V) is bonded to the shaft to redirect the current.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cavitation Bubble Collapse Pressure (Rayleigh–Plesset Approximation)

**Given:** Vapour bubble of radius $R_0 = 0.5$ mm collapses in seawater at $T = 25$ °C. $P_v = 3.17$ kPa, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, polytropic index $\gamma = 1.4$.

**Step 1 — Apply Rayleigh's collapse pressure equation.**

$$P_c = P_v \left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_v}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$P_c = 3.17 \times \left(\dfrac{101.325}{3.17}\right)^{\frac{0.4}{1.4}}$$

$$P_c = 3.17 \times (31.96)^{0.2857}$$

$$P_c = 3.17 \times 2.628$$

$$\boxed{P_c \approx 8.33 \text{ kPa (single-bubble, isothermal)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **real propeller cavitation**, asymmetric collapse (involving micro-jet impact on the blade surface at velocities > 100 m/s) produces **localised pressures of 100–400 MPa**. This is sufficient to **fatigue the aluminium oxide film** and initiate cavitation-corrosion.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Aluminium vs NAB Propeller

| Parameter | Aluminium Propeller | NAB (Bronze) Propeller |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Density | 2.71 g/cm³ (**lighter**) | 7.6 g/cm³ |
| Relative Weight | **~ 35 % of bronze** | 100 % |
| Seawater Corrosion Resistance | Moderate (requires protection) | Excellent |
| Cavitation Resistance | **Poor** (severe erosion) | Excellent |
| Galvanic Susceptibility | **Very High** (most anodic) | Low |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Typical Speed Application | High-speed craft (< 40 knots) | All vessels |
| Maintenance | **Frequent anode replacement** | Periodic polishing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Aluminium Alloy Classification & Marine Selection Flow


## 4.3 Cathodic Protection Block Diagram for Aluminium Propeller

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DC [Direct Current Source]
        PSU[Impressed Current CP Unit]
    end
    subgraph SEA [Seawater Electrolyte]
        WATER[Conductive Sea Water]
    end
    subgraph ANODE [Anode Assembly]
        SA[Sacrificial Zinc or Al Anode]
    end
    subgraph CATH [Cathode Protected Structure]
        PROP[Aluminium Propeller]
        SHAFT[Steel Shaft]
        HULL[Ship Hull]
    end
    PSU -->|positive cable| SA
    SA -->|ionic current through water| WATER
    WATER -->|ionic current| PROP
    PROP -->|negative cable| PSU
    SHAFT -->|bonded| PROP
    HULL -->|bonded| PROP
    style DC fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style ANODE fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style CATH fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

## 4.4 Galvanic Series Position Diagram (Seawater)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Most Anodic Active] --> B[Magnesium]
    B --> C[Zinc]
    C --> D[Aluminium Alloys]
    D --> E[Cadmium]
    E --> F[Mild Steel]
    F --> G[Cast Iron]
    G --> H[Stainless Steel Active]
    H --> I[Lead]
    I --> J[Tin]
    J --> K[Copper]
    K --> L[Bronze NAB]
    L --> M[Stainless Steel Passive]
    M --> N[Monel]
    N --> O[Titanium]
    O --> P[Most Cathodic Noble]
    style D fill:ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style L fill:fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style P fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **aluminium is more anodic than bronze (NAB)**. If an aluminium propeller is mounted on a bronze hub, **galvanic corrosion attacks the aluminium** — never the bronze. This is why the *hub is bronze and blades are aluminium*, but careful isolation is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Explain why pure aluminium is rarely used for marine propellers and list three aluminium alloy series preferred for marine service.**

**Model Answer:**
Pure aluminium has insufficient mechanical strength (tensile strength ≈ 90 MPa) to withstand hydrodynamic and centrifugal stresses on a propeller. Alloying with Mg, Si, Cu, or Zn increases strength 3–5× while retaining corrosion resistance.

The three preferred marine alloy series are:
1. **5xxx series (Al-Mg):** Excellent seawater resistance, e.g., 5083, 5086, 5454, 5456.
2. **6xxx series (Al-Mg-Si):** Good corrosion resistance plus structural strength, e.g., 6061, 6082.
3. **Cast Al-Si alloys (e.g., A356, A357):** Used for cast propeller blades and marine housings.

The 2xxx and 7xxx series are **avoided** in marine service due to poor corrosion resistance and susceptibility to SCC.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is cavitation corrosion? Why is it especially severe on aluminium propellers?**

**Model Answer:**
Cavitation corrosion is the synergistic degradation of a metal surface caused by the **formation and violent collapse of vapour bubbles** in a liquid under low-pressure conditions (typically on the suction side of rotating propeller blades).

It is especially severe on aluminium propellers because:
1. Aluminium has a **relatively soft matrix** (Brinell hardness ~ 50 HB for 5083) compared to bronze (~ 150 HB), so mechanically it is more easily eroded.
2. The protective Al₂O₃ film is **brittle** and easily removed by bubble-collapse shock waves (~ 100–400 MPa).
3. Once the film is stripped, **fresh aluminium is exposed** to chloride-rich seawater, accelerating electrochemical corrosion.
4. High propeller tip speeds (often > 30 m/s) create intense low-pressure regions that promote cavitation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the various forms of corrosion encountered on aluminium marine propellers. Illustrate with relevant reactions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An aluminium alloy 5083 propeller of surface area **1.2 m²** suffers a mass loss of **480 g** in **6 months** of service in seawater. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/year** and comment on whether cathodic protection is necessary. (Given: density of 5083 = 2.66 g/cm³; atomic mass of Al = 27; valence = 3; Faraday constant = 96,500 C/mol.)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

The major corrosion forms attacking aluminium propellers are:

**1. Pitting Corrosion [2 marks]**
- Chloride ions (Cl⁻) from seawater penetrate the Al₂O₃ passive film at weak points.
- Anodic reaction inside the pit: $\text{Al} \rightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^-$
- Cathodic reaction outside: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$
- Inside the pit, hydrolysis: $\text{Al}^{3+} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Al(OH)}_3 + 3\text{H}^+$ — the local pH drops, accelerating attack.
- Pits often hidden beneath white, gelatinous Al(OH)₃ corrosion product.

**2. Galvanic Corrosion [2 marks]**
- Aluminium is the most anodic metal in the marine galvanic series (−0.75 V vs SCE).
- When coupled to the steel tail shaft, bronze hub, or monel bolts, a galvanic cell forms.
- Aluminium dissolves preferentially while the noble metal is cathodically protected.
- Reaction at the aluminium anode: $\text{Al} \rightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^-$.

**3. Cavitation Erosion-Corrosion [1 mark]**
- Vapour bubbles form in low-pressure zones on the suction side of the blade.
- Bubble collapse generates shock pressures up to 400 MPa, stripping the oxide film.
- Combined mechanical + chemical attack yields characteristic **spongy, honeycombed** damage.

**4. Crevice Corrosion [1 mark]**
- Differential aeration at hub-blade joints, bolt threads, and under marine growth.
- Oxygen-starved crevice interior becomes anodic; exterior becomes cathodic.

**5. Stress Corrosion Cracking [1 mark]** (only for 2xxx / 7xxx alloys)
- Simultaneous action of tensile stress and chloride ions → intergranular cracking → catastrophic brittle failure of blades.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Given:**
- $W = 480$ g
- $A = 1.2 \text{ m}^2 = 12{,}000 \text{ cm}^2$
- $T = 6 \text{ months} = 4380 \text{ hr}$
- $D = 2.66$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Apply the corrosion rate formula.**

$$CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$$

$$CR = \dfrac{87.6 \times 480}{12{,}000 \times 4380 \times 2.66}$$

**Step 2 — Compute denominator.**

$$12{,}000 \times 4380 \times 2.66 = 1.398 \times 10^8$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator.**

$$87.6 \times 480 = 42{,}048$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation.**

$$CR = \dfrac{42{,}048}{1.398 \times 10^8} = 3.01 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm/hr}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mm/year.**

$$CR = 3.01 \times 10^{-4} \times 8760 = 2.64 \text{ mm/year}$$

$$\boxed{CR \approx 2.64 \text{ mm/year}}$$

**Comment:** A corrosion rate of 2.64 mm/year is **far above the acceptable limit** for marine aluminium (< 0.5 mm/yr). **Cathodic protection is absolutely necessary.** Recommendation: install zinc sacrificial anodes on the shaft and apply an impressed current CP system with a reference Ag/AgCl electrode, targeting a protection potential of −0.95 V to −1.10 V vs Ag/AgCl.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a sacrificial anode cathodic protection system for an aluminium propeller. What materials are suitable as sacrificial anodes and why?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare aluminium and nickel-aluminium bronze (NAB) as propeller materials under the heads: (i) density and weight saving, (ii) corrosion resistance in seawater, (iii) cavitation resistance, (iv) galvanic compatibility with steel shaft, (v) typical marine applications.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Concept [3 marks]:**
Sacrificial anode cathodic protection (SACP) is an electrochemical technique in which a more **electrochemically active metal** (the anode) is electrically connected to the structure to be protected (the cathode). The anode preferentially corrodes, "sacrificing" itself while supplying electrons that suppress corrosion of the protected metal.

**Working Principle [2 marks]:**
- Aluminium propeller is connected by an insulated cable to a zinc (or aluminium) anode bolted to the shaft or hull.
- Zinc potential ($E = -1.10$ V) is more negative than aluminium ($E = -0.78$ V).
- In seawater, zinc dissolves: $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$.
- These electrons flow to the aluminium propeller, suppressing its anodic dissolution: $\text{Al} \rightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^-$ is arrested.
- The propeller is held at a protective potential of approximately −0.95 V vs Ag/AgCl.

**Suitable Anode Materials [2 marks]:**
- **Zinc anodes** (MIL-A-18001 specification): traditional, reliable, potential ≈ −1.10 V.
- **Aluminium anodes** (Al-Zn-In alloy): lighter, higher capacity (≈ 2700 Ah/kg vs 780 Ah/kg for Zn), preferred for modern aluminium propellers.
- Magnesium anodes are **not** suitable for seawater (too active, cause over-protection and hydrogen evolution damage).

**Sketch (to be drawn by student):** Refer to the Block Diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

| Parameter | Aluminium Propeller | NAB Bronze Propeller |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|
| (i) Density / Weight | 2.71 g/cm³ — **~ 64 % weight saving** | 7.6 g/cm³ — heavy |
| (ii) Seawater Corrosion Resistance | Moderate; needs CP and coatings | Excellent (intrinsic) |
| (iii) Cavitation Resistance | Poor; rapid erosion on blade tips | Excellent; withstands bubble collapse |
| (iv) Galvanic Compatibility with Steel Shaft | Aluminium becomes anode → severe attack; **zinc anode required** | NAB is slightly cathodic to steel; minimal galvanic issue |
| (v) Typical Applications | High-speed patrol boats, hydrofoils, SES, pleasure craft | Naval ships, merchant vessels, tankers, tugs |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing the galvanic direction** — students often write "bronze corrodes when coupled to aluminium." The reverse is true: **aluminium is anodic and corrodes**.
> 2. **Forgetting density and atomic mass in CR problems** — many students skip unit conversion from m² to cm² and from hours to years, leading to answers off by 10⁴.
> 3. **Mixing up series numbers** — 5083 (5xxx) is marine-grade, but 7075 (7xxx) and 2024 (2xxx) are **aircraft grades**, NOT for marine use.
> 4. **Omitting Faraday's Law reference** — KTU expects explicit mention of $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.
> 5. **Stating "aluminium doesn't corrode"** — it does, primarily via **pitting**, which is more dangerous than uniform corrosion because pits are hidden and structurally weakening.
> 6. **Skipping the protective film chemistry** — every answer on aluminium corrosion should mention the **Al₂O₃ passive layer** and its breakdown by chloride.
> 7. **Not drawing the diagram** — in 7-mark sub-questions on cathodic protection, a **labelled sketch** is mandatory; absence typically loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Aluminium density:** **2.71 g/cm³**; NAB bronze: **7.6 g/cm³** → weight saving ≈ **64 %**.
- **Most anodic marine metal:** Aluminium → highly susceptible to **galvanic corrosion**.
- **Preferred marine alloy series:** **5xxx (Al-Mg)** — 5083, 5086, 5454, 5456.
- **Avoid in marine service:** 2xxx (Al-Cu) and 7xxx (Al-Zn) — susceptible to **SCC** and pitting.
- **Passive film:** Al₂O₃, 2–10 nm thick — attacked by Cl⁻ ions.
- **Primary corrosion forms on Al propellers:** Pitting, galvanic, cavitation-erosion, crevice, SCC, intergranular.
- **Cavitation collapse pressure:** Up to **400 MPa** locally — strips oxide, accelerates corrosion.
- **Pitting potential of Al in NaCl:** ~ **−0.75 to −0.85 V vs SCE**.
- **Cathodic protection anodes:** Zinc (traditional) or Al-Zn-In alloy (modern) — **never magnesium** in seawater.
- **Protective potential for Al in seawater:** **−0.95 V to −1.10 V vs Ag/AgCl** reference electrode.
- **Faraday constant (F):** **96,485 C/mol** (use 96,500 in KTU exams if specified).
- **Key corrosion-rate formula:** $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ — units: W in g, A in cm², T in hr, D in g/cm³.
- **Acceptable CR for marine Al:** Generally **< 0.5 mm/year**; above this → CP mandatory.
- **Aluminium propeller advantages:** Lightweight → higher RPM → better speed and fuel economy; cheaper.
- **Aluminium propeller disadvantages:** Poor cavitation resistance; severe galvanic coupling; needs frequent anode replacement.
- **Why NAB still dominates large ship propellers:** Superior cavitation resistance, fatigue life, and corrosion tolerance despite being heavy.
- **Anti-corrosion coatings for Al propellers:** Anodising (Type 2 / Type 3 hard anodising), epoxy primers, anti-fouling biocide layers, polymer cavitation-resistant overlays (e.g., polyurethane elastomers).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Machinery and Deck Fittings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Marine Machinery and Deck Fittings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Marine Machinery — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Marine Machinery** refers to the complete set of mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic equipment installed on a sea-going vessel, including the main propulsion system, auxiliary engines, boilers, pumps, purifiers, heat exchangers, compressors, steering gear, and shafting systems. From a **materials engineering** perspective (KTU OENST612 perspective), the focus is on the **metallurgy, fabrication, corrosion behaviour, and protective strategies** applied to the components of this machinery when exposed to the marine environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For Module 2 of OENST612, the examiner expects students to map every machinery/deck fitting to (a) its base material, (b) its operating environment, and (c) the relevant corrosion mitigation technique. Memorising only component names will NOT earn full marks.

## 1.2 Deck Fittings — Formal Definition

**Deck Fittings** are the structural and mechanical accessories mounted on the weather deck, superstructure, and hull penetrations of a ship. These include **mooring equipment (winches, windlasses, capstans, bollards, fairleads), anchoring equipment (anchors, chain cables, hawse pipes), cargo handling gear (derricks, cranes, hatch covers), and hull fittings (valves, sea chests, rudder, propeller).**

All deck fittings operate in the most corrosive zone of a ship — the **splash zone** and **atmospheric zone** — and hence are designed with special consideration for **galvanic compatibility, cathodic protection, and coating systems.**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Ship as a Human Body"**
> 
> Think of a ship as a human body:
> - **Main Engine** = the *heart* (pumps the lifeblood — torque — to the propeller).
> - **Boilers & Heat Exchangers** = the *lungs and liver* (process fuel, exchange heat, clean fluids).
> - **Pumps, Compressors, Purifiers** = the *kidneys and arteries* (circulate, filter, pressurise).
> - **Propeller & Shaft** = the *legs* (move the body forward).
> - **Steering Gear & Rudder** = the *brain directing the hands* (change direction).
> - **Deck fittings (winches, anchors, bollards)** = the *hands and grip* (hold the ship, grab cargo, tie up to port).
> 
> Just as a human body's organs are made of specialised tissues (bone, muscle, blood) — marine machinery is made of specialised **alloys, coatings, and composites** chosen for their resistance to **salt, water, heat, fatigue, and galvanic corrosion**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Engineering Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Numerical Benchmarks to Memorise for KTU 2024 Exams**
> - Seawater resistivity: **ρ ≈ 20–25 Ω·cm** (for impressed current cathodic protection design of hulls).
> - Design current density for bare steel hull: **i ≈ 100–150 mA/m²** (tropical waters).
> - Protected hull current density: **i ≈ 10–30 mA/m²** (with coatings).
> - Galvanic series potential of zinc anode (Ag/AgCl reference): **E ≈ −1.05 V**.
> - Standard propeller shaft material: **Stainless Steel Grade 316L** or **Ni-Al-Bronze (NAB)**.
> - Standard anchor chain grade: **U2 / U3 stud-link chain** with **40 kg/m** linear mass for 76 mm dia.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Marine Machinery (By Function)

| # | Category | Examples | Primary Material Concern |
|---|----------|----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Propulsion Machinery** | Main diesel engine, reduction gear, thrust bearing, tailshaft | High-cycle fatigue, corrosion fatigue, **cavitation erosion** of propeller |
| 2 | **Auxiliary Machinery** | Generators, compressors, purifiers, FW generators | Pitting, crevice corrosion, **hot corrosion** |
| 3 | **Steam Raising Plant** | Oil-fired boilers, exhaust gas boilers, economisers | **Caustic embrittlement**, scale, hot-spot corrosion |
| 4 | **Pumping & Piping** | Bilge, ballast, fire, lube-oil, cooling seawater pumps | **Galvanic corrosion** at dissimilar joints, erosion-corrosion at bends |
| 5 | **Deck Machinery** | Windlasses, mooring winches, capstans, cranes | Atmospheric corrosion, **fretting** at sheave pins |
| 6 | **Steering & Manoeuvring** | Rudder, steering gear, bow/stern thrusters | **Impingement attack**, weld decay |

## 2.2 The Five Critical Degradation Mechanisms on Marine Machinery

1. **Uniform (General) Corrosion** — Mild steel hull plates, ballast tanks. Controlled by **coatings + ICCP**.
2. **Pitting Corrosion** — Stainless steel pump impellers, heat exchanger tubes. Controlled by **Mo-bearing grades (316L, 904L, 6Mo)**.
3. **Crevice Corrosion** — Gaskets, flanges, bolted joints, under-deposit attack. Controlled by **elastomeric gaskets + design**.
4. **Galvanic Corrosion** — Bronze propeller ↔ steel hull, stainless shaft ↔ bronze bearing. Controlled by **sacrificial anodes (Zn, Al)** and **insulating bushes**.
5. **Erosion-Corrosion & Cavitation** — Pump impellers, propeller tips, overboard discharge nozzles. Controlled by **rubber lining, NAB, duplex stainless steels**.
6. **Corrosion Fatigue** — Propeller blade root, crankshafts, spring eyes. Controlled by **shot peening, nitriding, low residual stress welds**.

## 2.3 Classification of Deck Fittings (By Location on the Ship)

| Zone | Fittings | Material Used | Corrosion Mitigation |
|------|----------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Forecastle** (Bow) | Anchors, chain stoppers, hawse pipes, windlass | Cast steel (anchors), U2/U3 chain (galvanised), mild steel (windlass) | Hot-dip galvanising, sacrificial anodes, marine paint |
| **Main Deck** | Mooring winches, bollards, fairleads, chocks, cleats | Cast steel, ductile iron, mild steel | Heavy-duty epoxy + polyurethane topcoat |
| **Aft Deck** | Tug winches, stern roller, towing pins | High-tensile steel, alloy steel | Cathodic protection, grease-lubricated bearings |
| **Superstructure** | Ventilators, mushroom vents, weathertight doors | Mild steel, stainless steel (304) | Powder coating, galvanising |
| **Hull Penetrations** | Sea chests, overboard discharges, shaft brackets | Carbon steel with rubber lining / NAB | Rubber lining, Impressed Current CP |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table: Master Equation Bank for Module 2**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes / Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Corrosion rate (weight loss) | $$CR = \frac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}$$ | mm/yr | K = 8.76 × 10⁴ (SI); W = mass loss (g); A = area (cm²); t = time (hr); ρ = density (g/cm³) |
| 2 | Corrosion rate (from current density) | $$CR = \frac{i \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$ | mm/yr | i = current density (A/cm²); M = molar mass; n = valency; F = 96485 C/mol |
| 3 | Cathodic protection current | $$I_{total} = i_{design} \cdot A_{protected}$$ | A | i_design for coated hull ≈ 10–30 mA/m² |
| 4 | Zinc anode life | $$L = \frac{m \cdot \eta}{I_{total} \cdot 8760}$$ | years | m = anode mass (kg); η = utilisation factor (0.85 for Zn) |
| 5 | Galvanic series potential | $$E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$$ | V (vs SHE) | Used to predict galvanic corrosion direction |
| 6 | Heat input in welding | $$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$ | kJ/mm | Controls HAZ grain growth in marine shafts |
| 7 | Stress in propeller shaft | $$\tau = \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot D^{3}}$$ | MPa | T = torque; D = shaft diameter; design for fatigue |
| 8 | Reynolds number (cavitation) | $$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$$ | dimensionless | Re > 10⁵ ⇒ pump impeller erosion zone |
| 9 | NPSH (cavitation check) | $$NPSH_{avail} = \frac{p_{atm} - p_{vapor}}{\rho g} \pm h$$ | m | Must exceed NPSH_required for pump |
| 10 | Specific fuel consumption | $$sfc = \frac{m_{fuel}}{P \cdot t}$$ | g/kWh | Used in propulsion efficiency analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE** for KTU exams: Always state the **units of every numerical answer**. Examiners routinely deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) numerical questions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In shipping industry practice:
- **Classification Societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS)** mandate specific **corrosion allowances** (typically 0.5–2 mm) on structural members of marine machinery.
- **IMO PSPC (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings)** requires ballast tank coatings with **15-year** design life — this directly drives the material and coating selection in Module 2.
- **Selection of NAB (Nickel-Aluminium-Bronze) for propellers** is not arbitrary — it is the optimum trade-off between **cavitation resistance, biofouling resistance, and galvanic compatibility with steel hulls**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1 — Corrosion Rate from Weight Loss (Faraday's Law Application)

**Problem Statement:** A ship-board mild steel plate (density 7.85 g/cm³, area 200 cm²) loses 4.5 g due to uniform corrosion during 720 hours in seawater. Determine the corrosion rate in **mm/yr** using the standard formula.

**Step 1 — Recall the governing equation:**

$$CR = \frac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 2 — Identify known values:**

$$K = 8.76 \times 10^{4}, \quad W = 4.5\,\text{g}, \quad A = 200\,\text{cm}^{2}, \quad t = 720\,\text{hr}, \quad \rho = 7.85\,\text{g/cm}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values into the numerator:**

$$K \cdot W = (8.76 \times 10^{4}) \times 4.5 = 394{,}200$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$A \cdot t \cdot \rho = 200 \times 720 \times 7.85 = 1{,}130{,}400$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator by denominator:**

$$CR = \frac{394{,}200}{1{,}130{,}400} = 0.3487\,\text{mm/yr}$$

**Step 6 — Round to 3 significant figures (KTU standard):**

$$CR \approx 0.349\,\text{mm/yr}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** A rate of 0.35 mm/yr is considered **moderate-to-high** in marine service. A typical corrosion allowance of **2 mm on a 20 mm plate** gives a design life of approximately 5–6 years, which justifies the use of coatings + ICCP.

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2 — Galvanic Corrosion Current Between Bronze Propeller and Steel Hull

**Problem Statement:** A bronze propeller (cathode area = 1.2 m²) is coupled to a steel hull (anode area = 80 m²) via a stainless steel shaft with an insulating bush (assume 70% of hull is effectively galvanically coupled). Seawater resistivity ρ = 22 Ω·cm. The mixed potential of the galvanic couple is 250 mV anodic to the hull's free corrosion potential. Compute the **total galvanic current**.

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation for galvanic current (Ohm's law in electrolyte):**

$$I = \frac{\Delta E}{R_{electrolyte}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the electrolyte resistance for a planar couple (Wagner-Traud approximation):**

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A_{eff}}$$

**Step 3 — Assume an effective path length L = 0.5 m, effective cathode area A_eff = 1.2 m²:**

$$R = \frac{0.22\,\Omega \cdot m \times 0.5\,\text{m}}{1.2\,\text{m}^{2}} = 0.0917\,\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Apply Ohm's law:**

$$I = \frac{0.250\,\text{V}}{0.0917\,\Omega} = 2.727\,\text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to current density at the cathode (propeller):**

$$i_{cathode} = \frac{I}{A_{prop}} = \frac{2.727}{1.2} = 2.27\,\text{A/m}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to current density at the anode (hull):**

$$i_{anode} = \frac{I}{A_{hull,eff}} = \frac{2.727}{0.7 \times 80} = 0.0487\,\text{A/m}^{2} = 48.7\,\text{mA/m}^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** The hull current density of ~49 mA/m² is below the design value of 100–150 mA/m² for tropical waters. A **zinc sacrificial anode of 5 kg** is therefore sufficient to offset this galvanic current on this vessel. A larger ship or a bronze propeller with greater surface area would need proportionally more anodes.

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3 — Cathodic Protection Anode Life Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A ship hull (wetted area 1500 m²) is to be protected by zinc anodes. Design current density = 25 mA/m². Each anode has mass m = 12 kg. Anode utilisation factor η = 0.85. Compute the **service life** of the anodes.

**Step 1 — Total current required:**

$$I_{total} = i_{design} \times A = 25 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A/m}^{2} \times 1500\,\text{m}^{2} = 37.5\,\text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical capacity of one zinc anode (Faraday's law, using Zn²⁺, M = 65.4 g/mol, n = 2):**

$$Q_{theoretical} = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M} = \frac{12000 \times 2 \times 96485}{65.4} = 35{,}405{,}505\,\text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Practical capacity (multiply by utilisation factor):**

$$Q_{practical} = \eta \cdot Q_{theoretical} = 0.85 \times 35{,}405{,}505 = 30{,}094{,}680\,\text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Time in seconds:**

$$t = \frac{Q_{practical}}{I_{total}} = \frac{30{,}094{,}680}{37.5} = 802{,}524\,\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to years:**

$$t = \frac{802{,}524}{3600 \times 24 \times 365} = 25.5\,\text{years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** In practice, anode life is computed for the **full anode inventory** (e.g., 30 anodes = 360 kg total), giving ~25 years per anode. KTU 2024 examiners will accept either per-anode or total fleet lifetime; just **state the assumption clearly**.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Material Selection for Deck Fittings

| Deck Fitting | Material Options | Best Choice | Justification |
|--------------|------------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Anchor** | Cast iron / Mild steel / Cast steel | **Cast steel (Grade 430 / 2A)** | High impact strength, weld-repairable, galvanisable |
| **Anchor Chain** | U1 / U2 / U3 stud-link | **U2 or U3 (galvanised)** | U3 has 30% higher proof load, mandatory for tankers > 100,000 DWT |
| **Windlass Brake Band** | Carbon steel / Cast iron | **Carbon steel with liner** | Heat dissipation, friction coefficient |
| **Mooring Winch Drum** | Cast iron / Steel weldment | **Steel weldment (ASTM A516)** | Better fatigue life than cast iron |
| **Bollard** | Cast iron / Ductile iron / Cast steel | **Ductile iron or cast steel** | Cast iron is brittle and shatters under impact loads |
| **Rudder Stock** | Mild steel / Forged steel | **Forged steel (ASTM A668)** | High yield strength (≥ 250 MPa) for fatigue loading |
| **Rudder Blade** | Mild steel plate | **Mild steel with 2 mm CA + ICCP** | Weldable, repairable in service |
| **Propeller** | Manganese bronze / Ni-Al-Bronze / Muntz metal | **Ni-Al-Bronze (NAB, Cu-Al-Fe-Ni)** | Best cavitation, fatigue, and galvanic compatibility |
| **Propeller Shaft** | Carbon steel / 316L SS / 17-4 PH | **17-4 PH SS or 316L** | Corrosion fatigue resistance, ICCP compatible |
| **Sea Chest** | Carbon steel + rubber lining | **Carbon steel + chloroprene rubber** | Erosion-corrosion resistance at high seawater velocity |
| **Hatch Cover** | Mild steel | **Mild steel, hot-dip galvanised + epoxy** | Corrosion in cargo hold + atmospheric exposure |

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example — Cathodic Protection Design for a Tanker

**Problem:** A coastal product tanker has a wetted hull area of 4000 m² in tropical waters. The paint system provides 90% protection efficiency. ICCP system design output: 200 aluminium anodes of 8 kg each are fitted, plus 1 reference electrode. Verify that the system can supply the required current.

**Step 1 — Bare hull current density (tropical):**

$$i_{bare} = 130\,\text{mA/m}^{2} \quad \text{(assumed)}$$

**Step 2 — Effective current density after paint:**

$$i_{eff} = i_{bare} \times (1 - 0.90) = 130 \times 0.10 = 13\,\text{mA/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Total required current:**

$$I_{required} = 13 \times 10^{-3} \times 4000 = 52\,\text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Verify ICCP system output:** A typical 30 V / 50 A rectifier can supply 50 A, so a 75 A unit is selected with 20% safety margin → 60 A continuous output → **system adequate**.

**Step 5 — Anode life check (Al anode, 8 kg):**
Aluminium theoretical capacity ≈ 2980 A·hr/kg. Effective capacity = 0.90 × 2980 × 8 = 21,456 A·hr.
