---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, OENST612 Module 3]

**Define degreasing. Why is it performed as the first step in shipyard surface preparation?**

**Model Answer:**

Degreasing is the process of removing organic contaminants (oils, greases, waxes) and soluble inorganic salts from a steel surface using solvents, alkaline cleaners, steam, or mechanical means, as codified under **SSPC-SP1 (Solvent Cleaning)**.

It is performed as the first step because:
1. **Adhesion:** Residual oil prevents the primer from wetting the steel — the coating floats on the contaminant film.
2. **Abrasive contamination:** If blasting is done on oily steel, the abrasive media becomes impregnated with oil and redistributes it into the freshly prepared profile.
3. **Invisible salt contamination:** Fingerprint chlorides trapped under a coating cause osmotic blistering and underfilm corrosion within 1–3 years of marine service.

> **Key terms to mention in 3-mark answer:** SSPC-SP1, adhesion, soluble salts, oily film, underfilm corrosion.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, OENST612 Module 3]

**List any four degreasing methods used in shipyards. Compare alkaline cleaning and solvent cleaning in two points each.**

**Model Answer:**

Four degreasing methods:
1. **Solvent cleaning (SSPC-SP1)**
2. **Alkaline cleaning (hot dip / spray)**
3. **Steam cleaning**
4. **Vapor degreasing** (and optionally: emulsion cleaning, ultrasonic)

**Comparison:**

| Criterion | Alkaline Cleaning | Solvent Cleaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Chemical — saponification, emulsification | Physical — dissolution |
| **Removes soluble salts** | Yes (water-based, dissolves salts) | No (immiscible with salts) |
| **Fire / VOC hazard** | Low | High |
| **Cost** | Higher capital (heated tank) | Lower capital (rag + solvent) |

> **Tip:** Always present a comparison **table** in the exam — it is examiner-friendly and ensures you don't miss points.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU ESE December 2024 Style — Module 3]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **mechanism of alkaline degreasing** with relevant chemical equations. Discuss the role of **(i) sodium hydroxide, (ii) sodium silicate, (iii) surfactants, and (iv) sequestrants** in the cleaning bath. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard uses a hot alkaline dip tank of **8,000 litres** for pre-blast degreasing of hull plates. The bath operates at **75°C, pH 12.5**, and the soil load is **1.2 kg of oleic acid (C₁₇H₃₃COOH, M = 282 g/mol) per shift**. If a **50% stoichiometric excess of NaOH** is to be maintained, calculate the **mass and volume of commercial 50% w/w NaOH solution (ρ = 1.525 g/cm³)** to be added per shift. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Mechanism of Alkaline Degreasing:**

Alkaline degreasing operates on **four simultaneous mechanisms:**

**1. Saponification (Chemical reaction):**
Fatty acids (e.g., from fingerprints, vegetable cutting fluids) react with NaOH to form water-soluble sodium soaps:

$$
\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{33}\text{COOH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{33}\text{COO}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**2. Emulsification (Surfactant action):**
Surfactant molecules have a **hydrophobic tail** and a **hydrophilic head**. They lower the oil-water interfacial tension and lift non-polar oils (mineral oils, grease) off the steel as a milky oil-in-water emulsion.

**3. Dispersion & Wetting:**
Wetting agents (e.g., sodium silicate) lower the **contact angle** of water on steel, ensuring the cleaning solution reaches into crevices, weld roots, and surface pits.

**4. Dissolution of Salts:**
Water-soluble contaminants (chlorides, sulphates) are simply dissolved into the aqueous bath and carried away in the rinse.

**Roles of bath components:**

- **(i) Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH):** Provides alkalinity (pH 11–13), drives saponification of fatty acids, neutralizes acidic soils, and aids emulsification by raising the negative surface charge on dirt particles.
- **(ii) Sodium Silicate (Na₂SiO₃):** Acts as a **wetting agent** and **corrosion inhibitor** (forms a thin protective silicate film on steel). It also buffers the pH and stabilizes the emulsion.
- **(iii) Surfactants (e.g., nonylphenol ethoxylates):** Provide the **emulsification** function for non-saponifiable mineral oils. They adsorb at the oil-water interface and form micelles that suspend oil droplets.
- **(iv) Sequestrants (e.g., EDTA, sodium tripolyphosphate):** Chelate (bind) **hard-water ions** (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) and **dissolved metal ions** (Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺ from rust) that would otherwise precipitate as scum and re-deposit on the steel.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Moles of oleic acid per shift:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{acid}} &= \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{1200\ \text{g}}{282\ \text{g/mol}} \\[4pt]
n_{\text{acid}} &\approx 4.255\ \text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric NaOH (1:1 ratio for monocarboxylic acid):**

$$
n_{\text{NaOH, stoich}} = 4.255\ \text{mol}
$$

**Step 3 — With 50% excess:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{NaOH, actual}} &= 4.255 \times 1.50 \\[4pt]
n_{\text{NaOH, actual}} &= 6.383\ \text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of pure NaOH:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{NaOH, pure}} &= 6.383 \times 40 \\[4pt]
m_{\text{NaOH, pure}} &\approx 255.3\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass of 50% commercial solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{50\%}} &= \dfrac{255.3}{0.50} = 510.6\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Volume of 50% solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{50\%}} &= \dfrac{510.6\ \text{g}}{1.525\ \text{g/cm}^3} \\[4pt]
V_{\text{50\%}} &\approx 334.8\ \text{cm}^3 \approx 335\ \text{mL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Add **≈ 335 mL of 50% commercial NaOH solution per shift** to maintain the 50% excess.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently forget to **multiply by 1.5 (or whatever % excess is given)**. They also confuse **w/w %** with **w/v %** and apply the wrong density conversion. State your assumption clearly: *"Assuming 50% w/w commercial lye solution with density 1.525 g/cm³"*. The **molar mass of oleic acid (282 g/mol)** is a frequently misread value — write it down at the top of your solution.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [Internal Choice Alternative — KTU 2024 ESE]

**(a)** Discuss the **ISO 8502 surface cleanliness verification tests** used after degreasing. Explain the **Bresle patch test (ISO 8502-6)** and the **dust tape test (ISO 8502-3)** with procedure and acceptance criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard pre-blast inspection shows that 60% of plates fail the **chloride cleanliness test** with readings of **300 mg/m²** (Bresle patch, A = 1250 mm², V = 3 mL). The plant manager wishes to upgrade from solvent wipe to **hot alkaline dip (75°C, 30 min)** which historically achieves **95% chloride reduction**. Calculate the **expected residual chloride after alkaline dip** and determine if the plates will now pass the **50 mg/m² limit**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — ISO 8502 Verification Tests:**

Surface cleanliness **after degreasing and before primer application** is verified using the ISO 8502 family of standards. The two most relevant to degreasing are:

**1. Bresle Patch Test (ISO 8502-6 / ISO 8502-9) — Soluble Salt Detection:**

- **Purpose:** Quantify **water-soluble salts** (chlorides, sulphates, nitrates) on the steel surface.
- **Procedure:**
  - Apply an adhesive **Bresle patch** (flexible silicone well) of known area (typically A = 1250 mm²) to the cleaned steel.
  - Inject **3 mL of distilled water** through the patch's self-sealing membrane.
  - Allow **20 minutes** for salts to dissolve.
  - Withdraw the solution and test with **Quantab strip** (for chloride) or lab analysis.
  - Calculate **surface salt density** in mg/m².

- **Acceptance Criteria:**
  - **Marine immersion service:** **< 50 mg/m² chlorides**
  - **Atmospheric service:** **< 100 mg/m²**
  - Some specifications (e.g., NORSOK M-501) require **< 20 mg/m²** for critical service.

**2. Dust Tape Test (ISO 8502-3) — Dust Assessment:**

- **Purpose:** Quantify **residual dust** from blasting before primer application.
- **Procedure:**
  - Press a **black adhesive tape** (10 cm long) firmly onto the steel.
  - Peel off and place on a white card.
  - Compare the dust quantity on the tape against a **reference photograph scale (0 to 5)**.

- **Acceptance Criteria:**
  - **Class 1 or better** for most marine specifications.
  - **Class 2** is the minimum permissible for general marine atmospheric.
  - **Class 0** (no visible dust) is the most stringent.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Initial chloride: $\rho_{\text{Cl, i}} = 300\ \text{mg/m}^2$
- Reduction by alkaline dip: **95%**
- Target: $\rho_{\text{Cl, target}} \le 50\ \text{mg/m}^2$

**Step 1 — Expected residual after 95% reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{Cl, f}} &= \rho_{\text{Cl, i}} \times (1 - 0.95) \\[4pt]
\rho_{\text{Cl, f}} &= 300 \times 0.05 \\[4pt]
\rho_{\text{Cl, f}} &= 15\ \text{mg/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with limit:**

$$
\rho_{\text{Cl, f}} = 15\ \text{mg/m}^2 < 50\ \text{mg/m}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{PASS}
$$

**Final Answer:** Yes, the plates will now **pass** the chloride cleanliness test with a comfortable margin (15 mg/m² vs. 50 mg/m² limit). The upgrade to hot alkaline dip is justified.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** A common mistake is treating "95% reduction" as a multiplicative factor on the limit (i.e., $50 \times 0.95 = 47.5$ — **wrong**). The 95% applies to the **initial contaminant level**, not the limit. Always write: *"After 95% reduction, residual = Initial × 0.05"*. The Bresle patch area (1250 mm²) and extract volume (3 mL) are **distractors** in this question — they are not needed because the reading is already given in mg/m². Identifying distractors is itself a high-skill exam technique.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Module 3, Degreasing in Shipyards**

- **Definition:** Degreasing = removal of oils, greases, waxes, and soluble salts from steel **before** any other surface preparation.
- **Standard:** **SSPC-SP1** is the ONLY degreasing-specific standard. It is the *mandatory first step* before any mechanical preparation (SP2–SP10).
- **Sequence is fixed:** **Degrease (SP1) → Blast (SP6/SP10) → Dust-off (ISO 8502-3) → Bresle Test (ISO 8502-6) → Prime within 4 hours**.
- **Six methods to remember:** Solvent wipe, alkaline dip/spray, emulsion, steam, vapor, ultrasonic.
- **Alkaline cleaning mechanism:** Saponification (fatty acids) + Emulsification (mineral oils) + Wetting + Salt dissolution.
- **Bath components:** NaOH (saponification) + Na₂SiO₃ (wetting, inhibitor) + Surfactant (emulsification) + Sequestrant (chelation).
- **Operating window:** pH **10.5 – 13.5**, Temperature **60 – 90°C**, Time **5 – 30 min**.
- **Saponification stoichiometry:** $1\ \text{mol fatty acid} : 1\ \text{mol NaOH}$. Triglycerides need **3 mol NaOH**.
- **Arrhenius rule of thumb:** Cleaning rate **doubles to triples per 10°C rise**.
- **Chloride limit:** **< 50 mg/m²** for marine immersion (ISO 8502-6).
- **Dust limit:** **≤ Class 2** (ISO 8502-3 tape test).
- **Bresle patch area:** **1250 mm²**; extract volume: **3 mL**; dissolution time: **20 min**.
- **Why solvent wipe alone is insufficient:** Does not remove soluble salts; rag can re-deposit oil.
- **Vapor degreasing solvents:** Trichloroethylene (BP 87°C), perchloroethylene (BP 121°C), n-propyl bromide — all restricted under REACH/ODS rules.
- **Steam cleaning:** Typical **100 – 350 bar, 150°C**.
- **Ultrasonic:** **20 – 400 kHz**, cavitation removes soils from crevices.
- **Surface energy of clean steel:** **~70 mN/m** (high — good for wetting).
- **Acceptable contact angle for primer:** **< 60°** (verified by **dyne ink test**).
- **Why degrease before blasting:** Prevents abrasive media contamination and oil entrapment in blast profile.
- **Top 3 failure modes if degreasing is skipped:** Loss of adhesion (3–12 mo), osmotic blistering (6–24 mo), underfilm corrosion (1–3 yr).
- **Top 3 exam keywords:** **SSPC-SP1, Saponification, Bresle patch test (ISO 8502-6)**.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Degreasing | OENST612 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weathering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Weathering # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Weathering** is defined as the progressive deterioration or alteration of a material's physical, chemical, or mechanical properties due to its prolonged exposure to ambient atmospheric and environmental conditions. In the context of marine engineering, weathering encompasses a synergistic combination of **atmospheric corrosion**, **photochemical degradation**, **mechanical erosion**, and **biological fouling** acting simultaneously upon structural and functional marine materials. The KTU 2024 Scheme defines weathering in the marine context as a **multi-mechanism surface degradation phenomenon** distinct from, but often coexisting with, bulk aqueous corrosion. Unlike immersion corrosion (which proceeds uniformly under a continuous electrolyte layer), weathering is characteristically **intermittent, cyclic, and zone-dependent**, governed by the **wet–dry cycle frequency** at the material–atmosphere interface. ### 1.1.1 Standardized Terminology Table | Term | KTU 2024 Definition | Distinguishing Feature | |------|---------------------|------------------------| | **Weathering** | Cumulative environmental degradation | Surface-dominated, cyclic | | **Atmospheric Corrosion** | Electrochemical oxidation at the metal/atmosphere interface | Requires electrolyte film | | **Marine Fouling** | Biological colonization of submerged/splash surfaces | Biofilm-driven | | **Erosion-Corrosion** | Mechanical surface removal accelerating corrosion | Flow-assisted | | **Photo-oxidation** | UV-induced polymer chain scission | Solar-radiation dependent | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **The Rusty Iron-Gate Analogy:** Imagine an old iron gate at a seaside house. The paint peels in summer (UV degradation), rusts in monsoon (wet–dry chloride attack), gets pocked with pits near the ground (splash-zone corrosion), and green algae grows on the shaded side (biological fouling). None of these acts alone — they all reinforce one another. **That cumulative, multi-actor environmental breakdown of a material is precisely what marine engineers call "weathering."** ### 1.2.1 The Three Pillars of Marine Weathering Marine weathering operates through three parallel and interacting mechanisms: 1. **Physical (Mechanical) Weathering** — Caused by thermal cycling, wind-driven particulates, wave impact, salt-crystal growth, and freeze–thaw action (in temperate waters). These processes **mechanically disrupt protective films**. 2. **Chemical Weathering** — Dominated by atmospheric corrosion reactions, oxidation, hydrolysis, and salt-spray deposition. The classic example is the formation of **red-brown rust (Fe₂O₃·nH₂O)** on mild steel in marine atmospheres. 3. **Biological Weathering (Bio-weathering)** — Caused by marine microorganisms, barnacles, algae, and bacterial biofilms that secrete corrosive metabolic byproducts (e.g., **sulfate-reducing bacteria → H₂S, organic acids**). ## 1.3 The KTU 2024 Critical Distinction: Weathering ≠ Corrosion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Weathering is the broader umbrella; corrosion is one of its sub-processes.** A polymer composite exposed to UV light will weather (photo-degrade) without undergoing any electrochemical corrosion. A rubber seal in an engine room will weather (oxidize and crack) without forming rust. Therefore, KTU examiners expect students to recognize weathering as a **multi-physics degradation envelope**, not a synonym for rusting. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Standard Corrosion Rate for Mild Steel in Marine Atmosphere (ISO 9223, Category C5-M):** **80–200 µm/year** - **Salt Spray Deposition Rate (ASTM B117):** **1–2 mL/hr per 80 cm²** of NaCl solution - **Critical Relative Humidity for Carbon Steel:** **≈ 60%** (below this, atmospheric corrosion is negligible) - **UV Wavelength Range causing Polymer Degradation:** **280–400 nm** (UV-A and UV-B bands) - **Time of Wetness (TOW) Threshold:** **τ ≥ 0.5 hr at RH ≥ 80%** at 0 °C (per **ISO 9223**) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four-Stage Weathering Cycle (KTU 2024 Framework) The KTU 2024 Scheme organizes marine weathering into a **sequential four-stage cyclic model**, often appearing as a 14-mark ESE question: ### Stage I — Wetting (Electrolyte Formation) - Salt-laden air deposits hygroscopic chloride particles (**NaCl, MgCl₂**). - At RH > critical value, these deliquesce to form a thin, conductive **electrolyte film** (≈ 1–100 µm thick). - Oxygen from the atmosphere dissolves into this film. - **The corrosion cell is now established.** ### Stage II — Active Reaction (Electrochemical Corrosion) - Anodic dissolution: $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$ - Cathodic reduction: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ - Chloride ions ($\text{Cl}^-$) break down passive films (e.g., on stainless steel). - **Bulk rust formation begins.** ### Stage III — Drying (Concentration of Aggressors) - Evaporation concentrates chlorides in pores and crevices. - Differential aeration cells form under rust deposits. - **Pitting corrosion is initiated in this stage.** - Polymers undergo photo-oxidative chain scission (UV-driven). ### Stage IV — Re-Wetting (Repetition) - Next tide, splash, or rain event re-wets the surface. - The cycle repeats, with each pass depositing more corrosion product. - **Cumulative damage grows non-linearly.** ## 2.2 Governing Equations (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet) > [!NOTE] > All equations below are **directly testable** in KTU ESE questions. Master each row of the formula sheet. ### 2.2.1 Atmospheric Corrosion Rate (Bilamellar/Toumi-Ben Amor Model) The classic atmospheric corrosion mass-loss expression is: $$ C(t) = A \cdot t^{n} $$ Where: - $C(t)$ = cumulative mass loss per unit area at time $t$ (typically g/m²) - $A$ = corrosion constant (depends on environment, e.g., chloride deposition) - $n$ = time exponent, characteristically **0.5 < n < 1.0** for marine atmospheres - $t$ = exposure time in years > **For n = 0.5:** diffusion-controlled protective rust layer (typical tropical marine, **n ≈ 0.5–0.6**). > **For n → 1.0:** aggressive chloride attack, no protective barrier (e.g., splash zone, **n ≈ 0.8–1.0**). ### 2.2.2 Arrhenius Temperature Dependence The rate constant of the dominant electrochemical reaction follows: $$ k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) $$ Where: - $k$ = reaction rate constant - $A$ = pre-exponential factor - $E_a$ = activation energy (≈ 40–80 kJ/mol for marine atmospheric corrosion of steel) - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)** ### 2.2.3 Time of Wetness (TOW) — ISO 9223 $$ \text{TOW} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta t_i \quad \text{where } \text{RH}_i \geq 80\% \text{ and } T_i \geq 0\,°\text{C} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is the TOW threshold defined as 0 °C and 80% RH?"* > **Answer:** Below 0 °C, the electrolyte film freezes (ionic mobility vanishes). Below 80% RH, hygroscopic salt crystals cannot sustain a continuous aqueous film for carbon steel. ### 2.2.4 Salt Spray Deposition Rate (ASTM B117 Standardization) $$ R_{\text{salt}} = \frac{V_{\text{collected}}}{A_{\text{funnel}} \cdot t_{\text{exposure}}} $$ - $R_{\text{salt}}$ in mL/(cm²·hr) - Standard requirement: **1.0–2.0 mL/hr** of 5% NaCl solution at pH 6.5–7.2 ### 2.2.5 Pitting Penetration Rate $$ \text{PPR} = \frac{d_{\text{pit,max}}}{t_{\text{immersion}}} \quad \text{(mm/yr)} $$ The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that pitting penetration in marine splash zones often reaches **5–10× the uniform corrosion rate**. ### 2.2.6 Polymer Photo-oxidation Activation (UV-Driven) For polymeric marine components (FRP boat hulls, polymer coatings): $$ \text{degradation rate} \propto I_{\text{UV}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \cdot [\text{O}_2] $$ Where $I_{\text{UV}}$ is the integrated UV irradiance in W/m² over the damaging wavelength band (280–400 nm). ## 2.3 Master Formula Reference Table | Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Key Parameters | Engineering Application | |------------|-------------------|----------------|-------------------------| | Atmospheric corrosion mass loss | $C(t) = A \cdot t^{n}$ | $A, n$ | Lifetime prediction of marine steel | | Temperature effect | $k = A \cdot \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | $E_a, T$ | Tropical vs. arctic service design | | Time of wetness | $\text{TOW} = \sum \Delta t_i$ | RH, T | Site corrosivity classification (ISO 9223) | | Pitting rate | $\text{PPR} = d_{\text{pit}}/t$ | $d_{\text{pit}}, t$ | Pressure vessel inspection intervals | | UV polymer damage | $r \propto I_{\text{UV}} \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot [\text{O}_2]$ | $I_{\text{UV}}$ | Deck composite UV protection | | Salt spray flux | $R_{\text{salt}} = V/(A \cdot t)$ | $V, A, t$ | Lab test simulation fidelity | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > The $C(t) = A \cdot t^{n}$ model is used by **DNV-RP-B401**, **ISO 9224**, and **NORSOK M-001** to specify corrosion allowances for offshore platforms. For example, an unprotected structural steel in the **splash zone** with $n = 0.85$ will lose mass roughly **6× faster** than the same steel in the submerged zone, justifying the use of **monel sheathing, GRP wraps, or cathodic protection** in that critical band. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From First-Principles Electrochemistry to the Atmospheric Corrosion Law > [!IMPORTANT] > The following derivation connects **Fick's first law of diffusion** to the empirical $C(t) = A \cdot t^{n}$ law, which is a frequent **14-mark** KTU question. ### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Fick's First Law of Diffusion The flux of oxygen through a porous rust layer of thickness $x(t)$ at the metal surface is given by: $$ J_{O_2} = -D_{\text{eff}} \cdot \frac{dC_{O_2}}{dx} $$ Assuming a linear concentration gradient from the bulk air ($C_{O_2}^{\text{air}}$) to the metal surface ($C_{O_2}^{\text{metal}} \approx 0$): $$ J_{O_2} = D_{\text{eff}} \cdot \frac{C_{O_2}^{\text{air}}}{x(t)} $$ Where $D_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective diffusion coefficient of O₂ through the rust layer (m²/s). ### 3.1.2 Oxygen Consumption by Cathodic Reaction By stoichiometry, the cathodic reaction: $$ \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^- $$ consumes **1 mole of O₂ per 4 moles of electrons**, which corresponds to the oxidation of **2 moles of Fe**. The molar flux of iron dissolution is therefore: $$ J_{\text{Fe}} = 2 \cdot J_{O_2} = \frac{2 D_{\text{eff}} \, C_{O_2}^{\text{air}}}{x(t)} $$ ### 3.1.3 Mass Loss as a Function of Time Mass loss per unit area is: $$ C(t) = M_{\text{Fe}} \int_0^t J_{\text{Fe}}(\tau) \, d\tau $$ Where $M_{\text{Fe}} = 55.85\,\text{g/mol}$ is the molar mass of iron. Substituting the expression for $J_{\text{Fe}}$: $$ C(t) = 2 M_{\text{Fe}} D_{\text{eff}} C_{O_2}^{\text{air}} \int_0^t \frac{d\tau}{x(\tau)} $$ ### 3.1.4 Closing the Equation — Rust Layer Growth The rust layer grows as a linear function of mass loss (a constant fraction $\alpha$ converts Fe to Fe-oxide volume): $$ x(t) = \alpha \cdot C(t) $$ This yields an **integral equation**: $$ C(t) = \beta \int_0^t \frac{d\tau}{\alpha \, C(\tau)} = \frac{\beta}{\alpha} \int_0^t \frac{d\tau}{C(\tau)} $$ Where $\beta = 2 M_{\text{Fe}} D_{\text{eff}} C_{O_2}^{\text{air}}$. ### 3.1.5 Solution by Differentiation Differentiating both sides with respect to $t$: $$ \frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\alpha \, C(t)} $$ Separating variables: $$ C \, dC = \frac{\beta}{\alpha} \, dt $$ Integrating from $0$ to $C(t)$: $$ \frac{C^2}{2} = \frac{\beta}{\alpha} t $$ $$ C(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2\beta}{\alpha}} \cdot t^{1/2} $$ ### 3.1.6 The General Power-Law Result Recognizing that the exponent may deviate from 0.5 in aggressive environments (where the rust layer is non-protective), the empirical generalization is: $$ \boxed{\,C(t) = A \cdot t^{n}\,} $$ with the derived special case $n = 0.5$ for **diffusion-controlled protective rust growth**. > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip:** The KTU valuation key expects students to (i) start from Fick's law, (ii) explicitly state the assumption of linear concentration gradient, and (iii) close the problem by recognizing the self-limiting rust growth. Skipping step (iii) costs **2–3 marks** in the 7-mark sub-question. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A structural steel plate (C-Mn) is exposed to a tropical marine atmosphere for **6 years**. Field data yield $A = 0.55\,\text{g/m}^2$ and $n = 0.7$ in $C(t) = A \cdot t^{n}$. Calculate: 1. The total mass loss at $t = 6$ yr. 2. The instantaneous corrosion rate at $t = 6$ yr in g/(m²·yr). 3. The equivalent uniform thickness loss assuming a rust density of **3.7 g/cm³** (γ-FeOOH analogue). ### Step 1 — Total Mass Loss $$ C(6) = 0.55 \times 6^{0.7} $$ First, evaluate the exponent: $6^{0.7} = e^{0.7 \ln 6} = e^{0.7 \times 1.7918} = e^{1.2543} = 3.506$ $$ C(6) = 0.55 \times 3.506 = 1.928\,\text{g/m}^2 $$ ### Step 2 — Instantaneous Corrosion Rate Differentiating $C(t) = A t^{n}$: $$ \frac{dC}{dt} = A \cdot n \cdot t^{n-1} $$ At $t = 6$: $$ \frac{dC}{dt}\bigg\vert_{t=6} = 0.55 \times 0.7 \times 6^{0.7-1} = 0.55 \times 0.7 \times 6^{-0.3} $$ Computing $6^{-0.3} = 1/6^{0.3} = 1/1.771 = 0.5646$: $$ \frac{dC}{dt}\bigg\vert_{t=6} = 0.385 \times 0.5646 = 0.217\,\text{g/(m}^2 \cdot \text{yr)} $$ ### Step 3 — Equivalent Thickness Loss Converting mass loss to thickness using density: $$ d = \frac{C(6)}{\rho} = \frac{1.928\,\text{g/m}^2}{3.7 \times 10^6\,\text{g/m}^3} = 5.21 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 0.521\,\mu\text{m} $$ > **Engineering Comment:** A 0.52 µm/year loss seems small, but recall this is per square meter of **clean** surface. In the splash zone, the effective rate is often **30–50 µm/year** due to high $A$ and $n$ values, justifying corrosion allowance of **3–6 mm** on offshore jacket legs. ## 3.3 KTU 2024 Analytical Matrix: Weathering Mechanism vs. Material Class | Material Class | Dominant Weathering Mechanism | Key Degradation Product | Preventive Strategy | Service Life Impact | |---------------|------------------------------|-------------------------|---------------------|---------------------| | Mild Steel (C-Mn) | Atmospheric corrosion (electrochemical) | γ-FeOOH (lepidocrocite), Fe₃O₄·nH₂O | Coating + cathodic protection | High — annual wastage | | Stainless Steel (316) | Pitting + crevice corrosion | Cr-depleted zones, Cl⁻-induced pits | Mo alloying, surface passivation | Moderate | | Aluminium Alloys (5083) | Pitting + galvanic | Al(OH)₃, Al₂O₃ | Anodizing, chromate-free primers | Moderate–High | | Copper-Nickel (90/10) | Erosion + biofouling | Cu₂O, Cu₂(OH)₃Cl (atacamite) | Chlorination, low Fe content | Excellent (30+ yr) | | Marine Polymer (FRP) | UV + hydrolysis | Chain scission, ester breakdown | UV-stabilizers, gel-coat | Moderate | | Concrete (RCC jetty) | Chloride ingress + carbonation | Friedel's salt, CaCO₃ loss | Epoxy-coated rebar, cathodic prevention | Critical | | Marine Timber | Biological + cyclic moisture | Rot fungi, teredo worm attack | CCA treatment, plastic wrapping | Moderate | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Marine Atmospheric Corrosion Cell > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **wet–dry cycling mechanism** of marine atmospheric corrosion, which is the most-tested concept in KTU ESE Module 3. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Marine Atmosphere] --> B[Chloride Aerosol Deposition] A --> C[Oxygen and Moisture Supply] A --> D[UV Radiation Input] B --> E[Surface Electrolyte Film
thickness 1 to 100 micron] C --> E E --> F{Anodic Site} E --> G{Cathodic Site} F --> H[Fe dissolves to Fe2 plus 2 electrons] G --> I[O2 plus H2O reduced to OH minus] H --> J[Electron flow through metal] J --> G I --> K[Fe OH 2 nucleation] H --> K K --> L[FeOOH rust deposit] L --> M[Re-wetting cycle repeats] D --> N[Polymer Coating] N --> O[Photo oxidation Chain scission] M --> P[Long term mass loss
C of t equals A times t to the n] O --> P P --> Q[Engineering Service Limit] ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block Architecture of a Weathering Test Rig (Salt Spray Cabinet) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Input Boundary Conditions] I1[5 percent NaCl Solution Tank] I2[Compressed Air Supply 0.7 to 1.4 bar] I3[Heated Chamber 35 degrees C] I4[Saturated Air Saturator Tower] end subgraph CORE[Spray Generation and Exposure] C1[Atomizing Nozzle] C2[Salt Fog Cloud] C3[Test Specimen Rack 15 to 30 deg from vertical] C4[Condensation Collectors] end subgraph CONTROL[Instrumentation and Standards] K1[ASTM B117 Timer Controller] K2[pH Meter 6.5 to 7.2] K3[Fog Collection Rate 1 to 2 mL per hr per 80 cm2] K4[Chamber Temperature Logger] end subgraph OUTPUT[Outputs and Evaluation] O1[Mass Loss Measurement] O2[Visual Rating per ASTM D610] O3[Cross Section Microscopy] O4[Corrosion Rate Report g per m2 per yr] end I1 --> C1 I2 --> I4 I4 --> C1 I3 --> C2 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C3 --> C4 K1 --> C1 K2 --> I1 K3 --> C4 K4 --> C3 C3 --> O1 C3 --> O2 C3 --> O3 O1 --> O4 ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — KTU 2024 Weathering Mechanism Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart TD W[Weathering of Marine Materials] --> P[Physical Weathering] W --> C[Chemical Weathering] W --> B[Biological Weathering] P --> P1[Thermal Cycling Fatigue] P --> P2[Wind driven Particle Abrasion] P --> P3[Salt Crystal Growth in Pores] P --> P4[Freeze Thaw Damage in Cold Seas] C --> C1[Atmospheric Electrochemical Corrosion] C --> C2[UV Photo oxidation of Polymers] C --> C3[Hydrolysis of Composites] C --> C4[Acid Rain Sulfur Attack] B --> B1[Barnacle and Algal Fouling] B --> B2[Sulfate Reducing Bacteria H2S] B --> B3[Microbial Induced Corrosion MIC] B --> B4[Biofilm Differential Aeration] P1 --> OUT[Engineering Surface Degradation] P2 --> OUT P3 --> OUT P4 --> OUT C1 --> OUT C2 --> OUT C3 --> OUT C4 --> OUT B1 --> OUT B2 --> OUT B3 --> OUT B4 --> OUT ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2] > Define **weathering** of marine materials. How does it differ fundamentally from **immersion corrosion** in seawater? **Model Answer:** Weathering is the progressive deterioration of a material's properties due to prolonged exposure to atmospheric and environmental conditions, encompassing physical, chemical, and biological mechanisms acting at the material–atmosphere interface. **Key distinctions from immersion corrosion:** | Aspect | Weathering | Immersion Corrosion | |--------|------------|---------------------| | Electrolyte | Thin, intermittent film | Continuous bulk seawater | | Oxygen access | Unlimited (atmospheric) | Diffusion-limited | | Time dependence | Cyclic (wet–dry) | Continuous | | Mechanism mix | Physical + chemical + biological | Predominantly electrochemical | | Locality | Splash and atmospheric zones | Submerged zone | (3 marks — definition 1, comparison table 2) ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample] > What is **Time of Wetness (TOW)** as per ISO 9223? Why is the threshold defined as **T ≥ 0 °C and RH ≥ 80%**? **Model Answer:** TOW is the cumulative duration during which the metal surface is covered by an adsorbed or liquid water film sufficient to sustain atmospheric corrosion, defined by ISO 9223 as the time for which the surface temperature is **≥ 0 °C** and the **relative humidity ≥ 80%**. **Rationale for thresholds:** - **T ≥ 0 °C:** Below freezing, the adsorbed water film solidifies and ionic transport halts. - **RH ≥ 80%:** For carbon steel and zinc, this is the critical humidity above which a continuous aqueous film forms over hygroscopic corrosion products. (3 marks — definition 1, threshold justification 2) ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] #### (a) [7 Marks] Explain with a neat sketch the **four-stage cyclic mechanism of marine atmospheric corrosion** on mild steel. Identify the dominant electrochemical reactions in each stage. **Model Solution:** The four-stage cyclic model of marine atmospheric corrosion on mild steel proceeds as follows: **Stage 1 — Wetting (Electrolyte Formation):** Salt-laden marine air deposits hygroscopic chloride particles (NaCl, MgCl₂) on the surface. When relative humidity exceeds the **critical value (≈ 60% for steel, 80% per ISO 9223)**, these deliquesce to form a thin, continuous electrolyte film (1–100 µm). Atmospheric oxygen dissolves into the film. The corrosion cell is now established. **Stage 2 — Active Electrochemical Reaction:** - Anode: $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$ - Cathode: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ - Overall: $2\text{Fe} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe(OH)}_2$ The ferrous hydroxide oxidizes further to **lepidocrocite (γ-FeOOH)** and ultimately **goethite (α-FeOOH)**. **Stage 3 — Drying (Pitting Nucleation):** Evaporation concentrates chlorides in pores and beneath rust deposits. The oxygen concentration differential creates **differential aeration cells**, with the area beneath the rust deposit becoming anodic. **Pitting corrosion** is initiated. **Stage 4 — Re-wetting (Cumulative Damage):** A new wet event (rain, splash, dew) re-wets the surface, restarting the cycle. Each cycle deposits more corrosion product, and damage grows non-linearly according to $C(t) = A \cdot t^{n}$. **Sketch:** (Refer to Diagram 1 in Section 4.1 of these notes) (Mark distribution: 4 stages × 1 mark = 4, electrochemical reactions 2, sketch 1) #### (b) [7 Marks] Derive the atmospheric corrosion power law $C(t) = A \cdot t^{n}$ from Fick's law of diffusion. Under what marine conditions does the exponent **n** exceed 0.5, and what is its engineering significance? **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** Starting from Fick's first law of oxygen diffusion through the rust layer: $$ J_{O_2} = D_{\text{eff}} \frac{C_{O_2}^{\text{air}}}{x(t)} $$ Coupled with the cathodic stoichiometry (1 mol O₂ dissolves 2 mol Fe), we get: $$ \frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\alpha \, C(t)} $$ where $\beta$ and $\alpha$ are constants. Integrating: $$ C(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2\beta}{\alpha}} \cdot t^{1/2} $$ The generalized empirical form follows as: $$ \boxed{\,C(t) = A \cdot t^{n}\,} $$ **When does n > 0.5?** The exponent $n$ exceeds 0.5 in: - **Aggressive chloride splash zones** ($n \approx 0.7$–$0.9$) - **Tropical marine atmospheres** with frequent condensation - **Sulphur-polluted harbors** where the rust layer is non-protective This occurs because the rust layer fails to act as a diffusion barrier — it spalls, cracks, and permits continued oxygen access. **Engineering significance:** - $n = 0.5$ allows extrapolation from short-term data; **$n > 0.5$ predicts progressively accelerating damage.** - Corrosion allowances on offshore structures (per **DNV-RP-B401**) are calculated using site-specific $A$ and $n$ values from **ISO 9224** charts. - For $n \approx 0.85$, the **splash-zone allowance is typically 6–10 mm**, whereas for $n = 0.5$ it is only 2–3 mm. (Mark distribution: Derivation 4 marks, conditions for n > 0.5: 1.5 marks, engineering significance 1.5 marks) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] #### (a) [7 Marks] Compare and contrast **physical, chemical, and biological weathering** mechanisms in marine environments. Give two specific examples of damage for each category as observed on ship hulls and offshore structures. **Model Solution:** **Physical Weathering:** Mechanical breakdown of material without chemical change. - **Example 1:** Salt-crystal growth in concrete pores of a jetty pile → spalling of cover concrete, exposing rebar. - **Example 2:** Thermal cycling fatigue of an aluminum deck plate (–5 °C to +45 °C diurnal) → micro-cracking of paint system. **Chemical Weathering:** Alteration of material by chemical reaction with environmental agents. - **Example 1:** Atmospheric corrosion of unprotected mild-steel hatch coaming → uniform wastage, pitting under rust scale. - **Example 2:** UV photo-oxidation of FRP superstructure → chalking, gloss loss, and 50% reduction in tensile strength after 5 yr tropical exposure. **Biological Weathering:** Damage caused directly or indirectly by marine organisms. - **Example 1:** Barnacle colonization of a copper-nickel pipe → localized differential aeration and under-deposit pitting. - **Example 2:** Sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) in stagnant ballast tank bilge → microbiologically influenced corrosion (MIC) with deep tubercles. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Physical | Chemical | Biological | |---------|----------|----------|------------| | Driving force | Mechanical/thermal | Electrochemical/radiative | Metabolic | | Speed | Medium | Fast in chlorides | Slow but deep | | Prevention | Drainage design | Coatings, CP | Biocides, chlorination | | Damage type | Cracking, spalling | Mass loss, pitting | Tubercles, deep pits | (7 marks — 1.5 per mechanism + 0.5 for examples each) #### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the **ASTM B117 salt spray test** in detail. What does it evaluate, what are its limitations in simulating real marine atmospheric corrosion, and how does **ISO 9223** address those limitations? **Model Solution:** **ASTM B117 Description:** The ASTM B117 standard specifies a **continuous salt spray (fog) test** in which a 5% NaCl solution (pH 6.5–7.2) is atomized into a sealed chamber maintained at **35 ± 2 °C**. The fog is collected at a rate of **1–2 mL/hr per 80 cm²**. Specimens are mounted at **15–30° from vertical**, and exposure duration ranges from 24 hr to 3000 hr. **What it evaluates:** - Relative ranking of coatings, platings, and alloy compositions - Defect density in protective systems - Comparative performance under accelerated chloride attack **Limitations:** 1. **Continuous wet exposure** — eliminates the critical wet–dry cycle of real marine atmosphere. 2. **No UV radiation** — cannot predict polymer photo-degradation. 3. **Constant temperature** — does not model diurnal cycling. 4. **No biological component** — misses MIC and fouling. 5. **Excessively aggressive** — pure NaCl, no Mg/Ca sulfates; produces different corrosion products than field exposure. **How ISO 9223 addresses these limitations:** ISO 9223 introduces the **corrosivity classification system (C1 to CX)** using: - **Time of Wetness (TOW)** as the principal environmental driver - **Sulphur dioxide and chloride deposition** as supplementary pollutants - **Annual field exposure data** for empirical calibration The companion standard **ISO 9224** provides guidance on extrapolation from short-term data using $C(t) = A \cdot t^{n}$. Together, ISO 9223/9224 deliver a **dose–response framework** that accounts for the cyclic, multi-mechanism nature of real marine weathering, which the B117 test cannot. (Mark distribution: B117 description 2.5, evaluation 1, limitations 2, ISO 9223 advantage 1.5) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Conflating weathering with corrosion:** Students write "weathering is rusting" — instant loss of 2 marks. Weathering is the **umbrella**; corrosion is one sub-process. > 2. **Skipping the wet–dry cycle:** Any 14-mark answer on atmospheric corrosion must explicitly describe **Stages I–IV**. A bare electrochemical equation is worth at most 2–3 marks. > 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Mass loss must be in **g/m²**, rate in **mm/yr or µm/yr**. Unit errors cost 1 mark even when the number is correct. > 4. **Using B117 as a real-environment predictor:** B117 is a **ranking test only**. Stating it predicts field life is a common error that loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Wrong critical humidity:** Using 100% RH instead of 80% for TOW calculation — common mistake, 0.5 mark deduction. > 6. **No mention of TOW in B-part answers on atmospheric corrosion:** Examiners explicitly check for TOW as a **mandatory parameter** in any atmospheric corrosion discussion. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 3 — Weathering: High-Density Revision Summary** - ✅ **Definition:** Weathering = physical + chemical + biological surface degradation due to environment; broader than corrosion. - ✅ **Four-stage cycle:** Wetting → Active Reaction → Drying → Re-wetting (cyclic, not linear). - ✅ **Power law:** $C(t) = A \cdot t^{n}$ with $0.5 \leq n \leq 1.0$. - ✅ **n = 0.5:** diffusion-controlled protective rust; **n > 0.5:** aggressive splash/tropical. - ✅ **Arrhenius:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ for temperature dependence. - ✅ **TOW threshold:** T ≥ 0 °C AND RH ≥ 80% (ISO 9223). - ✅ **Critical RH for carbon steel:** ≈ 60%; for zinc ≈ 70%. - ✅ **B117 test conditions:** 5% NaCl, 35 °C, fog rate 1–2 mL/hr/80 cm², **ranking only**, not predictive of field life. - ✅ **ISO 9223 corrosivity categories:** C1 (very low) → CX (extreme) based on TOW, chloride, SO₂. - ✅ **Pitting penetration rate** in splash zone ≈ 5–10× uniform corrosion rate. - ✅ **UV damage band:** 280–400 nm (UV-A + UV-B); polymer chain scission. - ✅ **Key corrosion products on steel:** γ-FeOOH (lepidocrocite), α-FeOOH (goethite), Fe₃O₄·nH₂O. - ✅ **Biological agents:** SRB (H₂S, MIC), barnacles (differential aeration), algae (acidic metabolites). - ✅ **Protective strategies:** Coatings (barrier), cathodic protection (electrochemical), design (drainage, drainage holes), alloy selection (Cu-Ni, monel), UV stabilizers. - ✅ **Differentiate clearly:** Weathering ≠ Corrosion ≠ Erosion ≠ Fouling. Each is a distinct mechanism. - ✅ **Standards to memorize:** ISO 9223, ISO 9224, ASTM B117, ASTM D610, DNV-RP-B401, NORSOK M-001. - ✅ **Numerical fluency:** Be able to compute $C(t)$, $dC/dt$, and thickness loss from $A$ and $n$.

topics.title: Mechanical Surface Cleaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Surface Cleaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Mechanical Surface Cleaning** is defined as the physical removal of undesirable surface contaminants — including loose rust, loose mill scale, loose paint, weld spatter, marine fouling, salts, and other foreign matter — from a metallic substrate using non-chemical, energy-based mechanical actions (impact, abrasion, friction, or cutting), in order to achieve a defined cleanliness grade and surface profile suitable for the application of protective coatings, linings, or marine-grade paint systems.

In the marine context, surface preparation is governed by internationally recognised standards, primarily:

| Standard Body | Reference Code | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| SSPC / NACE | SSPC-SP2 to SSPC-SP10 | Visual cleanliness grades |
| ISO | ISO 8501-1 (Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3) | Rust grades and preparation grades |
| SSPC | SSPC-VIS 1 | Visual reference photographs |
| NACE | TM-01-70 / TM-03-70 | Visual comparators |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dirty Saucepan" Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine you are about to re-paint an old, rusted iron gate at your home. Before applying fresh paint, you **cannot** paint over rust and flaking paint — the new paint will simply peel off within weeks. What do you do? You grab a **wire brush, sandpaper, or an angle grinder**, and physically **scrape, rub, and grind away** the rust and old paint until shiny metal is exposed. You then slightly roughen the surface (called "**anchor profile**" or "**tooth**") so the new paint has something to grip onto.

**This is precisely what mechanical surface cleaning does in marine engineering** — it is the physical "surgery" performed on a ship's hull, deck, ballast tank, or offshore platform steel before anti-corrosive coating systems (e.g., epoxy, polyurethane, zinc-rich primers) are applied.

## 1.3 Why Mechanical Cleaning is Critical in Marine Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why This Topic is a KTU High-Yield Module**
> * Approximately **60–80% of all marine coating failures** are attributed to **inadequate surface preparation**, not to the coating material itself.
> * The **adhesion strength** of a marine coating is directly proportional to the **surface cleanliness** and **surface roughness (anchor profile)**.
> * In offshore and shipyard practice, surface preparation costs typically consume **30–50% of the total coating application budget**.

## 1.4 Classification Overview

Mechanical surface cleaning is broadly classified into **three hierarchical categories**, each delivering a progressively higher level of cleanliness:

1. **Hand Tool Cleaning** (lowest grade — SSPC-SP2 / ISO St 2)
2. **Power Tool Cleaning** (intermediate grade — SSPC-SP3 / ISO St 3)
3. **Abrasive Blast Cleaning** (highest grade — SSPC-SP5/SP6/SP10 / ISO Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3)

These will be expanded in detail in subsequent sections.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Mechanical Cleaning

The fundamental physical principle behind mechanical surface cleaning can be decomposed into four sequential energy-transfer events:

* **Energy Input Stage**: Compressed air, electrical energy, or human kinetic energy is delivered to a tool or abrasive media.
* **Impact / Abrasion Stage**: The energy is transferred to the substrate through impact (blasting), friction (brushing), or cutting (grinding).
* **Contaminant Detachment Stage**: The bond between the contaminant (rust, scale, coating) and the parent metal is mechanically broken. Forces involved exceed the **adhesive strength** of the contaminant film but remain **below the cohesive strength** of the base metal.
* **Surface Re-profiling Stage**: The cleaned surface is deliberately roughened to a controlled micro-geometry (anchor pattern) that maximises **mechanical interlocking** with the subsequent coating.

> [!NOTE] **The "Why" Behind Each Step**
> * **Why energy input?** — Mechanical adhesion requires kinetic energy; static contact alone cannot dislodge tenacious mill scale.
> * **Why impact/abrasion?** — It is the only non-chemical method capable of removing **tightly adherent mill scale** (the bluish-grey oxide layer formed during hot rolling of steel).
> * **Why detach contaminants?** — Any residual rust or scale acts as a **weak boundary layer**, causing cathodic disbondment of the coating.
> * **Why re-profile?** — A smooth, mirror-finish steel provides almost **zero mechanical key** for paint; an anchor profile of **50–100 µm** is industry standard for marine epoxies.

## 2.2 Detailed Method Analysis

### 2.2.1 Hand Tool Cleaning (SSPC-SP2 / ISO 8501-1 St 2)

**Tools Used:** Hand wire brushes, scrapers, chisels, chipping hammers, hand sandpaper, hand sanding blocks.

**Operational Principle:** Manual application of frictional and impact force.

**Cleaning Grade Achieved:** Removal of **loose rust**, **loose mill scale**, and **loose paint only**. Tightly adherent contaminants remain.

**Typical Productivity:** **1 to 5 m²/hour** per operator.

**Marine Application:** Used for **touch-up work**, small-area repairs, internal corners of ballast tanks where blasting cannot reach, and maintenance spot-preparation.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Common Mistake**
> Hand tool cleaning does **NOT** produce a commercially acceptable substrate for full marine coating systems. It is **only suitable for minor repairs** or as a temporary measure.

### 2.2.2 Power Tool Cleaning (SSPC-SP3 / ISO 8501-1 St 3)

**Tools Used:** Power wire brushes (rotary cup), pneumatic needle scalers, rotary flap discs, angle grinders with abrasive discs, rotary impact tools, rotary peeners.

**Operational Principle:** Rotational, reciprocating, or percussive mechanical action powered by electricity or compressed air.

**Cleaning Grade Achieved:** Removal of **loose and some tightly adherent rust/scale**, comparable to SSPC-SP2 but faster and with slightly improved profile.

**Typical Productivity:** **5 to 15 m²/hour**.

**Marine Application:** Repair yards, propeller shaft areas, anchor chain lockers, localised hull repairs where blasting is not permitted (e.g., near fuel tanks or hot work-restricted zones).

### 2.2.3 Abrasive Blast Cleaning — The Industry Benchmark

This is the **most important and exam-relevant** mechanical cleaning method in the KTU syllabus.

**Principle:** Abrasive media (sand, grit, shot) is propelled at high velocity against the steel surface using **compressed air**, **centrifugal force** (wheel blasting), or **water** (wet blasting). The abrasive particles act as millions of microscopic chisels, removing contaminants and creating a uniform anchor profile simultaneously.

**Resulting Grades (Ascending Quality):**

| SSPC Standard | NACE Standard | ISO 8501-1 | Description | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SSPC-SP7 | NACE 4 | Sa 1 | Brush-off Blast | Light-duty atmospheric |
| SSPC-SP6 | NACE 3 | Sa 2 | Commercial Blast | Standard marine hull |
| **SSPC-SP10** | **NACE 2** | **Sa 2½** | **Near-White Metal Blast** | **Most common marine grade** |
| SSPC-SP5 | NACE 1 | Sa 3 | White Metal Blast | Ballast tanks, critical service |
| SSPC-SP14 | NACE 8 | — | Commercial Brush-Off | Limited applications |

## 2.3 Abrasive Media — The Working "Tool" of Blast Cleaning

> [!IMPORTANT] **Hardness vs. Shape vs. Density — The Three Pillars of Abrasive Selection**
> The choice of abrasive media determines **productivity**, **profile depth**, **dust generation**, **recyclability**, and **cost**.

| Abrasive Type | Mohs Hardness | Shape | Recyclability | Typical Profile | Marine Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Silica sand | 7 | Angular | Single-use | 50–100 µm | ⚠️ Restricted (silicosis hazard) |
| Steel grit | 8–9 | Angular | 100–200 cycles | 75–125 µm | ✅ Excellent (shipyards) |
| Steel shot | 8–9 | Round | 100–200 cycles | 50–100 µm | ✅ Peening applications |
| Aluminum oxide | 9 | Angular | 10–30 cycles | 75–150 µm | ✅ Stainless steel prep |
| Garnet | 7.5–8.5 | Sub-angular | 1–5 cycles | 50–100 µm | ✅ Preferred for waterjet |
| Glass beads | 5–6 | Spherical | 10–30 cycles | 25–75 µm | Cleaning, not profiling |
| Plastic media | 3–4 | Angular | 10–20 cycles | 25–50 µm | Coating stripping (aviation) |
| Crushed slag (copper/coal) | 6–7 | Angular | Single-use | 50–100 µm | ✅ Cost-effective marine |
| Sodium bicarbonate | 2.5 | Angular | Single-use | 5–15 µm | Sensitive substrates |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Equations for KTU Numerical Problems**

### 2.4.1 Nozzle Pressure Equation

The **actual pressure at the abrasive blasting nozzle** is always less than the pressure at the air compressor due to frictional losses in the hose. This is a frequently tested equation:

$$
P_{nozzle} \;=\; P_{compressor} \;-\; (L \cdot R_{loss})
$$

where:
* $P_{nozzle}$ = effective pressure at the nozzle exit (in **psi** or **bar**)
* $P_{compressor}$ = discharge pressure at the compressor (typically **100 psi / 7 bar**)
* $L$ = length of the blast hose (in feet or metres)
* $R_{loss}$ = pressure drop per unit length (typically **0.5 to 1.5 psi per foot**)

### 2.4.2 Compressed Air Consumption

$$
Q \;=\; k \cdot d^{2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{gauge}}{P_{atm}}}
$$

where:
* $Q$ = volumetric air consumption (in **CFM** or **m³/min**)
* $d$ = nozzle orifice diameter (in inches or mm)
* $P_{gauge}$ = gauge pressure at the nozzle
* $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure (~14.7 psi)
* $k$ = empirical constant depending on nozzle type

### 2.4.3 Productivity Estimation

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{W_{abrasive} \cdot E_{removal}}{R_{required}}
$$

where:
* $A$ = area cleaned (m²)
* $W_{abrasive}$ = total abrasive consumed (kg)
* $E_{removal}$ = abrasive removal efficiency (m²/kg)
* $R_{required}$ = required cleanliness grade factor (1.0 for SP6, 1.4 for SP10, 1.8 for SP5)

### 2.4.4 Anchor Pattern / Profile Depth

$$
R_{a} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_{i} - \bar{y} \vert
$$

where $R_{a}$ is the **arithmetic mean roughness** (µm), measured using a **profile comparator** (e.g., ISO 8503-1 / Keane-Tator comparator) or a **stylus profilometer**.

### 2.4.5 Centrifugal (Wheel) Blast Cleaning Equation

$$
v_{abrasive} \;=\; \omega \cdot r
$$

where:
* $v_{abrasive}$ = tangential velocity of abrasive thrown by the wheel (m/s)
* $\omega$ = angular velocity of the blast wheel (rad/s)
* $r$ = radius of the wheel blade tip (m)

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> These equations are used daily by **marine coating inspectors (NACE Level 2/3)**, **shipyard blasting foremen**, and **offshore project engineers** to estimate compressor sizing, abrasive consumption, blasting time, and to verify that specifications (e.g., **PSPC ballast tank coating requirements under IMO Resolution MSC.215(82)**) are met.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nozzle Pressure Drop in a Blast Hose

**Problem Context (KTU Numerical Style):**
A marine shipyard uses a compressed air system where the compressor delivers air at a gauge pressure of **120 psi**. The blast hose connecting the compressor to the nozzle is **50 feet long**. Empirical data for the standard 1-inch blast hose gives a pressure loss of **0.8 psi per foot**. Determine the effective nozzle pressure available for abrasive propulsion.

### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation

The pressure drop along a blast hose is treated as approximately **linear** (laminar-dominated flow assumption), so:

$$
\Delta P \;=\; L \cdot R_{loss}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Given Numerical Values

$$
\Delta P \;=\; (50 \text{ ft}) \cdot (0.8 \text{ psi/ft})
$$

$$
\Delta P \;=\; 40 \text{ psi}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Nozzle Pressure Equation

$$
P_{nozzle} \;=\; P_{compressor} \;-\; \Delta P
$$

$$
P_{nozzle} \;=\; 120 \text{ psi} \;-\; 40 \text{ psi}
$$

$$
P_{nozzle} \;=\; 80 \text{ psi}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!NOTE] **Result & Significance**
> The nozzle is operating at only **80 psi**, which is the **lower threshold** for efficient abrasive blasting. Industry standard for SSPC-SP10 (near-white metal) requires **90–110 psi at the nozzle**. Therefore, this shipyard setup is **marginally acceptable** — the engineer must either:
> * Reduce the blast hose length below **37.5 feet** to retain ≥90 psi, or
> * Increase the compressor discharge pressure to **≥140 psi**, or
> * Use a larger-diameter hose to reduce frictional losses.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Air Consumption of a Venturi Nozzle

**Problem Context:**
A shipyard blast operator uses a **3/8-inch (9.5 mm) Venturi nozzle** at a nozzle pressure of **100 psi**. The empirical constant $k$ for Venturi nozzles is **150 CFM per square inch per square root of pressure ratio**. Calculate the required air supply in **CFM** and convert to **m³/min**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Effective Nozzle Diameter Squared

$$
d^{2} \;=\; (0.375 \text{ in})^{2}
$$

$$
d^{2} \;=\; 0.140625 \text{ in}^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Pressure Ratio Term

$$
\dfrac{P_{gauge}}{P_{atm}} \;=\; \dfrac{100 \text{ psi}}{14.7 \text{ psi}}
$$

$$
\dfrac{P_{gauge}}{P_{atm}} \;=\; 6.803
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Square Root

$$
\sqrt{6.803} \;=\; 2.608
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the Air Consumption Equation

$$
Q \;=\; k \cdot d^{2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{gauge}}{P_{atm}}}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; (150) \cdot (0.140625) \cdot (2.608)
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 55.01 \text{ CFM}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert CFM to m³/min

Using the conversion factor **1 CFM = 0.02832 m³/min**:

$$
Q_{metric} \;=\; 55.01 \cdot 0.02832
$$

$$
Q_{metric} \;=\; 1.558 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{min}
$$

### Step 6 — Practical Implication

> [!IMPORTANT] **Compressor Sizing Rule (Shipyard Practice)**
> Rule of thumb: select a compressor with a capacity of **at least 1.5 × nozzle consumption** to account for leakage, abrasive feed air, and pressure regulation losses.
> Required compressor capacity $= 1.5 \times 1.558 = \mathbf{2.34 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{min}}$. A standard **2.5 m³/min portable diesel compressor** would be the minimum selection.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hand vs. Power vs. Blast

| Parameter | Hand Tool (SP2) | Power Tool (SP3) | Abrasive Blast (SP6/SP10) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Productivity** | 1–5 m²/h | 5–15 m²/h | 30–100 m²/h |
| **Cost per m²** | High (labour) | Moderate | Lowest at scale |
| **Initial Profile** | Near zero | 10–25 µm | 50–125 µm |
| **Dust Generated** | Low | Moderate | Very high |
| **Skill Required** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Marine Suitability** | Touch-up only | Local repair | Full hull / ballast tank |
| **Equipment Cost** | Very low | Moderate | Very high |
| **Mobility** | Excellent | Good | Limited |
| **PSPC Compliance (IMO)** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Required grade |

---

## 3.4 Multi-Variable Decision Flow (Analytical Derivation)

Given a marine surface preparation specification, the engineer must select equipment based on **four interlinked variables**: *Substrate Condition → Grade Required → Productivity Required → Constraints (dust/hot work/access)*.

$$
\text{Method Selection} \;=\; f(S, G, P, C)
$$

where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \text{Initial substrate condition (rust grade A–D per ISO 8501-1)} \\
G &= \text{Target cleanliness grade (Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3)} \\
P &= \text{Required production rate (m}^{2}/\text{day)} \\
C &= \text{Site constraints (dust, hot-work permit, confined space)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision Heuristic:**

* If $G \geq \text{Sa 2½}$ (PSPC requirement) → **Abrasive blast mandatory**.
* If $G = \text{Sa 2}$ and $P > 50 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{day}$ → **Abrasive blast preferred**.
* If $G = \text{St 3}$ and $P < 20 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{day}$ → **Power tool cleaning acceptable**.
* If $C$ includes "hot work prohibited" → **Non-sparking abrasive (garnet, glass beads)** or **wet blast**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
