# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Mechanical Surface Cleaning. List any three methods. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Mechanical surface cleaning is the physical removal of rust, mill scale, old coatings, weld spatter, salts, and other foreign contaminants from a metallic substrate using non-chemical mechanical actions such as impact, abrasion, friction, or cutting, in order to achieve a defined cleanliness grade and surface profile suitable for the application of protective coatings.

**Three Methods (1 mark):**
1. Hand Tool Cleaning (SSPC-SP2)
2. Power Tool Cleaning (SSPC-SP3)
3. Abrasive Blast Cleaning (SSPC-SP5/SP6/SP10)

---

### Question 2: What is an Anchor Profile? State its importance in marine coating. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An anchor profile (also called surface profile or tooth) is the **micro-roughness** created on a steel surface by mechanical cleaning, measured as the **peak-to-valley height** in micrometres (µm), typically ranging from **50 to 100 µm** for marine epoxy systems.

**Importance (1.5 marks):**
1. It provides **mechanical interlocking** between the coating and the substrate, dramatically increasing **adhesion strength**.
2. It increases the **effective surface area** available for coating-substrate bonding.
3. It helps to **absorb thermal and mechanical stresses** at the coating-substrate interface, preventing premature delamination in marine service.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q. A(a)** Describe in detail the **Abrasive Blast Cleaning** method for marine steel surface preparation. Include its principle, types, abrasive media, and the cleanliness grades achieved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(1) Principle (2 marks):** Abrasive blast cleaning operates on the principle of **high-velocity impact**. Abrasive particles (sand, grit, shot) are propelled at velocities of **60–120 m/s** against the steel surface using **compressed air** (typically at **100 psi / 7 bar**) through a Venturi nozzle. Each particle acts as a microscopic chisel, simultaneously:
* **Detaching** rust, mill scale, and old coatings through impact force
* **Re-profiling** the substrate to a controlled anchor pattern
* **Cleaning** embedded contaminants from the surface micro-cavities

**(2) Types of Abrasive Blasting (2 marks):**
1. **Dry Compressed Air Blasting** — Most common; uses Venturi or straight-bore nozzles.
2. **Vacuum Blasting** — Closed-loop; recovers abrasive instantly; used in occupied spaces.
3. **Wet Abrasive Blasting (Slurry Blasting)** — Water + abrasive; suppresses dust.
4. **Centrifugal Wheel Blasting** — Abrasive thrown by rotating wheel; used in fabrication shops.

**(3) Abrasive Media (1.5 marks):**
* **Steel grit** (angular, recyclable 100–200 times) — most common in shipyards
* **Steel shot** (round, peening action)
* **Garnet** (sub-angular, low dust, premium finish)
* **Aluminum oxide** (very hard, for stainless steel)
* **Crushed slag** (cost-effective, single-use)
* **Glass beads** (delicate cleaning, no aggressive profiling)

**(4) Cleanliness Grades (1.5 marks):**
| Grade | Appearance | Marine Application |
|---|---|---|
| SSPC-SP6 / Sa 2 | ≥95% free of visible residue | Hull exterior, atmospheric |
| **SSPC-SP10 / Sa 2½** | **≥95% free, slight shadows** | **Most marine uses** |
| SSPC-SP5 / Sa 3 | 100% uniform metallic | Ballast tanks (PSPC) |

---

**Q. A(b)** A shipyard compressor delivers air at **110 psi** to a blast hose of length **60 ft**. The hose pressure loss is **0.9 psi/ft**. The nozzle diameter is **3/8 inch** and the empirical constant k = 150 CFM/(in²·√ratio).
* (i) Calculate the effective nozzle pressure.
* (ii) Determine the compressed air consumption in CFM and m³/min.
* (iii) Comment on whether this setup meets the SSPC-SP10 requirement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Nozzle Pressure (2 marks):**
$$
\Delta P = L \cdot R_{loss} = (60)(0.9) = 54 \text{ psi}
$$
$$
P_{nozzle} = 110 - 54 = 56 \text{ psi}
$$

**(ii) Air Consumption (3 marks):**
$$
d^{2} = (0.375)^{2} = 0.1406 \text{ in}^{2}
$$
$$
\dfrac{P_{gauge}}{P_{atm}} = \dfrac{56}{14.7} = 3.81
$$
$$
\sqrt{3.81} = 1.952
$$
$$
Q = (150)(0.1406)(1.952) = 41.18 \text{ CFM}
$$
$$
Q_{metric} = 41.18 \times 0.02832 = 1.166 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{min}
$$

**(iii) Comment (2 marks):**

> [!WARNING] **Engineering Verdict: NON-COMPLIANT**
> The calculated nozzle pressure of **56 psi** is **well below the SSPC-SP10 minimum requirement of 90–110 psi**. The resulting abrasive velocity will be insufficient to remove tightly adherent mill scale and to produce a proper anchor profile. **Recommendations:**
> 1. Reduce hose length to ≤ **22 ft** to retain ≥90 psi, or
> 2. Increase compressor discharge to **≥ 165 psi**, or
> 3. Use a larger 1.25-inch diameter hose to reduce frictional loss per foot.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. B(a)** Compare **Hand Tool Cleaning, Power Tool Cleaning, and Abrasive Blast Cleaning** with respect to principle, productivity, surface profile achieved, applicable SSPC grade, typical marine applications, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hand Tool (SP2) | Power Tool (SP3) | Abrasive Blast (SP6/SP10) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Manual friction + impact | Motorised friction/impact | High-velocity abrasive impact |
| **Productivity** | 1–5 m²/h | 5–15 m²/h | 30–100 m²/h |
| **Profile** | Near zero | 10–25 µm | 50–125 µm |
| **SSPC Grade** | SP2 | SP3 | SP6, SP10, SP5 |
| **Marine Use** | Touch-up, spot repair | Localised repair, prop areas | Full hull, ballast tanks, offshore |
| **Limitations** | Labour-intensive, low grade | Cannot achieve Sa 2½ | High dust, equipment cost |
| **PSPC Compliant?** | No | No | Yes |

**Key Conclusion (1 mark):** For full-scale marine coating work compliant with **IMO PSPC standards**, only **abrasive blast cleaning** is industrially acceptable.

---

**Q. B(b)** Explain the role of **anchor profile** in marine coating adhesion. Discuss the methods of **measurement** and the **recommended profile depths** for typical marine coating systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(1) Role of Anchor Profile (3 marks):**

The anchor profile is critical for three reasons:
* **Mechanical Interlock** — The peaks and valleys physically lock the coating to the substrate, preventing shear delamination under wave impact and mechanical stress.
* **Increased Surface Area** — A roughened surface may have **1.5 to 2.5 times** the actual area of a smooth surface, increasing the **van der Waals and chemical bonding** sites.
* **Stress Accommodation** — Micro-roughness absorbs differential **thermal expansion** (steel vs. coating) and **hull flexure stresses** in seaway conditions.

**(2) Measurement Methods (2.5 marks):**
1. **Visual Comparators** — ISO 8503-1 segment comparators (G type for grit, S type for shot) — quick field method.
2. **Replicate Tape (Testex Press-O-Film)** — Pressed onto surface; thickness measured with micrometer; ±5 µm accuracy.
3. **Stylus Profilometer** — Most accurate (±1 µm); laboratory method.
4. **Depth Micrometer** — Direct measurement from reference plate to peaks.

**(3) Recommended Profile Depths (1.5 marks):**
| Coating System | Recommended Profile |
|---|---|
| Zinc-rich epoxy primer | 50–75 µm |
| Marine epoxy (general) | 75–100 µm |
| Polyurethane topcoat (over epoxy) | 50–75 µm |
| Thick-film coal tar epoxy (ballast) | 100–125 µm |
| Glass-flake epoxy (splash zone) | 100–150 µm |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing SSPC and ISO grades** — They are NOT identical. SSPC-SP10 ≈ ISO Sa 2½, but they differ in wording and visual criteria. Always cite both.
> 2. **Stating "anchor profile is rough surface"** without quantifying — You MUST give a **numerical range** (e.g., 50–100 µm).
> 3. **Ignoring PSPC compliance** — In any modern KTU question on marine surface preparation, mention **IMO Resolution MSC.215(82)** and **PSPC** for ballast tanks.
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract atmospheric pressure** — In the air consumption derivation, students often forget the $P_{gauge}/P_{atm}$ ratio term.
> 5. **Treating blast pressure as constant** — Always show the **pressure drop calculation** along the hose.
> 6. **Wrong unit conversions** — 1 CFM = 0.02832 m³/min; students frequently use 0.0283 or 0.028, leading to non-integer marks.
> 7. **Sketching diagrams without labels** — In the 14-mark question, a properly labelled blast schematic can fetch 1–2 extra marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mechanical Surface Cleaning**

**Core Concepts:**
* Mechanical cleaning = **physical removal** of contaminants (no chemical reaction).
* Three primary categories: **Hand Tool (SP2) → Power Tool (SP3) → Abrasive Blast (SP5/SP6/SP10)**.
* Industry benchmark = **SSPC-SP10 (Near-White Metal)** = **ISO Sa 2½** = **NACE 2**.
* IMO **PSPC** for ballast tanks mandates **Sa 2½** minimum, with **profile 50–100 µm** and **dust ≤ ISO 8502-3 Class 1**.

**Key Standards to Memorise:**
* **SSPC-SP2, SP3, SP5, SP6, SP7, SP10, SP14**
* **ISO Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3**
* **ISO 8501-1** (visual cleanliness)
* **ISO 8503-1** (profile comparators)
* **ISO 8502-3** (dust assessment)

**Critical Equations:**
* $P_{nozzle} = P_{compressor} - (L \cdot R_{loss})$
* $Q = k \cdot d^{2} \cdot \sqrt{P_{gauge}/P_{atm}}$
* Productivity rule: **1.5 × nozzle CFM = compressor sizing**.

**Abrasive Memory Hooks:**
* **Steel Grit** → Angular → Profile → Most used in shipyards.
* **Steel Shot** → Round → Peening → Used on wheel machines.
* **Garnet** → Premium, low dust → Used in waterjet.
* **Glass Beads** → Cleaning only, no profiling → Cosmetic work.
* **Plastic Media** → Soft → Coating removal on thin steel.

**Anchor Profile Numbers:**
* General marine epoxy: **75–100 µm**
* Zinc primer: **50–75 µm**
* Thick-film ballast tank: **100–125 µm**

**Safety & Environmental Notes (always mention for full marks):**
* Silica sand blasting **banned/restricted** in most countries due to **silicosis risk**.
* PPE required: **abrasive blast hood (Grade CE-EN 14594)**, leather suit, hearing protection.
* Dust collection and **emission control** mandatory under shipyard HSE rules.

**One-Line Definition to Memorise:**
> *"Mechanical surface cleaning is the use of impact, abrasion, or friction to physically remove surface contaminants and produce a defined cleanliness and anchor profile on a marine steel substrate in accordance with SSPC-SP2/SP3/SP5/SP6/SP10 or ISO 8501-1 St 2/St 3/Sa 1/Sa 2/Sa 2½/Sa 3 standards."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pickling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pickling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pickling** is a *chemical surface treatment process* in which ferrous (steel) and non-ferrous marine alloys are immersed in a hot or cold aqueous acid solution to remove **mill scale, rust (iron oxides), welding scale, heat-treatment scale, and other inorganic surface contaminants** prior to subsequent operations such as hot-dip galvanizing, electroplating, painting, cold rolling, or marine service exposure.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (OENST612 – Marine Materials, Corrosion Prevention and Protection), pickling is classified as a **critical pre-treatment stage** in the marine corrosion-protection chain because residual mill scale and rust act as *cathodic sites* that drastically accelerate localized (pitting and crevice) corrosion in seawater environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *"Pickling is the chemical cleaning of metallic surfaces using acid solutions (typically hydrochloric acid, sulphuric acid, phosphoric acid, or nitric-hydrofluoric acid mixtures) to dissolve oxides, scale, and embedded contaminants, thereby producing a chemically active, clean metallic surface for subsequent protective coating or plating operations."*

## 1.2 The Mechanism in One Line

> Pickling works by **acid-attacking the metal oxide layer** (which is more reactive/brittle than the parent metal) and converting the oxides into **water-soluble salts** that can be washed away, while carefully controlling the reaction so the *base metal itself* is only minimally attacked.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have an old iron gate that has been exposed to the monsoon for years. The reddish-brown rust flaking off makes the paint peel within weeks. Now, picture the following:

- **Scenario A (No pickling):** You sand the gate lightly and apply fresh paint. Underneath, microscopic rust *pockets* remain, absorb moisture, and within 3–4 months, the paint bubbles and peels.
- **Scenario B (Pickling done):** You dip the entire gate into a tank of dilute acid (say, hydrochloric acid). The acid *eats away* every trace of rust, mill scale, and embedded dirt, leaving a *silvery-grey, chemically clean* iron surface. The moment you wash it and dry it, you apply paint. The paint now bonds at the **atomic level** and lasts for years.

This second scenario is exactly what is done in a shipyard or galvanizing plant — but on an industrial scale, inside huge acid baths, with strict control over **acid concentration, temperature, time, and inhibitors**.

## 1.4 Why Pickling is Essential in Marine Engineering

| Marine Component | Without Pickling | With Pickling |
|---|---|---|
| Ship hull plates | Mill scale causes paint delamination in 6–12 months | Coating life extended to **5–10 years** |
| Offshore pipework | Scale traps chlorides → pitting corrosion | Clean surface → uniform protective film |
| Propeller castings | Sand residues cause galvanic cells | Uniform passivation achievable |
| Storage tank internals | Rust contaminates cargo (e.g., oil, chemicals) | Smooth, contamination-free interior |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric (KPI):** In modern pickling lines, a **pickling loss of 1–3% of metal weight** is considered industrially acceptable. Anything above 5% indicates poor inhibitor control and wasted base metal.

## 1.5 Standard Acids Used in Marine Pickling

- **Hydrochloric Acid (HCl)** — most common for carbon steel
- **Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** — used at higher temperatures (50–70 °C)
- **Phosphoric Acid (H₃PO₄)** — for stainless steel and final rinse
- **Nitric Acid (HNO₃) + Hydrofluoric Acid (HF)** — for stainless steel and duplex alloys
- **Sulphamic Acid (NH₂SO₃H)** — safer alternative, used in field pickling

## 1.6 Standards Governing Pickling

- **ASTM A380** – Standard Practice for Cleaning, Descaling, and Passivation of Stainless Steel Parts
- **ASTM B600** – Standard Guide for Descaling and Cleaning Titanium Surfaces
- **IS 6128** – Indian Standard for Pickling of Steel
- **ISO 8501-1** – Surface preparation standard (relates to post-pickling finish)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pickling Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The pickling process is governed by **three coupled chemical phenomena**: (i) dissolution of the oxide layer, (ii) controlled attack of the base metal, and (iii) hydrogen evolution. Understanding these allows the engineer to control pickling rate, surface finish, and metal loss.

### Step 1 — Diffusion of Acid to the Metal Surface
- The acid solution must physically reach the metal through the porous oxide layer.
- This is controlled by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**:

$$ J = -D \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} $$

where $J$ = flux of acid species (mol/m²·s), $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s), and $\partial C / \partial x$ = concentration gradient.

### Step 2 — Chemical Attack on the Oxide Scale
- For iron oxide scale (Fe₃O₄ / Fe₂O₃ / FeO), the reactions with HCl are:

$$ \text{FeO} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\text{HCl} \rightarrow 2\text{FeCl}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ \text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + 8\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_3 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

> The products — **FeCl₂ and FeCl₃** — are *water-soluble* and are carried away by the bath, exposing fresh metal.

### Step 3 — Attack on the Base Metal (Undesirable)
- Once the scale dissolves, the bare metal is exposed to acid. The reaction is:

$$ \text{Fe} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2 + \text{H}_2 \uparrow $$

- This is the **main reason pickling inhibitors** are added — to suppress this reaction by 95–99% while still allowing oxide dissolution.

### Step 4 — Hydrogen Evolution and Embrittlement
- The H₂ gas produced can:
  1. Physically lift off remaining scale (mechanical assistance)
  2. Cause **hydrogen embrittlement** in high-strength steels (UTS > 1000 MPa)
  3. Lead to **hydrogen-induced blistering** if not baked out before plating

### Step 5 — Rinsing and Passivation
- The pickled surface is immediately rinsed with water, then often with a **mild alkaline solution (NaOH or Na₂CO₃)** to neutralize residual acid.
- Stainless steel is then treated with **passivation acid (HNO₃)** to restore the chromium oxide layer.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pickling Rate (empirical) | $R_p = k \cdot C^n \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | g/m²·h | Predicts metal loss per hour |
| 2 | Acid Diffusion Flux | $J = -D \cdot (\partial C / \partial x)$ | mol/m²·s | Controls acid penetration |
| 3 | Hydrogen Evolution Rate | $V_{H_2} = (I \cdot t \cdot R \cdot T) / (n \cdot F \cdot P)$ | m³/s | Safety & embrittlement calc. |
| 4 | Pickling Efficiency | $\eta = (M_{oxide} - M_{acid}) / M_{oxide} \times 100$ | % | Compares baths |
| 5 | Inhibitor Efficiency | $IE = (R_0 - R_i) / R_0 \times 100$ | % | Quality of inhibitor |
| 6 | Iron Build-up Limit | Fe²⁺ ≤ 120 g/L (HCl bath) | g/L | Bath life indicator |
| 7 | Free Acid (FA) Test | $FA = V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} / V_{sample}$ | g/L | Bath control |
| 8 | Total Acid (TA) Test | $TA = V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} / V_{sample}$ (with HF) | g/L | Bath control |
| 9 | Acid-to-Iron Ratio | $FA / Fe^{2+} \geq 1.5$ | – | Bath replacement trigger |
| 10 | Pickling Temperature | 40–70 °C (HCl), 60–80 °C (H₂SO₄) | °C | Process window |

Where:
- $k$ = rate constant, $C$ = acid concentration, $n$ = reaction order (≈1 for HCl on steel)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (≈ 60–80 kJ/mol for steel in HCl)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K)
- $F$ = Faraday constant (96,485 C/mol)
- $R_0$ = corrosion rate without inhibitor, $R_i$ = rate with inhibitor
- $V_{NaOH}, N_{NaOH}$ = volume and normality of NaOH used in titration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** The **Inhibitor Efficiency (IE)** formula is *frequently* asked in 3-mark and 14-mark questions. Memorize it as: *IE% = (Rate without – Rate with) ÷ Rate without × 100*.

## 2.3 Role of Pickling Inhibitors

Pickling inhibitors are **organic compounds** (heterocyclic N, S, O compounds) that adsorb preferentially on the *bare metal* but **not on the oxide**, thereby:

- Suppressing Fe + acid reaction by 95–99%
- Reducing acid consumption by 20–40%
- Producing smoother, brighter surfaces
- Decreasing hydrogen embrittlement risk

### Common Industrial Inhibitors

| Inhibitor | Type | Typical Concentration | Used With |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rodine 213** (Amine-based) | Film-forming | 0.1–0.5% v/v | HCl baths |
| **IBIT 30** | Imidazoline | 0.2–1.0% v/v | H₂SO₄ baths |
| **Formaldehyde** (historical) | Reducing | 0.1% v/v | HCl baths |
| **Thiourea** | N,S organic | 0.05–0.2% | H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄ |
| **PBTC** (Phosphonates) | Eco-friendly | 0.5% | Modern plants |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Shipbuilding:** Hull plates are continuous-pickled in 18% HCl at 50 °C for 8–12 min before primer application.
2. **Offshore Platforms:** Pickled + galvanized structural members last 25–40 years in seawater splash zones.
3. **Marine Pipelines:** API 5L X65 pipes are batch-pickled before 3LPE coating.
4. **Submarine Hulls:** HY-80 / HY-100 steels are carefully pickled with inhibitors to avoid hydrogen embrittlement.
5. **Desalination Plants:** Titanium tubes are pickled in HNO₃-HF to remove oxide before service in brine.

## 2.5 Pickling Bath Control Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Range (HCl) | Action if Exceeded |
|---|---|---|
| Free Acid | 100–180 g/L | Add fresh acid |
| Fe²⁺ content | < 120 g/L | Replace bath |
| Temperature | 40–60 °C | Cool / heat exchanger |
| Inhibitor | 0.2–0.5% v/v | Top up daily |
| Pickling time | 5–20 min | Adjust conveyor speed |
| Surface finish | Sa 2½ (ISO 8503) | Re-pickle or grind |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The **Free Acid Test (FA)** and **Acid-to-Iron Ratio (FA/Fe²⁺)** are part of the KTU Module 3 lab syllabus. Students should be able to perform acid titration and interpret when a bath needs replacement.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pickling Rate Equation (Arrhenius Form)

The pickling rate of mild steel in HCl follows an Arrhenius-type temperature dependence and a power-law concentration dependence.

**Starting Postulate:**

The pickling rate $R_p$ (g/m²·h) is experimentally found to depend on acid concentration $C$ (g/L) and absolute temperature $T$ (K) as:

$$ R_p = k \cdot C^{n} $$

**Step 1 — Apply the Arrhenius Equation for $k$:**

The rate constant $k$ varies with temperature as:

$$ k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} $$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (frequency factor)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Step 2 — Substitute $k$ back into the rate expression:**

$$ R_p = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \cdot C^{n} $$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithms to obtain a linearizable form:**

$$ \ln R_p = \ln A + n \cdot \ln C - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} $$

**Step 4 — Isolate the temperature-dependent term for graphical analysis:**

$$ \ln R_p = \left( \ln A + n \ln C \right) - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} $$

**Step 5 — Interpret the resulting linear equation:**

A plot of $\ln R_p$ versus $1/T$ (at constant $C$) yields a **straight line** with:
- Slope = $-E_a / R$ → allows calculation of activation energy
- Intercept = $\ln A + n \ln C$ → allows calculation of $A$ and $n$

> This linearized form is the basis of the **ASTM G111** standard practice for evaluating pickling kinetics.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inhibitor Efficiency (IE) from Weight-Loss Data

**Experimental Setup:**
- Coupon mass before pickling: $W_0$ (g)
- Coupon mass after pickling **without inhibitor**: $W_1$ (g)
- Coupon mass after pickling **with inhibitor**: $W_2$ (g)
- Exposure time: $t$ (hours), area: $A$ (cm²)

**Step 1 — Corrosion rate without inhibitor (g/m²·h):**

$$ CR_0 = \frac{(W_0 - W_1) \times 10{,}000}{A \times t} $$

(The factor 10,000 converts cm² to m².)

**Step 2 — Corrosion rate with inhibitor (g/m²·h):**

$$ CR_i = \frac{(W_0 - W_2) \times 10{,}000}{A \times t} $$

**Step 3 — Subtract to obtain the suppression of attack:**

$$ \Delta CR = CR_0 - CR_i $$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage (Inhibitor Efficiency):**

$$ IE = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100\% $$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**

$$ IE = \left( 1 - \frac{CR_i}{CR_0} \right) \times 100\% $$

$$ IE = \left( 1 - \frac{W_0 - W_2}{W_0 - W_1} \right) \times 100\% $$

> [!NOTE]
> A good inhibitor typically gives **IE > 90%**. Industrial standards (e.g., NACE TM0172) consider IE > 95% as excellent.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydrogen Evolution Stoichiometry and Embrittlement

The pickling reaction releases H₂. For 1 mole of Fe dissolved:

$$ \text{Fe} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2 + \text{H}_2 $$

**Step 1 — Molar mass of Fe:** 55.85 g/mol

**Step 2 — Moles of H₂ produced per mole of Fe:**

By stoichiometry, 1 mol Fe → 1 mol H₂.

**Step 3 — Moles of H₂ per gram of Fe dissolved:**

$$ n_{H_2} = \frac{1}{55.85} = 0.01791 \; \text{mol/g} $$

**Step 4 — Volume of H₂ at STP (0 °C, 1 atm):**

$$ V_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \times 22{,}400 \; \text{mL/mol} = 401.2 \; \text{mL/g of Fe} $$

**Step 5 — Volume at typical pickling temperature (60 °C = 333 K):**

Using ideal gas law $V_2 = V_1 \times (T_2 / T_1)$:

$$ V_{H_2}(60°C) = 401.2 \times \frac{333}{273} = 489.4 \; \text{mL/g of Fe} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Consequence:** If a 1000 kg structural member loses 1% metal (10 kg) during pickling, it liberates **~4,894 litres of H₂** at 60 °C — an **explosively flammable** quantity. Hence:
> - Baths must have **forced ventilation**
> - **No naked flames** within 5 m
> - Hydrogen must be vented **above roof level** in industrial plants

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Acid-to-Iron Ratio Threshold for Bath Replacement

A pickling bath loses effectiveness as Fe²⁺ accumulates.

**Step 1 — Define Free Acid (FA):** Mass of unreacted HCl per litre, determined by titration with standard NaOH.

$$ FA = \frac{V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH} \times 36.46}{V_{sample}} \; \text{g/L} $$

(36.46 = molecular weight of HCl)

**Step 2 — Define Iron Content (Fe²⁺):** Determined by redox titration with KMnO₄ or by spectrophotometry.

**Step 3 — Define the Operational Ratio:**

$$ \rho = \frac{FA}{\text{Fe}^{2+}} $$

**Step 4 — Empirically (plant data):** When $\rho < 1.5$, the bath becomes saturated with FeCl₂ and pickling slows drastically; sludge forms.

**Step 5 — Bath replacement criterion:**

$$ \boxed{\text{Replace bath when } \rho = FA / Fe^{2+} < 1.5 } $$

> **Working Example:** If FA = 90 g/L and Fe²⁺ = 80 g/L, then $\rho = 1.125$ → bath is exhausted and must be replaced.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Pickling Acids

| Property | HCl | H₂SO₄ | H₃PO₄ | HNO₃-HF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Typical conc. | 5–18% | 5–25% | 5–15% | 10–20% + 1–3% HF |
| Temperature | 30–60 °C | 60–90 °C | 40–70 °C | 25–50 °C |
| Pickling time | 5–20 min | 10–30 min | 10–20 min | 5–15 min |
| Scale removed | Excellent | Excellent | Good (forms FePO₄) | Excellent (passivates) |
| Base metal attack | Moderate | High (lower at low T) | Low | Very low |
| Inhibitor needed | Yes | Yes, critical | Optional | Optional |
| Bath life | Long | Medium | Short | Short (HF hazardous) |
| Cost | Low | Lowest | High | Very high |
| Waste treatment | Easy (neutralize) | Easy | Easy | Difficult (F⁻) |
| Best suited for | Carbon steel, ship plates | Heavy scale, low-alloy steel | Stainless steel, final rinse | Stainless, duplex, super alloys |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark style)

**Problem:**
A mild steel plate of area 500 cm² and initial mass 312.50 g is pickled in 10% HCl at 40 °C for 30 min. After pickling, mass = 311.85 g. The same plate is pickled in identical conditions with 0.3% Rodine inhibitor; new mass = 312.40 g. Calculate:
1. Corrosion rate without inhibitor (g/m²·h)
2. Corrosion rate with inhibitor (g/m²·h)
3. Inhibitor efficiency (IE %)

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Mass loss without inhibitor:
$\Delta W_0 = 312.50 - 311.85 = 0.65 \; \text{g}$

Step 2 — Corrosion rate without inhibitor:
$CR_0 = (0.65 \times 10{,}000) / (500 \times 0.5) = 26.0 \; \text{g/m²·h}$

Step 3 — Mass loss with inhibitor:
$\Delta W_i = 312.50 - 312.40 = 0.10 \; \text{g}$

Step 4 — Corrosion rate with inhibitor:
$CR_i = (0.10 \times 10{,}000) / (500 \times 0.5) = 4.0 \; \text{g/m²·h}$

Step 5 — Inhibitor efficiency:
$IE = ((26.0 - 4.0) / 26.0) \times 100 = 84.6\%$

> **Interpretation:** Rodine 213 at 0.3% v/v gives ~85% protection, which is acceptable but not excellent. To improve to >95%, increase concentration to 0.5% or switch to a more modern inhibitor.

    E3 --> F

## 4.3 Schematic — Pickling Reaction Mechanism (Chemical-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metal Surface with Oxide Layer] --> B[Step 1: Acid Diffuses Through Pores]
    B --> C[Step 2: Acid Reacts with Fe2O3 and Fe3O4]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define pickling. List any four acids commonly used in pickling of marine steel structures.**

**Model Answer:**

Pickling is a chemical surface cleaning process in which metal components are immersed in a hot or cold aqueous acid solution to remove mill scale, rust, heat-treatment scale, and other inorganic surface contaminants, thereby producing a chemically clean, active metallic surface suitable for subsequent protective coating, plating, or galvanizing operations.

The four acids commonly used in marine pickling are:

1. **Hydrochloric acid (HCl)** — most widely used for carbon steel ship plates
2. **Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄)** — used at 60–80 °C for heavy scale
3. **Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄)** — used for stainless steel and final rinse applications
4. **Nitric-hydrofluoric acid (HNO₃ + HF)** — used for stainless steel, duplex, and super-alloy components

> *(Examiner Note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four acids. Listing > 4 is acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a pickling inhibitor? Give two examples and state its function.**

**Model Answer:**

A pickling inhibitor is an organic chemical additive (typically containing N, S, or O heteroatoms) that, when added in small concentrations (0.1–0.5% v/v) to the pickling acid, preferentially adsorbs onto the bare metal surface and suppresses the attack of the acid on the base metal, while still allowing dissolution of the oxide scale.

**Function:** Inhibitors reduce metal loss, prevent excessive hydrogen evolution, decrease acid consumption, and produce a smoother surface finish.

**Two examples:**
1. **Rodine 213** — amine-based film-forming inhibitor (used with HCl)
2. **Thiourea** — nitrogen-sulphur organic inhibitor (used with H₂SO₄ and H₃PO₄)

> *(Examiner Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for function, 1 mark for examples. Naming 2 correct examples is mandatory for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Q(a)** Discuss in detail the chemistry of pickling of mild steel in hydrochloric acid. Write the balanced chemical equations and explain the role of inhibitors. **(7 Marks)**

**Q(b)** A mild steel coupon of area 400 cm² and initial mass 248.60 g is pickled in 15% HCl at 50 °C for 45 minutes without inhibitor. After pickling, its mass is 247.80 g. The same coupon is pickled in identical conditions with 0.4% inhibitor; new mass is 248.45 g. Calculate (i) corrosion rate without inhibitor, (ii) corrosion rate with inhibitor, and (iii) inhibitor efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Step 1 — The Three Stages of Pickling Chemistry:**

Pickling of mild steel in HCl proceeds through three sequential chemical stages:

**Stage 1 — Dissolution of the outer oxide scale (Fe₂O₃, hematite):**

$$ \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\text{HCl} \rightarrow 2\text{FeCl}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Stage 2 — Dissolution of the inner oxide scale (Fe₃O₄, magnetite):**

$$ \text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + 8\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_3 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Stage 3 — Dissolution of the base metal (undesirable, must be suppressed):**

$$ \text{Fe} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2 + \text{H}_2 \uparrow $$

**Step 2 — Side Reactions:**

- The H₂ gas released mechanically dislodges remaining scale flakes.
- Some FeCl₃ may be reduced by H₂: $2\text{FeCl}_3 + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{FeCl}_2 + 2\text{HCl}$ (regenerating acid).
- Fe²⁺ accumulates in the bath and forms FeCl₂ sludge when saturated.

**Step 3 — Role of Inhibitors:**

Inhibitors are organic molecules (amines, imidazolines, thioureas, pyridines) that:
- Adsorb physically/chemically on the *bare metal* via lone-pair donation from N, S, or O atoms.
- Form a **monolayer protective film** (~10 nm thick).
- Are **not** adsorbed on the oxide (which is more polar and rough), so oxide dissolution continues.
- Reduce metal loss by **95–99%** and hydrogen evolution proportionally.
- Common inhibitors: **Rodine 213, IBIT 30, thiourea, PBTC**.

**Step 4 — Practical Bath Control:**

- Free Acid (FA): 100–180 g/L
- Fe²⁺ content: < 120 g/L
- Acid-to-iron ratio (FA/Fe²⁺) ≥ 1.5
- Temperature: 40–60 °C
- Time: 5–20 min
- Inhibitor: 0.2–0.5% v/v

> *(Mark Distribution: 2 marks each for the three equations, 1 mark for inhibitor mechanism, 2 marks for examples & control.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Given:**
- Area $A = 400 \; \text{cm}^2 = 0.04 \; \text{m}^2$
- Initial mass $W_0 = 248.60 \; \text{g}$
- Time $t = 45 \; \text{min} = 0.75 \; \text{h}$
- Final mass (no inhibitor) $W_1 = 247.80 \; \text{g}$
- Final mass (with inhibitor) $W_2 = 248.45 \; \text{g}$

**Step 1 — Mass loss without inhibitor:**

$$ \Delta W_0 = 248.60 - 247.80 = 0.80 \; \text{g} $$

**Step 2 — Corrosion rate without inhibitor:**

$$ CR_0 = \frac{\Delta W_0 \times 10{,}000}{A \times t} = \frac{0.80 \times 10{,}000}{0.04 \times 0.75 \times 10{,}000} $$

> *Simplification:* Since area is already in cm² and we want g/m²·h, use:

$$ CR_0 = \frac{\Delta W_0}{A_{(m^2)} \times t_{(h)}} = \frac{0.80}{0.04 \times 0.75} = \frac{0.80}{0.03} = 26.67 \; \text{g/m}^2 \cdot \text{h} $$

**Step 3 — Mass loss with inhibitor:**

$$ \Delta W_i = 248.60 - 248.45 = 0.15 \; \text{g} $$

**Step 4 — Corrosion rate with inhibitor:**

$$ CR_i = \frac{0.15}{0.04 \times 0.75} = \frac{0.15}{0.03} = 5.00 \; \text{g/m}^2 \cdot \text{h} $$

**Step 5 — Inhibitor Efficiency:**

$$ IE = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100 = \frac{26.67 - 5.00}{26.67} \times 100 $$

$$ IE = \frac{21.67}{26.67} \times 100 = 81.25\% $$

> *(Mark Distribution: 2 marks for mass losses, 2 marks for both CR calculations with unit conversions, 2 marks for IE formula and final value, 1 mark for interpretation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Q(a)** Compare hydrochloric acid and sulphuric acid as pickling media for marine steel. Discuss the advantages, limitations, and typical operating conditions of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Q(b)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the industrial pickling process for ship hull plates. Explain the quality control tests performed on the pickling bath. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | HCl (Hydrochloric) | H₂SO₄ (Sulphuric) |
|---|---|---|
| Concentration | 5–18% | 5–25% |
| Temperature | 30–60 °C | 60–80 °C |
| Reaction rate | Faster (lower activation energy) | Slower (needs higher T) |
| Scale dissolution | Excellent | Excellent |
| Base metal attack | Moderate | High (more aggressive) |
| Inhibitor requirement | Moderate | Critical (high attack) |
| Bath life | Long (FeCl₂ soluble) | Shorter (FeSO₄ less soluble, sludge) |
| Waste treatment | Easy (neutralize with lime) | Easy but produces CaSO₄ sludge |
| Cost | Moderate | Lowest |
| Hydrogen embrittlement | Moderate | Higher (at high T) |
| Suitability | Continuous pickling lines | Batch pickling of heavy plates |

**Key Advantages of HCl:**
- Faster dissolution of scale at lower temperature
- Iron salts are highly water-soluble → less sludge
- Easier bath control (single-acid system)
- Preferred for **continuous in-line pickling** of ship plates

**Key Advantages of H₂SO₄:**
- Cheaper acid, widely available
- Can be used at very high temperatures for tough scale
- Suitable for **batch pickling** of thick forgings
- Forms a slight passivation layer (FeSO₄) that gives a temporary corrosion resistance

**Limitations of HCl:**
- Highly corrosive to equipment (needs rubber-lined tanks)
- Fumes (HCl gas) require scrubbers
- Cannot be used on stainless steel (causes pitting)

**Limitations of H₂SO₄:**
- Higher operating temperature → more energy
- Iron sulphate (FeSO₄) precipitates as sludge at high Fe²⁺
- More severe hydrogen embrittlement risk
- Requires strict temperature control to avoid *pickling burn*

> *(Mark Distribution: 3 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for advantages, 2 marks for limitations.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Process Flow (Already in Section 4.1):** Refer to the Mermaid flowchart.

**Quality Control Tests on the Pickling Bath:**

**1. Free Acid (FA) Test:**
- Pipette 10 mL of bath into a conical flask
- Add 2–3 drops of methyl orange indicator
- Titrate against standard 1.0 N NaOH until the colour changes from red → yellow
- Calculation: $\text{FA (g/L)} = V_{\text{NaOH}} \times N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 3.646$ (for HCl)
- Acceptance: FA should be 100–180 g/L

**2. Iron Content (Fe²⁺) Test:**
- Pipette 5 mL of bath, dilute with distilled water
- Add 10 mL of H₂SO₄ (1:1) and a pinch of indicator (barium diphenylamine sulphonate)
- Titrate against 0.1 N KMnO₄ until a persistent pink colour appears
- Calculation: $\text{Fe}^{2+} \text{ (g/L)} = V_{\text{KMnO}_4} \times N_{\text{KMnO}_4} \times 5.585$
- Acceptance: Fe²⁺ < 120 g/L for HCl bath

**3. Acid-to-Iron Ratio (FA/Fe²⁺):**
- Acceptance: ≥ 1.5 → bath healthy
- If < 1.5 → bath exhausted, replace

**4. Inhibitor Concentration Check:**
- Measured by HPLC or simple chemical assay of N-content
- Acceptance: 0.2–0.5% v/v

**5. Temperature Check:**
- Measured by thermistor or RTD sensor
- Acceptance: 40–60 °C (HCl) or 60–80 °C (H₂SO₄)

**6. Visual Inspection of Pickled Surface:**
- Compare against ISO 8501-1 reference panels
- Acceptance: Sa 2½ (commercial blast) or Sa 3 (white metal)

> *(Mark Distribution: 2 marks for flow diagram description, 3 marks for FA + Fe²⁺ tests, 2 marks for the other QC checks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Error in Corrosion Rate Calculation:** Forgetting to convert cm² to m² (×10,000) or minutes to hours (÷60). Always write units explicitly. Many students write 0.04 m² but actually use 400 cm² in the calculation — examiner *will* deduct 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Confusing Inhibitor Efficiency Formula:** Some students write $IE = (CR_i - CR_0)/CR_0 \times 100$ — this gives *negative* IE. The correct numerator is $(CR_0 - CR_i)$, not the reverse.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Balance Chemical Equations:** Pickling reactions must always be balanced. Unbalanced equations lose 0.5–1 mark each.
>
> 4. **Confusing Pickling with Passivation:** Pickling removes scale; passivation restores the chromium-rich oxide layer. KTU examiners frequently include a 3-mark question on this distinction.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Inhibitor Section in Long Answers:** Many students write the pickling chemistry correctly but forget the role of inhibitors entirely. This typically costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 6. **Numerical Rounding Errors:** Round only at the *final* step. Intermediate values should be kept to 4 significant figures.
>
> 7. **Not Mentioning Hydrogen Embrittlement:** For high-strength marine steels (HY-80, HY-100, 15-5PH), failing to mention hydrogen embrittlement and the need for post-pickle baking (190 °C × 4 h) is a common 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Pickling (Module 3)**

- [x] **Definition:** Pickling = acid cleaning of metallic surfaces to remove mill scale, rust, and inorganic contaminants.
- [x] **Most common acid for marine steel:** **HCl at 10–18%, 40–60 °C, 5–20 min.**
- [x] **Other acids:** H₂SO₄ (cheaper, hotter), H₃PO₄ (stainless, final rinse), HNO₃+HF (stainless, duplex).
- [x] **Three chemical stages:** (1) Fe₂O₃ dissolution, (2) Fe₃O₄ dissolution, (3) base metal attack (must be suppressed).
- [x] **Key reactions to memorize:**
  - $\text{Fe} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2 + \text{H}_2 \uparrow$
  - $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\text{HCl} \rightarrow 2\text{FeCl}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$
  - $\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + 8\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_3 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- [x] **Inhibitor function:** Adsorb on bare metal, suppress acid attack, reduce H₂ evolution.
- [x] **Inhibitor Efficiency formula:** $IE = (CR_0 - CR_i)/CR_0 \times 100\%$
- [x] **Pickling Rate equation:** $R_p = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot C^n$ (Arrhenius-type)
- [x] **Bath replacement criterion:** Acid-to-iron ratio (FA/Fe²⁺) ≥ 1.5
- [x] **Acceptable pickling loss:** 1–3% of metal weight; > 5% is poor control.
- [x] **Hydrogen embrittlement risk:** Significant for UTS > 1000 MPa steels; remedy is post-pickle **baking at 190 °C for 4 hours**.
- [x] **Surface finish standard:** **ISO 8501-1** — Sa 2½ (commercial) or Sa 3 (white metal) after pickling.
- [x] **H₂ gas safety:** Every gram of Fe dissolved liberates ~489 mL of H₂ at 60 °C → mandatory ventilation.
- [x] **Industrial inhibitors to name:** Rodine 213, IBIT 30, Thiourea, PBTC.
- [x] **Standards to cite:** ASTM A380, ASTM B600, IS 6128, ISO 8501-1.
- [x] **Key industrial applications:** Ship hull plates, offshore pipework (API 5L), submarine HY-steels, desalination plant titanium tubes.
- [x] **Pickling vs. Passivation:** Pickling = acid removes scale; Passivation = nitric acid restores Cr-oxide layer (for stainless steel only).
- [x] **Common exam trap:** Pickling *accelerates* corrosion if not followed by rinsing and protective coating → must immediately proceed to next stage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blast Cleaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blast Cleaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Blast Cleaning** (also termed *Abrasive Blast Cleaning* or *Abrasive Jet Cleaning*) is a **mechanical surface preparation process** in which abrasive media are propelled under high pressure against a metallic (or non-metallic) substrate to remove contaminants, mill scale, rust, old coatings, weld spatter, and other foreign matter, while simultaneously producing a controlled surface profile (anchor pattern) suitable for the adhesion of subsequent protective coatings.

In marine engineering practice, blast cleaning is governed primarily by the **ISO 8501-1:2007** standard (*Preparation of steel substrates before application of paints and related products — Visual assessment of surface cleanliness*) and the corresponding **ISO 8503** standard series for surface roughness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Syllabus Highlight:**
> Surface preparation accounts for **more than 80% of coating performance** in marine environments. Blast cleaning is the *primary* mechanical preparation technique taught in Module 3 because it is the only method that simultaneously *cleans* AND *profiles* a steel surface.

The four ISO 8501-1 visual cleanliness grades for blast cleaning are:

| Grade | Designation | Description (Concise) |
|---|---|---|
| Sa 1 | Light blast cleaning | Light cleaning; loose rust, loose mill scale, loose paint removed |
| Sa 2 | Thorough blast cleaning | ~95% of surface free from visible residues |
| Sa 2½ | Commercial blast cleaning | ~98% clean; the **marine industry standard** for most protective coatings |
| Sa 3 | Blast cleaning to visually clean steel | ~100% clean; required for immersion service and high-performance coatings |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine the deck of an old ship that has been at sea for ten years. It is covered in **rust, barnacles, salt crust, and flaking old paint**. If a fresh coat of marine epoxy is applied directly over this mess, it will simply *peel off* within months — because paint cannot grip a dirty, uneven, smooth surface.

**Blast cleaning is essentially "sand-blasting the ship's skin back to bare, healthy metal"** — just like how a dentist uses a high-speed air-abrasion tool to clean a tooth before filling it.

The analogy:
- **Abrasive particles (sand/grit/shot)** = the dentist's abrasive pumice
- **Compressed air stream** = the high-speed handpiece
- **Nozzle** = the tip of the dental tool
- **Steel substrate** = the tooth being cleaned
- **Anchor profile** = the microscopic roughness that helps the filling bond

Without this roughened, surgically clean surface, no marine coating — regardless of how expensive — will perform its function against **chloride-laden seawater**, **UV radiation**, and **cathodic shielding effects**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are universally quoted performance parameters in marine blast-cleaning operations:

- **Standard Nozzle Pressure:** **0.6 MPa to 0.8 MPa (≈ 80–110 psi)** at the nozzle for most abrasives.
- **Venturi Nozzle Pressure Drop:** **0.07 MPa (≈ 10 psi)** loss across a well-designed venturi bore.
- **Compressed Air Consumption:** **Q ≈ 0.028 × d² (m³/min)** where *d* is nozzle throat diameter in mm (rule of thumb).
- **Productivity (Blast Rate):** **8–30 m²/hr** for Sa 2½ on structural steel (operator-dependent).
- **Surface Profile Range:** **50–100 μm** (medium) for marine epoxy systems; **75–125 μm** for heavy-duty coatings.

> [!NOTE]
> **One Practical Rule of Thumb** every marine engineer should remember: *For every 25 mm (1 inch) increase in stand-off distance, the cleaning efficiency drops by approximately 20%.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mechanics of Blast Cleaning — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

Blast cleaning is governed by a combination of **kinetic energy transfer** and **impact mechanics**. Each abrasive particle acts as a tiny projectile that:

1. **Accelerates** in the nozzle (converting pressure energy to kinetic energy).
2. **Impacts** the substrate surface at high velocity.
3. **Fractures** and removes brittle surface films (rust, mill scale, old coating).
4. **Plastically deforms** the ductile base metal, creating the **anchor profile**.
5. **Shatters itself** (for friable abrasives like aluminum oxide) or **rounds off** (for steel shot).

The **kinetic energy** of a single abrasive particle is the fundamental driver:

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2}\,m\,v^{2}
$$

Where:
- $E_k$ = kinetic energy of one abrasive particle (Joules)
- $m$ = mass of one particle (kg)
- $v$ = impact velocity (m/s)

This single equation explains almost every operational rule used in marine blasting:

| If you change... | Effect on $E_k$ | Real-World Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Increase particle mass *m* | Linear increase in $E_k$ | Heavier grit (steel shot) cuts faster but may over-peen thin plate |
| Increase velocity *v* | **Quadratic** increase in $E_k$ | Higher nozzle pressure → much faster cleaning, but more abrasive consumption |
| Decrease particle size | Linear decrease in *m* | Finer abrasive → lower profile, used for thin coatings |

### 2.2 Air Flow Through a Blast Nozzle

The volumetric flow of compressed air through a circular nozzle is given by the **ISO 8502 / nozzle manufacturer** formula:

$$
Q = C_d \, A \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho_{air}}}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (≈ **0.85** for venturi nozzles)
- $A$ = nozzle throat cross-section area (m²)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across nozzle (Pa)
- $\rho_{air}$ = density of air (≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at 20 °C)

A widely-used industry simplification for **practical field estimation** is:

$$
Q \approx 0.028\,d^{2} \quad [\text{m}^{3/min}]
$$

where $d$ is the nozzle throat diameter in **mm**, valid at **0.7 MPa nozzle pressure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The quadratic velocity dependence means that **doubling the nozzle pressure does NOT double cleaning rate** — it approximately quadruples the kinetic energy per particle. This is why nozzle pressure is the *most powerful single variable* in blast-cleaning productivity.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All mathematical "vertical bar" symbols below use `\vert` so they never break markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Range / Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Particle kinetic energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$ | J | Explains productivity vs. pressure |
| 2 | Air flow rate (field rule) | $Q \approx 0.028\,d^{2}$ | m³/min | Compressor sizing |
| 3 | Air mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho_{air}\,Q$ | kg/s | Abrasive-to-air ratio |
| 4 | Productivity (empirical) | $A_{rate} = k\,\dfrac{P\,d}{H}$ | m²/hr | Estimating job duration |
| 5 | Abrasive consumption | $C_{ab} \approx 10$–$30\ \text{kg/m}^{2}$ | kg/m² | Sa 2½ on mild steel |
| 6 | Nozzle wear rate | $\Delta d \approx 1$ mm per 1000 hr | mm | Nozzle replacement schedule |
| 7 | Stand-off distance | $H$ | 200–400 mm | Operator efficiency |
| 8 | Impact angle | $\theta$ | 60°–90° to surface | Profile geometry |
| 9 | Surface profile (anchor) | $R_a$ or $R_z$ | 50–100 μm | Coating specification match |
| 10 | Air pressure at nozzle | $P_{noz}$ | 0.6–0.8 MPa | Field set-point |

Where, for productivity:
- $k$ = empirical constant (depends on abrasive and substrate)
- $P$ = nozzle pressure
- $d$ = nozzle diameter
- $H$ = stand-off distance

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In marine contexts, blast cleaning is the **non-negotiable precursor** to:

1. **Marine epoxy and polyurethane coating systems** (hulls, decks, ballast tanks).
2. **Thermal metal spray (zinc/aluminium)** for long-term cathodic-type protection.
3. **Welding preparation** on new-build ship plates (removing primer, mill scale).
4. **In-service maintenance** during dry-dock repairs.
5. **Tank coating** (IMO PSPC-compliant ballast tank coatings demand Sa 2½ minimum, with profile 30–75 μm).

The performance of a coating in a marine atmosphere is directly proportional to the *quality of blast cleaning*. ISO 12944 (the European standard for corrosion protection of steel structures by protective paint systems) explicitly mandates blast cleaning to a defined grade as a function of **corrosivity category** (C1–C5-M, Im1–Im3).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Nozzle Pressure and Cleaning Rate

We want to show *why* nozzle pressure is the dominant variable. We start from the kinetic energy equation and combine it with the volumetric flow equation.

**Step 1 — Kinetic energy of one abrasive particle**

A particle of mass $m$ leaving a nozzle at velocity $v$ carries kinetic energy:

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2}\,m\,v^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Particle velocity from pressure drop**

Assuming the particle is fully accelerated by the air stream, its exit velocity scales with the **square root of the nozzle pressure drop** (Bernoulli-type relation):

$$
v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho_{mix}}}
$$

where $\rho_{mix}$ is the effective density of the abrasive-laden air mixture.

**Step 3 — Substitute velocity back into kinetic energy**

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2}\,m\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho_{mix}}}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2}\,m \cdot \dfrac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho_{mix}}
$$

$$
E_k = \dfrac{m\,\Delta P}{\rho_{mix}}
$$

So kinetic energy is **linearly proportional** to nozzle pressure drop for a fixed particle mass.

**Step 4 — Cleaning rate (mass removal) per unit time**

If $n$ particles impact per second, the total energy delivered per second is:

$$
\dot{E} = n \cdot E_k = n \cdot \dfrac{m\,\Delta P}{\rho_{mix}}
$$

Now, the number-flux of particles $n$ is itself proportional to volumetric air flow $Q$, and $Q$ scales as $\sqrt{\Delta P}$:

$$
n \propto Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{E} \propto \sqrt{\Delta P} \cdot \Delta P
$$

$$
\dot{E} \propto (\Delta P)^{3/2}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering conclusion**

$$
\text{Cleaning Rate} \;\propto\; P_{nozzle}^{\,3/2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Doubling nozzle pressure increases the cleaning rate by a factor of $2^{1.5} \approx 2.83$ — not 2×, not 4×. This is the quantitative reason marine blasting contractors invest heavily in high-capacity compressors and *never* allow pressure to drop on site.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Compressor Sizing for a Blasting Job

**Problem (KTU-style numerical):** A shipyard must blast-clean a flat plate of total area **A = 200 m²** to **Sa 2½** using a **10 mm nozzle**. Standard productivity for Sa 2½ is **A_rate = 12 m²/hr**. Standard air consumption per nozzle is **Q = 2.8 m³/min** at 0.7 MPa. The yard uses **two nozzles simultaneously**. Determine:

(a) Total time required.
(b) Required compressor free-air delivery (FAD), accounting for a 50% leakage and simultaneous-use factor.
(c) Minimum receiver (air tank) volume.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total time required**

$$
t = \dfrac{A}{A_{rate}}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{200\ \text{m}^{2}}{12\ \text{m}^{2/hr}}
$$

$$
t \approx 16.67\ \text{hr} \;\approx\; 17\ \text{hours of blasting time}
$$

**(b) Required compressor FAD**

Air consumption per nozzle at 0.7 MPa (gauge) = 2.8 m³/min.

Convert to free-air equivalent (atmospheric). Approximating atmospheric pressure as 0.1 MPa gauge equivalent:

$$
Q_{free} = Q_{noz} \times \dfrac{P_{noz}}{P_{atm}} \approx 2.8 \times \dfrac{0.8}{0.1} \approx 22.4\ \text{m}^{3/min}
$$

For 2 nozzles:

$$
Q_{2-nozzle} = 2 \times 22.4 = 44.8\ \text{m}^{3/min}
$$

Apply 50% margin for leakage, hose losses, and after-cooler demand:

$$
Q_{FAD} = 1.5 \times 44.8
$$

$$
Q_{FAD} \approx 67.2\ \text{m}^{3/min}
$$

**(c) Minimum receiver volume**

A receiver must supply peak demand for at least **30 seconds** of buffer:

$$
V_{recv} = \dfrac{Q_{FAD} \times t_{buffer}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}
$$

Using $P_{max} = 0.8$ MPa, $P_{min} = 0.6$ MPa, $t_{buffer} = 30$ s = 0.5 min:

$$
V_{recv} = \dfrac{67.2 \times 0.5}{0.8 - 0.6}
$$

$$
V_{recv} = \dfrac{33.6}{0.2}
$$

$$
V_{recv} \approx 168\ \text{m}^{3} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Use a } 170\ \text{m}^{3} \text{ receiver bank}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Shipyards typically operate **two to three** diesel-driven screw compressors of **25–30 m³/min FAD each**, with a shared receiver bank — confirming our calculation is in the correct order of magnitude for a real dry-dock blasting operation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Blast-Cleaning Selection Decision Tree

The following analytical matrix guides the choice of abrasive, method, and parameters for marine applications:

| Surface Condition | Target Grade | Recommended Abrasive | Method | Nozzle Ø | Pressure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| New plate, light mill scale | Sa 2 | Steel grit | Pressure blast | 8–10 mm | 0.7 MPa |
| Heavy rust, pitted steel | Sa 2½ | Steel grit + garnet mix | Pressure blast | 10–12 mm | 0.8 MPa |
| Old paint + rust | Sa 2½ | Copper slag / garnet | Vacuum or pressure | 10 mm | 0.7 MPa |
| Tank coating prep (PSPC) | Sa 2½, profile 30–75 μm | Fine steel grit | Vacuum blast | 8 mm | 0.6 MPa |
| Stainless / non-ferrous | Sa 1 | Glass beads / aluminium oxide | Pressure (no ferrous contamination) | 8 mm | 0.5 MPa |
| Delicate surfaces | Sa 1 | Sodium bicarbonate / dry ice | Soft blast | 6 mm | 0.4 MPa |

---

### 3.4 Energy & Cost Per Square Metre (Sanity-Check Derivation)

For a steel grit abrasive (density $\rho_{grit} \approx 7.8 \times 10^{3}$ kg/m³), consider a typical G25 grit (particle diameter $\approx 1.0$ mm = $1 \times 10^{-3}$ m):

**Volume of one particle:**

$$
V_p = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi\,(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{3}
$$

$$
V_p \approx 5.24 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^{3}
$$

**Mass of one particle:**

$$
m = \rho_{grit} \cdot V_p = 7800 \times 5.24 \times 10^{-10}
$$

$$
m \approx 4.1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg} \quad (\approx 4.1\ \text{mg})
$$

**Exit velocity at 0.7 MPa nozzle pressure:**

$$
v = \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho_{mix}} \approx \sqrt{2 \cdot 7 \times 10^{5} / 4} \approx 592\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Kinetic energy per particle:**

$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (4.1 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (592)^{2}
$$

$$
E_k \approx 0.72\ \text{J per particle}
$$

**Energy flux** at, say, 5 kg/min abrasive flow:

$$
\dot{m}_{ab} = 5 / 60 \approx 0.083\ \text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{E} = \dot{m}_{ab} \cdot v^{2}/2 \approx 0.083 \cdot (592)^{2}/2
$$

$$
\dot{E} \approx 14{,}500\ \text{W} \approx 14.5\ \text{kW of impact power}
$$

This $\approx 14.5$ kW is consistent with the audible roar of a marine blast operation and explains why a **single blaster consumes the equivalent power output of ~15 household kettles** continuously.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Marine Blast-Cleaning Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Diesel / Electric Compressor] -->|Pressurised Air 0.7 MPa| B[After-Cooler and Moisture Separator]
    B -->|Dry Compressed Air| C[Air Receiver Tank]
    C -->|Regulated Pressure| D[Blast Pot Pressure Vessel]
    E[Abrasive Hopper] -->|Metered Flow| D
    D -->|Air-Abrasive Mixture| F[Blasting Hose]
    F --> G[Venturi Blast Nozzle]
    G -->|High Velocity Jet| H[Steel Substrate Surface]
    H -->|Removed Rust and Scale| I[Abrasive Recovery Cyclone]
    H -->|Dust and Fines| J[Dust Collector with HEPA Filter]
    I -->|Cleaned Abrasive| K[Recycle to Abrasive Sieve]
    K -->|Reusable Grit| E
    J -->|Exhaust| L[Stack to Atmosphere]
    M[Operator with Supplied-Air Helmet] --> G
    N[Air Fed from Compressor] --> M
    B --> N
```

### 4.2 ISO 8501-1 Visual Cleanliness Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Steel Surface to be Prepared]) --> Q1{Loose Mill Scale or Rust Present?}
    Q1 -- No --> Sa1[Grade Sa 1 Light Blast Cleaning]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Tightly Adherent Rust Allowed up to 5 percent?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Sa2[Grade Sa 2 Thorough Blast Cleaning]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Tightly Adherent Rust Allowed up to 2 percent?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Sa25[Grade Sa 2 Commercial Standard for Marine]
    Q3 -- No --> Sa3[Grade Sa 3 Visually Clean Steel for Immersion Service]
    Sa25 --> End([Surface Ready for Primer Application])
    Sa2 --> End
    Sa3 --> End
    Sa1 --> End
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Venturi Nozzle (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Blasting Hose Inlet] --> B[Converging Section Throat Diameter 8 to 12 mm]
    B --> C[Parallel Bore Mixing Zone Length 3 to 5 times Throat]
    C --> D[Diverging Section 8 Degree Half Angle]
    D --> E[Exit Orifice Liner Replaceable Carbide]
    E --> F[High Velocity Jet Impinges on Substrate]
    F --> G[Substrate Surface]
```

### 4.4 Abrasive Recycling Loop (Marine Dry-Dock Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sub1[Blast Cleaning Zone]
        OP[Blaster Operator] -->|Vacuum Recovery Hose| V1[Vacuum Head at Floor]
        V1 --> R1[Recovery Cyclone Separator]
    end
    R1 -->|Oversize Debris| SC[Sieve Shaker]
    R1 -->|Reusable Abrasive| HOP[Abrasive Hoppers]
    SC -->|Reject to Waste| WST[Industrial Waste Skip]
    HOP -->|Recycled to Blast Pot| BP[Blast Pot]
    BP --> OP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the schematics:** Block A is the energy source; Block B–C is the air treatment train; Block D–G is the *active blast loop*; Block I–L is the *recycling and emissions control loop*. In a real marine blast room, these four sub-systems are physically separated but functionally interlocked by valves and dust-collection ducting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
*Define blast cleaning. List the four ISO 8501-1 visual grades of blast-cleaned steel surfaces.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Blast cleaning is a mechanical surface preparation process in which abrasive particles are propelled at high velocity against a metallic surface using compressed air to remove contaminants (rust, mill scale, old coating) and produce a defined surface profile.

**Four ISO 8501-1 Grades (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

| Grade | Description |
|---|---|
| **Sa 1** | Light blast cleaning; only loose matter removed |
| **Sa 2** | Thorough blast cleaning; ≈ 95% of surface clean |
| **Sa 2½** | Commercial blast cleaning; ≈ 98% clean; **standard for marine coatings** |
| **Sa 3** | Visually clean steel; ≈ 100% clean; for immersion service |

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
*State any three factors that influence the productivity of abrasive blast cleaning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Nozzle pressure** — Higher pressure increases particle kinetic energy; cleaning rate scales approximately as $P^{3/2}$.
2. **Abrasive type and size** — Harder, denser, angular abrasives (e.g., steel grit) cut faster than soft abrasives (e.g., glass beads).
3. **Stand-off distance** — Optimal at **200–400 mm**; efficiency drops ~20% per additional 25 mm of distance.

*(Acceptable alternatives: nozzle diameter, abrasive flow rate, initial surface condition, operator skill.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of a **pressure blast-cleaning system**. Discuss the role of the **blast pot**, **moisture separator**, and **abrasive recovery cyclone**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine hull plate of area **350 m²** is to be blast-cleaned to **Sa 2½** using **8 mm venturi nozzles** at **0.7 MPa** nozzle pressure. Standard productivity is **10 m²/hr per nozzle**, and air consumption per nozzle is **2.0 m³/min** (free-air equivalent 16 m³/min). Four nozzles will operate simultaneously. Calculate: **(i)** total blasting time; **(ii)** total free-air delivery required; **(iii)** minimum receiver tank volume assuming 30-second buffer between **0.7 and 0.5 MPa**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Working of a pressure blast-cleaning system (7 marks)**

A pressure blast-cleaning system (block diagram shown in Section 4.1) consists of:

1. **Air compressor** (1 mark) — supplies oil-free compressed air at **0.7–0.8 MPa**; typically electric- or diesel-driven screw type for shipyard use.
2. **After-cooler and moisture separator** (1 mark) — cools the compressed air and removes entrained moisture via condensation; critical because water in the air stream **causes abrasive clogging** and **flash rusting** of freshly blasted steel.
3. **Air receiver** (1 mark) — buffers peak demand and stabilises pressure; sized as in derivation 3.2(c).
4. **Blast pot (pressure vessel)** (2 marks) — pressurised hopper containing abrasive; metered abrasive is met into the air stream via a *venture* inside the pot. Two configurations: *pressure* (pot pressurised to full line pressure — high productivity) and *suction* (abrasive drawn in by Venturi effect — lower productivity).
5. **Blasting hose and nozzle** (1 mark) — typically 25–50 mm ID rubber hose, terminated by a venturi nozzle of 6–12 mm throat, often tungsten-carbide lined for wear resistance.
6. **Abrasive recovery cyclone** (1 mark) — separates reusable abrasive from dust and debris by centrifugal action; reduces consumption by **50–80%** in dry-dock operations and enables compliance with environmental dust-emission regulations.

Operator wears a **supplied-air helmet** with positive-pressure breathing air from the same compressor (after suitable filtration).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks — 3 + 2 + 2)**

**(i) Total blasting time**

$$
t = \dfrac{\text{Total Area}}{\text{Productivity per nozzle} \times \text{Number of nozzles}}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{350}{10 \times 4}
$$

$$
t = 8.75\ \text{hours}
$$

**(ii) Required free-air delivery**

Per nozzle: 16 m³/min; for 4 nozzles:

$$
Q_{req} = 4 \times 16 = 64\ \text{m}^{3/min}
$$

Apply **1.3 safety factor** for leakage and demand peaks:

$$
Q_{FAD} = 1.3 \times 64
$$

$$
Q_{FAD} \approx 83.2\ \text{m}^{3/min}
$$

**(iii) Minimum receiver volume**

$$
V_{recv} = \dfrac{Q_{FAD} \times t_{buffer}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}
$$

$$
t_{buffer} = 30\ \text{s} = 0.5\ \text{min}
$$

$$
V_{recv} = \dfrac{83.2 \times 0.5}{0.7 - 0.5}
$$

$$
V_{recv} = \dfrac{41.6}{0.2}
$$

$$
V_{recv} \approx 208\ \text{m}^{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{recv} \approx 208\ \text{m}^{3}}
$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **dry abrasive blasting** and **wet abrasive blasting** in marine surface preparation. Discuss at least four points of difference and state **two advantages** of wet blasting for shipboard or near-water operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ballast tank of internal surface area **1200 m²** is to be blast-cleaned to **Sa 2½** with surface profile **50–75 μm** as required by the **IMO PSPC** performance standard. Using a productivity of **15 m²/hr per nozzle**, two nozzles, and 8-hour shifts, calculate: **(i)** total blasting hours required; **(ii)** number of full 8-hour shifts needed; **(iii)** total abrasive consumption at 18 kg/m². **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Dry vs. Wet Abrasive Blasting — Comparison (7 marks)**

| # | Parameter | Dry Blasting | Wet Blasting |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Medium | Abrasive + air only | Abrasive + air + water (or steam) |
| 2 | Dust generation | **Very high**; requires enclosure and PPE | **Suppressed by ~90%** |
| 3 | Visible cleanliness | Easy to inspect against white metal | Surface appears dark/wet; **requires** added **5% sodium nitrite** or **flash-rust inhibitor** in water |
| 4 | Productivity | Higher (no water dilution of impact energy) | Slightly lower (~10–15%) |
| 5 | Anchor profile | Excellent for marine epoxies | Comparable; slightly finer profile |
| 6 | Equipment cost | Lower | Higher (pump, inhibitor dosing, flash-rust control) |
| 7 | Environmental compliance | Difficult near water; large dust collectors | **Preferred for in-service ships and near-water work** |
| 8 | Recovery of abrasive | Cyclone + screen | Slurry handling; water treatment required |

**Two advantages of wet blasting for shipboard / near-water work (2 marks):**
1. **Greatly reduced atmospheric dust**, allowing work in or near occupied machinery spaces and alongside berths.
2. **No electrostatic hazard** — important when blasting near fuel tanks or in dry-dock with hydrocarbon residues.

*(Plus: less operator fatigue from dust inhalation, easier cleanup of spent abrasive.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — Ballast Tank (7 marks — 3 + 2 + 2)**

**(i) Total blasting hours**

$$
t = \dfrac{A}{n \times R} = \dfrac{1200}{2 \times 15}
$$

$$
t = 40\ \text{hours of active blasting}
$$

**(ii) Number of 8-hour shifts**

$$
N_{shifts} = \dfrac{t}{T_{shift}} = \dfrac{40}{8}
$$

$$
N_{shifts} = 5\ \text{full shifts}
$$

**(iii) Total abrasive consumption**

$$
C_{ab} = A \times c_{ab} = 1200 \times 18
$$

$$
C_{ab} = 21{,}600\ \text{kg} \approx 21.6\ \text{tonnes of abrasive}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by the number of nozzles** in productivity calculations — loses ~2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing gauge and absolute pressure** in compressor-sizing sums — costs 1 mark; always state "gauge" or "absolute".
> 3. **Omitting the safety factor (typically 1.3–1.5)** on FAD calculations — examiner deducts for unrealistic compressor sizing.
> 4. **Reporting productivity per nozzle instead of total** — a classic Part (b) error costing the answer key full credit.
> 5. **Using `Sa 3` when the question specifies `Sa 2½`** — a mark-losing mistake because the two grades have very different productivity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Blast cleaning = mechanical cleaning + surface profiling** in one operation; the *only* method that does both economically.
- [ ] **ISO 8501-1** grades: **Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3** — remember marine standard is **Sa 2½**.
- [ ] **Kinetic energy per particle:** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$; cleaning rate scales as $P^{3/2}$.
- [ ] **Air flow field rule:** $Q \approx 0.028\,d^{2}$ (m³/min, *d* in mm).
- [ ] **Nozzle pressure:** **0.6–0.8 MPa**; **Stand-off distance:** **200–400 mm**; **Impact angle:** **60°–90°**.
- [ ] **Standard productivity (Sa 2½):** **10–30 m²/hr** depending on condition.
- [ ] **Abrasive consumption:** **10–30 kg/m²** at Sa 2½; recycled abrasive cuts cost by half.
- [ ] **Anchor profile:** **50–100 μm** for marine epoxy; **30–75 μm** for IMO PSPC ballast tank coatings.
- [ ] **Dry vs. wet blasting:** wet reduces dust ~90% but needs **flash-rust inhibitor** in water.
- [ ] **Compressor sizing rule of thumb:** 1.3–1.5 × total nozzle consumption.
- [ ] **Safety:** operator must wear **supplied-air helmet** (not just a mask) because silica/abrasive dust causes **silicosis**.
- [ ] **Environmental:** spent abrasive is **hazardous waste** (heavy-metal contaminated) — never free-discharge.
- [ ] **Remember:** Nozzle pressure drop ≈ 10 psi across venturi — always measure *at the nozzle*, not at the compressor.
- [ ] **Standard reference triplet for marine coating specs:** **ISO 8501-1 (cleanliness)** + **ISO 8503 (roughness)** + **ISO 12944 (coating system selection)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Cleaning: Rust Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flame Cleaning: Rust Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Flame Cleaning — Formal Academic Definition

**Flame Cleaning** is a thermal surface preparation technique employed primarily on carbon steel and ferrous substrates in marine, structural, and industrial environments. It utilizes a high-temperature **oxy-acetylene flame** (approximately **3,000–3,200 °C**) that is swept rapidly across a corroded or contaminated metal surface. The technique relies on the **differential thermal expansion coefficient** between the rust/scale layer and the underlying parent metal. When heated instantaneously, the oxide layer expands at a rate vastly different from the base steel, causing the rust to **crack, blister, and spall off** due to thermal shock, while simultaneously burning off oil, grease, salt deposits, and entrapped moisture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Flame cleaning is a method of surface preparation in which an oxy-acetylene flame is passed over a steel surface to remove rust, mill scale, moisture, oil, grease, and other contaminants by virtue of thermal shock, leaving the surface in a dry, warm, and receptive condition for immediate priming.*

## 1.2 Rust Converters — Formal Academic Definition

A **Rust Converter** is a chemical surface treatment formulation (typically water- or solvent-based) containing active chelating or acid-reacting agents — most commonly **tannic acid**, **phosphoric acid**, or proprietary blends of **vinyl-acrylic emulsions with tannin**. When applied to a rusted ferrous surface, it chemically transforms the unstable, hydrated ferric oxide (`Fe₂O₃·nH₂O` — the rust) into a stable, adherent, dark blue-black organic-metallic complex (e.g., **iron tannate** or **iron phosphate**), which acts as a **passive primer layer** compatible with subsequent top-coat paints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Chemistry Summary:**
> Rust converters do **not remove** rust — they **convert** it. This is a critical conceptual distinction in marine corrosion engineering, where complete mechanical removal is often impractical.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are heating a layered cake (base metal at the bottom, sticky caramelized sugar crust on top representing rust). If you blast it with a very hot torch for just a split second, the caramel layer expands much faster than the soft sponge below and **pops off in brittle flakes** — that's flame cleaning.

For rust converters, imagine a rusty iron fence in your garden. Instead of scrubbing it (expensive, slow), you spray a magic chemical that turns the orange rust into a hard, black, paintable surface overnight — like turning soft chalk into a hardened lacquer shell. That's the rust converter.

## 1.4 Key Physical & Process Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Oxy-acetylene flame temperature | **3,000–3,200 °C** | Source of thermal shock |
| Surface temperature after pass | **100–150 °C** | Optimal for primer adhesion |
| Travel speed of flame | **3–6 m/min** | Avoids warping thin plates |
| Rust converter application temp | **10–35 °C** ambient | Chemical reaction kinetics |
| Drying time of converter | **2–6 hours** | Before overcoating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Remember (Bold Emphasis):**
> The **differential thermal expansion coefficient** of rust (≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) versus steel (≈ 11.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C at first, but the moisture vaporization in hydrated rust causes explosive spalling) is the **physical principle** behind flame cleaning. The actual dominant mechanism is **steam generation from hydrated Fe₂O₃·nH₂O**, not mere differential expansion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Flame Cleaning — Operational Logic Steps

The flame cleaning procedure follows a strict sequential protocol:

1. **Pre-Inspection:** Identify the substrate (must be steel or cast iron), thickness (> 5 mm preferred to avoid distortion), and assess degree of rusting (light, moderate, heavy).
2. **Equipment Setup:** Oxygen and acetylene cylinders connected via pressure regulators (O₂ at **0.7–1.4 bar**, C₂H₂ at **0.3–0.7 bar**) to a welding torch fitted with a multi-flame nozzle (3, 5, or 7 orifice heads).
3. **Flame Adjustment:** Set to a **neutral** or **slightly oxidizing** flame to maximize surface temperature without depositing carbon.
4. **Flame Pass:** Sweep the flame at **3–6 m/min** at an angle of **45°** to the surface, keeping the inner cone tip about **6–12 mm** from the surface.
5. **Physical Action:** Three simultaneous phenomena occur:
   - **Thermal Shock:** Instantaneous heating causes hydrated rust (`Fe₂O₃·H₂O`, `FeO(OH)`) to dehydrate explosively.
   - **Combustion of Contaminants:** Oil, grease, salt, and moisture are incinerated.
   - **Spalling:** Rust flakes detach due to vapor pressure from liberated steam.
6. **Mechanical Auxiliary Cleaning:** Immediately follow flame cleaning with **wire brushing** (manual or powered) to remove loosened but adherent flakes.
7. **Priming While Warm:** Apply the **first coat of primer** within the **"warm window"** while the substrate temperature is **40–80 °C** to ensure optimal wetting and adhesion.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why 45° angle?** A perpendicular flame heats a localized spot, causing warping and burnt primer site; a glancing 45° pass distributes heat uniformly and aids in directional spalling.
- **Why immediate priming?** A flame-cleaned surface is **highly reactive** and will **re-rust within minutes** in humid marine atmospheres due to the absence of any protective oxide; the warm surface lowers primer viscosity, enhancing penetration into surface micro-pores.
- **Why wire brushing after?** Flame cleaning alone leaves **60–80% rust removal**; wire brushing brings it to **SIS Sa 2** (visually clean steel per ISO 8501-1).

## 2.3 Rust Converters — Chemistry & Mechanism Steps

Rust converters work via **chelation** (tannin-based) or **acid-base neutralization** (phosphate-based):

### Mechanism A — Tannic Acid Reaction:
$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Tannic Acid} \longrightarrow \text{Iron Tannate (stable blue-black complex)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The tannic acid (a polyphenol) chelates the Fe³⁺ ions, forming a **passive, insoluble, polymeric iron-tannate film** that is electrically inert and seals the surface.

### Mechanism B — Phosphoric Acid Reaction:
$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow 2\text{FePO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The resulting **iron phosphate (FePO₄)** is a hard, adherent, dark gray passivation layer suitable for paint adhesion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Limitation:** Phosphoric acid can over-etch, producing **iron phosphate + free phosphoric acid residue**, which is **hygroscopic** and can cause **flash rusting** if not properly rinsed or neutralized. Tannic converters are generally preferred in marine environments.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat input per unit length | $Q = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | J/mm | Welding/Flame thermal load |
| Thermal expansion strain | $\varepsilon = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | dimensionless | Spalling mechanics |
| Steam pressure inside rust pore | $P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$ | Pa | Explosion force calc |
| Surface preparation grade | SIS Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3 | — | Standard cleanliness |
| Coverage of rust converter | $C = \dfrac{A}{R}$ where $R \approx 8\text{–}12\,\text{m}^2/\text{L}$ | m²/L | Field planning |
| Reaction stoichiometry (tannin) | Fe₂O₃ : Tannin ≈ 1:2 by mass | kg/kg | Dosage design |
| Primer wet film thickness | $WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{\text{Solids\%}}$ | µm | Paint specification |
| Substrate temp post-flame | $T_s = T_{amb} + \Delta T \approx 100\text{–}150\,°\text{C}$ | °C | Re-priming window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - Flame cleaning is the **primary in-situ surface preparation** for **ship hull maintenance**, **bridge girders**, **offshore platforms**, and **storage tank exteriors** where abrasive blasting (sand/grit) is environmentally restricted or cost-prohibitive.
> - Rust converters are the **go-to solution for maintenance repainting** of **weathered handrails, deck fittings, ballast tank internals**, and **engine room structures** where surface preparation is logistically constrained.

## 2.5 Comparative Analysis — Flame Cleaning vs. Rust Converter

| Criterion | Flame Cleaning | Rust Converter |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Thermal shock + spalling | Chemical chelation / neutralization |
| Removes rust? | Yes (physically detaches) | No — converts it |
| Surface cleanliness achieved | SIS Sa 1 to Sa 2 | Equivalent to **St 2–St 3** (manual) |
| Ideal substrate thickness | > 5 mm (thin plates warp) | Any thickness |
| Power / equipment | Gas cylinders + torch | Brush, roller, or spray |
| Operator skill | High (welding-grade) | Low (painter-grade) |
| Cost per m² | Moderate–High | Low |
| Marine use case | Hull plating, deck plates | Internal fittings, handrails, voids |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Thermal Shock Spalling Force in Flame Cleaning

We derive the theoretical pressure generated inside a hydrated rust pore when subjected to the rapid heating of a flame-cleaning torch. This pressure is what physically ejects the rust flake.
