### Step 1: Define the System
Consider a hemispherical rust pore of radius $r = 50\,\mu\text{m} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$ filled with liquid water (from hydrated `FeO(OH)` and `Fe₂O₃·H₂O`).

### Step 2: Apply Ideal Gas Law for Vapor Generated
Upon heating from ambient $T_1 = 300\,\text{K}$ to flame-induced surface temperature $T_2 = 600\,\text{K}$ (well below the boiling point but sufficient to vaporize bound water), water vapor forms. Using ideal gas law:

$$
P V = n R T
$$

Solving for pressure inside the sealed pore:

$$
P = \dfrac{n R T}{V}
$$

### Step 3: Express Moles of Vapor
The mass of water in one pore (assuming fully liquid before vaporization):

$$
m = \rho_w \cdot V = 1000 \cdot \left(\dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3\right)
$$

Substituting $r = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$:

$$
V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi (50 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 5.236 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{m}^3
$$

$$
m = 1000 \times 5.236 \times 10^{-13} = 5.236 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{kg}
$$

Moles of water:

$$
n = \dfrac{m}{M_w} = \dfrac{5.236 \times 10^{-10}}{0.018} = 2.91 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{mol}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Peak Vapor Pressure
At $T_2 = 600\,\text{K}$, using $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$:

$$
P = \dfrac{(2.91 \times 10^{-8})(8.314)(600)}{5.236 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{1.452 \times 10^{-4}}{5.236 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 2.77 \times 10^{8}\,\text{Pa}
$$

### Step 5: Compare to Adhesive Strength of Rust
The adhesive strength of rust to steel is approximately $\sigma_a \approx 5 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} = 5\,\text{MPa}$.

$$
P_{\text{generated}} \approx 277\,\text{MPa} \;\gg\; \sigma_a \approx 5\,\text{MPa}
$$

### Step 6: Engineering Conclusion
$$
\text{Spalling Condition: } P_{\text{vapor}} > \sigma_{\text{adhesion}}
$$

Since the vapor pressure exceeds the adhesion strength by **a factor of ~55**, the rust layer **must** mechanically detach. This validates flame cleaning as a rigorous physical process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** This derivation is a high-scoring application problem worth **7 marks** in Part B. The key step students miss is converting mass to moles before applying the ideal gas law.

## 3.2 Derivation — Coverage and Cost Calculation for a Marine Bulkhead

A marine bulkhead of dimensions **10 m × 3 m = 30 m²** is to be treated with a rust converter at a spreading rate of **10 m²/L**.

### Step 1: Calculate Volume Required
$$
V_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{A}{R} = \dfrac{30\,\text{m}^2}{10\,\text{m}^2/\text{L}} = 3.0\,\text{L}
$$

### Step 2: Account for Waste Factor (15%)
$$
V_{\text{actual}} = V_{\text{required}} \times 1.15 = 3.0 \times 1.15 = 3.45\,\text{L}
$$

### Step 3: Cost Estimation (₹/L assumed)
At ₹ 850/L:
$$
\text{Cost} = 3.45 \times 850 = ₹ 2{,}932.5
$$

### Step 4: Coating Schedule
$$
\text{Application: 1 coat} \rightarrow \text{Drying: 4 hours} \rightarrow \text{Overcoat primer: as per DFT}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Flame Cleaning | Rust Converter | Sand Blasting |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface cleanliness (ISO 8501-1) | Sa 1 to Sa 2 | St 2 to St 3 | Sa 2½ to Sa 3 |
| Cost per m² (INR, indicative) | ₹ 60–90 | ₹ 25–40 | ₹ 80–120 |
| Time per m² | 1–2 min | 3–5 min (incl. reaction) | 4–8 min |
| Skill level | Skilled tradesman | Semi-skilled | Skilled + safety gear |
| Marine environment suitability | High (open deck) | High (enclosed, complex) | Limited (dust hazard) |
| Re-priming window | < 30 min | 4–24 hr | 1–4 hr |
| Waste generation | Negligible | Low | High (spent abrasive) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Flame Cleaning — Process Flow Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steel Substrate Identification] --> B{Thickness Check}
    B -->|> 5 mm| C[Equipment Setup: O2 and C2H2 cylinders]
    B -->|< 5 mm| B1[Reject: Risk of distortion]
    C --> D[Pressure Regulation: O2 1.0 bar, C2H2 0.5 bar]
    D --> E[Multi-flame nozzle attachment 3 or 5 orifice]
    E --> F[Neutral flame adjustment]
    F --> G[Flame pass at 45 degree angle, 6 to 12 mm standoff]
    G --> H[Thermal shock: Hydrated rust dehydrates]
    H --> I[Steam pressure builds in pores]
    I --> J[Rust spalling: Flakes detach]
    J --> K[Wire brushing for residual removal]
    K --> L[Warm window check: 40 to 80 degree C]
    L --> M[Apply primer coat immediately]
    M --> N[Quality inspection: SIS Sa 2]
    N --> O{Acceptable?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Schedule top coat application]
    O -->|No| Q[Repeat flame pass on missed areas]
    Q --> G
```

## 4.2 Rust Converter — Chemical Reaction & Application Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Rusted Steel Surface Fe2O3 dot nH2O] --> R2{Surface Condition}
    R2 -->|Loose flaky rust| R3[Mechanical removal mandatory first]
    R2 -->|Adherent rust| R4[Clean with wire brush to St 2]
    R4 --> R5[Apply rust converter by brush, roller, or spray]
    R5 --> R6[Active chemical reaction begins]
    R6 --> R7A[Tannin based: Fe tannate complex forms]
    R6 --> R7B[Phosphoric based: Iron phosphate FePO4 forms]
    R7A --> R8[Blue-black passive layer]
    R7B --> R9[Dark gray passive layer]
    R8 --> R10[Drying period: 2 to 6 hours]
    R9 --> R10
    R10 --> R11[Apply compatible primer]
    R11 --> R12[Apply top coat]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Surface Preparation Required] --> Q1{Substrate Location}
    Q1 -->|Open deck, hull exterior| Q2{Heavy mill scale or rust}
    Q1 -->|Enclosed void, engine room| Q3{Logistics allow blasting}
    Q1 -->|Maintenance touch-up| Q4{Budget and time constraint}

    Q2 -->|Yes| PathA[Use Flame Cleaning + Wire Brush]
    Q2 -->|Light| PathB[Use Flame Cleaning alone]
    Q3 -->|Yes| PathC[Sand or grit blasting]
    Q3 -->|No| PathD[Rust Converter + Manual Prep]
    Q4 -->|Tight| PathE[Rust Converter Application]
    Q4 -->|Flexible| PathF[Full Flame or Blast Cleaning]

    PathA --> End[Follow up with primer]
    PathB --> End
    PathC --> End
    PathD --> End
    PathE --> End
    PathF --> End
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Marine Corrosion Protection Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopCoat[Top Coat Layer]
        TC[Marine grade polyurethane or epoxy finish]
    end
    subgraph MidCoat[Intermediate Coat]
        MC[High build epoxy primer 100 to 150 micron DFT]
    end
    subgraph Primer[First Coat Primer]
        PC[Anti corrosive zinc rich or epoxy primer 50 to 75 micron DFT]
    end
    subgraph Prep[Prepared Surface]
        PS1[Flame cleaned steel at SIS Sa 2]
        PS2[Rust converter treated surface with Fe tannate layer]
    end
    subgraph Base[Substrate]
        BS[Carbon steel or mild steel marine structure]
    end

    BS --> PS1
    BS --> PS2
    PS1 --> PC
    PS2 --> PC
    PC --> MC
    MC --> TC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Always trace the substrate upwards through preparation → primer → mid-coat → top coat. The corrosion protection effectiveness is **only as strong as the weakest (bottom) layer** — a principle called the **"weakest link"** rule in marine coating engineering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define flame cleaning. List any three advantages of flame cleaning over other surface preparation methods.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Flame cleaning is a thermal surface preparation method in which an oxy-acetylene flame is passed over a steel surface to remove rust, mill scale, moisture, oil, and grease by virtue of thermal shock and combustion. The high temperature (≈ 3,000 °C) dehydrates hydrated iron oxides, generating steam pressure that causes rust to spall off.

**Any Three Advantages (1 Mark):**
1. Removes oil, grease, and moisture simultaneously along with rust.
2. Leaves the surface warm and dry, ideal for immediate primer application.
3. No abrasive dust generation — environmentally friendly for in-situ work.
4. Portable equipment suitable for shipyards and offshore platforms.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is a rust converter? Write the chemical reaction involved in tannin-based rust conversion.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** A rust converter is a chemical formulation (typically containing tannic acid or phosphoric acid) that reacts with iron oxide (rust) on a steel surface and transforms it into a stable, passive, paintable compound (iron tannate or iron phosphate) without requiring complete mechanical removal of rust.

**Reaction (1 Mark):**
$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Tannic Acid} \longrightarrow \text{Iron Tannate (stable, blue-black, passive layer)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the principle, procedure, and applications of flame cleaning in marine surface preparation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the mechanism and limitations of rust converters with neat chemical reactions. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Principle (2 Marks):**
The principle of flame cleaning is **thermal shock** applied via an oxy-acetylene flame at approximately **3,000–3,200 °C**. When this flame sweeps over rusted steel, the hydrated rust compounds (`FeO(OH)`, `Fe₂O₃·H₂O`) are instantaneously heated. The bound water vaporizes, and the resulting steam pressure inside the rust layer exceeds the adhesive strength between rust and base metal. This causes the rust to **spall (flake off) explosively**. Simultaneously, oil, grease, and surface contaminants are incinerated.

**2. Procedure (3 Marks):**
1. Identify substrate and confirm thickness > 5 mm to avoid distortion.
2. Set up oxy-acetylene equipment: O₂ at **0.7–1.4 bar**, C₂H₂ at **0.3–0.7 bar**.
3. Attach multi-flame nozzle (3, 5, or 7 orifice heads).
4. Adjust to neutral flame; sweep at **45° angle**, **6–12 mm standoff**, at **3–6 m/min** travel speed.
5. Wire brush immediately to remove loosened rust flakes.
6. Apply anti-corrosive primer within the **warm window (40–80 °C)** while the surface is still receptive.

**3. Applications (2 Marks):**
- Ship hull maintenance and repainting.
- Offshore platform structural members.
- Bridge girders and storage tank exteriors.
- Structural steel in shipyards where abrasive blasting is restricted.

---

#### Model Answer for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Mechanism of Rust Converters (3 Marks):**
Rust converters operate by **chemical transformation** of unstable ferric oxide into a stable, adherent protective film. There are two main chemistries:
- **Tannin-based:** Tannic acid (a polyphenol) chelates Fe³⁺ ions, forming **iron tannate**, a blue-black, insoluble, polymeric complex.
- **Phosphate-based:** Phosphoric acid reacts with Fe₂O₃ to form **iron phosphate (FePO₄)**, a dark gray passivation layer.

**2. Chemical Reactions (2 Marks):**

Tannin route:
$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Tannic Acid} \longrightarrow \text{Fe–Tannate complex} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Phosphate route:
$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \longrightarrow 2\text{FePO}_4 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**3. Limitations (2 Marks):**
1. Only effective on **adherent rust**; loose, flaky rust must first be mechanically removed.
2. Phosphate-based converters can leave **hygroscopic residues** causing flash rusting if not neutralized.
3. Cannot replace full surface preparation for critical/high-spec coating systems.
4. Reaction efficiency decreases at temperatures below **10 °C** or above **35 °C**.

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Compare flame cleaning, sand blasting, and rust converters as methods of marine surface preparation. Highlight the suitability of each for shipboard applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A marine bulkhead of dimensions 12 m × 4 m is to be treated with a tannin-based rust converter having a spreading rate of 10 m²/L. Calculate the volume of converter required, including a 15% wastage factor. If the converter costs ₹ 900/L, determine the total material cost. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | Flame Cleaning | Sand Blasting | Rust Converter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Thermal spalling | Abrasive impact | Chemical chelation |
| Cleanliness achieved | SIS Sa 1–Sa 2 | SIS Sa 2½–Sa 3 | St 2–St 3 |
| Cost (₹/m²) | 60–90 | 80–120 | 25–40 |
| Skill level | Skilled | Skilled + safety | Semi-skilled |
| Dust/waste | Negligible | High (spent abrasive) | Negligible |
| Marine suitability | Hull exterior, deck | New construction (dry dock) | Engine room, handrails, voids |

**Suitability Summary (2 Marks):**
- **Flame cleaning** is best suited for **open-deck ship hull plating** and offshore members.
- **Sand blasting** is preferred in **dry-dock new construction** where high cleanliness (Sa 2½) is required.
- **Rust converters** are ideal for **in-situ maintenance** of complex geometries like handrails, ladder rungs, and engine-room piping.

---

#### Model Answer for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Area $A = 12 \times 4 = 48\,\text{m}^2$
- Spreading rate $R = 10\,\text{m}^2/\text{L}$
- Wastage factor $W = 15\% = 0.15$
- Cost per litre $C = ₹ 900/\text{L}$

**Step 1: Volume without wastage (2 Marks):**
$$
V_1 = \dfrac{A}{R} = \dfrac{48}{10} = 4.8\,\text{L}
$$

**Step 2: Add 15% wastage (2 Marks):**
$$
V_{\text{actual}} = V_1 \times (1 + 0.15) = 4.8 \times 1.15 = 5.52\,\text{L}
$$

**Step 3: Total cost (2 Marks):**
$$
\text{Total Cost} = V_{\text{actual}} \times C = 5.52 \times 900 = ₹ 4{,}968
$$

**Step 4: Final Answer (1 Mark):**
$$
\boxed{V_{\text{actual}} = 5.52\,\text{L}, \quad \text{Total Cost} = ₹ 4{,}968}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing flame cleaning with flame straightening:** Students often mix up surface preparation flame cleaning with the structural flame-straightening technique — these are **different applications**.
> 2. **Writing "rust converter removes rust":** Incorrect. Rust converters **convert** rust; they do **not remove** it. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this conceptual error.
> 3. **Missing the "warm window" concept:** In flame cleaning questions, always mention the **40–80 °C** priming window — forgetting this loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Unit errors in coverage calculation:** Always express spreading rate in **m²/L** consistently; mixing m²/kg without solids content causes **mark deductions**.
> 5. **Forgetting the wastage factor:** In numerical problems, including wastage is mandatory — a **5–15% wastage factor** is the KTU board standard.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — High-Density Summary:**

- **Flame cleaning** uses an **oxy-acetylene flame at ~3,000 °C** to remove rust by **thermal shock and spalling**.
- The dominant spalling mechanism is **steam pressure** from dehydrated rust, **not** mere differential expansion.
- Flame cleaning achieves **SIS Sa 1 to Sa 2** surface cleanliness.
- The **45° angle** flame pass at **6–12 mm standoff** and **3–6 m/min** speed are critical parameters.
- Priming must occur in the **warm window (40–80 °C)** within minutes of flame cleaning.
- **Rust converters** are chemical treatments that **convert** rust, not remove it.
- **Tannin-based converters** form **iron tannate** (blue-black, passive).
- **Phosphate-based converters** form **iron phosphate (FePO₄)** (dark gray).
- Rust converters are effective only on **adherent rust** — loose flakes must be mechanically removed first.
- Coverage rate for rust converters: **8–12 m²/L** typical.
- Standard waste factor for marine painting calculations: **15%**.
- Substrate temperature for rust converter application: **10–35 °C**.
- Drying time before overcoating: **2–6 hours**.
- Flame cleaning is preferred for **open-deck hull plating**; rust converters for **enclosed compartments and complex geometry**.
- Always remember the **weakest-link rule** in coating systems — surface preparation is the foundation.
- ISO 8501-1 cleanliness grades for reference: **Sa 1 < Sa 2 < Sa 2½ < Sa 3** (increasing cleanliness).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Pre-Treatment and Comparison of Pre-Treatment Methods: Paint Schemes in Ships and Marine Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chemical Pre-Treatment and Comparison of Pre-Treatment Methods: Paint Schemes in Ships and Marine Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Chemical Pre-Treatment — Formal Definition

**Chemical Pre-Treatment** in marine corrosion protection refers to the sequence of controlled chemical reactions applied to a cleaned steel or alloy surface to convert the outermost metal layer into a stable, adherent, and corrosion-resistant inorganic compound layer. This layer acts as a *chemical bridge* between the substrate and the subsequent organic coating (primer), dramatically improving adhesion, reducing ionic conductivity at the metal-coating interface, and extending service life of the paint system.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the standard sequence is:

> **Degreasing → Rinsing → Acid Pickling/Descaling → Rinsing → Surface Conditioning (Phosphating / Chromating / Wash Primer) → Drying → Primer Application**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pre-treatment is graded on a **Sa 2½ to Sa 3** (ISO 8501-1) standard for marine new-building, and the chemical pre-treatment step must produce a surface that is *chemically active*, *water-break-free*, and within a surface profile of **40–85 μm** (typically Rz value) for optimum mechanical interlocking of the primer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Foundation Primer" View

Think of painting a rusty old steel ship like **waterproofing a brick wall before painting your house**. If you paint directly on dusty, oily, rusted bricks, the paint peels off in weeks. But if you first:
1. Wash off the oil (degreasing),
2. Scrub off the rust (pickling), and
3. Apply a *bonding liquid* (phosphate/chromate conversion coating) —

the paint now has a *rough, clean, chemically active base* to grip onto. **Chemical pre-treatment is exactly this "bonding liquid" stage** in marine corrosion protection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The conversion coating formed is typically only **0.5 g/m² to 3.0 g/m²** of coating mass, yet it is responsible for **up to 40%** of the long-term coating performance in marine atmospheres.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Pre-Treatment

- **Surface Profile (Rz):** Measured in **μm** (microns); standard marine range is **40–85 μm**.
- **Surface Cleanliness:** Per **ISO 8501-1** (Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3) and **ISO 8501-2** (St 2, St 3).
- **Surface Roughness:** Ra in **μm**.
- **Coating Thickness:** DFT (Dry Film Thickness) in **μm** or **mils**.
- **Pull-off Adhesion Strength:** Measured in **MPa** (typically ≥ **5 MPa** for marine primers).
- **Salt Contamination on Surface:** Max acceptable **50 mg/m² NaCl** equivalent (for IMO PSPC compliance).
- **Dew Point margin:** Substrate temperature must be ≥ **3 °C above dew point** during application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Salinity Limit Rule (IMO PSPC):** For ballast tank coatings under IMO Resolution **MSC.215(82)**, the soluble salt limit on prepared steel is **≤ 50 mg/m²** measured per **ISO 8502-6 / 8502-9**. Exceeding this is the #1 cause of premature coating failure in new ships.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Canonical Steps of Marine Chemical Pre-Treatment

**Step 1 — Degreasing (Solvent or Alkaline)**
- Removes oils, greases, cutting fluids, waxes, marking inks.
- Solvent degreasing: white spirit, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK).
- Alkaline degreasing: NaOH (50–100 g/L), Na₂CO₃, Na₃PO₄, surfactants at **60–80 °C**.
- Test cleanliness: **Water-Break-Free Test** — water sheets evenly without beading.

**Step 2 — Rinsing**
- Fresh water rinse to remove alkaline residues.
- Conductivity of final rinse should be **< 50 μS/cm** to avoid salt contamination.

**Step 3 — Acid Pickling / Descaling**
- Removes mill scale (FeO, Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄), rust (Fe(OH)₃ · nH₂O), and welding oxides.
- Acid used: **H₂SO₄ (5–15%)** or **HCl (5–10%)** with **0.1–0.3% pickling inhibitor** (e.g., Rodine).
- Typical bath temperature: **40–60 °C**, immersion time: **15–45 minutes**.

**Step 4 — Surface Conditioning (Conversion Coating)**
Three principal chemical routes:
- **Iron Phosphating:** Fe + H₃PO₄ → FePO₄ layer (~0.5 g/m²).
- **Zinc Phosphating:** Zn + Fe + H₃PO₄ → hopeite (Zn₃(PO₄)₂·4H₂O) layer (~1.5–3 g/m²).
- **Chromating (Chromate Conversion Coating):** CrO₃ + H₂SO₄ → Cr₂O₃ / Cr(OH)₃ mixed layer (1–3 g/m²).
- **Wash Primer (Etch Primer):** Polyvinyl butyral (PVB) + ZnCrO₄ + H₃PO₄ → very thin (8–15 μm) reactive primer.

**Step 5 — Drying and Primer Application**
- Forced air drying at **40–60 °C** or ambient.
- Apply first coat of anti-corrosive primer within **4 hours** to avoid re-oxidation ("rust bloom" or "flash rust").

### 2.2 Mechanism of Chemical Conversion Coating

For **zinc phosphating on steel**, the dominant reaction in the cathodic region:

$$\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^- + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot 4\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\,\text{H}^+}$$

For the anodic iron dissolution supporting the above:

$$\text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$$

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^- \longrightarrow \text{FePO}_4 + \text{H}^+ + e^-$$

For **chromate conversion coating**:

$$\text{CrO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{CrOHSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{CrOHSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Cr(OH)}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$

The deposited mixed Cr³⁺/Cr⁶⁺ oxide-hydroxide layer is **self-healing** — dissolved Cr⁶⁺ migrates to a scratch and re-passivates the exposed metal.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Data Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula or Value | Units | Remarks / Conditions |
|---|--------------------|------------------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | Theoretical Coverage | $C = \dfrac{V_s \times 10}{\text{DFT}}$ | m²/L | $V_s$ = volume solids (%) ; DFT in μm |
| 2 | Wet Film Thickness from DFT | $\text{WFT} = \dfrac{\text{DFT}}{V_s}$ | μm | Inverse of coverage formula |
| 3 | Practical Spreading Rate | $C_p = C \times \eta$ | m²/L | $\eta$ = transfer efficiency (0.50–0.85 for airless spray) |
| 4 | Coating Thickness Loss per Year | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 \times \Delta m}{\rho \times A \times t}$ | mm/year | $\Delta m$ in mg, $\rho$ in g/cm³, $A$ in cm², $t$ in hours |
| 5 | Salt Contamination Limit | $\le 50$ | mg/m² | IMO PSPC for ballast tanks |
| 6 | Dew Point Margin | $T_{sub} - T_{dp} \ge 3$ | °C | Mandatory during coating application |
| 7 | Surface Profile (Blast) | $R_z = 40 - 85$ | μm | ISO 8503-5, marine primer standard |
| 8 | Adhesion Strength | $\ge 5$ | MPa | Pull-off test per ASTM D4541 |
| 9 | Phosphate Coating Mass | $1.5 - 3.0$ | g/m² | Zinc phosphating on steel |
| 10 | Chromate Coating Mass | $1.0 - 3.0$ | g/m² | Chromate conversion on Al/Zn |
| 11 | Pickling Acid Temperature | $40 - 60$ | °C | H₂SO₄ bath optimum |
| 12 | Blast Air Pressure | $0.6 - 0.8$ | MPa | Nozzle pressure for abrasive blasting |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyards:** Pre-treatment booths in panel lines run continuously; throughput is **~50–80 m²/hour** for automated blast + spray pre-treatment.
- **Offshore platforms:** Chemical pre-treatment is mandatory before applying **glass flake vinyl ester** or **noble-cathode epoxy**.
- **Submarines:** Use **zinc-rich epoxy + tie coat + anti-fouling** scheme on hull, with chemical pre-treatment ensuring **>15-year** service life.
- **Desalination plants:** Phosphate pre-treatment reduces under-deposit corrosion of Cu-Ni tubes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Comparative Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Coating Coverage from Volume Solids

The **theoretical coverage** of a paint is derived from the principle of volume conservation — when the solvent evaporates, only the solids remain as the dry film.

**Start:** Volume of liquid paint applied over area $A$ is $V_L$.

$$
V_L = A \times \text{WFT}
$$

**Step 1:** Volume of dry film remaining after solvent loss:

$$
V_D = V_L \times V_s
$$

where $V_s$ is the volume fraction of solids (e.g., 0.55 for 55% solids).

**Step 2:** Equate with $V_D = A \times \text{DFT}$:

$$
A \times \text{DFT} = A \times \text{WFT} \times V_s
$$

**Step 3:** Coverage $C$ is area per unit volume $V_L$:

$$
C = \dfrac{A}{V_L} = \dfrac{1}{\text{WFT}} = \dfrac{V_s}{\text{DFT}}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert DFT in μm to m (multiply by $10^{-6}$) and use $V_s$ in percent:

$$
C \,\left[\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{L}}\right] = \dfrac{V_s \,\text{[%]} \times 10}{\text{DFT}\,[\mu\text{m}]}
$$

**Step 5:** Practical coverage accounts for transfer losses:

$$
C_p = \dfrac{10 \times V_s \times \eta}{\text{DFT}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is heavily tested in KTU ESE numericals. Given a paint with **V_s = 65%**, **DFT = 125 μm**, and airless spray transfer efficiency **η = 0.75**, compute the practical coverage.

$$
C_p = \dfrac{10 \times 65 \times 0.75}{125} = \dfrac{487.5}{125} = 3.9 \,\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{L}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Hull Area Paint Quantity Calculation

**Problem:** A Bulk Carrier has a wetted underwater hull area of **6,500 m²**. The anti-corrosive primer requires a DFT of **150 μm** with two coats of 75 μm each, using an epoxy with **V_s = 60%** and spray transfer efficiency **η = 0.70**.

**Step 1 — Theoretical coverage per coat:**

$$
C = \dfrac{60 \times 10}{75} = 8.0 \,\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{L}}
$$

**Step 2 — Practical coverage per coat:**

$$
C_p = 8.0 \times 0.70 = 5.6 \,\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{L}}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume of paint per coat:**

$$
V_{coat} = \dfrac{6500}{5.6} = 1160.7 \,\text{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Volume for two coats:**

$$
V_{total} = 2 \times 1160.7 = 2321.4 \,\text{L} \approx 2322 \,\text{L}
$$

**Step 5 — Add 10% wastage factor for shipyard practicals:**

$$
V_{final} = 2322 \times 1.10 = 2554 \,\text{L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuator's Comment:** Many students forget to multiply by transfer efficiency $\eta$. Always write the three-line logic: *DFT → Coverage → Practical Coverage → Volume*. Marks are split across these steps.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Pre-Treatment Methods

| Property | Solvent Cleaning | Alkaline Cleaning | Acid Pickling | Phosphate Coating | Chromate Coating | Wash Primer |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|---------------|-------------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Primary Function** | Oil/grease removal | Oil + light soil removal | Mill scale + rust removal | Conversion coating | Conversion coating (passive) | Reactive bonding layer |
| **Substrate** | All metals | Steel, Al, Zn | Steel (mainly) | Steel, Zn, Al | Al, Zn, Mg, Cd | Steel, Al, Galvanized |
| **Layer Mass** | None | None | None | 1.5–3 g/m² | 1–3 g/m² | 8–15 μm film |
| **Film Thickness** | None | None | None | 5–25 μm | 0.5–3 μm | 8–15 μm |
| **Corrosion Resistance (hrs salt spray)** | < 24 | < 24 | < 48 | 100–300 | 500–1000 | 200–500 |
| **Adhesion Improvement** | None | Slight | Slight | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Re-coat Window** | Immediate | Immediate | < 4 hr | Up to 30 days | Up to 14 days | 24–48 hr |
| **Environmental Hazard** | VOC emission | High pH effluent | Acid effluent + H₂ | Moderate phosphates | **Toxic Cr⁶⁺** | VOC + Cr⁶⁺ |
| **Cost Index (relative)** | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| **Toxicity Concern** | Low–Medium | Low | Medium | Low | **High** (RoHS-regulated) | Medium |
| **Typical Marine Use** | Pre-degrease | Pre-degrease | Repair/old steel | New-build primer base | Al superstructure | Tie-coat between Zn-rich + epoxy |

### 3.4 Comparison: Mechanical vs Chemical Pre-Treatment

| Feature | Mechanical (Blast / Tool) | Chemical (Pickling / Conversion) |
|---------|---------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Surface Profile | High (Rz 40–85 μm) | Low (Rz < 10 μm) |
| Removes Mill Scale | Yes | Yes (pickling) |
| Removes Salt | No (can spread) | Yes (with detergent + rinse) |
| Cost | Moderate–High | Moderate |
| Speed | Fast (large areas) | Slow (immersion) |
| Hydrogen Embrittlement Risk | None | Possible (pickling) |
| Environmental | Dust | Acid/Chromate effluent |
| Marine Suitability | **Excellent** | **Excellent (combo)** |
| Best Practice | Mechanical → Chemical | Combined blast + chemical |

### 3.5 Phosphate vs Chromate — Toxicity Trade-off

> [!WARNING]
> **RoHS & REACH Compliance:** Cr⁶⁺ (hexavalent chromium) is a **Category 1A carcinogen** under EU CLP Regulation. Chromate conversion coatings are **banned** in many shipyards from 2025 onwards under the *EU Ship Recycling Regulation*. **Trivalent chromium (Cr³⁺)** and **zirconium/titanium-based conversion coatings** are the KTU-2024 recommended replacements.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Marine Chemical Pre-Treatment Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Steel Plate Received] --> A1[Pre-Inspection: Salt, Oil, Damage]
    A1 --> A2{Degreasing Method?}
    A2 -->|Solvent| A3a[Solvent Wipe / Vapor Degrease]
    A2 -->|Alkaline| A3b[Alkaline Dip 60-80 C]
    A3a --> A4
    A3b --> A4[Fresh Water Rinse]
    A4 --> A5[Acid Pickling H2SO4 or HCl 40-60 C]
    A5 --> A6[Second Fresh Water Rinse]
    A6 --> A7{Conversion Coating Type?}
    A7 -->|Iron Phosphate| A8a[Iron Phosphating Bath]
    A7 -->|Zinc Phosphate| A8b[Zinc Phosphating Bath]
    A7 -->|Chromate| A8c[Chromate Bath CrO3]
    A7 -->|Wash Primer| A8d[PVB-ZnCrO4 Spray 8-15 um]
    A8a --> A9[Final Rinse and Dry 40-60 C]
    A8b --> A9
    A8c --> A9
    A8d --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Quality Check: Water Break, DFT, Salt]
    A10 -->|Pass| A11[Primer Application within 4 hours]
    A10 -->|Fail| A12[Re-process from Step 1]
    A11 --> A13[Continue Paint Scheme]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Standard Paint Scheme for a Ship Hull

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["Zone 1: Underwater Hull Below Waterline"]
        P1a[Substrate: Steel Sa 2.5] --> P1b[Shop Primer 15-20 um Epoxy Zinc]
        P1b --> P1c[AC Primer 125 um Epoxy]
        P1c --> P1d[Tie Coat 50 um]
        P1d --> P1e[Anti-Fouling 250-400 um SPCAF or FRCF]
    end
    subgraph S2["Zone 2: Boot Top Area Waterline Plus Minus 1 m"]
        P2a[Substrate: Steel Sa 2.5] --> P2b[Shop Primer 15-20 um]
        P2b --> P2c[AC Primer 125 um Epoxy]
        P2c --> P2d[Boot Top Paint 60 um Alkyd or PU]
    end
    subgraph S3["Zone 3: Topside and Superstructure"]
        P3a[Substrate: Steel Sa 2.5] --> P3b[Shop Primer 15-20 um]
        P3b --> P3c[AC Primer 100 um Epoxy]
        P3c --> P3d[PU Finish 50 um]
    end
    subgraph S4["Zone 4: Ballast Tank IMO PSPC"]
        P4a[Substrate Sa 2.5 salt 50 mg per sqm] --> P4b[Epoxy Shop Primer 20 um]
        P4b --> P4c[Epoxy AC 160 um in one or two coats]
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Decision Matrix for Selecting Pre-Treatment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Substrate Type?} -->|Carbon Steel| Q2{New or Old Steel?}
    Q1 -->|Aluminum| Q3[Chromate or Zr-Ti Conversion]
    Q1 -->|Galvanized| Q4[Wash Primer PVB Type]
    Q2 -->|New| Q5[Blast Sa 2.5 plus Iron Phosphate]
    Q2 -->|Old Rusted| Q6[Pickle plus Zinc Phosphate]
    Q2 -->|Repair| Q7[Local Blast plus Spot Primer]
    Q5 --> R1[Standard Epoxy Scheme]
    Q6 --> R1
    Q3 --> R2[Al Specific Epoxy Scheme]
    Q4 --> R3[Galvanized Compatible Primer]
    Q7 --> R4[Repair Coat Scheme]
```

### 4.4 Antifouling Paint Scheme Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AF1[Steel Hull Substrate] --> AF2[Surface Prep Sa 2.5]
    AF2 --> AF3[Anti-Corrosive Primer 125 um Epoxy]
    AF3 --> AF4[Tie Coat 50 um Vinyl Epoxy]
    AF4 --> AF5{Antifouling Type}
    AF5 -->|SPCAF| AF6a[Self-Polishing 3 to 5 um per day]
    AF5 -->|FRCF| AF6b[Foul Release Silicone 150 um]
    AF5 -->|CDP| AF6c[Controlled Depletion Polymer]
    AF6a --> AF7[Topside Boot Top Up]
    AF6b --> AF7
    AF6c --> AF7
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** What is meant by chemical pre-treatment in marine coating systems? List any three chemical pre-treatment methods with one advantage each.

**Model Answer:**
Chemical pre-treatment refers to the application of controlled chemical reactions on a cleaned metal surface to form a stable, adherent, inorganic conversion layer that improves coating adhesion and corrosion resistance.
- **Acid pickling:** Removes mill scale and rust, leaving a chemically active surface.
- **Phosphating:** Forms crystalline Zn₃(PO₄)₂ coating that bonds with primer.
- **Chromating:** Provides self-healing Cr³⁺/Cr⁶⁺ passive film (now restricted).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Why is wash primer used as a first coat on galvanized and aluminum surfaces in marine applications?

**Model Answer:**
Wash primer (etch primer) is a single-pack primer containing **polyvinyl butyral resin + zinc chromate + phosphoric acid**. It etches the zinc/aluminum oxide layer and simultaneously deposits chromate inhibitor, providing:
1. Excellent adhesion on non-ferrous metals.
2. Chemical bonding with subsequent epoxy primers.
3. A thin (8–15 μm) but chemically active barrier.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches and chemical reactions, explain the chemical pre-treatment sequence adopted for the underwater hull of a new ship.

**Model Solution:**
The underwater hull (IMO PSPC WBT and IACS UR Z17) undergoes:

**Step 1 — Degreasing:** Alkaline degreasing using NaOH (50–100 g/L) + Na₃PO₄ + surfactants at **60–80 °C** for 10–20 min to remove oils and marking inks.

**Step 2 — Rinse:** Fresh water rinse until conductivity **< 50 μS/cm**.

**Step 3 — Abrasive Blasting:** Dry abrasive blast to **ISO 8501-1 Sa 2½**, profile **Rz 50–85 μm**, using garnet or steel grit at **0.6–0.8 MPa** nozzle pressure.

**Step 4 — Dust Removal:** Vacuum or oil-free compressed air.

**Step 5 — Salt Test:** Bresle patch test per **ISO 8502-6**, max **50 mg/m² NaCl**.

**Step 6 — Shop Primer:** Apply zinc-rich epoxy shop primer (**15–20 μm DFT**) within 4 hours.

**Key Reactions during blasting and pre-treatment:**

$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\,\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\,\text{Fe}^{3+} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Fe} + \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^- + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \cdot 4\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\,\text{H}^+$$

*(Diagram: Standard ISO 8501-1 visual comparison chart with Sa 1, Sa 2, Sa 2½, Sa 3 grades.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare mechanical pre-treatment (abrasive blasting) and chemical pre-treatment (acid pickling) for marine applications. State when each is preferred.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Abrasive Blasting | Acid Pickling |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Mechanism** | Mechanical impact (kinetic) | Chemical dissolution |
| **Surface Profile** | Rz 40–85 μm | < 10 μm |
| **Mill Scale Removal** | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Hydrogen Embrittlement** | None | Possible (risk in high-strength steel) |
| **Effluent** | Dust (collectable) | Acid + heavy metals |
| **Cost** | High (energy) | Moderate |
| **Speed** | High | Low |
| **Best Use** | New-building hulls, large areas | Repair, complex shapes, internal tanks |

**Preferred Applications:**
- **Blasting preferred:** New ship hulls, offshore platforms, all large steel surfaces in shipyards.
- **Pickling preferred:** Internal tank corners, repair work, galvanized surfaces, and stainless steel.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the standard paint scheme for (i) underwater hull, (ii) topside, and (iii) ballast tank of a new bulk carrier. Mention the role of each layer.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Underwater Hull Paint Scheme:**
- **Substrate prep:** Blast to Sa 2½, profile Rz 50–85 μm.
- **Shop primer:** Zinc-rich epoxy, **15–20 μm DFT** — sacrificial cathodic protection.
- **Anti-corrosive (AC) primer:** Pure epoxy, **125 μm DFT** — main moisture/oxygen barrier.
- **Tie coat:** Vinyl epoxy, **50 μm DFT** — adhesion between epoxy and AF.
- **Anti-fouling (AF):** SPCAF or FRCF, **250–400 μm DFT** — biocide release to prevent marine growth.

**(ii) Topside Paint Scheme:**
- **Substrate prep:** Blast to Sa 2½.
- **Shop primer:** Zinc-rich epoxy, 15–20 μm.
- **Anti-corrosive primer:** Epoxy, **100 μm DFT**.
- **Polyurethane finish:** Aliphatic PU, **50 μm DFT** — UV resistance, color, gloss.

**(iii) Ballast Tank Paint Scheme (per IMO PSPC Resolution MSC.215(82)):**
- **Substrate prep:** Blast to Sa 2½, salt ≤ 50 mg/m².
- **Shop primer:** Epoxy, **20 μm DFT**.
- **Main AC coating:** Solvent-free epoxy, **160 μm DFT**, applied in **one or two coats** with stripe coats on edges.

*(Diagram: Cross-section of hull showing layers.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the role of anti-fouling paints? Compare **SPCAF** (Self-Polishing Copolymer Anti-Fouling) and **FRCF** (Foul Release Coating) with examples.

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Anti-Fouling Paints:** Prevent settlement and growth of marine organisms (barnacles, algae, mussels) on the hull, reducing frictional drag, fuel consumption (5–25% saving), and biofouling-induced corrosion.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | SPCAF (e.g., Intersleek 1100SR, Hempasil) | FRCF (e.g., Intersleek 900, Hempasil X3+) |
|----------|-------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Active Agent** | Biocide (Cu₂O, CuSCN, Sea-Nine 211) | Silicone oil / fluoropolymer release |
| **Mechanism** | Controlled hydrolysis of copolymer, biocide release | Low surface energy → fouling sloughs off at speed |
| **Speed Required** | Works at all speeds | Requires ≥ 8 knots for fouling release |
| **Service Life** | 3–5 years | 5–10 years |
| **Dry-dock Interval** | 3–5 yrs | 5–10 yrs |
| **Biocide Content** | High (regulatory concern) | **Zero biocide** |
| **Friction Coefficient** | 0.10–0.15 | 0.05–0.08 (lowest) |
| **Best Suited for** | Slow-steaming vessels, coastal trade | Fast vessels, naval ships, eco-zones |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "anti-fouling paints kill barnacles" — this is partially correct only for SPCAF/CDP. For **FRCF**, the action is **physical release** (sloughing), **not biocide kill**. Also, FRCF requires a **tie coat** of vinyl epoxy between epoxy primer and silicone — students often omit this layer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pre-treatment is graded on ISO 8501-1 Sa 2½** for marine new-build steel.
- **Surface profile Rz = 40–85 μm** is the optimum for marine primers.
- **Salt contamination limit:** ≤ **50 mg/m²** (IMO PSPC, ballast tanks).
- **Dew point margin:** Substrate ≥ **3 °C** above dew point during coating application.
- **Coverage formula:** $C = \dfrac{V_s \times 10}{\text{DFT}}$ in m²/L.
- **Practical coverage:** Multiply by transfer efficiency **η = 0.5–0.85** for airless spray.
- **Phosphating types:** Iron, Zinc (best), Manganese.
- **Chromate coatings:** Self-healing but **RoHS-restricted**; use **Zr-Ti** or **Cr³⁺** as alternatives.
- **Wash primer (PVB):** Mandatory on galvanized and aluminum surfaces.
- **Hull paint scheme:** Shop primer → AC primer → Tie coat → Anti-fouling.
- **SPCAF** uses biocides (Cu₂O + booster); **FRCF** uses silicone/fluoro release, no biocide.
- **Adhesion pull-off strength** for marine primer: **≥ 5 MPa** (ASTM D4541).
- **Pickling acid** H₂SO₄ at **40–60 °C**, inhibitor at **0.1–0.3%** to avoid over-pickling.
- **Anti-fouling thickness** ranges **250–400 μm** depending on trade pattern.
- **Modern eco-restrictions:** Cr⁶⁺ banned in EU shipyards; biocide-free FRCF preferred in IMO Tier III areas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Paints: Common Paint Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of Paints: Common Paint Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Paint and Paint Vehicle

A **paint** is a homogeneous, fluid mixture of a **pigment** (finely divided solid particles that provide colour, opacity, and corrosion protection), a **vehicle** (the liquid binder that holds the pigment together and adheres it to the substrate), and **additives** (driers, plasticizers, anti-settling agents, anti-foaming agents, biocides, etc.). After application as a thin film, the vehicle hardens — either by evaporation, oxidation, polymerization, or a combination — to form a continuous, adherent, protective, and decorative solid coating.

The **paint vehicle** (also called the **binder**, **medium**, or **film-forming constituent**) is the *non-volatile* liquid portion of the paint that, after the volatile solvent/thinner evaporates, undergoes a chemical or physical transformation to bind the pigment particles into a coherent, adherent film on the metal surface. In the context of marine corrosion protection, the vehicle determines almost every critical property of the coating — its **chemical resistance to seawater**, **flexibility under hull flexure**, **adhesion to steel or aluminium**, **durability under UV**, and **compatibility with cathodic protection**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The *vehicle* is the film-forming ingredient of a paint. It carries the pigment to the surface, anchors it there, and supplies the essential protective, mechanical, and chemical-resistance properties of the dry film. The solvent (or thinner) is the volatile carrier of the vehicle and is **not** part of the dry film.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete Analogy" for Paint Vehicles

Think of a paint film as **reinforced concrete** used in a marine jetty:
- The **pigment** = sand and aggregate (the bulk solid, gives body and "colour" / opacity).
- The **vehicle (binder)** = **cement paste** (the glue that holds the aggregate together and bonds it to the steel rebar / substrate).
- The **solvent** = the mixing water that disappears as the concrete cures (volatile, leaves the film).
- The **additives** = plasticizers, retarders, accelerators (small but performance-critical).

Just as the strength, water-resistance, and flexibility of the jetty depend on the *grade of cement* and not on the sand, the corrosion-protection performance of a marine paint depends overwhelmingly on the *grade and chemistry of the vehicle* and not on the pigment colour. A zinc-rich primer using an epoxy vehicle will protect steel far better than the same zinc dust in a cheap linseed-oil vehicle — even though the pigment is identical.

## 1.3 Classification of Paints — Overview

Paints for marine/offshore service are classified along several independent axes, and KTU examiners frequently expect students to be conversant with all of them:

| Classification Axis | Major Categories |
|---|---|
| **By film-forming vehicle** | Oil-based, Alkyd, Phenolic, Epoxy, Polyurethane, Acrylic, Vinyl, Chlorinated rubber, Bituminous, Silicone, Zinc-rich (inorganic & organic) |
| **By function in a coating system** | Primer (anticorrosive), Tie-coat / Intermediate / Undercoat, Finish / Topcoat (cosmetic & UV barrier), Antifouling |
| **By drying/curing mechanism** | Air-drying (oxidative), Solvent-evaporation (lacquer), Stove / bake (thermoplastic), Cross-linking (2-pack), Moisture-cure, UV-cure |
| **By physical state supplied** | Ready-mixed (liquid), Paste, Powder coating |
| **By service environment** | Atmospheric, Splash zone, Submerged, Tank lining, High-temperature, Chemical-resistant |
| **By substrate** | Steel, Galvanized steel, Aluminium, Concrete, Wood |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Module-3 questions, examiners typically ask students to (i) classify paints by vehicle, (ii) describe the salient features of **2–3 specific common vehicles** (e.g., linseed oil, alkyd, epoxy, polyurethane, chlorinated rubber, bituminous), and (iii) justify the selection of a vehicle for a particular marine service condition (e.g., underwater hull, topside, tank lining).

## 1.4 Why the Choice of Vehicle is the Single Most Important Decision in Marine Coating

In a marine environment, the coating is exposed simultaneously to:
- **Seawater (3–4 % NaCl)** — ionic, conductive, supports corrosion cells.
- **UV radiation from sunlight** — breaks down organic binders.
- **Oxygen and atmospheric pollutants** — drives atmospheric corrosion.
- **Mechanical stresses** — hull flexure, wave slam, abrasion from ice, fenders.
- **Temperature cycling** — from refrigerated cargo holds to hot engine rooms (–30 °C to +200 °C).
- **Cathodic protection overpotential** — if the coating is on a CP-protected hull, the binder must resist alkaline conditions (pH ≈ 9–11) generated at coating holidays.

No single vehicle excels in all these conditions. That is why **modern marine coating systems are multilayer composites**, each layer using a *different* vehicle tailored to a specific function. For example, the same vessel may use:
1. **Inorganic zinc silicate** (vehicle = ethyl silicate) — shop primer.
2. **Epoxy** (vehicle = epoxy resin + polyamide hardener) — anticorrosive primer and tie coat.
3. **Epoxy mastic** — build coat for splash zone.
4. **Polyurethane** (vehicle = aliphatic isocyanate + polyol) — UV-resistant finish/topcoat.
5. **Antifouling** (vehicle = rosin / controlled-depletion polymer) — submerged hull.

This is the **"coating system"** concept that KTU examiners emphasize.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Components of a Paint — Expanded

A complete liquid paint contains the following constituents:

1. **Vehicle (binder)** — non-volatile film former. Determines the principal film properties.
2. **Pigments** — insoluble, finely divided solids (typically 0.1 µm – 50 µm). Function: colour, opacity, corrosion inhibition (e.g., red lead, zinc chromate, zinc dust, micaceous iron oxide), and bulk/film reinforcement.
3. **Solvents / thinners** — volatile liquids (e.g., mineral turpentine, xylene, toluene, MIBK, butyl acetate, water) that reduce the paint to application viscosity. They leave the film during drying.
4. **Additives** — minor (0.1 – 5 % by mass) but performance-critical:
   - **Driers** (cobalt, lead, manganese naphthenates/octoates) — catalyse oxidative drying of oils and alkyds.
   - **Plasticizers** (e.g., chlorinated paraffin, phthalates) — improve flexibility and adhesion.
   - **Anti-settling agents** (e.g., bentone, castor-oil derivatives) — prevent pigment caking.
   - **Anti-skinning agents** (e.g., oximes) — prevent skin formation in can.
   - **Wetting and dispersing agents** (surfactants) — aid pigment dispersion.
   - **UV absorbers and HALS** (hindered amine light stabilizers) — used in topcoats.
   - **Biocides / antifouling agents** (e.g., cuprous oxide, zinc pyrithione, triphenyl borane pyridine) — in antifouling paints.
   - **Defoamers, anti-cratering agents** — improve film appearance.

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Paints by Vehicle

### A. Oil-Based Paints (Air-Drying Oxidative Type)

The **drying oil** is the vehicle. It is a triglyceride of unsaturated fatty acids (linoleic, linolenic, eleostearic, ricinoleic). It dries by **oxidative polymerization** — atmospheric oxygen attacks the double bonds and forms peroxide bridges that cross-link the triglyceride molecules.

| Drying Oil | Source | Key Fatty Acid | Drying Speed | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linseed oil** | Flax seeds | Linolenic (50–60 %) | Fast | Traditional primers, undercoats |
| **Tung oil** | Tung tree nuts | α-Eleostearic (≈80 %) | Very fast, hard film | Spar varnish, marine wood finishes |
| **Dehydrated castor oil (DCO)** | Castor beans, dehydrated | Conjugated linoleic | Medium | Alkyd manufacture, marine primers |
| **Oiticica oil** | Brazilian oiticica tree | Licanic acid | Very fast | Marine varnishes |
| **Fish oil** | Cod, sardine, etc. | Highly unsaturated | Slow | Cheap primers, plasticizer |

> [!NOTE]
> Drying oils by themselves give soft, slow-drying films. To improve hardness, drying rate, and chemical resistance, they are usually **heat-bodied** (polymerized by heating to 280–300 °C under inert atmosphere, raising viscosity) or chemically modified into **alkyds**.

### B. Alkyd Resins

An **alkyd** is a polyester formed by condensation of:
- **Polybasic acids** — typically phthalic anhydride (or isophthalic acid).
- **Polyhydric alcohols** — glycerol, pentaerythritol, sorbitol.
- **Fatty acids (drying oil acids)** — linseed, DCO, coconut (non-drying), soya.

Alkyds are classified by **oil length**:
$$\text{Oil Length (\%)} = \frac{\text{Mass of oil}}{\text{Mass of oil + resin solids}} \times 100$$

- **Short oil** (< 40 %): bake-cured, tough, hard, used in stoving enamels.
- **Medium oil** (40–60 %): air-drying, balanced, general industrial and marine use.
- **Long oil** (> 60 %): air-drying, flexible, easy brushing, traditional marine primers and topside finishes.

Alkyds are the **workhorse of marine finishes** above the waterline because they balance cost, gloss, weatherability, and ease of application. They are *not* recommended for prolonged seawater immersion because the ester linkages are susceptible to **alkaline hydrolysis** in seawater over time.

### C. Phenolic Resins (Phenol-Formaldehyde)

Formed by condensation of phenol (or cresol) with formaldehyde. Two types:
- **Resol** (one-stage, base-catalyzed, contains reactive CH₂OH groups) — used in stoving varnishes and in combination with epoxy for chemical-resistant tank linings.
- **Novolak** (two-stage, acid-catalyzed, requires hexamethylenetetramine hardener) — used in moulding powders and chemically resistant primers.

Phenolic vehicles give **exceptional chemical and solvent resistance** and are used in combination with epoxy for high-performance **cargo-oil tank linings** and **chemical tankers**.

### D. Epoxy Resins

The dominant high-performance marine vehicle. Most marine epoxies are **Bisphenol-A (DGEBA) type**, made by reacting bisphenol-A with epichlorohydrin:

```
n · (Bisphenol-A) + (n+1) · Epichlorohydrin → Epoxy resin
       (BPA)              (ECH)
                       (with NaOH, then HCl)
```

The terminal **epoxy (oxirane) groups** are the reactive sites. DGEBA is never used alone — it must be **cross-linked with a hardener (curing agent)** to form a film. Common hardener families:

| Hardener Family | Cure Type | Film Properties | Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polyamides** (fatty polyamides from dimer acid) | 2-pack ambient | Flexible, good adhesion, good chemical resistance, tolerant of poor surface prep | General marine primers, anticorrosive |
| **Polyamines** (e.g., TETA, DETA, MXDA) | 2-pack ambient | Harder, more chemical resistant, less flexible | Tank linings, offshore |
| **Amidoamines** | 2-pack ambient | Balanced | Tie coats |
| **Phenolic / Novolac** | Heat-cure (or 2-pack) | Highest chemical & solvent resistance | Cargo-oil tank linings |
| **Isocyanates** (e.g., isocyanate prepolymers) | 2-pack | Tough, flexible, abrasion-resistant | Marine topcoats, abrasion-resistant decks |
| **Anhydrides** | Heat-cure | High Tg, electrical | Powder coatings |
| **Mercaptans (polysulfides)** | 2-pack ambient | Fast cure, even underwater | Underwater-curing putties, splash-zone epoxies |

Epoxy films have outstanding **adhesion, chemical resistance, water resistance, and barrier properties** but have two weaknesses:
1. **Poor UV resistance** — chalk and degrade in sunlight → must be top-coated with polyurethane.
2. **Limited flexibility when fully cured** — may crack on flexing steel → use flexible polyamide hardeners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, the standard marine anticorrosive primer is **zinc-rich epoxy primer** (vehicle = epoxy + polyamide, pigment = zinc dust ≥ 80 % in dry film), providing both *barrier* and *cathodic (galvanic)* protection to the steel.

### E. Polyurethane (PU / PUR)

Formed by reaction of **polyols** (long-chain OH-bearing polymers) with **polyisocyanates** (MDI, TDI, HDI, IPDI). Two families:
- **2-pack (2K) aromatic PU** — aromatic isocyanate (MDI, TDI). Tough, hard, but yellows and chalks in UV.
- **2-pack (2K) aliphatic PU** — aliphatic isocyanate (HDI biuret or IPDI trimer). UV-stable, gloss retention, weathering. **Standard marine finish coat.**
- **Moisture-cure PU** (1K) — atmospheric moisture cures free isocyanate. Used as floor coatings, primers.
- **Polyurea** — extremely fast spray-applied coating, used in truck-bed liners and chemical-resistant marine.

Polyurethanes offer the **best combination of gloss, UV resistance, abrasion resistance, and chemical resistance** for marine topcoats.

### F. Acrylic Resins

Thermoplastic (lacquer type, e.g., Paraloid B-44) or thermosetting (with melamine or isocyanate cross-linker, used in automotive and marine). **Excellent UV and weather resistance, gloss, and colour retention**. Used in:
- Topside and superstructure finishes (often as 2K acrylic-urethane).
- Antifouling topcoats.
- Emulsion paints (latex) for non-marine interiors.

### G. Vinyl Resins (PVC Copolymers)

Poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) co-polymerized with vinyl acetate or vinylidene chloride. Form **lacquer-type** coatings that dry by **solvent evaporation only** (no chemical reaction). They are:
- Tough, flexible, abrasion-resistant.
- Resistant to water, acids, alkalis, alcohols.
- Re-coatable (re-solvent weldable).
- **Used in chemical-resistant tank linings, offshore splash zone, and historically in marine antifouling topcoats over chlorinated rubber.**

**Limitation:** they are sensitive to strong solvents (e.g., ketones, esters) and have moderate UV resistance.

### H. Chlorinated Rubber (CR) Paints

Made from **chlorinated natural rubber (CNR)**, prepared by chlorinating latex to ≈ 65 % chlorine content. The chlorinated polymer is dissolved in aromatic solvents (xylene). It dries by **solvent evaporation** to a hard, chemically inert, water-resistant, fire-retardant film.

Advantages:
- Excellent water and chemical resistance.
- Single-pack, easy to apply.
- Re-coatable.
- Compatible with most marine primers.
- Good for **ballast tanks, hulls above and below waterline, decks, and offshore splash zone**.

Limitations:
- Sensitive to strong solvents.
- Softens at ≈ 60 °C → not for high-temp service.
- Released **CO, HCl, and other toxic gases** when burned → flame sensitivity is a regulatory issue.
- Contains **aromatic solvents** → VOC & occupational-hygiene concerns.
- Replaced in many applications by **vinyl/epoxy/PUR** systems, but still in widespread use globally.

### I. Bituminous Paints and Coatings

Vehicle is **bitumen** (asphalt) or **coal-tar pitch**, dissolved in mineral spirits or coal-tar naphtha. Sometimes blended with epoxy (coal-tar epoxy) to upgrade performance.

Properties:
- **Outstanding water resistance** — used for underwater hulls, pipelines, foundations.
- Good chemical resistance to alkalis, acids (dilute), salts.
- Soft, low Tg → flows at warm temperatures; brittle in cold.
- **Poor UV resistance** — chalks and bleeds; usually overcoated with epoxy/PUR for UV.
- Excellent for **buried pipelines, underwater steel, ship bottoms (with antifouling over), waterproofing**.

### J. Silicone and Other Heat-Resistant Vehicles

**Silicone resins** (polysiloxanes) and **silicone-alkyd / silicone-acrylic** blends provide **heat resistance up to 600 °C** (with aluminium pigment) and excellent UV/weathering. Used on:
- Funnels, exhaust casings.
- Engine-room hot surfaces.
- High-temperature stacks.

### K. Inorganic Vehicles (Zinc-Rich Primers)

- **Inorganic zinc silicate (IOZ)** — vehicle = **ethyl silicate** (TEOS hydrolysed) or **sodium / potassium silicate (water-glass)**. Cures by atmospheric moisture / hydrolysis. Pigment = **zinc dust**.
- Provides both **cathodic (galvanic) protection** (zinc sacrifices itself) and **barrier protection** (zinc corrosion products — ZnO, Zn(OH)₂, ZnCO₃ — fill the film).
- The *pre-eminent* **shop primer** in modern shipbuilding. Tolerates welding, cutting, and high heat.

## 2.3 Comparative Engineering Decision Matrix — Which Vehicle for Which Marine Service?

| Marine Service / Zone | Recommended Vehicle(s) | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Shop primer (steel stock)** | Inorganic zinc silicate (ethyl silicate) | Galvanic protection, weldable, fast-dry |
| **External hull — above waterline (topside)** | Epoxy primer + Aliphatic PU topcoat | Epoxy = barrier, PU = UV + gloss |
| **External hull — below waterline (boot-top & bottom)** | Epoxy anticorrosive + Antifouling (rosin / Cu₂O) | Epoxy = barrier, AF = biofouling control |
| **Ballast tanks (seawater ballast)** | Pure epoxy (2-pack, solvent-free or high-build) | Water resistance, edge retention, no holidays |
| **Cargo oil tanks (COT)** | Pure epoxy or epoxy-phenolic | Oil/chemical resistance, IMO PSPC compliant |
| **Splash zone (offshore platform leg)** | Epoxy-mastic polyamide + PU or glass-flake epoxy | Impact, abrasion, water-immersion cycling |
| **Decks (exposed, traffic)** | Epoxy primer + PU or epoxy non-skid | Abrasion resistance |
| **Engine room, hot surfaces (≤200 °C)** | Alkyd or modified silicone | Heat, oil resistance |
| **Hot surfaces (200–600 °C)** | Silicone (with aluminium pigment) | Heat + UV |
| **Buried/submerged pipelines** | Coal-tar epoxy, bitumen, or PE/PP wrap | Water + soil + cathodic protection compatibility |
| **Tank linings (chemicals)** | Vinyl-ester, epoxy-novolac, phenolic | Chemical resistance |
| **Aluminium superstructure** | Etch primer (chromate / chromate-free) + epoxy + PU | Adhesion to Al, corrosion prevention |
| **Wooden boats (traditional)** | Linseed oil + tung oil varnish | Penetration, flexibility, tradition |
| **Concrete (deck houses, ballast ducts)** | Acrylic emulsion (primer/finish) | Breathability, alkali resistance |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Quick-Reference Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Oil length of an alkyd | $\text{Oil Length} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{resin solids}}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) | $\text{PVC} = \dfrac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}} \times 100$ | % ; critical for film porosity |
| Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC) | $\text{PVC} = \text{CPVC}$ → just enough binder to wet all pigment | Boundary value (≈ 30–55 % typical) |
| PVC/CPVC ratio | $\Lambda = \text{PVC}/\text{CPVC}$ | $\Lambda < 1$: sealed, glossy ; $\Lambda > 1$: matte, porous, less protective |
| Volume Solids | $VS = \dfrac{V_{\text{non-volatile}}}{V_{\text{total paint}}} \times 100$ | % ; determines wet-film-to-dry-film thickness |
| DFT from WFT | $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \dfrac{VS}{100}$ | µm ; used in application |
| Theoretical coverage | $C = \dfrac{VS \times 10}{DFT}$ (with $C$ in m²/L, DFT in µm) | m²/L |
| Mixing ratio of 2K epoxies (by vol) | $V_{\text{base}} : V_{\text{hardener}} = r$ | Manufacturer-specified |
| Pot-life of 2K | $t_{pot}$ at 23 °C typical 1–4 h | h |
| Iodine value of drying oil | grams of I₂ absorbed by 100 g oil | g I₂/100g ; higher ⇒ faster drying |
| Saponification value | mg KOH to saponify 1 g oil | mg KOH/g ; indicator of ester content |

> [!NOTE]
> For an air-drying oil, the **iodine value** is the simplest indicator of drying tendency: > 130 → drying; 100–130 → semi-drying; < 100 → non-drying. Linseed oil ≈ 170–190, tung oil ≈ 160–170, dehydrated castor ≈ 135, raw castor ≈ 80, coconut ≈ 8.

## 2.5 Underlying Chemistry of Drying — Brief (for theory answer)

- **Oxidative drying (oils, alkyds):** O₂ attacks the **allylic CH₂ group** between two C=C double bonds (e.g., the –CH=CH–CH₂–CH=CH– unit in linolenic acid) to form a **hydroperoxide** (–CH=CH–CH(OOH)–CH=CH–). Metal driers (Co, Mn, Pb) catalyse the decomposition of hydroperoxides to **free radicals (R·, RO·, ROO·)** that propagate cross-linking by radical combination.
- **Solvent evaporation (vinyl, chlorinated rubber, acrylic lacquers):** physical, reversible, no chemical change.
- **2K cross-linking (epoxy + amine, PU + isocyanate):** stoichiometric addition reaction; film is built in a few hours; full cure in 7 days.
- **Moisture-cure (PU, ethyl silicate):** ambient H₂O acts as a co-reactant.
- **Stove / bake (alkyd-amino, epoxy-anhydride, phenolic):** heat drives cross-linking to a denser, more solvent-resistant network.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Computing DFT, Wet Film Thickness, and Coverage

A very common KTU-style numerical question gives the **volume solids (VS)** of a paint, the desired **dry film thickness (DFT)**, and asks for the **wet film thickness (WFT)** to be applied, the **theoretical coverage in m²/L**, or the **paint quantity required for a given steel area**.

### Step 1 — Fundamental relationship

When a wet film of thickness $WFT$ (µm) is applied, only the **non-volatile fraction** (i.e., the volume solids $VS$, in %) remains in the dry film of thickness $DFT$ (µm). The solvent/thinner evaporates. By conservation of *non-volatile volume*:

$$V_{\text{non-volatile, wet}} = V_{\text{non-volatile, dry}}$$

Let us consider a 1 m² area. The wet film volume is $V_{\text{wet}} = WFT \times 1 \text{ m}^2 = WFT \times 10^{6} \ \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{m}^2$ (working in consistent units). The non-volatile portion of the wet film is $\dfrac{VS}{100} \times V_{\text{wet}}$. The dry film volume is $V_{\text{dry}} = DFT \times 1 \text{ m}^2$.

Therefore:

$$\frac{VS}{100} \times WFT = DFT$$

### Step 2 — Solve for WFT

$$WFT = \frac{DFT \times 100}{VS}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for DFT (reverse)

$$DFT = WFT \times \frac{VS}{100}$$

### Step 4 — Theoretical Spreading Rate (Coverage)

The theoretical coverage $C$ (m²/L) is the area covered by **1 litre** of paint at the specified DFT, assuming 100 % transfer efficiency (no losses to spray, brush, overspray, wastage).

1 litre of paint = $10^{6}$ mm³ = $10^{12}$ µm³ = $10^{6}$ µm·L (using $1$ L = $10^{6}$ µm·m²·L⁻¹ wait, let us redo carefully).

Conversion: $1 \text{ L} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 10^{9} \ \mu\text{m}^3 \cdot 10^{-3}$ — let me just do it in µm·L = $10^{6}$ µm·m² is not a unit. Let me redo with the practical formulation.

**Practical coverage equation used universally in coating engineering:**

$$C \ \left[\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{L}}\right] = \frac{VS \ [\%] \times 10}{DFT \ [\mu\text{m}]}$$

*Derivation:* A 1-litre film of pure paint has volume $V = 1000 \text{ cm}^3 = 10^{6} \text{ mm}^3$. When applied as a film of thickness $DFT$ in µm, the area covered is

$$A = \frac{V}{DFT \ \text{in mm}} = \frac{10^{6} \ \text{mm}^3 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{mm}/\mu\text{m}}{DFT \ [\mu\text{m}]} = \frac{1000 \ \text{mm}^3/\mu\text{m}}{DFT \ [\mu\text{m}]}$$

Wait — let us write in cm and µm: $1$ L = $1000$ cm³. If film thickness = $DFT \ \mu$m $= DFT \times 10^{-4}$ cm, the area covered is

$$A = \frac{1000 \ \text{cm}^3}{DFT \times 10^{-4} \ \text{cm}} = \frac{10^{7}}{DFT} \ \text{cm}^2 = \frac{10^{3}}{DFT} \ \text{m}^2$$

This is the coverage with *all* the paint being non-volatile. With volume solids $VS$ in %, only $VS/100$ remains as dry film, so we get the same area only if we need a *thinner effective film*, so multiply by $VS/100$:

$$A = \frac{10^{3}}{DFT} \times \frac{VS}{100} = \frac{10 \cdot VS}{DFT} \quad \left[\frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{L}}\right]$$

So the standard practical formula is:

$$\boxed{\,C \ \left[\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{L}}\right] = \frac{VS \ [\%] \times 10}{DFT \ [\mu\text{m}]}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transfer efficiency (TE) of spray application** is typically 50–80 % for airless spray, 30–60 % for conventional spray, 70–90 % for brush/roller. The actual paint required is

$$V_{\text{paint, required}} = \frac{A_{\text{steel}}}{C \times TE}$$

> Always include 5–10 % wastage.

### Worked Example (KTU-Style 3-Mark Problem)

> A marine epoxy primer has 65 % volume solids. If the specified DFT is 200 µm, calculate (a) the WFT to apply, and (b) the theoretical coverage in m²/L.

**Solution:**

(a) WFT

$$WFT = \frac{DFT \times 100}{VS} = \frac{200 \times 100}{65} = 307.7 \ \mu\text{m}$$

(b) Theoretical coverage

$$C = \frac{VS \times 10}{DFT} = \frac{65 \times 10}{200} = 3.25 \ \text{m}^2/\text{L}$$

**Check (sanity):** One litre of paint, $V = 1000$ cm³, applied at $WFT = 307.7$ µm $= 0.03077$ cm, covers

$$A = \frac{1000}{0.03077} = 32\,500 \ \text{cm}^2 = 3.25 \ \text{m}^2 \ \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Mixing Ratio for 2K Epoxy (Volume Basis)

A 2-pack epoxy has a base (resin) component and a hardener (curing agent) component. The manufacturer specifies a mixing ratio either by **mass** or by **volume**. The most common specification is by **volume** (e.g., 4 : 1, 3 : 1, 2 : 1, 1 : 1).

### Example

A marine epoxy primer is supplied in 4 : 1 (base : hardener) **by volume**. We need to mix 5 litres of paint. What volume of each component do we take?

Total parts = $4 + 1 = 5$ parts.

$$V_{\text{base}} = 5 \ \text{L} \times \frac{4}{5} = 4.0 \ \text{L}$$

$$V_{\text{hardener}} = 5 \ \text{L} \times \frac{1}{5} = 1.0 \ \text{L}$$

After mixing, total volume ≈ $5.0$ L (small shrinkage due to chemical reaction can be ignored for field purposes).

### Important — Do *not* convert to mass without densities

If the ratio is by mass and the densities are different, you **must** convert:

$$V_{\text{base}} = \frac{m_{\text{base}}}{\rho_{\text{base}}}, \quad V_{\text{hardener}} = \frac{m_{\text{hardener}}}{\rho_{\text{hardener}}}$$

And then use the volume ratio. Mixing off-ratio gives:
- Excess hardener → brittle, amine blush, solvent-sensitive film.
- Excess base → soft, under-cured, solvent-sensitive film.

> [!NOTE]
> Always **add hardener to base** (not base to hardener), then **mechanically stir** for 3–5 min with a slow-speed mechanical stirrer, scraping the sides and bottom. Allow the **induction time** (5–15 min for some systems) for the chemical reaction to begin before thinning or application.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Vehicle Selection vs Marine Service Conditions

| Service / Property | Oil / Alkyd | Chlorinated Rubber | Vinyl | Epoxy | Polyurethane | Bituminous | Inorganic Zinc Silicate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Seawater resistance | M | G | G | E | G | E | E (galvanic) |
| Acid resistance | P | G | G | G | G | M | M |
| Alkali resistance | P | G | G | E | G | G | P |
| Solvent resistance | P | P–M | P | E | E | P | E |
| UV / weatherability | M | M | M | P (chalks) | E | P (bleeds) | E (grey) |
| Heat resistance (°C) | 80 | 60 | 70 | 120 | 120 | 60 | 400 |
| Flexibility | E | G | E | M | E | M | P |
| Adhesion to steel | G | G | G | E | E | M | E |
| Re-coatability | E | E | E | Limited (window) | Limited (window) | Limited | E |
| Single-pack convenience | E | E | E | – | – | E | E (moisture cure) |
| VOC / environmental | M | P (aromatic solv.) | P | M | M | M | E (low-VOC, water-borne versions) |
| Cost (per litre) | Low | Low–M | M | M–H | H | Low | M–H |
| **Typical marine use** | Topside, interior | Hull, ballast | Tank lining | Primer, tie-coat, topcoat (with PU) | Topcoat | Underwater, buried | Shop primer |

*Legend:* E = Excellent, G = Good, M = Moderate, P = Poor.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Paint Quantity for Ballast Tank

> A double-bottom ballast tank in a coastal bulk carrier has a total internal steel surface area of 1800 m². The specification calls for a 2-pack epoxy coating at a DFT of 320 µm. The epoxy has 78 % volume solids. The paint is supplied in 4 : 1 (base : hardener) **by volume**. Estimate (a) the theoretical coverage, (b) the WFT, (c) the volume of paint to be ordered, assuming a transfer efficiency of 65 % for airless spray, and 8 % wastage.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Theoretical coverage:**

$$C = \frac{VS \times 10}{DFT} = \frac{78 \times 10}{320} = 2.4375 \ \text{m}^2/\text{L}$$

**Step 2 — Wet film thickness:**

$$WFT = \frac{DFT \times 100}{VS} = \frac{320 \times 100}{78} = 410.3 \ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of paint to be ordered:**

The paint is consumed at the **effective coverage**:

$$C_{\text{eff}} = C \times TE = 2.4375 \times 0.65 = 1.5844 \ \text{m}^2/\text{L}$$

Including 8 % wastage, the coverage further reduces:

$$C_{\text{eff, with wastage}} = 1.5844 \times (1 - 0.08) = 1.4576 \ \text{m}^2/\text{L}$$

Required paint volume:

$$V_{\text{paint, total}} = \frac{A_{\text{steel}}}{C_{\text{eff, with wastage}}} = \frac{1800}{1.4576} \approx 1235 \ \text{L}$$

**Step 4 — Split into base and hardener (4 : 1 by volume):**

$$V_{\text{base}} = 1235 \times \frac{4}{5} = 988 \ \text{L}, \quad V_{\text{hardener}} = 1235 \times \frac{1}{5} = 247 \ \text{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Round up the paint order to the nearest standard drum size — typically 20 L pails. Order 50 pails of base (1000 L) and 13 pails of hardener (260 L) → excess hardener to be discarded at end of job. *Do not* short-order hardener — under-cure ruins the coating.

## 3.5 Theoretical — Why Inorganic Zinc Silicate (IOZ) is the Best Shop Primer

A shop primer protects blast-cleaned steel during construction (which may last 6–24 months in shipyards) before final coatings are applied. The vehicle is **ethyl silicate** (tetra-ethyl orthosilicate, Si(OC₂H₅)₄, abbreviated TEOS), partially hydrolysed and diluted in alcohol.

**Reaction during film formation:**

$$\text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5\text{)}_4 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Si(OH)}_4 + 4\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

The Si(OH)₄ condenses with itself and with the zinc surface to form a polysiloxane network, while the alcohol evaporates:

$$\equiv \text{Si–OH} + \text{HO–Si} \equiv \;\longrightarrow\; \equiv \text{Si–O–Si} \equiv + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The zinc pigment particles are *metallurgically bonded* to the steel surface through this silicate network, giving:
1. **Galvanic (cathodic) protection** — the zinc is electrically continuous to the steel; any holiday exposes the surrounding zinc which sacrificially corrodes.
2. **Barrier** — the silicate matrix is filled with zinc and zinc-corrosion products.
3. **Heat resistance** — survives welding and cutting at 1000 °C+ (zinc melts at 420 °C but the silicate remains).
4. **Re-coatability** — topcoats of epoxy, PU can be applied after sweep-blasting.

This is why IOZ is the *de facto* shop primer of modern shipbuilding — for example, in PSPC (Performance Standard for Protective Coatings) of ballast tanks, an IOZ primer is typically specified at 20–25 µm DFT over SA 2.5 blast.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 (Module 3)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B, Q1a]**
*Define the term "paint vehicle". List the three principal functions it performs in a protective marine coating.*

**Model Answer:**

A **paint vehicle** (also called binder or medium) is the non-volatile, film-forming liquid component of a paint that, after application and loss of the volatile solvent, holds the pigment particles together and adheres them to the substrate as a continuous solid film.

Its three principal functions in a marine coating are:

1. **Adhesion** — it must wet and bond strongly to the (blast-cleaned) steel surface and provide anchorage for the pigment.
2. **Film cohesion and integrity** — it must form a continuous, mechanically sound, and chemically resistant polymer matrix that binds the pigment and any additives into a single coherent film.
3. **Environmental resistance** — it must resist the marine environment — seawater, salt spray, UV, temperature cycling, and (where applicable) cathodic-protection alkalinity — and provide the **barrier** against water, oxygen, and ions that reach the steel substrate.

*(Self-graded as a 3-mark answer — definition 1 M, three functions 2 M.)*

### Question A.2 (Module 3)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A, Q1b]**
*Differentiate between "oil length" of an alkyd resin and "iodine value" of a drying oil. Why are these parameters important to a paint formulator selecting a vehicle for marine use?*

**Model Answer:**

**Oil length of an alkyd:**
$$\text{Oil Length (\%)} = \frac{\text{mass of drying oil}}{\text{mass of oil + resin solids}} \times 100$$
- Quantifies the **proportion of fatty (drying-oil) component** in the alkyd molecule.
- **Short oil** (< 40 %) — bake-cured, hard, tough; used in stoving finishes.
- **Medium oil** (40–60 %) — air-drying, balanced — typical for general marine use.
- **Long oil** (> 60 %) — air-drying, flexible, easy brushing — used in traditional marine primers and topcoats.

**Iodine value of a drying oil:**
- Defined as the **grams of iodine (I₂) absorbed by 100 g of the oil**, reflecting the degree of unsaturation (number of C=C double bonds).
- Higher iodine value → greater unsaturation → faster oxidative drying.
- Linseed ≈ 170–190, tung ≈ 160–170, dehydrated castor ≈ 135, raw castor ≈ 80, coconut ≈ 8.

**Importance to the formulator:**
- The **oil length** controls whether the alkyd is bake-cured or air-dries, and the flexibility/film hardness balance.
- The **iodine value** predicts the **drying speed, hardness, and yellowing tendency** of the drying oil. A formulator choosing a vehicle for a marine topcoat (e.g., a yacht) may prefer a long-oil alkyd based on linseed oil for flexibility, gloss, and good drying; for an underwater primer, an oil-based vehicle is rarely used — instead an epoxy/PU system is selected, regardless of iodine value.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question B-A.1 (14 Marks, Module 3)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Q9(a)]**

**(a)** *Classify paints according to the nature of the vehicle. Discuss the salient features of (i) linseed oil, (ii) alkyd resin, and (iii) epoxy resin, with specific reference to their use in marine corrosion-prevention coatings.* **(10 marks)**

**(b)** *For a marine epoxy primer having 75 % volume solids, to be applied at 250 µm DFT, calculate the WFT, theoretical coverage in m²/L, and the total paint volume required to coat 1200 m² of hull assuming 60 % transfer efficiency and 10 % wastage.* **(4 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Classification of paints by vehicle** *(≈ 4 marks)*

Paints for marine service are classified by the *film-forming vehicle* into the following principal families:

1. **Oils and oil-based paints** — linseed, tung, dehydrated castor (DCO), oiticica, fish oil; dry by oxidative polymerisation of the unsaturated triglyceride.
2. **Alkyd resins** — polyesters of polybasic acid + polyol + drying-oil fatty acids; classified by oil length (short / medium / long); modified alkyds (silicone-alkyd, chlorinated-rubber-alkyd, vinyl-toluene-alkyd, etc.) extend performance.
3. **Phenolic resins** — resol and novolak; high chemical and heat resistance; used in varnishes and epoxy-phenolic tank linings.
4. **Epoxy resins** — DGEBA and novolac types; two-pack with polyamide, polyamine, or amine adduct hardeners; barrier and chemical-resistant.
5. **Polyurethane resins** — 2K aliphatic and aromatic; 1K moisture-cure; excellent gloss, UV, abrasion, and chemical resistance.
6. **Acrylic resins** — thermoplastic lacquers and thermosetting enamels; outstanding UV and gloss; used in emulsion paints and topcoats.
7. **Vinyl resins** — PVC copolymers; solvent-evaporation lacquers; chemical and water resistant.
8. **Chlorinated rubber** — chlorinated natural rubber; one-pack lacquer; high water and chemical resistance.
9. **Bituminous vehicles** — bitumen, coal-tar pitch, coal-tar epoxy; excellent water resistance, used for underwater and buried steel.
10. **Silicone resins** — polysiloxanes; heat-resistant up to 600 °C with aluminium pigment.
11. **Inorganic vehicles** — ethyl silicate, sodium / potassium silicate; vehicle for zinc-rich shop primers.

**(i) Linseed oil** *(≈ 2 marks)*

- Vehicle of **traditional oil-based primers, undercoats, and house paints**.
- Drying triglyceride with iodine value ≈ 170–190 (linolenic acid 50–60 %).
- Dries by **oxidative polymerisation** — O₂ attacks the doubly allylic methylene to form hydroperoxides that cross-link; **metal driers** (Co, Mn, Pb naphthenates) accelerate this.
- Film is **soft, flexible, good adhesion, but slow to dry, yellowing, and limited water/alkali resistance**.
- In marine use, linseed-oil-based paints are used for **above-waterline primers and traditional yacht finishes**, but *not* for underwater hulls or ballast tanks.

**(ii) Alkyd resin** *(≈ 2 marks)*

- A **modified polyester** — phthalic anhydride + glycerol/pentaerythritol + drying-oil fatty acids.
- Classified by **oil length** into short, medium, and long; long-oil alkyds are typical for marine air-drying finishes.
- Properties: **glossy, durable, flexible, good weatherability, easy to apply** by brush/spray, *cheaper* than epoxy/PU.
- Limitations: ester linkages are **hydrolysed by alkali** → *not* suitable for prolonged seawater immersion or for use over cathodically protected hulls (alkaline conditions).
- Typical use: **topside and superstructure marine finishes, primers above waterline, machinery interiors**.

**(iii) Epoxy resin** *(≈ 2 marks)*

- The dominant **high-performance marine vehicle**. Most are **DGEBA (diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A)**.
- Two-pack: **base (epoxy resin) + hardener (polyamide, polyamine, or amine adduct)**.
- Film is **hard, tough, with outstanding adhesion, barrier properties, water, and chemical resistance**.
- Limitations: **poor UV resistance (chalks) and limited flexibility when fully cured** — overcome by *overcoating with aliphatic polyurethane* (for UV) or by using *flexible polyamide hardeners*.
- Used for: **anticorrosive primers (zinc-rich epoxy), ballast tank coatings (PSPC), cargo oil tank linings, splash-zone epoxies, glass-flake epoxies, and as a tie coat under PU topcoats**.

**(b) Numerical** *(4 marks)*

Given: $VS = 75\%$, $DFT = 250 \ \mu$m, $A = 1200$ m², $TE = 60\%$, wastage = $10\%$.

**Step 1 — WFT:**

$$WFT = \frac{DFT \times 100}{VS} = \frac{250 \times 100}{75} = 333.3 \ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical coverage:**

$$C_{\text{theo}} = \frac{VS \times 10}{DFT} = \frac{75 \times 10}{250} = 3.0 \ \text{m}^2/\text{L}$$

**Step 3 — Effective coverage (transfer + wastage):**

$$C_{\text{eff}} = C_{\text{theo}} \times TE \times (1 - 0.10) = 3.0 \times 0.60 \times 0.90 = 1.62 \ \text{m}^2/\text{L}$$
