Peroxide Bridge] X2 --- OIL3[Adjacent Chain Oil Unit] ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Block — Function of Each Component in Marine Service ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Alkyd Resin Binder] --> F1[Film Formation] R --> F2[Adhesion to Substrate] R --> F3[Flexibility and Impact Resistance] R --> F4[UV and Salt Spray Durability] R --> F5[Corrosion Inhibition
Barrier Effect] F5 --> M1[Marine Hull Protection] F5 --> M2[Superstructure Coating] F5 --> M3[Ballast Tank Lining] F1 --> AIR[Air Drying via
Oxygen Absorption] AIR --> CURE[Oxidative Cross-Linking
Drying Time 4 to 24 h] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define alkyd resin. List the three primary raw materials used in its synthesis and state the role of each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Alkyd resin is a **polyester resin modified with fatty acids/oils**, formed by the polycondensation of a **polybasic acid/anhydride**, a **polyhydric alcohol**, and a **monobasic fatty acid (or drying oil)**. | Raw Material | Example | Role | |---|---|---| | Polybasic acid | Phthalic Anhydride | Provides rigid aromatic backbone, hardness, and chemical resistance | | Polyol | Glycerol / Pentaerythritol | Supplies –OH groups for esterification; tri/tetra functionality enables branching | | Fatty acid / Oil | Linseed oil | Imparts flexibility, air-drying ability, water repellency, and weather durability | > [!NOTE] > **Marking Note (1+1+1):** Definition = 1 mark; naming 3 materials = 1 mark; stating roles = 1 mark. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Explain the term "oil length" of an alkyd resin. How does it influence the properties of marine coatings?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Oil length** is the percentage weight of oil in the total alkyd formulation: $$\text{Oil Length (\%)} = \frac{W_{oil}}{W_{oil} + W_{polyol} + W_{acid}} \times 100$$ **Influence on marine coatings:** - **Short oil (≤40%):** Fast drying, hard, brittle, poor outdoor durability → used in **shop primers** and stoving finishes. - **Medium oil (40–60%):** Balanced drying, flexibility, gloss → used in **marine primers and undercoats** (industry standard for ships). - **Long oil (≥60%):** Slow drying, very flexible, excellent weathering, brush-friendly → used in **exterior marine topcoats** and house paints. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Do **NOT** confuse *oil length* with *oil content* — OL is expressed as a percentage of total resin solids. > - When asked to "discuss" properties, always link them back to the **marine application context** (salt-spray, water immersion, UV). > - Stoichiometric balance must be computed using **equivalents, not masses** — this is a very common, mark-losing error. > - Acid Value and Saponification Value have **identical formulae** but different meanings: AV measures **free** acid; SV measures **total** ester + free acid. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With the help of a neat reaction scheme, explain the synthesis of a **medium-oil alkyd resin** from phthalic anhydride, glycerol, and linseed oil. Discuss the role of each raw material. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Carothers equation** for the number-average degree of polymerisation. Using this, determine $\overline{X_n}$ for a system with $f_{avg} = 2.4$ and extent of reaction $p = 0.92$. Comment on whether the system is approaching gelation. **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Alcoholysis (1.5 marks)** Linseed oil (triglyceride) reacts with glycerol at 220–240 °C in the presence of a litharge (PbO) or lithium hydroxide catalyst: $$\text{Triglyceride} + \text{Glycerol} \xrightarrow{\text{220-240 °C, catalyst}} \text{Monoglyceride} + \text{Diglyceride}$$ The **monoglyceride** provides free –OH groups for esterification and solubilises phthalic anhydride. **Step 2: Polycondensation with Phthalic Anhydride (2.5 marks)** Phthalic anhydride is added; the reaction is heated to 230–260 °C. Water of condensation is removed via a Dean–Stark trap with xylene as entrainer: $$\text{Phthalic Anhydride} + \text{–OH of Monoglyceride} \rightarrow \text{Aromatic Ester} + H_2O$$ **Step 3: Chain growth and branching (1.5 marks)** Glycerol (functionality = 3) creates **branched polyester networks**; the linseed oil's unsaturated fatty acid chains hang pendant to the backbone, providing flexibility and air-drying unsaturation sites. **Step 4: Roles (1.5 marks)** | Material | Role | |---|---| | Phthalic Anhydride | Rigid aromatic segment; raises Tg; chemical resistance | | Glycerol | Tri-functional; creates branching and cross-link sites | | Linseed Oil | Flexibility + oxidative drying (conjugated unsaturation) | ##### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 Marks** **Derivation (already given in Section 3.1, summarised here):** Starting from $N_0$ molecules, with $N$ unreacted, and extent of reaction $p$: $$\begin{aligned} N_0 - N &= \frac{N_0 \, f_{avg}\, p}{2} \quad \text{(each bond consumes 2 functional groups)} \\ \overline{X_n} &= \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{2}{2 - p\, f_{avg}} \end{aligned}$$ **Numerical evaluation (3 marks):** $$\overline{X_n} = \frac{2}{2 - (0.92)(2.4)} = \frac{2}{2 - 2.208} = \frac{2}{-0.208} = -9.62$$ A **negative** value indicates that $p \cdot f_{avg} > 2$, which means the system has **already passed the gel point** and would, in practice, be an infusible, insoluble cross-linked network (gel). **Gel point check:** $$p_c = \frac{2}{f_{avg}} = \frac{2}{2.4} = 0.833$$ Since $p = 0.92 > p_c = 0.833$, the system has gelled. In a real cook, the reaction would have been terminated at $p \approx 0.80$ to keep the resin **soluble and processable**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** Differentiate between **short-oil, medium-oil, and long-oil alkyds** with respect to oil length, drying mechanism, film properties, and typical marine applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A coating resin is formulated with **300 g linseed oil, 100 g glycerol, and 220 g phthalic anhydride**. Calculate the **oil length, equivalent weights, and stoichiometric balance (K)**. Comment on the expected molecular architecture. **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 Marks** | Parameter | Short-Oil (≤40%) | Medium-Oil (40–60%) | Long-Oil (≥60%) | |---|---|---|---| | Oil Length | 30–40% | 40–60% | 60–70%+ | | Drying | Stoving/forced; oxidative is slow | Air-drying, balanced | Slow air-drying | | Film Hardness | Hard, brittle | Moderate | Soft, flexible | | Gloss | High | Good | Moderate | | Marine Use | Shop primers, anti-fouling binders | **Marine primers & undercoats (KTU focus)** | Hull topcoats, superstructure | | Solvent | Aromatic (xylene) | Mixed | Aliphatic (white spirit) | **Conclusion:** Marine service typically demands *medium-oil alkyds* — the sweet spot between drying speed and salt-spray durability. ##### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Oil Length (1.5 marks)** $$W_{total} = 300 + 100 + 220 = 620 \text{ g}$$ $$\text{OL\%} = \frac{300}{620} \times 100 = 48.39\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Medium-oil alkyd}$$ **Step 2: Equivalent Weights (3 marks)** $$E_{PA} = \frac{148.12}{2} = 74.06 \text{ g/eq}$$ $$E_{glycerol} = \frac{92.09}{3} = 30.70 \text{ g/eq}$$ $$E_{linseed\ oil} \approx 290 \text{ g/eq (literature value)}$$ **Step 3: Stoichiometric Balance (2.5 marks)** $$\begin{aligned} \sum E_{acid} &= \frac{220}{74.06} = 2.971 \text{ eq} \\ \sum E_{OH} &= \frac{100}{30.70} + \frac{300}{290} = 3.257 + 1.034 = 4.291 \text{ eq} \\ K &= \frac{2.971}{4.291} = 0.692 < 1 \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation (1 mark):** $K < 1$ indicates **OH-excess**, yielding a **branched, slightly higher-functional resin** — appropriate for a medium-oil marine primer that must cross-link upon air-drying. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Alkyd Resins (Module 3)** - [x] **Alkyd** = **Al**(cohol) + **(A)kyd** — a polyester modified with fatty acid/oil. - [x] Three monomers: **Polybasic acid** (PA), **Polyol** (glycerol), **Fatty acid** (linseed oil). - [x] **Oil length** is the master property — short ≤ 40%, medium 40–60%, long ≥ 60%. - [x] **Drying** is **oxidative** — atmospheric O₂ attacks doubly allylic CH₂ groups in unsaturation. - [x] **Acid Value (AV)** is the **endpoint indicator** in the resin cook; target ≤ 15 mg KOH/g. - [x] **Saponification Value (SV)** = AV + OHV (measures total ester content). - [x] **Iodine Value (IV)** quantifies unsaturation: >120 = drying, <120 = non-drying. - [x] **Carothers equation:** $\overline{X_n} = 2/(2 - p\, f_{avg})$. - [x] **Gel point:** $p_c = 2/f_{avg}$; for $f_{avg} = 2$, $p_c = 1$ (no gelation in pure difunctional). - [x] **Marine standard:** medium-oil PA + glycerol + linseed/soya for primers; long-oil for topcoats. - [x] **Phthalic Anhydride** → hardness, aromatic UV stability. - [x] **Glycerol ($f=3$) / Pentaerythritol ($f=4$)** → branching and cross-link potential. - [x] **Drying oils** with conjugated double bonds (tung, oiticica) dry faster than isolated double bonds (linseed). - [x] **Anti-gelling additives:** triphenyl phosphite, antioxidants (BHT). - [x] **Never use masses** directly in stoichiometry — always convert to **equivalents**. ---

topics.title: Polymerizing Chemicals and Bitumen: Role of Constituents of Paints, Suitability of Each for Various Applications, Typical Paint Schemes for Underwater Areas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polymerizing Chemicals and Bitumen: Role of Constituents of Paints, Suitability of Each for Various Applications, Typical Paint Schemes for Underwater Areas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Paint — The Marine Engineer's Working Definition

In the context of marine corrosion protection, a **paint** (also called a *coating*) is a chemically formulated, liquid or semi-liquid mixture that, when applied to a properly prepared metallic substrate, undergoes a controlled physical or chemical transformation (drying/curing) to form a continuous, adherent, and protective solid film. The film isolates the underlying steel from the aggressive marine environment (seawater, chlorides, dissolved oxygen, biofouling organisms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Paint is a composite mixture of four essential constituents — Binder (Polymerizing Chemical), Pigment, Solvent/Thinner, and Additives — engineered to perform protective, decorative, or specific functional roles on a marine substrate."*

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Sandwich Analogy"

Imagine a paint film as a **layered sandwich**:

- **The Bread (Top & Bottom):** The **Binder** — the structural backbone that holds everything together. Just as bread holds sandwich fillings, the binder holds pigments and additives and bonds them to the metal.
- **The Filling (Middle):** The **Pigments & Extenders** — provide flavor (color), nutrition (corrosion protection), and texture (mechanical strength).
- **The Butter / Moisture (Wetting the bread):** The **Solvent/Thinner** — keeps the sandwich moist and spreadable until it's plated.
- **The Spices (Tiny but powerful):** **Additives** — improve shelf life, drying, flow, and antifouling behavior.

Without the binder (bread), the sandwich collapses. Without solvent (moisture), you cannot spread it. Without pigments, you get a transparent, weak film. Without additives, the sandwich spoils quickly. Each constituent plays a non-negotiable role.

### 1.3 Polymerizing Chemicals — The "Heart" of the Paint

**Polymerizing chemicals** are the *high-molecular-weight film-forming substances* that constitute the **binder** portion of the paint. They are called "polymerizing" because they undergo **polymerization** (linking of small monomer units into long-chain macromolecules) during the drying/curing process, forming the continuous protective film.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point:** The binder is the **single most important constituent** of a paint because it determines:
> - Adhesion to substrate
> - Mechanical strength and flexibility
> - Chemical and water resistance
> - Durability in marine service
> - Compatibility with pigments

### 1.4 Bitumen — Nature's Ancient Marine Protector

**Bitumen** is a sticky, black, viscous liquid or semi-solid composed primarily of **high-molecular-weight hydrocarbons** (asphaltenes, resins, and oils). It has been used since 3000 BCE for waterproofing ships and continues to be a cost-effective marine coating material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Main Sources of Bitumen:**
> - **Natural Bitumen** — Found in geological deposits (e.g., Trinidad Lake Asphalt)
> - **Petroleum Bitumen** — Residue from crude oil refining (most common today)

**Penetration Grade:** Bitumen is graded by its *penetration depth* (in tenths of a millimeter, 25 °C, 100 g, 5 s). Common marine grades: **80/100, 60/70, 30/40** (lower penetration = harder bitumen).

---

### 1.5 The Five Functional Constituents of a Marine Paint

| # | Constituent | Primary Function | Typical Marine Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Binder (Polymerizing Chemical)** | Film formation, adhesion, durability | Epoxy resin, Alkyd, Chlorinated Rubber |
| 2 | **Pigment** | Color, opacity, corrosion inhibition, UV barrier | Red oxide (Fe₂O₃), Zinc chromate, Micaceous Iron Oxide (MIO) |
| 3 | **Extender (Filler)** | Cost reduction, film reinforcement, barrier | Barium sulfate (BaSO₄), Talc, Mica |
| 4 | **Solvent / Thinner** | Viscosity reduction, application ease | Xylene, Toluene, Mineral spirits |
| 5 | **Additives** | Specialized performance enhancement | Driers (cobalt naphthenate), Anti-skinning agents, Plasticizers, Antifouling biocides |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Role of Each Paint Constituent

#### A. Binder / Polymerizing Chemical (20–60% by volume)

The binder is the **non-volatile film-forming matrix**. When the solvent evaporates and chemical reactions (oxidation, polymerization, cross-linking) occur, the binder converts from a liquid to a tough, adherent solid film.

**Mechanism of Film Formation:**
- **Oxidative Polymerization (Alkyds, Oils):** Oxygen from air reacts with double bonds in unsaturated fatty acids, forming cross-linked polymer networks.
- **Solvent Evaporation (Lacquers, Vinyls, Chlorinated Rubber):** Simple physical drying as solvent leaves.
- **Chemical Cure (Epoxy, Polyurethane):** Two-component system where resin reacts with hardener (amine, polyamide, isocyanate) to form cross-linked 3D network.

#### B. Pigments (5–35% by volume)

Pigments are **finely divided, insoluble, discrete particles** that remain suspended in the binder. They serve four primary functions:

1. **Color & Opacity:** Provide aesthetic appearance and hiding power.
2. **Corrosion Inhibition:** Active pigments (e.g., **zinc chromate, red lead, zinc phosphate**) chemically passivate the steel substrate.
3. **Barrier Protection:** Plate-like pigments (e.g., **Micaceous Iron Oxide (MIO), glass flakes**) create a tortuous path for water and ions.
4. **UV Absorption:** Protect the binder from photodegradation (e.g., carbon black, titanium dioxide).

**Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC):**
The CPVC is the pigment concentration at which the binder just fills all voids between pigment particles. Above CPVC, the film becomes porous and weak.

$$ \text{CPVC} = \frac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}} \times 100\% $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule for Marine Paints:** Formulate at **PVC < CPVC** (typically 30–50% for primers, 15–25% for topcoats) to ensure a continuous, non-porous film.

#### C. Extenders (Fillers)

Extenders are **low-cost, inert pigments** that:
- Reduce overall cost
- Improve mechanical properties (hardness, abrasion resistance)
- Modify rheology (flow, leveling)
- Reinforce the film (especially lamellar extenders like **mica, talc**)

#### D. Solvents / Thinners

Solvents dissolve or disperse the binder and reduce viscosity for application. After application, they evaporate and are not part of the final film.

**Key Properties of Marine Solvents:**
- **Solvency Power:** Ability to dissolve the specific binder.
- **Evaporation Rate:** Affects drying time and film quality.
- **Flash Point:** Safety consideration (must be high for marine use).

| Solvent | Evaporation Rate | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Xylene | Medium | Epoxy, Alkyd, Chlorinated Rubber |
| Toluene | Fast | Vinyl, Epoxy |
| Mineral Spirits | Slow | Alkyd primers |
| MIBK (Methyl Isobutyl Ketone) | Fast | Epoxy, Polyurethane |

#### E. Additives (0.1–5% by weight)

Despite their small proportion, additives critically modify paint behavior:

- **Driers (Siccatives):** Cobalt, Manganese, Lead naphthenates — accelerate oxidative drying of alkyds.
- **Anti-skinning Agents:** Methyl ethyl ketoxime (MEKO) — prevent gelling in the can.
- **Plasticizers:** Phthalate esters — improve flexibility and adhesion.
- **Antifouling Biocides:** Cuprous oxide (Cu₂O), Tributyltin (TBT) — prevent marine growth.
- **Thixotropes:** Bentonite, fumed silica — prevent sagging on vertical surfaces.

---

### 2.2 Polymerizing Chemicals — Detailed Classification & Suitability

| Polymer | Chemical Type | Film Formation | Key Properties | Marine Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Alkyd Resin** | Polyester + Fatty Acid | Oxidative | Good gloss, flexibility, poor chemical resistance | Topcoat for superstructure, alkyd primers |
| **Epoxy Resin** | Bisphenol-A + Epichlorohydrin | 2-pack chemical cure | Excellent adhesion, chemical & water resistance | Underwater hull primer, ballast tanks |
| **Polyurethane** | Isocyanate + Polyol | 2-pack chemical cure | Outstanding UV resistance, abrasion resistance | Topcoat for boottop, decks, superstructure |
| **Chlorinated Rubber** | Chlorinated Polyisoprene | Solvent evaporation | Excellent water & chemical resistance | Boot-top, underwater hull, swimming pools |
| **Vinyl Resin (PVC/PVAc)** | Polyvinyl chloride/acetate | Solvent evaporation | Flexible, good chemical resistance | Chemical-resistant linings |
| **Phenolic Resin** | Phenol-Formaldehyde | Heat/chemical cure | Excellent water & solvent resistance | Marine primers, tank linings |
| **Silicone Resin** | Polysiloxane | Heat cure / moisture cure | Exceptional heat & UV resistance | Stack coatings, exhaust systems |
| **Acrylic Resin** | Polymethyl methacrylate | Solvent evaporation | Excellent UV & weather resistance | Topcoats for superstructure |

### 2.3 Bitumen — Chemistry and Marine Suitability

**Chemical Composition of Bitumen:**
- **Asphaltenes (5–25%):** High molecular weight, dark, polar — provide hardness and rigidity.
- **Resins / Maltenes (30–45%):** Polar aromatics — provide adhesion and ductility.
- **Oils / Saturates (30–50%):** Non-polar — provide viscosity and flexibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Bitumen is Excellent for Marine Use:**
> 1. **Outstanding Water Resistance** — hydrophobic hydrocarbons repel water.
> 2. **Chemical Inertness** — resistant to salts, acids (mild), alkalis.
> 3. **Flexibility** — accommodates substrate movement without cracking.
> 4. **Self-Healing** — minor scratches flow and re-seal at warm temperatures.
> 5. **Low Cost** — economically attractive for large marine structures.

**Limitations of Bitumen:**
- **Softens at elevated temperatures** (60–80 °C) — not for hot-service areas.
- **Poor UV resistance** — degrades to chalky residue in sunlight.
- **Limited mechanical strength** — easily damaged by impact.
- **Solvent sensitivity** — attacked by fuels and oils.

**Forms of Bitumen Used in Marine Coatings:**
- **Cutback Bitumen:** Bitumen dissolved in solvent (for ease of application).
- **Bituminous Emulsion:** Bitumen dispersed in water (eco-friendly, cold-applied).
- **Modified Bitumen:** Bitumen blended with polymers (SBS, APP, epoxy) for enhanced properties.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)** | $\text{PVC} = \dfrac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100$ | Determines film porosity |
| **Critical PVC (CPVC)** | Property-dependent (typically 30–50%) | Boundary between good and porous films |
| **Binder Demand** | $\text{BO} = \dfrac{V_b}{V_p + V_b}$ | Oil absorption of pigment |
| **Spreading Rate** | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{1}{\text{WFT} \times \rho}$ (m²/L) | Coverage per liter |
| **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** | $\text{WFT} = \text{DFT} \times \dfrac{100}{\text{Solids \%}}$ | Application control |
| **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** | $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \dfrac{\text{Solids \%}}{100}$ | Specification compliance |
| **Bitumen Penetration Grade** | $\text{Penetration (dmm)}$ at 25°C, 100g, 5s | Hardness classification |
| **Softening Point (Ring & Ball)** | °C | Temperature resistance |
| **Ductility** | cm at 25°C | Flexibility measure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to Remember:**
> - Standard test temperature for paint properties: **25 °C (298 K)**
> - Conversion: **1 mil = 25.4 μm = 0.0254 mm**
> - DFT ranges for marine primers: **75–200 μm per coat**

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of This Knowledge

In the **marine industry**, this knowledge is applied for:

1. **Ship Hull Protection:** Selecting the right binder/pigment combination to resist corrosion and biofouling.
2. **Offshore Structures:** Specifying coatings for splash zones, atmospheric zones, and submerged zones.
3. **Port Infrastructure:** Coating piles, dolphins, and quay structures exposed to seawater.
4. **Cost Optimization:** Balancing expensive binders (epoxy, polyurethane) with cheaper alternatives (bitumen, alkyd) where appropriate.
5. **Failure Analysis:** Diagnosing paint failures (blistering, delamination, chalking) by understanding constituent interactions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) and Its Critical Value

The **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)** defines the volume fraction of pigment in the total non-volatile content of the paint.

**Step 1: Define Total Non-Volatile Volume**

$$ V_{\text{total}} = V_p + V_b $$

where $V_p$ = volume of pigment, $V_b$ = volume of binder (in the dried film).

**Step 2: Express PVC as a Percentage**

$$ \text{PVC (\%)} = \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100 $$

**Step 3: Identify CPVC (Critical Pigment Volume Concentration)**

The CPVC is the **specific PVC value** at which the binder just exactly fills the interstitial voids between the tightly packed pigment particles.

$$ \text{CPVC (\%)} = \frac{V_p^{\text{cpvc}}}{V_p^{\text{cpvc}} + V_b^{\text{cpvc}}} \times 100 $$

**Step 4: Analyze Film Behavior**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If PVC} < \text{CPVC}: &\quad \text{Excess binder → continuous, glossy, dense film (DESIRED)} \\
\text{If PVC} = \text{CPVC}: &\quad \text{Binder exactly fills voids → matte, porous} \\
\text{If PVC} > \text{CPVC}: &\quad \text{Voids unfilled → porous, weak film (UNDESIRED)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Engineering Significance for Marine Paints**

- **Primers (Anti-corrosive):** PVC ≈ 30–40% (below CPVC) — dense barrier.
- **Topcoats (Glossy):** PVC ≈ 15–25% (well below CPVC) — smooth, glossy finish.
- **Flat/Matte Coatings:** PVC ≈ at or near CPVC.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Wet Film Thickness to Dry Film Thickness Conversion

In marine specifications, film thickness is always expressed as **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)**. The applicator measures **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** during application.

**Step 1: Identify Solids by Volume ($S_v$)**

The **Volume Solids** is the percentage of paint that remains as film after the solvent evaporates.

**Step 2: Express the Volume Conservation**

The volume of solids in the wet film equals the volume of the dry film:

$$ V_{\text{wet}} \times S_v = V_{\text{dry}} $$

**Step 3: Convert to Thickness**

Since $V = A \times t$ (Area × Thickness), and assuming constant area:

$$ t_{\text{wet}} \times A \times S_v = t_{\text{dry}} \times A $$

**Step 4: Derive the WFT-DFT Relationship**

$$ \text{WFT} = \text{DFT} \times \frac{100}{S_v} $$

Equivalently:

$$ \text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \frac{S_v}{100} $$

**Numerical Example:**
A marine epoxy primer has 60% volume solids. Specified DFT = 150 μm. Calculate WFT:

$$ \text{WFT} = 150 \times \frac{100}{60} = 250 \, \mu m $$

**Spreading Rate Calculation:**

$$ \text{Spreading Rate (m}^2\text{/L)} = \frac{1000}{\text{WFT (}\mu m\text{)} \times \text{Density (g/cm}^3\text{)} / 10} $$

Or simplified for practical use:

$$ \text{SR} = \frac{10}{\text{WFT (mm)}} \times \frac{1}{\rho} \approx \frac{10 \times S_v}{\text{DFT} \times \rho} \text{ m}^2/\text{L} $$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bitumen Service Temperature and Softening Point

The maximum service temperature of a bitumen coating is governed by its **Softening Point (Ring & Ball test)**.

**Step 1: Define Softening Point**

The temperature at which the bitumen sample deforms and touches a plate 25 mm below a steel ball (3.5 g) under controlled heating (5°C/min).

**Step 2: Empirical Relationship to Penetration**

For many bitumens, an empirical correlation exists:

$$ \log_{10}(P) = A - B \times T_{\text{soft}} $$

where $P$ = penetration (dmm), $T_{\text{soft}}$ = softening point (°C), $A, B$ = empirical constants.

**Step 3: Practical Marine Selection**

For a ship hull operating in tropical waters (max ambient 35°C + solar heating on dark hull = ~70°C):

$$ T_{\text{service}} < T_{\text{soft}} - 20°C \quad \text{(safety margin)} $$

If $T_{\text{service,max}} = 70°C$, then $T_{\text{soft}} > 90°C$ is required.

**Conclusion:** Soft-grade bitumen (80/100) is unsuitable; hard-grade (30/40) or modified bitumen must be used.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Constituent Selection by Application Zone

| Marine Zone | Preferred Binder | Preferred Pigment | Typical DFT (μm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Underwater Hull (Submerged)** | Epoxy, Chlorinated Rubber, Vinyl Tar | Red Oxide, MIO, Zinc-rich | 250–500 (total system) |
| **Boot-Top (Tidal Zone)** | Epoxy + Polyurethane | MIO, TiO₂ | 200–350 |
| **Superstructure (Atmospheric)** | Alkyd, Polyurethane, Acrylic | TiO₂, color pigments | 100–200 |
| **Ballast Tanks** | Pure Epoxy (solvent-free), Coal Tar Epoxy | MIO, Glass flakes | 300–500 |
| **Cargo Holds** | Epoxy, Phenolic Epoxy | Red oxide | 200–400 |
| **Decks (Walkway)** | Polyurethane (non-skid), Epoxy + Aggregate | Aluminum, MIO | 300–500 |
| **Splash Zone (Piling)** | Bitumen Mastic, Coal Tar Epoxy | Carbon black, Graphite | 500–2000 |
| **Chain Locker** | Coal Tar Epoxy | MIO | 400–600 |

---

| **Cost** | Low | High | High | Medium | Medium | **Very Low** |
