## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** List the four essential constituents of a paint with one example each.

**(b)** What is meant by "polymerizing chemical" in the context of paints?

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** The four essential constituents of a paint are:

| Constituent | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Binder (Polymerizing Chemical)** | Film formation, adhesion | Epoxy resin, Alkyd resin |
| **Pigment** | Color, opacity, anti-corrosion | Red oxide (Fe₂O₃), Zinc chromate |
| **Solvent / Thinner** | Viscosity reduction | Xylene, Toluene |
| **Additive** | Drying, flow, anti-skinning | Cobalt naphthenate (drier) |

**(b)** A **polymerizing chemical** (also called the *binder* or *film former*) is the high-molecular-weight resin in a paint that undergoes **polymerization** during drying/curing to form a continuous, adherent, and protective solid film on the substrate. Examples: epoxy resin, alkyd resin, polyurethane resin, chlorinated rubber. *(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Differentiate between *pigments* and *extenders* in a paint.

**(b)** Why is bitumen commonly used for marine splash-zone coatings?

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** **Difference between Pigments and Extenders:**

| Property | Pigment | Extender |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Color, hiding, corrosion inhibition | Cost reduction, film reinforcement |
| Refractive Index | High (>1.7) — provides hiding | Low (~1.5) — transparent |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Examples | TiO₂, Red oxide, Zinc chromate | Barium sulfate, Talc, Mica |

**(b)** Bitumen is preferred for splash-zone marine coatings because:
1. **Excellent water resistance** — hydrophobic in nature.
2. **Chemical inertness** to salts and mild seawater.
3. **Flexibility** to accommodate thermal expansion and structural movement.
4. **Self-healing** behavior under warm temperatures seals minor damage.
5. **Low cost** makes it economical for large marine structures like piles and dolphins.
6. Can be applied in **thick films** (mastics up to 5–10 mm) for impact resistance. *(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1 distributed)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **role of polymerizing chemicals (binders)** in marine paints. Discuss any **five binders** with their characteristic properties and suitability for marine applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **chemistry, properties, advantages, and limitations of bitumen** as a marine coating material. Explain the different forms in which bitumen is used in marine applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

**(a) Role and Types of Polymerizing Chemicals (Binders):**

The **binder** is the most critical constituent of a marine paint because it determines:
- Adhesion to the steel substrate
- Mechanical strength, flexibility, and toughness
- Chemical, water, and corrosion resistance
- Long-term durability under marine service
- Compatibility with pigments and other additives

**Five Important Binders for Marine Applications:**

**1. Epoxy Resin:**
- **Chemistry:** Bisphenol-A + Epichlorohydrin; cured with polyamide or amine hardeners.
- **Film Formation:** Two-pack chemical cure at ambient temperature.
- **Properties:** Excellent adhesion, outstanding chemical and water resistance, good mechanical strength, low shrinkage.
- **Limitations:** Poor UV resistance (chalks on exposure), limited re-coatability window.
- **Marine Use:** Universal underwater hull primer, ballast tank coatings, cargo hold linings.

**2. Polyurethane Resin:**
- **Chemistry:** Isocyanate (NCO) reacts with polyol (OH) to form urethane linkages.
- **Film Formation:** Two-pack moisture/chemical cure.
- **Properties:** Outstanding UV and weather resistance, excellent abrasion resistance, high gloss retention, flexibility.
- **Limitations:** Sensitive to moisture during application, expensive, isocyanate health hazards.
- **Marine Use:** Topcoats for boottop, superstructure, decks (with non-skid aggregate).

**3. Alkyd Resin:**
- **Chemistry:** Polyester backbone modified with drying oils (linseed, soya).
- **Film Formation:** Oxidative polymerization with driers.
- **Properties:** Good gloss, flexibility, easy application, good re-coatability.
- **Limitations:** Poor chemical and alkali resistance, slow drying in cold weather.
- **Marine Use:** Conventional topcoats for superstructure, decks, machinery spaces.

**4. Chlorinated Rubber:**
- **Chemistry:** Chlorinated polyisoprene (~67% Cl).
- **Film Formation:** Solvent evaporation (single-pack, physical drying).
- **Properties:** Excellent water and chemical resistance, good re-coatability, fast drying.
- **Limitations:** Poor UV resistance, emits solvents (VOC concern), limited temperature resistance (~70°C).
- **Marine Use:** Boot-top paints, underwater hull systems (older systems), swimming pool coatings.

**5. Phenolic Resin:**
- **Chemistry:** Phenol-formaldehyde condensation.
- **Film Formation:** Heat or chemical cure.
- **Properties:** Excellent water and solvent resistance, good chemical resistance, hard film.
- **Limitations:** Brittle, dark color, requires heat for full cure.
- **Marine Use:** Tank linings, high-temperature primers, marine primers in chemical cargo holds.

*(7 marks distributed: ~1 mark for role of binder + ~1.2 marks per binder covering chemistry, properties, and marine use)*

---

**(b) Bitumen as a Marine Coating Material:**

**Chemistry:**
Bitumen is a complex colloidal mixture of high-molecular-weight hydrocarbons, classified into three main fractions:
- **Asphaltenes** (5–25%): Dark, polar, high MW — provide rigidity and hardness.
- **Resins / Maltenes** (30–45%): Polar aromatics — provide adhesion and ductility.
- **Oils / Saturates** (30–50%): Non-polar aliphatic — provide viscosity and flexibility.

**Properties:**
- Black/dark brown color, sticky and viscous at room temperature.
- Density ~1.0–1.03 g/cm³.
- Softening point typically 40–100°C (Ring & Ball test).
- Penetration grade 10–300 dmm.
- Excellent water absorption resistance (<0.2%).
- Dielectric strength ~10–30 kV/mm.

**Advantages for Marine Use:**
1. Outstanding water and moisture resistance.
2. Good chemical resistance to salts, mild acids, and alkalis.
3. High flexibility — accommodates substrate movement.
4. Self-healing at warm temperatures.
5. Low cost — economical for large structures.
6. Easy application (brush, spray, hot-mop).
7. Can be built up in thick films (5–10 mm) for impact protection.

**Limitations:**
1. **Softens at elevated temperatures** (60–80°C) — not suitable for hot service.
2. **Poor UV resistance** — degrades to chalky residue in sunlight.
3. **Limited mechanical strength** — vulnerable to mechanical damage.
4. **Solvent-sensitive** — attacked by fuels, oils, and organic solvents.
5. **Dark color** — limits decorative use.

**Forms of Bitumen Used in Marine Applications:**

1. **Cutback Bitumen:** Bitumen dissolved in petroleum solvent (naphtha, kerosene) for ease of cold application. Dries by solvent evaporation. Used in primers and sealers.

2. **Bituminous Emulsion:** Bitumen dispersed in water with emulsifying agents (cationic/anionic). Eco-friendly, non-flammable, cold-applied. Used on marine structures where solvent use is restricted.

3. **Hot-Applied Bitumen:** Heated to 150–200°C and applied in thick layers. Used for waterproofing of ship's bilges, cofferdams, and below-water-line hulls of barges.

4. **Bitumen Mastics:** Bitumen + mineral fillers (sand, asbestos, limestone) + fibers. Applied in very thick layers (5–20 mm). Used for splash zones, jetty piles, and offshore platform legs.

5. **Modified Bitumen:** Bitumen blended with polymers:
   - **SBS (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene):** Improves elasticity and low-temperature flexibility.
   - **APP (Atactic Polypropylene):** Improves high-temperature resistance.
   - **Epoxy-modified bitumen:** Improves chemical resistance.
   - **Coal-tar-modified bitumen:** Improves water resistance and adhesion.

*(7 marks: ~2 marks chemistry, ~2 marks advantages + limitations, ~3 marks forms of bitumen)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Describe the **role of pigments** in marine paints. Explain the concept of **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)** and **Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC)** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss a **typical paint scheme for the underwater hull of a ship**, detailing each coat with its function, binder type, pigment type, and typical Dry Film Thickness (DFT). **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

**(a) Role of Pigments and PVC/CPVC Concept:**

**Role of Pigments in Marine Paints:**
Pigments are insoluble, finely divided solid particles dispersed in the binder. They perform four essential roles:

1. **Color and Aesthetic Appearance:** Provide hiding power and decorative finish. Examples: Titanium dioxide (TiO₂) for white, red oxide (Fe₂O₃) for red, carbon black for black, phthalocyanine blue for blue.

2. **Corrosion Inhibition:** Active pigments chemically passivate the steel substrate.
   - **Zinc chromate:** Releases chromate ions that form a passive oxide film.
   - **Red lead (Pb₃O₄):** Forms lead soap with binder that inhibits corrosion.
   - **Zinc phosphate:** Modern non-toxic replacement; forms iron phosphate passivation layer.

3. **Barrier Protection:** Plate-like (lamellar) pigments create a tortuous diffusion path for water and ions.
   - **Micaceous Iron Oxide (MIO):** Flaky particles aligned parallel to substrate.
   - **Glass flakes:** Create multi-layer barrier effect.
   - **Aluminum flakes:** Also provide reflective and sacrificial action.

4. **UV Protection:** Pigments absorb harmful UV radiation and protect the binder from photodegradation.
   - **Carbon black, TiO₂, Iron oxide** pigments absorb UV.

**Other Functions:**
- Modify mechanical properties (hardness, flexibility).
- Improve opacity and hiding power (high refractive index pigments).
- Reduce gloss (matting agents like silica).
- Provide anti-fouling action (cuprous oxide, TBT — now restricted).

---

**Concept of PVC and CPVC:**

The **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)** is defined as:

$$ \text{PVC} = \frac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}} \times 100 \, \% $$

where $V_p$ = volume of pigment and $V_b$ = volume of binder in the dry film.

**Critical PVC (CPVC)** is the specific PVC value at which the binder just exactly fills the interstitial voids between the densely packed pigment particles.

**Schematic Representation:**

```
BELOW CPVC:           AT CPVC:                ABOVE CPVC:
+---------+           +---------+             +---------+
| Pigment |           | Pigment |             | Pigment |
| o o o   |           | o o o   |             | o o o   |
| o o o   |           | o o o   |             | o o o   |
| o o o   |  BINDER   +---------+  VOIDS      +---------+  AIR/VOID
| o o o   |           | BINDER  |             |   o o o |
| o o o   |           | o o o   |             | o o o   |
| o o o   |           | o o o   |             | o o o   |
+---------+           +---------+             +---------+
DENSE FILM           MATTE FILM              POROUS FILM
(GLOSS, GOOD)        (PROPERTIES CHANGE)     (WEAK, PERMEABLE)
```

**Effect on Film Properties at and Above CPVC:**

| Property | Below CPVC | At CPVC | Above CPVC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gloss | High | Reduced | Low/Matte |
| Permeability | Low | Increases | High |
| Stain Resistance | Good | Decreases | Poor |
| Tensile Strength | High | Decreases | Low |
| Blistering | Resistant | Begins | Severe |

**Engineering Practice:** Marine primers are formulated at PVC ~30–40% (below CPVC), and topcoats at PVC ~15–25% (well below CPVC), to ensure a dense, continuous, impermeable film.

*(7 marks: ~3 marks pigment roles, ~2.5 marks PVC/CPVC with sketch, ~1.5 marks engineering significance)*

---

**(b) Typical Paint Scheme for Underwater Hull of a Ship:**

A ship hull underwater area (below the lightship waterline) is subject to:
- **Continuous seawater exposure** (high chloride, dissolved O₂)
- **Cathodic protection** interaction (if fitted)
- **Biofouling** (barnacles, algae, mussels)
- **Mechanical abrasion** (berthing, anchor chain)
- **Galvanic corrosion** at propeller/brass fittings

**Typical 4-Coat Paint Scheme:**

| Coat | Paint Type | Binder | Pigment | DFT (μm) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Coat (Primer)** | Zinc-rich Primer | Epoxy / Inorganic Silicate | Metallic Zinc (>85%) | 50–75 | **Cathodic (galvanic) protection** of steel; provides active anti-corrosion. |
| **2nd Coat (Anti-corrosive)** | Epoxy MIO | Epoxy (Polyamide-cured) | Micaceous Iron Oxide (MIO) | 100–150 | **Barrier protection**; lamellar MIO flakes create tortuous path; high-build. |
| **3rd Coat (Tie Coat)** | Tie Coat / Seal Coat | Epoxy or Vinyl | Red oxide / Inert | 30–50 | **Bond integrity** between anti-corrosive and anti-fouling coats; prevents bleeding. |
| **4th Coat (Anti-fouling)** | Self-Polishing Copolymer (SPC) or Controlled Depletion Polymer (CDP) | Acrylic / Vinyl Copolymer | Cuprous Oxide (Cu₂O) + Biocide | 100–150 | **Fouling prevention**; controlled release of biocides; self-polishing action maintains smoothness. |
| **TOTAL** | — | — | — | **~280–425 μm** | Comprehensive protection |

**Alternative Schemes:**

1. **Coal Tar Epoxy (CTE) Scheme:** Used for cheaper vessels.
   - 1st: Zinc-rich epoxy primer (50 μm)
   - 2nd: Coal tar epoxy (150 μm)
   - 3rd: Coal tar epoxy (150 μm)
   - Total: ~350 μm
   - **Disadvantage:** Dark color, poor UV resistance, restricted use in potable water tanks.

2. **Pure Epoxy Scheme (High-Performance):** For chemical tankers, naval vessels.
   - 1st: Zinc-rich epoxy (75 μm)
   - 2nd: Epoxy MIO (125 μm)
   - 3rd: Pure epoxy (solvent-free) (200 μm)
   - 4th: SPC anti-fouling (150 μm)
   - Total: ~550 μm

**Critical Notes on Application:**
- **Surface Preparation:** Sa 2.5 (ISO 8501-1) blast cleaning — commercial blast.
- **Profile:** 50–85 μm roughness.
- **DFT Verification:** Magnetic or eddy-current gauges at every coat.
- **Re-coat Intervals:** Strictly observed per manufacturer data sheet.
- **Compatibility:** Each coat must be from the same manufacturer system.

*(7 marks: ~3 marks scheme explanation, ~2 marks alternative schemes, ~2 marks application requirements)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Binder" with "Pigment":** Many students interchange these. Binder is the *film-forming polymer*; pigment is the *solid powder* providing color/inhibition.
>
> 2. **Forgetting CPVC Concept:** When asked about pigments, students list examples but forget to mention PVC/CPVC — a 2–3 mark loss.
>
> 3. **Wrong DFT Values:** Common mistake is specifying 50 μm for underwater hull primers. Correct: 75 μm minimum for zinc-rich, 100–150 μm for anti-corrosive build coats.
>
> 4. **Omitting Tie Coat:** Many schemes skip the tie coat, leading to anti-fouling delamination. Always mention its critical role.
>
> 5. **Misidentifying Bitumen Forms:** "Cutback" and "emulsion" are NOT the same. Cutback uses organic solvent; emulsion uses water. Examiner will deduct for confusion.
>
> 6. **Epoxy UV Weakness:** Students often praise epoxy as "best in everything." Examiner expects you to state that epoxy *chalks under UV* — this is a classic 1–2 mark question.
>
> 7. **No Mention of Surface Preparation:** In any paint scheme question, mention *Sa 2.5 blast cleaning* and *surface profile*. KTU examiners allocate marks here.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

### A. Constituents of Paint (Must Know All)
- **Binder (Polymerizing Chemical)** → Film formation. Examples: Epoxy, Alkyd, PU, Chlorinated Rubber.
- **Pigment** → Color + Corrosion inhibition + UV barrier. Examples: Red oxide, MIO, Zinc chromate.
- **Extender (Filler)** → Cost reduction + reinforcement. Examples: Talc, Mica, BaSO₄.
- **Solvent** → Application viscosity. Examples: Xylene, Toluene.
- **Additive** → Specialized performance. Examples: Driers, Plasticizers, Biocides.

### B. Binders — Quick Memory Map
- **Best Water Resistance:** Epoxy, Chlorinated Rubber
- **Best UV Resistance:** Polyurethane, Acrylic
- **Cheapest Marine Binder:** Alkyd, Bitumen
- **Best Adhesion:** Epoxy
- **Best Re-coatability:** Chlorinated Rubber, Alkyd
- **Best Chemical Resistance:** Epoxy, Phenolic

### C. Critical Equations (Must Memorize)
- $\text{PVC} = \dfrac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100$
- $\text{WFT} = \text{DFT} \times \dfrac{100}{\text{Volume Solids \%}}$
- $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \dfrac{\text{Volume Solids \%}}{100}$

### D. Bitumen — Critical Points
- Composed of **Asphaltenes + Resins + Oils**
- Softens at **60–80 °C** — not for hot service
- **Excellent water resistance** — ideal for underwater and splash zone
- Forms: **Cutback, Emulsion, Hot-applied, Mastic, Modified (SBS/APP/Epoxy)**
- Penetration grades: **30/40, 60/70, 80/100** (lower = harder)

### E. Underwater Hull Paint Scheme (4 Coats)
1. **Zinc-rich epoxy primer** (50–75 μm) — Galvanic protection
2. **Epoxy MIO** (100–150 μm) — Barrier protection
3. **Tie coat** (30–50 μm) — Bond integrity
4. **Self-polishing anti-fouling** (100–150 μm) — Fouling prevention
- **Total DFT:** ~280–425 μm
- **Surface Prep:** Sa 2.5 blast cleaning

### F. KTU Frequently Asked Topics
- ✅ Difference between pigment and extender
- ✅ PVC and CPVC concept
- ✅ Wet film vs. dry film thickness calculation
- ✅ Epoxy vs. polyurethane comparison
- ✅ Why bitumen is used in marine coatings
- ✅ Typical underwater hull paint scheme
- ✅ Role of MIO pigment
- ✅ Function of tie coat

### G. Standard Values to Remember
- **Sa 2.5** — Commercial blast cleaning (most common for marine)
- **Volume solids for epoxy:** 50–70%
- **DFT for marine primers:** 75–200 μm
- **Surface profile:** 50–85 μm
- **CPVC range for marine primers:** 30–50%

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boot Top Above Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Boot Top Above Water # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In marine coating engineering, the **Boot Top** is the **narrow vertical band of a ship's hull plating located between the Light Waterline (LWL) and the Plimsoll Load Waterline (LWL-loaded)**. This region is unique because it is **periodically immersed and alternately exposed** to the marine atmosphere depending on the vessel's draft (loaded vs. ballast condition). The term **"Boot Top Above Water"** specifically refers to the **upper portion of the boot topping region** that remains visible above the operating waterline during the vessel's **light or ballast condition**. This sub-zone is the most demanding region of the hull from a coating perspective, because: - It is **continuously subjected to the splash zone** — direct wave impact, spray, and intermittent wetting/drying cycles. - It receives **maximum ultraviolet (UV) radiation** since it is rarely submerged. - It must satisfy **regulatory visibility requirements** (IMO Load Line Convention, 1966) — hence the traditional **red, black, or dark contrast paint**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The boot top above water is the transition zone between the **submerged hull (antifouling zone)** and the **topside (decorative/anti-corrosive zone)**. It demands a coating system that simultaneously resists **corrosion, biofouling initiation, abrasion from berthing, and UV degradation**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tide Line" on Your Beach Shoes Imagine you are standing at the seashore wearing rubber boots. The part of the boot that is **always wet** stays flexible but accumulates barnacles (like the antifouling zone). The part that is **always dry** fades and cracks from the sun (like the topside). But the **middle strip** — the area that the waves kiss every minute, sometimes wet, sometimes dry, sometimes salty, sometimes sun-baked — this is where the rubber **cracks fastest, fades fastest, and peels fastest**. That middle strip is the **Boot Top Above Water** on a ship. It is the **corrosion hotspot** of the hull, and marine engineers have developed a dedicated paint system exclusively for this region. ## 1.3 Why This Zone Demands Special Attention > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Facts to Memorize:** > - **Temperature differential:** Up to **30–40 °C** between wet and dry cycles. > - **Oxygen availability:** Maximum — alternates between dissolved O₂ (wet) and atmospheric O₂ (dry). > - **Salinity:** Concentrated salt crystals deposit during drying phases → aggressive chloride attack. > - **Mechanical impact:** Fender abrasion, rope chafe, gangway impact. > - **Regulatory:** Must contrast with topside and bottom paint per **SOLAS / IMO Load Line** visibility rules. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hull Zone Classification (Vertical Reference) A ship's underwater and waterline hull is conventionally divided into **four corrosion zones**. Understanding these is mandatory for KTU 2024. | Zone | Location | Exposure Type | Coating Priority | |------|----------|---------------|------------------| | **Bottom (Underwater)** | Below LWL-loaded | Continuous immersion | Antifouling + Antitrust | | **Boot Top** | Between LWL-light & LWL-loaded | Alternating wet/dry | Dual-purpose: AF + AC + UV | | **Boot Top Above Water** | Upper boot top, normally visible in light condition | Splash, spray, UV, abrasion | High-build AC + UV-resistant finish | | **Topside** | Above boot top | Atmospheric | Decorative AC | ## 2.2 Properties Required of a Boot Top Above Water Coating A board-exam-correct coating system for this zone must exhibit: 1. **High build capability** — typical Dry Film Thickness (DFT) of **250–400 µm** total system. 2. **Excellent adhesion** to epoxy primer — pull-off strength **> 10 MPa**. 3. **Abrasion resistance** — Taber abrasion loss < **50 mg / 1000 cycles**. 4. **UV stability** — ΔE (colour shift) < **2.0** after 1000 h QUV exposure. 5. **Salt spray resistance** — **> 2000 hours** per ASTM B117 without blistering. 6. **Flexibility** — Pass **1/4 inch mandrel bend test** at 23 °C. 7. **Chemical resistance** to fuels, bilge water, and mild acids/alkalis. ## 2.3 Recommended Coating System (KTU High-Yield) The industry-standard paint scheme for boot top above water uses the following **layered architecture**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard 4-Layer Boot Top Above Water Paint System:** > 1. **Surface Preparation:** Grit blast to **ISO 8501-1 Sa 2½** (near-white metal), surface profile **50–75 µm**. > 2. **Primer (1st coat):** Zinc-rich epoxy or pure epoxy anticorrosive — DFT **75 µm**. > 3. **Intermediate Coat:** Coal-tar epoxy OR high-build epoxy — DFT **125–150 µm**. > 4. **Finish / Boot Top Coat:** Chlorinated rubber, vinyl, or polyurethane (typically **RED** for visibility) — DFT **50–75 µm**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Paint Quantity, DFT, WFT & Coverage These formulas appear frequently in 2-mark and 14-mark numerical problems. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |-----------|---------|-------|-------| | **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** | $WFT = \dfrac{DFT \times 100}{VS}$ | microns (µm) | VS = Volume Solids (%) | | **Theoretical Coverage (TC)** | $TC = \dfrac{VS \times 10}{DFT}$ | m² / L | DFT in µm | | **Practical Coverage (PC)** | $PC = TC \times LF$ | m² / L | LF = Loss Factor (typically 0.65–0.80) | | **Paint Quantity (V)** | $V = \dfrac{A \times DFT}{10 \times VS \times LF}$ | Litres | A = Area in m² | | **Number of Coats (n)** | $n = \dfrac{Total\,DFT}{DFT\,per\,coat}$ | dimensionless | Round UP to integer | | **Boot Top Area (approx.)** | $A_{BT} = \pi \times D \times H_{BT} \times (1 + f)$ | m² | D = Hull girth, H_BT = boot top height, f = curvature factor (0.1–0.2) | | **Vertical boot top height (typical)** | $H_{BT} \approx 0.3 - 0.6\,m$ | metres | Between light & load waterlines | > [!WARNING] > **Vertical Pipe Restriction Respected:** All mathematical separators use $\mid$ or $\vert$ form to preserve markdown table integrity. **Never use raw `\|` inside table cells.** ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Shipyards (dry-dock maintenance):** Estimating paint quantity before blasting. - **Classification Societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS):** Verifying DFT using **elcometer** during survey. - **Naval Architecture:** Selecting boot top height based on **freeboard calculations** and load line rules. - **Failure Analysis:** 70% of premature boot top failures occur due to **inadequate surface preparation**, not paint chemistry. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Full KTU Exam Style > **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A coastal cargo vessel has a hull girth (boot top circumference) of **120 m** at the boot top level. The boot top vertical height is **0.5 m**, and a curvature factor of **0.15** applies. The coating specification is: > - 1 coat of epoxy primer at **75 µm DFT**, Volume Solids = **60%**. > - 1 coat of coal-tar epoxy at **125 µm DFT**, Volume Solids = **75%**. > - 1 coat of chlorinated rubber finish at **50 µm DFT**, Volume Solids = **45%**. > > The loss factor for spray application is **0.70**. Calculate: > **(a)** The total boot top above water area. > **(b)** The total paint quantity (in litres) required for the entire system. ### Step 1 — Compute the Boot Top Area The boot top is essentially a curved rectangular band wrapping the hull. The projected area is multiplied by a curvature factor to account for the hull's bilge curvature. $$ A_{BT} = \pi \times D \times H_{BT} \times (1 + f) $$ Substitute D = 120 m, H_BT = 0.5 m, f = 0.15: $$ A_{BT} = \pi \times 120 \times 0.5 \times (1 + 0.15) $$ $$ A_{BT} = \pi \times 120 \times 0.5 \times 1.15 $$ $$ A_{BT} = \pi \times 69.0 $$ $$ A_{BT} \approx 216.77\,m^2 $$ > **Logic Note:** The factor $(1 + f)$ inflates the flat-projected area (which would be $D \times H_{BT} = 60\,m^2$) to a curved surface area. Without this factor, you would under-purchase paint by ~13%. ### Step 2 — Paint Quantity per Coat The general formula: $$ V_i = \dfrac{A \times DFT_i}{10 \times VS_i \times LF} $$ #### Coat 1 — Epoxy Primer (DFT = 75 µm, VS = 60%) $$ V_1 = \dfrac{216.77 \times 75}{10 \times 60 \times 0.70} $$ $$ V_1 = \dfrac{16257.75}{420.0} $$ $$ V_1 \approx 38.71\,L $$ #### Coat 2 — Coal-Tar Epoxy (DFT = 125 µm, VS = 75%) $$ V_2 = \dfrac{216.77 \times 125}{10 \times 75 \times 0.70} $$ $$ V_2 = \dfrac{27096.25}{525.0} $$ $$ V_2 \approx 51.61\,L $$ #### Coat 3 — Chlorinated Rubber Finish (DFT = 50 µm, VS = 45%) $$ V_3 = \dfrac{216.77 \times 50}{10 \times 45 \times 0.70} $$ $$ V_3 = \dfrac{10838.5}{315.0} $$ $$ V_3 \approx 34.41\,L $$ ### Step 3 — Total Quantity $$ V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 $$ $$ V_{total} = 38.71 + 51.61 + 34.41 $$ $$ \boxed{V_{total} \approx 124.73\,L} $$ ### Step 4 — Verification of Total System DFT $$ DFT_{system} = 75 + 125 + 50 = 250\,\mu m $$ This matches the KTU syllabus range of **250–400 µm** for boot top above water systems. ✔ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Failure Modes in Boot Top Above Water | Failure Mode | Root Cause | Visual Symptom | Preventive Measure | |--------------|------------|----------------|---------------------| | **Blistering** | Osmotic pressure, salt contamination | Dome-shaped bubbles | Sa 2½ prep + freshwater wash | | **Delamination** | Poor inter-coat adhesion | Peeling sheets | Strict recoat intervals | | **Chalking** | UV degradation of binder | Powdery white surface | Use polyurethane topcoat | | **Rust spotting** | Low DFT, pinholes | Red-brown dots | Holiday detector check | | **Edge failure** | Low film build at sharp radii | Premature rust at welds | Stripe coat application | | **Fouling creep** | Insufficient biocide release | Barnacle line at waterline | Extend AF coat 50 mm above WL | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Hull Zone Architecture & Boot Top Location ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Topside Zone
Above boot top
Decorative + AC] --> B[Boot Top Above Water
UV + Splash + Abrasion
RED contrast paint] B --> C[Boot Top Below Waterline
Intermittent immersion
AF + AC hybrid] C --> D[Bottom Underwater Zone
Permanent immersion
Antifouling dominant] B --> E[Light Waterline LWL-light] C --> F[Load Waterline LWL-loaded] D --> G[Keel Plate] B --> H[Coating Layers Applied:
Primer 75um
CT Epoxy 125um
Finish 50um
Total DFT 250um] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Layered Coating Stack-Up (Above Water Boot Top) ```mermaid flowchart LR L0[Substrate Mild Steel Hull Plate] --> L1[Surface Prep ISO Sa 2.5 Profile 50 to 75um] L1 --> L2[Coat 1 Zinc Rich Epoxy Primer DFT 75um] L2 --> L3[Coat 2 Coal Tar Epoxy Intermediate DFT 125um] L3 --> L4[Coat 3 Chlorinated Rubber or PU Finish DFT 50um RED Colour] L4 --> L5[Marine Environment Splash Spray UV Salt] L3 --> M1[Stripe Coat at Welds and Edges Extra 50um] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Functional Decision Flow for Coating Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Identify Hull Zone] --> Q{Zone Above or Below LWL-light} Q -->|Above| R[Boot Top Above Water] Q -->|Below| S[Boot Top Below Waterline] R --> T{Critical Property} T -->|UV Resistance| U[Polyurethane Topcoat] T -->|Abrasion| V[High Build Epoxy with Stripe Coat] T -->|Visibility| W[Red or Black Contrast Pigment] S --> X[Antifouling Dominant System] R --> Y[Apply Total DFT 250 to 400um] X --> Y Y --> Z[Inspect via DFT Gauge and Holiday Detector] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric prefixed (A, L0, L1, M1, P, Q, etc.). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as standalone nodes. Labels contain raw uppercase text only — no bold/italic markdown embedded inside double-quoted strings. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question] **Define "Boot Top" in marine painting terminology. Why is the boot top above water considered a critical corrosion zone?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) **Definition:** The boot top is the narrow vertical band of the ship's hull located between the light waterline and the load (Plimsoll) waterline. It is alternately immersed and exposed depending on vessel loading. - (1 mark) **Location of "Above Water" portion:** The upper part of this band that remains visible during light/ballast condition, extending from the light waterline upward. - (1 mark) **Why critical:** It simultaneously experiences **UV radiation, splash/spray wetting-drying cycles, salt deposition, oxygen availability, and mechanical abrasion** — making it the most aggressive corrosion zone on the topside hull. Standard hull paints cannot survive here; a dedicated 3-coat system totalling **250–400 µm DFT** is mandated. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted] **List any four properties required of a paint system applied on the boot top above water region.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):** 1. **High build / high DFT capability** (250–400 µm total system) to withstand abrasion. 2. **Excellent UV and weather resistance** to prevent chalking and colour fade. 3. **Strong adhesion and flexibility** to endure thermal cycling (–10 °C to +40 °C). 4. **Resistance to salt water, fuels, and mild chemicals** encountered in port. *(Acceptable alternatives: abrasion resistance, anti-corrosive pigmentation, contrasting colour for visibility per IMO Load Line rules, compatibility with antifouling coat below.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Length Board Question **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3 Choice Pattern]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **vertical zonation of a ship's hull** with reference to corrosion exposure. Clearly mark the position of the boot top above water region. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe in detail the **recommended 4-stage surface preparation and 3-coat paint system** for the boot top above water, including DFT, type of paint, and function of each coat. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Vertical Hull Zonation (7 marks):** | Sub-part | Content | Marks | |----------|---------|-------| | Zones listed | Topside, Boot Top Above Water, Boot Top Below WL, Underwater Bottom, Bilge Keel | 2 | | Exposure characteristics of each | Atmospheric, Splash/UV, Intermittent immersion, Continuous immersion | 2 | | Position of boot top above water | Between light waterline (top of zone) and load waterline (bottom of zone); usually 0.3–0.6 m height | 2 | | Critical nature | Highest corrosion rate due to alternating wet-dry, oxygen availability, salt deposition | 1 | **Sketch Description (to be drawn by student):** A side elevation of a ship showing: - WL-light and WL-load lines marked. - Boot top band (typically red) shaded. - Arrows indicating wave splash direction. --- **(b) Recommended Paint System for Boot Top Above Water (7 marks):** | Stage | Operation | Specification | Function | Marks | |-------|-----------|---------------|----------|-------| | 1 | Surface Preparation | Grit blast to **ISO 8501-1 Sa 2½**, profile 50–75 µm | Remove mill scale, rust, old paint; provide anchor profile | 2 | | 2 | Primer Coat (1st) | Zinc-rich epoxy / pure epoxy, DFT **75 µm**, VS ~60% | Sacrificial / barrier anti-corrosion | 2 | | 3 | Intermediate Coat (2nd) | Coal-tar epoxy OR high-build epoxy, DFT **125–150 µm**, VS ~75% | Build film thickness, water resistance | 1.5 | | 4 | Finish / Boot Top Coat (3rd) | Chlorinated rubber / Vinyl / PU, DFT **50–75 µm**, VS ~45%, **RED colour** | UV resistance, abrasion, regulatory visibility | 1.5 | **Total System DFT:** ~250–300 µm. **Stripe coats** mandatory at edges and welds. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Alternative Question]** **(a)** Discuss the **causes and types of paint failures** commonly observed on the boot top above water region. How can each be prevented? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A ship's boot top has a curved surface area of **300 m²**. Compute the **practical coverage per litre** and **total litres required** for a 2-coat epoxy system where each coat is specified at **100 µm DFT**, Volume Solids = **65%**, and Loss Factor = **0.70**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Causes and Prevention of Paint Failures on Boot Top Above Water (7 marks):** | Failure | Cause | Prevention | Marks | |---------|-------|------------|-------| | **Blistering** | Salt contamination, osmotic pressure | Freshwater wash, Sa 2½ prep, correct DFT | 1.5 | | **Delamination / Peeling** | Exceeded recoat window, poor surface prep | Strict recoat interval, dew point control | 1.5 | | **Chalking / Fading** | UV attack on binder (esp. epoxy alone) | PU topcoat with UV stabilizers | 1.5 | | **Edge Failure / Rust Creep** | Low film build at sharp edges | **Stripe coat** at all welds, cut-edges | 1.5 | | **Fouling Creep (at WL)** | Antifouling coat stops short of boot top | Extend AF coat 50 mm above WL into boot top | 1 | --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** A = 300 m², DFT_per_coat = 100 µm, VS = 65%, LF = 0.70, n = 2 coats. **Step 1 — Theoretical Coverage per coat:** $$ TC = \dfrac{VS \times 10}{DFT} = \dfrac{65 \times 10}{100} = 6.5\,m^2/L $$ **Step 2 — Practical Coverage per coat:** $$ PC = TC \times LF = 6.5 \times 0.70 = 4.55\,m^2/L $$ **Step 3 — Paint required for 1 coat:** $$ V_1 = \dfrac{A}{PC} = \dfrac{300}{4.55} \approx 65.93\,L $$ **Step 4 — Paint required for 2 coats:** $$ V_{total} = 2 \times 65.93 = \boxed{131.87\,L} $$ **Step 5 — Verification using master formula:** $$ V_{total} = \dfrac{A \times (DFT_{total})}{10 \times VS \times LF} = \dfrac{300 \times 200}{10 \times 65 \times 0.70} = \dfrac{60000}{455} \approx 131.87\,L \checkmark $$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost 3–5 Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:** > 1. **Forgetting the curvature factor (1+f)** when computing boot top area — examiners specifically test this with a "flat" vs "curved" distinction. You will lose **2 full marks**. > 2. **Mixing up WFT and DFT formulas** — remember: WFT > DFT always, because solvent evaporates. If your WFT comes out less than DFT, your Volume Solids value is wrong. > 3. **Not specifying units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per quantity** missing units. > 4. **Ignoring stripe coats** in paint scheme description — this is a **favourite 1-mark catch question**. > 5. **Writing "boot top paint" generically** without naming chemistry (coal-tar epoxy, chlorinated rubber, PU, vinyl) — examiners expect **specific resin chemistry**. > 6. **Confusing light waterline vs load waterline** — the boot top is *between* them; if you place it below LWL-load, the diagram is **wrong**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Boot Top** = band between Light Waterline and Load Waterline (~0.3–0.6 m vertical). - **Boot Top Above Water** = upper part visible in light/ballast condition; the **most aggressive corrosion zone** of topside. - **Standard system:** 3 coats totalling **250–400 µm DFT** — Primer (epoxy) + Intermediate (CT epoxy) + Finish (chlorinated rubber / PU). - **Surface prep:** ISO **Sa 2½**, profile **50–75 µm** — non-negotiable. - **Visibility colour:** Traditionally **red or black** for IMO Load Line contrast. - **Key formulas:** $WFT = \dfrac{DFT \times 100}{VS}$ and $V = \dfrac{A \times DFT}{10 \times VS \times LF}$. - **Curvature factor (1+f)** must be applied to convert projected area to actual hull area. - **Stripe coats** mandatory at welds and edges — frequently asked as a 1-mark short question. - **Failure modes to memorize:** Blistering, Delamination, Chalking, Edge Failure, Fouling Creep. - **Standards referenced:** ISO 8501-1, ASTM B117 (salt spray), IMO Load Line Convention 1966. - **Inspection tools:** **Elcometer** (DFT gauge), **Holiday detector** (for pinholes), **Pull-off adhesion tester**.

topics.title: Weather Decks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Weather Decks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Weather Deck** on a marine vessel is defined (per **ISO 12944** and **IACS S24 Standards**) as any deck or portion of a deck that is **permanently exposed to the open marine atmosphere**, including direct sunlight, salt-laden wind, cyclic wetting/drying, and mechanical abrasion from foot traffic, cargo handling, and wave impact. Weather decks represent the **most corrosive service environment** encountered on a ship, classified under **Corrosivity Category C5-M (Very High - Marine)** in ISO 12944.

The primary structural components of a weather deck system include:
- **Deck Plating** (typically 4 mm to 12 mm mild steel, AH36/DH36 grade)
- **Underdeck Stiffeners** (T-bars, L-bars, flat bars)
- **Deck Coatings** (multi-layer anti-corrosive + anti-slip systems)
- **Sheathing/Coverings** (teak, synthetic tiles, rubberized compounds)
- **Drainage Scuppers** and **Bulwarks**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Weather decks are *not* just structural plates — they are **integrated corrosion protection systems** combining material selection, coating chemistry, cathodic protection (where applicable), and mechanical design. Examiners frequently test the *interrelationship* between these subsystems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Think of a ship's weather deck like the **roof of a coastal house facing the ocean**. While the house roof is hit by rain and salty wind occasionally, a ship's weather deck lives in this environment **24/7, 365 days a year**, plus it has to support people walking, cargo being dragged across it, and equipment being dropped on it. Worse, every wave crashing over the bow sends **salt water directly onto the deck**, which then evaporates and leaves behind salt crystals that aggressively attack the steel underneath.

It is essentially a **battle zone** where **four enemies attack simultaneously**:
1. **Salt (Cl⁻ ions)** – eats through coatings
2. **UV Radiation** – breaks down paint binders
3. **Mechanical Wear** – scrapes off protective layers
4. **Moisture Cycling** – prevents any drying/regeneration

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric:** A typical unprotected weather deck steel plate in tropical waters corrodes at **0.3 to 1.0 mm/year**, while a properly coated system reduces this to **< 0.05 mm/year** — a **20× improvement** driven entirely by the protective system design.

## 1.3 Classification of Weather Decks (Per KTU 2024 Syllabus)

| Deck Type | Location | Primary Exposure | Corrosion Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forecastle Deck | Forward of bridge | Heavy spray, green seas | **Very High** |
| Main Deck (Aft) | Midships/aft | General atmosphere | **High** |
| Poop Deck | Aft of superstructure | Moderate, sheltered | **Moderate** |
| Boat Deck | Above superstructure | UV dominant | **Moderate** |
| Helicopter Deck | Aft/forward extreme | Impact + UV | **High** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Corrosivity Category C5-M (ISO 12944-2):** Atmospheric environments with **high salinity** where the mass loss of zinc exceeds **610 g/m²** and steel exceeds **1500 g/m²** after 1 year of exposure. This is the **mandatory design benchmark** for all unprotected marine weather deck steelwork.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Stage Corrosion Mechanism on Weather Decks

The corrosion process on an unprotected weather deck follows a **predictable, cyclical, four-stage mechanism**:

**Stage 1 – Salt Deposition (Electrolyte Formation)**
- Airborne salt spray (NaCl, MgCl₂) deposits on the deck surface
- Critical salt deposition rate in tropical waters: **> 60 mg Cl⁻/m²·day**
- Hygroscopic salts absorb moisture → **electrolyte film forms at RH > 40%**

**Stage 2 – Wet/Dry Cycling (Time-of-Wetness)**
- ISO 9223 defines **Time of Wetness (TOW)** as hours where RH > 80% AND T > 0°C
- TOW on weather decks: **4000–6000 hours/year** (tropical), **2000–3000 hours/year** (temperate)
- Each cycle = 1 wet period + 1 dry period → accelerates **differential aeration cells**

**Stage 3 – Coating Breakdown (Defect Initiation)**
- UV degrades organic binders → chalking, micro-cracks
- Cl⁻ ions penetrate → osmotic blistering
- Mechanical abrasion enlarges defects → **cathodic areas** (intact coating) surround **anodic areas** (defects)

**Stage 4 – Active Steel Corrosion (Propagation)**
- Anode reaction: $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$
- Cathode reaction: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$
- Products: $\text{Fe(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{FeOOH}$ (rust) — **4× volume expansion** → coating delamination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind cyclic corrosion:** Wet/dry cycling is **far more damaging** than continuous immersion because during drying, oxygen concentration cells form under salt deposits (the **salt-cake differential aeration cell**), producing corrosion rates **5–10× higher** than fully immersed steel.

## 2.2 Protective Coating System Architecture (Per IMO PSPC & ISO 12944-5)

A KTU-compliant weather deck coating is a **multi-layer laminate**, not a single paint. The standard system (Total DFT = 250–500 μm):

| Layer | Function | Typical Material | DFT (μm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primer (1st coat) | Adhesion + corrosion inhibition | Epoxy with zinc phosphate | 50–75 |
| Primer (2nd coat) | Barrier + build-up | High-build epoxy | 100–150 |
| Tie coat | Inter-coat adhesion | Polyurethane/epoxy hybrid | 50–75 |
| Topcoat (anti-slip) | UV resistance + traction | Polyurethane + aluminum oxide/PSU beads | 75–150 |
| **TOTAL** | — | — | **275–450** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Zinc-rich primers (≥ 80% Zn dust by weight in dry film)** provide **cathodic (sacrificial) protection** to exposed steel at coating defects — this is the **galvanic action** of the weather deck system.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 6 equations** — they cover approximately 85% of all numerical problems on weather deck corrosion/protection in KTU examinations.

| # | Equation | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | CR = corrosion rate (mm/yr), W = mass loss (mg), D = density (g/cm³), A = area (cm²), T = time (hr) | Calculate deck plate wastage |
| 2 | $\text{CR}_{\text{mpy}} = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | Same as above, result in mils/yr (mpy) | Imperial unit problems |
| 3 | $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times V_s$ | DFT = dry film thickness, WFT = wet film thickness, $V_s$ = volume solids fraction | Quality control of coatings |
| 4 | $\text{Cov} = \dfrac{V_s \times 10}{\text{DFT}}$ | Cov = theoretical coverage (m²/L), DFT in μm | Estimate paint quantity |
| 5 | $I_{\text{req}} = i_c \times A_{\text{protected}}$ | $I_{\text{req}}$ = total current (A), $i_c$ = current density (A/m²), A = protected area | Cathodic protection design |
| 6 | $N_a = \dfrac{I_{\text{req}} \times L \times 8760}{C_a \times U_a}$ | $N_a$ = number of anodes, L = design life (yr), $C_a$ = anode capacity (Ah/kg), $U_a$ = utilization factor (typically 0.85) | Anode quantity calculation |
| 7 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z_{\text{section}}}$ | $\sigma$ = bending stress, $M_{\max}$ = max bending moment, $Z_{\text{section}}$ = section modulus | Deck plate thickness check |
| 8 | $t = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times M_{\max}}{w \times \sigma_{\text{allow}}}}$ | t = required thickness, w = panel width, $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$ = allowable stress | Minimum plate thickness |
| 9 | $R_{\text{coat}} = \dfrac{\rho \times t_{\text{coat}}}{A}$ | $R_{\text{coat}}$ = coating electrical resistance, $\rho$ = resistivity, $t_{\text{coat}}$ = coating thickness, A = area | Coating barrier efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equation (1) and (2) are routinely used during **in-service surveys** (every 2.5–5 years) by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) to determine whether a weather deck plate has corroded below the **allowable wastage limit** (typically 25% of original thickness before mandatory renewal).

## 2.4 Galvanic Series Context (Critical for Weather Decks)

Weather decks often house **dissimilar metals**: steel plating, aluminum railings, copper-nickel piping, bronze fittings. The **galvanic series in seawater** dictates the corrosion risk:

- **Most Active (Anode):** Zinc → Magnesium → Aluminum → Steel → Cast Iron → Lead
- **Most Noble (Cathode):** Brass → Copper → Bronze → Monel → Titanium → Graphite

If aluminum railing brackets are bolted directly to a steel deck **without isolation gaskets**, the **galvanic corrosion rate of aluminum can exceed 5 mm/year** — destroying the bracket in under 6 months.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mitigation:** Always use **PVC or neoprene insulating gaskets** + **mastic sealing** at all dissimilar metal joints on weather decks. This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law (Equation 1)

### Starting Point — Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of metal dissolved (grams)
- $I$ = corrosion current (Amperes)
- $t$ = time (seconds)
- $M$ = atomic mass of the metal (g/mol)
- $n$ = valence (number of electrons transferred)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**

### Step 1 — Convert Time Units
Weather deck surveys are recorded in **hours**, not seconds.

$$t_{\text{sec}} = t_{\text{hr}} \times 3600$$

### Step 2 — Express Corrosion Current in Practical Units
For a small steel coupon of area $A$ (cm²) and known density $D$ (g/cm³):

$$I = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot t_{\text{sec}}}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Mass Loss to Linear Penetration Depth
The linear corrosion rate equals the volume of metal lost divided by the exposed area:

$$\text{Depth} = \frac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Area}} = \frac{m / D}{A}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Normalize to mm/year
Combining Steps 1–3 and converting cm → mm (×10) and hours → years (×8760):

$$\text{CR}_{\text{mm/yr}} = \frac{m \times 10 \times 8760}{D \times A \times t_{\text{hr}} \times 1000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}}$$

**Where $W$ is now expressed in milligrams (mg).** This is the **universal KTU corrosion rate formula** for steel and aluminum.

### Worked Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A weather deck steel plate sample (area = 100 cm², density = 7.85 g/cm³) is exposed for 6 months (4380 hours) in tropical waters. Mass loss measured = 4500 mg. Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/year.

**Solution:**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times 4500}{7.85 \times 100 \times 4380}$$

$$\text{CR} = \frac{394{,}200}{3{,}438{,}300}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CR} = 0.1146 \text{ mm/year}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a **moderately high** corrosion rate, typical of an unprotected tropical weather deck. The classification society **IACS** would flag this plate for **renewal at the next drydock** if wastage approaches 25% of original thickness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Coating Coverage (Equation 4)

### Starting Point — Volume Conservation
A wet film of thickness WFT (μm) is applied to an area $A$. The volume of liquid paint used is:

$$V_{\text{liquid}} = \text{WFT} \times A$$

### Step 1 — Account for Volatiles
Only the **solids fraction** $V_s$ remains after drying (solvents evaporate). The dry film thickness becomes:

$$\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times V_s$$

### Step 2 — Express in Practical Units
For 1 litre (1000 cm³) of paint covering area $A$ (m²):

$$\text{DFT} = \frac{V_s \times 10^4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{cm}^2 \times 10^3 \text{ μm/mm}}{A \times 10^4}$$

Simplifying:

$$\boxed{\text{Cov} = \frac{V_s \times 10}{\text{DFT}} \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{L}}$$

### Worked Example

**Problem:** A polyurethane topcoat has $V_s = 0.55$ (55% solids by volume). Target DFT = 125 μm. How many litres are required to coat 500 m² of weather deck?

**Solution:**

$$\text{Cov} = \frac{0.55 \times 10}{125} = 0.044 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$$

$$\text{Volume required} = \frac{500}{0.044} = 11{,}363.6 \text{ L}$$

**Note:** Add **20% wastage factor** for practical field application:

$$V_{\text{actual}} = 11{,}363.6 \times 1.20 = 13{,}636.4 \text{ L}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Anode Quantity for Cathodic Protection of Aluminum Fittings

### Problem Context
A weather deck has **aluminum rainwater pipe supports** requiring galvanic anode cathodic protection. The exposed aluminum area is $A = 8$ m², the design current density is $i_c = 0.015$ A/m² (aluminum in seawater splash zone), and the design life is $L = 5$ years. Anode specification: zinc anode with $C_a = 780$ Ah/kg and $U_a = 0.85$.

### Step 1 — Total Current Requirement

$$I_{\text{req}} = i_c \times A$$

$$I_{\text{req}} = 0.015 \times 8 = 0.12 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2 — Total Anode Capacity Required (Ah)

$$Q_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{req}} \times L \times 8760 \text{ h/yr}$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 0.12 \times 5 \times 8760 = 5256 \text{ Ah}$$

### Step 3 — Mass of Anode Material Required

$$m_{\text{anode}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{C_a \times U_a}$$

$$m_{\text{anode}} = \frac{5256}{780 \times 0.85} = 7.93 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4 — Number of Anodes
Standard zinc anode mass per unit = 5 kg. Therefore:

$$N_a = \frac{7.93}{5} = 1.586 \rightarrow \boxed{N_a = 2 \text{ anodes (rounded up)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Coating System by Service Zone

| Service Zone | Coating System | Total DFT (μm) | Expected Life (yrs) | Cost Factor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forecastle (extreme) | Zn-rich epoxy + glass flake epoxy + PU anti-slip | 500 | 7–10 | 1.8× |
| Main working deck | Epoxy primer ×2 + PU anti-slip | 350 | 5–7 | 1.0× |
| Boat/Poop deck | Epoxy primer + PU topcoat | 250 | 7–12 | 0.8× |
| Helicopter deck | High-build epoxy + PU + aluminum oxide broadcast | 600 | 4–6 | 2.0× |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-slip aggregates** (aluminum oxide, PSU beads) are embedded at **2.5–4.0 kg/m²** broadcast density into the topcoat. This is a **mandatory SOLAS / IMO MSC.1/Circ.1321** requirement for all working weather decks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Weather Deck Cross-Section — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **layered architecture** of a typical weather deck, showing the interactions between structural, protective, and operational subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[EXPOSURE ENVIRONMENT]
    A2[Salt Spray NaCl MgCl2]
    A3[UV Radiation 280 to 400 nm]
    A4[Mechanical Load Foot Traffic Cargo]
    A5[Wet Dry Cycling RH 40 to 95 percent]

    B1[WEATHER DECK ASSEMBLY]
    B2[ANTI SLIP TOPCOAT 75 to 150 um]
    B3[TIE COAT Polyurethane 50 to 75 um]
    B4[EPOXY PRIMER LAYER 2 100 to 150 um]
    B5[EPOXY PRIMER LAYER 1 Zn Rich 50 to 75 um]
    B6[STEEL DECK PLATE AH36 DH36 4 to 12 mm]
    B7[UNDERCECK STIFFENER T Bar L Bar]
    B8[CATHODIC ANODE Zn or Al Alloy]

    C1[CORROSION RESPONSE]
    C2[Barrier Protection DFT]
    C3[Cathodic Protection Zn Sacrificial]
    C4[Galvanic Isolation PVC Gasket]

    A1 --> A2
    A1 --> A3
    A1 --> A4
    A1 --> A5

    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    A5 --> B2

    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7
    B6 --> B8

    B2 --> C2
    B5 --> C3
    B6 --> C4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows on the **left side** (exposure) represent *attack vectors* on the deck. The arrows on the **right side** represent the **three protection mechanisms** working in parallel. The deck is a **layered defense system** — failure of any one layer increases the burden on the others.

---

## 4.2 Coating Failure Progression — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Salt Deposition] --> S2[Stage 2 Wet Dry Cycling]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Coating Defect Initiation]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Active Steel Corrosion]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Rust Volume Expansion 4x]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 Coating Delamination]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7 Pitting Perforation]

    S1 --- D1[Cl minus 60 mg per sqm per day]
    S3 --- D2[UV Cracking Osmotic Blistering]
    S4 --- D3[Fe to Fe2 plus plus 2e minus]
    S5 --- D4[FeOH2 to FeOOH Rust]
    S7 --- D5[Plate Renewal Mandatory]
```

---

## 4.3 Cathodic Protection Schematic for Weather Deck Fittings

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Cathode Protected Fitting Steel or Aluminum] -->|Electrons Flow| P2[Connection Cable]
    P2 --> P3[Zinc Sacrificial Anode]
    P3 -->|Ionic Path Through Seawater Splash| P1

    P3 --- P4[Anode Capacity 780 Ah per kg]
    P3 --- P5[Utilization Factor 0.85]
    P3 --- P6[Design Life 5 to 10 years]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a weather deck and state the three primary factors that distinguish its corrosion environment from that of an immersed hull.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A weather deck is the portion of a ship's structure **permanently exposed to the open marine atmosphere**, including sun, salt spray, rain, and mechanical traffic (1 mark).

The three distinguishing corrosion factors are:
1. **Time of Wetness (TOW):** Controlled by cyclic wetting/drying — never constant immersion (1 mark).
2. **UV Exposure:** Continuous photodegradation of organic coating binders (0.5 marks).
3. **Mechanical Abrasion:** Foot traffic and cargo handling physically remove coating (0.5 marks).

These factors place weather decks in **ISO 12944 Corrosivity Category C5-M**, the most severe atmospheric marine class.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Why are zinc-rich primers used as the first coat on weather deck steel plates, and what is the minimum zinc content by weight in the dry film?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Zinc-rich primers provide **cathodic (sacrificial) protection** to the underlying steel at coating defects, scratches, and pinholes (1.5 marks). The zinc particles (more electronegative than steel) preferentially corrode, **protecting the steel substrate** through galvanic action. This complements the **barrier protection** of the overlying epoxy layers (1 mark).

**Minimum zinc content:** **80% by weight in the dry film** (as per **ISO 12944-5** and **SSPC Paint 20, Level 1**) (0.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Full-Length Problem `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe, with a neat sketch, the **layered coating system** recommended by ISO 12944-5 for a working weather deck. State the function and typical dry film thickness (DFT) of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square steel coupon of side **5 cm** and density **7.85 g/cm³** is exposed on a ship's main deck for **1 year (8760 hours)**. The mass loss is recorded as **2.2 grams**. Calculate:
- (i) The corrosion rate in **mm/year** (4 marks)
- (ii) The corrosion rate in **mpy** (mils per year) (1 mark)
- (iii) Comment on the severity and likely service condition (2 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (a)

A weather deck coating system per **ISO 12944-5 / NORSOK M-501** consists of the following layers (each function described):

| Layer | Material | Function | DFT (μm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Primer (Zinc-rich) | Epoxy + 80% Zn dust | Sacrificial cathodic protection | 50–75 |
| 2. Epoxy mid-coat | High-build epoxy | Barrier, build thickness | 100–150 |
| 3. Tie coat | PU/epoxy hybrid | Inter-coat adhesion | 50–75 |
| 4. Anti-slip topcoat | PU + Al₂O₃ aggregate | UV resistance + traction | 75–150 |
| **Total DFT** | — | — | **275–450** |

**Sketch:**

```
|==================================|  ← Anti-slip topcoat (PU + Al2O3)
|----------------------------------|  ← Tie coat (PU/Epoxy)
|==================================|  ← Epoxy mid-coat (HBE)
|----------------------------------|  ← Zinc-rich epoxy primer
|##################################|  ← Steel deck plate (AH36)
|==================================|
|##########[T-stiffener]###########|  ← Underdeck structure
```

*(Marks: Layer identification — 2; Function — 2; DFT values — 2; Sketch — 1)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (b)

**Given:**
- Side = 5 cm → Area $A = 5 \times 5 = 25$ cm²
- Density $D = 7.85$ g/cm³
- Time $T = 8760$ hours
- Mass loss $W = 2.2$ g = 2200 mg

**(i) Corrosion rate in mm/year:**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$$

$$\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times 2200}{7.85 \times 25 \times 8760}$$

$$\text{CR} = \frac{192{,}720}{1{,}719{,}450}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CR} = 0.1121 \text{ mm/year}}$$

**(ii) Corrosion rate in mpy:**

$$\text{CR}_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{534 \times 2200}{7.85 \times 25 \times 8760}$$

$$\text{CR}_{\text{mpy}} = \frac{1{,}174{,}800}{1{,}719{,}450}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CR}_{\text{mpy}} = 0.683 \text{ mpy}}$$

**(iii) Severity comment:**

A corrosion rate of **0.11 mm/year** is **high** and characteristic of an **unprotected or inadequately coated weather deck** in a tropical marine atmosphere. **Class society acceptance limit** for wastage is typically **0.3–0.5 mm/year for unprotected steel**. This plate would reach **25% wastage** of an 8 mm plate (i.e., 2 mm) in approximately:

$$t = \frac{2 \text{ mm}}{0.1121 \text{ mm/yr}} = 17.8 \text{ years}$$

**Recommendation:** Apply a full **ISO 12944-5 C5-M coating system** (DFT ≥ 350 μm) to extend service life to 15+ years.

---

### Question B — Alternative Question `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **galvanic corrosion problem** encountered on weather decks when **aluminum railings or fittings** are bolted directly to steel structures. With a neat diagram, describe the **two mitigation measures** that must be implemented at the design stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A weather deck area of **800 m²** is to be protected with a 3-coat epoxy-polyurethane system. Specifications:
- Primer DFT: 75 μm, $V_s$ = 0.65
- Mid-coat DFT: 125 μm, $V_s$ = 0.60
- Topcoat DFT: 100 μm, $V_s$ = 0.55
- Wastage factor: **15%**

Calculate:
- (i) The total volume of paint required in **litres** (5 marks)
- (ii) The **number of 20-litre cans** to procure (1 mark)
- (iii) State **two quality control tests** performed on the applied coating (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (a)

When aluminum fittings are bolted directly to a steel deck, a **galvanic cell** forms because the two metals have different electrode potentials in the **saltwater electrolyte** (from spray, rain, condensation). Looking at the **galvanic series**:

- **Aluminum** is the **anode** (more active, $E^0 \approx -1.66$ V)
- **Steel** is the **cathode** (more noble, $E^0 \approx -0.76$ V)

The aluminum will corrode at an **accelerated rate**, sometimes exceeding **5 mm/year**, while the steel is protected (but generates a heavy cathodic deposit of hydroxides on its surface, which can also damage coatings).

**Galvanic Cell Diagram:**

```
   ALUMINUM (ANODE)        |        STEEL (CATHODE)
   Eo = -1.66 V            |        Eo = -0.76 V
   e⁻ e⁻ e⁻ ──────FLOW────┼────► e⁻ e⁻ e⁻
         ▲                 |                ▲
         | IONIC PATH      |                |
         | (Saltwater)     |                |
         |                 |                |
    Al → Al³⁺ + 3e⁻        |     O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻
   (METAL DISSOLVES)        |   (REDUCTION REACTION)
```

**Two Mitigation Measures (Design Stage):**

1. **Electrical Isolation** — Insert **PVC, neoprene, or EPDM gaskets and sleeves** between the aluminum fitting and the steel substrate at all bolt points (3 marks).
2. **Sealing/Encapsulation** — Apply a continuous **polysulfide or polyurethane sealant** around the joint to exclude the saltwater electrolyte, plus install a **sacrificial zinc anode** nearby to divert corrosion current (2 marks).

Additionally, **isolation bushes** in bolt holes and **fibre washers** under bolt heads are mandatory. (2 marks for diagram and overall coherence)

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (b)

**Given:** Area = 800 m², wastage = 15% (multiply final answer by 1.15).

**(i) Volume of paint per coat using** $\text{Cov} = \dfrac{V_s \times 10}{\text{DFT}}$:

**Primer:**
$$\text{Cov}_1 = \frac{0.65 \times 10}{75} = 0.0867 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$$

$$V_1 = \frac{800}{0.0867} = 9228 \text{ L}$$

**Mid-coat:**
$$\text{Cov}_2 = \frac{0.60 \times 10}{125} = 0.0480 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{800}{0.0480} = 16{,}666.7 \text{ L}$$

**Topcoat:**
$$\text{Cov}_3 = \frac{0.55 \times 10}{100} = 0.0550 \text{ m}^2/\text{L}$$

$$V_3 = \frac{800}{0.0550} = 14{,}545.5 \text{ L}$$

**Sub-total:**
$$V_{\text{sub}} = 9228 + 16{,}666.7 + 14{,}545.5 = 40{,}440.2 \text{ L}$$

**Apply 15% wastage:**
$$V_{\text{total}} = 40{,}440.2 \times 1.15 = 46{,}506.2 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{total}} \approx 46{,}506 \text{ L}}$$

**(ii) Number of 20-litre cans:**

$$N = \frac{46{,}506}{20} = 2325.3 \rightarrow \boxed{N = 2326 \text{ cans}}$$

**(iii) Two Quality Control Tests:**
1. **Dry Film Thickness (DFT) measurement** using a **magnetic or eddy-current gauge** (per **ISO 19840**).
2. **Holiday/Pinhole detection** using a **wet sponge or high-voltage DC holiday detector** (per **ASTM D5162**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to **convert units consistently** in the corrosion rate formula. A common mistake is mixing **cm² and m²** for area, or **mg and g** for mass loss. Always write down the units in a row before substitution. KTU examiners allocate **1 mark specifically for unit consistency** in numerical problems. Also remember: 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/year, so you can cross-check your answers using this conversion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Weather Decks"**

- ☐ **Definition:** Weather deck = permanently exposed deck; corrosivity **C5-M (Very High Marine)** per ISO 12944.
- ☐ **Four attack vectors:** Salt (Cl⁻) + UV + Abrasion + Wet/Dry cycling.
- ☐ **Coating System:** Minimum 4 layers → Zn-rich epoxy primer + epoxy mid-coat + tie coat + PU anti-slip topcoat. **Total DFT = 275–450 μm**.
- ☐ **Zinc-rich primer:** ≥ **80% Zn by weight in dry film** (SSPC Level 1, ISO 12944-5).
- ☐ **Anti-slip requirement:** SOLAS mandatory; aggregate broadcast = **2.5–4.0 kg/m²**.
- ☐ **Corrosion rate formula:** $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ with W in mg, A in cm², T in hr.
- ☐ **Imperial version:** $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ — remember the **6.1× ratio** (87.6/534 × ... actually 534/87.6 = 6.1, since 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr and 1 cm² = 0.155 in²).
- ☐ **Coverage formula:** $\text{Cov (m}^2/\text{L)} = \dfrac{V_s \times 10}{\text{DFT (μm)}}$.
- ☐ **Galvanic series (seawater):** **Zn → Al → Steel → Brass → Cu → Bronze → Ti → Graphite** (anode to cathode).
- ☐ **Dissimilar metal rule:** **Always use PVC/neoprene gaskets + sealant** at aluminum/steel joints.
- ☐ **Cathodic protection anode formula:** $N_a = \dfrac{I_{\text{req}} \times L \times 8760}{C_a \times U_a}$.
- ☐ **Standard Zn anode parameters:** $C_a = 780$ Ah/kg, $U_a = 0.85$.
- ☐ **Tropical deck TOW:** 4000–6000 hr/yr → unprotected corrosion rate = **0.3–1.0 mm/yr**.
- ☐ **Class society wastage limit:** Typically **25% of original thickness** before mandatory renewal.
- ☐ **Rust volume expansion:** **4× original steel volume** → causes coating delamination.
- ☐ **Key standards to remember:** **ISO 12944, ISO 9223, NORSOK M-501, IMO PSPC, SOLAS, ASTM D5162, SSPC Paint 20**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Superstructures and Tank Interiors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Superstructures and Tank Interiors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Superstructures** in marine engineering refer to all enclosed decked structures situated above the freeboard deck of a ship, extending either from side to side (full superstructure) or from one side to the centerline only (partial superstructure). According to SOLAS and IACS UR S21, they include deckhouses, poop, forecastle, bridge wings, mast houses, and funnels. They serve operational, navigational, habitational, and safety functions.

**Tank Interiors** refer to the internal surfaces of all liquid-carrying or liquid-containing compartments within a ship's hull envelope. These include ballast tanks (peak tanks, wing tanks, double bottom tanks), cargo oil tanks (COT), fuel oil tanks (FOT), freshwater tanks, lubricating oil tanks, sewage tanks, and bilge wells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> From a **corrosion engineering standpoint**, tank interiors are classified as the **most corrosive micro-environments** on a vessel because they combine: (a) continuous electrolyte contact, (b) alternate wet/dry cycling, (c) dissolved gases (O₂, CO₂, H₂S), and (d) temperature fluctuations. **IMO PSPC (MSC.215(82))** and **IACS UR Z17.1/Z23** prescribe mandatory protective coating performance standards with a target coating service life of **15 years**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a ship as a **floating layered sandwich**:

- **Outer Hull Plate (below waterline)** = the "skin in seawater" — fights against marine biofouling and galvanic corrosion.
- **Inner Hull / Tank Interiors** = the "wet lungs of the ship" — they breathe seawater in and out (ballasting), so they are like a sponge that is constantly squeezed and released.
- **Superstructures** = the "hat and coat of the ship" — exposed to rain, sun, salt spray, and salty humid air, but never fully submerged.

A superstructure is like the **outer wall of a coastal house** — it gets wet, dries, gets painted, repainted, and rusted around fasteners. A ballast tank is like the **inside of an old underground water cistern** — perpetually wet, alternately empty and full, with no sunlight, poor ventilation, and corners where silt collects and corrosion eats silently.

## 1.3 Key Terminology with Callouts

> [!NOTE]
> **Coating Life (Target Service Life — TSL):** The period (in years) for which the coating system is expected to maintain "GOOD" condition (ISO 4628 Ri 3 ≤ 1% / Re 3 ≤ 1%) without major repair. **IMO PSPC TSL = 15 years** for dedicated seawater ballast tanks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Splash Zone (Upper Deck Plate Region):** The narrow horizontal band on a ship's hull around the waterline, typically 1–2 m above and 1 m below the design waterline, subject to **continuous wet/dry cycling, UV, mechanical impact, and the most aggressive corrosion on a ship**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PSPC** = Performance Standard for Protective Coatings, adopted by IMO Maritime Safety Committee under **Resolution MSC.215(82)** for new buildings of all types of ships (excluding naval and short-sea vessels below 500 GT) with ballast tanks.

## 1.4 Classification of Superstructures (IACS UR S21 / SOLAS)

| Type | Location | Primary Function |
|------|----------|------------------|
| Poop | Aft, raised deck | Crew accommodation, steering gear access |
| Forecastle | Forward, raised deck | Mooring equipment protection, chain locker housing |
| Bridge / Navigation Deckhouse | Midships / forward | Navigation, communication, command |
| Deckhouse | Anywhere on freeboard deck | Workshops, galleys, hospital |
| Mast House | Around mast base | Signal lights, antenna mounting |
| Funnel | Above engine casing | Exhaust gas ducting |

## 1.5 Classification of Tank Interiors

| Tank Type | Cargo/Media | Criticality | IMO PSPC Applicable? |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Double Bottom (DB) Ballast | Sea water | Very High | Yes |
| Wing Ballast Tank | Sea water | Very High | Yes |
| Peak Tank (Fore/Aft) | Sea water / Fresh water | High | Yes |
| Cargo Oil Tank (COT) | Crude oil / refined products | High | Yes (COT PSPC – MSC.288(87)) |
| Fuel Oil Tank (FOT) | HFO / Diesel | Medium | Optional |
| Lub Oil Tank | Lub oil | Low | No |
| Fresh Water Tank | Potable water | Medium | No (NSF/SSPC standards) |
| Sewage / Bilge Tank | Waste | High | No |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Corrosion Mechanisms in Superstructures

Superstructure corrosion is dominated by **atmospheric corrosion** but aggravated by specific local mechanisms:

**Step 1 — Electrolyte Film Formation:** Salt-laden humid air (chloride concentration ~ 1–3% by mass near coasts) deposits a thin electrolyte film on all exposed steel surfaces. Critical humidity threshold: **> 60% RH** for carbon steel corrosion initiation.

**Step 2 — Galvanic Couples at Penetrations:** Bolts, rivets, hinges, mast steps, navigation light brackets, and dissimilar metal pipe penetrations (Cu-Ni, bronze, Monel) form galvanic cells against carbon steel. **Galvanic corrosion rate can be 10×–50× the uniform atmospheric rate.**

**Step 3 — Crevice and Pack Corrosion:** Behind brackets, under cable trays, around stiffener intersections, and at sealing gaskets — oxygen differential cells develop, accelerating localized attack.

**Step 4 — Condensation Cycling:** Daily thermal cycling (sun heating → night cooling) causes repeated condensation in unventilated voids, producing "sweat corrosion" on internal superstructure surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why superstructure painting fails:** Standard marine alkyd topcoats have chloride permeability 100× higher than epoxy systems. On splash zone and upper deck regions, alkyds blister and disbond within 3–5 years, requiring **abrasive blast re-preparation to Sa 2½ (ISO 8501-1)** before recoating.

## 2.2 Corrosion Mechanisms in Tank Interiors

Ballast tanks experience the **most severe corrosion environment** on any ship:

1. **Upper zone (above WL):** wet/dry cycling → oxygen concentration cells → pitting attack reaching 1.0–1.5 mm/year without coating.
2. **Mid zone (around WL):** continuous aeration → uniform corrosion 0.3–0.5 mm/year.
3. **Lower zone (permanently submerged):** anaerobic conditions → MIC (Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion) by sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB), producing FeS tubercles and deep pits up to **2–4 mm/year**.
4. **Splash zones on internal web frames and stiffeners:** edges corrode preferentially due to lower DFT (Dry Film Thickness) achievable on edges.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Symbols |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot \Delta m}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Mass-loss corrosion rate (ASTM G1) | CR in mm/yr; K = 8.76 × 10⁴; Δm in g; D = density (g/cm³); A in cm²; t in hours |
| 2 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot \Delta m}{M \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Faraday's law — corrosion current density | i_corr in A/m²; n = electrons transferred; F = 96485 C/mol; M = molar mass (g/mol) |
| 3 | $\eta = \pm \beta \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{i}{i_{0}}\right)$ | Tafel equation (overpotential) | η in V; β = Tafel slope (V/decade); i, i₀ in A/m² |
| 4 | $\Delta E = E_{cathode}^{o} - E_{anode}^{o}$ | Galvanic potential difference | ΔE in V (driving EMF) |
| 5 | $WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{\% \text{ solids by volume} / 100}$ | Wet-to-Dry Film Thickness conversion | WFT and DFT in microns (μm) |
| 6 | $TSL = \dfrac{DFT_{actual}}{CR_{protected}}$ | Estimated Coating Service Life | TSL in years; DFT in μm; CR in μm/yr |
| 7 | $I_{req} = J \cdot A_{wetted}$ | Cathodic protection current requirement | I_req in A; J = current density (A/m²); A in m² |
| 8 | $m_{Zn} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{Zn}}{n \cdot F}$ | Sacrificial anode mass consumption | m in kg; I in A; t in seconds |
| 9 | $R_{a} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$ | Anode-to-cathode electrolyte resistance | R in Ω; ρ = seawater resistivity (~ 0.25 Ω·m) |
| 10 | $L_{corr} = \dfrac{CR \cdot Y \cdot SF}{1000}$ | Corrosion allowance for plate thickness | L in mm; CR in mm/yr; Y = design life (yr); SF = safety factor (1.0–2.0) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition for PSPC Acceptance (IMO MSC.215(82)):**
> - 98% of all measurements ≥ nominal DFT
> - Remaining 2% ≥ 0.8 × nominal DFT
> - No individual reading < 0.8 × nominal DFT

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Corrosion rate (CR)** is used by **classification societies (DNV, LR, ABS, BV)** to assign **corrosion addition (CA)** values to scantlings — typically **0.5–1.0 mm/year** for uncoated ballast tank internals.
- **Tafel equation** is critical for designing **ICCP (Impressed Current Cathodic Protection)** systems for submerged hull and ballast tanks.
- **TSL equation** is used by shipyards to estimate the **coating maintenance schedule** — determines the docking interval.
- **Anode mass formula** governs the **mass and number of zinc/aluminum sacrificial anodes** required for ballast tank protection.
