<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **cathodic protection**. List the two methods of cathodic protection and state one application of each.

**Model Answer:**

Cathodic protection is an electrochemical technique used to control corrosion of a metal surface by making it the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell. This is achieved by supplying electrons to the metal, thereby suppressing the anodic (metal dissolution) reaction.

The two methods are:
1. **Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic) Cathodic Protection**: A more electrochemically active metal (e.g., Zn, Mg, Al) is electrically connected to the structure. The active metal corrodes preferentially, "sacrificing" itself to protect the structure. **Application**: Ship hulls, small storage tanks, offshore platform jacket legs.
2. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)**: An external DC power source is used to drive current from an inert anode through the electrolyte to the protected structure. **Application**: Long subsea pipelines, large ship hulls, reinforced concrete structures.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What are **corrosion inhibitors**? Differentiate between **anodic and cathodic inhibitors** with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

Corrosion inhibitors are chemical substances that, when added in small concentrations to a corrosive environment, **significantly reduce the rate of corrosion** of the metal without altering the bulk properties of the medium.

| Feature | Anodic Inhibitor | Cathodic Inhibitor |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Reacts with metal ions to form a **passive oxide film** on anodic sites | Forms a **protective precipitate** on cathodic sites, blocking oxygen reduction |
| **Typical Examples** | Chromates, nitrites, molybdates | Polyphosphates, zinc salts, calcium bicarbonate |
| **Risk if underdosed** | **Pitting corrosion** (dangerous!) | Generally safe |
| **Where used** | Industrial water systems, antifreeze | Cooling water systems, oil-field brines |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, **Part B questions offer Module-Internal Choice**. Question A OR Question B must be attempted in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the various **design modifications** that can be made to minimise corrosion in engineering structures. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A mild steel specimen of exposed area **20 cm²** and density **7.86 g/cm³** lost **180 mg** in **500 hours** when exposed to seawater. Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/yr** and **mpy**. Comment on the severity of corrosion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

Effective corrosion minimisation through design includes the following strategies:

1. **Avoid Crevices**: Crevices trap stagnant electrolyte, causing differential aeration and crevice corrosion. Use welded joints instead of bolted/riveted joints. Use continuous welds instead of spot welds.

2. **Ensure Drainage and Ventilation**: All surfaces should be sloped to allow water drainage. Enclosed spaces should be vented to prevent humidity buildup. Avoid flat horizontal surfaces where water can pond.

3. **Avoid Contact Between Dissimilar Metals**: When two metals of different electrode potential are in contact in an electrolyte, galvanic corrosion occurs. Use **insulating gaskets, sleeves, or washers** to separate dissimilar metals. If contact is unavoidable, choose metals close in the galvanic series.

4. **Avoid Sharp Corners and Stress Concentrations**: Stress-corrosion cracking (SCC) initiates at sharp corners. Use rounded fillets, smooth transitions, and avoid sudden changes in cross-section.

5. **Allow Access for Maintenance**: All critical areas should be accessible for inspection, cleaning, and recoating. Avoid burying parts in insulation without proper sealing.

6. **Prevent Localised Heating**: Avoid hot spots (e.g., heat exchanger dead zones) that can cause differential aeration cells and under-deposit corrosion.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $W = 180$ mg, $D = 7.86$ g/cm³, $A = 20$ cm², $t = 500$ hr.

**Step 1: Corrosion Rate in mm/yr**

Using $CR = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ (valid for $A$ in cm², $W$ in mg, $t$ in hr):

$$
CR = \frac{87.6 \times 180}{7.86 \times 20 \times 500}
$$

$$
CR = \frac{15{,}768}{78{,}600} = 0.2006 \text{ mm/yr}
$$

**Step 2: Corrosion Rate in mpy**

$$
CR = 0.2006 \times \dfrac{1000}{25.4} = \frac{200.6}{25.4} = 7.90 \text{ mpy}
$$

(Alternatively using $CR = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$, but must convert cm² to in²: $A = 20 / 6.4516 = 3.10$ in².)

$$
CR = \frac{534 \times 180}{7.86 \times 3.10 \times 500} = \frac{96{,}120}{12{,}183} = 7.89 \text{ mpy} \approx 7.90 \text{ mpy}
$$

**Step 3: Severity Assessment**

| Corrosion Rate (mpy) | Severity |
|---|---|
| < 1 | Negligible |
| 1 – 5 | Moderate |
| 5 – 20 | **High (in this range)** |
| > 20 | Severe |

At **7.90 mpy**, the corrosion is classified as **HIGH severity**. The unprotected mild steel is unsuitable for long-term marine service without additional protection (coatings and/or cathodic protection).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the **sacrificial anode cathodic protection method** in detail. Discuss the criteria for selecting anode material and list commonly used anode materials with their properties. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **zinc sacrificial anode** of mass **15 kg** is used to protect a buried pipeline. The total protective current required is **0.8 A**. Calculate the **life of the anode** in years, assuming utilisation factor = 0.85. Given: Atomic weight of Zn = 65.4, Valency = 2, Faraday's constant = 96,487 C/mol. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (Galvanic CP):**

In this method, a metal more electrochemically active (more negative electrode potential) than the structure to be protected is **electrically connected** to the structure and placed in the same electrolyte. The active metal (anode) corrodes preferentially, "sacrificing" itself, while the structure (cathode) is protected.

**Criteria for Selecting Anode Material:**
1. **More negative electrode potential** than the metal to be protected (e.g., Zn, Mg, Al are more active than steel).
2. **High electrochemical capacity** (ampere-hours per kg) — long life per unit mass.
3. **High current efficiency** — close to theoretical Faraday capacity.
4. **Uniform corrosion** — no localised pitting of the anode (predictable life).
5. **Low cost and ready availability**.
6. **Non-toxic** (especially for marine applications — restricts Cd use).

**Commonly Used Sacrificial Anode Materials:**

| Anode | Potential (V vs SHE) | Capacity (A·hr/kg) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | −1.60 to −1.75 | 1,230 | Soil (high resistivity), buried pipelines |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | −0.95 to −1.10 | 780 | Seawater (≤ 1000 Ω-cm resistivity) |
| **Aluminium alloys (Al-Zn-In)** | −1.05 to −1.15 | 2,500–2,800 | Offshore structures, marine hulls (most efficient) |

Aluminium alloys (e.g., **Galvalum III**, **Hiloy III**) are now the industry standard for offshore marine CP due to their highest capacity per kg.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $m_{anode} = 15$ kg = 15,000 g, $I = 0.8$ A, $u = 0.85$, $M_{Zn} = 65.4$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}487$ C/mol.

**Formula:**

$$
L = \frac{m_{anode} \cdot u \cdot F}{M_{Zn} \cdot n \cdot I}
$$

**Step 1: Substitute values:**

$$
L = \frac{15{,}000 \times 0.85 \times 96{,}487}{65.4 \times 2 \times 0.8}
$$

**Step 2: Numerator calculation:**

$$
15{,}000 \times 0.85 = 12{,}750
$$

$$
12{,}750 \times 96{,}487 = 1{,}230{,}209{,}250 \text{ C}
$$

**Step 3: Denominator calculation:**

$$
65.4 \times 2 = 130.8
$$

$$
130.8 \times 0.8 = 104.64
$$

**Step 4: Compute L in seconds:**

$$
L = \frac{1{,}230{,}209{,}250}{104.64} = 11{,}757{,}354 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to years:**

$$
L = \frac{11{,}757{,}354}{31{,}536{,}000} \approx 0.373 \text{ years}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 0.373 \text{ years} \approx 136 \text{ days}}
$$

> **Comment**: This relatively short life (~4.5 months) demonstrates why multiple anodes are installed in parallel and why scheduled anode replacement (typically every 3–5 years) is a routine part of pipeline maintenance programs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in Module 4 questions — avoid these to secure full marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Corrosion Rate Formula**: Students often mix the constants 534 and 87.6 with mismatched units. **Rule of thumb**: If area is in **in²** and answer in **mpy**, use **534**. If area is in **cm²** and answer in **mm/yr**, use **87.6**. Cross-check units before plugging in!
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Utilisation Factor (u) in Anode Life**: A common error is to set $u = 1$, which gives a falsely optimistic life. Examiners specifically test whether you include $u = 0.85$ for Zn and the appropriate value for Al alloys.
>
> 3. **Confusing Anodic and Cathodic Protection**: Students sometimes swap the applications. **Remember**: Cathodic protection is for **active metals in neutral electrolytes** (steel in seawater). Anodic protection is for **active-passive metals in aggressive environments** (stainless steel in H₂SO₄).
>
> 4. **Not Converting Faraday's Constant Units**: $F = 96{,}487$ **C/mol**, but if current is in mA, convert to A first. Time must be in **seconds**, not hours.
>
> 5. **Mixing Up Galvanic Series and EMF Series**: The **EMF Series** is for pure metals in standard conditions. The **Galvanic Series** is for commercial alloys in a specific environment (e.g., seawater). For practical corrosion, always use the **Galvanic Series**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Measures to Minimise Corrosion**

### Core Concepts to Memorise:
- ☐ **Five Pillars**: Material Selection | Design | Coatings | Electrochemical (CP & AP) | Environmental Control (Inhibitors).
- ☐ **Cathodic Protection**: Two methods — **Sacrificial Anode** (Zn, Mg, Al) vs. **Impressed Current** (external DC source + inert anode).
- ☐ **Anodic Protection**: Used only for active-passive metals (e.g., SS in H₂SO₄); opposite concept — keeps metal in passive region.
- ☐ **Coatings**: Organic (paints, epoxy), Metallic (galvanising = Zn), Conversion (phosphating, chromating).
- ☐ **Inhibitors**: Anodic (chromates — risky), Cathodic (polyphosphates — safe), Mixed (amines).

### Key Numerical Constants:
- ☐ **Faraday's Constant** $F = 96{,}487$ **C/mol**.
- ☐ **Corrosion Rate**: $CR_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ ; $CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$.
- ☐ **Anode Life**: $L = \dfrac{m \cdot u \cdot F}{M \cdot n \cdot I}$ (in seconds).
- ☐ **CP Voltage Criterion**: Steel in seawater → **−0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** reference electrode.

### Common Design Rules:
- ☐ Avoid **crevices** — use welded joints.
- ☐ Avoid **galvanic couples** — use insulating gaskets.
- ☐ Always combine **coatings + CP** for severe environments.
- ☐ Specify **rounded corners** to prevent stress-corrosion cracking.
- ☐ Ensure **drainage and ventilation** in enclosed compartments.

### Common Exam Pitfalls:
- ☐ Watch the **constant 534 vs. 87.6** based on units.
- ☐ Always include the **utilisation factor (u)** in anode life calculations.
- ☐ For **Al anodes**, typical $u \approx 0.50$ to $0.85$ (lower than Zn).
- ☐ Cathodic protection **increases** the rate of the protected metal — **NO**, it suppresses it; current flows **onto** the structure.
- ☐ In impressed current CP, the **anode is the inert auxiliary electrode**, not the protected structure.

### Quick Mnemonic — **"M-D-C-E-En"**:
- **M**aterial selection
- **D**esign modifications
- **C**oatings
- **E**lectrochemical methods (CP & AP)
- **En**vironmental control (inhibitors)

---

*End of Module 4 Notes — Measures to Minimise Corrosion*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion Control by Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion Control by Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion Control by Design** is a proactive engineering strategy that incorporates corrosion-mitigation principles at the **conceptual, geometric, and material-selection stages** of a structure or component, rather than relying solely on *after-the-fact* protective measures such as coatings or cathodic protection.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 4) and aligned with **NACE/AMPP standards** and **ASTM G46**, corrosion control by design is formally defined as:

*"The systematic application of engineering design rules — covering material selection, geometry, fabrication methods, joining techniques, and accessibility — to minimize the **thermodynamic driving force**, the **kinetic rate**, and the **environmental aggressiveness** that lead to metal loss in service."*

The fundamental design equation governing the **service life** of a structural component is expressed as:

$$t_{service} = \frac{t_{initial} - t_{allowable}}{CR_{design}}$$

Where:
- $t_{service}$ = Predicted service life (years)
- $t_{initial}$ = Initial wall thickness (mm)
- $t_{allowable}$ = Minimum allowable thickness before failure (mm)
- $CR_{design}$ = Design corrosion rate (mm/year), typically taken as **0.5 mm/year to 1.0 mm/year** for marine steel in tidal/splash zones

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Raincoat Designed Before the Storm"**
> Imagine building a house in Kerala during monsoon season. You have two choices:
> 1. **Reactive design** — Build the house flat and rectangular, then patch every leak with tar and paint after the rain damages it.
> 2. **Design-stage corrosion control** — Slope the roof at 15°, add overhangs, raise the foundation 1.5 m above the ground, install gutters, and pre-paint every joint.
>
> **Design-stage control is ALWAYS cheaper, longer-lasting, and structurally superior.** This is precisely the philosophy of *Corrosion Control by Design*.

## 1.3 The Eight Pillars of Corrosion Control by Design

The KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus organizes design-stage corrosion control into the following eight pillars:

| Pillar # | Design Principle | Primary Failure Mode Prevented |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Material Selection** | General/uniform corrosion |
| 2 | **Geometric Design (Shape & Form)** | Crevice corrosion, pitting |
| 3 | **Drainage & Ventilation** | Differential aeration, stagnant water |
| 4 | **Galvanic Compatibility** | Bimetallic (galvanic) corrosion |
| 5 | **Welding & Joining Design** | Weld decay, knifeline attack |
| 6 | **Stress & Vibration Management** | Stress corrosion cracking (SCC), corrosion fatigue |
| 7 | **Corrosion Allowance** | Wall thinning under uniform loss |
| 8 | **Accessibility for Inspection & Maintenance** | Hidden, undetected corrosion |

## 1.4 Why Design-Stage Control is Critical for Marine Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The marine environment is **classically the most aggressive** natural corrosion medium on Earth because it combines **high Cl⁻ concentration (~19,000 ppm)**, **continuous moisture**, **high electrical conductivity (~50,000 µS/cm)**, **biological fouling**, and **cyclic wet-dry exposure**.

Because retrofits offshore are **astronomically expensive** (a single platform shutdown can cost **₹ 50 crore/day**), KTU examiners and marine industry codes (DNV-RP-B401, ISO 12944) place *design-stage prevention* as the **first line of defense** in the corrosion control hierarchy:

**Hierarchy of Marine Corrosion Protection:**
1. **Design** (proper material, geometry, drainage) ← *Most effective*
2. **Material selection** (corrosion-resistant alloys)
3. **Corrosion allowance** (extra thickness)
4. **Coatings** (barrier protection)
5. **Cathodic protection** (electrochemical control)
6. **Chemical inhibitors** (environmental modification)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Eight Design Pillars

### 🔩 Pillar 1: Material Selection

The first decision a marine designer makes is **WHAT** to build with. The designer's job is to match the alloy's corrosion resistance to the **specific marine zone** of service.

**Marine Zones (ISO 12944 / KTU Module 4 classification):**

| Zone | Environment | Typical CR for Carbon Steel |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| **Atmospheric (Marine)** | Salt-laden air, splash | 0.05 – 0.20 mm/yr |
| **Splash & Spray** | Alternate wet/dry | 0.30 – 0.80 mm/yr |
| **Tidal** | Cyclic immersion/emergence | 0.10 – 0.30 mm/yr |
| **Submerged (Shallow)** | Continuously in seawater | 0.10 – 0.20 mm/yr |
| **Submerged (Deep)** | Cold, low-O₂, high-pressure | 0.05 – 0.10 mm/yr |
| **Mud Zone** | Anaerobic, sulfate-reducing bacteria | 0.05 – 0.15 mm/yr |

**Material selection rule of thumb (Galvanic Series awareness):**
> Materials close together in the **Galvanic Series in Seawater** can be safely coupled; materials far apart **must be electrically isolated** (e.g., with dielectric gaskets, insulating sleeves, or coatings).

### 📐 Pillar 2: Geometric Design (Shape & Form)

The **shape** of a structure determines where moisture, debris, and chloride ions accumulate. The cardinal rule is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Design for drainage, not for water entrapment."**

**Geometric Avoidance Rules:**
- ❌ **Avoid** horizontal flat surfaces → ✅ Use slopes of **≥ 5° (1:12)** for drainage
- ❌ **Avoid** sharp 90° internal corners → ✅ Use **fillet radii ≥ 3 mm** to allow coating coverage
- ❌ **Avoid** crevices, lap joints, and gasket seats → ✅ Use **continuous welded butt joints**
- ❌ **Avoid** pockets that trap debris → ✅ Provide **drainage holes** of **≥ 10 mm diameter** at all low points
- ❌ **Avoid** contact between dissimilar metals in stagnant zones

### 💧 Pillar 3: Drainage & Ventilation

**Stagnant water creates three corrosion problems simultaneously:**
1. Differential aeration cells (O₂-rich vs O₂-poor areas)
2. Chloride concentration by evaporation
3. Marine biofouling that creates under-deposit corrosion

**Design equations for drainage:**

For a horizontal tank or compartment, the drainage hole diameter is given by:

$$d_{drain} \geq \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot Q_{in}}{\pi \cdot v_{drain}}}$$

Where:
- $d_{drain}$ = Drain hole diameter (m)
- $Q_{in}$ = Anticipated inflow rate (m³/s)
- $v_{drain}$ = Required drainage velocity, minimum **0.5 m/s** for self-cleaning

For ventilation of enclosed compartments, the air-change rate to keep relative humidity below the **critical 60% RH threshold** (below which atmospheric corrosion drops dramatically) is:

$$N = \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1}{1 - RH_{crit}/RH_{ambient}}\right)}{t}$$

Where $N$ = air changes per hour (ACH), $RH_{crit}$ = **60%**, and $RH_{ambient}$ = ambient relative humidity.

### ⚡ Pillar 4: Galvanic Compatibility

When two dissimilar metals are electrically coupled in an electrolyte (seawater), the **more active (anodic)** metal corrodes preferentially. The severity is determined by the **Galvanic Series in Seawater** (NOT the standard EMF series, since seawater potentials differ from standard conditions).

**Galvanic corrosion current density (Mixed Potential Theory — Wagner & Traud, 1938):**

$$i_{galvanic} = \frac{E_{cathode}^{eq} - E_{anode}^{eq}}{R_a + R_c + R_{electrolyte}}$$

Where $R_a$ and $R_c$ are the **anodic and cathodic polarization resistances**, and $R_{electrolyte}$ is the **electrolyte resistance** between the two metals.

**Design mitigation strategies:**
- Use **metals within 0.25 V** of each other in the galvanic series
- **Area effect rule:** Avoid **small anode + large cathode** geometry (catastrophic)
- Paint **both** surfaces, never just the anode
- Install **dielectric breaks** (PVC, neoprene, FRP gaskets)

### 🔥 Pillar 5: Welding & Joining Design

> [!WARNING]
> **Welded joints are the #1 corrosion-failure initiation site in marine structures.** KTU examiners frequently ask why this is so.

**Reasons:**
1. **Weld metal** and the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** have different microstructures, hence different corrosion potentials
2. **Residual stresses** from welding promote SCC and hydrogen embrittlement
3. **Slag inclusions** and **undercut** create crevices
4. **Sensitization** in austenitic SS welds depletes Cr at grain boundaries, causing **intergranular corrosion (IGC)**

**Design rule:** Specify **low-carbon (L-grade) stainless steels** (e.g., **304L, 316L** with C ≤ 0.03%) or **stabilized grades** (321 with Ti, 347 with Nb) to prevent chromium carbide precipitation.

### 📊 Pillar 6: Stress & Vibration Management

**Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** requires three simultaneous conditions (the "SCC triangle"):
1. **Susceptible alloy** (e.g., austenitic SS in Cl⁻, carbon steel in OH⁻)
2. **Tensile stress** (residual or applied)
3. **Specific corrosive environment**

**Corrosion fatigue design** uses the Goodman or Soderberg relation, modified for environmental knockdown:

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e^{env}} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

Where $S_e^{env}$ = endurance limit in environment (typically **40–60%** of the in-air value for marine steels).

### 📏 Pillar 7: Corrosion Allowance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion Allowance (CA)** is the **extra wall thickness** added to a component's calculated structural thickness to compensate for expected metal loss over the design life.

$$t_{design} = t_{structural} + t_{CA}$$

Where:
$$t_{CA} = CR_{design} \times t_{service} \times SF$$

**SF = Safety Factor (typically 1.5 to 2.0)** accounts for local corrosion, weld defects, and unforeseen environmental excursions.

### 🔍 Pillar 8: Accessibility for Inspection & Maintenance

A design that *cannot be inspected* will *not be maintained*. KTU Module 4 emphasizes:
- Minimum **600 mm clearance** for entry into tanks
- Minimum **150 mm** for tool access around fittings
- Permanent **inspection ports** at critical locations
- **Walkways** and **platforms** for safe access to high-corrosion zones

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols & Units | KTU Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $t_{service} = \dfrac{t_{initial} - t_{allowable}}{CR_{design}}$ | $t$ in mm; $CR$ in mm/yr | Service life calculation |
| 2 | $t_{CA} = CR \times t_{life} \times SF$ | mm | Corrosion allowance |
| 3 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Volts (V) | Galvanic cell potential |
| 4 | $\Delta E = E_{cathode}^{eq} - E_{anode}^{eq}$ | V | Driving force for galvanic corrosion |
| 5 | $i_{galv} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_a + R_c + R_e}$ | A/m² | Galvanic current density |
| 6 | $i = i_{corr} \left[\exp\left(\dfrac{\eta_a}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\dfrac{\eta_c}{\beta_c}\right)\right]$ | A/m² | Butler–Volmer polarization |
| 7 | $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ | V | Polarization (overpotential) |
| 8 | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{corr}}{\rho \cdot n \cdot F}$ | mm/yr | Faraday's corrosion rate |
| 9 | $d_{drain} \geq \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi v}}$ | m | Drainage hole diameter |
| 10 | $\sigma_a/S_e^{env} + \sigma_m/S_{ut} = 1/n$ | MPa | Corrosion fatigue (Soderberg) |
| 11 | $W = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | kg | Mass loss (Faraday's Law) |
| 12 | $N_{ach} = \dfrac{1}{t}\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1-RH_{crit}/RH}\right)$ | ACH | Ventilation requirement |

Where:
- $\beta_a, \beta_c$ = anodic/cathodic Tafel slopes (V/decade)
- $K = 3272$ (Faraday constant for mm/yr when $i_{corr}$ in A/m², $\rho$ in g/cm³)
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $M$ = atomic/molar mass (g/mol)
- $n$ = electrons transferred per atom

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Corrosion Allowance Calculation

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A marine cargo piping system carries ballast seawater. The design service life is **25 years**. Structural thickness required is **8.0 mm**. The expected uniform corrosion rate is **0.15 mm/yr**. If a safety factor of **1.75** is mandated by DNV-RP-B401, calculate (a) the corrosion allowance, (b) the total design thickness, and (c) the residual thickness after 25 years.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters**

$$t_{life} = 25 \text{ years}, \quad t_{struct} = 8.0 \text{ mm}, \quad CR = 0.15 \text{ mm/yr}, \quad SF = 1.75$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Corrosion Allowance Formula**

$$t_{CA} = CR \times t_{life} \times SF$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values**

$$t_{CA} = 0.15 \times 25 \times 1.75$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerically**

$$t_{CA} = 6.5625 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Round Up to Engineering Standard (KTU convention)**

Since fractional mm thicknesses are unavailable, round **UP**:

$$t_{CA} \approx 7.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Total Design Thickness**

$$t_{design} = t_{struct} + t_{CA} = 8.0 + 7.0 = 15.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Residual Thickness at End of Service Life**

$$t_{residual} = t_{design} - (CR \times t_{life})$$

$$t_{residual} = 15.0 - (0.15 \times 25) = 15.0 - 3.75 = 11.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8 — Verify Structural Integrity**

$$\text{Required residual} \geq t_{struct}$$

$$11.25 \text{ mm} \geq 8.0 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Galvanic Corrosion Current Using Mixed-Potential Theory

> **Problem Statement:** A **steel plate (anode)** is coupled to a **copper plate (cathode)** in seawater with electrolyte resistance $R_e = 0.5 \, \Omega$. The equilibrium potentials are $E_{Fe}^{eq} = -0.55$ V and $E_{Cu}^{eq} = +0.15$ V (vs SCE). The Tafel slopes are $\beta_a = 0.06$ V/dec (Fe) and $\beta_c = 0.12$ V/dec (Cu). Cathode area is **10× the anode area**. Estimate the galvanic current density at the anode.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Driving Voltage (EMF of Galvanic Cell)**

$$\Delta E = E_{Cu}^{eq} - E_{Fe}^{eq} = (+0.15) - (-0.55) = 0.70 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize Area Effect (Cathode/Anode Ratio = 10:1)**

The cathodic current $I_c$ distributes over a 10× larger area, while the anodic current $I_a$ is concentrated:

$$\frac{i_c}{i_a} = \frac{A_a}{A_c} = \frac{1}{10}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Mixed-Potential Theory (Wagner–Traud)**

At the coupled (mixed) potential $E_{mix}$, the anodic and cathodic currents must balance when scaled by area:

$$i_a \cdot A_a = \left|\frac{i_c}{\text{(Tafel)}}\right| \cdot A_c$$

Using Butler–Volmer simplifications with both metals under activation control:

$$E_{mix} = \frac{\beta_a \cdot E_{Cu}^{eq} + \beta_c \cdot E_{Fe}^{eq}}{\beta_a + \beta_c}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$E_{mix} = \frac{(0.06)(0.15) + (0.12)(-0.55)}{0.06 + 0.12}$$

$$E_{mix} = \frac{0.009 - 0.066}{0.18} = \frac{-0.057}{0.18} = -0.3167 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Anodic Overpotential (Polarization of Iron)**

$$\eta_a = E_{mix} - E_{Fe}^{eq} = (-0.3167) - (-0.55) = +0.2333 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Apply Tafel Equation for Iron Dissolution**

$$i_a = i_{0,Fe} \cdot 10^{\eta_a / \beta_a}$$

Assuming exchange current density of iron in seawater $i_{0,Fe} = 10^{-4}$ A/m²:

$$i_a = 10^{-4} \times 10^{0.2333 / 0.06} = 10^{-4} \times 10^{3.889}$$

$$i_a = 10^{-4} \times 7745 \approx 0.7745 \text{ A/m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Compare with Uncoupled Corrosion Rate**

A bare iron surface in seawater corrodes at $i_{corr} \approx 0.05$ A/m². The galvanic coupling has accelerated the corrosion by a factor of:

$$\text{Acceleration Factor} = \frac{0.7745}{0.05} \approx 15.5\times$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** This is **why** small steel fasteners in a copper-rich marine structure can fail within months — the area effect (small anode/large cathode) is **catastrophic**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: Faraday's Law Applied to Service Life

> **Problem Statement:** A zinc sacrificial anode is to protect a hull section. The hull steel corrodes at an uncoupled rate of $i_{corr} = 0.08$ A/m² over a wetted area of 50 m². If a **100% protection efficiency** is desired, calculate (a) the total galvanic current required, (b) the daily mass consumption of zinc (assuming $i_{Zn} = 5$ A/m² on the anode, with utilization factor 0.85), and (c) the required anode mass for a 5-year service interval.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Total Current Required for Full Cathodic Protection**

$$I_{total} = i_{corr} \times A_{steel} = 0.08 \times 50 = 4.0 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Anode Surface Area Required**

$$A_{anode} = \frac{I_{total}}{i_{Zn}} = \frac{4.0}{5.0} = 0.80 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Mass Consumption Rate (Faraday's Law)**

$$m_{dot} = \frac{I_{total} \cdot M_{Zn}}{n \cdot F \cdot \eta_u}$$

With $M_{Zn} = 65.38$ g/mol, $n = 2$ electrons, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $\eta_u = 0.85$:

$$m_{dot} = \frac{4.0 \times 65.38}{2 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.85}$$

$$m_{dot} = \frac{261.52}{164{,}024.5} = 1.594 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/s}$$

**Step 4 — Daily Consumption**

$$m_{day} = 1.594 \times 10^{-3} \times 86{,}400 = 137.7 \text{ g/day}$$

**Step 5 — Annual Consumption**

$$m_{year} = 137.7 \times 365 = 50{,}260 \text{ g/yr} \approx 50.26 \text{ kg/yr}$$

**Step 6 — Mass Required for 5 Years (with utilization factor)**

$$m_{anode} = \frac{m_{year} \times t_{life}}{\eta_u} = \frac{50.26 \times 5}{0.85}$$

$$m_{anode} = 295.6 \text{ kg of zinc}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Realization**

If a single zinc anode has $m_{unit} = 25$ kg, number of anodes required:

$$N_{anodes} = \frac{295.6}{25} \approx 12 \text{ anodes}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Failure Mode vs. Design Countermeasure

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Design-Stage Countermeasure | KTU Weight |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| **Uniform Corrosion** | General electrochemical attack | Material upgrade (e.g., SS, Cu-Ni) | ★★★ |
| **Galvanic Corrosion** | Dissimilar metal coupling | Dielectric insulation, area balancing | ★★★★ |
| **Pitting Corrosion** | Localized Cl⁻ attack on passive film | Smooth surfaces, ≥316 SS, avoid deposits | ★★★★ |
| **Crevice Corrosion** | Stagnant electrolyte in gaps | Continuous welds, drainage holes | ★★★★★ |
| **Intergranular Corrosion** | Cr-depletion at grain boundaries | L-grade or stabilized SS | ★★★ |
| **SCC** | Tensile stress + specific ion | Stress-relief, lower-residual weld procedure | ★★★★ |
| **Corrosion Fatigue** | Cyclic stress + corrosion | Increased section, peening, coatings | ★★★ |
| **Erosion-Corrosion** | High-velocity particle-laden flow | Avoid sharp bends, limit velocity ≤ 3 m/s | ★★ |
| **MIC** | Microbial colonization | Avoid stagnant zones, biocide dosing ports | ★★★ |

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *Define "Corrosion Control by Design." List any four geometric design rules that minimize corrosion in marine structures.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Corrosion control by design refers to the incorporation of corrosion-prevention principles at the **conceptual and engineering design stage** itself, rather than relying on secondary protection methods such as coatings or cathodic protection.
>
> **Four Geometric Design Rules:**
> 1. **Drainage** — All horizontal surfaces shall be sloped at **≥ 5°** with drainage holes of ≥ **10 mm diameter** at all low points.
> 2. **Avoid crevices** — Use continuous butt welds instead of lap or bolted joints.
> 3. **Fillet radii** — Internal corners shall have a minimum radius of **3 mm** to ensure full coating coverage.
> 4. **Accessibility** — Provide minimum **600 mm** clearance for human entry into enclosed compartments for inspection.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *Explain the concept of "corrosion allowance" with a suitable numerical example for a marine ballast pipe.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion allowance (CA)** is the extra wall thickness added to the calculated structural thickness of a component to compensate for expected metal loss during its design service life. It is a *design-stage* insurance against uniform corrosion.
>
> **Numerical Example:**
> For a marine ballast pipe:
> - Structural thickness required: $t_{struct} = 6.0$ mm
> - Design life: $t_{life} = 20$ years
> - Corrosion rate (carbon steel in ballast water): $CR = 0.20$ mm/yr
> - Safety factor: $SF = 1.5$
>
> $$t_{CA} = CR \times t_{life} \times SF = 0.20 \times 20 \times 1.5 = 6.0 \text{ mm}$$
>
> Total design thickness: $t_{design} = 6.0 + 6.0 = 12.0$ mm (next standard: **12 mm schedule pipe**).

---

## 5.2 PART B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Corrosion Allowance & Galvanic Design

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** *A carbon steel pipe of structural thickness 10 mm is to carry seawater in a marine platform for 30 years. The design corrosion rate is 0.25 mm/yr. Calculate (i) the corrosion allowance using a safety factor of 1.6, (ii) the total design thickness, (iii) the residual thickness after 15 years of service, and (iv) whether the pipe requires replacement at year 15. Comment on the design adequacy.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *A steel propeller shaft (cathode) is to be coupled with a brass bearing housing (anode) in seawater. Discuss the design strategies you would adopt to minimize galvanic corrosion. With a neat sketch, explain the role of a dielectric break.*

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Given:**
$$t_{struct} = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad t_{life} = 30 \text{ yr}, \quad CR = 0.25 \text{ mm/yr}, \quad SF = 1.6$$

**Part (i) — Corrosion Allowance:**

$$t_{CA} = CR \times t_{life} \times SF = 0.25 \times 30 \times 1.6 = 12.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (ii) — Total Design Thickness:**

$$t_{design} = t_{struct} + t_{CA} = 10.0 + 12.0 = 22.0 \text{ mm}$$

(Next standard plate: **22 mm or 24 mm** — select 24 mm for safety.)

**Part (iii) — Residual Thickness After 15 Years:**

$$t_{residual, 15} = t_{design} - (CR \times 15) = 22.0 - (0.25 \times 15) = 22.0 - 3.75 = 18.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (iv) — Replacement Decision:**

Structural requirement: $t_{struct} = 10.0$ mm.
Available after 15 years: $t_{residual, 15} = 18.25$ mm ≥ 10.0 mm.

**Conclusion:** **Replacement is NOT required at year 15.** The pipe has 18.25 mm remaining, which is far above the 10 mm structural requirement. However, in-service inspection is recommended every 5 years due to possible pitting.

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Galvanic Corrosion Concerns:**

The brass bearing (Cu-Zn alloy) and steel shaft are far apart in the galvanic series in seawater. The steel becomes the **anode** and corrodes preferentially.

**Design Strategies:**

1. **Dielectric Break:** Insert a non-conductive spacer (e.g., **nylon, PTFE, or G-10 fiberglass**) between the steel and brass to break the electrical circuit. This eliminates the ionic path for galvanic current.

2. **Sacrificial Anode Installation:** Fit a **zinc or aluminum bracelet anode** to preferentially corrode instead of the steel.

3. **Area Balancing:** The brass area (cathode) should be **small** relative to the steel shaft (anode), avoiding the dangerous "small anode / large cathode" configuration.

4. **Coating Strategy:** Apply a **complete coating system** (primer + epoxy + antifouling topcoat) to **both** the shaft and the bearing housing. **Never** coat only the anode — pinholes in the coating accelerate localized attack dramatically.

5. **Material Upgrade:** Replace brass with a more **galvanically compatible** alloy such as **nickel-aluminum-bronze (NAB)** or **monel**, which are closer to steel in the galvanic series.

**Sketch — Dielectric Break Assembly:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["STEEL SHAFT"] --> B["DIELECTRIC SLEEVE"]
    B --> C["BRASS BEARING HOUSING"]
    B -. INSULATION .-> A
    C -. INSULATION .-> A
    A --> D["ZINC SACRIFICIAL ANODE"]
    style A fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students **paint only the anode** in a galvanic couple. This is **disastrous** — coating pinholes become **tiny anodes** coupled to a massive coated cathode, accelerating corrosion by **100× to 1000×**. Always coat **both** surfaces in a galvanic pair.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Design Failures & Welding Metallurgy

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** *Discuss, with examples, three (i) design-stage failures and (ii) remedial design measures that lead to premature corrosion in marine structures.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *Why are welded joints considered the most common initiation site for corrosion in marine structures? Explain the role of (i) low-carbon (L-grade) stainless steel and (ii) stabilized grades in preventing intergranular corrosion.*

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**Three Design-Stage Failures and Their Remedies:**

| # | Design Failure (Example) | Resulting Corrosion | Design-Stage Remedy |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Flat-horizontal deck plates** without slope on a coastal jetty | Water pools → **differential aeration corrosion & pitting** | Slope all horizontal surfaces **≥ 5°** with welded drainage spouts |
| 2 | **Lap joint with sealant** in a ballast tank | Sealant degrades → **crevice corrosion** under the lap | Replace with **continuous full-penetration butt welds** |
| 3 | **Steel fastener** securing a **copper-nickel pipe** to a hull | Massive area effect → fastener dissolves in weeks | Use **monel** or **NAB fasteners**, OR insert **nylon isolating washers** |

---

#### Model Solution (b):

**Why Welded Joints are the Primary Corrosion Initiation Site:**

1. **Microstructural Inhomogeneity:** The weld metal (WM), heat-affected zone (HAZ), and parent metal (PM) have **three distinct microstructures** and thus **three different corrosion potentials**. A galvanic micro-cell forms *within the joint itself*.

2. **Residual Tensile Stresses:** Welding induces locked-in residual stresses approaching the **yield strength**. Combined with Cl⁻ ions, this triggers **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)**.

3. **Geometric Imperfections:** Undercut, overlap, slag inclusions, and root porosity act as **crevice-formers** and **stress-raisers**.

4. **Sensitization in Stainless Steels:** In the **450–850 °C** temperature range experienced by the HAZ, chromium combines with carbon to form **Cr₂₃C₆** at grain boundaries, depleting adjacent zones of Cr (below the 12% Cr passivation threshold) → **intergranular corrosion (IGC)**.

**Role of L-Grade Stainless Steels (e.g., 304L, 316L):**

$$C \leq 0.03\%$$

Reducing carbon below 0.03% means there is **insufficient carbon** to migrate to grain boundaries and form chromium carbides during the brief sensitization window of welding. **No Cr-depletion → No IGC.**

**Role of Stabilized Grades (e.g., 321 with Ti, 347 with Nb):**

Titanium and niobium have a **higher affinity for carbon** than chromium. They form **TiC** and **NbC** preferentially, locking up the carbon before it can combine with chromium. Chromium remains in solid solution, preserving passivity.

**Stabilization Condition:**

$$\frac{\text{Ti or Nb}}{\text{C}} \geq 10 \text{ (minimum stoichiometric ratio)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse *sensitization* with *weld decay.* **Weld decay** occurs in the **HAZ**, away from the weld, where the temperature window (450–850 °C) is optimal for carbide precipitation. The weld metal itself is usually hotter and re-dissolves carbides. **Knifeline attack**, by contrast, is a narrow groove *immediately adjacent* to the weld fusion line in stabilized grades (321/347) — caused by TiC/NbC dissolution at temperatures above **1200 °C**. Both are common 7-mark KTU questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Corrosion Control by Design**

- ✅ **Definition:** Designing *out* corrosion problems at the drawing-board stage, not retrofitting solutions afterwards.
- ✅ **Eight Pillars:** Material selection → Geometry → Drainage → Galvanic compatibility → Welding → Stress → Corrosion allowance → Accessibility.
- ✅ **Drainage Rule:** Slope ≥ **5°**, drain holes ≥ **10 mm diameter**, fillet radius ≥ **3 mm**.
- ✅ **Galvanic Compatibility:** Avoid coupling metals with $\Delta E > 0.25$ V in the seawater galvanic series. Never **small anode + large cathode**.
- ✅ **Painting Rule:** Always paint **both** surfaces in a galvanic couple — never just the anode.
- ✅ **Welding:** Specify **L-grades (C ≤ 0.03%)** or **stabilized (321 Ti / 347 Nb)** stainless steels to prevent IGC.
- ✅ **Corrosion Allowance Formula:** $t_{CA} = CR \times t_{life} \times SF$ (with $SF = 1.5 - 2.0$).
- ✅ **Service Life Formula:** $t_{service} = (t_{initial} - t_{allowable}) / CR_{design}$.
- ✅ **SCC Triangle:** Susceptible alloy + Tensile stress + Specific environment.
- ✅ **Critical Marine Corrosion Rates:** Splash zone **0.30–0.80 mm/yr** (highest), Submerged deep **0.05–0.10 mm/yr** (lowest).
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Protection:** Design → Material → CA → Coatings → CP → Inhibitors.
- ✅ **Inspection Clearance:** ≥ **600 mm** for human entry, ≥ **150 mm** for tool access.
- ✅ **Design-Stage Principle:** *Always design for drainage — never design for water entrapment.*

---

### 📌 Examiner's Final Tip

> **For maximum marks in KTU ESE Module 4 answers:**
> - Always draw a **neat labeled sketch** of any design modification you propose.
> - Quote **specific numerical values** (slopes, diameters, radii, voltages) from standards (ISO 12944, DNV-RP-B401).
> - Link each design rule to the **specific failure mode** it prevents (crevice, pitting, SCC, IGC, etc.).
> - Mention **life-cycle cost** benefits — design-stage fixes are 5–10× cheaper than retrofits.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrosion Inhibitors: Cathodic Protection - Mechanism of Cathodic Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical method of corrosion control in which the metal structure to be protected is forced to become the **cathode** of a deliberately engineered galvanic (or electrolytic) cell. This is accomplished by supplying external electrons to the metal surface from a more electrochemically active (more anodic) source, thereby thermodynamically suppressing the anodic dissolution reaction of the parent metal.

Mathematically, the protective condition is expressed in terms of the **mixed (corrosion) potential**:

$$E_{corr} \;<\; E_{protected} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; i_{a,Fe} \;\to\; 0$$

where $E_{corr}$ is the open-circuit corrosion potential of the unprotected metal and $E_{protected}$ is the polarized (shifted) potential under the influence of the protective current. The protective current density is denoted $i_{p}$ (in **A/m²** or **mA/cm²**).

In marine engineering practice, the standard protection potential criterion adopted by NACE (now AMPP) is:

$$E_{protected} \;\leq\; -0.850 \text{ V vs. Cu/CuSO}_4 \text{ reference electrode (CSE)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 4 of *Marine Materials, Corrosion Prevention and Protection* groups cathodic protection under the broader umbrella of **Corrosion Inhibitors / Electrochemical Protection Methods**. A clear understanding of its *mechanism* is essential because KTU examiners frequently pose derivation-based and comparison-based questions on this topic.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Sacrificial Bodyguard"**
>
> Imagine a VIP (your valuable steel pipeline) being escorted through a dangerous crowd (the aggressive seawater electrolyte). Thieves (chloride ions + dissolved oxygen) are constantly trying to attack the VIP. Cathodic protection is like hiring a **bodyguard (a sacrificial anode made of zinc, aluminium, or magnesium)** whose job is to be attacked *instead of* the VIP. The bodyguard willingly gives away his "energy" (electrons) to satisfy every threat, so the VIP stays completely safe. In the **Impressed Current** version, the bodyguard is replaced by a remote controlled **drone (a rectifier-fed inert anode)** that continuously zaps the attackers with protective electrons.

The corrosion process is a *competition* between the anodic dissolution of the metal and cathodic reduction reactions. By shifting the metal's electrode potential in the **negative (active) direction**, the anodic dissolution current collapses toward zero, while harmless cathodic reactions (oxygen reduction, hydrogen evolution) consume the supplied electrons.

---

## 1.3 Why Cathodic Protection is Critical in Marine Engineering

Marine structures — ship hulls, offshore platforms, sub-sea pipelines, ballast tanks, jetties, and pier piles — are permanently exposed to a highly aggressive electrolyte (seawater with a typical **chloride concentration of ~19,000 ppm**, dissolved oxygen of **~6–8 mL/L**, and conductivity of **~50 mS/cm**). Under these conditions, bare carbon steel corrodes at rates between **0.1 and 0.5 mm/year**, often locally accelerated under marine growth or differential aeration. Cathodic protection is the **only proven electrochemical technique** that can guarantee service life exceeding 20–30 years for these structures without relying solely on coatings.

---

## 1.4 The Two Principal Variants

| Variant | Driving Force | Anode Material | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Galvanic (Sacrificial) Anode CP — SACP** | Galvanic potential difference | Zn, Al, Mg alloys | Ship hulls, short pipelines, internal tank protection |
| **Impressed Current CP — ICCP** | External DC rectifier | Inert anodes (Ti/MMO, graphite, Pb-Ag, Si-Cr-Fe cast iron) | Long pipelines, jetties, offshore platforms, jacking systems |

> [!TIP]
> For Part A 3-mark questions, KTU examiners expect students to state at least **two distinct methods** of cathodic protection along with their operating principles and anode materials. Memorize the table above verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrochemical Foundation — The Mixed Potential Concept

The corrosion of any metal in an electrolyte is governed by **mixed potential theory** (Wagner–Traud, 1938). At the open-circuit corrosion potential $E_{corr}$, the total anodic current equals the total cathodic current:

$$\sum i_{a}(E_{corr}) \;=\; \sum i_{c}(E_{corr}) \;=\; i_{corr}$$

Cathodic protection intervenes by **polarizing** the metal cathodically (shifting its potential toward more negative values), thereby reducing the metal's dissolution rate. The polarization diagram on a semi-log (Evans-style) plot is the standard analytical tool.

### Step-by-step Polarization Logic

1. **Identify the natural corrosion potential $E_{corr}$** of the metal in the given electrolyte (e.g., steel in seawater: ≈ **−0.65 V vs CSE**).
2. **Apply an external cathodic current** to drive the metal potential toward more negative values.
3. The **anodic dissolution curve** for the metal (Tafel slope $\beta_a$) decreases exponentially as the potential becomes more negative.
4. Simultaneously, the **cathodic reduction curve** (oxygen reduction Tafel slope $\beta_c$) carries the bulk of the applied current.
5. When the metal potential reaches the **protection potential** $E_{p}$ (typically **−0.85 V vs CSE**), the anodic current of the metal is reduced to **≤ 0.01 mA/cm²** — effectively negligible.
6. The minimum external current density required to achieve this is called the **protective current density** $i_{p}$, typically **10–110 mA/m²** for bare steel in seawater (depending on water resistivity, temperature, and biofouling).

---

## 2.2 Cathodic Reactions Driving Protection

When electrons are pumped into the protected metal, the following **cathodic reduction reactions** consume them. Which one dominates depends on the local pH and dissolved oxygen availability:

$$O_{2} \;+\; 2H_{2}O \;+\; 4e^{-} \;\rightarrow\; 4OH^{-} \quad \text{(aerobic, neutral/alkaline — seawater)}$$

$$O_{2} \;+\; 4H^{+} \;+\; 4e^{-} \;\rightarrow\; 2H_{2}O \quad \text{(aerobic, acidic)}$$

$$2H_{2}O \;+\; 2e^{-} \;\rightarrow\; H_{2} \;+\; 2OH^{-} \quad \text{(neutral, oxygen-depleted, over-protection)}$$

$$2H^{+} \;+\; 2e^{-} \;\rightarrow\; H_{2} \quad \text{(acidic, oxygen-depleted)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Over-Protection Hazard:** If the metal is polarized *too far* negative (below **−1.10 V vs CSE**), excess hydrogen evolution can occur. On high-strength steels (yield strength > 690 MPa) this causes **Hydrogen Induced Cracking (HIC)** and **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)**. This is a frequent 3-mark and 7-mark question in KTU papers.

---

## 2.3 Anodic Reactions — Sacrificial Anode Behaviour

For a **zinc anode** (the classic galvanic anode):

$$Zn \;\rightarrow\; Zn^{2+} \;+\; 2e^{-} \quad (E^{0}_{Zn/Zn^{2+}} = -0.763 \text{ V vs SHE})$$

For an **aluminium anode** (preferred for marine service because of high capacity):

$$Al \;\rightarrow\; Al^{3+} \;+\; 3e^{-} \quad (E^{0}_{Al/Al^{3+}} = -1.662 \text{ V vs SHE})$$

For a **magnesium anode** (used only in low-resistivity soil or freshwater):

$$Mg \;\rightarrow\; Mg^{2+} \;+\; 2e^{-} \quad (E^{0}_{Mg/Mg^{2+}} = -2.363 \text{ V vs SHE})$$

These anodes spontaneously oxidize because they occupy a more negative position in the galvanic series than the protected steel structure.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table lists every formula KTU examiners expect students to reproduce from memory in numerical 14-mark problems. Master these before sitting for the ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law for mass of metal dissolved | kg |
| 2 | $i_{p} = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Current density (protective) | A/m² or mA/cm² |
| 3 | $\Delta W = i_{corr} \cdot t \cdot \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass loss per unit area (corrosion rate form) | kg/m² |
| 4 | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate in mm/yr | mm/yr |
| 5 | $I_{total} = i_{p} \cdot A_{wetted}$ | Total protective current required | A |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{I_{total} \cdot t \cdot 8760}{C_{a} \cdot m_{a}}$ | Number of anodes required (galvanic) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $L = \dfrac{I_{total} \cdot \rho_{s} \cdot k}{V}$ | Maximum cathodic protection circuit length | m |
| 8 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode}^{0} - E_{anode}^{0}$ | Driving voltage of galvanic CP | V |
| 9 | $\eta_{a} = \beta_{a} \log \dfrac{i_{a}}{i_{0,a}}$ | Tafel equation (anodic branch) | V |
| 10 | $\eta_{c} = \beta_{c} \log \dfrac{i_{c}}{i_{0,c}}$ | Tafel equation (cathodic branch) | V |

**Symbol glossary** (avoid the pipe symbol `|` — always use `\vert` or `mid`):

- $I$ = current (A)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $M$ = molar mass (kg/mol)
- $n$ = valency number (electrons transferred)
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol** (= **96,485 A·s/mol**)
- $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density (A/m²)
- $EW$ = equivalent weight (g/eq) = $M \div n$
- $\rho$ = density of metal (g/cm³)
- $\rho_{s}$ = soil / seawater resistivity (Ω·m)
- $A_{wetted}$ = wetted surface area (m²)
- $C_{a}$ = anode electrochemical capacity (A·h/kg)
- $m_{a}$ = mass of one anode (kg)
- $V$ = allowable voltage drop (V)
- $k$ = geometric constant
- $\eta$ = overpotential (V)
- $\beta$ = Tafel slope (V/decade)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to memorize for KTU exam:** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol} \approx 96{,}500 \text{ C/mol}$. Use whichever the question specifies. Some problems round to **96,500** for ease.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In marine practice, the choice between SACP and ICCP is dictated by the **resistivity of the electrolyte** and the **scale of the structure**:

- **Low-resistivity seawater** ($\rho_{s} < 50$ Ω·cm): SACP works well; aluminium–indium–zinc anodes are the industry standard.
- **High-resistivity environments** ($\rho_{s} > 1000$ Ω·cm, e.g., seabed mud, certain soils): SACP fails because the galvanic driving voltage (~0.25 V for Al–Zn) is insufficient; **ICCP** with a rectifier delivering up to **50 V DC** is mandatory.
- **Very long structures** (trans-oceanic pipelines > 100 km): ICCP is the only feasible option; multiple **Transformer-Rectifier (TR) stations** are spaced at intervals.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is impressed current CP preferred for buried pipelines but sacrificial anode CP is preferred for ship hulls?"* Use the resistivity argument above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (Foundation of All CP Calculations)

**Statement:** The mass $W$ of a substance liberated or dissolved at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge $Q$ that passes through the electrolyte.

### Starting Principle

Charge is the time-integral of current:

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

The number of moles of electrons transferred is:

$$n_{e} = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

For a metal of atomic mass $M$ and valency $z$ (so each ion requires $z$ electrons to dissolve), the number of moles of metal atoms dissolved is:

$$n_{metal} = \dfrac{n_{e}}{z} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$$

The mass dissolved is therefore:

$$W = n_{metal} \cdot M = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{z \cdot F}$$

This is the **fundamental Faraday equation** used in every cathodic protection design calculation.

### Standard Form

$$\boxed{W \;=\; \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}}$$

**Sanity check (units):**

- $I$ in **A** = **C/s**
- $t$ in **s**
- $Q = I \cdot t$ in **C**
- $M$ in **g/mol**
- $n$ dimensionless
- $F$ in **C/mol**

Therefore:

$$[W] = \dfrac{C \cdot (g/mol)}{C/mol} = g$$

Units balance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Corrosion Rate from Current Density

**Given:** A steel surface with area $A$ is corroding at corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ for time $t$.

### Step 1 — Total Charge Transferred

$$Q = i_{corr} \cdot A \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Mass Loss Per Unit Area

$$\dfrac{W}{A} = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Linear Penetration Rate (mm/yr)

Using the density $\rho$ (g/cm³) of the metal:

$$CR = \dfrac{(W/A)}{\rho} = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

For engineering convenience, substitute $EW = M/n$ (equivalent weight) and convert units so that $CR$ emerges in **mm/year**:

- $i_{corr}$ in **mA/cm²** → multiply by $10^{-3}$ A/mA, then by $10^{4}$ cm²/m² = **0.01 conversion factor → $i_{corr}$ in A/m²** … but to keep mA/cm² explicit:

$$CR \;=\; \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$$

The constant **0.00327** bundles: $10^{-3}$ (mA → A) $\times$ $10^{4}$ (cm² → m²) $\times$ $3600 \cdot 24 \cdot 365$ (s → yr) $\div$ $F = 31{,}536{,}000$ s/yr. Then $31{,}536{,}000 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} / 96{,}485 \approx 0.327$, divided by 100 (m → mm conversion, applied via $\rho$ in g/cm³) → **0.00327**.

$$\boxed{CR_{mm/yr} \;=\; \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr_{(mA/cm^2)}} \cdot EW_{(g/eq)}}{\rho_{(g/cm^3)}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is the single most-asked numerical conversion in KTU ESE for this module. Practice it until you can write it in under 30 seconds.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Galvanic Anodes Required

**Given:** A marine pipeline of length $L$ (m), diameter $D$ (m), submerged in seawater with protective current density $i_p$ (A/m²). Each galvanic anode weighs $m_a$ (kg), with electrochemical capacity $C_a$ (A·h/kg). Design life required is $T$ (years).

### Step 1 — Wetted Surface Area

$$A_{wetted} = \pi \cdot D \cdot L$$

### Step 2 — Total Protective Current

$$I_{total} = i_p \cdot A_{wetted} = i_p \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L$$

### Step 3 — Total Ampere-Hours Required Over Design Life

Convert design life from years to hours:

$$T_{hr} = T \cdot 365.25 \cdot 24 = T \cdot 8766$$

Therefore:

$$Q_{required} = I_{total} \cdot T_{hr} = i_p \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot T \cdot 8766 \quad \text{(A·h)}$$

### Step 4 — Total Anode Mass Required

Each kg of anode delivers $C_a$ A·h. Hence:

$$M_{total} = \dfrac{Q_{required}}{C_{a}} = \dfrac{i_p \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot T \cdot 8766}{C_{a}}$$

### Step 5 — Number of Anodes

Each anode weighs $m_a$ kg, so:

$$\boxed{N \;=\; \dfrac{i_p \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot T \cdot 8766}{C_{a} \cdot m_{a}}}$$

(Sometimes textbooks use **8760** as an approximation; either is acceptable.)

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question)

**Question:** A seawater pipeline of diameter **0.30 m** and length **2 km** is to be protected by sacrificial aluminium anodes. The protective current density is **120 mA/m²**, design life is **15 years**, anode capacity is **2,600 A·h/kg**, and each anode weighs **20 kg**. Find the number of anodes.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Area

$$A_{wetted} = \pi \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2000 = 1884.96 \text{ m}^2$$

Step 2: Total current

$$I_{total} = 120 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1884.96 = 226.20 \text{ A}$$

Step 3: Charge required

$$Q_{required} = 226.20 \cdot 15 \cdot 8766 = 29{,}739{,}628 \text{ A·h} \approx 2.974 \times 10^{7} \text{ A·h}$$

Step 4: Anode mass

$$M_{total} = \dfrac{2.974 \times 10^{7}}{2600} = 11{,}438 \text{ kg}$$

Step 5: Number of anodes

$$N = \dfrac{11{,}438}{20} = 571.9 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{572 anodes}$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners always round **up** the number of anodes (you cannot install 0.9 of an anode). State the rounding rule explicitly in your answer.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Polarization Criterion (Humble / Schwerdtfeger Equation)

The most widely accepted cathodic protection criterion in marine service is the **−100 mV polarization criterion**:

$$\Delta E \;=\; E_{corr} - E_{protected} \;\geq\; 100 \text{ mV}$$

This requires measurement of the **instant-off potential** (to eliminate IR drop errors). The derivation rests on the Stern–Geary linear polarization equation:

$$i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_{p}}$$

where $R_{p}$ is the polarization resistance (Ω·cm²) and $B = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303 (\beta_a + \beta_c)]$ (≈ **26 mV** for steel in seawater).

For full cathodic protection, the applied current density must satisfy:

$$i_{applied} \;\geq\; \dfrac{\Delta E \cdot i_{corr}}{B}$$

A practical engineering rule: $i_p \approx 2 \cdot i_{corr}$ provides a safe margin.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mechanism Flowchart — How Cathodic Protection Suppresses Metal Loss

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Marine Environment Seawater Electrolyte"] --> B["Bare Steel Surface"]
    B --> C{"Corrosion Cell Active?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Anodic Sites Fe dissolves to Fe2plus"]
    C -- No --> E["Protected Structure Cathode Dominant"]
    D --> F["Cathodic Sites O2 Reduction to OHminus"]
    D --> G["External Electron Source Activated"]
    G --> G1["SACP Zinc Aluminium Magnesium Anode"]
    G --> G2["ICCP Transformer Rectifier DC Source"]
    G1 --> H["Electrons Flow to Steel"]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I["Steel Potential Shifts Negative"]
    I --> J["Potential Reaches minus 0.85 V vs CSE"]
    J --> K["Anodic Dissolution Current Collapses to Near Zero"]
    K --> E
    F --> L["Cathodic Reactions Consume Electrons"]
    L --> E
    E --> M["Steel Surface Fully Protected"]
    M --> N["Service Life Extended 20 to 30 Years"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The flowchart above shows how the **electron supply mechanism** (whether galvanic or impressed current) is the *cause*, and the **collapse of the anodic current** is the *effect* — this is the central mechanistic insight KTU examiners test.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SACP vs ICCP Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SACP["Galvanic Anode CP System SACP"]
        SA1["Anode Alloy Zn Al Mg"] --> SA2["Spontaneous Galvanic Oxidation"]
        SA2 --> SA3["Electron Release into Seawater"]
        SA3 --> SA4["Steel Structure Acts as Cathode"]
    end

    subgraph ICCP["Impressed Current CP System ICCP"]
        IC1["AC Power Source 230 V or 440 V"] --> IC2["Transformer Rectifier TR Unit"]
        IC2 --> IC3["DC Output 0 to 50 V"]
        IC3 --> IC4["Inert Anode MMO Ti or Graphite"]
        IC4 --> IC5["Forced Electron Injection"]
        IC5 --> IC6["Steel Structure Polarized Cathodically"]
    end

    SACP --> OUT["Protected Steel Surface"]
    ICCP --> OUT

    OUT --> MON["Reference Electrodes Cu CuSO4 and Zinc"]
    MON --> CTRL["Monitoring and Auto Control"]
    CTRL --> IC2
```

---

## 4.3 Polarization Schematic — E vs log(i) Diagram (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Log Current Density Axis"] --> P2["Anodic Tafel Line Steel Dissolution"]
    P1 --> P3["Cathodic Tafel Line Oxygen Reduction"]
    P2 --> P4["Intersection Point Ecorr and icorr"]
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5["Apply Cathodic Current ip"]
    P5 --> P6["New Operating Point Eprotected equals minus 0.85 V vs CSE"]
    P6 --> P7["Steel Dissolution Current Reduced by 99 percent"]
```

> [!TIP]
> When drawing the E–log i diagram in an exam, always label: (i) both Tafel slopes, (ii) the corrosion point, (iii) the protection point, (iv) the protective current density. KTU evaluators award full marks only when all four annotations are present.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define cathodic protection and list any **two criteria** used to confirm effective protection of a buried marine pipeline.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cathodic protection is an electrochemical technique in which the metal to be protected is made the cathode of an electrochemical cell, thereby suppressing its anodic dissolution reaction. **(1 mark)**

The two standard criteria are: **(2 marks)**

1. **Potential criterion:** The protected metal potential should be more negative than **−0.850 V vs. Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode (CSE)**.
2. **Polarization criterion:** A minimum **polarization shift of −100 mV** from the free corrosion potential, measured as the *instant-off* potential.

*(Alternative third criterion sometimes accepted: Tafel slope criterion / negative swing of 300 mV.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Why is **aluminium** preferred over **zinc** as a sacrificial anode material in modern seawater applications?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Aluminium anodes are preferred because of their: **(1 mark per point)**

1. **Higher electrochemical capacity** — Aluminium delivers approximately **2,600 A·h/kg** compared to zinc's **780 A·h/kg** (roughly 3.3× longer life per unit mass).
2. **Lower density** (Al ≈ 2.7 g/cm³ vs Zn ≈ 7.1 g/cm³) — gives a higher charge-to-weight ratio, reducing the number of anodes needed.
3. **Lower cost** and abundant availability — making it economically viable for large offshore installations.
4. *(Bonus 1 mark)* Modern Al–Zn–In alloys overcome aluminium's natural tendency to passivate, ensuring reliable performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake to avoid:** Students often say "aluminium has a more negative potential than zinc." This is **incorrect** — zinc is actually slightly more active in many formulations; aluminium's advantage lies in its **capacity**, not its driving voltage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question, Module 4]** Discuss the **mechanism of cathodic protection** in detail. Compare **sacrificial anode cathodic protection (SACP)** with **impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP)** under the following heads:
- (a) Working principle and construction, **(7 marks)**
- (b) Anode materials, current output, advantages, limitations and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Working Principle and Construction (7 marks)

**Cathodic Protection — Underlying Mechanism:**

Cathodic protection works on the principle of **mixed potential theory** and the **Tafel polarization law**. When a metal corrodes freely in an electrolyte, anodic dissolution (M → M^n+ + ne⁻) and a cathodic reduction (typically O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻) occur simultaneously at equal rates $i_{corr}$ on different microscopic surface sites. **(2 marks)**

CP forces all surface sites of the protected metal to behave cathodically by **injecting electrons** from an external source. The metal potential $E$ shifts in the negative direction along the anodic polarization curve; when it reaches the **protection potential** $E_p = -0.85$ V vs CSE, the anodic dissolution current of the parent metal becomes negligible. **(2 marks)**

**Construction of SACP:**

A galvanic (more active) metal — typically an Al–Zn–In or Zn alloy — is metallurgically bonded (welded, bolted, or cast onto a steel core) to the protected structure and immersed in the same electrolyte. The galvanic potential difference (~0.25–0.30 V) drives a spontaneous DC current. **(1.5 marks)**

**Construction of ICCP:**

An external **Transformer-Rectifier (TR)** unit converts AC mains to low-voltage DC (typically 0–50 V), the negative terminal of which is connected to the protected structure and the positive terminal to an **inert (insoluble) anode** such as mixed-metal-oxide-coated titanium (MMO/Ti), graphite, or silicon-chrome-iron, placed remotely in the electrolyte. **(1.5 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Comparison Table + Discussion (7 marks)

| Parameter | SACP (Galvanic Anode) | ICCP (Impressed Current) |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving force** | Galvanic potential difference | External DC rectifier |
| **Anode material** | Zn, Al–Zn–In, Mg alloys (active/sacrificial) | MMO/Ti, graphite, Pb–Ag, Si–Cr–Fe (inert) |
| **Current output** | Limited (~0.25–0.30 V driving EMF) | Adjustable, high (up to 50 V DC, hundreds of A) |
| **Current capacity per anode** | Low (~2–5 A typical) | High (50–400 A typical) |
| **Distance of protection** | Short (< 50 m radius in seawater) | Long (km-scale pipelines, whole offshore platforms) |
| **Resistivity tolerance** | Low-resistivity media only (seawater) | Works in high-resistivity media (soil, mud) |
| **Cost — installation** | Low | High (rectifier + cabling) |
| **Cost — operation** | Zero (no power required) | Continuous AC power consumption |
| **Maintenance** | Replace anodes periodically | Monitor TR output, refit anodes rarely |
| **Risk of overprotection** | Negligible | Possible → causes HIC/SCC on high-strength steel |
| **Typical applications** | Ship hulls, internal tank protection, short subsea templates | Long pipelines, offshore jackets, jetties, storage tank bases |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** SACP is preferred where electrolyte resistivity is low and the structure is small/medium-sized. ICCP is preferred for large or high-resistivity systems where galvanic driving voltage is insufficient.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question, Module 4]**

(a) Derive an expression for the **mass of metal corroded** using Faraday's laws. A **carbon-steel plate** of area **0.50 m²** is exposed to seawater. The measured corrosion current density is **$i_{corr} = 0.10$ mA/cm²**. Calculate the **mass loss in grams** over **30 days**, given atomic mass of iron **$M = 55.85$ g/mol**, valency **$n = 2$**, and $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol. **(7 marks)**

(b) A **ship's hull** is to be protected by sacrificial aluminium anodes. The hull wetted area is **1500 m²**, design life **5 years**, protective current density **100 mA/m²**, anode capacity **2600 A·h/kg**, and each anode weighs **15 kg**. Calculate the **number of anodes required**. State two advantages of ICCP over SACP. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Faraday Derivation + Calculation (7 marks)

**Derivation (3 marks):**

By Faraday's first law, mass $W$ dissolved at an electrode is proportional to charge $Q$ passed. By his second law, $W$ is proportional to the equivalent weight $M/n$. Combining:

$$W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

This is the universal form used in all corrosion rate and anode design calculations. The derivation follows from $Q = I t$, $n_e = Q/F$, $n_{metal} = n_e / n$, and $W = n_{metal} \cdot M$.

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Given:
- $A = 0.50 \text{ m}^2 = 5000 \text{ cm}^2$
- $i_{corr} = 0.10 \text{ mA/cm}^2 = 0.10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A/cm}^2$
- $t = 30 \text{ days} = 30 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2{,}592{,}000 \text{ s}$
- $M = 55.85 \text{ g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$

Total current:

$$I = i_{corr} \cdot A = 0.10 \times 10^{-3} \times 5000 = 0.50 \text{ A}$$

Mass loss:

$$W = \dfrac{0.50 \times 2{,}592{,}000 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$W = \dfrac{72{,}381{,}600}{192{,}970} = 375.1 \text{ g}$$

**Answer:** $W \approx \mathbf{375 \text{ grams}}$ of iron dissolves from the plate in 30 days.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Unit Conversion:** Students frequently forget to convert mA/cm² → A/cm² (multiply by 10⁻³) or cm² → m² (multiply by 10⁴). Forgetting these two factors gives an answer off by **10×**. Always show the conversion line explicitly in your answer.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Anode Calculation + Advantages (7 marks)

**Calculation (5 marks):**

Given:
- $A_{wetted} = 1500 \text{ m}^2$
- $i_p = 100 \text{ mA/m}^2 = 0.100 \text{ A/m}^2$
- $T = 5 \text{ years}$, $C_a = 2600 \text{ A·h/kg}$, $m_a = 15 \text{ kg}$

Total current required:

$$I_{total} = 0.100 \times 1500 = 150 \text{ A}$$

Total charge over design life (hours):

$$Q_{req} = 150 \times 5 \times 8766 = 6{,}574{,}500 \text{ A·h}$$

Total anode mass:

$$M_{total} = \dfrac{6{,}574{,}500}{2600} = 2528.65 \text{ kg}$$

Number of anodes:

$$N = \dfrac{2528.65}{15} = 168.58$$

Rounding up:

$$\boxed{N = 169 \text{ anodes}}$$

**Two advantages of ICCP over SACP (2 marks):**

1. **Higher and adjustable current output** — A single TR unit can deliver hundreds of amperes and be tuned to seasonal/resistivity changes; a single galvanic anode typically delivers only 2–5 A.
2. **Effective in high-resistivity media** — ICCP works in soils and seabed muds where the limited galvanic driving voltage (~0.25 V) of SACP cannot push protective current.
3. *(Optional third)* Lower long-term operating cost on very large structures due to fewer anode replacements.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on CP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the reference electrode** when quoting the protection potential (must specify "vs CSE" or "vs SCE"). Loss: 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up anode vs cathode reactions** in ICCP — students often write that the rectifier's *positive* terminal connects to the structure. Wrong: the *negative* terminal connects to the structure (it becomes the cathode). Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not converting mA/cm² to A/cm²** or cm² to m² in numericals. Always show the conversion line. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **Rounding down anode count** instead of up. Loss: 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Omitting Tafel slope annotations** on the E–log i diagram. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing SACP current output** (typically 2–5 A per anode) with ICCP (up to 400 A per anode). Loss: 1 mark.
> 7. **Missing the overprotection hazard** on high-strength steel (HIC/SCC). Loss: 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**
>
> - [x] **CP definition:** Making the metal the cathode by supplying electrons externally.
> - [x] **Protection potential:** **−0.85 V vs CSE** (Humble criterion).
> - [x] **Polarization criterion:** **−100 mV** shift from $E_{corr}$ (instant-off).
> - [x] **Faraday's law:** $W = I t M / (n F)$, with $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.
> - [x] **Corrosion rate formula:** $CR_{mm/yr} = 0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW / \rho$.
> - [x] **Anode count:** $N = (i_p \cdot A_{wetted} \cdot T \cdot 8766) / (C_a \cdot m_a)$; always **round up**.
> - [x] **SACP anodes:** Zn, Al–Zn–In, Mg (active/sacrificial, dissolves).
> - [x] **ICCP anodes:** MMO/Ti, graphite, Pb–Ag, Si–Cr–Fe (inert, does not dissolve).
> - [x] **Cathodic reactions:** $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \to 4OH^-$ (dominant in seawater).
> - [x] **Anodic reactions:** $Zn \to Zn^{2+} + 2e^-$; $Al \to Al^{3+} + 3e^-$.
> - [x] **SACP:** limited current, low resistivity only, no power, simple.
> - [x] **ICCP:** adjustable high current, works in soil/mud, needs TR unit + power.
> - [x] **Overprotection hazard:** below −1.10 V vs CSE → HIC/SCC on high-strength steel.
> - [x] **Reference electrodes:** Cu/CuSO₄ (seawater/soil), Ag/AgCl (seawater), Zn (seawater portable), SCE (lab).
> - [x] **Typical $i_p$ for bare steel in seawater:** 80–150 mA/m².
> - [x] **Anode capacity benchmark:** Zn ≈ 780 A·h/kg, Al ≈ 2600 A·h/kg, Mg ≈ 1230 A·h/kg.
> - [x] **Application rule:** Small/short + low resistivity → SACP; Large/long + high resistivity → ICCP.

---

**End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Corrosion Inhibitors: Cathodic Protection — Mechanism of Cathodic Protection**
