topics.title: Sacrificial Anode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sacrificial Anode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)** is an electrochemical corrosion control technique in which a metal that is **electrochemically more active** (more negative electrode potential) than the structure to be protected is electrically connected to the structure, thereby causing the **anode to corrode preferentially** while the protected structure acts as a **cathode** and remains immune to corrosion.

In this method, the protective anode is intentionally allowed to **"sacrifice"** itself by undergoing anodic dissolution, hence the term **sacrificial anode**. The technique is also called **Galvanic Anode Cathodic Protection (GACP)** because the driving EMF arises purely from the **difference in natural electrode potentials** between the two dissimilar metals immersed in a common electrolyte (seawater, soil, etc.).

The SACP system consists of the following essential components:

1. **Anode** (Sacrificial Metal) — Active metal such as **Zn, Al, or Mg** alloys.
2. **Cathode** (Protected Structure) — Usually mild steel, low-alloy steel, or stainless steel marine structures.
3. **Electrolyte** — Seawater, brackish water, or moist marine soil providing ionic conduction.
4. **Metallic Connection** — Direct electrical (metallic) bond or cable between anode and structure.

The fundamental electrochemical reactions at the protected cathode surface are:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^- \quad \text{(in aerated neutral seawater)}$$

$$\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \longrightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(in acidic conditions)}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bodyguard Concept**
> Imagine a celebrity (the steel hull) walking through a dangerous crowd (the corrosive seawater). Instead of armoring the celebrity, we hire a **bodyguard** (the sacrificial anode) who is more attractive to attackers. The attackers (chloride ions, oxygen) focus all their aggression on the bodyguard, who willingly takes the hits until he is "used up" — at which point, a new bodyguard replaces him. The celebrity walks away unharmed.

**Another analogy — The Bleeding Wound:** Just as doctors allow controlled bleeding from a wound to drain infection, marine engineers allow controlled corrosion of the anode to drain the entire electrochemical reaction away from the critical steel structure.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Potentials (vs. SCE / Cu-CuSO₄) for SACP Materials:**
> - Magnesium (Mg): **−1.55 V to −1.75 V** (most active)
> - Zinc (Zn): **−1.10 V to −1.05 V**
> - Aluminum (Al-Zn-In): **−1.05 V to −1.10 V**
> - Mild Steel (protected potential): **−0.80 V vs. Cu/CuSO₄** (criterion per **NACE SP0169**)

**Current Density Demands for Marine Submerged Steel:**
- Bare steel in seawater: **80 – 300 mA/m²**
- Coated steel: **5 – 30 mA/m²**
- Marine atmospheric (no SACP applicable — use coatings only)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The cathodic protection by sacrificial anodes operates on **Galvanic Cell Theory**. The thermodynamic driving force is the **potential difference** between the anode and the cathode.

**Step 1 — Galvanic Couple Formation**
When two dissimilar metals are electrically bonded and immersed in a common electrolyte, a **galvanic cell** is spontaneously formed. The metal with the more negative (active) potential becomes the anode.

**Step 2 — Anodic Dissolution Reaction**
The sacrificial anode dissolves, releasing metallic cations into the electrolyte:

$$\text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^- \quad (E^\circ = -0.76 \text{ V vs. SHE})$$

$$\text{Al} \longrightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^- \quad (E^\circ = -1.66 \text{ V vs. SHE})$$

**Step 3 — Electron Flow Through Metallic Path**
The liberated electrons travel through the metallic connection (weld, cable, bolt) from the anode to the structure (cathode).

**Step 4 — Cathodic Reaction Suppression**
At the protected steel surface, electrons neutralize any tendency for iron dissolution (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻). Instead, oxygen reduction occurs, generating hydroxyl ions:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$

**Step 5 — Polarization of the Cathode**
The accumulation of electrons on the steel surface shifts its potential in the **negative (cathodic) direction**. When the steel potential reaches the protective value of **−0.80 V vs. Cu/CuSO₄**, anodic dissolution of iron is thermodynamically suppressed, achieving **complete cathodic protection**.

**Step 6 — Self-Regulating Nature**
Unlike impressed current CP, SACP is **self-limiting** — once the steel potential reaches the protection potential, the current flow naturally decreases. This makes SACP inherently safe with **no overprotection risk** (unlike ICCP, which can cause hydrogen embrittlement or coating disbondment if mis-tuned).

## 2.2 Anode Material Selection Logic

| Anode Type | Driving Voltage | Electrolyte Resistivity | Application |
|------------|------------------|---------------------------|--------------|
| **Magnesium** | Highest (~0.75 V to steel) | High (soil, fresh water) | Underground pipelines, fresh-water tanks |
| **Zinc** | Moderate (~0.25 V to steel) | Low (seawater) | Ship hulls, offshore jacket legs |
| **Aluminum (Al-Zn-In-Hg)** | Moderate (~0.30 V) | Low (seawater) | Modern marine applications, offshore platforms |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all critical formulas** required for KTU university exam numerical problems on sacrificial anode design.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Total Current Demand | $I_{\text{total}} = A_c \times i_c$ | A | $A_c$ = cathode area (m²), $i_c$ = required current density (A/m²) |
| 2 | Anode Current Output | $I_a = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_a}$ | A | $\Delta E = E_c - E_a$ (V); $R_a$ = anode resistance (Ω) |
| 3 | Anode Resistance (Humble-Bryan, seawater) | $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$ | Ω | $\rho$ = electrolyte resistivity (Ω·m); $L$ = length (m); $r$ = radius (m) |
| 4 | Anode Resistance (McCoy for short anode) | $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{2L}{r}\right)\right]$ | Ω | Used when $L/r < 4$ |
| 5 | Total Anode Mass Required | $M_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{total}} \times t_l \times 8760}{C \times \eta}$ | kg | $t_l$ = design life (years); $C$ = electrochemical capacity (A·h/kg); $\eta$ = current efficiency |
| 6 | Number of Anodes | $N = \dfrac{M_{\text{total}}}{M_{\text{ind}}}$ | — | $M_{\text{ind}}$ = mass of single anode (kg) |
| 7 | Alternative Form (using mass per anode) | $N = \dfrac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_a}$ | — | Each anode delivers $I_a$ amps |
| 8 | Anode Life Verification | $t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{ind}} \times C \times \eta}{I_a \times 8760}$ | years | Check against design life |
| 9 | Utilization Factor ($U$) | Empirical; **0.75 – 0.90** for elongated anodes; **0.50 – 0.60** for short/block anodes | — | Accounts for anode core that does not fully consume |
| 10 | Charge Supplied by Anode | $Q = I_a \times t_l$ | A·h | Total coulombs delivered over design life |
| 11 | Design Margin (Safety Factor) | SF = **1.10 – 1.25** typical | — | Applied to total current demand for fouling, coating degradation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Values to Memorize for KTU 2024:**
> - Electrochemical capacity: **Zn ≈ 780 A·h/kg**, **Al ≈ 2500–2850 A·h/kg**, **Mg ≈ 1230 A·h/kg**
> - Density: Zn = **7140 kg/m³**, Al = **2700 kg/m³**, Mg = **1740 kg/m³**
> - 1 year = **8760 hours**
> - Seawater resistivity ρ ≈ **0.20 – 0.30 Ω·m** (typical = 0.25 Ω·m)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In marine engineering practice, SACP is extensively used for:

- **Ship hulls** (combined with anti-fouling coatings) — brackish water with poor ICCP suitability
- **Offshore jacket structures**, monopiles, and wind turbine foundations
- **Ballast tanks** of tankers (often alongside ICCP)
- **Harbor structures**, jetties, sheet pile walls
- **Pipeline risers**, subsea manifolds, and wellheads
- **Internal surfaces of storage tanks** carrying seawater ballast

The **advantage** of SACP over ICCP is that it requires **no external power source**, no rectifier, no cables from shore, making it ideal for **remote offshore installations** and **hazardous (explosive) zones**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Anode Resistance Formula (Humble-Bryan Equation)

> [!NOTE]
> The anode resistance equation is derived by solving **Laplace's equation** $\nabla^2 V = 0$ in cylindrical coordinates for a hemispherical or cylindrical anode buried/disposed in a semi-infinite electrolyte medium.

### Starting Equation (Cylindrical Anode in Semi-Infinite Medium)

The potential distribution around a cylindrical current source of length $L$ and radius $r$ in a homogeneous electrolyte is given by:

$$V(r,z) = \dfrac{I_a \rho}{4\pi L} \ln\left[\dfrac{z + \sqrt{z^2 + r^2}}{z - L + \sqrt{(z-L)^2 + r^2}} \cdot \dfrac{z - L + \sqrt{(z-L)^2 + r^2}}{z + \sqrt{z^2 + r^2}} \right]$$

For a long, slender anode where $L \gg r$ and assuming remote reference at infinity, the anode-to-electrolyte resistance simplifies via asymptotic expansion. The integration of current density over the surface gives:

$$\boxed{R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Begin with the assumption of a long, isolated cylindrical anode of length $L$ and equivalent radius $r$, with current uniformly distributed.

$$R_a = \dfrac{V_{\text{surface}}}{I_a}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the boundary condition — at the anode surface (r → a, the equivalent anode radius), the potential equals $V_a$.

$$V_a = \dfrac{I_a \rho}{2\pi L} \int_0^L \dfrac{dx}{\sqrt{x^2 + a^2}}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral:

$$V_a = \dfrac{I_a \rho}{2\pi L} \left[\sinh^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{a}\right)\right]_0^L = \dfrac{I_a \rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left[\dfrac{L + \sqrt{L^2 + a^2}}{a}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Apply the long-anode approximation $L \gg a$:

$$\sqrt{L^2 + a^2} \approx L + \dfrac{a^2}{2L}$$

$$V_a \approx \dfrac{I_a \rho}{2\pi L} \ln\left[\dfrac{2L}{a}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Introduce the end-effect correction (the actual anode is in a half-space, not infinite medium, and end caps contribute additional current path):

$$V_a \approx \dfrac{I_a \rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{a}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 6:** Solve for resistance:

$$\boxed{R_a = \dfrac{V_a}{I_a} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{a}\right) - 1\right]}$$

This is the famous **Humble-Bryan equation (1953)**, universally used in marine CP design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass of Anode Required

### Governing Principle (Faraday's First Law)

The mass of metal dissolved is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed:

$$m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $M$ = atomic weight (kg/kmol)
- $n$ = valency (number of electrons per atom)
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol** = **26.8 A·h/g**

### Electrochemical Capacity (C)

$$C = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M \times 1000} \quad \text{[A·h/kg]}$$

For Zinc (M = 65.4, n = 2):

$$C_{\text{Zn}} = \dfrac{2 \times 26.8}{65.4} = 0.820 \text{ A·h/g} = 820 \text{ A·h/kg}$$

> Note: In practice, due to side reactions (self-corrosion, H₂ evolution on Zn), the **practical capacity** is **780 A·h/kg**, slightly less than theoretical.

### Total Anode Mass Required

The total charge needed over design life $t_l$ (in years):

$$Q_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{total}} \times t_l \times 8760 \quad \text{[A·h]}$$

The mass required to deliver this charge at efficiency $\eta$ and capacity $C$:

$$M_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{C \times \eta} = \dfrac{I_{\text{total}} \times t_l \times 8760}{C \times \eta}$$

### Number of Anodes

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{M_{\text{total}}}{M_{\text{ind}}}}$$

Or equivalently, by current demand:

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_a}}$$

where $I_a$ is the current delivered by one anode (from $I_a = \Delta E / R_a$).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A steel pipeline of surface area **1200 m²** is to be protected by **zinc sacrificial anodes** in seawater. The design life is **15 years**. Required current density is **150 mA/m²**. Each zinc anode is a cylinder of length **600 mm** and diameter **40 mm**. Seawater resistivity = **0.25 Ω·m**. Zinc practical capacity $C$ = **780 A·h/kg**. Current efficiency $\eta$ = **0.95**. Calculate:
> (a) Total current demand
> (b) Anode resistance and current output per anode
> (c) Total mass of anodes required
> (d) Number of anodes required

### Given Data
- $A_c = 1200 \text{ m}^2$
- $i_c = 150 \text{ mA/m}^2 = 0.150 \text{ A/m}^2$
- $t_l = 15 \text{ years}$
- $L = 600 \text{ mm} = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- $r = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
- $\rho = 0.25 \text{ Ω·m}$
- $C = 780 \text{ A·h/kg}$, $\eta = 0.95$
- $\Delta E = E_c - E_a = -0.80 - (-1.05) = 0.25 \text{ V}$ (Zn vs protected steel)

### Part (a) — Total Current Demand

$$I_{\text{total}} = A_c \times i_c = 1200 \times 0.150 = 180 \text{ A}$$

### Part (b) — Anode Resistance & Current Output

$$R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$$

Substituting values:

$$\dfrac{4L}{r} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.6}{0.020} = 120$$

$$\ln(120) = 4.787$$

$$R_a = \dfrac{0.25}{2\pi \times 0.6} \times [4.787 - 1] = \dfrac{0.25}{3.7699} \times 3.787$$

$$R_a = 0.0663 \times 3.787 = 0.2511 \text{ Ω}$$

Current per anode:

$$I_a = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_a} = \dfrac{0.25}{0.2511} = 0.9956 \text{ A} \approx 1.00 \text{ A}$$

### Part (c) — Total Anode Mass

$$M_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{total}} \times t_l \times 8760}{C \times \eta}$$

$$M_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{180 \times 15 \times 8760}{780 \times 0.95} = \dfrac{23{,}652{,}000}{741} = 31{,}919 \text{ kg}$$

### Part (d) — Number of Anodes

First, compute mass of single anode (volume × density):

$$V_{\text{anode}} = \pi r^2 L = \pi \times (0.020)^2 \times 0.6 = 7.540 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$M_{\text{ind}} = V \times \rho_{\text{Zn}} = 7.540 \times 10^{-4} \times 7140 = 5.383 \text{ kg}$$

Number of anodes:

$$N = \dfrac{M_{\text{total}}}{M_{\text{ind}}} = \dfrac{31{,}919}{5.383} = 5930 \text{ anodes}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Note for KTU valuation:** Always show units explicitly at every step. Marks are awarded for **each substitution step**, not just the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the principle of sacrificial anode cathodic protection. Why is zinc commonly used as a sacrificial anode for marine applications?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Sacrificial anode cathodic protection is a galvanic corrosion control technique in which a more electrochemically active metal (anode) is electrically connected to the structure to be protected. The sacrificial anode preferentially corrodes, releasing electrons that flow to the protected structure, suppressing its anodic dissolution.

Zinc is preferred for marine applications because:
1. Its electrode potential (**−1.05 V vs SCE**) is sufficiently negative relative to mild steel to drive protective current.
2. It has stable electrochemical behaviour in seawater with **high current efficiency (~95%)**.
3. It possesses a **practical capacity of ~780 A·h/kg**, ensuring long service life.
4. The corrosion products of zinc are **non-toxic and adherent**, environmentally acceptable.
5. Zinc is **readily available, inexpensive, and dimensionally stable** in chloride environments.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Compare sacrificial anode cathodic protection (SACP) with impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP) in terms of driving EMF, current source, and application suitability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | SACP | ICCP |
|-----------|------|------|
| Driving EMF | Natural galvanic potential difference (~0.25 V Zn–steel) | External DC rectifier (up to 50 V) |
| Current source | Sacrificial metal dissolution | Transformer–rectifier from AC mains |
| Power requirement | **None** (passive) | **Continuous AC power required** |
| Current capacity | **Low to moderate** (limited by anode area) | **Very high** (adjustable) |
| Application | Small/remote structures, marine, soil | Large pipelines, ships, storage tanks |
| Risk of overprotection | **Nil** (self-limiting) | **Yes** — H₂ embrittlement, coating damage |
| Maintenance | Periodic anode replacement | Rectifier maintenance |

---

## 5.2 PART B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

### Question A `(a)` (7 Marks)
**With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of sacrificial anode cathodic protection. Discuss the criteria for selection of sacrificial anode materials for marine applications.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Working Principle:**

In SACP, a metal more active than the protected structure (the sacrificial anode) is metallically bonded to the structure and immersed in a common electrolyte (seawater). The natural potential difference between the anode and the structure establishes a galvanic cell. The anode undergoes anodic dissolution:

$$\text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$$

The released electrons travel through the metallic connection to the steel (cathode), where they participate in the cathodic oxygen reduction reaction:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$

This shifts the steel potential in the negative direction (cathodic polarization). When the potential reaches **−0.80 V vs. Cu/CuSO₄**, the corrosion rate of iron becomes negligible, achieving full protection.

**Selection Criteria for Marine Anode Materials:**

1. **Electrode Potential:** Sufficiently negative relative to steel (~−0.8 V). Mg, Zn, and Al alloys satisfy this.
2. **Theoretical and Practical Capacity:** High A·h/kg. Al (~2800) > Mg (~1230) > Zn (~780).
3. **Current Efficiency:** Should be high (>90%) with minimal self-corrosion.
4. **Corrosion Behaviour:** Should corrode uniformly, not pitting or passivating.
5. **Density:** Lower density (Al) gives more ampere-hours per kilogram.
6. **Environmental Compatibility:** Non-toxic, environmentally safe corrosion products.
7. **Cost and Availability:** Economical for the scale of application.
8. **Mechanical Integrity:** Should not become brittle, crack, or detach during consumption.

For marine seawater service, **aluminum-zinc-indium (Al-Zn-In)** alloys are most widely used today, followed by **zinc** (preferred where Al passivation risk exists, e.g., warm brackish water).

### Question A `(b)` (7 Marks)
**A ship hull of wetted area 2500 m² is to be protected using aluminum sacrificial anodes in seawater. Design current density = 120 mA/m². Each anode is a 500 mm long, 50 mm diameter cylinder. Design life = 10 years. Seawater resistivity = 0.30 Ω·m. Al anode: capacity = 2700 A·h/kg, density = 2700 kg/m³, current efficiency = 0.90. Calculate: (i) total current demand, (ii) anode resistance, (iii) current output per anode, (iv) total mass of anodes, and (v) number of anodes required. Take ΔE = 0.30 V.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $A_c = 2500 \text{ m}^2$; $i_c = 0.120 \text{ A/m}^2$
- $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$; $r = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- $t_l = 10 \text{ yr}$; $\rho = 0.30 \text{ Ω·m}$
- $C = 2700 \text{ A·h/kg}$; $\eta = 0.90$; $\Delta E = 0.30 \text{ V}$
- $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**(i) Total Current Demand:**
$$I_{\text{total}} = A_c \times i_c = 2500 \times 0.120 = 300 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Anode Resistance (Humble-Bryan):**
$$\dfrac{4L}{r} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.5}{0.025} = 80$$
$$\ln(80) = 4.382$$
$$R_a = \dfrac{0.30}{2\pi \times 0.5} \times (4.382 - 1) = \dfrac{0.30}{3.1416} \times 3.382$$
$$R_a = 0.0955 \times 3.382 = 0.3230 \text{ Ω}$$

**(iii) Current per Anode:**
$$I_a = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_a} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.3230} = 0.929 \text{ A}$$

**(iv) Total Mass of Anodes:**
$$M_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{total}} \times t_l \times 8760}{C \times \eta} = \dfrac{300 \times 10 \times 8760}{2700 \times 0.90}$$
$$M_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{26{,}280{,}000}{2430} = 10{,}815 \text{ kg}$$

**(v) Number of Anodes:**

Volume of single anode:
$$V = \pi r^2 L = \pi \times (0.025)^2 \times 0.5 = 9.817 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

Mass of single anode:
$$M_{\text{ind}} = 9.817 \times 10^{-4} \times 2700 = 2.651 \text{ kg}$$

Number of anodes:
$$N = \dfrac{M_{\text{total}}}{M_{\text{ind}}} = \dfrac{10{,}815}{2.651} = 4080 \text{ anodes}$$

---

## 5.3 PART B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

### Question B `(a)` (7 Marks)
**Discuss the advantages and limitations of sacrificial anode cathodic protection. What are the various types of anodes used? Compare aluminum, zinc, and magnesium anodes in detail.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Advantages of SACP:**

1. **No external power source** required — ideal for remote marine locations.
2. **Simple installation** — anodes are bolted or welded to the structure.
3. **Self-regulating** — current decreases automatically as protection is achieved (no overprotection risk).
4. **Low maintenance** — only periodic anode replacement after design life.
5. **Safe in hazardous/explosive atmospheres** — no sparking from electrical equipment.
6. **Uniform current distribution** — multiple small anodes provide better spread than few large ICCP anodes.
7. **Lower initial cost** for small installations.

**Limitations:**

1. **Low driving voltage** (~0.25 V) — unsuitable for high-resistivity electrolytes (dry soil).
2. **Limited current capacity** — not viable for very large structures requiring >500 A.
3. **Anode consumption** — requires periodic replacement, raising long-term cost.
4. **Ineffective in highly resistive media** (deserts, frozen ground).
5. **Anode mass adds weight** — undesirable on floating structures.

**Comparison of Anode Materials:**

| Property | Magnesium | Zinc | Aluminum |
|----------|-----------|------|----------|
| Theoretical Capacity (A·h/kg) | 2200 | 820 | 2980 |
| Practical Capacity (A·h/kg) | 1100–1230 | 780 | 2500–2850 |
| Density (kg/m³) | 1740 | 7140 | 2700 |
| Open-circuit potential (V vs SCE) | −1.55 to −1.75 | −1.05 | −1.05 to −1.10 |
| Current efficiency (%) | 50–55 | 90–95 | 85–95 |
| Driving voltage to steel | 0.75 V | 0.25 V | 0.30 V |
| Best electrolyte | Soil/resistive | Seawater | Seawater |
| Self-corrosion tendency | High | Low | Low |

### Question B `(b)` (7 Marks)
**An offshore jacket structure of wetted area 5000 m² is protected by zinc bracelet anodes in seawater. Required current density = 180 mA/m². Each bracelet anode has mass 250 kg. Seawater resistivity = 0.25 Ω·m. Zinc capacity = 780 A·h/kg, current efficiency = 0.90, utilization factor = 0.85. Design life = 20 years. Verify whether the anode design is adequate and calculate the number of bracelet anodes required.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $A_c = 5000 \text{ m}^2$; $i_c = 0.180 \text{ A/m}^2$
- $M_{\text{ind}} = 250 \text{ kg}$
- $\rho = 0.25 \text{ Ω·m}$; $C = 780 \text{ A·h/kg}$; $\eta = 0.90$; $U = 0.85$
- $t_l = 20 \text{ yr}$

**Step 1 — Total Current Demand:**
$$I_{\text{total}} = A_c \times i_c = 5000 \times 0.180 = 900 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Mass Available per Anode:**
$$M_{\text{eff}} = M_{\text{ind}} \times U = 250 \times 0.85 = 212.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Charge per Anode over 20 years:**
$$Q_{\text{anode}} = M_{\text{eff}} \times C \times \eta = 212.5 \times 780 \times 0.90 = 149{,}205 \text{ A·h}$$

**Step 4 — Time duration corresponding to charge:**
$$Q_{\text{yearly demand}} = 900 \times 8760 = 7{,}884{,}000 \text{ A·h/year}$$

**Step 5 — Number of Anodes:**
$$N = \dfrac{I_{\text{total}} \times t_l \times 8760}{Q_{\text{anode}}} = \dfrac{900 \times 20 \times 8760}{149{,}205}$$
$$N = \dfrac{157{,}680{,}000}{149{,}205} \approx 1057 \text{ anodes}$$

**Step 6 — Adequacy Check (Total Mass Required):**
$$M_{\text{required}} = N \times M_{\text{ind}} = 1057 \times 250 = 264{,}250 \text{ kg}$$

Alternatively, applying the mass formula:
$$M_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{total}} \times t_l \times 8760}{C \times \eta \times U} = \dfrac{157{,}680{,}000}{780 \times 0.90 \times 0.85}$$
$$M_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{157{,}680{,}000}{596.7} = 264{,}252 \text{ kg}$$ ✓

**Conclusion:** Approximately **1057 bracelet anodes of 250 kg each** (total ~264 tonnes) are required to protect the jacket for 20 years. The design is adequate provided anode distribution ensures current coverage within 25 m radial distance per anode.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion error** — Mixing cm/mm with m in resistance formula (KTU deducts 1 mark).
> 2. **Forgetting the 8760 conversion** — Using 365 days instead of 8760 hours per year (full marks lost on mass calculation).
> 3. **Not applying utilization factor (U)** — Many students calculate total mass ignoring that not 100% of the anode is consumed.
> 4. **Confusing "current capacity" with "current output"** — Capacity (A·h/kg) is charge per mass; Output (A) is instantaneous current from one anode.
> 5. **Wrong sign convention for ΔE** — Always take $|E_c - E_a|$; never subtract to give negative.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch** in Part A — KTU board examiners award 1–2 marks for a neat, labeled diagram.
> 7. **Forgetting to state protection criterion** — Always write: "Steel polarized to **−0.80 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** reference electrode (NACE SP0169)."
> 8. **Applying SACP where resistivity is high** — Marks deducted if student does not mention limitation w.r.t. soil resistivity > 5000 Ω·cm.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - **Definition:** SACP = cathodic protection using a sacrificial (more active) anode.
> - **Driving force:** Galvanic potential difference (no external power).
> - **Common anodes:** Mg (soil), Zn and Al (seawater).
> - **Protection criterion:** Steel potential = **−0.80 V vs. Cu/CuSO₄** (or −0.77 V vs. Ag/AgCl/seawater).
> - **Anode capacity (memorize):** Zn ≈ 780 A·h/kg; Al ≈ 2700 A·h/kg; Mg ≈ 1230 A·h/kg.
> - **Humble-Bryan anode resistance formula:** $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$
> - **Current output per anode:** $I_a = \Delta E / R_a$.
> - **Total current demand:** $I_{\text{total}} = A_c \times i_c$.
> - **Total mass:** $M = \dfrac{I \cdot t_l \cdot 8760}{C \cdot \eta \cdot U}$.
> - **Number of anodes:** $N = M_{\text{total}} / M_{\text{ind}}$ OR $N = I_{\text{total}} / I_a$.
> - **Current densities:** Bare steel 80–300 mA/m²; Coated 5–30 mA/m².
> - **Seawater resistivity:** 0.20–0.30 Ω·m (use 0.25 if not given).
> - **Hours per year:** 8760 = 365 × 24.
> - **Advantages:** No power, simple, self-regulating, safe in explosive zones.
> - **Limitations:** Low voltage, not for high-resistivity media, periodic anode replacement.
> - **Applications:** Ship hulls, offshore jackets, pipelines, ballast tanks, jetties.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Sacrificial Anode System for Ships and Marine Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Design of Sacrificial Anode System for Ships and Marine Structures # ⚓ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)** is an electrochemical corrosion-mitigation technique in which a more electrochemically *active* metal (the **anode**) is electrically bonded to the less active structural metal (the **cathode**) and is allowed to corrode preferentially, thereby protecting the structure. The driving force is the natural galvanic potential difference between the two metals when immersed in a common electrolyte (seawater). In marine service, the three commercially accepted sacrificial anode alloys are: 1. **Aluminium–Zinc–Indium (Al–Zn–In)** alloys – *most widely used in modern offshore and ship-hull installations*. 2. **Zinc (Zn) alloys** – *traditionally used; specification controlled by MIL-A-18001 equivalent*. 3. **Magnesium (Mg) alloys** – *restricted to low-conductivity or fresh-water media, not preferred in open sea because of passivation risk at high temperatures and over-driving*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The design of a sacrificial anode system is fundamentally a *current-balance* and *mass-balance* problem. The anode must deliver sufficient current to polarise the entire structure to a protective potential (typically **−0.80 V vs. Ag/AgCl/seawater** for clean steel) **and** possess enough electrochemical mass to do so for the stipulated design life (commonly 5, 10, 15, or 25 years depending on the asset class). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Rusty Knight and His Copper Shield" Imagine a medieval knight whose armour is forged from **steel (the structure)**. The knight wades into a swamp (seawater) and the swamp begins to eat the iron — that is corrosion. To protect him, a clever blacksmith rivets **blocks of pure zinc (sacrificial anodes)** all over the armour. The zinc is *more reactive* than iron (lies higher in the galvanic series). When the swamp's salty brine attacks, it attacks the zinc first, dissolving it slowly. The steel knight is **cathodically polarised** — converted into a benign, nobler, non-corroding role. The zinc dissolves, sacrifices itself, and the knight survives. Three corollaries follow directly from this analogy: - **Quantity of zinc needed** = how big the swamp is, and how long the knight must stay dry. - **Distance matters** — the knight's shoulders are far from the zinc rivets; resistance in the swamp ("seawater resistivity") limits how far the protective current can travel. - **Time matters** — every gram of zinc carries a finite "charge" (its **electrochemical capacity**, in Ampere-hours per kg). When the zinc is consumed, the knight rusts again. > [!NOTE] > The complete engineering design of a sacrificial anode system is the quantitative translation of this analogy: choose the *anode alloy*, *anode mass*, *anode count*, *anode geometry*, and *anode placement* such that the protective current reaches every square metre of the structure for the full design life. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Seawater) | |---|---|---| | Seawater resistivity | $\rho$ | **0.20 – 0.30 Ω·m** (≈ 0.25 Ω·m at 20 °C, 35 ppt salinity) | | Protection potential for steel | $E_{c,prot}$ | **−0.80 V (Ag/AgCl)**; −0.95 V (Zn); −0.85 V (Cu/CuSO₄) | | Open-circuit Al–Zn–In potential | $E_{a,Al}$ | **−1.05 to −1.10 V (Ag/AgCl)** | | Open-circuit Zn alloy potential | $E_{a,Zn}$ | **−1.00 to −1.05 V (Ag/AgCl)** | | Al–Zn–In electrochemical capacity | $C_{Al}$ | **2400 – 2700 Ah/kg** | | Zn alloy electrochemical capacity | $C_{Zn}$ | **780 Ah/kg** | | Anode utilisation factor | $U$ | **0.50 – 0.95** (typically 0.85 for Al) | | Faraday constant | $F$ | **96 485 C/mol** (≈ 26.8 Ah/mol) | | Mean current density (bare steel, tropical) | $i_{bare}$ | **100 – 150 mA/m²** (initial); 80 – 120 (mean) | | Mean current density (temperate) | $i_{bare}$ | **70 – 100 mA/m²** | # 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step The sacrificial anode system protects a marine structure by converting the **entire** cathodic surface (hull, ballast tank, jacket leg, pipeline, mooring buoy) into a cathode of an electrolytic cell. The current path is: - **Anode surface** (active metal dissolving) → **Seawater electrolyte** (ionic conduction) → **Cathode surface** (steel absorbing electrons, no longer oxidising) → **Electrical bond / cable** → back to the **anode core**. The logic chain a KTU examiner expects: 1. Establish the **design basis**: protected area, environmental parameters (T, salinity, dissolved O₂), design life, coating type and breakdown assumption. 2. Select the **anode alloy** and its electrochemical properties ($C$, $E_a$). 3. Determine the **current demand** $I_{total}$ of the structure to be polarised to the protective potential. 4. Compute the **anode resistance** $R_a$ in seawater from geometry and resistivity. 5. Compute the **current output of a single anode** $I_a$ using Ohm's law applied to the anode/electrolyte/cathode circuit. 6. Calculate the **number of anodes** $N$ from $N = I_{total} / I_a$. 7. Verify **anode life** $T$ from Faraday's law — it must exceed the design life. 8. Establish **distribution rules** (linear spacing on hulls, sub-circuits on platforms) so that the protective current reaches every part of the structure within throwing-distance limits. --- ## 2.2 The Two Governing Equations The entire design rests on **two equations**: Ohm's law (to size the current) and Faraday's law (to size the mass). ### 2.2.1 Ohm's Law: Anode Current Output $$I_a = \frac{\Delta E}{R_a}$$ where the **driving voltage** $\Delta E$ is the difference between the anode closed-circuit potential and the protected structure potential: $$\Delta E = E_a - E_c \quad \text{(absolute values, V)}$$ ### 2.2.2 Faraday's Law: Anode Mass Required The mass of anode consumed when delivering a current $I$ continuously for a time $t$ is: $$M = \frac{I \cdot t}{C \cdot U}$$ with $t$ in **hours** if $C$ is in Ah/kg. Inserting $t = 8760 \cdot T$ for time in years: $$\boxed{\,M = \frac{I \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot U} \quad \text{[kg]}\,}$$ and the corresponding anode *life* equation (used for verification): $$\boxed{\,T = \frac{C \cdot U \cdot M}{8760 \cdot I_a} \quad \text{[years]}\,}$$ --- ## 2.3 Anode Resistance Formulas (McCoy-Dwigt Family) The anode-to-seawater resistance $R_a$ depends on geometry. The most-cited KTU formulas are: ### (a) Long Slender Rod (Cylindrical Anode in Seawater) $$R_a = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$$ - Valid for $L \gg r$ (length at least 4× the equivalent radius). ### (b) Long Flush-Mounted Plate Anode (e.g. ship's side or bilge keel) $$R_a = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{w}\right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{t}\right) - 2\right]$$ - $L$ = length, $w$ = width, $t$ = thickness of the plate. ### (c) Short Stand-Off Anode (Bracelet / Doubler Plate on Piles) $$R_a = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{2L}{r}\right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{d}\right) - 0.23\right]$$ - $d$ = stand-off distance from the parent metal. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorise the structure of the table — the absolute values inside formulas (such as protection potential, $\rho$, capacity $C$) are *variables* chosen by the designer; the equations themselves are the *skeleton* the examiner expects you to reproduce. | # | Design Parameter | Equation | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Wetted structure area | $A_c$ | m² | Hull area below waterline; jacket wetted area; pipe OD × π × L | | 2 | Mean current demand | $I_{total} = A_c \cdot i_c$ | A | $i_c$ = design current density (mA/m²); use mean over life | | 3 | Effective demand (coated surface) | $I_{total} = A_c \cdot i_c \cdot f_{bd}$ | A | $f_{bd}$ = coating-breakdown fraction (0.05 → 0.50 over life) | | 4 | Anode driving voltage | $\Delta E = E_a - E_{c,prot}$ | V | Use tabulated closed-circuit potentials | | 5 | Long rod resistance (Dwight) | $R_a = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$ | Ω | For $L/r \ge 4$ | | 6 | Anode current output | $I_a = \Delta E \,/\, R_a$ | A | Ohm's law applied to the anode circuit | | 7 | Anode mass (Faraday) | $M = \frac{I_a \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot U}$ | kg | $T$ in years; $C$ in Ah/kg | | 8 | Anode life verification | $T = \frac{C \cdot U \cdot M}{8760 \cdot I_a}$ | years | Must be ≥ design life | | 9 | Number of anodes | $N = I_{total} \,/\, I_a$ | — | Round up; then add contingency | | 10 | Net anode mass per unit | $M_{net} = N \cdot M_{anode}$ | kg | Total anode inventory | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** The exact same procedure is mandated by **DNV-RP-B401** (Cathodic Protection Design), **NACE SP0176** (Impressed Current), and **ISO 13174** (Sacrificial Anodes for Marine Structures) — i.e. the procedure is *codified international engineering practice*, not a textbook exercise. A shipyard's CP design report is essentially a worked-example of these equations. # 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday's Law for Anode Mass **Starting point:** Faraday's first law of electrolysis states that the mass of a metal dissolved at an electrode is proportional to the total electric charge passed: $$m = \frac{Q \cdot M_{w}}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{w}}{n \cdot F}$$ where $M_{w}$ is the molar mass and $n$ the valence (number of electrons per atom). For *engineering* sacrificial-anode design, we collapse the constants $M_{w}$, $n$, and $F$ into a single, **alloy-specific** measured quantity — the **electrochemical capacity** $C$, defined experimentally as: $$C = \frac{n \cdot F}{M_{w}} \quad \text{[Ah/kg]}$$ Therefore Faraday's law in engineering form is: $$m = \frac{I \cdot t}{C}$$ Two refinements are required for the real world: - Only a fraction $U$ of the geometric mass is consumed before the anode becomes mechanically/electrically useless (the *utilisation factor*, $0 < U \le 1$). - The total delivered charge per anode = $I_a \cdot t$ where $t = 8760 \cdot T$ (h per year × years). Hence the engineering mass-balance equation: $$M_{gross} = \frac{I_a \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot U} \quad \text{[kg]} \qquad \text{(Eq. A)}$$ The utilisation factor accounts for the "stub" of anode that remains at end-of-life, when the remaining cross-section is too small to support a uniform current distribution. For Al–Zn–In anodes, the KTU/DNV accepted value is $U = 0.85$ for stand-off anodes and $U = 0.80$ for flush-mounted types. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dwight's Anode Resistance Formula For a long cylindrical electrode in an infinite homogeneous electrolyte, the potential distribution is axisymmetric. The differential current crossing a cylindrical surface of radius $x$ from the electrode axis is: $$I = 4\pi x L \cdot \frac{1}{\rho} \cdot \left(-\frac{dV}{dx}\right)$$ Integrating from $r$ (anode surface) to $\infty$ (far-field reference at zero potential): $$V(r) = \frac{I \rho}{4\pi L} \ln\!\left(\frac{\infty}{r}\right)$$ For a finite rod of length $L$, an image-source correction and end-effect correction are introduced. The classical Dwight result is: $$R_a = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right] \qquad \text{(Eq. B)}$$ Valid when $L / r \ge 4$, which is satisfied for almost every commercial an anode (typical bracelet: $L = 0.5$–$2$ m, $r = 0.025$–$0.05$ m, so $L/r = 10$–$80$). > [!NOTE] > **Why two factors of 2 appear:** The factor $2\pi$ in the denominator is the full circumference of the cylindrical current sheet; the factor 4 in the log is the image-source doubling for the equipotential at infinity. The −1 term corrects for the finite end (compared to the ideal infinite line-source solution). --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Ship Hull Cathodic Protection A VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) is to be protected by an Al–Zn–In bracelet-anode system. The design data are: | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Wetted hull area, $A_c$ | 12 000 m² | | Initial current density (bare steel, tropical) | $i_i = 130$ mA/m² | | Mean current density (mean life) | $i_m = 90$ mA/m² | | Final current density (end of life, coated-degraded) | $i_f = 150$ mA/m² | | Coating breakdown fraction (mean) | $f_{bd} = 0.20$ (mean) → 0.50 (final) | | Anode alloy | Al–Zn–In | | Anode closed-circuit potential | $E_a = -1.05$ V (Ag/AgCl) | | Protected hull potential | $E_c = -0.80$ V (Ag/AgCl) | | Electrochemical capacity of Al–Zn–In | $C = 2700$ Ah/kg | | Utilisation factor | $U = 0.85$ | | Seawater resistivity | $\rho = 0.25$ Ω·m | | Anode dimensions (slender rod) | $L = 1.5$ m, $r = 0.05$ m | | Design life | $T = 5$ years | --- ### Step 1 — Total current demand at mean life $$I_{total,m} = A_c \cdot i_m \cdot f_{bd} = 12\,000 \times 0.090 \times 0.20$$ $$I_{total,m} = 216 \text{ A}$$ ### Step 2 — Total current demand at end of life (governs anode count) $$I_{total,f} = A_c \cdot i_f \cdot f_{bd,f} = 12\,000 \times 0.150 \times 0.50$$ $$I_{total,f} = 900 \text{ A}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Pitfall: Many students use the *initial* current density to size the anode count. This **under-designs** the system because the coated surface progressively exposes more bare metal, and anodes lose mass, increasing their resistance. The **end-of-life** current demand is the governing case. ### Step 3 — Driving voltage $$\Delta E = E_a - E_c = \left(-1.05\right) - \left(-0.80\right) = -0.25 \text{ V}$$ Magnitude (always quoted positive in design): $$\left\vert \Delta E \right\vert = 0.25 \text{ V}$$ ### Step 4 — Anode-to-seawater resistance (Dwight) $$R_a = \frac{0.25}{2\pi \times 1.5}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4 \times 1.5}{0.05}\right) - 1\right]$$ Numerator: $\rho = 0.25$; denominator $2\pi L = 9.425$. $$R_a = \frac{0.25}{9.425}\left[\ln(120) - 1\right] = 0.02653 \times \left(4.7875 - 1\right)$$ $$R_a = 0.02653 \times 3.7875 = 0.1005 \text{ Ω}$$ ### Step 5 — Current output of one anode $$I_a = \frac{\Delta E}{R_a} = \frac{0.25}{0.1005} = 2.488 \text{ A}$$ ### Step 6 — Number of anodes (governed by end-of-life demand) $$N = \frac{I_{total,f}}{I_a} = \frac{900}{2.488} = 361.7$$ Round up and apply 10 % contingency: $$N_{design} = 400 \text{ anodes}$$ ### Step 7 — Mass of each anode (verify life) The geometric mass of an anode of density 2700 kg/m³ for a 1.5 m × 0.05 m radius cylinder is: $$V_{anode} = \pi r^2 L = \pi (0.05)^2 \times 1.5 = 1.178 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3$$ $$M_{geo} = 2700 \times 1.178 \times 10^{-2} = 31.81 \text{ kg}$$ Life verification: $$T = \frac{C \cdot U \cdot M}{8760 \cdot I_a} = \frac{2700 \times 0.85 \times 31.81}{8760 \times 2.488}$$ $$T = \frac{72\,953.4}{21\,794.9} = 3.35 \text{ years}$$ **This is less than the 5-year design life — anode is under-sized!** ### Step 8 — Iterate: required mass for 5-year life $$M_{req} = \frac{I_a \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot U} = \frac{2.488 \times 5 \times 8760}{2700 \times 0.85}$$ $$M_{req} = \frac{108\,962.4}{2295} = 47.48 \text{ kg}$$ ### Step 9 — Geometric redesign (length increase) Solving $M_{req} = \rho_{Al} \cdot \pi r^2 L$ for $L$ (keeping $r = 0.05$ m): $$L = \frac{M_{req}}{\rho_{Al} \cdot \pi r^2} = \frac{47.48}{2700 \times \pi \times 0.0025} = \frac{47.48}{21.21} = 2.238 \text{ m}$$ So a new anode size of $L = 2.25$ m, $r = 0.05$ m gives 47.6 kg — meets the design life. Recheck Dwight's resistance with the new length: $$R_{a,new} = \frac{0.25}{2\pi \times 2.25}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4 \times 2.25}{0.05}\right) - 1\right]$$ $$R_{a,new} = \frac{0.25}{14.137}\left[\ln(180) - 1\right] = 0.01769 \times (5.193 - 1) = 0.0741 \text{ Ω}$$ Recheck current output: $$I_{a,new} = \frac{0.25}{0.0741} = 3.374 \text{ A}$$ Recheck anode count (end-of-life demand): $$N = \frac{900}{3.374} = 266.7 \rightarrow 280 \text{ anodes (with 5 % contingency)}$$ ### Step 10 — Distribution rules For a VLCC hull of 240 m length, 280 anodes → roughly one anode every $\frac{240 \times 2}{280} \approx 1.7$ m on each side of the keel, distributed in two parallel rows port and starboard. This satisfies the **maximum-throwing-distance** rule (in seawater, anode effective radius is typically 5–25 m, so 1.7 m spacing is conservative). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Design Choices | Variable increased | Effect on $I_a$ | Effect on $N$ | Effect on $T$ | Engineering trade-off | |---|---|---|---|---| | Anode length $L$ | Increases (R drops) | Decreases | Increases | Larger anodes = fewer count but more weld-repairs | | Anode radius $r$ | Increases (R drops, but weakly) | Decreases | Increases | Marginal — log term depends weakly on $r$ | | Water resistivity $\rho$ | Decreases | Increases | Decreases | Polar / cold water requires more anodes | | Coating breakdown $f_{bd}$ | — | Increases (linearly) | — | Always design for *final* $f_{bd}$ | | Design life $T$ | — | — | — | Mass $M$ scales linearly with $T$ | | Utilisation factor $U$ | — | — | — | Always assume $U \le 0.85$ for Al; 0.50 for Mg | # 🗂️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Flow of the Sacrificial Anode Design Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Design Basis] --> B[Establish protected area Ac in m2] B --> C[Select environment T salinity rho] C --> D[Choose design life T in years] D --> E[Select anode alloy: Al-Zn-In or Zn] E --> F[Determine current density ic initial mean final] F --> G[Compute total current demand I total] G --> H[Assume anode geometry L and r] H --> I[Compute anode resistance Ra via Dwight formula] I --> J[Compute anode current output Ia = dE divided by Ra] J --> K[Compute number of anodes N = I total divided by Ia] K --> L[Compute anode mass M via Faraday law] L --> M{Life check T calc greater than T design} M -- No --> H[Revise geometry increase L or r] M -- Yes --> N[Add 5 to 10 percent contingency] N --> O[Plan distribution on hull jacket pipeline] O --> P[Specify anode type mass and location] P --> Q[End: Bill of Materials and Layout Drawing] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic: Current Flow in a Cathodic Protection Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Anode Surface
Al-Zn-In
Oxidation: Al to Al3 plus plus plus
Electrons released] -->|Electron flow through metal bond| B1[Steel Structure Cathode
Reduction: O2 plus H2O to OH minus
No metal loss] B1 -->|Ionic current in seawater| A1 A1 -->|Current Ia exits anode| A1 B1 -->|Current Ia returns to cathode| B1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g. `A1`, `B1`) with no reserved keywords. Labels are raw uppercase text to ensure clean rendering in Mermaid 10.x parsers. ## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Choosing the Anode Alloy ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start: Choose Anode Alloy] --> Q1{Environment saline or fresh water} Q1 -- Fresh water --> MG[Use Mg alloy
Capacity 1100 to 1230 Ah per kg
Potential minus 1.5 V] Q1 -- Seawater --> Q2{Resistivity below 0.30 ohm m} Q2 -- Yes --> AL[Default to Al-Zn-In
Capacity 2400 to 2700 Ah per kg
Potential minus 1.05 V] Q2 -- Brackish 0.3 to 1.0 --> ZN[Consider Zn
Capacity 780 Ah per kg
Potential minus 1.00 V] Q2 -- High above 1.0 --> MG2[Re-evaluate Mg if practical] AL --> DONE[Output Anode Material Spec] ZN --> DONE MG --> DONE MG2 --> DONE ``` # 🎯 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List the three commercially used sacrificial anode alloys for marine applications and state the standard protection potential for bare steel in seawater.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three commercial sacrificial anode alloys for marine use are: 1. **Aluminium–Zinc–Indium (Al–Zn–In)** alloys — most commonly used, capacity ≈ 2400–2700 Ah/kg, closed-circuit potential −1.05 V (Ag/AgCl). 2. **Zinc (Zn) alloys** — traditional, capacity ≈ 780 Ah/kg, potential ≈ −1.00 V (Ag/AgCl). 3. **Magnesium (Mg) alloys** — used mainly in fresh or low-resistivity water, capacity ≈ 1100–1230 Ah/kg, potential ≈ −1.5 V (Ag/AgCl). The standard protection potential for bare mild steel in aerated seawater is **−0.80 V vs. Ag/AgCl reference electrode** (equivalently −0.85 V vs. Cu/CuSO₄). > **Marking split:** (1 mark) listing three alloys; (1 mark) typical capacities/potentials; (1 mark) protection potential with reference electrode specified. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define the term "anode utilisation factor" and explain why it is always less than unity.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **anode utilisation factor $U$** is the fraction of the *geometric* mass of a sacrificial anode that can be consumed before the anode becomes mechanically or electrically ineffective at the end of its useful life. Mathematically, $U = m_{consumed,\,max} / m_{gross}$. It is always less than unity because: 1. **Mechanical stub loss** — toward end-of-life, the anode cross-section becomes very small, and a residual "core" must remain for the anode to retain its mechanical attachment and current-distribution geometry. This stub is thrown away unused. 2. **Passivation effects** — especially in hot or high-resistivity water, a passive oxide film can form on the surface, halting current output before complete dissolution. 3. **Non-uniform consumption** — the an anode typically erodes non-uniformly (more at the bottom in a vertical stand-off anode), reducing the effective lifetime below the theoretical Faraday prediction. Typical KTU values: $U = 0.85$ for stand-off Al anodes, $0.50$ for Mg, $0.90$ for high-purity Zn. --- ## 📗 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice) > Each 14-mark question is split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, with complete step-by-step model solutions. --- ### ✏️ **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]` **(a) Derive the engineering form of Faraday's law used in sacrificial anode mass calculation, starting from the fundamental Faraday's first law of electrolysis. State clearly all assumptions and the physical meaning of each term. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Faraday's First Law (fundamental form):** The mass $m$ of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge $Q$ passed through the electrolyte: $$m \propto Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \frac{Q \cdot M_w}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_w}{n \cdot F}$$ where: - $m$ = mass dissolved in kg - $I$ = current in A - $t$ = time in s (or h, with constant adjusted) - $M_w$ = molar mass of the dissolving metal in kg/mol - $n$ = valency (electrons per atom) - $F$ = Faraday constant = 96 485 C/mol = 26.8 Ah/mol **Conversion to engineering form:** Define the **electrochemical capacity** $C$ as the charge delivered per kg of metal consumed: $$C = \frac{n \cdot F}{M_w} \quad \text{[Ah/kg]}$$ Substituting into Faraday's equation (with $t$ in hours): $$m = \frac{I \cdot t}{C}$$ **Assumptions in the engineering form:** 1. Single, well-defined oxidation reaction (no side reactions, no passivation). 2. Uniform anode current density. 3. All mass dissolved is the active metal — no oxide skin accumulation. **Final engineering form for design (with utilisation factor $U$ and time in years):** $$\boxed{\,M_{gross} = \frac{I_a \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot U}\,}$$ where $8760$ converts years to hours, and $U$ accounts for the unused stub at end of life. --- **(b) A ship hull of wetted area 4 000 m² is to be protected by Zn alloy bracelet anodes. Each anode has length 600 mm, equivalent radius 38 mm. Seawater resistivity is 0.30 Ω·m. Zn alloy capacity = 780 Ah/kg, utilisation factor = 0.90, closed-circuit potential = −1.00 V (Ag/AgCl), protection potential = −0.80 V. Design current density (mean) = 80 mA/m², coating-breakdown fraction = 0.25, design life = 4 years. Calculate: (i) total current demand, (ii) number of anodes, and (iii) required mass per anode. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given data:** - $A_c = 4000$ m², $i_m = 0.080$ A/m², $f_{bd} = 0.25$, $T = 4$ yr - $L = 0.6$ m, $r = 0.038$ m, $\rho = 0.30$ Ω·m - $C = 780$ Ah/kg, $U = 0.90$ - $E_a = -1.00$ V, $E_c = -0.80$ V → $\Delta E = 0.20$ V **(i) Total current demand:** $$I_{total} = A_c \cdot i_m \cdot f_{bd} = 4000 \times 0.080 \times 0.25 = 80 \text{ A}$$ **(ii) Anode resistance (Dwight):** $$R_a = \frac{0.30}{2\pi \times 0.6}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4 \times 0.6}{0.038}\right) - 1\right]$$ $$R_a = \frac{0.30}{3.770}\left[\ln(63.16) - 1\right] = 0.0796 \times (4.146 - 1)$$ $$R_a = 0.0796 \times 3.146 = 0.2504 \text{ Ω}$$ **Anode current output:** $$I_a = \frac{\Delta E}{R_a} = \frac{0.20}{0.2504} = 0.799 \text{ A}$$ **Number of anodes:** $$N = \frac{I_{total}}{I_a} = \frac{80}{0.799} = 100.1 \rightarrow 101 \text{ anodes}$$ Add 10 % contingency: $$\boxed{N_{design} = 110 \text{ anodes}}$$ **(iii) Required mass per anode:** $$M_{anode} = \frac{I_a \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot U} = \frac{0.799 \times 4 \times 8760}{780 \times 0.90}$$ $$M_{anode} = \frac{27\,998.4}{702} = 39.88 \text{ kg} \approx 40 \text{ kg per anode}$$ $$\boxed{M_{anode} \approx 40 \text{ kg}}$$ **Verification of anode life:** $$T_{calc} = \frac{780 \times 0.90 \times 40}{8760 \times 0.799} = \frac{28\,080}{6999.2} = 4.01 \text{ years} \;\; \checkmark$$ --- ### ✏️ **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]` **(a) Explain, with a neat sketch, the principle of sacrificial anode cathodic protection of a ship hull. Indicate the electron and ion flow directions and label the anode, cathode, electrolyte, and external metallic path. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Sketch description (verbal — to be drawn by student):** A ship hull cross-section is shown immersed in seawater. Discrete blocks of Al–Zn–In alloy are welded to the hull (e.g. on the bilge keel, side shell, and bottom plating). A wire/bracket is shown connecting the anode block to the hull plate. **Labelled elements:** 1. **Anode (Al–Zn–In block)** — undergoes oxidation: $$\text{Al} \rightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^-$$ The anode mass is consumed; the surface is covered with an adherent oxide film. 2. **Cathode (hull steel plate)** — supports the cathodic reduction reaction (oxygen + water): $$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$ The steel surface is polarised to −0.80 V (Ag/AgCl) and is *not* attacked. 3. **Electrolyte (seawater)** — provides ionic conduction between the two electrodes. Ions (Cl⁻, Na⁺, OH⁻) migrate to complete the circuit. 4. **External metallic path (anode–cable–hull connection)** — provides *electronic* (electron) conduction, completing the loop. **Directions of flow:** - **Electron flow:** anode → metallic bond → hull plate (cathode) — *opposite* to conventional current. - **Conventional current:** hull plate → seawater → anode (i.e. from cathode to anode in the electrolyte, completing the loop). - **Ion flow in seawater:** Cl⁻ migrates toward the anode, cations and OH⁻ toward the cathode. **Design implication:** the anode must be in **direct metallic contact** with the hull — if the connection breaks (corroded stud, failed weld), the system fails silently. **Marking split:** (3) sketch + labels; (2) equations; (2) flow directions and connection requirement. --- **(b) A fixed offshore platform has 8 jacket legs in seawater. Each leg is 60 m long, 1.2 m diameter. Design an Al–Zn–In bracelet-anode CP system for 20-year life. Data: $\rho = 0.25$ Ω·m, $C = 2700$ Ah/kg, $U = 0.85$, $E_a = -1.10$ V, $E_c = -0.80$ V, $i_{mean} = 100$ mA/m², $f_{bd} = 1.0$ (bare steel). Anode dimensions: $L = 1.0$ m, $r = 0.06$ m. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Wetted area of one leg:** $$A_{leg} = \pi D L_{leg} = \pi \times 1.2 \times 60 = 226.2 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Total area (8 legs):** $$A_{total} = 8 \times 226.2 = 1809.6 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Total current demand (bare, 20-yr mean):** $$I_{total} = A_{total} \times i_{mean} \times f_{bd} = 1809.6 \times 0.100 \times 1.0 = 181.0 \text{ A}$$ **Step 4 — Driving voltage:** $$\Delta E = 1.10 - 0.80 = 0.30 \text{ V}$$ **Step 5 — Anode resistance (Dwight):** $$R_a = \frac{0.25}{2\pi \times 1.0}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4 \times 1.0}{0.06}\right) - 1\right] = \frac{0.25}{6.283}\left[\ln(66.67) - 1\right]$$ $$R_a = 0.0398 \times (4.200 - 1) = 0.0398 \times 3.200 = 0.1274 \text{ Ω}$$ **Step 6 — Anode current output:** $$I_a = \frac{0.30}{0.1274} = 2.355 \text{ A}$$ **Step 7 — Number of anodes:** $$N = \frac{181.0}{2.355} = 76.86 \rightarrow 77 \text{ anodes minimum}$$ Apply 10 % contingency and even distribution: $$\boxed{N_{design} = 85 \text{ anodes total}}$$ Distribution: $\frac{85}{8} = 10.6 \rightarrow 11$ anodes per leg, spaced at $\frac{60}{11} = 5.45$ m vertically. **Step 8 — Required mass per anode (20-year life):** $$M_{anode} = \frac{I_a \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot U} = \frac{2.355 \times 20 \times 8760}{2700 \times 0.85}$$ $$M_{anode} = \frac{412\,596}{2295} = 179.8 \text{ kg per anode}$$ $$\boxed{M_{anode} \approx 180 \text{ kg}}$$ **Step 9 — Geometric check (density of Al alloy 2700 kg/m³):** $$V_{anode} = \frac{180}{2700} = 0.0667 \text{ m}^3 = \pi r^2 L = \pi (0.06)^2 \times L$$ $$L = \frac{0.0667}{\pi \times 0.0036} = 5.89 \text{ m}$$ So a single bracelet of length 5.9 m at radius 0.06 m meets the 20-year life. Practical industry solution: use **two bracelet anodes per leg** (each ≈ 3 m long, 90 kg) rather than one 5.9 m anode — easier fabrication, installation, and redundancy. > **Final specification:** **2 anodes per leg × 8 legs = 16 bracelet anodes**, each ≈ 3 m long, ≈ 90 kg, distributed at the top and bottom of each leg, providing ≈ 20-year life and ≈ 7 m vertical spacing. --- > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting units in Faraday's equation** — The 8760 factor converts *years to hours*. If you write $M = I \cdot T / (C \cdot U)$ without 8760, your answer is **off by 3 orders of magnitude**. Examiners award **0 marks** for the mass calculation if units are not shown. > > 2. **Designing to initial current density** — End-of-life current demand (with $f_{bd} \approx 0.5$ and reduced anode mass) is the *governing* case. Designing to the initial value **always** under-designs the system. > > 3. **Using open-circuit potentials instead of closed-circuit potentials** — The driving voltage $\Delta E$ must use the *closed-circuit* (working) potential of the anode, which is 50–100 mV more positive than the open-circuit value. Common KTU trap: writing $E_a$ as −1.15 V for Al when the working value is −1.05 V → 10 % error in $I_a$. > > 4. **Forgetting to verify anode life** — A common KTU sub-question (b) requires the life equation. Skipping it loses 2–3 marks. > > 5. **Ignoring contingency** — Industry practice (DNV/NACE) adds 5–10 % spare anodes. KTU mark schemes often give 1 mark for "contingency/allowance" — leave it out and you forfeit it. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density rapid-revision checklist — read this 30 minutes before the exam.** ### 🔑 Core Equations (must be memorised in symbol form) - **Current demand:** $I_{total} = A_c \cdot i_c \cdot f_{bd}$ - **Anode output:** $I_a = \Delta E / R_a$ - **Dwight resistance (long rod):** $R_a = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(4L/r) - 1\right]$ - **Faraday mass:** $M = I_a \cdot T \cdot 8760 / (C \cdot U)$ - **Anode life:** $T = C \cdot U \cdot M / (8760 \cdot I_a)$ - **Anode count:** $N = I_{total} / I_a$ (round up + contingency) ### 🔑 Numerical Constants to Remember - Hours per year: **8760 h/yr** - Faraday constant: **96 485 C/mol ≈ 26.8 Ah/mol** - Seawater resistivity: **0.20 – 0.30 Ω·m** (use 0.25) - Steel protection potential: **−0.80 V (Ag/AgCl)** - Driving voltages: **Al: 0.25–0.30 V; Zn: 0.20 V; Mg: 0.50 V** - Capacities: **Al: 2400–2700 Ah/kg; Zn: 780 Ah/kg; Mg: 1100 Ah/kg** - Utilisation: **Al 0.85; Zn 0.90; Mg 0.50** ### 🔑 Conceptual Must-Knows - The sacrificial anode must be **electrically bonded** to the structure — corrosion of the bonding stud = system failure. - **Al–Zn–In dominates** modern marine CP because of high capacity × high driving voltage × low density. - The **Dwight formula** is valid only for $L/r \ge 4$. - **Coating-breakdown** is a *time-dependent* parameter — always check end-of-life values. - **Anode distribution** must keep the maximum *throwing distance* (typically 5–25 m in seawater) less than the spacing. - For **ballast tanks, internals, and complex geometries**, a *hybrid* design (sacrificial anodes + coating) is the norm. - The two governing equations are **Ohm's law** (current) and **Faraday's law** (mass).

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Sacrificial Anode System: Impressed Current Cathodic Protection System in Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST612 - MARINE MATERIALS, CORROSION PREVENTION AND PROTECTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages of Sacrificial Anode System: Impressed Current Cathodic Protection System in Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) System — Recap

A **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)** system is a passive form of electrochemical protection in which a metal that is **more electrochemically active** (i.e., has a more negative standard electrode potential) is electrically connected to the structure to be protected. Because of the potential difference in the electrolyte (seawater), the sacrificial anode preferentially corrodes (oxidises), while the protected structure (hull, propeller, rudder, etc.) is forced to behave as a **cathode**, thereby suppressing its anodic dissolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> *Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection is a method of corrosion control in which the metal to be protected is made the cathode of a galvanic cell by coupling it with a more active metal (anode) which preferentially corrodes and is sacrificed to protect the structure.*

The active metals commonly used in marine service are:
- **Zinc (Zn)** — Standard potential −1.10 V (SHE), used historically in seawater.
- **Magnesium (Mg)** — Standard potential −2.37 V (SHE), used in low-conductivity media (soil, fresh water).
- **Aluminium (Al)** alloys (Al–Zn–In, Al–Zn–Sn) — Standard potential ≈ −1.10 V (SHE), now the dominant choice for offshore/marine use due to high theoretical capacity.

## 1.2 The Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) System in Ships

An **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** system uses an **external DC power source** (transformer–rectifier unit, TRU) to drive a protective current from an essentially **inert anode** (platinum, mixed-metal-oxide coated titanium, lead–silver alloy, or silicon iron) through the electrolyte onto the hull, which is the cathode. By adjusting the rectifier output, the **hull-to-electrolyte (or hull-to-reference-electrode) potential** is held in the protected range of **−800 mV to −950 mV vs. Ag/AgCl** (or **−850 mV vs. Cu/CuSO₄** reference).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Distinction Between SACP and ICCP**
> - In **SACP**, the protective current is generated by the **galvanic potential difference** between anode and hull (≈ 0.2 V to 0.5 V only).
> - In **ICCP**, the protective current is **impressed (forced)** from an external DC source (≈ 5 V to 50 V), allowing vastly higher current outputs suitable for large ship hulls, jetties, and offshore platforms.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — Sacrificial Anode (The Sacrificial Sheep)**
> Imagine a herd of sheep where one is dressed in a wool sweater the wolves love (the *anode*). The wolves attack that sheep and ignore the rest of the herd (the *cathode/structure*). Once the sacrificed sheep is consumed, a new one replaces it. The herd (ship hull) stays unharmed.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — Impressed Current CP (The Battery Charger of the Sea)**
> Think of ICCP as plugging a leaking iron boat into a *mains electricity-powered pump* that continuously pushes electrons into the iron. The leaking boat (corroding iron) is "charged up" with extra electrons so that the oxidation reaction simply cannot occur. The pump's electrode (the inert anode) wears down very slowly because it is made of corrosion-resistant material.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Metrics

| Reference Electrode | Potential (V vs. SHE) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) | **0.000 V** | Universal reference |
| Copper/Copper Sulphate (CSE) | +0.316 V | Used in soil and shipboard checks |
| Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl) | +0.222 V | Most common in seawater |
| Zinc (seawater) | −0.800 V | Used as portable reference |

Standard density of seawater is **1.025 g/cm³**, and its electrical conductivity is roughly **4 S/m (40,000 µS/cm)** — about 1000 times higher than fresh water, which is why ICCP excels in marine service.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Advantages of the Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection System

1. **No external power source required** — operates purely on the galvanic potential difference between anode and hull.
2. **Simple installation** — anodes can be welded, bolted, or suspended from the hull with minimal cabling.
3. **Low capital cost** for small structures (small boats, short pipelines, internal tank surfaces).
4. **Almost zero maintenance** — once installed, the system requires no operator intervention.
5. **Even current distribution** in low-resistivity electrolytes such as seawater.
6. **Safe in hazardous (explosive) atmospheres** because there is no spark risk from a rectifier.
7. **Self-regulating** — current output automatically adjusts to the electrolyte resistivity; no over-protection normally occurs.
8. **Suitable for coated structures** — adds protection at coating holidays without complex electronics.
9. **Easy retrofit** on in-service vessels during dry-docking.

## 2.2 Disadvantages of the Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection System

1. **Low driving voltage** (0.20 – 0.50 V) — limits the current output, especially in high-resistivity media (estuarine, brackish, or fresh water).
2. **Limited current capacity** per anode — typically **1 A to 5 A** per zinc/aluminium anode in seawater.
3. **Frequent anode replacement** — anode life is consumed, requiring regular dry-dock renewals (typically 3–5 years).
4. **High overall weight** — to deliver large currents, a large number of heavy anodes must be fitted, reducing payload.
5. **Inefficient in large structures** — uncoated or large bare-steel ship hulls (> 10,000 m² wetted area) cannot be protected economically.
6. **Cost escalates with structure size** — material and replacement costs grow linearly with wetted area.
7. **Low utilisation efficiency** — typically only **50 % – 90 %** of the anode mass is consumed before the anode becomes mechanically ineffective ("fall-off" before full dissolution).
8. **Sensitivity to passivation** — zinc anodes may form a passive oxide film in warm, well-aerated water, reducing output.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall**
> Students often write that sacrificial anodes "*never need replacement*". This is wrong — the anode is *consumed* and must be replaced at every dry-docking interval.

## 2.3 Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) in Ships — Components

A marine ICCP system on a ship hull consists of the following sub-systems:

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transformer–Rectifier Unit (TRU)** | Converts ship's AC supply (440 V / 60 Hz) to low-voltage DC (typically 5–24 V, up to 50 V). |
| 2 | **Inert (Impressed Current) Anodes** | Platinised titanium, MMO/Ti, Pb–Ag, or Si–Fe. Mounted in sea chests or on hull via dielectric shields. |
| 3 | **Reference Electrodes (RE)** | Ag/AgCl or Zn-seawater cells used as sensors to measure hull potential. |
| 4 | **Control Panel** | Compares RE signal with set-point and adjusts TRU output automatically (potentiostatic mode). |
| 5 | **Anode Cables & Hull Cables** | Heavy cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) insulated DC cables, well above 95 mm² for ship service. |
| 6 | **Dielectric Shields (Epoxy / FRP)** | Around the hull outlet of each anode to reduce localised overprotection and current crowding. |

## 2.4 Advantages of ICCP in Ships

1. **High driving voltage (5 – 50 V)** — can overcome high electrolyte resistance; suited to large hulls.
2. **Large current output** — single TRU can deliver **100 A to 600 A** continuously.
3. **Adjustable and automatic** — TRU output is regulated by reference electrodes to maintain the optimum protective potential (typically −850 mV vs. Ag/AgCl).
4. **Long life of inert anodes** — 10 – 20 years or more, since corrosion rate of MMO/Ti is essentially zero.
5. **Economical for large wetted areas** — typical on VLCCs, naval combatants, LNG carriers.
6. **Continuous protection in port, at anchor, and underway**.
7. **Remote monitoring** — modern systems (Cathelco, Imenco, MGD) integrate with ship management systems for unattended operation.

## 2.5 Disadvantages of ICCP in Ships

1. **Requires continuous DC power** — failure of TRU immediately stops protection.
2. **High capital cost** — TRU, reference electrodes, cabling, and inert anodes are expensive.
3. **Risk of over-protection** — if hull potential goes too negative (more negative than −1100 mV vs. Ag/AgCl), **hydrogen embrittlement** of high-strength steel and **cathodic blistering of coatings** may occur.
4. **Interference with adjacent structures** — ICCP current may stray and accelerate corrosion of neighbouring moored vessels (so-called **"stray-current corrosion"**).
5. **Complex installation** — needs specialist divers and certified electrical installers.
6. **Dielectric shield maintenance** — coating failure under shields leads to severe localised attack.
7. **Sacrificial anodes still needed** for propeller, rudder, and sea-chest internals because ICCP cannot easily reach internals.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Cathodic Protection Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All numerical problems in the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) for Module 4 are solved using the formulas below. Memorise the symbols, units, and limiting conditions.

| # | Formula | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{total} = A_s \times i_{prot}$ | $I_{total}$ = total current (A), $A_s$ = wetted steel area (m²), $i_{prot}$ = protective current density (A/m²) | Total current requirement |
| 2 | $i_{prot} = \dfrac{i_{coat}}{100} + i_{bare}$ | $i_{coat}$ = current at coating defect (A/m²), $i_{bare}$ = bare steel current density (A/m²) | Coated hulls |
| 3 | $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]$ (Dwight, long slender anode) | $R_a$ = anode-to-electrolyte resistance (Ω), $\rho$ = resistivity (Ω·m), $L$ = anode length (m), $r$ = equivalent radius (m) | Anode resistance in seawater |
| 4 | $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2 \pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | $d$ = anode diameter (m) | Equivalent form using diameter |
| 5 | $I_{anode} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{R_a}$ | $\Delta V$ = driving voltage (V) | Current per anode |
| 6 | $N_{anodes} = \dfrac{I_{total}}{I_{anode}}$ | — | Number of anodes required |
| 7 | $m_{anode} = \dfrac{I_{total} \times t \times 8760}{u \times C_{th}}$ | $m$ = anode mass (kg), $t$ = design life (years), $u$ = utilisation factor (0.5–0.9), $C_{th}$ = theoretical capacity (A·h/kg) | Anode mass |
| 8 | $C_{th}$ (Al) = **2980 A·h/kg**, Zn = **820 A·h/kg**, Mg = **1230 A·h/kg** | — | Theoretical electrochemical capacity |
| 9 | $\eta_{anode} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}}$ | — | Anode utilisation efficiency |
| 10 | $V_{TRU} = I_{total} \times (R_a + R_{cable} + R_{cathode})$ | $V_{TRU}$ = rectifier voltage | TRU sizing |
| 11 | Cathodic protection criterion: $E_{Cu/CuSO_4} \le -0.850\ V$ (or $-0.800\ V$ vs. Ag/AgCl) | — | Acceptance criterion |

## 2.7 Typical Protective Current Densities in Seawater

| Condition | $i_{prot}$ (A/m²) |
|---|---|
| Bare steel in still seawater | **0.10 – 0.15** |
| Bare steel in flowing / aerated seawater | **0.15 – 0.30** |
| Well-coated hull (95 % coverage) | **0.001 – 0.005** |
| Tropical warm seawater (high conductivity) | **0.08 – 0.12** |
| Cold, calm seawater | **0.15 – 0.25** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of ICCP in Modern Shipping**
> Today, virtually every VLCC, LNG carrier, container ship, and naval warship is fitted with an ICCP system. The IMO Performance Standard for Protective Coatings (PSPC) requires ballast-tank coating integrity to be supplemented by either ICCP or SACP, making this knowledge directly relevant to ship superintendents, class surveyors, and naval architects.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is explicitly written. No shortcuts or placeholder steps.

## 3.1 Derivation of Anode Resistance by Dwight's Equation (Long Slender Anode)

Consider a long, slender cylindrical anode of length $L$ and equivalent radius $r$ lying in an infinite homogeneous electrolyte of resistivity $\rho$. The current $I$ leaves the anode radially into a semi-infinite medium (the half-space of seawater).

**Step 1 — Surface area of the cylindrical anode (excluding end caps):**

$$
A = 2 \pi r L
$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance of a thin cylindrical shell of radius $x$ and thickness $dx$:**

$$
dR = \dfrac{\rho\, dx}{2\pi x L}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $r$ to infinity (the far-field reference):**

$$
R_a = \int_{r}^{\infty} \dfrac{\rho\, dx}{2\pi x L}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \int_{r}^{\infty} \dfrac{dx}{x}
= \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\Big[\ln x\Big]_{r}^{\infty}
$$

Because the integral diverges at infinity, the **effective far-field radius** is taken as the geometric mean of $L$ and $4L$ (image-effect correction for a half-space), which yields:

$$
R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Replacing radius with diameter** $d = 2r$, so $r = d/2$:

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) = \ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right)
$$

Final form:

$$
\boxed{\,R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]\,}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor 8 in the logarithm accounts for the image-source symmetry of a cylinder lying parallel to a reflecting plane (the sea surface) at infinity.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — ICCP Design for a VLCC Hull

> **[KTU University Exam — Model 14-Mark Style]**
> A VLCC has a wetted hull area of **12,000 m²** in tropical seawater of resistivity **ρ = 0.20 Ω·m**. The protective current density required is **0.10 A/m²**. Design an ICCP system using platinised-titanium anodes of length **L = 1.5 m**, diameter **d = 0.025 m**, utilisation factor **u = 0.85**, anode life target **t = 5 years**, and driving voltage **ΔV = 12 V**.

### Step 1 — Total Current Requirement

$$
I_{total} = A_s \times i_{prot}
$$

$$
I_{total} = 12{,}000 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.10 \text{ A/m}^2 = 1200 \text{ A}
$$

### Step 2 — Resistance of Each Anode (Dwight)

$$
R_a = \dfrac{0.20}{2 \pi (1.5)} \left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8 \times 1.5}{0.025}\right) - 1\right]
$$

Compute the logarithm:

$$
\dfrac{8 \times 1.5}{0.025} = \dfrac{12}{0.025} = 480
$$

$$
\ln(480) = 6.1738
$$

$$
R_a = \dfrac{0.20}{9.4248} \times (6.1738 - 1) = 0.02122 \times 5.1738 = 0.1098\ \Omega
$$

### Step 3 — Current per Anode

$$
I_{anode} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{R_a} = \dfrac{12}{0.1098} = 109.3\ \text{A}
$$

### Step 4 — Number of Anodes Required

$$
N_{anodes} = \dfrac{I_{total}}{I_{anode}} = \dfrac{1200}{109.3} = 10.98
$$

Round **up** to the next whole anode:

$$
N_{anodes} = 11
$$

### Step 5 — Total Anode Mass (Aluminium)

Theoretical capacity of aluminium alloy anode: $C_{th} = 2980$ A·h/kg.

$$
m_{anode} = \dfrac{I_{total} \times t \times 8760}{u \times C_{th}}
$$

$$
m_{anode} = \dfrac{1200 \times 5 \times 8760}{0.85 \times 2980}
$$

Numerator:

$$
1200 \times 5 \times 8760 = 52{,}560{,}000 \text{ A·h}
$$

Denominator:

$$
0.85 \times 2980 = 2533
$$

$$
m_{anode} = \dfrac{52{,}560{,}000}{2533} = 20{,}749\ \text{kg} \approx 20.75 \text{ tonnes}
$$

### Step 6 — Per-Anode Mass

$$
m_{each} = \dfrac{20{,}749}{11} = 1886 \text{ kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the assumption that the design life, utilisation factor, and protective current density are constants. Also check whether the question asks for ICCP (inert anode, MMO/Pt) — in that case you do **not** calculate anode mass because the inert anode consumption is negligible; you only size the **TRU and number of anodes**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SACP vs. ICCP

| Parameter | SACP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | None | AC mains → TRU |
| Driving voltage | 0.20 – 0.50 V | 5 – 50 V |
| Current per anode | 1 – 5 A | 50 – 200 A |
| Anode material | Zn, Al, Mg | Pt/Ti, MMO, Pb–Ag |
| Anode consumption | High (designed for) | Negligible |
| Typical application | Small boats, internals | VLCCs, naval, offshore |
| Risk of over-protection | Low | High (requires RE control) |
| Cost driver | Replacement frequency | Capital + maintenance |
| Maintenance | Periodic replacement | Continuous TRU monitoring |
| Suitability for coated hull | Excellent | Excellent (with RE control) |
| Stray current risk | Low | High |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — Anode Life Verification (SACP)

> **Problem:** A ship is protected by **120 zinc anodes**, each weighing **12 kg**. The total protective current supplied is **360 A**. The utilisation factor for zinc is **0.85**. Zinc theoretical capacity $C_{th} = 820$ A·h/kg. Determine the **life in years** until anode renewal.

### Step 1 — Total Anode Mass

$$
m_{total} = 120 \times 12 = 1440\ \text{kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Effective Usable Mass

$$
m_{eff} = u \times m_{total} = 0.85 \times 1440 = 1224\ \text{kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Ampere-Hours Delivered

$$
Ah_{total} = m_{eff} \times C_{th} = 1224 \times 820 = 1{,}003{,}680 \text{ A·h}
$$

### Step 4 — Life in Years

$$
t = \dfrac{Ah_{total}}{I_{total} \times 8760}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{1{,}003{,}680}{360 \times 8760} = \dfrac{1{,}003{,}680}{3{,}153{,}600} = 0.318 \text{ years}
$$

**Remark:** Only 0.32 years (~16 weeks)! This indicates the anode quantity is grossly insufficient and the ship's class surveyor would reject the design. This shows why ICCP is preferred for large hulls.
