# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric. Labels contain only uppercase, lowercase, digits and spaces — no markdown tags.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ICCP System in a Ship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC["AC SUPPLY 440 V 60 HZ"] --> TRU["TRANSFORMER RECTIFIER UNIT TRU"]
    TRU -->|POSITIVE DC CABLE| ANODES["IMPRESSED CURRENT ANODES MMO TI PT"]
    TRU -->|NEGATIVE DC CABLE| HULL["SHIP HULL CATHODIC SURFACE"]
    ANODES -->|PROTECTIVE CURRENT| SEAWATER["SEAWATER ELECTROLYTE"]
    SEAWATER --> HULL
    RE1["REFERENCE ELECTRODE 1 AG AGCL"] --> CTRL["CONTROL PANEL POTENTIOSTATIC"]
    RE2["REFERENCE ELECTRODE 2 AG AGCL"] --> CTRL
    CTRL -->|SET POINT FEEDBACK SIGNAL| TRU
    CTRL -->|ALARM AND MONITORING| BRIDGE["BRIDGE ALARM DISPLAY"]
    HULL -.->|SAMPLED POTENTIAL| RE1
    HULL -.->|SAMPLED POTENTIAL| RE2
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

1. The **AC mains** of the ship (440 V, 60 Hz, three-phase) is fed into the **TRU**, which steps down and rectifies it to low-voltage DC (typically 12 – 24 V, up to 100 A per leg).
2. The **positive terminal** of the TRU is connected via heavy DC cables to one or more **impressed current anodes** mounted in sea chests or on the outer hull behind dielectric shields.
3. The **negative terminal** of the TRU is bonded to the **ship's hull**, which becomes the cathode.
4. The DC current flows from anode, through seawater, onto the hull, and back to the TRU.
5. Two **reference electrodes** continuously measure the hull-to-seawater potential at different positions.
6. The **control panel** compares this with a set-point (typically −800 mV vs. Ag/AgCl) and adjusts the TRU output to maintain constant protection.
7. An alarm is raised on the bridge if the potential drifts outside ±50 mV of the set-point.

## 4.2 Functional Flow of Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA["SACRIFICIAL ANODE ZN OR AL"] -->|GALVANIC CORROSION| EL["SEAWATER ELECTROLYTE"]
    EL -->|ELECTRON FLOW VIA STRUCTURE| HULL["SHIP HULL PROTECTED CATHODE"]
    HULL -->|METALLIC BOND CABLE OR WELD| SA
    subgraph DRIVING_FORCE["GALVANIC DRIVING FORCE 0.2 TO 0.5 V"]
        SA
        HULL
    end
```

**Interpretation:** No external power is involved. The galvanic potential difference between the sacrificial anode and the hull drives the protective current through the electrolyte. The anode is slowly consumed and must be replaced.

## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — SACP vs ICCP Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START DECIDE PROTECTION TYPE"] --> B["HULL AREA M2"]
    B --> C{"AREA LESS THAN 500 M2 OR SIMPLE STRUCTURE"}
    C -->|YES| D["USE SACP SACRIFICIAL ANODES"]
    C -->|NO| E{"AREA GREATER THAN 500 M2 OR HIGH POWER REQUIRED"}
    E -->|YES| F["USE ICCP IMPRESSED CURRENT SYSTEM"]
    E -->|NO| G{"HIGH RESISTIVITY ELECTROLYTE OR COATED HULL"}
    G -->|YES| F
    G -->|NO| D
    D --> H["CONSIDER PROPELLER AND RUDDER INTERNALS USE HYBRID"]
    F --> H
    H --> I["FINAL SYSTEM INSTALLATION AND CLASS APPROVAL"]
```

## 4.4 Current Distribution Around a Ship Hull (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BOW["BOW HIGH TURBULENCE HIGHER CURRENT DEMAND"] --> N1["ANODE 1"]
    N1 --> MIDSEC["MIDSHIP SECTION COVERED BY ANODE 2 AND 3"]
    MIDSEC --> STERN["STERN AND RUDDER HIGH VELOCITY ANODE 4"]
    STERN --> PROP["PROPELLER SACRIFICIAL ANODE BLOCK"]
    PROP --> BOW
    N1 -.->|REFERENCE ELECTRODE NEAR ANODE| CTRL1["CONTROL LOOP 1"]
    MIDSEC -.->|REFERENCE ELECTRODE NEAR MIDSHIP| CTRL2["CONTROL LOOP 2"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Note:** Reference electrodes are placed near each anode to compensate for **IR drop** (the resistive drop in seawater) and ensure the **true polarised potential** of the hull, not the ohmic potential, satisfies the protection criterion.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**
**"List any three advantages and three disadvantages of the Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection system as applied to ship hulls."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Advantages:*
1. Requires no external power source; operation is purely based on galvanic potential difference.
2. Simple design and installation; anodes can be welded or bolted directly to the hull.
3. Self-regulating current output; safe in hazardous, potentially explosive compartments.

*Disadvantages:*
1. Low driving voltage (0.2 – 0.5 V) limits current output, especially in high-resistivity water.
2. Limited current capacity (1 – 5 A per anode) makes it uneconomical for large hulls.
3. Anodes are consumed and require periodic replacement during dry-docking.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**
**"What is an Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) system? Name its four major components on a ship."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An ICCP system is a method of corrosion protection in which a **direct current** from an external DC source (transformer–rectifier unit) is forced through inert anodes, the seawater electrolyte, and onto the ship's hull, making the hull cathodic. The four major components on a ship are:

1. **Transformer–Rectifier Unit (TRU)** — converts ship's AC supply to DC.
2. **Inert (impressed current) anodes** — platinised titanium or MMO-coated titanium.
3. **Reference electrodes** — Ag/AgCl cells for measuring hull potential.
4. **Control panel** — automatically regulates TRU output to maintain the set-point potential.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question contains sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. The student must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Compare the SACP and ICCP systems used in ships with respect to: (i) driving voltage, (ii) anode material, (iii) current capacity per anode, (iv) maintenance requirement, and (v) typical application. Tabulate your answer."**

#### Model Answer:

| Parameter | SACP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| Driving voltage | 0.20 – 0.50 V (galvanic) | 5 – 50 V (impressed) |
| Anode material | Active — Zn, Al, Mg | Inert — Pt/Ti, MMO, Pb–Ag |
| Current per anode | 1 – 5 A | 50 – 200 A |
| Maintenance | Replace anodes 3 – 5 yearly | Inspect TRU and RE; anode life 10 – 20 yr |
| Application | Small boats, propeller, internals | VLCC, naval, offshore structures |

(1 mark each row, 1 mark for presentation = 6 + 1 = **7 marks**)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"A coated ship hull of wetted area 8000 m² has 99 % coating efficiency. The bare-steel current density is 0.15 A/m², and the coated-holiday current density is 0.005 A/m². Calculate the total current requirement for an ICCP system."**

#### Model Answer:

**Step 1 — Effective coated and bare fractions:**

$$
\text{Coated fraction} = 0.99,\quad \text{Bare fraction} = 0.01
$$

**Step 2 — Effective coated area:**

$$
A_{coat} = 0.99 \times 8000 = 7920\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Effective bare area:**

$$
A_{bare} = 0.01 \times 8000 = 80\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Total current:**

$$
I_{total} = (A_{coat} \times i_{coat}) + (A_{bare} \times i_{bare})
$$

$$
I_{total} = (7920 \times 0.005) + (80 \times 0.15)
$$

$$
I_{total} = 39.6 + 12 = 51.6\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor of 1.10 (KTU recommended):**

$$
I_{design} = 1.10 \times 51.6 = 56.76 \approx 57\ \text{A}
$$

**Conclusion:** A TRU rated at **60 A** at 12 V DC would be specified for this vessel.

(Marks distribution: 1 mark area split, 2 marks current calculation, 2 marks summation, 1 mark safety factor, 1 mark conclusion = **7 marks**)

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of an Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) system for a ship's hull. Label the components clearly."**

#### Model Answer (Descriptive with Diagram Reference):

The ICCP system for a ship's hull works on the principle of **impressing a direct current** from an external source onto the hull to make it cathodic.

**Block Diagram Description (textual form for answer sheet):**

```
AC MAINS (440 V) → TRU → ANODES (Pt/Ti) → SEAWATER → HULL → (back to TRU)
                          ↑                                 ↓
                          |                          REFERENCE ELECTRODE
                          └──────── CONTROL PANEL ←── (hull potential)
```

**Working Steps:**
1. The TRU takes the ship's AC supply (440 V / 60 Hz) and converts it to a regulated low-voltage DC (typically 5 – 24 V).
2. The **positive terminal** of the TRU is connected to **inert anodes** (platinum-coated titanium or MMO) mounted on the hull behind dielectric shields.
3. The **negative terminal** is bonded to the ship's hull, which therefore becomes the cathode.
4. DC current flows from the anode, through seawater, onto the hull, and back to the TRU.
5. Two **Ag/AgCl reference electrodes** mounted near the anodes continuously sense the **hull-to-seawater potential**.
6. The **control panel** compares this potential with the desired set-point of **−800 mV vs. Ag/AgCl**.
7. If the potential becomes too positive (under-protection), the TRU output is increased; if too negative (over-protection → hydrogen embrittlement risk), the TRU output is reduced.
8. The system displays status and alarms on the bridge.

(7 marks: 2 diagram, 3 explanation, 2 component labelling)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"Calculate the resistance and current output of a single cylindrical platinised-titanium anode of length 1.2 m and diameter 0.020 m immersed in seawater of resistivity 0.25 Ω·m, when the impressed voltage is 10 V."**

#### Model Answer:

**Step 1 — Dwight's formula for cylindrical anode resistance:**

$$
R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
R_a = \dfrac{0.25}{2\pi (1.2)} \left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8 \times 1.2}{0.020}\right) - 1\right]
$$

Compute:

$$
\dfrac{8 \times 1.2}{0.020} = \dfrac{9.6}{0.020} = 480
$$

$$
\ln(480) = 6.1738
$$

$$
R_a = \dfrac{0.25}{7.5398} \times (6.1738 - 1) = 0.03316 \times 5.1738 = 0.1716\ \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Current output:**

$$
I_{anode} = \dfrac{V}{R_a} = \dfrac{10}{0.1716} = 58.28\ \text{A}
$$

**Conclusion:** Each anode delivers approximately **58 A** at 10 V impressed. To protect a hull requiring 500 A, **9 anodes** would be needed.

(Marks: 1 formula, 2 substitution, 2 computation, 1 conclusion, 1 unit consistency = **7 marks**)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Module 4 questions — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** — Always multiply the calculated current by **1.10 – 1.25** to account for coating deterioration, marine growth, and anode ageing. Examiners deduct 1 mark if omitted.
>
> 2. **Confusing sacrificial vs. impressed-current anode materials** — Writing *"Pt/Ti"* for a SACP question or *"zinc"* for an ICCP question instantly scores zero for that line.
>
> 3. **Using inconsistent units** — Resistivity must be in **Ω·m** (not Ω·cm); length in metres; area in m². The Dwight equation gives resistance in Ω only if SI units are used.
>
> 4. **Ignoring anode utilisation factor** — For SACP, always use $u = 0.85$ (Al), $u = 0.90$ (Zn), or $u = 0.50$ (Mg). Examiners specifically look for this factor.
>
> 5. **Not stating the protection criterion** — Any cathodic-protection answer should explicitly mention the criterion: **−850 mV vs. Cu/CuSO₄** *or* **−800 mV vs. Ag/AgCl**. Without this, full marks are not awarded.
>
> 6. **Forgetting stray-current interference** — When discussing ICCP disadvantages, examiners expect a mention of *stray current corrosion of nearby moored vessels*. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Drawing Mermaid-style diagrams on paper** — In KTU exam scripts, always draw a *neat labelled block diagram*. Do not paste printed diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — SACP vs. ICCP)**

- ✅ **SACP** = passive, galvanic; uses Zn / Al / Mg anodes; low voltage (≈ 0.25 V); suitable for small structures; current 1 – 5 A per anode.
- ✅ **ICCP** = active, impressed; uses Pt/Ti or MMO anodes; voltage 5 – 50 V; suitable for large ships; current up to 200 A per anode.
- ✅ **Driving-force order:** ICCP > SACP. **Cost order:** ICCP (capital) > SACP (replacement).
- ✅ **Protection criterion:** $E \le -0.800\ V$ vs. Ag/AgCl **or** $E \le -0.850\ V$ vs. Cu/CuSO₄.
- ✅ **Over-protection limit:** $E > -1.10\ V$ vs. Ag/AgCl → hydrogen evolution → coating blistering and **hydrogen embrittlement of high-strength steel**.
- ✅ **Dwight equation** for long slender anode: $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$.
- ✅ **Theoretical capacities** — Zn = 820 A·h/kg; Al = 2980 A·h/kg; Mg = 1230 A·h/kg.
- ✅ **Typical protective current densities in seawater** — bare steel 0.10 – 0.30 A/m²; coated hull 0.001 – 0.005 A/m².
- ✅ **Number of anodes** = $I_{total} / I_{anode}$; **always round up**.
- ✅ **TRU sizing:** $V_{TRU} = I_{total} \times (R_a + R_{cable} + R_{cathode})$.
- ✅ **Reference electrodes** are mandatory in ICCP; one per anode or per zone; they enable potentiostatic control.
- ✅ **Dielectric shields** are mandatory around anode outlets to prevent current crowding.
- ✅ **Anode utilisation factor** — Al ≈ 0.85, Zn ≈ 0.90, Mg ≈ 0.50.
- ✅ **Hybrid systems** — modern ships use **ICCP for the main hull** and **SACP for propellers, rudders, and sea-chest internals** because ICCP current cannot easily reach shadowed internals.
- ✅ **Stray-current corrosion** is a critical ICCP disadvantage — bonds to shore power and nearby vessels must be carefully managed.
- ✅ **IMO PSPC** requires ballast tanks to have either SACP or ICCP as secondary protection.
